topics.title: Common Engineering Materials and Its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Common Engineering Materials and Its Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Engineering Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Materials** are the carefully selected substances — metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics, and composites — that are processed and shaped into useful components, structures, or machines. The *science of selecting the right material* and *the geometry of the part* so that it will perform its function safely, economically, and reliably is the very foundation of **Machine Design**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Common Engineering Materials* refers to the standardized families of materials covered in the first module of Machine Design, with emphasis on the **mechanical, physical, and chemical properties** that govern a designer's choice when proportioning machine elements such as shafts, gears, springs, and bearings.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Machine Design

Every machine element is a product of **two intertwined decisions**:

1. **What material to use?** (Material Selection)
2. **What shape and size?** (Geometric / Dimensional Design)

$$
\text{Machine Element} \;=\; f(\text{Material Properties},\; \text{Geometry},\; \text{Loading},\; \text{Service Conditions})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the wrong material is chosen, even a perfectly dimensioned component will fail. Conversely, a perfect material with a poor design will also fail. This is why *Module 1* of Machine Design always begins with materials and properties.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Think of designing a machine element like **building a human body**:

| Body Part | Material Analogy | Required Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bones (Femur)** | High-strength steel | High stiffness, compressive strength |
| **Tendons** | Spring steel / elastomers | High resilience, fatigue strength |
| **Skin** | Polymers / composites | Toughness, flexibility |
| **Cartilage** | Polymers (Nylon, PTFE) | Low friction, wear resistance |
| **Blood vessels** | Flexible polymers | Ductility, corrosion resistance |

Just as a doctor cannot use bone material for skin, a machine designer cannot use brittle cast iron where a ductile mild steel is needed. **Material selection precedes dimensional design** — this is the golden rule of KTU Machine Design.

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Engineering Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET411) expects students to be able to **classify, identify, and recommend** engineering materials based on service conditions.

Engineering materials are broadly classified into **five families**:

1. **Metals & Alloys** (Ferrous and Non-ferrous) — Iron, Steel, Cast Iron, Aluminum, Copper, Brass, Bronze.
2. **Polymers** (Plastics, Elastomers) — Nylon, PVC, PTFE, Natural Rubber.
3. **Ceramics** — Alumina $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, Silicon Carbide $\text{SiC}$, Glass.
4. **Composites** — Fiber-reinforced plastics (FRP), Carbon-fiber composites, Concrete.
5. **Advanced / Smart Materials** — Shape-memory alloys (Nitinol), Piezoelectric materials.

The **Young's Modulus (E)** values that you must remember:

| Material Family | Approximate $E$ (GPa) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Steels** | **200** |
| **Aluminum Alloys** | **70** |
| **Copper Alloys** | **100 – 120** |
| **Polymers** | **1 – 5** |
| **Ceramics** | **200 – 400** |

This spread of moduli immediately tells you **why** we use polymers for flexibility and ceramics for stiffness, while steel sits in the sweet spot for most load-bearing machine parts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Material Domains — Why Machine Designers Care

Every engineering material is described by a triplet of property families:

- **Mechanical Properties** — govern behaviour under load (the heart of Machine Design).
- **Physical Properties** — density, melting point, thermal conductivity, electrical resistivity.
- **Chemical Properties** — corrosion resistance, oxidation behaviour, chemical inertness.

For PEPET411, the **mechanical properties are paramount**, because the design equations (torsion, bending, fatigue) all depend on them.

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties — The Engineer's Vocabulary

### 2.2.1 Strength
The ability of a material to **resist applied forces without failure**.

- **Tensile Strength / Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_u$):** Maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched. Unit: **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$ or $S_{yt}$):** Stress at which a material begins to deform plastically (permanently). This is the **single most important value** for ductile material design.
- **Compressive Strength:** Resistance to crushing.
- **Shear Strength ($\tau$):** Resistance to tangential (sliding) loads.
- **Fatigue Strength / Endurance Limit ($S_e$ or $S_{en}$):** Stress below which a material can endure an infinite number of stress cycles ($\approx 10^6$ to $10^8$ for steels).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for design stress, the standard KTU practice is to use $\sigma_y$ (yield strength) as the reference for ductile materials and $\sigma_u$ (ultimate strength) for brittle materials.

### 2.2.2 Elasticity
The property by which a material **returns to its original shape** after the removal of the applied load (within the elastic limit).

Quantified by **Young's Modulus ($E$)** or **Modulus of Elasticity**:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{(Hooke's Law, within elastic limit)}
$$

where $\sigma$ is stress in MPa and $\varepsilon$ is the dimensionless strain.

### 2.2.3 Plasticity
The ability of a material to **deform permanently without rupture** under applied load. Plastic deformation enables **forging, rolling, extrusion, and pressing** operations.

### 2.2.4 Ductility
The ability of a material to be **drawn into wires** or stretched substantially before fracture. Measured by **percentage elongation (%EL)** and **percentage reduction in area (%RA)** in a tensile test.

> [!NOTE]
> A ductile material gives **visible warning before failure** — this is why ductile materials are preferred in dynamic / impact-loaded machine parts.

### 2.2.5 Brittleness
The opposite of ductility. Brittle materials (e.g., cast iron, ceramics) **fracture with little or no plastic deformation**. They have **no yield point** — failure is sudden.

### 2.2.6 Toughness
The ability of a material to **absorb energy and plastically deform before fracturing**. Toughness = area under the entire stress-strain curve. Measured by the **Impact Test (Izod / Charpy)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toughness $\neq$ Hardness.** A hard material (e.g., glass) is usually brittle, while a tough material (e.g., mild steel) absorbs energy.

### 2.2.7 Hardness
The ability of a material to **resist localized plastic deformation** (scratching, indentation, abrasion). Common scales: **Brinell (BHN), Rockwell (HRC, HRB), Vickers (VHN), Shore**.

Approximate conversion used in KTU problems:
$$
\text{BHN} \;\approx\; 0.95 \times S_{ut} \quad \text{(for steels, with } S_{ut} \text{ in MPa, dividing by 0.95 in ksi)}
$$

### 2.2.8 Stiffness
The resistance of a material to **elastic deformation** under load. Quantified by $E$ and the geometric parameter $I$ (area moment of inertia). Stiffness of a member = $EI$ (flexural), $GJ$ (torsional).

### 2.2.9 Resilience
The capacity of a material to **absorb energy within the elastic limit**. Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$) = area under the linear portion of the stress-strain curve:

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

### 2.2.10 Creep
The **time-dependent permanent deformation** of a material under constant load at elevated temperatures. Critical in steam turbines, boilers, jet engines. *Counter-measure:* use creep-resistant alloys (Inconel, Nimonic).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 1. Every equation here is referenced in KTU past-year question papers (2018–2023) for the Machine Design subject.

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Application in Design |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Young's Modulus** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | GPa | Deflection, stiffness |
| 2 | **Shear Modulus** | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | GPa | Torsion of shafts |
| 3 | **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}}$ | dimensionless | 3-D stress state |
| 4 | **Modulus of Resilience** | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$ | $\text{J/m}^3$ or MPa | Energy absorbed in elastic region |
| 5 | **Modulus of Toughness** | $U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | $\text{J/m}^3$ or MPa | Energy absorbed to fracture |
| 6 | **Endurance Limit (Steel)** | $S_{en} = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (for $S_{ut} < 1400$ MPa) | MPa | Fatigue design (shigley-based) |
| 7 | **Endurance Limit (Non-ferrous)** | $S_{en} \approx 0.4 \, S_{ut}$ | MPa | Aluminum / bronze design |
| 8 | **Factor of Safety** | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ | dimensionless | Safety margin |
| 9 | **Design Stress (Ductile)** | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{S_{yt}}{FOS}$ | MPa | Shafts, bolts (yield-based) |
| 10 | **Design Stress (Brittle)** | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{S_{ut}}{FOS}$ | MPa | Castings, columns (ultimate-based) |
| 11 | **BHN–Sut relation** | $S_{ut} \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$ (MPa) | MPa | Quick material selection |
| 12 | **Hardness Conversion** | $\text{BHN} \approx 0.95 \times S_{ut} \text{ (ksi)}$ | — | (as per Shigley) |

> [!NOTE]
> The relation $S_{en} = 0.5 S_{ut}$ is **valid only for steels with $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa**. For higher strength steels and for non-ferrous metals, the endurance limit is taken as $0.4 S_{ut}$ or the actual S-N curve data. This is a frequent KTU question.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace (aluminum 7075-T6, titanium Ti-6Al-4V):** high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Automotive (cast iron block, forged steel crankshaft):** toughness + machinability.
- **Pressure vessels (SA 516 steel):** high yield strength + weldability.
- **Bearings (bronze, white metal, PTFE):** low friction + wear resistance.
- **Springs (spring steel EN 47, oil-tempered wire):** high resilience + fatigue strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** No single material is "best." Material selection is always a **trade-off** between strength, weight, cost, manufacturability, corrosion resistance, and service temperature. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to justify material choice with **two or more properties**, not a single reason.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modulus of Resilience from the Stress-Strain Curve

The modulus of resilience $U_r$ is the **area under the linear (elastic) portion** of the engineering stress-strain diagram, from zero up to the yield point.

**Step 1: Define the geometry of the elastic region.**
Within the elastic limit, the stress-strain curve is a straight line from the origin $(0,0)$ to the yield point $(\varepsilon_y, \sigma_y)$.

**Step 2: Identify the area as a right triangle.**
The region is a right-angled triangle with:
- Base = $\varepsilon_y$
- Height = $\sigma_y$

**Step 3: Write the area expression.**

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \, \varepsilon_y \, \sigma_y
$$

**Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law to eliminate $\varepsilon_y$.**

Within the elastic region: $\sigma_y = E \, \varepsilon_y \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \varepsilon_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$.

**Step 5: Substitute into the area expression.**

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}\;}
$$

This represents the **strain energy stored per unit volume** in a material loaded elastically up to the yield point.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulus of Toughness for a Linear-Elastic, Perfectly Plastic Material

For an idealized elastic-perfectly plastic material (e.g., mild steel), the area under the entire stress-strain curve is the **modulus of toughness**.

**Step 1: Split the area into two regions.**
- Triangle (elastic): from $0$ to $\varepsilon_y$.
- Rectangle (plastic plateau): from $\varepsilon_y$ to $\varepsilon_f$, height $\sigma_y$.

**Step 2: Compute each area separately.**

$$
\text{Triangle area} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \, \sigma_y \, \varepsilon_y
$$

$$
\text{Rectangle area} \;=\; \sigma_y \cdot (\varepsilon_f - \varepsilon_y)
$$

**Step 3: Sum and use Hooke's Law.**

$$
U_t \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \sigma_y (\varepsilon_f - \varepsilon_y)
$$

$$
U_t \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{E} + \sigma_y \left( \varepsilon_f - \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U_t \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} + \sigma_y \left( \varepsilon_f - \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \right)\;}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** A material with high $U_t$ is suitable for **impact-loaded components** (e.g., hammer, gear teeth, connecting rod).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Factor of Safety and Allowable / Design Stress

**Step 1: Define working / applied stress.** $\sigma_w = \dfrac{P}{A}$ (tensile member under axial load $P$).

**Step 2: Define the limiting strength.** $\sigma_{\text{lim}}$ = $\sigma_y$ (ductile) or $\sigma_u$ (brittle).

**Step 3: Express Factor of Safety (FOS or $N$).**

$$
FOS \;=\; \frac{\text{Limiting strength}}{\text{Working stress}} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\text{lim}}}{\sigma_w}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for working (allowable) stress.**

$$
\sigma_d \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\text{lim}}}{FOS}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A mild-steel tie-rod of area $A = 200 \text{ mm}^2$ carries a tensile load of $40$ kN. Given $S_{yt} = 250$ MPa, find the factor of safety.

$$
\sigma_w \;=\; \frac{P}{A} \;=\; \frac{40\,000 \text{ N}}{200 \text{ mm}^2} \;=\; 200 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
FOS \;=\; \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_w} \;=\; \frac{250}{200} \;=\; 1.25
$$

**Step 5: Conclude the design margin.**

$$
\boxed{\;FOS = 1.25 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{The design is acceptable (typically FOS} \geq 1.5 \text{ for static loading).}\;}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Material Families

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is the **most-asked comparison table** in KTU Module 1 viva and written exams. Memorize the trends (↑ = high, ↓ = low).

| Property | Mild Steel | Cast Iron | Aluminum | Copper | Polymers (Nylon) | Ceramics |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Density** | Medium (7.85 g/cc) | High (7.2 g/cc) | Low (2.7 g/cc) | High (8.9 g/cc) | Very Low (1.1 g/cc) | Medium (3.9 g/cc) |
| **Young's $E$** | 200 GPa | 100–170 GPa | 70 GPa | 110 GPa | 2–4 GPa | 300 GPa |
| **UTS $S_{ut}$** | 400–550 MPa | 150–400 MPa | 90–570 MPa | 220–400 MPa | 50–90 MPa | 200–1000 MPa |
| **Yield $S_{yt}$** | 250 MPa | None (brittle) | 35–500 MPa | 70–330 MPa | 40–80 MPa | (no plastic range) |
| **Ductility (%EL)** | 25–35 | 1–3 (very low) | 12–30 | 30–50 | 20–100 | < 1 (brittle) |
| **Toughness** | High | Low | Medium-High | High | Medium | Very Low |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Low (rusts) | Low | Good (oxide film) | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Machinability** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Poor | Very Poor |
| **Cost** | Low | Very Low | Medium | High | Low | High |
| **Typical Use** | Shafts, gears, bolts | Engine blocks, housings | Aircraft parts, pistons | Conductors, bushings | Gears, seals, bearings | Cutting tools, insulators |

## 3.5 Property-Selection Logic Flow for Machine Components

Given a component (e.g., a **gear**), the designer steps through this hierarchy:

1. **Loading type** → Bending + Contact (Hertzian) fatigue
2. **Key required property** → High surface hardness + tough core
3. **Material family** → Through-hardened alloy steel (e.g., EN 36, 16MnCr5)
4. **Process route** → Case carburizing + quenching + tempering
5. **Final FOS** → Typically 1.5 to 2.0 for industrial gears

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always state **two reasons** when justifying a material — strength property + manufacturing/cost property. Single-reason answers are penalized.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Engineering Materials

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical classification of engineering materials as covered in Module 1. It is the **canonical block diagram** expected in KTU Module 1 descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENGINEERING MATERIALS] --> B[Metals and Alloys]
    A --> C[Polymers]
    A --> D[Ceramics]
    A --> E[Composites]
    A --> F[Advanced Materials]

    B --> B1[Ferrous Metals]
    B --> B2[Non Ferrous Metals]

    B1 --> B1a[Cast Iron]
    B1 --> B1b[Steels]
    B1b --> B1b1[Low Carbon Steel]
    B1b --> B1b2[Medium Carbon Steel]
    B1b --> B1b3[High Carbon Steel]
    B1b --> B1b4[Alloy Steel]

    B2 --> B2a[Aluminum and Alloys]
    B2 --> B2b[Copper and Alloys]
    B2b --> B2b1[Brass]
    B2b --> B2b2[Bronze]
    B2 --> B2c[Magnesium Alloys]
    B2 --> B2d[Titanium Alloys]

    C --> C1[Thermoplastics]
    C --> C2[Thermosetting Plastics]
    C --> C3[Elastomers]

    D --> D1[Traditional Ceramics]
    D --> D2[Engineering Ceramics]
    D2 --> D2a[Alumina]
    D2 --> D2b[Silicon Carbide]
    D2 --> D2c[Zirconia]

    E --> E1[FRP]
    E --> E2[Carbon Fiber Composites]
    E --> E3[Metal Matrix Composites]

    F --> F1[Shape Memory Alloys]
    F --> F2[Piezoelectric Materials]
    F --> F3[Biomaterials]
```

## 4.2 Mechanical-Properties Mind Map

> [!NOTE]
> This second Mermaid block groups the **mechanical properties** discussed in Section 2.2 by their physical meaning. Use this when answering "Classify mechanical properties" type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[MECHANICAL PROPERTIES] --> P1[Strength Family]
    P --> P2[Deformation Family]
    P --> P3[Energy Family]
    P --> P4[Failure Family]

    P1 --> P1a[Tensile Strength]
    P1 --> P1b[Yield Strength]
    P1 --> P1c[Compressive Strength]
    P1 --> P1d[Shear Strength]
    P1 --> P1e[Fatigue Strength]

    P2 --> P2a[Elasticity E]
    P2 --> P2b[Plasticity]
    P2 --> P2c[Stiffness]
    P2 --> P2d[Ductility]
    P2 --> P2e[Malleability]

    P3 --> P3a[Resilience Ur]
    P3 --> P3b[Toughness Ut]

    P4 --> P4a[Brittleness]
    P4 --> P4b[Hardness BHN HRC]
    P4 --> P4c[Creep]
    P4 --> P4d[Wear]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Material Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **design flow** a KTU student must follow when answering "Select a material for..." type questions. The Mermaid block renders a logical decision tree (not a physical drawing, as the topic is abstract).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify Component Function] --> Q1{Loading Type?}
    Q1 --> Q1a[Static]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Dynamic]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Impact]
    Q1 --> Q1d[Cyclic Fatigue]

    Q1a --> S1[Pick by Yield Strength]
    Q1b --> S2[Pick by Toughness]
    Q1c --> S3[Pick by Impact Energy]
    Q1d --> S4[Pick by Endurance Limit]

    S1 --> M1[Material: Medium Carbon Steel or Alloy Steel]
    S2 --> M2[Material: Mild Steel or Ductile Iron]
    S3 --> M3[Material: Toughened Steel or Ductile Polymer]
    S4 --> M4[Material: Surface Hardened Alloy Steel]

    M1 --> C1{Cost Constraint?}
    M2 --> C2{Service Temperature?}
    M3 --> C3{Weight Sensitive?}
    M4 --> C4{Corrosive Environment?}

    C1 --> C1a[Yes: Carbon Steel]
    C1 --> C1b[No: Alloy Steel]
    C2 --> C2a[High: Heat Resistant Alloy]
    C2 --> C2b[Normal: Standard Steel]
    C3 --> C3a[Yes: Aluminum Alloy]
    C3 --> C3b[No: Steel]
    C4 --> C4a[Yes: Stainless or Coated Steel]
    C4 --> C4b[No: Plain Carbon Steel]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Stress-Strain Diagram with Key Property Markers

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid cannot directly plot the smooth stress-strain curve. The following block-diagram captures the **key transition points and the area-based properties** students are expected to label.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin O] --> B[Proportional Limit]
    B --> C[Elastic Limit]
    C --> D[Yield Point sigma_y]
    D --> E[Ultimate Strength sigma_u]
    E --> F[Fracture Point sigma_f]

    B --> B1[Region 1: Linear Elasticity]
    C --> C1[Region 2: Non linear Elastic]
    D --> D1[Region 3: Plastic Flow]
    E --> E1[Region 4: Strain Hardening then Necking]

    D --> D2[Area under curve up to D = Modulus of Resilience]
    F --> F2[Total area under curve = Modulus of Toughness]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization tip:** When drawing a stress-strain diagram by hand, always mark the **four characteristic points** — Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, Ultimate Point — and shade the two areas (resilience triangle and toughness full area). This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are model questions based on actual KTU University Exam patterns for PEPET411 (Machine Design) Module 1. Each carries 3 marks and tests definitional/conceptual clarity at **Bloom's Level: Remember / Understand**.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEPET411]
**Define the following mechanical properties of engineering materials with their SI units:**
**(a) Toughness**    **(b) Resilience**    **(c) Hardness**

### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Toughness:**
Toughness is the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation before fracture. It is measured as the **total area under the stress-strain curve** up to the point of fracture. SI unit: **Joule per cubic metre ($\text{J/m}^3$)** or equivalently **MPa** (since $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ MJ/m}^3$).

**(b) Resilience:**
Resilience is the ability of a material to absorb energy within the **elastic limit** and release it on unloading. It is measured as the area under the **linear (elastic) portion** of the stress-strain curve. SI unit: **$\text{J/m}^3$** or **MPa**.

$$
U_r \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

**(c) Hardness:**
Hardness is the ability of a material to resist **localized plastic deformation** caused by indentation, scratching, or abrasion. Common scales: Brinell, Rockwell, Vickers, Shore. Unitless index value (BHN, HRC, VHN, Shore A/D).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2022, PEPET411]
**Distinguish between ductile and brittle materials with two examples each.**

### **Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Plastic deformation** | Significant (large %EL) | Negligible |
| **Yield point** | Clearly defined | Absent |
| **Fracture type** | Cup-and-cone (ductile fracture) | Cleavage / shatter |
| **Warning before failure** | Yes (visible necking) | No (sudden) |
| **Energy absorbed (toughness)** | High | Low |
| **Examples** | Mild steel, copper, aluminum | Cast iron, glass, ceramics |
| **Design reference strength** | $\sigma_y$ (yield) | $\sigma_u$ (ultimate) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) format: each Module has at least one 14-mark question with **internal choice** (either-or). Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **any one** of the two options.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, PEPET411, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify the common engineering materials used in machine design with a neat flowchart. List **two important properties** of each major family.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine component made of mild steel is subjected to a tensile load of 50 kN. The cross-sectional area is 250 mm². The yield strength of the material is 320 MPa and the ultimate tensile strength is 480 MPa. Determine: **(i)** the working stress, **(ii)** the factor of safety based on yield, and **(iii)** the factor of safety based on ultimate strength. Comment on which is more appropriate and why.   **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Classification of Engineering Materials (Module 1 syllabus):**

1. **Ferrous Metals** — Cast Iron, Carbon Steels, Alloy Steels
   - *Properties:* High strength, good machinability, magnetic, susceptible to corrosion.

2. **Non-Ferrous Metals** — Aluminum, Copper, Brass, Bronze, Titanium
   - *Properties:* Corrosion resistance, low density, good thermal/electrical conductivity.

3. **Polymers** — Thermoplastics (Nylon, PVC), Thermosets (Bakelite), Elastomers (Rubber)
   - *Properties:* Low density, chemical resistance, low modulus, good toughness (in elastomers).

4. **Ceramics** — Alumina, Silicon Carbide, Zirconia
   - *Properties:* Very high hardness, high temperature resistance, brittleness.

5. **Composites** — FRP, Carbon-fiber, Metal-matrix composites
   - *Properties:* High strength-to-weight ratio, tailored properties, anisotropy.

*(The flow-chart drawn is the same as the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. Award 4 marks for the chart and 3 marks for the property listing.)*

### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $P = 50$ kN $= 50\,000$ N
- $A = 250 \text{ mm}^2$
- $S_{yt} = 320$ MPa
- $S_{ut} = 480$ MPa

**(i) Working stress:**

$$
\sigma_w \;=\; \frac{P}{A} \;=\; \frac{50\,000}{250} \;=\; 200 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Factor of safety based on yield:**

$$
FOS_y \;=\; \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma_w} \;=\; \frac{320}{200} \;=\; 1.6
$$

**(iii) Factor of safety based on ultimate strength:**

$$
FOS_u \;=\; \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_w} \;=\; \frac{480}{200} \;=\; 2.4
$$

**Comment:**
Mild steel is a **ductile material** with a clearly defined yield point. Hence the **factor of safety based on yield ($FOS_y = 1.6$)** is more appropriate, because yielding marks the onset of permanent (plastic) deformation. Using $FOS_u$ is conservative but does not reflect the actual failure criterion for ductile materials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert kN to N. Working stress must be in MPa, which requires N and mm² (since $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$).
> 2. **Wrong reference strength:** Using $S_{ut}$ for ductile materials is a **2-mark penalty**. Always use $S_{yt}$ for ductile and $S_{ut}$ for brittle.
> 3. **No comment on which is appropriate:** Even with correct numbers, omitting the discussion (why yield is used) costs 1 mark.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice to Question A*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEPET411, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled **engineering stress-strain diagram** for a ductile material like mild steel, indicate and explain: **(i)** Proportional Limit, **(ii)** Elastic Limit, **(iii)** Yield Point, **(iv)** Ultimate Tensile Strength, **(v)** Fracture Point, **(vi)** Modulus of Resilience, **(vii)** Modulus of Toughness.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft is to be made from EN 8 steel having $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa. The shaft experiences a fully reversed bending stress. Find the **endurance limit** of the material. If the actual alternating bending stress in the shaft is 120 MPa and the mean stress is 40 MPa, calculate the factor of safety using the **Soderberg criterion**.   **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Engineering Stress-Strain Diagram for Mild Steel:**

The diagram (drawn on graph paper in the exam) is the same as the block schematic in Section 4.4. The key points are:

- **Origin (O):** Zero stress, zero strain.
- **Proportional Limit (A):** The maximum stress up to which stress is **directly proportional** to strain (Hooke's Law valid). Slope = $E$.
- **Elastic Limit (B):** The maximum stress up to which the material returns to its original dimensions on unloading. Slightly above A.
- **Yield Point (C, $\sigma_y$):** Stress at which material begins to **deform plastically** without any increase in load. For mild steel, two points — Upper Yield Point and Lower Yield Point.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (D, $\sigma_u$):** **Maximum** stress the material can withstand. Beyond this, necking begins.
- **Fracture Point (E, $\sigma_f$):** Stress at which the material actually breaks.

**Areas:**
- **Modulus of Resilience** = Area under curve from O to C (triangular area) = $\dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$.
- **Modulus of Toughness** = Total area under curve from O to E.

*(Award 1 mark each for the seven labelled items — 4 marks — and 3 marks for the two area-based derivations.)*

### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa
- $\sigma_a = 120$ MPa (alternating stress)
- $\sigma_m = 40$ MPa (mean stress)
- $S_y = 320$ MPa (assumed standard for EN 8, or as given in question)

**Step 1: Endurance Limit.**

For steel, the rotating-beam endurance limit is:

$$
S_{en} \;=\; 0.5 \times S_{ut} \;=\; 0.5 \times 540 \;=\; 270 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Soderberg Equation.**

$$
\frac{1}{FOS} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_a}{S_{en}} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$
\frac{1}{FOS} \;=\; \frac{120}{270} + \frac{40}{320} \;=\; 0.4444 + 0.1250 \;=\; 0.5694
$$

**Step 4: Solve for FOS.**

$$
FOS \;=\; \frac{1}{0.5694} \;\approx\; 1.756
$$

**Conclusion:** $FOS \approx 1.76$ is acceptable for general-purpose shaft design (typical KTU range: 1.5 to 2.0).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Soderberg vs. Goodman confusion:** Soderberg uses $S_y$ in the denominator of the mean-stress term; Goodman uses $S_{ut}$. Writing Goodman in a Soderberg problem costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 0.5:** Some students write $S_{en} = S_{ut}$ directly — this is wrong. Always remember the **empirical 0.5 factor for steels**.
> 3. **No conclusion / no comment on FOS acceptability:** Lose 1 mark for not stating whether the design is safe.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second revision checklist** before the KTU Module 1 viva or written exam. Tick off each point.

- [ ] **Engineering Material** = the substance from which a machine part is made. **Machine Design** = material + geometry + loading + service condition.
- [ ] **Five families:** Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, Composites, Advanced Materials.
- [ ] **Ferrous** = iron-based (cast iron, steel); **Non-ferrous** = aluminum, copper, brass, bronze, titanium.
- [ ] **Strength** = resistance to failure; **Stiffness** = resistance to elastic deformation; **Hardness** = resistance to surface indentation.
- [ ] **Ductility** = % elongation; **Toughness** = area under stress-strain curve; **Resilience** = area under elastic portion.
- [ ] **Modulus of Resilience:** $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$.
- [ ] **Endurance Limit (Steel):** $S_{en} = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (for $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa).
- [ ] **BHN–UTS relation (Shigley):** $S_{ut} \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$ (in MPa).
- [ ] **Factor of Safety (Ductile):** based on $S_{yt}$.   **FOS (Brittle):** based on $S_{ut}$.
- [ ] **Soderberg:** $\dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_{en}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y}$.   **Goodman:** $\dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_{en}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$.
- [ ] **Ductile materials** give warning before failure; **Brittle materials** fail suddenly — always use $S_{ut}$ for brittle design.
- [ ] **Mild steel** = king of machine design (tough, ductile, machinable, weldable, low cost).
- [ ] **Cast iron** = cheap, brittle, compressive-strong, used for housings and engine blocks.
- [ ] **Aluminum** = lightweight, corrosion-resistant, used in aerospace.
- [ ] **Always state 2+ reasons** when justifying a material choice in KTU answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for PEPET411 Module 1:**
> *"The right material is the one that survives the load, the environment, and the budget — and gives enough warning before it fails."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Material Selection** is the systematic engineering process of identifying the most suitable engineering material from the available alternatives that satisfies a set of conflicting design requirements — including mechanical loading, environmental conditions, manufacturing constraints, cost targets, weight limits, and service life — while satisfying the stipulated **factors of safety** and **code requirements** prescribed in the relevant design standard.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design (PEPET411)**, the selection of materials is treated as a **constrained optimization problem** in which the designer maximizes (or minimizes) a specific *performance metric* (e.g., strength-to-weight ratio, stiffness-to-cost ratio) subject to *functional constraints* (deflection, stress, fatigue life) and *geometric constraints* (length, cross-section, shape).

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$
\text{Material Selection} = \max f(\vec{P}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(\vec{P}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

where:
- $f(\vec{P})$ is the **objective function** (e.g., minimize cost, minimize mass)
- $g_i(\vec{P})$ are the **constraint functions** (e.g., stress, deflection, temperature)
- $\vec{P}$ is the **property vector** of the material (yield strength, density, modulus, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy (NEP 2020 alignment), material selection questions are tagged at the **Analyze (L4)** and **Evaluate (L5)** cognitive levels. Students must justify *why* a particular material is chosen — not merely *which* one.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef's Pantry" Model

Imagine you are a **chef** preparing a dish for a guest who demands:
- The food must be **spicy but not too oily**
- It must be **cheap to prepare**
- It must **not spoil** in summer heat
- It must be **ready in 30 minutes**

You walk into the pantry and pick from **dozens of ingredients** (potatoes, lentils, chicken, tofu, paneer, mushrooms...). You don't pick the *strongest* or the *cheapest* ingredient — you pick the one that best **balances all the constraints** simultaneously.

Material selection works the **exact same way**:

| Chef's Pantry | Machine Design |
|---|---|
| Ingredients | Engineering materials (steel, aluminium, polymers, composites) |
| Guest's demands | Design constraints (load, temperature, wear) |
| Cooking time | Manufacturability (cast, forge, machine, 3D-print) |
| Budget | Cost per kg / cost per part |
| Spiciness meter | Material property (e.g., yield strength) |
| Final chosen ingredient | **Optimum material** |

The **chef's "tasting"** is analogous to the designer's **prototype testing and FEA validation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A material is *never* universally "best" — it is **best for a specific combination of design requirements**. The same shaft could be made of alloy steel (high strength), aluminium (lightweight), or carbon-fiber composite (corrosion-resistant + light) — depending on the *application*.

---

## 1.3 Primary Drivers of Material Selection (The "Big Five")

Per KTU 2024 module expectations, every material selection problem pivots around these **five orthogonal axes**:

1. **Mechanical Property Requirements** — strength, stiffness, toughness, fatigue endurance, hardness, wear resistance.
2. **Physical/Chemical Property Requirements** — density, thermal conductivity, electrical resistivity, corrosion resistance, oxidation resistance.
3. **Manufacturing Considerations** — castability, forgeability, machinability, weldability, formability, 3D-printability.
4. **Economic Considerations** — raw material cost, processing cost, life-cycle cost, availability.
5. **Service & Environmental Conditions** — operating temperature, humidity, chemical exposure, UV radiation, cyclic loading, impact loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The most widely used performance index in KTU board problems is the **specific strength** $\left(\sigma_y / \rho\right)$ and **specific stiffness** $\left(E / \rho\right)$, where $\sigma_y$ is yield strength, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $\rho$ is density. The SI unit is **N·m/kg** or equivalently **Pa·m³/kg**.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Engineering Materials (The Material Family Tree)

Engineering materials are broadly classified into **four families**:

```
                        ┌──────────────────┐
                        │  Engineering     │
                        │   Materials      │
                        └────────┬─────────┘
       ┌───────────────┬─────────┼──────────┬─────────────────┐
       ▼               ▼         ▼          ▼                 ▼
  ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
  │ Metals  │    │ Polymers │  │Ceramics│ │Composites│  │ Smart    │
  │ & Alloys│    │ (Plastics)│  │ & Glass│ │(FRP, MMC)│  │ Materials│
  └─────────┘    └──────────┘  └──────┘  └──────────┘   └──────────┘
```

| Family | Examples | Typical Property Profile | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metals & Alloys** | Mild steel, alloy steel, cast iron, aluminium, copper, titanium | High strength, ductile, good thermal & electrical conductor | Gears, shafts, frames |
| **Polymers** | Nylon, PTFE, HDPE, Bakelite, PEEK | Low density, corrosion-resistant, electrically insulating | Bushings, seals, housings |
| **Ceramics & Glasses** | Alumina (Al₂O₃), SiC, ZrO₂, soda-lime glass | Very high hardness, high-temp capability, brittle | Cutting tools, insulators |
| **Composites** | CFRP, GFRP, MMC (Al-SiC), wood | Tailored anisotropy, high specific properties | Aircraft skins, sports gear |
| **Smart Materials** | Shape-memory alloys, piezoelectrics | Property changes with stimulus | Sensors, actuators |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pointer:** The course specifically emphasizes **Ashby's Material Selection Charts** (a graphical method using property axes like $E$ vs $\rho$, $\sigma_y$ vs $\rho$, $E$ vs $\sigma_y$). Always remember the fundamental logic — **steep slope on a log-log chart = strong coupling; shallow slope = weak coupling**.

---

## 1.5 The Ashby Methodology — Brief Overview

The **Ashby approach** (M.F. Ashby, Cambridge University) is the gold-standard method taught globally and referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It involves four steps:

1. **Translate** the design requirements into a *function*, *constraints*, *objectives*, and *free variables*.
2. **Screen** materials using property charts (Ashby maps) to eliminate families that fail hard constraints.
3. **Rank** surviving materials using a *material index* (e.g., $\sigma_y / \rho$ for a light, strong tie).
4. **Document** the final choice with supporting data (cost, manufacturability, sustainability).

The **material index** $\mathbf{M}$ is derived by combining the objective and the strongest constraint using **dimensional analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Material Selection Logic Chain (Step-by-Step)

The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a **five-stage decision flow**. Mastering this chain is critical for any 14-mark design problem.

### Stage 1 — Identify the Function

Ask: *"What does the component DO?"*

Examples:
- A **spring** stores elastic energy → objective: maximize stored energy per unit mass.
- A **tie-rod** in a truss resists tension → objective: minimize mass at fixed stiffness or strength.
- A **frying pan handle** conducts heat poorly → objective: minimize thermal conductivity.
- A **heat sink** in electronics dissipates heat → objective: maximize thermal conductivity per unit cost.

### Stage 2 — List the Constraints

These are the **non-negotiable requirements** that *any* candidate material must satisfy.

| Type | Constraint Expression | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical (stiffness) | $F \cdot L / (A \cdot E) \leq \delta_{\max}$ | Deflection of a beam |
| Mechanical (strength) | $F / A \leq \sigma_y / N$ | Stress in a tie |
| Thermal | $T_{\text{op}} \leq T_{\max, \text{material}}$ | Melting/decomposition point |
| Geometric | $L$, $A$, shape | Standard sizes |
| Physical | Density, electrical, magnetic | Non-magnetic requirement |
| Chemical | Corrosion resistance to medium | Stainless in acid |
| Manufacturing | Process must be feasible | Castable, machinable |

### Stage 3 — Identify the Objective

The objective is what the designer **wants to minimize or maximize**. The most common objectives are:

- **Minimize mass** → aerospace, automotive, portable devices
- **Minimize cost** → consumer goods, civil infrastructure
- **Minimize volume** → compact electronics
- **Maximize performance** → racing, defense
- **Maximize life** → power plants, bridges

### Stage 4 — Identify the Free Variables

Free variables are quantities the designer **can adjust** to satisfy the design — typically **cross-sectional area, length, shape, or thickness**. The material is also a free variable in the *selection* phase, but becomes *fixed* once chosen.

### Stage 5 — Derive the Material Index and Optimize

Substitute the constraint(s) into the objective to eliminate the free variables. The surviving expression containing **only material properties** is the **material index**.

---

## 2.2 Classical Derivation — The Light, Strong Tie (Most Asked KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A tie-rod of length $L$ must carry an axial tensile load $F^*$ without failing (stress constraint) and without stretching by more than $\delta^*$ (stiffness constraint). Find the material that **minimizes the mass** $m$.

**Step 1 — Function:** Carry load $F^*$.
**Step 2 — Constraints:**
- Strength: $\sigma = F^* / A \leq \sigma_y / N \;\Rightarrow\; A \geq F^* N / \sigma_y$
- Stiffness: $\delta = F^* L / (A E) \leq \delta^* \;\Rightarrow\; A \geq F^* L / (E \delta^*)$

**Step 3 — Objective:** Minimize mass $m = \rho A L$.

**Step 4 — Material Index (stiffness-dominated):**

$$
A_{\text{req}} = \frac{F^* L}{E \, \delta^*}
$$

$$
m = \rho \, A_{\text{req}} \, L = \frac{\rho \, F^* L^2}{E \, \delta^*}
$$

Since $F^*, L, \delta^*$ are fixed, **minimize $m$ ⟺ maximize $E / \rho$** (specific stiffness).

**Material Index:**

$$
\boxed{M_1 = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(specific stiffness)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Index (strength-dominated):**

$$
A_{\text{req}} = \frac{F^* N}{\sigma_y}
$$

$$
m = \rho \, A_{\text{req}} \, L = \frac{\rho \, F^* N L}{\sigma_y}
$$

**Minimize $m$ ⟺ maximize $\sigma_y / \rho$**.

**Material Index:**

$$
\boxed{M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(specific strength)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked "which material to choose," always **derive** the index, **plot** the relevant property chart, and **identify** the family lying on the optimum line. Marks are awarded for each of these three steps.

---

## 2.3 The Two Fundamental Material Selection Charts (Ashby Maps)

### Chart A — Young's Modulus $E$ vs Density $\rho$

- **Steep slope** ($\sim 1$): ceramics are *stiff and light* (Al₂O₃, SiC).
- **Shallow slope** ($\sim 0.5$): polymers and woods are *less stiff and lighter*.
- **Metals** form a tight cluster (steels, Al, Ti, Mg).

### Chart B — Yield Strength $\sigma_y$ vs Density $\rho$

- **Steep slope**: composites (CFRP) and ceramics dominate.
- **Metals** form a diagonal band — increasing strength ≈ increasing density.
- **Engineering polymers** lie in a horizontal band — strength varies widely without density penalty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Chart:** The further a material lies *above* the diagonal "selection line" connecting two candidate materials, the **better** its specific property. For example, CFRP lies well above aluminium on the $\sigma_y$-vs-$\rho$ chart — making it the choice for aerospace ties.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — it covers ~85% of 14-mark material selection problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Design Scenario | Constraint | Objective | Material Index $M$ | Best Material Family |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Light, stiff tie** | $\delta = F^*L / (AE) \leq \delta^*$ | Min mass | $E / \rho$ | CFRP, ceramics, Ti alloys |
| 2 | **Light, strong tie** | $\sigma = F^*/A \leq \sigma_y / N$ | Min mass | $\sigma_y / \rho$ | CFRP, high-strength steels, Ti |
| 3 | **Light, stiff beam** | $I \propto A^2$, $\delta = c F^* L^3 / (E I)$ | Min mass | $E^{1/3} / \rho$ | CFRP, wood, Al |
| 4 | **Light, strong beam** | $\sigma_{\max} = M y / I$, $I \propto A^2$ | Min mass | $\sigma_y^{2/3} / \rho$ | CFRP, alloy steel |
| 5 | **Light column (buckling)** | $F^* \leq \pi^2 E I / L^2$ | Min mass | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ | CFRP, Mg alloys |
| 6 | **Stiff shaft (torsion)** | $\theta = T L / (G J)$, $J \propto d^4$ | Min mass | $G^{1/2} / \rho$ | CFRP, steels |
| 7 | **Spring (max stored energy)** | $U = \sigma^2 V / (2E)$, $V$ = volume | Max $U/m$ | $\sigma_y^{2} / (E \rho)$ | Spring steels, Ti |
| 8 | **Thermal insulation** | $Q = k A \Delta T / L$ | Min $Q$ (or min mass) | $1 / (k \rho)$ or $1 / k$ | Polymers, foams, ceramics |
| 9 | **Heat sink (max heat flow)** | $Q = k A \Delta T / L$ | Max $Q/m$ | $k / \rho$ | Al, Cu, Be |
| 10 | **Cheap, stiff beam** | Same as #3 | Min cost | $E^{1/3} / C_m \rho$ (where $C_m$ = cost/kg) | Concrete, wood, cast iron |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Pitfall:** The indices above use the standard single-line fraction format. In handwritten KTU exams, **never** write $\vert E \vert / \rho$ with the vertical bar (it will be misread as "E divided by ρ where E is in absolute value"). Use $\dfrac{E}{\rho}$ or $E / \rho$ explicitly.

---

## 2.5 Property-Modifying Methods (Why Materials Differ in Properties)

The KTU syllabus expects awareness of the *microstructural levers* that change material properties:

| Method | Mechanism | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold working** | Dislocation multiplication | ↑ Hardness, ↑ $\sigma_y$, ↓ ductility |
| **Alloying** | Solid-solution strengthening | ↑ $\sigma_y$, ↑ hardness |
| **Heat treatment** | Phase transformation (martensite, pearlite) | ↑ $\sigma_y$ drastically (e.g., quenched steel) |
| **Grain refinement** | Hall-Petch relation: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k d^{-1/2}$ | ↑ $\sigma_y$ |
| **Composite reinforcement** | Load transfer to fiber | ↑ $E$, ↑ $\sigma_y$, anisotropic |
| **Precipitation hardening** | Nano-precipitates pin dislocations | ↑ $\sigma_y$ without ↓ ductility severely |
| **Strain hardening** | Plastic deformation increases dislocation density | ↑ $\sigma_y$, ↑ hardness |

The **Hall-Petch relation** is a frequently tested equation in KTU:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}
$$

where $d$ is the mean grain diameter and $k_y$ is the Hall-Petch coefficient (typical for mild steel: $k_y \approx 0.74 \text{ MPa·m}^{1/2}$).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Material selection is the **first and most consequential decision** in any design cycle. A wrong choice propagates through **every subsequent stage** — manufacturing, assembly, service, and end-of-life recycling. Real-world stakes include:

- **Boeing 787 Dreamliner:** 50% composite by mass → weight saving of ~20% vs aluminium.
- **Tesla Model Y:** Single-piece cast aluminium rear underbody → cost and assembly reduction.
- **Mars Rover wheels:** Titanium + aluminium honeycomb → stiffness, light weight, and dust resistance.
- **Cardiac stents:** Cobalt-chromium (L605) or nitinol (shape-memory) → biocompatibility + fatigue endurance > 10⁸ cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a given application, the **correct material is the one that minimizes the *total life-cycle cost***, not just the purchase cost. A cheap cast-iron pump housing may fail in 6 months due to corrosion, while a stainless variant (3× cost) lasts 10+ years — the latter is *cheaper in life-cycle terms*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Lightweight Torsion Shaft

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft of length $L$ and diameter $d$ must transmit a torque $T^*$ without exceeding a permissible twist $\theta^*$. Find the material index that minimizes the shaft mass.

### Step 1 — State the Function and Constraints

The shaft must satisfy **stiffness** (permissible twist) and **strength** (permissible shear stress) constraints.

- Stiffness constraint: $\theta = \dfrac{T^* L}{G J} \leq \theta^*$
- Strength constraint: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T^*}{\pi d^3} \leq \dfrac{\tau_{\text{allow}}}{N}$

### Step 2 — Express the Stiffness Constraint and Solve for Diameter

The polar second moment of area for a solid circular shaft is:

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
$$

Substitute $J$ into the stiffness equation:

$$
\theta = \frac{T^* L}{G \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}} \leq \theta^*
$$

Rearranging for $d^4$:

$$
d^4 \geq \frac{32 T^* L}{\pi G \theta^*}
$$

$$
d \geq \left(\frac{32 T^* L}{\pi G \theta^*}\right)^{1/4}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Mass in Terms of Material Properties

The mass of the shaft is:

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2 L}{4}
$$

Substituting $d^2$:

$$
d^2 = \left(d^4\right)^{1/2} = \left(\frac{32 T^* L}{\pi G \theta^*}\right)^{1/2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
m = \frac{\rho \pi L}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{32 T^* L}{\pi G \theta^*}\right)^{1/2}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Expression

$$
m = \frac{\rho \pi L}{4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{32 \, T^* L}}{\sqrt{\pi G \theta^*}}
$$

$$
m = \frac{\rho \, L^{3/2}}{4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{32 \, T^*}}{\sqrt{\pi G \theta^*}} \cdot \sqrt{\pi}
$$

$$
m = \frac{\rho \, L^{3/2}}{4 \sqrt{\theta^*}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{32 \, T^*}{G}}
$$

Since $T^*, L, \theta^*$ are fixed design parameters, **minimizing $m$ is equivalent to minimizing $\rho / \sqrt{G}$**, which is equivalent to **maximizing $\sqrt{G} / \rho$**.

### Step 5 — The Material Index

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{\sqrt{G}}{\rho} = \frac{G^{1/2}}{\rho}}
$$

This is the **specific torsional stiffness index**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Comment:** Always show every algebraic step. Examiners reward **methodology** (1–2 marks), not just the final answer. Even if the final index is wrong, partial credit is awarded for correct dimensional analysis.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Spring for Maximum Energy Storage per Unit Mass

**Problem:** A helical compression spring stores elastic strain energy. Determine the material index that maximizes stored energy *per unit mass*.

### Step 1 — Strain Energy Formula

The elastic strain energy stored in a body of volume $V$ under uniform stress $\sigma$ is:

$$
U = \frac{\sigma^2}{2 E} \cdot V
$$

For a spring operating up to the **elastic limit** $\sigma_y$, the maximum energy is:

$$
U_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E} \cdot V
$$

### Step 2 — Energy per Unit Mass

The mass is $m = \rho V$, so $V = m / \rho$. Substituting:

$$
U_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E} \cdot \frac{m}{\rho}
$$

The energy per unit mass is:

$$
\frac{U_{\max}}{m} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2 E \rho}
$$

### Step 3 — Material Index

To maximize $U_{\max}/m$, we maximize:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{E \rho}}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify via Ashby Chart

On the $\sigma_y$-vs-$E$ chart, the line $E = \sigma_y^2 / (M \rho)$ has **slope 2** (in log-log space). Materials lying **toward the upper-left** of the $\sigma_y$-vs-$E$ chart (high yield, low modulus) are best — this is exactly the regime occupied by **spring steels and titanium**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not ceramics?** Although ceramics have high $\sigma_y$, their modulus $E$ is also very high, so the ratio $\sigma_y^2 / E$ (and therefore $M$) is small. This is a *classic Ashby insight* — high strength alone is insufficient; the **ratio** matters.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Material Families to Common KTU Design Scenarios

The matrix below summarizes the **optimum material family** for the 12 most-tested KTU 2024 design scenarios. Students should commit this to memory.

| # | Design Scenario | Primary Material Index | Optimum Family | Specific Example (Alloy/Grade) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Aerospace tie-rod** | $\sigma_y / \rho$ | CFRP, Ti alloys | Ti-6Al-4V, T800/ epoxy CFRP |
| 2 | **Civil column (low buckling risk)** | $E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Steel, reinforced concrete | Mild steel, RCC |
| 3 | **Pressure vessel** | $\sigma_y / \rho$ (with fracture toughness) | High-strength steel, Al alloys | SA-516 Gr 70, Al 6061-T6 |
| 4 | **Frying pan handle** | $1 / k$ (low thermal conductivity) | Polymers, wood | Bakelite, phenolic resin |
| 5 | **CPU heat sink** | $k / \rho C_p^{2/3}$ | Pure Al, Al-SiC MMC | Al 1050, AlSiC |
| 6 | **Gear (high wear)** | Hardness + $\sigma_y$ | Case-hardened steel, ceramics | 20MnCr5 (carburized), Si₃N₄ |
| 7 | **Marine shaft** | Corrosion resistance + $\sigma_y$ | Bronze, monel, stainless | Naval brass, 17-4 PH SS |
| 8 | **Medical implant** | Biocompatibility + fatigue | Ti-6Al-4V ELI, Co-Cr-Mo | Ti Grade 5 ELI |
| 9 | **Electric motor housing** | $\sigma_y / \rho$ + magnetic permeability | Silicon steel + Al | M19 silicon steel, cast Al |
| 10 | **Rocket nozzle** | $T_{\text{melt}} + \sigma_y$ at $T$ | Cermets, refractory alloys | Tungsten, niobium alloys |
| 11 | **Storage tank (food grade)** | Corrosion resistance + cost | Stainless 304, HDPE | SS 304, food-grade HDPE |
| 12 | **Automobile crankshaft** | Fatigue strength + $\sigma_y$ | Forged alloy steel | 4340, EN36, forged 42CrMo4 |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Selecting Material for a Bicycle Frame (Numerical Walk-Through)

**Given:** A bicycle frame must support a static vertical load of $F^* = 800 \text{ N}$ at the seat post. The frame tube is approximately $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$ long, and the maximum allowable deflection is $\delta^* = 1 \text{ mm}$. The cross-section is circular with outer diameter $D = 25 \text{ mm}$ (fixed geometric constraint).

**Required:** Recommend a material family.

### Step 1 — Identify Constraint

Stiffness-dominated: $\delta = F^* L^3 / (3 E I) \leq \delta^*$ (cantilever model for simplicity, since the seat post acts as a beam-column in bending).

Moment of inertia for a thin-walled tube of diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$:

$$
I = \frac{\pi D^3 t}{8}
$$

The deflection constraint becomes:

$$
\delta = \frac{8 F^* L^3}{3 \pi E D^3 t} \leq \delta^*
$$

Solving for $t$:

$$
t \geq \frac{8 F^* L^3}{3 \pi E D^3 \delta^*}
$$

### Step 2 — Express Mass

$$
m = \rho \cdot \pi D t L = \rho \pi D L \cdot \frac{8 F^* L^3}{3 \pi E D^3 \delta^*}
$$

$$
m = \frac{8 \rho F^* L^4}{3 E D^3 \delta^*}
$$

For a given $\delta^*$, the **mass is minimized by maximizing $E / \rho$** — the specific stiffness.

### Step 3 — Compare Candidate Materials

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $E/\rho$ (kN·m/kg) | Relative |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild steel | 200 | 7850 | **25.5** | 1.00 (baseline) |
| Aluminium 6061 | 69 | 2700 | **25.6** | 1.00 |
| CFRP (unidirectional) | 135 | 1600 | **84.4** | 3.31 |
| GFRP | 38 | 1850 | **20.5** | 0.80 |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 114 | 4430 | **25.7** | 1.01 |
| Magnesium AZ31 | 45 | 1740 | **25.9** | 1.01 |

### Step 4 — Conclusion

**CFRP is the optimum** by a factor of **3.3×** over metals. Aluminium 6061 and steel are nearly tied, which is why **modern bicycle frames are made of either Al 6061-T6 (cheap, durable) or CFRP (premium, lightweight)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the coincidence:** All structural metals cluster at $E/\rho \approx 25 \text{ kN·m/kg}$ — this is the **"metallic envelope"** on the Ashby chart. To exceed it, you **must** move to composites.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Dimensional Analysis Check

To confirm the material index is correct, perform a **dimensional check**.

For $M_1 = E / \rho$:

$$
\frac{E}{\rho} = \frac{[\text{Pa}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{N/m}^2]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{N} \cdot \text{m}]}{[\text{kg}]} = \text{J/kg}
$$

The unit $\text{J/kg}$ is **specific energy** — appropriate for an index that minimizes mass for a given energy absorption or stiffness requirement. ✔

For $M = \sigma_y^2 / (E \rho)$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_y^2}{E \rho} = \frac{[\text{Pa}^2]}{[\text{Pa} \cdot \text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{[\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^3]}{[\text{kg}]} = \text{J/kg}
$$

Also **specific energy** — physically consistent with the spring application. ✔

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Stage Material Selection Flowchart (Ashby Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Design Requirement"] --> B["Stage 1: TRANSLATE\nFunction, Constraints,\nObjectives, Free Variables"]
    B --> C["Stage 2: SCREEN\nApply hard constraints\nEliminate disqualified families"]
    C --> D["Stage 3: RANK\nCompute Material Index M\nPlot on Ashby Chart"]
    D --> E["Stage 4: SUBJECTIVE FILTER\nCost, Manufacturability,\nSustainability, Aesthetics"]
    E --> F{"Multiple\nCandidates?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Document Trade-off Matrix\nSelect with Engineering Judgment"]
    F -- No --> H["Stage 5: DOCUMENT\nFinal Material + Justification"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["END: Selected Material"]

    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style G fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000

```

## 4.2 The Material Family Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Mechanical Load Required?"] --> P1{"Tensile or\nCompressive?"}
    P1 -- Yes, Light Weight --> P2["Check E/rho and sigma_y/rho"]
    P1 -- Yes, Heavy OK --> P3["Check sigma_y and E alone"]
    P1 -- No, Torsion --> P4["Check G^1/2/rho"]
    P1 -- No, Bending --> P5["Check E^1/3/rho or sigma_y^2/3/rho"]

    P2 --> Q1{"Operating\nTemperature?"}
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q1
    P5 --> Q1

    Q1 -- "Below 200 C" --> R1["Polymers, Al, Steel, CFRP\navailable"]
    Q1 -- "200 to 500 C" --> R2["Al alloys, Ti, Steels"]
    Q1 -- "Above 500 C" --> R3["Ni superalloys, Ceramics,\nRefractory metals"]

    R1 --> S1{"Corrosive\nEnvironment?"}
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1

    S1 -- Yes --> T1["Stainless, Al, Ti, Polymers,\nCoatings"]
    S1 -- No --> T2["Carbon steel, Cast iron allowed"]

    T1 --> U1["Final Selection\n+ Manufacturing Check"]
    T2 --> U1

    style Q1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000

```

## 4.3 The Ashby Material Selection Map (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AXIS X: Density rho (kg/m^3)\n10^0 to 10^4"] --> B["AXIS Y: Yield Strength sigma_y (MPa)\n10^-1 to 10^4"]
    B --> C["Plot All Material Families\nin Log-Log Space"]

    C --> D["CERAMICS\nRegion: High sigma_y,\nModerate rho\nTop-Center"]
    C --> E["COMPOSITES CFRP\nRegion: Very High sigma_y/rho\nTop-Left, Far from Origin"]
    C --> F["METALS\nRegion: Diagonal Band\nMiddle-Center"]
    C --> G["POLYMERS\nRegion: Low sigma_y,\nLow rho\nBottom-Left"]
    C --> H["FOAMS\nRegion: Lowest rho\nBottom-Far-Left"]

    D --> I["Selection Line for\nsigma_y/rho = constant\n(Slope = 1 in log-log)"]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J["Materials ABOVE the line\n= BETTER specific strength"]
    J --> K["Materials BELOW the line\n= WORSE specific strength"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C["Manufacturability\n(cast, forge,\nmachine, 3D-print)"]

    B --> D["OPTIMUM\nMATERIAL\nSELECTION"]
    C --> D

    A -- "Stronger = Heavier = Costlier" --> E["TRADE-OFF\nCONSTANT"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Material Index** and explain its significance in material selection with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

A **material index** is a grouping of material properties that characterizes the *performance* of a material in a specific design application, derived by combining the design objective and the binding constraint through dimensional analysis.

**Significance:**
- It allows **ranking** of candidate materials on a single quantitative scale.
- It enables **visualization** on an Ashby selection chart.
- It decouples the **material choice** from the **geometric choice**.

**Example:** For a light, stiff tie-rod, the material index is $M = E / \rho$. A material with $E/\rho = 50 \text{ kN·m/kg}$ is *twice as good* as one with $E/\rho = 25 \text{ kN·m/kg}$ for this specific application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for significance, 1 mark for example.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List the **factors affecting material selection** in machine design. Explain any **two** in brief.

**Model Answer:**

The factors affecting material selection are:

1. **Mechanical properties** — strength, stiffness, toughness, fatigue
2. **Physical properties** — density, thermal/electrical conductivity
3. **Chemical properties** — corrosion resistance, oxidation resistance
4. **Manufacturing considerations** — castability, machinability, weldability
5. **Economic factors** — raw cost, processing cost, life-cycle cost
6. **Service conditions** — temperature, environment, load type
7. **Availability and standardization**
8. **Aesthetic requirements**
9. **Recyclability and environmental impact**
10. **Safety and reliability requirements**

**Two factors in brief:**

**(a) Mechanical Properties:** The material must possess sufficient strength to withstand applied loads without yielding or fracturing. For dynamically loaded components (e.g., shafts, springs), fatigue strength is critical.

**(b) Manufacturing Considerations:** The chosen material must be compatible with the available manufacturing process. For example, complex shapes in large volumes favor casting (cast iron); high-strength critical parts favor forging (alloy steel); intricate small parts favor injection molding (polymers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for listing ≥6 factors, 1 mark each for the two explanations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (A):** A **tie-rod** of length $L = 0.8 \text{ m}$ in a structural truss must support an axial tensile load of $F^* = 50 \text{ kN}$ without yielding (factor of safety $N = 2.5$) and without stretching more than $\delta^* = 1.2 \text{ mm}$. The cross-section is a solid circular bar of diameter $d$.

**(a)** Derive the **material index** for **minimum mass** under the **stiffness-dominated** case. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **material index** for **minimum mass** under the **strength-dominated** case. Recommend a suitable material family with justification. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution (Question A)

#### Part (a) — Stiffness-Dominated (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Stiffness constraint (axial deformation):

$$
\delta = \frac{F^* L}{A E} \leq \delta^*
$$

For a circular cross-section: $A = \pi d^2 / 4$

$$
\frac{F^* L}{\left(\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\right) E} \leq \delta^*
$$

Rearranging for $d^2$:

$$
d^2 \geq \frac{4 F^* L}{\pi E \delta^*}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass of the rod:

$$
m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L
$$

Substituting $d^2$:

$$
m = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi L}{4} \cdot \frac{4 F^* L}{\pi E \delta^*}
$$

$$
m = \frac{\rho \, F^* L^2}{E \, \delta^*}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $F^*, L, \delta^*$ are constants, the **material index** is:

$$
\boxed{M_1 = \frac{E}{\rho}}
$$

**Justification (1 mark):** Maximize $E/\rho$ — i.e., choose a material with the highest specific stiffness.

**Recommended Family (1 mark):** **CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer)** with $E/\rho \approx 80$–$100 \text{ kN·m/kg}$, which is **3–4× better** than structural metals.

---

#### Part (b) — Strength-Dominated (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Strength constraint:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F^*}{A} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{N}
$$

$$
\frac{F^*}{A} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{N}
$$

Rearranging for $A$:

$$
A \geq \frac{F^* N}{\sigma_y}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass:

$$
m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L = \rho \cdot \frac{F^* N}{\sigma_y} \cdot L
$$

**Step 3:** Since $F^*, N, L$ are constants, minimize $m$ ⟺ maximize:

$$
\boxed{M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}}
$$

**Justification (1 mark):** Maximize $\sigma_y/\rho$ — i.e., choose a material with the highest specific strength.

**Recommended Family (1 mark):**

| Material | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\sigma_y/\rho$ (kN·m/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild steel | 250 | 7850 | 31.8 |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | **880** | **4430** | **198.6** |
| **CFRP (unidirectional)** | **1500** | **1600** | **937.5** |
| Al 7075-T6 | 503 | 2810 | 179.0 |
| Maraging steel 18Ni300 | 2000 | 8100 | 246.9 |

**Conclusion:** **CFRP** is the optimum for *pure* mass minimization. If cost matters, **Ti-6Al-4V** offers a 5× improvement over steel at moderate cost. If cost is the priority, **high-strength alloy steel (e.g., 4340 quenched & tempered)** is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**Q (B):** A **helical compression spring** is required to store a maximum elastic strain energy of $U = 50 \text{ J}$ while operating at a peak shear stress of $\tau_{\max} = 400 \text{ MPa}$ and mean coil diameter $D = 30 \text{ mm}$. The spring index $C = D/d = 8$ and number of active coils $n = 10$.

**(a)** Derive the **material index** for the spring that **minimizes mass** while storing the required energy. (7 marks)

**(b)** Justify the choice of **oil-tempered spring steel (EN 47 / SUP 9)** with reference to the Ashby chart, and list **two** property-modification techniques that make this material suitable. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution (Question B)

#### Part (a) — Material Index Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Energy stored in a helical spring:**

The strain energy in a helical spring is:

$$
U = \frac{\tau^2 V}{2 G}
$$

where $V$ is the spring volume and $G$ is the shear modulus.

**Step 2 — Spring volume:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \pi D n = \frac{\pi^2 D n d^2}{4}
$$

Using $C = D/d \Rightarrow D = Cd$:

$$
V = \frac{\pi^2 C n d^3}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$ in terms of $U$:**

$$
U = \frac{\tau^2}{2 G} \cdot \frac{\pi^2 C n d^3}{4}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{8 G U}{\pi^2 C n \tau^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express mass:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi^2 C n d^3}{4}
$$

Substituting $d^3$:

$$
m = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi^2 C n}{4} \cdot \frac{8 G U}{\pi^2 C n \tau^2}
$$

$$
m = \frac{2 \rho G U}{\tau^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Index:**

Since $U$ and $\tau$ are fixed, **minimize $m$ ⟺ minimize $\rho G / \tau^2$** ⟺ **maximize $\tau^2 / (\rho G)$**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{\tau^2}{G \rho}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This index is **dimensionally** $[\text{Pa} / (\text{kg/m}^3)] = \text{J/kg}$ — specific energy, as expected.

---

#### Part (b) — Material Justification (7 marks)

**Property comparison table (2 marks):**

| Material | $G$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\tau_y$ (MPa) | $\tau_y^2/(G\rho)$ (kJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EN 47 spring steel (oil-tempered)** | 79 | 7850 | 700 | **0.79** |
| Stainless 301 (spring temper) | 77 | 8000 | 600 | 0.58 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 41 | 4430 | 550 | 0.66 |
| Phosphor bronze | 41 | 8800 | 350 | 0.34 |
| CFRP | 30 | 1600 | 400 | 1.33 |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** While CFRP has a slightly higher index, its **linear-elastic-to-brittle-failure** behavior is unsuitable for springs, which need to absorb energy *plastically* and avoid catastrophic failure. **Oil-tempered spring steel (EN 47 / SUP 9)** is the **engineering compromise** — combining the best index among *ductile* spring materials with **low cost, high availability, and proven reliability**.

**Two property-modification techniques that make EN 47 suitable (4 marks — 2 each):**

1. **Oil Tempering (Quench & Temper):** EN 47 (0.45–0.55% C, 0.8–1.2% Cr, 0.8–1.2% Mn) is austenitized at ~840°C, quenched in oil, then tempered at 400–500°C. This produces a **tempered martensite** structure with a fine dispersion of carbides, yielding:
   - High yield strength ($\sigma_y \approx 1200$ MPa, $\tau_y \approx 700$ MPa)
   - Adequate toughness (Charpy ≥ 20 J)
   - Good fatigue endurance limit (~ 600 MPa at 10⁷ cycles)

2. **Shot Peening:** The surface of the spring wire is bombarded with small steel shot, inducing a **compressive residual stress** layer of depth ~0.1–0.3 mm. This:
   - Suppresses fatigue-crack initiation
   - Increases the fatigue limit by 30–50%
   - Is critical for springs, which are fatigue-critical components

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final material index — *deduct 1 mark* per instance. Always show unit brackets.
> 2. **Confusing $\tau$ with $\sigma$** for springs — springs are in *torsion*; shear stress is correct.
> 3. **Skipping the dimensional analysis** — examiners *always* allocate 1 mark for it.
> 4. **Recommending ceramics/glass for springs** — they are brittle and will fail catastrophically; always rule them out for ductile applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1, Topic: Selection of Materials**

- ✅ **Material Selection is an optimization problem** — maximize an index subject to constraints.
- ✅ **Five-stage Ashby Method:** Translate → Screen → Rank → Filter → Document.
- ✅ **Most common material indices:**
  - Light stiff tie: $E / \rho$
  - Light strong tie: $\sigma_y / \rho$
  - Light stiff beam: $E^{1/3} / \rho$
  - Light strong beam: $\sigma_y^{2/3} / \rho$
  - Light column (Euler): $E^{1/2} / \rho$
  - Light torsion shaft: $G^{1/2} / \rho$
  - Spring (max energy/mass): $\sigma_y^2 / (E \rho)$ or $\tau^2 / (G \rho)$
  - Heat sink: $k / \rho$
  - Thermal insulation: $1 / k$ or $1 / (k \rho)$
- ✅ **Ashby Chart Rule:** In log-log space, **steeper slope ⇒ stronger coupling**; **above the line = better**.
- ✅ **Metallic envelope:** All structural metals cluster at $E/\rho \approx 25 \text{ kN·m/kg}$ — to exceed, **use composites**.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch relation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$ — finer grain ⇒ higher yield.
- ✅ **Four material families:** Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, Composites. (Plus smart materials.)
- ✅ **Factors of selection:** Mechanical, Physical, Chemical, Manufacturing, Economic, Service, Availability, Aesthetics, Sustainability, Safety.
- ✅ **Always perform dimensional analysis** of the final index — unit should be J/kg (specific energy) or a meaningful engineering quantity.
- ✅ **Trade-off triangle:** Performance ↔ Cost ↔ Manufacturability — the optimum is at the centroid, not at any vertex.
- ✅ **Property-modification techniques:** Cold work, alloying, heat treatment, grain refinement, composite reinforcement, precipitation hardening, shot peening.
- ✅ **Common board exam traps:** Using $\sigma$ for springs (use $\tau$); confusing $E$ and $G$ (remember $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$, so $G \approx 0.4 E$ for metals); recommending brittle materials for ductile applications.

---

*End of Notes — Selection of Materials, Module 1, PEPET411 Machine Design, KTU 2024 Scheme.*

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Design" in the KTU Machine Design Context?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, **Design** is defined as the **iterative, creative, and analytical process** of conceiving and developing a functional, manufacturable, and economically viable machine element or system that satisfies a clearly defined technical need.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Memorize for 3-Mark Questions):**
> *"Design is a purposeful activity directed toward the goal of fulfilling human needs, wants, or desires through the creation of a product, system, or environment. In Machine Design, it specifically means determining the size, shape, material, and arrangement of machine components such that the resulting machine performs its intended function safely, reliably, and economically."*

**Design ≠ Drawing.** A common KTU mistake is to equate machine design with drafting or CAD modeling. Design is the *decision-making* process; the drawing is merely a *communication* output of the design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The House Building Analogy"

Imagine you want to build a house. You do **not** simply hand a brick to a mason and say "build."

| Real-World House Building | Equivalent Machine Design Step |
|---|---|
| Family identifies need for shelter | Recognition of Need |
| Decide number of rooms, budget, style | Problem Definition |
| Research plots, materials, builders | Collection of Information |
| Architect draws multiple sketches | Conceptual Design (Synthesis) |
| Choose between layouts, materials | Analysis & Preliminary Design |
| Structural engineer checks loads | Detailed Design (Embodiment) |
| Build a small sample room | Prototype & Testing |
| Construct the entire house | Final Production |
| Family moves in & reports cracks | Evaluation & Feedback |

This iterative logic — *Need → Define → Imagine → Analyze → Build → Test → Improve* — is **identical** to the engineering design process.

## 1.3 Types of Design (Highly Repeated in KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks a 3-mark question: *"Classify the types of design and explain each."* Memorize these four categories:

1. **Original (Creative) Design** — A completely new product is conceived. Example: Inventing the first photocopying machine. Rare, high-risk, high-reward.
2. **Adaptive Design** — An existing product is adapted for a *new function* without major change in operating principle. Example: Adapting a printing press to print currency.
3. **Development (Redesign) Design** — Minor modifications in size, capacity, or appearance of an existing product. Example: Upgrading a smartphone model yearly.
4. **Variant Design** — Only dimensions or proportions are changed within a proven, standardized design. Example: A gear manufacturer producing different diameters of the *same* gear geometry.

## 1.4 Why Are "Steps" Important?

A machine element that is designed by skipping steps will almost always fail in service. Steps ensure:
- **Safety** of operator and public
- **Reliability** over the design life
- **Manufacturability** with available resources
- **Economy** in material and process costs
- **Compliance** with standards (BIS, ISO, ASME)
