# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define variable stress. Differentiate between completely reversed and repeated stress cycles. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Variable stress (1 mark):** A stress whose magnitude varies with time during the operation of a machine component. It is the principal cause of fatigue failure.
- **Completely reversed stress cycle (1 mark):** A cycle in which $\sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min}$ and the mean stress is zero. The stress ratio $R = -1$. Example: rotating shaft under pure bending.
- **Repeated stress cycle (1 mark):** A cycle in which $\sigma_{min} = 0$ and $\sigma_{max}$ is non-zero. The mean stress equals the amplitude. Stress ratio $R = 0$. Example: leaf spring loaded from zero to a maximum each cycle.

### Q2. Define mean stress and stress amplitude. Give the relevant formulas. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Mean stress (1.5 marks):** $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$. It is the steady (DC) component of the cyclic stress.
- **Stress amplitude (1.5 marks):** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$. It is the magnitude of the alternating (AC) component.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common pitfall is **sign confusion** when $\sigma_{min}$ is negative. Many students compute $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ forgetting the sign of $\sigma_{min}$, which gives an **erroneously small** amplitude. Always substitute with the sign: $\sigma_{min} = -100$ MPa means the *value* is negative, not a magnitude to be subtracted as a positive number. The correct operation is $\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 200 - (-100) = 300$ MPa, not $200 - 100 = 100$ MPa.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Goodman-Based Design

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question | Dec 2024]**

> A steel shaft is subjected to a bending moment that fluctuates between $M_{max} = 2{,}000$ N·m and $M_{min} = -500$ N·m. The shaft has a shoulder fillet with a theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 1.8$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.85$. The material properties are: ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, endurance limit $S_e = 280$ MPa. Using the **Goodman criterion** and a factor of safety $n = 2$, determine the **minimum required shaft diameter**.

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

#### Part (a) — Compute mean and amplitude stresses in terms of diameter (7 Marks)

For a solid circular shaft under bending, the maximum bending stress is:
$$
\sigma = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{32 \times 2{,}000 \times 10^3}{\pi d^3} \text{ N/mm}^2 = \dfrac{64{,}000{,}000}{\pi d^3} \text{ MPa (with } d \text{ in mm)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{min} = \dfrac{32 \times (-500) \times 10^3}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{-16{,}000{,}000}{\pi d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

Mean stress (nominal):
$$
\sigma_{m,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{64{,}000{,}000 + (-16{,}000{,}000)}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{24{,}000{,}000}{\pi d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

Amplitude stress (nominal):
$$
\sigma_{a,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{64{,}000{,}000 - (-16{,}000{,}000)}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{40{,}000{,}000}{\pi d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

Fatigue stress concentration factor:
$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85 \times (1.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.85 \times 0.8 = 1 + 0.68 = 1.68
$$

The *effective* mean and amplitude stresses (used in the Goodman equation) incorporate $K_f$. For the **bending case**, the convention in KTU is to apply $K_f$ to the amplitude only when the mean stress is steady (does not fluctuate). Since the bending moment itself fluctuates through zero, $K_f$ is applied to both the maximum and minimum. Using the *von Mises* equivalent for $K_f$-multiplied stresses is also accepted in KTU, but the simplified approach (apply $K_f$ to both $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$) is followed here:

$$
\sigma_m = K_f \cdot \sigma_{m,\text{nom}} = 1.68 \times \dfrac{24{,}000{,}000}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{40{,}320{,}000}{\pi d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = K_f \cdot \sigma_{a,\text{nom}} = 1.68 \times \dfrac{40{,}000{,}000}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{67{,}200{,}000}{\pi d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Part (b) — Apply the Goodman criterion and solve for $d$ (7 Marks)

The Goodman equation with factor of safety $n$ is:
$$
\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{67{,}200{,}000}{\pi d^3 \times 280} + \dfrac{40{,}320{,}000}{\pi d^3 \times 600}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^3} \left( \dfrac{67{,}200{,}000}{280} + \dfrac{40{,}320{,}000}{600} \right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^3} \left( 240{,}000 + 67{,}200 \right) = \dfrac{307{,}200}{\pi d^3}
$$

With $n = 2$, we require:
$$
\dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{307{,}200}{\pi d^3}
$$

$$
d^3 = \dfrac{2 \times 307{,}200}{\pi} = \dfrac{614{,}400}{\pi} = 195{,}577.7 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{195{,}577.7} \approx 58.06 \text{ mm}
$$

Rounding up to a standard size: $d = 60$ mm (or the next preferred standard size per IS 2102).

**Answer:** Minimum required shaft diameter $\approx 58$ mm; standardise to $d = 60$ mm.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Gerber-Based Alternative

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question | June 2024]**

> A connecting rod is subjected to an axial load that varies from $P_{max} = 50$ kN (tension) to $P_{min} = 10$ kN (tension). The rod has a transverse hole with $K_t = 2.1$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.9$. The material has $S_{ut} = 500$ MPa and $S_e = 230$ MPa. Using the **Gerber criterion** with $n = 1.5$, find the **required cross-sectional area** of the rod.

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

#### Part (a) — Compute mean and amplitude stresses (7 Marks)

The nominal axial stress in the rod is $\sigma = P / A$. Let the cross-sectional area be $A$ in mm².

$$
\sigma_{max,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{A} \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{min,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{A} \text{ MPa}
$$

Mean:
$$
\sigma_{m,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{50{,}000 + 10{,}000}{2A} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{A} \text{ MPa}
$$

Amplitude:
$$
\sigma_{a,\text{nom}} = \dfrac{50{,}000 - 10{,}000}{2A} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{A} \text{ MPa}
$$

Fatigue stress concentration factor:
$$
K_f = 1 + 0.9 \times (2.1 - 1) = 1 + 0.9 \times 1.1 = 1 + 0.99 = 1.99
$$

For axial loading, KTU convention applies $K_f$ to both $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$:
$$
\sigma_m = 1.99 \times \dfrac{30{,}000}{A} = \dfrac{59{,}700}{A} \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = 1.99 \times \dfrac{20{,}000}{A} = \dfrac{39{,}800}{A} \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Part (b) — Apply Gerber criterion and solve for $A$ (7 Marks)

Gerber equation with $n$:
$$
\left( \dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^2 + \dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1
$$

Substituting:
$$
\left( \dfrac{1.5 \times 59{,}700}{500 \cdot A} \right)^2 + \dfrac{1.5 \times 39{,}800}{230 \cdot A} = 1
$$

$$
\left( \dfrac{89{,}550}{500 A} \right)^2 + \dfrac{59{,}700}{230 A} = 1
$$

$$
\left( \dfrac{179.1}{A} \right)^2 + \dfrac{259.57}{A} = 1
$$

$$
\dfrac{32{,}076.81}{A^2} + \dfrac{259.57}{A} = 1
$$

Multiply through by $A^2$:
$$
32{,}076.81 + 259.57 A - A^2 = 0
$$

Rearrange into standard quadratic form:
$$
A^2 - 259.57 A - 32{,}076.81 = 0
$$

Apply the quadratic formula:
$$
A = \dfrac{259.57 \pm \sqrt{259.57^2 + 4 \times 32{,}076.81}}{2}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{259.57 \pm \sqrt{67{,}376.6 + 128{,}307.2}}{2} = \dfrac{259.57 \pm \sqrt{195{,}683.8}}{2}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{259.57 \pm 442.36}{2}
$$

Taking the positive root:
$$
A = \dfrac{259.57 + 442.36}{2} = \dfrac{701.93}{2} = 350.97 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Rounding up: $A \approx 360$ mm² (or use a standard rod diameter, e.g., $d = 22$ mm gives $A = 380$ mm²).

**Answer:** Required cross-sectional area $\approx 351$ mm²; standardise to $A = 380$ mm² ($d = 22$ mm).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Quadratic Sign Error in Gerber:** In the Gerber equation, students frequently write the quadratic as $A^2 + 259.57 A - 32{,}076.81 = 0$ (positive coefficient on $A$) instead of $A^2 - 259.57 A - 32{,}076.81 = 0$. The sign of the linear term **flips** when moving the term across the equality. Cross-check by substituting $A = 1$: the original equation $32{,}076.81/A^2 + 259.57/A = 1$ should yield a value greater than 1 for small $A$ and less than 1 for large $A$; the root lies between these regions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist as a **last-minute revision sheet** the night before your KTU PEPET411 exam.

- [ ] **Sign convention:** Tensile stress is positive, compressive is negative. Always substitute with the sign into $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ *before* computing $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$.
- [ ] **Mean stress formula:** $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$. Memorise this — it is asked in nearly every KTU Part-A paper.
- [ ] **Amplitude formula:** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$. Always $\ge 0$ by convention.
- [ ] **Stress ratio $R$:** $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$. $R = -1$ for completely reversed, $R = 0$ for repeated, $0 < R < 1$ for fluctuating.
- [ ] **Fatigue stress concentration factor:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$. Apply to the fluctuating component, or to both $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$ for bending/axial fully fluctuating loads, per KTU convention.
- [ ] **Goodman is the default in KTU** unless the question specifies Soderberg, Gerber, or ASME-Elliptic. The Gerber parabola gives a slightly higher (less conservative) factor of safety.
- [ ] **Soderberg is the most conservative** — use it when the question explicitly says *no yielding permitted*.
- [ ] **Soderberg equation:** $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$.
- [ ] **Goodman equation:** $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$.
- [ ] **Gerber equation:** $\left( \dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^2 + \dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1$. Leads to a quadratic in $n$ or in the unknown dimension.
- [ ] **Marin's equation** (for modified endurance limit): $S_e' = k_a k_b k_c S_e$. Remember $k_c = 0.85$ for bending, $0.59$ for torsion, $1.0$ for axial.
- [ ] **Units:** Use SI throughout — $M$ in N·mm, $d$ in mm, stress in MPa (which is N/mm²).
- [ ] **Round up the final answer** to the next standard size. KTU penalises undersized designs.
- [ ] **In the solution, always show:** (i) computation of $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$, (ii) computation of $K_f$, (iii) substitution into the chosen criterion, (iv) algebraic solution for the unknown dimension. Steps skipped = marks lost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue Failure** is defined as the failure of a material under the action of **fluctuating (cyclic) stresses** that are considerably below the ultimate tensile strength of the material, after a sufficient number of stress cycles. The fracture occurs without any visible warning and is progressive in nature, initiating from a microscopic crack which propagates gradually until sudden catastrophic fracture takes place.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET411)**, fatigue failure is treated as a **time-dependent, progressive failure mechanism** that governs the design of all dynamically loaded machine components such as shafts, springs, gears, connecting rods, and turbine blades.

**Key Governing Nomenclature:**
- **Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$ or $S_e$):** The maximum completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles (typically $N = 10^6$ for steels)** without failure.
- **Fatigue Strength ($S_f$):** The maximum stress a material can withstand for a **specified finite number of cycles** (used for non-ferrous metals like Al, Cu which do not exhibit a true endurance limit).
- **Fatigue Life ($N$):** The total number of stress cycles sustained before failure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paperclip Analogy**
> Imagine bending a standard steel paperclip. A single bend will not break it. But if you bend it back and forth repeatedly (say 20–30 times), it suddenly snaps cleanly. Notice that:
> 1. The force you applied each time was far less than what would be needed to break it in a single pull.
> 2. The break occurred without any prior warning (no necking, no visible deformation).
> 3. The break surface was smooth at first (the crack initiation zone) and rough at the end (sudden fracture).
>
> This is **fatigue failure in miniature**. Machine components like an axle, a rotating shaft, or a leaf spring experience millions of these microscopic "bends" and eventually fail in the same way.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for engineers?** Approximately **80% of all mechanical failures** in industry are attributed to fatigue. Designing against static failure alone is fundamentally unsafe for any component experiencing vibration or cyclic loading.

## 1.3 Types of Fluctuating Stresses (KTU Classification)

The fluctuating stress $\sigma(t)$ at any point in a cycle can be described by:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$$

where $\sigma_m$ is the **mean stress** and $\sigma_a$ is the **stress amplitude**. The KTU syllabus recognizes the following standard cases:

| Case | $\sigma_{max}$ | $\sigma_{min}$ | $\sigma_m$ | $\sigma_a$ | Example |
|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| Pure Reversed | $+\sigma$ | $-\sigma$ | $0$ | $\sigma$ | Rotating shaft with bending |
| Repeated (Zero to Max) | $+\sigma$ | $0$ | $\sigma/2$ | $\sigma/2$ | Tension spring during compression |
| Repeated (Zero to Min) | $0$ | $-\sigma$ | $-\sigma/2$ | $\sigma/2$ | Leaf spring (one-way flexure) |
| Static (Tension) | $+\sigma$ | $+\sigma$ | $\sigma$ | $0$ | Static bolt loading |
| Static (Compression) | $-\sigma$ | $-\sigma$ | $-\sigma$ | $0$ | Column under constant load |
| Fluctuating (Asymmetric) | $+\sigma_1$ | $+\sigma_2$ | $(\sigma_1+\sigma_2)/2$ | $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)/2$ | Combined steady + cyclic load |

> [!NOTE]
> The **stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ is a critical parameter used throughout KTU problem statements to classify loading.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — The Heart of Fatigue

The fundamental experimental tool in fatigue analysis is the **S-N Diagram** (Stress vs Number of cycles to failure), obtained from a **rotating beam fatigue test** on a standard specimen.

**Operating Logic — Why the curve has two regions:**

- **Region 1 (Finite Life Region, $10^3 < N < 10^6$):** For steels, the curve slopes downward. Higher stress $\Rightarrow$ fewer cycles to failure. The relationship is approximately logarithmic (Basquin's Law).
- **Region 2 (Infinite Life Region, $N \ge 10^6$):** For steels, the curve becomes a horizontal asymptote. The value of stress at this plateau is the **endurance limit $\sigma_e$**, typically about **50% of the ultimate tensile strength** for polished specimens.
- **Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu alloys):** Do not show a sharp knee. The curve continues to slope down. Hence, fatigue strength is quoted at a fixed life, e.g., $S_f$ at $5 \times 10^8$ cycles.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Fatigue Design

The KTU-recommended design philosophy follows this rigorous 5-step logic:

1. **Identify the stress cycle** — Compute $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ from loading. Calculate $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$.
2. **Determine the endurance limit of the standard specimen** — $\sigma_e' = 0.5 \, \sigma_{ut}$ (for steels with $\sigma_{ut} < 1400$ MPa).
3. **Apply modifying factors** — Convert the standard specimen endurance limit to the actual component endurance limit using Marin's Equation.
4. **Apply a failure criterion** — Use Goodman, Gerber, or Soderberg line to find the factor of safety.
5. **Compare with required safety factor** — If calculated FOS $\ge$ required FOS, the design is safe; otherwise iterate the dimensions.

## 2.3 Marin's Equation — Modifying the Endurance Limit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marin's Equation** (a KTU board favorite) decomposes the correction from a polished laboratory specimen to a real engineering component as a product of independent factors:
> $$\sigma_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot \sigma_e'$$

| Factor | Symbol | Effect | Governing Relation |
|--------|--------|--------|---------------------|
| Surface Factor | $k_a$ | Reducer (rough surfaces $\downarrow$ endurance) | $k_a = a \cdot \sigma_{ut}^b$ (from table of surface finish) |
| Size Factor | $k_b$ | Reducer (larger parts $\downarrow$ endurance) | $k_b = 0.85$ for $d \le 51$ mm; $(51/d)^{0.11}$ for $d > 51$ mm (bending/torsion) |
| Load Factor | $k_c$ | Reducer (axial is worst) | $k_c = 1.0$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion) |
| Temperature Factor | $k_d$ | Varied | $k_d = 1.0$ for $T \le 450^\circ$C; else from chart |
| Reliability Factor | $k_e$ | Reducer | $k_e = 1.0$ (50%), $0.897$ (90%), $0.868$ (95%), $0.753$ (99.9%) |
| Miscellaneous Effects | $k_f$ | Reducer | $k_f = 1.0$ (no extra effects) |

**Engineering Utility:** This equation is used in every KTU numerical where a part is being designed for infinite life under combined loading. The most commonly tested factors are $k_a$, $k_b$, and $k_c$.

## 2.4 Stress Concentration & Notch Sensitivity

A sudden change in geometry (hole, keyway, shoulder fillet) creates **localized stress concentration** quantified by the **theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t$**.

The actual fatigue effect is captured by the **fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$**:

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

where $q$ is the **notch sensitivity** ($0 \le q \le 1$). For KTU problems where notch sensitivity is not given, the conservative assumption $K_f = K_t$ is adopted.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description / KTU Application |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ | Mean stress |
| 2 | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ | Stress amplitude |
| 3 | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$ | Maximum stress |
| 4 | $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$ | Minimum stress |
| 5 | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | Stress ratio |
| 6 | $\sigma_e' = 0.5 \sigma_{ut}$ | Unmodified endurance limit (steel, $\sigma_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa) |
| 7 | $\sigma_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \sigma_e'$ | **Marin's Equation** |
| 8 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue stress concentration factor |
| 9 | $\sigma_a^* = K_f \sigma_a$ | Amplitude with notch effect |
| 10 | $\sigma_m^* = K_f \sigma_m$ | Mean with notch (for brittle) / unchanged for ductile |
| 11 | $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_{ut} = 1/n$ | **Goodman Criterion** (most used) |
| 12 | $(\sigma_a/\sigma_e)^2 + (\sigma_m/\sigma_{ut})^2 = 1/n^2$ | **Gerber Criterion** (most accurate for ductile) |
| 13 | $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_y = 1/n$ | **Soderberg Criterion** (most conservative) |
| 14 | $\sigma_a'/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_{ut} = 1/n$ | **Modified Goodman** (most commonly tested) |
| 15 | $n = 1 / (\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_{ut})$ | Factor of safety (Modified Goodman) |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on $\sigma_{ut}$ units:** Throughout KTU boards, all stresses must be in **MPa ($N/mm^2$)** unless stated otherwise. Always show unit conversions in step 1 of every numerical.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean and Alternating Stresses

**Given:** A fluctuating load produces $\sigma_{max} = +75$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -25$ MPa.

The total stress is decomposed into a steady (mean) component and a reversing (alternating) component:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(t) &= \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t) \\
\sigma_{max} &= \sigma_m + \sigma_a \\
\sigma_{min} &= \sigma_m - \sigma_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Add the two equations to eliminate $\sigma_a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} &= (\sigma_m + \sigma_a) + (\sigma_m - \sigma_a) \\
\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} &= 2\sigma_m \\
\sigma_m &= \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract to eliminate $\sigma_m$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} &= (\sigma_m + \sigma_a) - (\sigma_m - \sigma_a) \\
\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} &= 2\sigma_a \\
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_m &= \frac{75 + (-25)}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_a &= \frac{75 - (-25)}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Modified Goodman Criterion

The **Goodman Line** is an empirical straight line connecting two fatigue limit points on the $\sigma_m$ vs $\sigma_a$ plane:
- Point 1: $(\sigma_m = 0, \; \sigma_a = \sigma_e)$ — pure reversed loading.
- Point 2: $(\sigma_m = \sigma_{ut}, \; \sigma_a = 0)$ — static fracture.

**Step 1 — Equation of the straight line** in intercept form:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Introduction of a Factor of Safety $n$:** The criterion must be safe under combined loading. The design point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ must lie inside the Goodman envelope at a factor of safety $n$ from the boundary. Both coordinates are scaled by $n$:

$$
\frac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the factor of safety:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right) &= 1 \\
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — When stress concentration is present**, the **alternating component** is amplified by $K_f$ (notch effect dominates cyclic damage), while the mean component for ductile materials is left unchanged:

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{K_f \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gerber Parabolic Criterion

The Gerber line is a parabola passing through the same two points as Goodman. It lies **above** the Goodman line (less conservative, more accurate for ductile steels).

**Step 1 — Standard form of a parabola** opening towards $\sigma_m$ axis with vertex at $(\sigma_{ut}, 0)$:

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Rearranging to symmetric form:**

$$
\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right) + \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Including factor of safety $n$:**

$$
\left( \frac{n \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{n \sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 = 1 \quad \text{(not standard)}
$$

The KTU-recommended form (which the board uses most often) is:

$$
\frac{1}{n^2} = \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right)^2 + 2 \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right)
$$

For conservative design, the simplified form $\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 = 1/n^2$ is used.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical: Modified Goodman with Stress Concentration

**Problem:** A shaft of diameter $d = 50$ mm, made of steel with $\sigma_{ut} = 650$ MPa, is subjected to a fluctuating bending load. The maximum and minimum loads correspond to stresses $\sigma_{max} = 240$ MPa, $\sigma_{min} = -80$ MPa. Surface finish is machined. A shoulder fillet has theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 1.8$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.85$. Required factor of safety = 2. Find the actual factor of safety using Modified Goodman.

**Step 1 — Compute mean and alternating stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_m &= \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{240 + (-80)}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_a &= \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{240 - (-80)}{2} = 160 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the unmodified endurance limit:**

$$
\sigma_e' = 0.5 \times \sigma_{ut} = 0.5 \times 650 = 325 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Marin's modifying factors:**
- Surface factor (machined, $\sigma_{ut} = 650$ MPa): $k_a = 4.51 \times (650)^{-0.265} \approx 0.836$
- Size factor ($d = 50$ mm, $\le 51$ mm): $k_b = 0.85$
- Load factor (bending): $k_c = 1.0$
- Temperature, reliability, miscellaneous: $k_d = k_e = k_f = 1.0$

**Step 4 — Modified endurance limit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_e &= k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \sigma_e' \\
\sigma_e &= 0.836 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 325 \\
\sigma_e &= 230.9 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_f &= 1 + q(K_t - 1) \\
K_f &= 1 + 0.85(1.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.68 = 1.68
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Modified Goodman with FOS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n} &= \frac{K_f \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \\
\frac{1}{n} &= \frac{1.68 \times 160}{230.9} + \frac{80}{650} \\
\frac{1}{n} &= 1.1641 + 0.1231 = 1.2872 \\
n &= \frac{1}{1.2872} = 0.777
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $n = 0.777 < 2.0$ (required). **The design is unsafe and must be revised** — either increase diameter, use a better surface finish, or add a larger fillet radius.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Diagram) — Block Architecture

The S-N curve is conceptualized as a piecewise functional flow that maps stress input to life output:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Cyclic Stress sigma] --> B{Region Identification}
    B --> C[Finite Life Region N between 10^3 and 10^6]
    B --> D[Infinite Life Region N greater than 10^6]
    B --> E[Low Cycle Region N less than 10^3]
    C --> F[Apply Basquins Law: sigma_a = sigma_f prime times N to power b]
    D --> G[Endurance Limit sigma_e reaches horizontal asymptote]
    E --> H[Apply Coffin Manson low cycle fatigue relation]
    F --> I[Output Predicted Cycles to Failure N]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Compare with Required Life}
    J --> K[N greater than required: SAFE]
    J --> L[N less than required: REVISE DESIGN]
```

## 4.2 The Mean-Stress Fatigue Envelope (Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg)

The three classical failure criteria form a family of curves on the $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ plane. The safe design region lies below the line/curve:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sigma m axis mean stress] --> B[Sigma a axis alternating stress]
    B --> C[Static Fracture Point sigma_ut on mean axis]
    C --> D[Pure Reversed Point sigma_e on amplitude axis]
    D --> E[Connecting Curves]
    E --> F[Soderberg Line Most Conservative Through Yield Point sigma_y]
    E --> G[Goodman Line Straight Line Through sigma_ut]
    E --> H[Gerber Parabola Most Accurate for Ductile]
    F --> I[SAFE DESIGN ZONE Below all three curves]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Design Point must lie in safe zone with FOS n greater than 1]
```

## 4.3 Fatigue Design Block Functional Architecture

A complete KTU-style fatigue design proceeds as a sequential functional pipeline:

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Loading sigma_max sigma_min] --> S2[Step 2 Compute sigma_m and sigma_a]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute stress ratio R]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Material Selection sigma_ut sigma_y sigma_e prime]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Apply Marins Modifying Factors]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compute modified sigma_e]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Apply Stress Concentration K_f]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Select Failure Criterion Goodman Gerber Soderberg]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Compute Factor of Safety n]
    S9 --> S10{Compare n with n required}
    S10 -->|Safe| S11[STOP Design Accepted]
    S10 -->|Unsafe| S12[Revise Geometry Material Heat Treat]
    S12 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Types of Stress Cycles (Visual Classification)

The five canonical stress cycles are mapped as a function-generation block:

```mermaid
graph TD
    SC[Stress Cycle sigma t] --> T1[Static Tension sigma constant positive]
    SC --> T2[Static Compression sigma constant negative]
    SC --> T3[Pure Reversed sigma_m equals zero]
    SC --> T4[Repeated zero to max sigma_m equals sigma_a]
    SC --> T5[Fluctuating Asymmetric general case]
    T3 --> R1[Example Rotating shaft with pure bending]
    T4 --> R2[Example Compression spring during one cycle]
    T5 --> R3[Example Combined steady and cyclic loading]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]

**Define fatigue failure. Explain the terms (a) endurance limit and (b) fatigue strength.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fatigue Failure:** Failure of a material under fluctuating stresses of magnitude smaller than the ultimate tensile strength, after a sufficient number of stress cycles. The failure occurs suddenly without any prior plastic deformation.

**(a) Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$):** The maximum value of completely reversed stress which a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (conventionally $N = 10^6$ for steels) without failure. Exists only for ferrous metals.

**(b) Fatigue Strength ($S_f$):** The maximum stress that a material can withstand for a **specified finite number of cycles**. Used for non-ferrous metals (e.g., aluminium, copper alloys) which do not show a true endurance limit.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Distinguish between stress amplitude and mean stress. Define stress ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** The time-averaged (steady) component of the fluctuating stress, given by $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$. It is the "DC offset" of the stress cycle.

**Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$):** The magnitude of the reversing component of the fluctuating stress, given by $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$. It is the "AC amplitude" of the cycle.

**Stress Ratio ($R$):** The dimensionless ratio of minimum to maximum stress in a cycle: $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$.
- $R = -1$ : Pure reversed
- $R = 0$ : Repeated (zero to max)
- $R = +1$ : Static

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Numerical]

A steel shaft of diameter $d = 60$ mm is subjected to a fluctuating torque. The maximum and minimum torques are $T_{max} = 2.4$ kN·m and $T_{min} = 0.6$ kN·m. The material has $\sigma_{ut} = 700$ MPa and $\sigma_y = 500$ MPa. The surface is ground. Assume a theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 1.6$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.9$. Using the **Modified Goodman criterion** and required FOS = 2, find the factor of safety.

#### Part (a) — Compute mean and alternating shear stresses. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Polar section modulus of the shaft:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (60)^3}{16} \\
Z_p &= \frac{\pi \times 216000}{16} = 42411.5 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum and minimum shear stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{T_{max}}{Z_p} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{42411.5} = 56.59 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_{min} &= \frac{T_{min}}{Z_p} = \frac{0.6 \times 10^6}{42411.5} = 14.15 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean and alternating shear stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_m &= \frac{\tau_{max} + \tau_{min}}{2} = \frac{56.59 + 14.15}{2} = 35.37 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_a &= \frac{\tau_{max} - \tau_{min}}{2} = \frac{56.59 - 14.15}{2} = 21.22 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Apply Modified Goodman and find the FOS. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert shear endurance to equivalent bending endurance using distortion energy:**

$$
\sigma_e' = 0.5 \sigma_{ut} = 0.5 \times 700 = 350 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Marin's factors:**
- Ground surface ($\sigma_{ut} = 700$ MPa): $k_a = 1.58 \times (700)^{-0.085} \approx 0.97$
- Size ($d = 60$ mm $> 51$ mm, torsion): $k_b = (51/60)^{0.11} = 0.97$
- Load (torsion): $k_c = 0.59$
- Others = 1.0

$$
\sigma_e = 0.97 \times 0.97 \times 0.59 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 350 = 194.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Fatigue stress concentration factor for torsion:**

$$
K_{fs} = 1 + 0.9(1.6 - 1) = 1.54
$$

**Step 4 — Modified Goodman (using von-Mises equivalent for shear, conservatively use $\sigma_e$ in place of $\tau_e$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n} &= \frac{K_{fs} \tau_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\tau_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \\
\frac{1}{n} &= \frac{1.54 \times 21.22}{194.0} + \frac{35.37}{700} \\
\frac{1}{n} &= 0.1684 + 0.0505 = 0.2189 \\
n &= \frac{1}{0.2189} = 4.57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $n = 4.57 > 2.0$. **Design is SAFE.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use $\sigma_e$ as the shear endurance limit. For KTU boards, you must state explicitly that you are using the **distortion-energy equivalent** and either convert $\tau_e = 0.577 \sigma_e$ and work entirely in shear, or keep the form shown above with justification. Failure to justify the conversion loses **1 mark**.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Theory + Design]

**(a)** Compare the **Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg** failure criteria. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. State the design rule of thumb used in industry. (7 marks)

**(b)** A rotating machine component experiences a bending stress that fluctuates between $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma/3$ with $\sigma = 150$ MPa. The material has $\sigma_{ut} = 500$ MPa, endurance limit $\sigma_e = 230$ MPa (already modified). The component has a hole with $K_t = 2.1$ and $q = 0.8$. Using the **Gerber criterion**, find the factor of safety. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Comparison of Failure Criteria (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tabular Comparison (recommended for board exam):**

| Criterion | Equation (with FOS $n$) | Nature | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|-----------|-------------------------|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{n\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{n\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$ | Linear (most conservative) | Accounts for yielding; safest for ductile materials | Over-designs; rare in practice |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{n\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{n\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} = 1$ | Linear (moderately conservative) | Simple, widely used, safe for brittle | Conservative for ductile; ignores yielding |
| **Gerber** | $\left( \dfrac{n\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right)^2 + \left( \dfrac{n\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 - 2 \left( \dfrac{n\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right) \left( \dfrac{n\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right) \cdot 0 = 1$ (parabolic) | Parabolic (most accurate for ductile) | Closest to experimental data; less conservative | Algebraically more complex |

**Industry Rule of Thumb:** **Modified Goodman** with a factor of safety between **1.5 and 3.0** is the de-facto industrial standard for ductile materials under combined cyclic loading.

#### Part (b) — Gerber Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mean and alternating stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= +150 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{min} = -50 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_m &= \frac{150 + (-50)}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_a &= \frac{150 - (-50)}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$
K_f = 1 + 0.8(2.1 - 1) = 1 + 0.88 = 1.88
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Gerber's criterion in the standard KTU form:**

$$
\left( \frac{K_f \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{ut}} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{n^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{1.88 \times 100}{230} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{50}{500} \right)^2 &= \frac{1}{n^2} \\
(0.8174)^2 + (0.1)^2 &= \frac{1}{n^2} \\
0.6681 + 0.0100 &= \frac{1}{n^2} \\
0.6781 &= \frac{1}{n^2} \\
n^2 &= \frac{1}{0.6781} = 1.4747 \\
n &= \sqrt{1.4747} = 1.214
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $n \approx 1.21$ using Gerber. (Compare: Modified Goodman would give $1/n = 0.8174 + 0.1 = 0.9174 \Rightarrow n = 1.09$, which is more conservative, as expected.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse which stress to amplify with $K_f$. Remember: **only the alternating component** ($\sigma_a$) is amplified, NOT the mean component, when applying the modified Goodman or Gerber to ductile materials. Amplifying both is a common error that costs **2 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Fatigue failure** = progressive fracture under cyclic stress $\ll \sigma_{ut}$, without warning. 80% of all mechanical failures are fatigue-driven.
- **Stress decomposition** is the entry point of every problem: $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$, $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$.
- **Stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ classifies the cycle: $R=-1$ (reversed), $R=0$ (repeated), $R=+1$ (static).
- **Endurance limit** for steel $\sigma_e' = 0.5 \sigma_{ut}$ when $\sigma_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa. For non-ferrous, quote $S_f$ at fixed $N$.
- **Marin's Equation** $\sigma_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \sigma_e'$ is non-negotiable. Surface ($k_a$), size ($k_b$), and load ($k_c$) factors are the most-tested in KTU.
- **Stress concentration** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ applies to the **alternating** stress only (for ductile materials).
- **Goodman** is the KTU-preferred criterion: linear, moderately conservative, simple.
- **Gerber** is most accurate for ductile metals (parabolic, less conservative).
- **Soderberg** is the most conservative (uses yield, not ultimate).
- For **torsion**, always state the distortion-energy conversion explicitly.
- Always end by **comparing calculated $n$ with required $n$** and stating whether the design is safe, marginal, or unsafe.
- Common KTU values to memorize: $k_b = 0.85$ for $d \le 51$ mm; $k_c = 1.0$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endurance Limit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Endurance Limit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Limit (Sₑ)** is defined as the maximum completely reversed bending or torsional stress that a polished standard specimen of a metallic material can withstand for an infinite number of stress cycles (conventionally **10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles** for steels) without exhibiting fatigue failure.

The endurance limit is fundamentally a **fatigue property** of a material, distinct from its static (monotonic) strength properties such as ultimate tensile strength (Sut) or yield strength (Sy). It is read directly from the **Wöhler Curve (S–N Diagram)**, which plots alternating stress amplitude (S) on the linear Y-axis against the number of cycles to failure (N) on a logarithmic X-axis.

For **ferrous metals (steels)**, the S–N curve becomes asymptotic and horizontal beyond approximately **10⁶ cycles**, giving a well-defined endurance limit. For **non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper alloys, etc.)**, the curve continues to slope downward, so engineers use the term **Fatigue Strength** at a specified number of cycles (commonly **5 × 10⁸ or 10⁹ cycles** for aluminum alloys).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The course code PEPET411 (Machine Design) uses the nomenclature **Sₑ** for the *modified endurance limit* and **S'ₑ** (or sometimes Sut' for endurance baseline) for the *uncorrected endurance limit*. The standard reference for a polished, room-temperature, R = –1 rotating beam specimen.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Take a standard **paperclip** and bend it gently back and forth. Each individual bend applies a stress *far below* the paperclip's yield strength — you would never break it in a single bend. Yet after **20 to 30 cycles**, it snaps cleanly. This is **fatigue failure**, and the stress at which it would theoretically never fail no matter how many bends is the **endurance limit**.

A more industrial example is the **rotating axle of an automobile**:
- The surface fibers at the top experience **tensile** stress, and at the bottom **compressive** stress during each rotation.
- After millions of rotations, a microscopic crack initiates at the surface, propagates, and finally causes sudden, brittle-looking fracture — even though the material is ductile and the peak stress was well below the yield point.
- This is why machine designers *cannot* simply use static strength for dynamically loaded components; they must use the endurance limit.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Standard Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Uncorrected Endurance Limit | **S'ₑ** | **0.5 × Sut** (for Sut < 1400 MPa) |
| Modified Endurance Limit | **Sₑ** | **0.3 × Sut to 0.7 × Sut** (after Marin factors) |
| Reference Test Cycles | **N_ref** | **10⁶** cycles (Rotating Beam) |
| Stress Ratio for Test | **R** | **–1** (Fully Reversed) |

> [!TIP]
> The "0.5 × Sut" rule is the **most important empirical shortcut** in KTU Machine Design problems. Memorize it as: *S'ₑ ≈ 0.5 Sut for steels with Sut ≤ 1400 MPa; for Sut > 1400 MPa, use the S-N curve directly.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S–N (Wöhler) Curve — Why It Exists

The S–N curve is the graphical foundation of fatigue design. It is obtained experimentally by testing multiple identical specimens at different stress amplitudes (S) and recording the number of cycles (N) until failure.

- **High Stress Region (Low Cycle Fatigue, LCF):** N < 10³ to 10⁴ cycles. Failure is dominated by **plastic strain**. The Coffin–Manson relation governs.
- **High Cycle Fatigue (HCF):** N > 10⁴ to 10⁶ cycles. Failure is **elastic-strain dominated** and the S–N curve is linear on a semi-log plot.
- **Very High Cycle Region (> 10⁶):** For steels, the curve **flattens (asymptotes)** — this plateau value is the **endurance limit S'ₑ**.
- **For non-ferrous metals (Al, Mg, Cu):** No true asymptote exists; designers use **Fatigue Strength at N = 5×10⁸** (or similar specified life).

## 2.2 The Six Marin Modifying Factors

A standard polished specimen tested in a R.R. Moore rotating-beam machine represents an *ideal* condition. Real machine components have **surface roughness, are larger, may operate at elevated temperature, and are loaded differently**. The KTU syllabus (and the Shigley/V.B. Bhandari standard reference) introduces the **Marin Equation** to modify the uncorrected endurance limit:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each *k* factor is **always ≤ 1** (or 1 in the ideal case), so the modified Sₑ is **always less than or equal to** S'ₑ. This is intuitively correct: real components are weaker than ideal lab specimens.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

| # | Factor | Symbol | Governing Equation | KTU Standard Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surface Factor** | kₐ | kₐ = a · Sutᵇ (units: MPa) | Use **Shigley's Table** for a, b: Ground = 1.58, –0.085; Machined = 4.51, –0.265; Cold-drawn = 4.51, –0.265; Hot-rolled = 57.7, –0.718; As-forged = 272, –0.995 |
| 2 | **Size Factor** | k_b | k_b = 0.879 d^(–0.107) for 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm (axial/rotating); k_b = 1.24 d^(–0.107) for 51 < d ≤ 254 mm | d is the **critical diameter** in mm |
| 3 | **Loading Factor** | k_c | k_c = 1 (axial or bending); k_c = 0.85 (torsion) | Pure torsion drops strength due to **strain gradient** |
| 4 | **Temperature Factor** | k_d | k_d = (Sut @ T) / (Sut @ RT) | For T < 450 °C use tabulated ratio; for T ≤ 50 °C, k_d ≈ 1 |
| 5 | **Reliability Factor** | kₑ | kₑ = 1.000 (50%), 0.897 (90%), 0.868 (95%), 0.753 (99.9%), 0.659 (99.99%) | Statistical confidence; **0.868 for 95% reliability is the KTU default** |
| 6 | **Miscellaneous Factor** | k_f | k_f = 1 / K_f, where K_f is **fatigue stress concentration factor** | Accounts for notches, keyways, holes, threads; K_f = 1 + q(K_t – 1) |

### 2.4 Baseline Uncorrected Endurance Limit Formulas

- **For Steel** (Sut ≤ 1400 MPa):
$$S'_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$$

- **For Steel** (Sut > 1400 MPa): Read from S–N curve at 10⁶ cycles.
- **For Gray Cast Iron:**
$$S'_e = 0.4 \, S_{ut}$$

- **For Aluminum Alloys** (no true endurance limit; use fatigue strength at 5×10⁸):
$$S'_e = 0.4 \, S_{ut}$$ (approximate)

- **For Copper Alloys:**
$$S'_e = 0.3 \, S_{ut}$$ (approximate)

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The modified endurance limit Sₑ is the **foundation** for:
1. **Goodman Criterion** — σₐ/Sₑ + σₘ/Sut = 1/n
2. **Gerber Criterion** — parabolic, more liberal
3. **Soderberg Criterion** — conservative, uses yield
4. **DE-Goodman** (modified Goodman, the **KTU default** in design problems)

> [!TIP]
> In **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems**, the final machine design part almost always asks you to compute Sₑ by chaining the Marin factors, then apply the **Modified Goodman line** for combined steady + alternating stresses. Practice this two-step workflow until it becomes second nature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Marin Surface Factor (kₐ)

The empirical relationship between surface finish and endurance limit was fitted by Shigley from experimental data:

**Starting form (log-linear fit on Sut):**

$$\log(k_a) = \log(a) + b \cdot \log(S_{ut})$$

**Exponentiating both sides:**

$$k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}$$

**Typical constants from the Shigley reference table:**

| Surface Finish | a (Sut in MPa) | b |
|---|---|---|
| Ground | 1.58 | –0.085 |
| Machined or Cold-Drawn | 4.51 | –0.265 |
| Hot-Rolled | 57.7 | –0.718 |
| As-Forged | 272 | –0.995 |

**Logic behind the formula:**
- Higher Sut → the kₐ exponent b is less negative, so the surface factor stays closer to 1.
- This reflects that **high-strength steels are less sensitive to surface flaws** than low-strength steels.
- For Sut = 1000 MPa, machined: kₐ = 4.51 × 1000^(–0.265) = 4.51 × 0.0947 ≈ **0.427**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Size Factor (k_b)

For **rotating bending or axial loading** with critical diameter d in the range 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm:

$$k_b = 0.879 \cdot d^{-0.107}$$

**Step-by-step manipulation:**

- Logarithm: log(k_b) = log(0.879) – 0.107 · log(d)
- The exponent **–0.107** comes from the volume/surface area ratio effect: larger specimens have **higher statistical probability of containing a critical flaw** at the surface.
- For d = 51 mm (the upper limit): k_b = 0.879 × 51^(–0.107) ≈ 0.879 × 0.610 ≈ **0.536**.
- For 51 < d ≤ 254 mm (axial/torsion only): k_b = 1.24 · d^(–0.107), which gives k_b ≈ 0.768 at d = 51 mm and decays slowly.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor (K_f)

K_f is **not** the elastic stress concentration factor K_t alone; it accounts for the **sensitivity of the material to notches** via the **Neuber equation**:

$$K_f = 1 + q\,(K_t - 1)$$

**Where the notch sensitivity q is given by:**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a'/\sqrt{r}}}$$

- **r** = notch radius (in mm)
- **a'** = Neuber's constant (mm¹ᐟ²), material property

| Material | Sut (MPa) | a' (√mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 550 | 0.062 |
| Medium Steel | 650 | 0.076 |
| Hard Steel | 1700 | 0.130 |
| Aluminum Alloy | 350 | 0.0404 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Exam Point:** The miscellaneous factor **k_f = 1/K_f**, NOT K_f. The "reduction" in endurance limit from stress concentration is **inversely proportional** to K_f.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example (KTU 2024 Standard)

**Problem:** A 35 mm shaft is made of machined steel with Sut = 600 MPa, Sy = 380 MPa, subjected to reversed bending, d = 35 mm, 95% reliability, room temperature, with a shoulder fillet of r = 2 mm and K_t = 2.0. Find Sₑ.

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$S'_e = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor (machined):**

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 600^{-0.265}$$

**Computing the exponent:**

$$600^{-0.265} = 10^{-0.265 \times \log_{10}(600)} = 10^{-0.265 \times 2.778} = 10^{-0.7362}$$

$$\Rightarrow 600^{-0.265} \approx 0.1836$$

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 0.1836 = 0.828$$

**Step 3 — Size factor (d = 35 mm, in range):**

$$k_b = 0.879 \times 35^{-0.107}$$

$$35^{-0.107} = 10^{-0.107 \times 1.544} = 10^{-0.1652} \approx 0.684$$

$$k_b = 0.879 \times 0.684 = 0.601$$

**Step 4 — Loading factor (bending):**

$$k_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor (room temperature):**

$$k_d = 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor (95%):**

$$k_e = 0.868$$

**Step 7 — Notch sensitivity (assuming Sut = 600 MPa → medium steel → a' = 0.076):**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.076/\sqrt{2}}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.0537}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.232} = 0.812$$

**Step 8 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.812 \times (2.0 - 1) = 1.812$$

**Step 9 — Miscellaneous factor:**

$$k_f = \frac{1}{K_f} = \frac{1}{1.812} = 0.552$$

**Step 10 — Modified Endurance Limit:**

$$S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$$

$$S_e = 0.828 \times 0.601 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.868 \times 0.552 \times 300$$

**Chain multiplication:**

$$0.828 \times 0.601 = 0.4976$$

$$0.4976 \times 0.868 = 0.4320$$

$$0.4320 \times 0.552 = 0.2385$$

$$0.2385 \times 300 = 71.55 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{S_e \approx 71.6 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice how drastically Sₑ (71.6 MPa) drops from S'ₑ (300 MPa). This ~76% reduction is **typical** for a real-world, machined, notched shaft with 95% reliability. Always use Sₑ in design — never the textbook 0.5 × Sut value directly.

## 3.5 Summary Table of Numerical Magnitudes

| Property | Magnitude | % of Sut |
|---|---|---|
| S'ₑ (uncorrected) | 300 MPa | 50% |
| × kₐ = 0.828 | 248.4 | 41.4% |
| × k_b = 0.601 | 149.3 | 24.9% |
| × kₑ = 0.868 | 129.6 | 21.6% |
| × k_f = 0.552 | 71.6 | 11.9% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Marin Factor Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Material and Geometry Inputs]) --> node1[Step 1: Compute S prime e from Sut using 0.5 Sut rule]
    node1 --> node2{Step 2: Surface Finish}
    node2 --> n2a[Ground, Machined, Cold-Drawn, Hot-Rolled, As-Forged] --> node3[Compute k sub a]
    node3 --> n3a[Step 3: Size in mm] --> node4[Compute k sub b using 0.879 d power minus 0.107]
    node4 --> n4a[Step 4: Type of Loading] --> node5[Choose k sub c: 1.0 bending, 0.85 torsion]
    node5 --> n5a[Step 5: Temperature] --> node6[Compute k sub d from ratio of Sut at T to Sut at room]
    node6 --> n6a[Step 6: Reliability percentage] --> node7[Look up k sub e: 0.897, 0.868, 0.753]
    node7 --> n7a[Step 7: Notch and Stress Concentration] --> node8[Compute q from Neuber then K sub f then k sub f equals 1 over K sub f]
    node8 --> node9[Step 8: Multiply all six k factors with S prime e]
    node9 --> node10[Final Output: S sub e = Modified Endurance Limit]
    node10 --> node11[Step 9: Apply to Goodman or Soderberg line for design]
    node11 --> stopA([End: Design Stress Calculated])
```

## 4.2 S-N (Wohler) Curve Schematic via Mermaid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[Vertical Axis: Alternating Stress S in MPa] --> point1[High S, Low N region: Low Cycle Fatigue, plastic strain dominated, less than 10 to power 4]
    axisB[Horizontal Axis: Log Scale N to failure] --> point2[Mid S, Mid N region: High Cycle Fatigue, elastic strain, 10 to power 4 to 10 to power 6]
    point1 --> point2
    point2 --> point3[Low S plateau at 10 to power 6: Endurance Limit S prime e for steels]
    point3 --> point4[For non-ferrous: continuous slope, no plateau, use Fatigue Strength at 5 times 10 to power 8]
    style point3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style point4 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: From Material Data to Design Stress

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input: Sut, Sy, Type of Load, Geometry, Surface, T, Reliability, Notch Radius] --> procA[Pre-Processing: Lookup Marin Table Constants]
    procA --> procB[Compute S prime e equals 0.5 Sut baseline]
    procB --> procC[Apply Marin Chain: k sub a to k sub f]
    procC --> outputA[Output: S sub e Modified Endurance Limit]
    outputA --> procD[Combine with Steady Stress and Alternating Stress]
    procD --> procE[Apply Modified Goodman Line: sigma a over S sub e plus sigma m over Sut equals 1 over n]
    procE --> outputB[Output: Factor of Safety n or Allowable Stress]
    outputB --> decisionA{n greater than or equal to 1.5?}
    decisionA -- Yes --> finalA[Design ACCEPTED]
    decisionA -- No --> finalB[Redesign: Increase Diameter or Change Material]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the design for fatigue," always start by computing S'ₑ, then walk through all six Marin factors in order, then apply the **Modified Goodman line**. Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE award 1 mark per correct factor application.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "endurance limit" of a material. Why is it not applicable to non-ferrous metals?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The endurance limit (Sₑ) is the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a polished standard specimen can withstand for an infinite number of stress cycles (10⁶ to 10⁷) without fatigue failure. For **ferrous metals** such as steels, the S–N curve becomes asymptotic and horizontal, providing a well-defined endurance limit.

For **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum, copper, magnesium alloys), the S–N curve **does not become horizontal**; it continues to slope downward even at 5 × 10⁸ or 10⁹ cycles. Hence, no true endurance limit exists. For these, designers use the term **fatigue strength at a specified number of cycles**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for ferrous behavior, 1 for non-ferrous behavior)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List the six Marin modifying factors and state the typical value of the reliability factor (kₑ) for 95% reliability.**

**Model Answer:**

The six Marin factors are:

1. **kₐ** — Surface modification factor
2. **k_b** — Size modification factor
3. **k_c** — Load modification factor
4. **k_d** — Temperature modification factor
5. **kₑ** — Reliability modification factor
6. **k_f** — Miscellaneous effects modification factor (stress concentration)

For 95% reliability, **kₑ = 0.868**.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for listing six factors, 1.5 marks for the kₑ value)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. The two alternatives below are completely independent and cover different aspects of the endurance limit.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q:** A shaft of diameter **d = 40 mm** is made of **medium carbon steel** with the following properties: **Sut = 620 MPa, Sy = 390 MPa**. The shaft is **machined** (surface finish), operates at **room temperature**, is loaded in **reversed bending**, and must be designed for **99% reliability**. The shaft has a **shoulder fillet** with notch radius **r = 3 mm** and theoretical stress concentration factor **K_t = 1.8**.

**(a) [7 Marks] Compute the uncorrected endurance limit S'ₑ and the modified endurance limit Sₑ using all six Marin factors.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the shaft is subjected to a steady bending moment of 200 N·m and a completely reversed bending moment of 120 N·m, determine the factor of safety using the Modified Goodman criterion.**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Computing S'ₑ and Sₑ

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$S'_e = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 620 = 310 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor (machined):**

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 620^{-0.265}$$

$$\log(620) = 2.7924$$

$$620^{-0.265} = 10^{-0.265 \times 2.7924} = 10^{-0.7400} = 0.1820$$

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 0.1820 = 0.821$$

**Step 3 — Size factor (d = 40 mm, in 2.79–51 range):**

$$k_b = 0.879 \times 40^{-0.107}$$

$$40^{-0.107} = 10^{-0.107 \times 1.6021} = 10^{-0.1714} = 0.6739$$

$$k_b = 0.879 \times 0.6739 = 0.5924$$

**Step 4 — Loading factor (reversed bending):**

$$k_c = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor (room temperature):**

$$k_d = 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor (99% reliability):**

$$k_e = 0.753$$

**Step 7 — Notch sensitivity (medium steel, Sut = 620 MPa, a' = 0.076):**

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a'/\sqrt{r}}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{0.076/\sqrt{3}}}$$

$$\sqrt{3} = 1.732$$

$$\frac{0.076}{1.732} = 0.04389$$

$$\sqrt{0.04389} = 0.2095$$

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2095} = \frac{1}{1.2095} = 0.8268$$

**Step 8 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.8268 \times (1.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.8268 \times 0.8 = 1 + 0.6614 = 1.6614$$

**Step 9 — Miscellaneous factor:**

$$k_f = \frac{1}{K_f} = \frac{1}{1.6614} = 0.6020$$

**Step 10 — Modified Endurance Limit:**

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e$$

$$S_e = 0.821 \times 0.5924 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.753 \times 0.6020 \times 310$$

**Chain multiplication:**

$$0.821 \times 0.5924 = 0.4864$$

$$0.4864 \times 0.753 = 0.3663$$

$$0.3663 \times 0.6020 = 0.2205$$

$$0.2205 \times 310 = 68.36 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{S_e \approx 68.4 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Modified Goodman Factor of Safety

**Step 1 — Section modulus for circular shaft:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 64000}{32} = 6283.2 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Alternating bending stress (fully reversed):**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{32 \times M_a}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 120 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 40^3} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{201062} = 19.10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Mean bending stress:**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{32 \times M_m}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 200 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 40^3} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^6}{201062} = 31.83 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Modified Goodman equation:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{19.10}{68.4} + \frac{31.83}{620}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = 0.2792 + 0.05134 = 0.3305$$

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{0.3305} \approx 3.02}$$

> [!TIP]
> Since n = 3.02 > 1.5 (typical KTU design safety factor), the design is **safe**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q:** A round bar of **diameter 50 mm** is subjected to **reversed torsion** (completely reversed). The material has **Sut = 500 MPa** and the surface is **ground**. The component operates at **50 °C**, with **90% reliability**, and has a **transverse hole** with K_t = 2.2 and r = 4 mm.

**(a) [7 Marks] Compute the uncorrected endurance limit in shear, the corrected endurance limit in shear, and explain why torsion uses a different loading factor than bending.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the bar carries a steady torque of 400 N·m and a fluctuating torque of ±200 N·m, calculate the factor of safety using the Modified Goodman criterion in shear.**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Endurance Limit in Shear

**Step 1 — Uncorrected endurance limit in bending (then convert to shear):**

$$S'_e = 0.5 \times 500 = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Marin factors:**

**Surface (ground):**

$$k_a = 1.58 \times 500^{-0.085}$$

$$500^{-0.085} = 10^{-0.085 \times 2.699} = 10^{-0.2294} = 0.5902$$

$$k_a = 1.58 \times 0.5902 = 0.9325$$

**Size (d = 50 mm, at boundary of range 2.79–51 mm):**

$$k_b = 0.879 \times 50^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.6433 = 0.5655$$

**Loading (torsion):**

$$k_c = 0.85$$

**Temperature (≤ 50 °C):**

$$k_d \approx 1.0$$

**Reliability (90%):**

$$k_e = 0.897$$

**Notch sensitivity (medium steel, a' = 0.076, r = 4 mm):**

$$\sqrt{4} = 2$$

$$\frac{0.076}{2} = 0.038$$

$$\sqrt{0.038} = 0.1949$$

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1949} = 0.8368$$

**K_f in bending:**

$$K_f = 1 + 0.8368 \times (2.2 - 1) = 1 + 1.0042 = 2.0042$$

$$k_f = \frac{1}{2.0042} = 0.4990$$

**Step 3 — Corrected endurance limit in bending:**

$$S_e = 0.9325 \times 0.5655 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 0.4990 \times 250$$

$$= 0.9325 \times 0.5655 = 0.5274$$

$$\times 0.85 = 0.4483$$

$$\times 0.897 = 0.4021$$

$$\times 0.4990 = 0.2007$$

$$\times 250 = 50.17 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{S_e \approx 50.2 \text{ MPa (in bending)}}$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to shear endurance limit:**

> [!NOTE]
> Empirical relation: **Sₛₑ = 0.577 × Sₑ ≈ 0.577 × 50.17 = 28.95 MPa**

**Step 5 — Explanation for k_c = 0.85 in torsion:**

> The loading factor for torsion is 0.85 (instead of 1.0 for bending/axial) because **pure torsional loading produces a uniform stress state across the entire cross-section** (no stress gradient). Under bending, only the outermost fibers carry maximum stress, giving the material more "opportunity" to redistribute and resist failure. In torsion, **every fiber is equally stressed** at the surface, so the volume of highly stressed material is greater, and the effective strength is reduced. Empirically, this corresponds to a 15% reduction in fatigue strength, hence k_c = 0.85.

---

### Part (b) — Modified Goodman in Shear

**Step 1 — Section modulus in torsion:**

$$Z_t = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 50^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 125000}{16} = 24544 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Alternating shear stress:**

$$\tau_a = \frac{T_a}{Z_t} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{24544} = 8.149 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Mean shear stress:**

$$\tau_m = \frac{T_m}{Z_t} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{24544} = 16.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Ultimate shear strength** (use S_sut = 0.577 × Sut):

$$S_{su} = 0.577 \times 500 = 288.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Modified Goodman in shear:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\tau_a}{S_{se}} + \frac{\tau_m}{S_{su}}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{8.149}{28.95} + \frac{16.30}{288.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = 0.2815 + 0.0565 = 0.3380$$

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{0.3380} \approx 2.96}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #1:** Forgetting to apply **k_c = 0.85** in torsion. Students write k_c = 1.0 (bending value) and get inflated Sₑ. This costs **1.5 marks**.
> 2. **MISTAKE #2:** Using **Sut** directly in the Goodman line for torsion instead of **S_sut = 0.577 × Sut**. Always convert to shear.
> 3. **MISTAKE #3:** Mixing up **K_f** (fatigue stress concentration factor) with **K_t** (theoretical/elastic stress concentration factor). K_f accounts for notch sensitivity *q*, K_t does not. Using K_t directly gives overly conservative answers.
> 4. **MISTAKE #4:** Forgetting to **square K_f = 1.6614 → 2.760** when working in the Goodman line. The Marin equation uses linear K_f in the k_f factor, but the stress concentration *applied to the stress* uses **K_f itself**, not its reciprocal. Re-read the question carefully to know which form to use.
> 5. **MISTAKE #5:** Computing the size factor with **0.879 d^(-0.107)** formula even when **d > 51 mm**. The larger range uses 1.24 d^(-0.107).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Endurance Limit (Module 1)**

- **Definition (Sₑ):** Maximum reversed stress for infinite life; for steels it occurs at **N ≈ 10⁶ cycles**.
- **Baseline formula for steels (Sut ≤ 1400 MPa):** **S'ₑ = 0.5 × Sut**. Memorize this; it is asked in 80% of KTU problems.
- **The Marin Equation:** Sₑ = kₐ · k_b · k_c · k_d · kₑ · k_f · S'ₑ — six factors, each ≤ 1.
- **Surface factor (kₐ):** Strongest steels are least sensitive. Ground ≈ 1.0, As-forged ≈ 0.2–0.4.
- **Size factor (k_b):** Always ≤ 1. Use **0.879 d^(–0.107)** for 2.79 ≤ d ≤ 51 mm.
- **Loading factor (k_c):** 1.0 for bending/axial, **0.85 for torsion**.
- **Temperature factor (k_d):** T < 50 °C → ≈ 1.0; T = 200 °C → ≈ 0.85; T = 400 °C → ≈ 0.55.
- **Reliability factor (kₑ):** 50% = 1.000; 90% = 0.897; **95% = 0.868 (KTU default)**; 99% = 0.753.
- **Notch sensitivity (q):** Use **Neuber equation** with a' from material tables.
- **Miscellaneous factor:** k_f = 1 / K_f, where K_f = 1 + q(K_t – 1).
- **Modified Goodman line** is the KTU default for combined steady + alternating stress:
  - Bending: σₐ/Sₑ + σₘ/Sut = 1/n
  - Torsion: τₐ/Sₛₑ + τₘ/Sₛᵤₜ = 1/n
- **Sₛₑ = 0.577 × Sₑ** and **Sₛᵤₜ = 0.577 × Sut** for shear conversions.
- **No endurance limit for non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Mg) — use **fatigue strength at specified N**.
- **Typical reduction** from S'ₑ to Sₑ in real components is **60–80%** — the textbook 0.5 Sut value is rarely used directly.
- **Factor of safety check:** n ≥ 1.5 for KTU 2024 ESE design problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Finite and Infinite Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design for Finite and Infinite Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design (PEPET411)**, the concept of **Design for Finite and Infinite Life** refers to a deterministic fatigue-based design philosophy that determines the geometric dimensions of a machine element subjected to **fluctuating (cyclic) loads** such that the element either survives an *unlimited* number of stress cycles (Infinite Life Design) or a *specified, limited* number of cycles before the onset of fatigue failure (Finite Life Design).

The two regimes are governed by the **S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)** of the material:

- **Infinite Life Regime:** A stress level below the **Endurance Limit (σe or Se)** — typically $10^6$ to $10^8$ cycles for steels — at which the component has theoretically infinite life. The classical assumption is that failures occur only due to wear, corrosion, or accidental overloads, not fatigue.
- **Finite Life Regime:** A stress level *above* the endurance limit, where the component has a *predictable, finite* number of cycles to failure (Nf). Used for components with a predetermined service life (e.g., aircraft components, automotive parts scheduled for replacement, springs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Machine Design stresses that the designer must **always** first attempt an **Infinite Life Design** (because it is safer, cheaper, and avoids in-service inspection), and only opt for **Finite Life Design** when weight, size, or cost constraints make infinite life impractical — typically for non-ferrous metals and welded structures which *do not* possess a true endurance limit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Paper Clip Story"

Imagine bending a paper clip back and forth:

- **First few bends (High Stress Amplitude, σa > σe):** The clip breaks after ~8–10 bends. This is **Finite Life** — every bend consumes a portion of the clip's "fatigue life budget."
- **If you could bend it with a *very* small force (σa < σe):** It would never break, no matter how many times you bend it. This is **Infinite Life** — the stress is so small it is below the threshold of damage.
- **Halfway (Moderate Stress):** The clip survives 50, 100, or 1000 bends depending on how hard you bend it. The harder you bend (higher σa), the fewer the cycles to failure. This predictable degradation is governed by the **S-N curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The **S-N curve** is essentially a "lifetime chart" — telling you *how many cycles* a material survives at *each stress level*. Your design task is to pick a point on this chart that satisfies the part's required service life.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma_{ut}$ (or $S_{ut}$) | Ultimate Tensile Strength | MPa (N/mm²) |
| $\sigma_{y}$ (or $S_{yt}$) | Yield Strength | MPa (N/mm²) |
| $\sigma_{e}$ (or $S_{e}'$) | Uncorrected Endurance Limit | MPa |
| $\sigma_{e}'$ (or $S_{e}$) | Corrected Endurance Limit | MPa |
| $K_{a}$ | Surface Finish Modification Factor | Dimensionless |
| $K_{b}$ | Size Modification Factor | Dimensionless |
| $K_{c}$ | Load Modification Factor | Dimensionless |
| $K_{d}$ | Temperature Modification Factor | Dimensionless |
| $K_{e}$ | Reliability Modification Factor | Dimensionless |
| $K_{f}$ | Miscellaneous Effects Factor | Dimensionless |
| $N$ | Number of Cycles | Dimensionless |
| $\sigma_{a}, \sigma_{m}$ | Stress Amplitude, Mean Stress | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention:** In KTU 2024 textbooks, the **uncorrected endurance limit** is given by the empirical relation: $S_{e}' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa. Beyond 1400 MPa, the line slopes downward following the empirical Gerber–Scribner relation. The *corrected* endurance limit applied to the actual part is $S_{e} = K_{a} K_{b} K_{c} K_{d} K_{e} K_{f} \, S_{e}'$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Fluctuating Stress Cycles

Before any fatigue design, the KTU 2024 Scheme mandates identification of the *type* of stress cycle. The classification depends on $\sigma_{max}$, $\sigma_{min}$, mean stress $\sigma_m$, amplitude $\sigma_a$, and the stress ratio $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$.

| Cycle Type | Stress Ratio $R$ | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reversed (Symmetric)** | $R = -1$ | Equal tension and compression (e.g., rotating shaft with bending) |
| **Repeated (Tension-Tension)** | $0 \le R < 1$ | Same sign, $\sigma_{min} > 0$ (e.g., tension spring) |
| **Repeated (Compression-Compression)** | $R > 1$ | Same sign, $\sigma_{max} < 0$ |
| **Fluctuating (Asymmetric)** | $-1 < R < 0$ or $R > 0$ | Both signs may occur |
| **Static** | $R = 1$ | Constant stress (no fatigue, use static design) |

## 2.2 Fundamental Mean and Amplitude Stress Equations

The **decomposition theorem** (Merriam–Shigley) states that *any fluctuating stress cycle can be uniquely decomposed into a steady mean stress and a fully reversed stress component*:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{m} + \sigma_{a} \qquad \sigma_{min} = \sigma_{m} - \sigma_{a}
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
\sigma_{m} = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} \qquad \sigma_{a} = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

The **stress range** is $\sigma_{r} = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a$, and the **amplitude ratio** is $A = \sigma_a / \sigma_m$.

## 2.3 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Theoretical Foundation

The S-N curve is the *graphical backbone* of fatigue design. The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes two key regions:

1. **The Endurance Region (Plateau):** For most steels, the curve becomes horizontal at $\sigma_a = S_e'$ (the fatigue limit). This is the **Infinite Life** region.
2. **The Finite Life Region (Sloped Line):** For $N < 10^6$ cycles, the curve follows a power law known as **Basquin's Equation** (used for finite life design).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery of this table is non-negotiable** for KTU ESE. Memorize symbols, units, and applicability.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Applicability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma_{m} = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}) / 2$ | Mean stress of cycle |
| 2 | $\sigma_{a} = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}) / 2$ | Alternating (amplitude) stress |
| 3 | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | Stress ratio (characterizes cycle type) |
| 4 | $A = \sigma_{a} / \sigma_{m}$ | Amplitude ratio |
| 5 | $S_{e}' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa | Uncorrected endurance limit (steels) |
| 6 | $S_{e} = K_{a} K_{b} K_{c} K_{d} K_{e} K_{f} \, S_{e}'$ | Corrected endurance limit |
| 7 | $K_{a} = a \cdot S_{ut}^{b}$ (Shigley) | Surface modification factor (function of finish) |
| 8 | $K_{b} = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm) | Size factor for axial/bending, round |
| 9 | $K_{b} = 0.91 \, d^{-0.157}$ (for $51 \le d \le 254$ mm) | Size factor (larger) |
| 10 | $K_{c} = 1$ (axial), $0.85$ (bending), $0.59$ (torsion) | Load factor (for steel under torsion) |
| 11 | $\sigma_{a}' = \sigma_{a} \cdot (1 - \sigma_{m}/S_{ut})$ | Equivalent fully-reversed stress (Goodman) |
| 12 | $\sigma_{a}' = \sigma_{a} \cdot (1 - \sigma_{m}/S_{yt})$ | Equivalent fully-reversed stress (Soderberg) |
| 13 | $S_{N} = a \, N^{b}$ (Basquin) | Finite life fatigue strength at N cycles |
| 14 | $b = \frac{1}{3} \log(0.5 \, S_{ut} / S_{3\times 10^{3}})$ | Slope exponent of S-N line |
| 15 | $\sum_{i} (n_{i} / N_{i}) = 1$ (Miner's rule) | Cumulative damage criterion (failure) |
| 16 | $\sigma_{a}/S_{e} + \sigma_{m}/S_{ut} = 1/n$ | Modified Goodman line (factor of safety n) |
| 17 | $\sigma_{a}/S_{e} + \sigma_{m}/S_{yt} = 1/n$ | Soderberg line (more conservative) |
| 18 | $(n \, \sigma_{a}/S_{e})^{2} + n \, \sigma_{m}/S_{ut} = 1$ | Gerber parabola (less conservative, realistic) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Crankshafts:** Infinite life design — must outlast the vehicle (use $S_e$ with $K_d, K_e = 0.814$ for 90% reliability).
- **Aircraft Wing Spars:** Finite life design — designed for ~50,000 flight cycles with periodic inspection (use $S_N$ at $N = 50{,}000$).
- **Springs, Bolts, Gears:** Typically use the **modified Goodman** line for the elliptical parabolic description of static + cyclic failure.
- **Welding & Non-ferrous Alloys:** No true endurance limit → strictly **finite life** design per S-N curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Specific:** KTU typically uses the **Modified Goodman criterion** (worst-case, conservative) for ESE numericals, with the **Soderberg criterion** used when the question specifically mentions "no yielding permitted."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Basquin's Equation for Finite Life Design

**Statement:** The Basquin relation states that the finite-life fatigue strength $S_N$ at a given number of cycles $N$ is a power-law function of $N$:

$$
S_{N} = a \cdot N^{b}
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are material constants. We must determine $a$ and $b$ from the two known points of the S-N line.

**Step 1 — Identify the two known points on the S-N line:**

For steels, the standard S-N line is anchored at:
- Point 1: $N_{1} = 10^{3}$ cycles, $S_{N_1} \approx 0.9 \, S_{ut}$
- Point 2: $N_{2} = 10^{6}$ cycles, $S_{N_2} = S_{e}' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$

> **Logic:** The material survives 1000 cycles at ~90% of ultimate strength and 1 million cycles at the endurance limit.

**Step 2 — Apply the power-law form at both points:**

$$
S_{N_1} = a \cdot (N_{1})^{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.9 \, S_{ut} = a \cdot (10^{3})^{b}
$$

$$
S_{N_2} = a \cdot (N_{2})^{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.5 \, S_{ut} = a \cdot (10^{6})^{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide the two equations to eliminate $a$:**

$$
\frac{0.5 \, S_{ut}}{0.9 \, S_{ut}} = \frac{a \cdot (10^{6})^{b}}{a \cdot (10^{3})^{b}} = (10^{6}/10^{3})^{b} = (10^{3})^{b}
$$

$$
\frac{0.5}{0.9} = (10^{3})^{b}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the slope exponent $b$:**

$$
(10^{3})^{b} = 0.5556 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3b \log_{10}(10) = \log_{10}(0.5556)
$$

$$
3b = -0.2553 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -0.0851
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the constant $a$:**

$$
a = \frac{0.9 \, S_{ut}}{(10^{3})^{-0.0851}} = 0.9 \, S_{ut} \cdot (10^{3})^{0.0851} = 0.9 \, S_{ut} \cdot 1.464
$$

$$
a \approx 1.317 \, S_{ut}
$$

**Step 6 — Write the final S-N equation (Basquin's form):**

$$
S_{N} = 1.317 \, S_{ut} \cdot N^{-0.0851} \quad \text{(valid for } 10^{3} \le N \le 10^{6} \text{)}
$$

**Step 7 — Generalized form for any two known points (KTU exam tip):**

If only the *uncorrected* endurance limit $S_e'$ and the stress at $N_1$ are known, take logarithms:

$$
\log(S_{N}) = \log(a) + b \cdot \log(N)
$$

This is a straight line on log-log axes. The slope is $b$ and intercept is $\log(a)$. KTU exam questions often give you two $(N, S_N)$ points and ask you to derive the equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Modified Goodman Line (Factor of Safety Form)

**Statement:** For a combined steady (mean) and alternating (fatigue) load, the modified Goodman line defines the boundary between safe and unsafe regions. The criterion is:

$$
\frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

where $n$ is the factor of safety. Derivation from first principles:

**Step 1 — Define the load line on the Haigh diagram:**

The Haigh diagram is a $\sigma_m$ (x-axis) vs $\sigma_a$ (y-axis) plot. A real operating load $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ is connected to the origin by a "load line" with slope $A = \sigma_a / \sigma_m$.

**Step 2 — Goodman line equation (no safety factor):**

The original Goodman line connects two anchor points: $(\sigma_m = 0, \sigma_a = S_e)$ on the y-axis, and $(\sigma_m = S_{ut}, \sigma_a = 0)$ on the x-axis. The straight line through them is:

$$
\frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce factor of safety $n$:**

The factor of safety $n$ radially scales the load line from the origin. If the load point $(n \, \sigma_m, n \, \sigma_a)$ just touches the Goodman line:

$$
\frac{n \, \sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{n \, \sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
n \left( \frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} \right) = 1
$$

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}}
$$

This is the **Modified Goodman Factor of Safety** — the workhorse KTU formula for fatigue design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Completely-Reversed Stress

**Purpose:** Reduce a fluctuating stress cycle to an *equivalent* symmetric-reversed cycle so that it can be checked against $S_e$ directly.

**Step 1 — Start with the Goodman equation (no safety factor):**

$$
\frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $\sigma_a$:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a} = S_{e} \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define the equivalent reversed stress $\sigma_{a}'$:**

When the original cycle is converted to a fully-reversed one, the amplitude is:

$$
\sigma_{a}' = \sigma_{a} \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Safety check:** For infinite life, $\sigma_{a}' \le S_{e}$ (with safety factor: $n = S_e / \sigma_{a}'$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Criterion

| Criterion | Equation (with $n$) | Use Case (KTU context) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Soderberg** | $\sigma_{a}/S_{e} + \sigma_{m}/S_{yt} = 1/n$ | **Most conservative.** Use when *no yielding* is allowed. |
| **Modified Goodman** | $\sigma_{a}/S_{e} + \sigma_{m}/S_{ut} = 1/n$ | **KTU default.** Use for ductile materials under fluctuating load. |
| **Gerber** | $(n\sigma_{a}/S_{e})^{2} + n\sigma_{m}/S_{ut} = 1$ | **Less conservative.** Use for ductile materials where experimental data fits. |
| **Elliptic (Sines)** | $(n\sigma_{a}/S_{e})^{2} + (n\sigma_{m}/S_{yt})^{2} = 1$ | **Intermediate.** Use for brittle or notch-sensitive materials. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Conceptual Block Diagram

The S-N curve is the visual heart of fatigue design. Below is a Mermaid-rendered block diagram showing the *behavioral phases* of the curve (since Mermaid cannot render cartesian plots, this is a functional state-machine representation):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Cyclic Stress sigma_a] --> B{Is sigma_a greater than S_e prime?}
    B -- "Yes" --> C[Finite Life Region: 10^3 to 10^6 cycles]
```
