```mermaid
    B -- "No" --> D[Infinite Life Region: sigma_a less than S_e prime]
    C --> C1[Calculate using Basquin: S_N = a N to the power b]
    C1 --> C2[Check cumulative damage using Miner's rule]
    C2 --> C3{N exceeds allowable N?}
    C3 -- "Yes" --> FAIL[FAILURE: Fatigue crack initiates]
    C3 -- "No" --> SAFE[SAFE for finite life N]
    D --> D1[Design Check: sigma_a less than S_e]
    D1 --> D2[Apply Marin Factors K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e K_f]
    D2 --> D3{Sigma_a less than corrected S_e?}
    D3 -- "Yes" --> INFS[SAFE: Infinite life ensured]
    D3 -- "No" --> FAIL
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top with the applied cyclic stress. The decision branch (B) is the critical check — is the stress amplitude above or below the material's endurance limit? Each path leads to a distinct design philosophy.

---

## 4.2 The Haigh Diagram — Mean vs Alternating Stress Failure Envelopes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Haigh Diagram - 2D Failure Map"
        X[Mean Stress sigma_m] --> OR[Origin]
        OR --> YP[Vertical axis: sigma_a]
        YP --> SE[Point: sigma_m equals 0, sigma_a equals S_e]
        X --> SUT[Point: sigma_a equals 0, sigma_m equals S_ut]
        SY[Point: sigma_m equals 0, sigma_a equals S_yt] --> X
        X --> SYT[Point: sigma_a equals 0, sigma_m equals S_yt]
    end
    SE -. "Goodman Line" .-> SUT
    SY -. "Soderberg Line" .-> SYT
    SE -. "Gerber Parabola" .-> SUT
    OR --> LP["Operating Load Point (sigma_m, sigma_a)"]
    LP -. "Load Line" .-> OR
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The operating load point (a single location) is compared against the failure envelope (Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber). If the point lies *inside* the envelope → SAFE. If *outside* → FAILURE.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Marin Factor Cascade for Corrected Endurance Limit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S_ut[S_ut: Ultimate Tensile Strength] --> S_e_prime["Compute S_e prime equals 0.5 S_ut"]
    S_e_prime --> K_a[Step 1: Apply Surface Factor K_a]
    K_a --> K_b[Step 2: Apply Size Factor K_b]
    K_b --> K_c[Step 3: Apply Load Factor K_c]
    K_c --> K_d[Step 4: Apply Temperature Factor K_d]
    K_d --> K_e[Step 5: Apply Reliability Factor K_e]
    K_e --> K_f[Step 6: Apply Miscellaneous Factor K_f]
    K_f --> S_e[Final Corrected Endurance Limit S_e]
    S_e --> CHK{Compare with sigma_a prime}
    CHK -- "sigma_a prime less than S_e" --> OK[Design is Safe Infinite Life]
    CHK -- "sigma_a prime greater than S_e" --> FL[Re-design or Finite Life]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The Marin factors are applied *sequentially as multipliers* to the uncorrected endurance limit. Each factor accounts for a different real-world degradation effect.

---

## 4.4 Process Flowchart: Decision Tree for Choosing Design Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Identify Load Type] --> STATIC{Is the load static?}
    STATIC -- "Yes" --> STATIC_DESIGN[Use Static Failure Theories]
    STATIC -- "No fluctuating" --> CALC_MEAN[Compute sigma_m and sigma_a]
    CALC_MEAN --> S_E_PRIME[Compute S_e prime equals 0.5 S_ut]
    S_E_PRIME --> MARIN[Apply Marin Factors to get S_e]
    MARIN --> CHECK_INF{Does Infinite Life Satisfy Constraints?}
    CHECK_INF -- "Yes weight, size acceptable" --> INFINITE[Infinite Life Design]
    CHECK_INF -- "No size, weight, cost too high" --> FINITE_REQ[Determine Required Cycles N]
    FINITE_REQ --> BASQUIN[Use Basquin to get S_N at N]
    BASQUIN --> CHECK_FIN{Is sigma_a less than S_N at N?}
    CHECK_FIN -- "Yes" --> FINITE_OK[Finite Life Design Valid]
    CHECK_FIN -- "No" --> INCREASE[Increase Size or Change Material]
    INCREASE --> S_E_PRIME
    INFINITE --> DONE[End Design]
    FINITE_OK --> DONE
    STATIC_DESIGN --> DONE
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the *exact decision tree* KTU expects students to follow in ESE numerical problems. Always default to infinite life; switch to finite life only when *constrained*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (3 Marks)
**Q: Distinguish clearly between Design for Infinite Life and Design for Finite Life. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**

**Design for Infinite Life:** This design philosophy ensures the machine element survives an *unlimited* number of stress cycles. It is based on keeping the operating alternating stress $\sigma_a$ *below* the **corrected endurance limit** $S_e$ of the material, i.e., $\sigma_a' \le S_e$ (where $\sigma_a'$ is the equivalent reversed stress). It is applied to components that must operate for the entire service life of the parent machine without inspection (e.g., crankshafts, turbine shafts).

**Design for Finite Life:** This design philosophy is used when the component is required to survive only a *predetermined, limited* number of cycles $N$ before scheduled replacement. The design is based on the S-N curve (Basquin's equation) at the specific required $N$, i.e., $\sigma_a \le S_N$ where $S_N$ is the finite-life fatigue strength at $N$ cycles. It is applied to aircraft structures, springs, and welded assemblies.

**Preference:** Infinite life is always preferred when *size, weight, and cost constraints permit*, because it eliminates the need for periodic inspection. Finite life is chosen only when infinite life is *impractical* due to weight/size (e.g., aircraft components, non-ferrous metals without a true endurance limit, or high-stress lightweight parts). **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (3 Marks)
**Q: List the Marin modification factors used to compute the corrected endurance limit and state the effect of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The corrected endurance limit is computed as $S_e = K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e K_f \cdot S_e'$, where $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ is the uncorrected endurance limit. The six Marin factors are:

1. **$K_a$ — Surface Finish Factor:** Reduces $S_e$ for rough surfaces (e.g., as-forged, machined) due to surface micro-notches that act as crack initiators. $K_a \le 1$.

2. **$K_b$ — Size Factor:** Reduces $S_e$ for larger cross-sections because a larger volume has a higher probability of containing a critical flaw. $K_b \le 1$, decreases with increasing diameter.

3. **$K_c$ — Load Factor:** Accounts for the type of loading — $K_c = 1$ for axial, $0.85$ for bending, $0.59$ for torsion (in steels). Torsion has lower endurance than bending.

4. **$K_d$ — Temperature Factor:** Reduces $S_e$ at elevated temperatures due to reduced yield strength. At room temperature, $K_d = 1$.

5. **$K_e$ — Reliability Factor:** Reduces $S_e$ for higher reliability requirements — $K_e = 1$ for 50% reliability, $K_e = 0.814$ for 90% reliability, etc.

6. **$K_f$ — Miscellaneous Effects Factor:** Accounts for residual stresses, corrosion, electrolytic plating (e.g., chromium plating can reduce $S_e$ significantly). $K_f = 1$ for normal conditions. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] (14 Marks)

**A shaft is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa and $S_{yt} = 400$ MPa. It is subjected to a fluctuating bending moment that varies from $+2500$ N·m to $-1000$ N·m. The shaft has a ground surface finish, diameter 50 mm, operates at room temperature, and the reliability required is 90%. Assume the load factor for bending is 0.85.**

#### (a) Compute the mean and alternating bending stresses, and the corrected endurance limit. State whether the design is safe for infinite life. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the bending stresses (assuming a diameter $d$ to be verified, but for stress calculation we use the section modulus):**

For a circular shaft, section modulus $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$. With $d = 50$ mm:

$$
Z = \frac{\pi \times 50^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 125000}{32} = 12271.85 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean and alternating moments:**

$$
M_{m} = \frac{M_{max} + M_{min}}{2} = \frac{2500 + (-1000)}{2} = 750 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
M_{a} = \frac{M_{max} - M_{min}}{2} = \frac{2500 - (-1000)}{2} = 1750 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert moments to stresses:**

$$
\sigma_{m} = \frac{M_{m}}{Z} = \frac{750 \times 10^{3}}{12271.85} = 61.12 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a} = \frac{M_{a}}{Z} = \frac{1750 \times 10^{3}}{12271.85} = 142.61 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$
S_{e}' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Marin factors:**

- $K_a$ (Ground surface, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa): $K_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{b}$. For ground, $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$. So $K_a = 1.58 \times (600)^{-0.085} = 1.58 \times 0.628 = 0.992$

- $K_b$ (Size factor, $d = 50$ mm): Using $K_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$:
$$
K_b = 0.879 \times (50)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.604 = 0.531
$$

- $K_c$ (Load factor, bending): $K_c = 0.85$ (given)

- $K_d$ (Temperature, room): $K_d = 1.0$

- $K_e$ (Reliability 90%): $K_e = 0.814$

- $K_f$ (Miscellaneous): $K_f = 1.0$

**Step 6 — Compute corrected endurance limit:**

$$
S_{e} = K_{a} K_{b} K_{c} K_{d} K_{e} K_{f} \cdot S_{e}'
$$

$$
S_{e} = 0.992 \times 0.531 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 1.0 \times 300
$$

$$
S_{e} = 0.365 \times 300 = 109.45 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the equivalent fully-reversed stress (Goodman):**

$$
\sigma_{a}' = \sigma_{a} \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} \right) = 142.61 \times \left( 1 - \frac{61.12}{600} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_{a}' = 142.61 \times (1 - 0.1019) = 142.61 \times 0.8981 = 128.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 8 — Compare and conclude:**

Since $\sigma_{a}' = 128.08$ MPa $> S_e = 109.45$ MPa, the design is **NOT safe for infinite life**. We must redesign (increase diameter) or switch to finite life design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) If the same shaft is required to operate for $5 \times 10^4$ cycles, determine the finite-life fatigue strength and compute the factor of safety. Use the Modified Goodman criterion. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Establish the two anchor points of the S-N line:**

- Point 1: $(N_1 = 10^3, \, S_{N_1} = 0.9 \, S_{ut}) = (10^3, 540 \text{ MPa})$
- Point 2: $(N_2 = 10^6, \, S_{N_2} = S_e' = 300 \text{ MPa})$

**Step 2 — Compute the slope $b$ of the S-N line (log-log form):**

$$
b = \frac{\log(S_{N_1}) - \log(S_{N_2})}{\log(N_1) - \log(N_2)} = \frac{\log(540/300)}{\log(10^3/10^6)} = \frac{\log(1.8)}{-3} = \frac{0.2553}{-3} = -0.0851
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intercept $\log(a)$:**

$$
\log(a) = \log(S_{N_1}) - b \cdot \log(N_1) = \log(540) - (-0.0851) \times 3 = 2.7324 + 0.2553 = 2.9877
$$

$$
a = 10^{2.9877} = 972.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute finite-life fatigue strength at $N = 5 \times 10^4$ cycles:**

$$
S_{N} = a \cdot N^{b} = 972.7 \times (5 \times 10^{4})^{-0.0851}
$$

$$
\log(S_{N}) = \log(972.7) + (-0.0851) \times \log(5 \times 10^{4}) = 2.9877 + (-0.0851)(4.6990)
$$

$$
\log(S_{N}) = 2.9877 - 0.3999 = 2.5878
$$

$$
S_{N} = 10^{2.5878} = 387.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Modified Goodman Criterion to find factor of safety $n$:**

$$
\frac{n \cdot \sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{n \cdot \sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}}
$$

But for finite life, we replace $S_e$ with the finite-life strength at $N$ cycles. Using $S_N$ in place of $S_e$ (this is the standard KTU finite-life approach):

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{N}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{142.61}{387.3} + \dfrac{61.12}{600}} = \frac{1}{0.3682 + 0.1019} = \frac{1}{0.4701}
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 2.13}
$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft has a factor of safety of approximately **2.13** for the required life of $5 \times 10^4$ cycles. Since this is well above the typical KTU minimum of $n = 1.5$ for general machinery, the finite-life design is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] (14 Marks)

**A steel rotating shaft has $S_{ut} = 550$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 380$ MPa. The bending stress varies from 80 MPa to $-40$ MPa, and a steady torsion stress of 35 MPa is also applied. Surface finish is machined, diameter 40 mm, 95% reliability required.**

#### (a) Compute the equivalent fully-reversed stresses using both Goodman and Soderberg criteria, and comment on infinite life safety. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean and alternating bending stresses:**

$$
\sigma_{m, \text{bending}} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{bending}} = \frac{80 - (-40)}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the steady torsion:**

Torsion is steady (not fluctuating) — i.e., $\tau_{m} = 35$ MPa, $\tau_{a} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Convert torsion to equivalent normal stress (using von Mises / distortion energy):**

$$
\sigma_{m, \text{eq from torsion}} = \sqrt{3} \times \tau_{m} = \sqrt{3} \times 35 = 60.62 \text{ MPa}
$$

(For steady torsion, only the mean component is non-zero.)

**Step 4 — Total mean and alternating stresses (using the von Mises combination):**

$$
\sigma_{m, \text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{m, \text{bending}}^{2} + 3 \, \tau_{m}^{2}} = \sqrt{20^{2} + 3 \times 35^{2}} = \sqrt{400 + 3675} = \sqrt{4075} = 63.84 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{total}} = \sigma_{a, \text{bending}} = 60 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{no alternating torsion})
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the uncorrected endurance limit:**

$$
S_{e}' = 0.5 \times 550 = 275 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Marin factors:**

- $K_a$ (Machined, $S_{ut} = 550$): $K_a = 4.51 \times (550)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1668 = 0.752$

- $K_b$ (Size, $d = 40$ mm): $K_b = 0.879 \times (40)^{-0.107} = 0.879 \times 0.6227 = 0.547$

- $K_c$ (Bending + torsion combined): Since torsion is present, use $K_c$ for torsion = 0.59 (conservative).

- $K_d$ (Room temperature): $K_d = 1.0$

- $K_e$ (95% reliability): $K_e = 0.868$

- $K_f$ (Miscellaneous): $K_f = 1.0$

**Step 7 — Corrected endurance limit:**

$$
S_{e} = 0.752 \times 0.547 \times 0.59 \times 1.0 \times 0.868 \times 1.0 \times 275
$$

$$
S_{e} = 0.1897 \times 275 = 52.18 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent fully-reversed stress using Goodman:**

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{eq}}^{\text{Goodman}} = \sigma_{a, \text{total}} \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_{m, \text{total}}}{S_{ut}} \right) = 60 \times \left( 1 - \frac{63.84}{550} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{eq}}^{\text{Goodman}} = 60 \times (1 - 0.1161) = 60 \times 0.8839 = 53.03 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $53.03$ MPa $> S_e = 52.18$ MPa, the design is **marginally unsafe for infinite life** by the Goodman criterion.

**Step 9 — Equivalent fully-reversed stress using Soderberg:**

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{eq}}^{\text{Soderberg}} = \sigma_{a, \text{total}} \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_{m, \text{total}}}{S_{yt}} \right) = 60 \times \left( 1 - \frac{63.84}{380} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{eq}}^{\text{Soderberg}} = 60 \times (1 - 0.168) = 60 \times 0.832 = 49.92 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $49.92$ MPa $< S_e = 52.18$ MPa, the design is **safe for infinite life** by the Soderberg criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Compute the factor of safety using both the Goodman and Soderberg criteria. Comment on which criterion is more conservative and why. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Factor of safety using Modified Goodman:**

$$
n_{\text{Goodman}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a, \text{total}}}{S_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m, \text{total}}}{S_{ut}}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{Goodman}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{60}{52.18} + \dfrac{63.84}{550}} = \frac{1}{1.1499 + 0.1161} = \frac{1}{1.266} = 0.79
$$

Since $n < 1$, the design fails the Goodman criterion.

**Step 2 — Factor of safety using Soderberg:**

$$
n_{\text{Soderberg}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a, \text{total}}}{S_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m, \text{total}}}{S_{yt}}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{Soderberg}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{60}{52.18} + \dfrac{63.84}{380}} = \frac{1}{1.1499 + 0.168} = \frac{1}{1.318} = 0.759
$$

Since $n < 1$, the design also fails the Soderberg criterion (and is therefore more conservative).

**Step 3 — Comparison and Conclusion:**

- $n_{\text{Goodman}} = 0.79$ (close to 1 — marginally unsafe)
- $n_{\text{Soderberg}} = 0.76$ (further from 1 — more conservative)

**Soderberg is more conservative** than Goodman because it uses the *yield strength* $S_{yt}$ (lower than $S_{ut}$) in the mean-stress denominator, ensuring that *no yielding occurs* during the cycle. Goodman permits some localized yielding and uses $S_{ut}$, giving a slightly higher (less conservative) factor of safety.

**Recommendation:** Since both $n < 1$, the shaft must be *redesigned* (e.g., increase diameter, use a higher-strength material, or improve surface finish). If infinite life cannot be achieved, switch to a finite-life design at the required service cycles. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Load Factor $K_c$:** Many students compute $S_e$ with $K_c = 1$ even for torsion. KTU mandates $K_c = 0.59$ for torsion in steel — losing this means losing 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Using $S_{ut}$ Instead of $S_e$:** When the question asks for "infinite life check," you MUST use the *corrected* $S_e$ (with all Marin factors), not the raw $S_{ut}$ or even the uncorrected $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$. This is a very common error.
> 3. **Mixing Units:** $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ in MPa, but the moment in N·m and section modulus in mm³ — always convert moments to N·mm before dividing. KTU examiners deduct heavily for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Confusing Goodman with Soderberg:** The default in KTU is **Goodman** unless the question explicitly says "no yielding permitted," in which case use **Soderberg**. Always state the criterion you are using.
> 5. **Skipping the Stress Decomposition:** Many students jump directly to $\sigma_{a}/S_{e}$ without computing $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$ first. This shows incomplete understanding and loses partial marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to Apply the Equivalent Stress for Combined Loading:** When bending + torsion are both present, you must combine them using von Mises *before* applying the Goodman/Soderberg criterion. Mixing the criteria with raw uncombined stresses is a fatal error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** Design for Infinite Life = operating stress below endurance limit ($S_e$); Design for Finite Life = operating stress above $S_e$ but with a specified $N$ cycles via S-N curve.
- [ ] **Stress Decomposition:** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$, $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ — always compute these first.
- [ ] **Uncorrected Endurance Limit:** $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ (for steels, $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa).
- [ ] **Marin Factors:** $S_e = K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e K_f \cdot S_e'$ — apply ALL six in order; missing one is a 1-mark penalty per KTU convention.
- [ ] **Size Factor Formula:** $K_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm; $K_b = 0.91 d^{-0.157}$ for $51 \le d \le 254$ mm.
- [ ] **Load Factor Values:** $K_c = 1$ (axial), $0.85$ (bending), $0.59$ (torsion) — remember 0.59 for torsion!
- [ ] **Reliability Factor:** $K_e = 0.814$ for 90%, $0.868$ for 95%, $0.753$ for 99% — memorize at least 0.814.
- [ ] **Modified Goodman (KTU Default):** $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ — straight line, conservative.
- [ ] **Soderberg (Most Conservative):** $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt} = 1/n$ — use when "no yielding" is the constraint.
- [ ] **Gerber (Least Conservative):** $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + n\sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1$ — parabolic; use for ductile materials.
- [ ] **Equivalent Reversed Stress (Goodman):** $\sigma_a' = \sigma_a (1 - \sigma_m/S_{ut})$ — useful for direct comparison with $S_e$.
- [ ] **Basquin's Equation (Finite Life):** $S_N = a N^b$ where $b = -0.0851$ for standard steel S-N line.
- [ ] **Miner's Rule (Cumulative Damage):** $\sum (n_i / N_i) = 1$ at failure — apply for variable-amplitude loading.
- [ ] **Combined Loading:** Use von Mises equivalent $\sigma_m = \sqrt{\sigma_{m,b}^2 + 3\tau_m^2}$ when torsion is steady.
- [ ] **Always Default to Infinite Life:** Only switch to finite life when weight/size/cost is a binding constraint.
- [ ] **KTU Marks Tactic:** Always state your criterion (Goodman/Soderberg/Gerber) explicitly — silent assumption = 0.5 mark deduction.
- [ ] **Memorize the Marin Factor Table** — examiners expect you to know it without derivation for $K_a, K_b, K_e$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soderberg and Goodman Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Soderberg and Goodman Criteria # MODULE 1 — STATIC AND FATIGUE LOADING IN DESIGN ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Soderberg Criterion The **Soderberg criterion** is a *conservative* linear failure theory for machine components subjected to a combination of **steady (mean) stress** and **alternating (variable) stress**. It is the most cautious of the classical mean-stress failure lines because it uses the **yield strength (Syt)** as the limiting static property rather than the ultimate tensile strength (Sut). Mathematically, for a 2-D stress state acting on a critical plane, the Soderberg line is expressed as: $$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n}$$ where **S_e** is the *endurance limit (fatigue strength at N = 10^6 cycles)*, **S_yt** is the *yield tensile strength*, **σ_a** is the alternating stress amplitude, **σ_m** is the mean (static) stress, and **n** is the **factor of safety**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Soderberg vs. Yield Strength** > Soderberg is unique among the classical mean-stress theories because it ties the static-axis intercept to the **yield point**, not the ultimate strength. This makes it the *governing criterion* in ductile components where permanent (plastic) deformation — not fracture — is the design limit. ### 1.2 Formal Definition — Goodman Criterion The **Goodman criterion** is a *linear* mean-stress failure theory that connects the fatigue limit (**S_e**) on the variable-stress axis to the ultimate tensile strength (**S_ut**) on the mean-stress axis. It is the *most widely used* relationship in classical machine design textbooks. $$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Favourite** > Whenever a problem states "fluctuating/cyclic load with both mean and alternating components," examiners almost always expect either a Soderberg, Goodman, or Gerber line, **or** a comparative statement between them. Memorise the four classical lines (Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, ASME) and the algebraic derivation of the factor of safety — questions of 7 marks and 14 marks are routinely framed on this. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Rope Bridge" Picture Imagine a rope bridge where two workers pull on the cable at the **same time**: - Worker A pulls *back and forth rhythmically* (this is the **alternating stress, σ_a** — repeated loading). - Worker B pulls *continuously in one direction* (this is the **mean stress, σ_m** — steady preload). A fatigue failure occurs when the **sum** of these two "pulls" at any instant exceeds what the rope can endure. Soderberg, Goodman, and Gerber are simply three different **diagonal lines** drawn on a chart that describe the *boundary* between *safe* and *failed* regions. - The **horizontal axis** is the steady pull (mean stress). - The **vertical axis** is the rhythmic pull amplitude (alternating stress). - A line that is **flatter** (closer to the horizontal) is *less conservative*; a line that is **steeper** (closer to the vertical) is *more conservative*. - Soderberg's line is the **steepest** of the classical family (because yield is reached long before ultimate), while Goodman's line is **steeper than Gerber** but **flatter than Soderberg**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU** > "**S**oderberg = **S**afer (steepest line, yield-bound)" and "**G**oodman = **G**ood compromise (straight line between S_e and S_ut)". --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Scheme) The following symbols form the **standard KTU notation** for fatigue design and must be used identically in board answers: | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (Steel) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | S_ut | Ultimate Tensile Strength | 400 – 1500 MPa | | S_yt | Yield Tensile Strength | 250 – 1200 MPa | | S_e | Endurance Limit (corrected) | 0.4 S_ut to 0.6 S_ut | | σ_m | Mean Stress | computed from loads | | σ_a | Alternating Stress Amplitude | computed from loads | | σ_max | Maximum Stress | σ_m + σ_a | | σ_min | Minimum Stress | σ_m − σ_a | | n | Factor of Safety | 1.25 to 4 (typical) | | N | Number of Cycles | 10^3 to 10^6 | --- # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Stress Coordinate System — Mean and Amplitude For any *fluctuating* load, the stress at any instant can be written as: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \cos(\omega t)$$ The two scalar descriptors that completely define the load history (assuming a sinusoidal waveform) are: $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} \qquad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ The **stress ratio R = σ_min / σ_max** fully classifies the loading: - **R = +1** → Purely static (no alternation, σ_a = 0) - **R = 0** → Pulsating (zero to maximum, e.g., a punching press) - **R = −1** → Fully reversed (sinusoidal, σ_m = 0) - **R → −∞** → Purely compressive reversed (rarely used for design) ## 2.2 The Four Classical Mean-Stress Failure Lines The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) requires mastery of all four. Each "line" is a **boundary** drawn on the (σ_m, σ_a) plane; points *inside* the enclosed region are safe, points *outside* are predicted to fail. ### (A) Soderberg Line — Most Conservative $$\boxed{\;\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n}\;}$$ - **Vertical intercept** (σ_m = 0): σ_a = S_e / n → fully reversed fatigue - **Horizontal intercept** (σ_a = 0): σ_m = S_yt / n → pure yielding - **Slope** = − S_e / S_yt (steep, conservative) ### (B) Goodman Line — Most Commonly Used $$\boxed{\;\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}\;}$$ - **Vertical intercept**: σ_a = S_e / n - **Horizontal intercept**: σ_m = S_ut / n → ultimate strength - **Slope** = − S_e / S_ut (gentler than Soderberg) ### (C) Gerber Line — Parabolic (Most Accurate, Non-Linear) $$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{n \, \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \frac{n \, \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1\;}$$ - A *parabola* that bulges outward, allowing higher mean stresses for the same alternating component. - Best fit to experimental data but algebraically complex. ### (D) ASME-Elliptic (Modified Goodman) — Required for Combined Loading For **combined normal and shear stresses** with mean and alternating components, the ASME code uses an *elliptic* interaction: $$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{n \, \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n \, \tau_a}{S_{se}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n \, \sigma_m}{S_{yt}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n \, \tau_m}{S_{sy}}\right)^2 = 1\;}$$ This is the standard KTU board format when a problem states "combined bending and torsion, with steady and fluctuating components." ## 2.3 Why Four Different Lines? — The Engineering Trade-off > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy of Conservatism (most → least conservative):** > Soderberg > ASME-Elliptic > Goodman > Gerber > **(for the same operating stress point)** The choice depends on: 1. **Material ductility** — Ductile materials allow Gerber's higher predicted capacity. 2. **Consequence of failure** — Aerospace uses Soderberg; consumer machinery often uses Goodman. 3. **Code requirement** — ASME Pressure Vessel Code mandates the elliptic form; AGMA uses Goodman for gear design. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every formula a KTU 2024 examiner can ask in Module 1**. Master the algebra of each row. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Used For | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Mean stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa | All criteria | | 2 | Alternating stress | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa | All criteria | | 3 | Stress ratio | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | dimensionless | Load classification | | 4 | Endurance limit (uncorrected) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa) | MPa | Iron/Steel baseline | | 5 | Marin correction factor | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$ | MPa | Final endurance limit | | 6 | Soderberg FOS | $1/n = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt}$ | — | Conservative design | | 7 | Goodman FOS | $1/n = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut}$ | — | General design | | 8 | Gerber FOS | $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + n\sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1$ | — | Ductile materials | | 9 | ASME-Elliptic (Combined) | $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (n\tau_a/S_{se})^2 + (n\sigma_m/S_{yt})^2 + (n\tau_m/S_{sy})^2 = 1$ | — | Bending + Torsion | | 10 | Deformation FOS | $n_d = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max}$ | — | Static yield check | | 11 | Ultimate FOS | $n_u = S_{ut}/\sigma_{max}$ | — | Static fracture check | | 12 | Combined FOS (n × n_d)/(n² − n_d²)^(1/2) | $n = n_d n_u / \sqrt{n_u^2 - n_d^2}$ | — | Static + Fluctuating | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Automotive crankshafts** — Goodman with S_ut intercept is industry standard. - **Pressure vessels** — ASME elliptic with S_yt on the mean axis. - **Aircraft landing gear** — Soderberg preferred for life-critical ductile parts. - **Helical springs** — Goodman with a *curvature correction* on the mean axis. - **Roller bearings & Gears (AGMA)** — Goodman on Hertzian contact stress. --- # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Soderberg Equation (Line of Best Safety) ### Step 1 — Identify the Two Critical Limiting States A linear failure locus on the (σ_m, σ_a) plane is fully defined by **two boundary points**: 1. **Purely Reversed Loading** (σ_m = 0, σ_a = S_e) → the material survives only if the alternating stress is less than the endurance limit. 2. **Purely Static Loading** (σ_a = 0, σ_m = S_yt) → the material yields only if the mean stress reaches yield. ### Step 2 — Write the Two-Point Form of a Straight Line Let the line be $\sigma_a = A + B \sigma_m$. Substituting the two boundary points: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Point 1: } & \sigma_a = S_e, \quad \sigma_m = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = S_e \\ \text{Point 2: } & \sigma_a = 0, \quad \sigma_m = S_{yt} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 = S_e + B \, S_{yt} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve for the Slope B $$ B = -\frac{S_e}{S_{yt}} $$ ### Step 4 — Write the Governing Equation $$ \sigma_a = S_e - \frac{S_e}{S_{yt}} \, \sigma_m $$ ### Step 5 — Introduce the Factor of Safety (n) When a factor of safety **n** is required, the line moves *inward* toward the origin by a factor of **1/n** on *both* axes (proportional scaling). This gives: $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n} $$ This is the **canonical Soderberg equation** as it appears in KTU answers. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Goodman Equation The algebra is **identical** to Soderberg's — only the horizontal-axis intercept changes from S_yt to S_ut: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Boundary 1: } & (\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (0,\; S_e) \\ \text{Boundary 2: } & (\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (S_{ut},\; 0) \\ \text{Two-point form: } & \frac{\sigma_a - S_e}{0 - S_e} = \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{S_{ut} - 0} \\ \text{Simplify: } & \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ Introducing the factor of safety: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}\;} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Gerber Parabolic Equation Goodman *under-predicts* life for ductile alloys because the true failure boundary is curved. Gerber fitted a parabola through the two end points: $$ \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 $$ **Derivation in full:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Parabola form: } & \sigma_a^2 = a \, \sigma_m + b \\ \text{Boundary 1: } & \sigma_m = 0,\; \sigma_a = S_e \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; S_e^2 = b \\ \text{Boundary 2: } & \sigma_m = S_{ut},\; \sigma_a = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 = a \, S_{ut} + S_e^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = -S_e^2/S_{ut} \\ \text{Substitute back: } & \sigma_a^2 = -\frac{S_e^2}{S_{ut}} \, \sigma_m + S_e^2 \\ \text{Divide both sides by } S_e^2 \text{ and rearrange: } & \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ With factor of safety *n* (proportional scaling on both axes): $$ \boxed{\;\left(\frac{n \, \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \frac{n \, \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **How to solve the Gerber equation for n explicitly** > This is a *non-linear* equation in *n*. Multiply out: > $n^2 \sigma_a^2 / S_e^2 + n \sigma_m / S_{ut} - 1 = 0$ > which is a quadratic in *n*: > $A n^2 + B n - 1 = 0$, where $A = \sigma_a^2/S_e^2$ and $B = \sigma_m/S_{ut}$. > Apply the quadratic formula and keep the *positive root*: > $n = \dfrac{-B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4A}}{2A}$. ## 3.4 Derivation of the Combined Static-Fatigue Factor of Safety (Shigley/KTU Standard) When a component has a *static* overload *in addition to* a *fluctuating* cyclic load, the total factor of safety combines the static and fatigue parts as: $$ n = \frac{n_d \, n_u}{\sqrt{n_u^2 - n_d^2}} $$ **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Static FOS against yield: } & n_d = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max} \\ \text{Static FOS against ultimate: } & n_u = S_{ut}/\sigma_{max} \\ \text{Mean stress in terms of } n_d: & \sigma_m = \sigma_{max}(1+n_d - n_d)/n_d \quad \text{(see Shigley Ch. 7)} \\ \text{Combine with Goodman line: } & \frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \\ \text{After algebraic substitution of } \sigma_a, \sigma_m \text{ in terms of } n_d, n_u: & \\ \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{1}{n_d^2} - \frac{1}{n_u^2} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad n = \frac{n_d n_u}{\sqrt{n_u^2 - n_d^2}} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical answer for KTU 14-mark problems** that give σ_max, σ_min, S_yt, and S_ut together. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Anchor — Goodman Criterion **Given** (a KTU-style problem): A steel shaft has S_ut = 600 MPa, S_yt = 420 MPa, S_e = 280 MPa. The shaft experiences σ_max = 200 MPa, σ_min = −100 MPa. Find the factor of safety using (a) Soderberg, (b) Goodman, (c) Gerber. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_m &= \frac{200 + (-100)}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa} \\ \sigma_a &= \frac{200 - (-100)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **(a) Soderberg:** $$ \frac{1}{n} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{50}{420} = 0.5357 + 0.1190 = 0.6548 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n = 1.527 $$ **(b) Goodman:** $$ \frac{1}{n} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{50}{600} = 0.5357 + 0.0833 = 0.6190 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n = 1.615 $$ **(c) Gerber (quadratic in n):** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= (\sigma_a/S_e)^2 = (150/280)^2 = 0.2870 \\ B &= \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 50/600 = 0.0833 \\ n &= \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4A}}{2A} = \frac{-0.0833 + \sqrt{0.00694 + 1.1480}}{2 \times 0.2870} \\ &= \frac{-0.0833 + 1.0760}{0.5740} = \frac{0.9927}{0.5740} = 1.730 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Expected Ordering:** $n_{Soderberg}(1.527) < n_{Goodman}(1.615) < n_{Gerber}(1.730)$ — confirms the conservatism hierarchy. Always quote all three in a KTU answer to score full 14 marks. --- # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 The Mean-Stress Failure Diagram (Haigh Diagram) The **Haigh diagram** is the central visual aid for every Soderberg/Goodman problem. The following Mermaid block renders the four classical lines on the (σ_m, σ_a) plane with labelled intercepts and safe/failed regions. ```mermaid graph LR A["Sigma m Axis
Mean Stress"]:::axis B["Sigma a Axis
Alternating Stress"]:::axis C["Soderberg Line
Steep Conservative
Intercept Syt on Mean Axis"]:::sod D["Goodman Line
Moderate Linear
Intercept Sut on Mean Axis"]:::good E["Gerber Parabola
Curved Most Accurate
Bulges Outward"]:::gerb F["Safe Region
Below All Lines"]:::safe G["Failure Region
Above Lines"]:::fail H["Operating Point P
Sigma m Sigma a"]:::pt A --- C A --- D A --- E B --- C B --- D B --- E C --- F D --- F E --- F C --- G D --- G E --- G H --- F classDef axis fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px classDef sod fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:2px classDef good fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px classDef gerb fill:#d5e3d0,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px classDef safe fill:#ddebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:1px classDef fail fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#c65911,stroke-width:1px classDef pt fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Factor-of-Safety Calculation Flow The following block-level flow describes the **complete algorithm** a student must follow when solving a KTU 14-mark Soderberg/Goodman problem. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Input Sigma max and Sigma min"]:::inp A1["Compute Sigma m and Sigma a"]:::calc A2["Select Design Criterion
Soderberg Goodman Gerber ASME"]:::sel B1["Soderberg 1 over n equals
Sigma a over Se plus Sigma m over Syt"]:::sod B2["Goodman 1 over n equals
Sigma a over Se plus Sigma m over Sut"]:::good B3["Gerber Quadratic in n
A n squared plus B n minus 1 equals 0"]:::gerb B4["ASME Elliptic Equation
Combined Bending and Torsion"]:::asme C1["Solve for n directly"]:::solve C2["Solve Quadratic
Keep Positive Root"]:::solveq C3["Iterative or Substitution"]:::asmeslv D1["Compare n with Required FOS"]:::cmp D2["Safe if n greater than n required"]:::ok D3["Redesign Section or Material"]:::redo A0 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A2 --> B3 A2 --> B4 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B4 --> C3 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D1 --> D3 classDef inp fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79 classDef calc fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d classDef sel fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00 classDef sod fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000 classDef good fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000 classDef gerb fill:#d5e3d0,stroke:#38761d classDef asme fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 classDef solve fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79 classDef solveq fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79 classDef asmeslv fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79 classDef cmp fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47 classDef ok fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d classDef redo fill:#ea9999,stroke:#cc0000 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Criterion to Choose? ```mermaid graph TD Q1{"Is the material ductile
Sut over Syt less than 1.5"}:::q Q2{"Is failure by yielding
a design concern"}:::q Q3{"Is load combined
bending plus torsion"}:::q R1["Use Gerber Parabola"]:::res R2["Use Soderberg Line"]:::res R3["Use Goodman Line
Industry Standard"]:::res R4["Use ASME Elliptic"]:::res Q1 -->|Yes| R1 Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| R2 Q2 -->|No| Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| R4 Q3 -->|No| R3 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — Define Soderberg criterion. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Soderberg criterion is a *conservative linear failure theory* used in machine design for components subjected to combined steady (mean) and alternating stresses. It relates the alternating stress (σ_a) and mean stress (σ_m) to the endurance limit (S_e) and yield strength (S_yt) respectively. The governing equation is: $$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n}$$ where *n* is the factor of safety. Soderberg is the most conservative of the four classical mean-stress criteria because it ties the static-axis intercept to the **yield strength** (a more cautious property) rather than the ultimate strength. *(Mark split: Definition 1M, Equation 1M, Conservatism note 1M.)* --- ### Question 2 — Distinguish between Goodman and Soderberg criteria. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Soderberg | Goodman | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mean-axis intercept | S_yt (yield) | S_ut (ultimate) | | Conservatism | Most conservative | Moderate | | Slope | Steep (S_e / S_yt) | Gentler (S_e / S_ut) | | Used when | Life-critical ductile parts | General machine design | | Governing property | Yield (plastic deformation) | Ultimate (fracture) | Both are *linear* in (σ_m, σ_a); both share the *same* vertical intercept at σ_a = S_e (fully reversed endurance). *(Mark split: 1M each for two-line answer, 1M for comparison table or one-line summary.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Full-Length 14-Mark Problem (Soderberg + Goodman + Gerber) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** A shaft of alloy steel has the following properties: S_ut = 650 MPa, S_yt = 480 MPa, S_e = 320 MPa. The shaft is subjected to a fluctuating load such that the maximum bending stress is 250 MPa and the minimum bending stress is −50 MPa. **(a)** Determine the factor of safety using the Soderberg criterion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the factor of safety using the Goodman criterion and compare with the Soderberg result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Step 1 — Compute mean and alternating stresses (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_m &= \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 + (-50)}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa} \\ \sigma_a &= \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 - (-50)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Stress ratio and load classification (1 mark):** $$ R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{-50}{250} = -0.2 $$ Since −1 < R < 0, the load is *alternating with a tensile bias* (partially reversed). **Step 3 — (a) Apply Soderberg's equation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{n} &= \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{150}{320} + \frac{100}{480} \\ &= 0.46875 + 0.20833 = 0.67708 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;n_{Soderberg} = \frac{1}{0.67708} = 1.477\;} $$ **Step 4 — (b) Apply Goodman's equation (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{n} &= \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{150}{320} + \frac{100}{650} \\ &= 0.46875 + 0.15385 = 0.62260 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;n_{Goodman} = \frac{1}{0.62260} = 1.606\;} $$ **Step 5 — Comparison (1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for table):** The Soderberg criterion gives a *lower* (more conservative) factor of safety because it is anchored to the yield strength S_yt = 480 MPa, which is smaller than the ultimate strength S_ut = 650 MPa used in Goodman. The design engineer should use the Soderberg result (n ≈ 1.48) if permanent deformation is unacceptable. | Criterion | FOS | Comment | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Soderberg | 1.477 | Most conservative (yield-bound) | | Goodman | 1.606 | Less conservative (fracture-bound) | --- ### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Problem (Goodman + Gerber + Combined FOS) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** A circular bar is made of steel with the following properties: S_ut = 720 MPa, S_yt = 540 MPa, S_e = 360 MPa. The bar is subjected to a fluctuating axial load that produces σ_max = 300 MPa and σ_min = 60 MPa. **(a)** Calculate the factor of safety using the Goodman line. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the factor of safety using the Gerber parabola and comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Step 1 — Compute σ_m and σ_a (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_m &= \frac{300 + 60}{2} = 180 \text{ MPa} \\ \sigma_a &= \frac{300 - 60}{2} = 120 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — (a) Apply Goodman's equation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{n} &= \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{120}{360} + \frac{180}{720} \\ &= 0.3333 + 0.2500 = 0.5833 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;n_{Goodman} = \frac{1}{0.5833} = 1.714\;} $$ **Step 3 — (b) Set up the Gerber quadratic (2 marks):** The Gerber equation is $A n^2 + B n - 1 = 0$ where $$ A = \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{120}{360}\right)^2 = 0.1111 \\ B = \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{180}{720} = 0.2500 $$ **Step 4 — Solve the quadratic (1 mark) and select positive root (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4A}}{2A} = \frac{-0.2500 + \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.4444}}{0.2222} \\ &= \frac{-0.2500 + \sqrt{0.5069}}{0.2222} = \frac{-0.2500 + 0.7120}{0.2222} = \frac{0.4620}{0.2222} = 2.080 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;n_{Gerber} = 2.080\;} $$ **Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):** Since $n_{Gerber} > n_{Goodman}$, the Goodman criterion is the more conservative of the two, as expected. The Gerber parabola bulges outward, allowing a higher predicted FOS for ductile materials. | Criterion | FOS | Conservatism | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Goodman | 1.714 | Higher (conservative) | | Gerber | 2.080 | Lower (more realistic for ductile) | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Module 1 — Soderberg/Goodman)** > 1. **Forgetting to compute σ_m and σ_a** from σ_max and σ_min. Direct substitution of σ_max alone is a *fatal* error that loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Mixing S_ut and S_yt in the denominator** of the Soderberg/Goodman equation. Soderberg uses **S_yt** on the mean-axis; Goodman uses **S_ut**. Confusing these two costs 1 full mark. > 3. **Failing to solve Gerber's quadratic** because the equation is non-linear. Use the explicit quadratic formula given in §3.3 — do not attempt linearisation. > 4. **Not labelling units** for stress (MPa) and clearly stating the value of *n* in a boxed answer. > 5. **Omitting the comparison** when the question asks "compare Soderberg and Goodman" — always end with a one-line ranking and a one-line *reason*. > 6. **Ignoring the stress ratio R** — examiners often award 1 mark for correctly identifying whether the load is fully reversed (R = −1), pulsating (R = 0), or static (R = +1). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Mean stress**: $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ - **Alternating stress**: $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ - **Soderberg line**: $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt} = 1/n$ → *most conservative* (yield-bound) - **Goodman line**: $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ → *general-purpose linear* (fracture-bound) - **Gerber parabola**: $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + n\sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1$ → *quadratic, most accurate for ductile* - **ASME elliptic (combined)**: $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (n\tau_a/S_{se})^2 + (n\sigma_m/S_{yt})^2 + (n\tau_m/S_{sy})^2 = 1$ - **Conservatism ranking**: Soderberg > ASME-Elliptic > Goodman > Gerber - **Stress ratio definitions**: R = +1 (static), 0 (pulsating), −1 (fully reversed) - **Endurance limit (uncorrected)**: $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa (steel) - **Marin's correction**: $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$ - **Gerber solution**: Quadratic $A n^2 + B n - 1 = 0$, take positive root. - **Combined static + fatigue FOS**: $n = n_d n_u / \sqrt{n_u^2 - n_d^2}$ - **Always quote the FOS** in a box, with units of MPa for stresses. - **Always show intermediate algebra** — never write a one-line jump from given data to final *n*. ---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Detachable Joints

A **detachable joint** (also called a *non-permanent joint*) is a type of mechanical connection between two or more machine components that can be readily disconnected without destroying the joining element or the parent members. Screws, bolts, nuts, studs, pins, keys, and cotter joints fall under this category.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A *detachable joint* allows repeated assembly and disassembly without damage. The screw is the most versatile detachable fastener because it combines the functions of **clamping force generation**, **accurate alignment**, and **load transfer** in a single compact element.

## 1.2 Classification of Screw Fasteners

Screws used in machine design fall into **two broad families** (must be memorized — frequently asked):

### Family 1: Threaded Fasteners (Load-Carrying Joints)
Used primarily to clamp two members together and resist external tensile/shear loads.
- **Bolt** – head + shank with external thread; used with a separate nut.
- **Stud** – headless, threaded on both ends (one end permanent in base material).
- **Cap Screw (Machine Screw)** – threaded into one of the joined members; no nut required.
- **Set Screw** – secures a hub on a shaft against rotation/sliding.

### Family 2: Power Screws (Motion-Conversion Screws)
Used to convert **rotary motion → linear motion** and amplify force.
- **Square Thread** – highest efficiency, used in screw jacks, presses.
- **Acme / Trapezoidal Thread** – easier to cut, used in lathes (lead screws).
- **Buttress Thread** – carries heavy axial load in one direction only (gun breech, jacks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Favorite):** *Threaded fasteners* are governed by **bolt-strength equations (UNIFIED/ISO 898)**. *Power screws* are governed by the **screw-jack efficiency equations (friction-wedge analysis)**. Examiners often ask students to *distinguish* between these two.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pressing a wet shirt against a stone to squeeze out water using your **two hands** (you can only generate a limited squeezing force). Now replace your hands with a **C-clamp** — by rotating the screw handle, you generate a **much larger clamping force** from a small hand effort. The C-clamp's screw is acting as a **simple machine** — exactly like the power screw in a screw jack.

Similarly, when a **mechanic tightens a bolt** on a car wheel, the small torque applied on the wrench creates a huge **pre-tension (clamping force)** inside the bolt, holding the wheel firmly. This pre-tension is what prevents the wheel from loosening under vibration.

## 1.4 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values must be memorized for KTU board exams:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Modulus of Elasticity (Steel) | $E$ | **2.07 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (≈ 207 GPa) |
| Modulus of Elasticity (Cast Iron) | $E$ | **1.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (≈ 100 GPa) |
| Poisson's Ratio (Steel) | $\nu$ | **0.30** |
| Shear Modulus (Steel) | $G$ | **8.0 × 10⁴ N/mm²** (≈ 80 GPa) |
| Proof Stress (Commercial Steel Bolt) | $\sigma_{proof}$ | **0.7 × σ_uts** |
| Proof Stress (High-Tensile Bolt) | $\sigma_{proof}$ | **0.85 × σ_uts** |
| Factor of Safety (Static Load) | FOS | **3 to 5** |
| Factor of Safety (Dynamic Load) | FOS | **6 to 10** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **nominal stress area** $A_s$ (or $A_t$) of a bolt is **NOT** the cross-sectional area of the shank. It is the area calculated from the **mean diameter minus a correction factor**, because the load is shared unevenly between the major and minor thread diameters. Standard ISO/IS tables provide $A_s$ directly for each nominal diameter.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stresses Induced in a Bolt Under Preload

When a bolt is tightened, it experiences a **combined state of stress** that must be analyzed carefully:

### (a) Tensile Stress (σ_t)
Due to the pre-tension force $P_0$ acting along the bolt axis:
$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_0}{A_s}$$

### (b) Torsional Shear Stress (τ)
Due to the tightening torque $T$ acting on the shank circumference:
$$\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d_c^{\,3}}$$
where $d_c$ is the **core (minor) diameter** of the bolt thread.

### (c) Combined Principal Stress (σ_max) — *Maximum Shear Stress Theory*
The bolt experiences **tensile + torsional** simultaneously. By **Maxwell's Principal Stress theory**:
$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_t}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$
$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\sigma_t + \sqrt{\sigma_t^{\,2} + 4\tau^2}\right]$$

### (d) Equivalent Torsional Moment (T_e) — *Energy Distortion (von Mises) Approach*
Since steel bolts are ductile, **Distortion Energy Theory** is preferred:
$$T_e = T + \dfrac{P_0 \cdot d_m}{2} \cdot \tan\!\left(\alpha + \phi'\right)$$
This combines the tightening torque and the thread helix effect into a single equivalent torsional moment.

## 2.2 Preload and Load Redistribution Under External Tension

When an external tensile load $P$ is applied to a joint, the **bolt tension increases** and the **member compression relaxes**. This is called the **load-sharing principle**.

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Bolt stiffness | $k_b$ | $k_b = \dfrac{A_s \cdot E_b}{L_b}$ |
| Member stiffness | $k_m$ | $k_m = \dfrac{A_m \cdot E_m}{L_m}$ |
| Joint stiffness constant | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{k_b}{k_b + k_m}$ |
| Bolt load fraction | $P_b$ | $P_b = C \cdot P$ |
| Member load fraction | $P_m$ | $P_m = (1 - C) \cdot P$ |

**Total bolt load:**
$$P_{max} = P_0 + C \cdot P$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** If a bolt is over-tightened ($P_0$ too high), even a small external load $P$ can push the bolt beyond its proof load and cause **fatigue failure**. Conversely, under-tightening allows the joint to **separate**, causing impact loading.

## 2.3 Bolt of Uniform Strength

To prevent stress concentration at the **thread root** (which is the weakest section), the shank is turned down to a diameter equal to the **core diameter** $d_c$ of the thread. This makes the **threaded portion as strong as the shank**, eliminating the weak link.

**Condition for uniform strength:**
$$d_c = d \quad \text{(shank diameter = core diameter)}$$

The shank is stress-free in the threaded region, so only the threaded portion transmits the load at uniform stress $\sigma_t = P/A_s$.

## 2.4 Eccentric Loading (Direct vs. Bending Shear)

When a bolt group carries a load whose line of action **does not pass through the centroid** of the bolt group, each bolt experiences:
- **Direct shear** $F_d = P/n$ (where $n$ = number of bolts)
- **Torsional shear** $F_t = \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{max}}{\sum r_i^{\,2}}$ (where $e$ = eccentricity, $r_i$ = radial distance of $i$-th bolt)

**Resultant shear on the critical bolt:**
$$F_{max} = \sqrt{F_d^{\,2} + F_t^{\,2} + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta}$$

For most KTU problems, when the load is in the **same plane** as the bolt group and the critical bolt is the **farthest**:
$$F_{max} = F_d + F_t \quad \text{(most conservative)}$$

## 2.5 Power Screw — Screw Jack Analysis

### Kinematic Geometry
- **Lead** ($L$) — axial advance per revolution (for single-start thread = pitch).
- **Mean diameter** ($d_m$) — pitch diameter used for friction calculation.
- **Helix angle** ($\alpha$): $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}$
- **Friction angle** ($\phi$): $\tan\phi = \mu$, where $\mu$ = coefficient of thread friction.

### Torque to Raise Load $W$
$$T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

### Torque to Lower Load $W$
$$T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi - \alpha) \quad \text{(positive = self-locking)} $$

### Efficiency of Screw Jack
$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

### Self-Locking Condition
The screw jack is **self-locking** if:
$$\alpha < \phi \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \tan\alpha < \mu$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD. Memorize the equation, the symbol meaning, AND the conditions of validity.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nominal tensile stress | $\sigma_t = P/A_s$ | $A_s$ from IS/ISO tables |
| 2 | Torsional shear stress | $\tau = 16T/(\pi d_c^3)$ | $d_c$ = core diameter |
| 3 | Principal stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\sigma_t + \sqrt{\sigma_t^2 + 4\tau^2}\right]$ | Brittle / conservative |
| 4 | Combined stress (Max Shear) | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{(\sigma_t/2)^2 + \tau^2}$ | Ductile (preferred for steel) |
| 5 | Proof load | $P_{proof} = \sigma_{proof} \cdot A_s$ | Sets max preload |
| 6 | Preload recommendation | $P_0 = 0.75 \cdot P_{proof}$ (permanent); $P_0 = 0.90 \cdot P_{proof}$ (non-permanent) | Industry standard |
| 7 | Bolt stiffness | $k_b = A_s E_b / L_b$ | $L_b$ = effective bolt length |
| 8 | Joint stiffness ratio | $C = k_b/(k_b + k_m)$ | Typical: $C \approx 0.15$ to $0.30$ |
| 9 | Max bolt tension | $P_{max} = P_0 + C P$ | Critical for fatigue check |
| 10 | Eccentric shear resultant | $F_{max} = F_d + F_t$ | Worst-case bolt |
| 11 | Helix angle | $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$ | Single-start thread |
| 12 | Torque to raise | $T_R = (W d_m/2)\tan(\alpha+\phi)$ | Sign convention: positive |
| 13 | Torque to lower | $T_L = (W d_m/2)\tan(\phi-\alpha)$ | Negative if back-driving |
| 14 | Screw jack efficiency | $\eta = \tan\alpha/\tan(\alpha+\phi)$ | $0 < \eta < 0.5$ typical |
| 15 | Self-locking condition | $\tan\alpha \le \mu$ | Mandatory for screw jack |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Stress Formula in a Preloaded Bolt

### Step 1 — Establish the Loading State
Consider a bolt of nominal diameter $d$, core diameter $d_c$, subjected to:
- Axial preload tension $P_0$ (Newton)
- Tightening torque $T$ about the bolt axis (N·mm)

The bolt is in a state of **axial tension + pure torsion**.

### Step 2 — Compute the Tensile Stress
The tensile force $P_0$ is shared over the **nominal stress area** $A_s$ (NOT the gross area $A$):

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_0}{A_s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Pure Torsional Shear Stress
The torque $T$ acts on a circular cross-section of diameter equal to the **core diameter** $d_c$ of the threaded portion, because that is where the equivalent cylindrical stress field is concentrated.

$$\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r_c}{J} = \dfrac{T \cdot (d_c/2)}{\dfrac{\pi d_c^4}{32}} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d_c^{\,3}}$$

### Step 4 — Combine Using Principal Stress Theory
For a 2-D stress element with $\sigma_x = \sigma_t$, $\sigma_y = 0$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_t}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

Therefore the **maximum principal (tensile) stress** is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\sigma_t + \sqrt{\sigma_t^{\,2} + 4\tau^2}\right]$$

And the **maximum shear stress** (for ductile failure check):

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_t}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_t^{\,2} + 4\tau^2}$$

### Step 5 — Verification with von Mises Equivalent Stress
Using the **Distortion Energy Theory**, the equivalent stress combines axial and shear into a single scalar:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^{\,2} + 3\tau^2}$$

For design, we compare $\sigma_{eq}$ with the **proof stress** $\sigma_{proof} = 0.7\,\sigma_{uts}$ divided by an appropriate **factor of safety** $N$.

$$\sigma_{eq} \le \dfrac{\sigma_{proof}}{N}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Simplification (Design Charts)
For ISO metric bolts tightened to **75% of proof load**, Shigley's *Mechanical Engineering Design* shows the **equivalent tensile stress** in the bolt is approximately:

$$\sigma_{eq} \approx 1.30 \cdot \sigma_t$$

This is the **1.30 factor rule** widely used in industry and KTU design problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a bolt under combined loading", the cleanest approach is:
> (i) Calculate $\sigma_t = P_0/A_s$
> (ii) Apply the factor 1.30 (if 75% proof load assumed): $\sigma_{design} = 1.30\,\sigma_t$
> (iii) Compare with $\sigma_{proof}/FOS$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Screw-Jack Efficiency

### Setup
- Single-start square thread with mean diameter $d_m$, lead $L$.
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = \tan\phi$ at thread surfaces.
- Helix angle $\alpha$ at mean radius: $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$.
- Load to be raised = $W$.

### Step 1 — Unwrap the Helical Surface
Imagine cutting the helical thread along an axial line and flattening it into a straight **inclined plane** of slope $\tan\alpha$. The block of weight $W$ rests on this plane with friction angle $\phi$.

### Step 2 — Resolve Forces on the Inclined Plane
Two horizontal force components act along the unwrapped plane to drive the block upward:
- **Tangential component** (due to applied torque): $F_t$
- **Normal component** (due to vertical load): $F_n$

The applied torque at mean radius $r_m = d_m/2$ is:
$$T_R = F_t \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2}$$

For a block of weight $W$ on an incline of angle $\alpha$ with friction angle $\phi$ being raised (so friction acts *down* the plane opposing motion), force equilibrium along the plane gives:
$$F_t = F_n \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

### Step 3 — Vertical Equilibrium
$$F_n \cdot \cos\alpha - F_t \cdot \sin\alpha = W$$

Substituting $F_t$ from Step 2:
$$F_n \cdot \cos\alpha - F_n \tan(\alpha+\phi)\sin\alpha = W$$
$$F_n \cdot \cos\alpha \left[1 - \dfrac{\tan(\alpha+\phi)\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha}\right] = W$$

After trigonometric simplification:
$$F_n = \dfrac{W \cos\phi}{\cos(\alpha + \phi)}$$

### Step 4 — Torque Required to Raise Load
Substitute back:
$$F_t = F_n \tan(\alpha+\phi) = \dfrac{W \cos\phi \cdot \sin(\alpha+\phi)}{\cos(\alpha+\phi)\cos\phi}$$
$$F_t = W \tan(\alpha+\phi)$$

Therefore the **raising torque**:
$$\boxed{T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha+\phi)}$$

### Step 5 — Lowering Torque
If friction now *opposes* downward motion (i.e., friction acts *up* the plane), the sign of $\phi$ reverses:
$$\boxed{T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi-\alpha)}$$

If $T_L > 0$, the screw **does not back-drive** — it is **self-locking**.

### Step 6 — Efficiency
The work output per revolution = $W \cdot L$ (raising load $W$ by lead $L$).
The work input per revolution = $T_R \cdot 2\pi$ (rotating through $2\pi$ rad).

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work output}}{\text{Work input}} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{T_R \cdot 2\pi} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{\dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha+\phi)\cdot 2\pi}$$

Since $L = \pi d_m \tan\alpha$:
$$\eta = \dfrac{W \cdot \pi d_m \tan\alpha}{\pi d_m W \tan(\alpha+\phi)}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Joint Stiffness Ratio $C$

When external load $P$ is applied:
- Bolt elongates by $\delta_b = P_b / k_b$
- Members compress by $\delta_m = P_m / k_m$
- Compatibility: $\delta_b = \delta_m$
- Equilibrium: $P_b + P_m = P$

From compatibility:
$$\dfrac{P_b}{k_b} = \dfrac{P_m}{k_m} \Rightarrow P_m = P_b \cdot \dfrac{k_m}{k_b}$$

Substitute into equilibrium:
$$P_b + P_b \cdot \dfrac{k_m}{k_b} = P \Rightarrow P_b \left(1 + \dfrac{k_m}{k_b}\right) = P$$

$$P_b = \dfrac{P}{1 + k_m/k_b} = P \cdot \dfrac{k_b}{k_b + k_m} = C \cdot P$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{C = \dfrac{k_b}{k_b + k_m}}$$

This fraction $C$ (typically $0.15$–$0.30$ for steel-on-steel joints) determines how much of the external load is felt by the bolt.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bolted Joint Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Load Conditions P, e, Cyclic"] --> B["Select Bolt Material sigma_uts"]
    B --> C["Choose Trial Nominal Diameter d"]
    C --> D["Lookup Stress Area As from IS 1367"]
    D --> E["Compute Preload P0 = 0.75 Pproof"]
    E --> F["Compute Tensile Stress sigma_t"]
    F --> G["Compute Torsional Shear tau"]
    G --> H["Apply Combined Stress sigma_eq = sqrt sigma_t sq + 3 tau sq"]
    H --> I{"sigma_eq le sigma_proof / FOS"}
    I -->|"YES"| J["Check Fatigue: sigma_a vs sigma_e"]
    I -->|"NO"| C
    J --> K{"Fatigue OK"}
    K -->|"YES"| L["Finalize Bolt: d, Grade, As"]
    K -->|"NO"| C
    L --> M["OUTPUT: Bolt Specification"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Power Screw Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["INPUT: Load W, Lift L, Material mu"] --> H["Compute Helix Angle tan alpha = L over pi dm"]
    H --> K{"Self-Lock Check tan alpha le mu"}
    K -->|"NO"| X["RE-DESIGN: Reduce alpha or Use Buttress"]
    K -->|"YES"| R["Compute Raising Torque TR = W dm over 2 times tan alpha plus phi"]
    R --> E["Compute Efficiency eta = tan alpha over tan alpha plus phi"]
    E --> S["Compute Lowering Torque TL = W dm over 2 times tan phi minus alpha"]
    S --> V["Verify Bearing Pressure p = 4W over pi dm sq lt p_allow"]
    V --> OK{"All Checks Pass"}
    OK -->|"YES"| OUT["OUTPUT: Screw Dimensions and Efficiency"]
    OK -->|"NO"| H
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stress Decomposition in a Bolt

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Preload P0 acting axially"] --> SIG_T["Tensile Stress sigma_t = P0 over As"]
    TT["Tightening Torque T about axis"] --> TAU["Torsional Shear tau = 16T over pi dc cubed"]
    SIG_T --> COMB["Combined 2D Stress Element"]
    TAU --> COMB
    COMB --> PRIN["Principal Stresses sigma1 and sigma2"]
    PRIN --> VMS["von Mises sigma_eq = sqrt sigma_t sq + 3 tau sq"]
    VMS --> SAFE{"sigma_eq le sigma_proof / FOS"}
    SAFE -->|"YES"| PASS["Bolt is Safe"]
    SAFE -->|"NO"| FAIL["REDESIGN: Increase d or Use Higher Grade"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Bolt-Group Under Eccentric Shear

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P_EXT["External Load P"] --> P1["Direct Shear per Bolt Fd = P over n"]
    P_EXT --> P2["Eccentric Moment M = P times e"]
    P2 --> P3["Torsional Shear on Bolt i: Ft_i = M times r_i over sum r_i squared"]
    P1 --> P4["Resolve into Components"]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5["Resultant F_max for Farthest Bolt"]
    P5 --> P6["Check Shear Stress tau = F_max over As le tau_allow"]
    P6 --> SAFE2{"tau le tau_allow"}
    SAFE2 -->|"YES"| ACC["Joint Acceptable"]
    SAFE2 -->|"NO"| REDO["REDESIGN: Increase n or Bolt Grade"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "nominal stress area" of a threaded bolt and explain why it is preferred over the core area for tensile strength calculations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **nominal stress area** $A_s$ of a bolt is a fictitious area calculated from the mean of the minor and pitch diameters, or directly obtained from standard tables (IS 1367 / ISO 898) for each nominal bolt size.

$$A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(\dfrac{d + d_c}{2}\right)^{\!2}$$

It is preferred over the core area because:
1. The thread load is **not** carried entirely by the minor diameter; the load is distributed over the thread engagement.
2. Using $d_c$ alone is **over-conservative** and gives non-standard, oversized designs.
3. $A_s$ accounts for **stress concentration**, **pitch error**, and **thread shear effects** based on empirical testing. (3 marks for complete definition + 2 reasons)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State the condition for self-locking of a power screw and explain its significance in the design of a screw jack.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A power screw is **self-locking** when the helix angle $\alpha$ is less than or equal to the friction angle $\phi$:

$$\tan\alpha \le \mu \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \alpha \le \phi$$

**Significance in screw-jack design:**
- A self-locking jack **will not unwind** (back-drive) under the applied load even when the handle is released. This is **critical for safety** — the load stays supported without continuous input from the operator.
- Without self-locking, the jack would "run back" and the load could drop, causing accidents.
- A typical self-locking screw jack has $\eta < 50\%$, which is acceptable for safety-driven applications. (3 marks — 1 for condition + 2 for significance)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Threaded Fastener Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A steel bolt of M20 × 2.5 grade 8.8 is used to clamp two members. The joint is subjected to a fluctuating external tensile load varying from $P_{min} = 20$ kN to $P_{max} = 60$ kN. The joint stiffness ratio is $C = 0.25$ and the recommended preload is $75\%$ of the proof load. The bolt has 11 engaged threads. Compute:
- (i) The proof load and the preload. (3 marks)
- (ii) The maximum and minimum bolt tensions. (4 marks)

**(b)** Determine the factor of safety against yielding using the **von Mises equivalent stress criterion**, assuming the bolt is tightened such that the torsional shear stress is $0.5\,\sigma_t$ during assembly. Use $\sigma_{uts} = 800$ MPa for grade 8.8. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Bolt: M20 × 2.5, Grade 8.8
- $\sigma_{uts} = 800$ MPa
- $A_s$ for M20 (from IS 1367) = **245 mm²**
- $\sigma_{proof} = 0.7 \times 800 = 560$ MPa
- $C = 0.25$
- External load range: $P_{min} = 20$ kN, $P_{max} = 60$ kN

**(a)(i) Proof Load and Preload:**

$$P_{proof} = \sigma_{proof} \cdot A_s = 560 \times 245 = 137{,}200 \text{ N} = 137.2 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_0 = 0.75 \times P_{proof} = 0.75 \times 137.2 = 102.9 \text{ kN}$$

**(a)(ii) Maximum and Minimum Bolt Tensions:**

The total bolt load under external load $P$ is:
$$P_b = P_0 + C \cdot P$$

For $P = P_{max} = 60$ kN:
$$P_{b,max} = 102.9 + 0.25 \times 60 = 102.9 + 15 = 117.9 \text{ kN}$$

For $P = P_{min} = 20$ kN:
$$P_{b,min} = 102.9 + 0.25 \times 20 = 102.9 + 5 = 107.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Alternating and Mean Bolt Loads:**
$$P_a = \dfrac{P_{b,max} - P_{b,min}}{2} = \dfrac{117.9 - 107.9}{2} = 5 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_m = \dfrac{P_{b,max} + P_{b,min}}{2} = \dfrac{117.9 + 107.9}{2} = 112.9 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Von Mises Factor of Safety:**

Given: $\tau = 0.5\,\sigma_t$ during assembly, so the worst-case tensile stress is at $P_{b,max}$:

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_{b,max}}{A_s} = \dfrac{117{,}900}{245} = 481.22 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau = 0.5 \times \sigma_t = 240.61 \text{ MPa}$$

von Mises equivalent stress:
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^{\,2} + 3\tau^2} = \sqrt{(481.22)^2 + 3(240.61)^2}$$
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{231{,}572 + 173{,}680} = \sqrt{405{,}252} = 636.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Yield strength for grade 8.8: $\sigma_y = 0.8\,\sigma_{uts} = 0.8 \times 800 = 640$ MPa.

$$\boxed{FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}} = \dfrac{640}{636.6} = 1.005}$$

**The bolt is critically loaded. We must upgrade to grade 10.9 or use a larger diameter (M24).** (7 marks — 3 for FOS computation, 2 for interpretation, 2 for design recommendation)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Power Screw / Screw Jack Design

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Design a **screw jack** to lift a maximum load of $W = 50$ kN. The screw is to have a **square thread** of mean diameter $d_m = 50$ mm and lead $L = 10$ mm. The coefficient of friction at the thread is $\mu = 0.12$ and at the collar (mean radius $r_c = 40$ mm) is $\mu_c = 0.10$. Compute:
- (i) The helix angle and friction angle. (3 marks)
- (ii) The torque required to raise the load. (4 marks)

**(b)** Calculate (i) the torque to lower the load, (ii) the efficiency of the screw jack, and (iii) state with justification whether the screw is self-locking. Also find the collar torque required to overcome collar friction. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given:** $W = 50$ kN = 50,000 N, $d_m = 50$ mm, $L = 10$ mm, $\mu = 0.12$, $\mu_c = 0.10$, $r_c = 40$ mm.

**(a)(i) Helix Angle and Friction Angle:**

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m} = \dfrac{10}{\pi \times 50} = \dfrac{10}{157.08} = 0.06366$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.06366) = 3.644°$$

$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.12$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.843°$$

**(a)(ii) Torque to Raise Load:**

$$T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi) = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 50}{2}\tan(3.644° + 6.843°)$$
$$T_R = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \tan(10.487°)$$
$$T_R = 1{,}250{,}000 \times 0.1851 = 231{,}375 \text{ N·mm} = 231.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**(b)(i) Torque to Lower Load:**

$$T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi - \alpha) = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \tan(6.843° - 3.644°)$$
$$T_L = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \tan(3.199°)$$
$$T_L = 1{,}250{,}000 \times 0.0559 = 69{,}880 \text{ N·mm} = 69.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**(b)(ii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \dfrac{0.06366}{0.1851} = 0.344 = 34.4\%$$

**(b)(iii) Self-Locking Check:**

Since $\alpha = 3.644° < \phi = 6.843°$, the condition $\tan\alpha < \mu$ is satisfied.

$$\therefore \text{The screw jack IS self-locking.}$$

This is also confirmed because $T_L > 0$ (positive, meaning an external torque is required to lower the load).

**Collar Friction Torque:**

$$T_{collar} = \mu_c \cdot W \cdot r_c = 0.10 \times 50{,}000 \times 40 = 200{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 200 \text{ N·m}$$

**Total Torque to Raise (including collar):**

$$T_{R,total} = T_R + T_{collar} = 231.4 + 200 = 431.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**Overall Efficiency (including collar):**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_{R,total}} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 10}{2\pi \times 431.4 \times 1000} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{2{,}710{,}643} = 0.1845 = 18.45\%$$

(7 marks — distributed across each sub-part)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the collar torque** — A screw jack has TWO friction sources: threads AND collar. Always add $T_{collar} = \mu_c W r_c$ to $T_R$ for total raising torque.
> 2. **Using $d$ instead of $d_m$** — All screw-jack formulas use **mean (pitch) diameter**, NOT nominal or core diameter.
> 3. **Sign error in $T_L$** — If $\phi < \alpha$, the screw back-drives and $T_L$ is negative. Always check the self-locking condition first.
> 4. **Using $A$ (gross area) instead of $A_s$ (stress area)** — Bolts must always use $A_s$ from standard tables.
> 5. **Mixing static and dynamic FOS** — For fluctuating loads, apply fatigue check (Goodman/Soderberg), NOT just static yielding.
> 6. **Confusing lead and pitch** — Single-start thread: lead = pitch. Multi-start thread: lead = pitch × number of starts.
> 7. **Ignoring the 1.30 factor** for combined stress under 75% proof load — a very common KTU trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Screws are classified** into *Threaded Fasteners* (clamping bolts) and *Power Screws* (motion conversion). They are governed by different design philosophies.
- **Nominal stress area** $A_s$ (NOT core area) is the standard reference area for all bolt strength calculations.
- **Preload** is set to **75% of proof load** for permanent joints and **90%** for non-permanent joints to balance fatigue life and clamping security.
- **Combined loading** during tightening gives a von Mises equivalent stress approximately **1.30 × pure tensile stress** — this 1.30 factor is widely used in design checks.
- **Joint stiffness ratio** $C = k_b/(k_b + k_m)$ determines how much external load reaches the bolt. Smaller $C$ (softer members or stiffer bolts) means lower dynamic bolt load.
- **Bolt of uniform strength** is achieved when the shank diameter equals the core diameter $d_c$, eliminating the weak thread-root section.
- **Eccentric bolt groups** experience direct shear + torsional shear; the critical (farthest) bolt governs the design.
- **Power screw helix angle** $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$ must satisfy **self-locking**: $\tan\alpha \le \mu$.
- **Torque to raise**: $T_R = (W d_m/2)\tan(\alpha + \phi)$.
- **Torque to lower**: $T_L = (W d_m/2)\tan(\phi - \alpha)$ — positive if self-locking.
- **Screw jack efficiency** $\eta = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha+\phi)$, typically **less than 50%**.
- **Collar friction torque** $T_c = \mu_c W r_c$ must be added to thread torque for realistic designs.
- **Material choice**: Grade 8.8 ($\sigma_{uts}=800$ MPa), Grade 10.9 ($\sigma_{uts}=1000$ MPa), Grade 12.9 ($\sigma_{uts}=1200$ MPa) are standard bolt grades.
- **Effective bolt length** $L_b$ includes grip length + half-head height + half-nut height + washer thickness (whichever apply).
- **Always verify bearing pressure** on the thread and collar surfaces: $p = W / (\pi d_m \cdot h \cdot n_{threads}) \le p_{allow}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Standards # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **STANDARD (Definition as per ISO/IEC Guide 2 & KTU Module 2):** > A **standard** is a document, established by consensus and approved by a recognized body, that provides, for common and repeated use, rules, guidelines, or characteristics for activities or their results, aimed at the achievement of the optimum degree of order in a given context. In the context of **Machine Design (PEPET411)**, standards are codified technical specifications that govern: - **Material properties** (chemical composition, mechanical strength, heat treatment) - **Dimensional tolerances** (shaft and hole sizes, fits, GD&T) - **Manufacturing processes** (machining allowances, surface finish grades) - **Testing and quality assurance** (NDT procedures, acceptance criteria) - **Design procedures** (load factors, fatigue cycles, reliability targets) The major standardization bodies referenced in KTU's Machine Design syllabus are: | Body | Full Name | Scope | |------|-----------|-------| | **BIS** | Bureau of Indian Standards | National (India) | | **ISO** | International Organization for Standardization | Global | | **IS** | Indian Standard (prefix used with BIS codes, e.g., IS 2062) | National designation | | **DIN** | Deutsches Institut für Normung | Germany | | **BS** | British Standards | United Kingdom | | **ASTM** | American Society for Testing and Materials | USA | | **AISI/SAE** | American Iron and Steel Institute / Society of Automotive Engineers | Steel grading (USA) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Light" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of standards as the "Traffic Rules" of Engineering.** Imagine driving without traffic rules — every driver would invent their own meaning of "stop," "go," and "right of way." Chaos, collisions, and confusion would dominate the road. Now imagine if every car manufacturer decided its own bolt size — a Maruti mechanic could not fix a Hyundai, and a Tata engineer could not procure a Bajaj fastener. **Standards are the traffic rules of the engineering world:** they ensure that a bolt made in Coimbatore fits a nut made in Ludhiana, that steel from Jamshedpur behaves identically to steel from Pittsburgh, and that a designer in Kerala can confidently use a design code developed in Germany. ## 1.3 The "Why" of Standards in Machine Design - **Interchangeability:** A component from one manufacturer can replace that of another without rework. - **Quality assurance:** Standards encode decades of empirical testing into prescriptive numbers. - **Safety:** Design standards (e.g., IS 800, ASME BPVC) embed safety factors that prevent catastrophic failure. - **Economy:** Mass production of standardized parts reduces per-unit cost. - **Legal compliance:** Many standards are mandated by law (e.g., BIS certification under BIS Act, 2016). - **Communication:** Provides a common technical language across engineering disciplines and geographies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Standards):** > Students are expected to understand the role of BIS and ISO standards in machine design, recognize preferred number series (Renard series), and apply standardized material designations (e.g., IS, AISI/SAE) when selecting materials for design problems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Standards Standards in Machine Design are broadly classified into the following categories: ### A. By Origin / Governing Body 1. **International Standards** — e.g., **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization), headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland. Founded: **23 February 1947**. 2. **National Standards** — e.g., **BIS** (India), **DIN** (Germany), **BS** (UK), **ANSI** (USA). 3. **Industry / Professional Standards** — e.g., **ASME** (American Society of Mechanical Engineers), **SAE**, **AGMA** (American Gear Manufacturers Association). 4. **Company / Internal Standards** — proprietary specifications used within an organization. ### B.
