In **Machine Design**, you will design shafts, springs, gears, and fasteners. Almost all of them operate under **non-static** conditions. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that a designer who calculates only the **static strength** is producing an *unsafe, academically incomplete* design. This topic provides the analytical tools to quantify dynamic and cyclic effects so that the final design has an **acceptable factor of safety** against both fatigue and shock.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Foundation of Fatigue Analysis

The **S-N curve** is a semi-log plot of alternating stress amplitude ($S_a$) on the linear Y-axis and number of cycles to failure ($N$) on the logarithmic X-axis. Two distinct families of materials exist:

| Material Family | Behavior at High $N$ | Endurance Limit | KTU 2024 Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrous metals (Steel, Cast Iron)** | Curve becomes horizontal | $S_e'$ exists (a true endurance limit) at $N = 10^6$ to $10^8$ | Use $S_e'$ directly as design stress |
| **Non-ferrous (Aluminum, Copper alloys)** | Curve continues to decline | No true endurance limit | Use **Fatigue Strength** at $N = 5 \times 10^8$ as design stress |

### 2.1.1 The Five Critical Stress Parameters in a Cycle

For any general cyclic stress, two extremes exist:
- $S_{max}$ = maximum stress
- $S_{min}$ = minimum stress

From these we derive the **five canonical stress parameters** (highly tested in KTU 2024 ESE):

> [!NOTE]
> **Master These Five Equations:**
> 1. **Mean Stress:** $S_m = \dfrac{S_{max} + S_{min}}{2}$
> 2. **Alternating (Amplitude) Stress:** $S_a = \dfrac{S_{max} - S_{min}}{2}$
> 3. **Stress Range:** $S_r = S_{max} - S_{min}$
> 4. **Stress Ratio:** $R = \dfrac{S_{min}}{S_{max}}$
> 5. **Amplitude Ratio:** $A = \dfrac{S_a}{S_m}$

### 2.1.2 Standard Loading Cycles (KTU Favourite Diagram)

| Cycle Type | $R$ value | Description | Real Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully Reversed** | $R = -1$ | $S_{max} = -S_{min}$ | Rotating shaft, torsion spring |
| **Pulsating (Zero-to-Max)** | $R = 0$ | $S_{min} = 0$ | Tooth loading during one-way meshing |
| **Static** | $R = +1$ | $S_{max} = S_{min}$ | Bolted joint pre-load |
| **Fluctuating (general)** | $-1 < R < 1$ | Both $S_m$ and $S_a$ nonzero | Pressurized pressure vessel (varying) |

---

## 2.2 Endurance Limit Modifying Factors — The Heart of KTU Module 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *uncorrected* endurance limit ($S_e'$) is obtained from a polished, standard test specimen rotating in bending. Real machine components are **not** ideal — they are larger, rougher, subjected to non-bending loads, may operate at elevated temperature, etc. KTU 2024 requires the application of a chain of correction factors.

$$
S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'
$$

### Detailed Factor-by-Factor Breakdown

| Factor | Symbol | Accounts For | KTU-Mandated Formula | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Factor** | $k_a$ | Machining roughness | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | KTU Data Handbook |
| **Size Factor** | $k_b$ | Component size | $k_b = 0.879 \cdot d^{-0.107}$ for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm (bending/torsion) | Shigley / KTU Handbook |
| **Load Factor** | $k_c$ | Type of loading | $k_c = 1.0$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion) | Standard |
| **Temperature Factor** | $k_d$ | Operating temperature | $k_d = 1.0$ for $T \le 450^\circ C$ (decreases above) | KTU Handbook |
| **Reliability Factor** | $k_e$ | Statistical confidence | $k_e = 1.0$ (50%), $0.897$ (90%), $0.814$ (99%), $0.753$ (99.9%) | Standard |
| **Miscellaneous Factor** | $k_f$ | Residual stress, corrosion, etc. | $k_f = 1.0$ unless otherwise specified | Designer choice |

---

## 2.3 Stress Concentration & Notch Sensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$):** The ratio of the theoretical maximum local stress at a discontinuity (hole, fillet, keyway) to the nominal (average) stress in the net section.
> $$K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$$

Real materials are **notch-sensitive** to varying degrees. The **Notch Sensitivity Index ($q$)** quantifies this:

$$
q = \dfrac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}
$$

where $K_f$ = **fatigue stress concentration factor** (the *effective* value actually realized in cyclic loading).

Rearranging gives the **KTU 2024 Critical Formula**:

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Behaviour of $q$:**
> - $q = 0 \Rightarrow K_f = 1$ → Material is *notch-insensitive* (rare, e.g., very ductile cast iron)
> - $q = 1 \Rightarrow K_f = K_t$ → Material is *fully notch-sensitive* (typical for brittle, high-strength steels)
> - $0 < q < 1$ → Real engineering metals (use charts in KTU Data Handbook)

---

## 2.4 Failure Criteria Under Fluctuating Stress (Mean Stress Effects)

The S-N curve gives endurance limit for **fully reversed** ($R=-1$) conditions only. To handle cycles with a non-zero mean stress, KTU 2024 mandates the following three classical failure criteria:

| Criterion | Equation | When Used (KTU 2024) | Conservatism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Most conservative; required for ductile materials under static + cyclic | **Most conservative** |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Standard choice for brittle + ductile; KTU board favourite | Conservative |
| **Gerber** | $\left(\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | Best fit to experimental data; used for ductile materials | **Least conservative** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **factor-of-safety-based** design (typical KTU 2024 problem), the **Goodman criterion** is most commonly tested.

---

## 2.5 Shock Load Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sudden Load (No Initial Velocity):** When a load $P$ is applied instantly to a structure, the resulting stress, deflection, and load are **twice** the static values.
> $$P_{shock} = 2P, \quad \delta_{shock} = 2\delta_{static}, \quad \sigma_{shock} = 2\sigma_{static}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Falling Weight / Drop Impact:** When a weight $W$ falls from height $h$ onto a structure of static deflection $\Delta$ (or static stress $\sigma_{static}$):
> $$P_{shock} = P \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta}}\right)$$
> $$\sigma_{shock} = \sigma_{static} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta}}\right)$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Fatigue/Shock Theory |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Crankshafts** | Endurance limit governs design life of 10⁸ cycles |
| **Aircraft Landing Gear** | Shock loading during touchdown |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Cyclic pressurization fatigue |
| **Civil Bridges** | Wind/fatigue + earthquake shock |
| **Spring Design** | Coil springs absorb shock via stored strain energy |
| **Gearbox Shafts** | Combined bending + torsion fatigue |

---

## 2.6 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Stress | $S_m = (S_{max}+S_{min})/2$ | MPa | KTU 2024 base equation |
| 2 | Amplitude Stress | $S_a = (S_{max}-S_{min})/2$ | MPa | KTU 2024 base equation |
| 3 | Corrected Endurance Limit | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ | MPa | KTU 2024 mandatory chain |
| 4 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa) | MPa | Memorize! |
| 5 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e' = 700$ MPa (for $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa) | MPa | Saturates |
| 6 | Fatigue Stress Concentration | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless | KTU 2024 most tested |
| 7 | Notch Sensitivity | $q = (K_f - 1)/(K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless | $0 \le q \le 1$ |
| 8 | Goodman Criterion | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ | dimensionless | KTU ESE favourite |
| 9 | Soderberg Criterion | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y = 1/n$ | dimensionless | Most conservative |
| 10 | Gerber Criterion | $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + n\sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1$ | dimensionless | Least conservative |
| 11 | Sudden Load Factor | Factor = **2** | — | No initial velocity |
| 12 | Shock Load (Falling) | $P_{shock} = P(1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\Delta})$ | N | KTU 2024 derivation favourite |
| 13 | Shock Stress (Falling) | $\sigma_{shock} = \sigma_{static}(1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\Delta})$ | MPa | KTU 2024 derivation favourite |
| 14 | Surface Factor | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | dimensionless | $a, b$ from KTU Handbook |
| 15 | Size Factor (Bending) | $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (mm) | dimensionless | For $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 Rule — Avoid Table-Breaking Pipes:** When typing formulas in answer sheets, **never** use the bare `|` character (which the markdown table engine interprets as a column separator). Always use the LaTeX vertical bar macro `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: write $\vert S_{min}/S_{max} \vert$ rather than $\vert S_{min}/S_{max} \vert$ with bare pipes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **complete, line-by-line derivations** of the most important equations for the KTU 2024 ESE. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Endurance Limit Modifying Factor Chain

**Starting premise:** The basic endurance limit $S_e'$ is measured for a polished specimen under fully reversed bending at standard size.

**Step 1 — Surface Roughness Adjustment:**
The actual machined surface has micro-notches that act as fatigue crack initiators. A roughness factor $k_a$ is introduced, which is empirically fitted as a power function of the ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut}$:

$$
k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{b}
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are tabulated (e.g., for *ground* surface, $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$).

**Step 2 — Size Effect:**
A larger volume of material under cyclic stress increases the statistical probability of containing a critical flaw. For round shafts in bending/torsion:

$$
k_b = 0.879 \cdot d^{-0.107}
$$

For non-round sections, use the *equivalent diameter* $d_e = 0.808 \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ for rectangular sections.

**Step 3 — Load Type Correction:**
Axial loading puts the entire cross-section in uniform tension (no stress gradient), making it more damaging. Torsion has a lower endurance ratio. Hence:

$$
k_c = 1.0 \text{ (bending)}, \quad 0.85 \text{ (axial)}, \quad 0.59 \text{ (torsion)}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiplicative Combination:**
All six factors act *simultaneously* and *independently* on the failure mechanism, justifying a **multiplicative** correction:

$$
\boxed{S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Goodman Failure Criterion

**Premise:** Failure occurs when the alternating stress combined with the mean stress reaches a critical boundary in the $S_m$–$S_a$ plane.

**Step 1 — Identify Two Failure Limits:**
- When the cycle is **fully reversed**, $S_m = 0$, and failure occurs at $S_a = S_e$.
- When the cycle is **purely static**, $S_a = 0$, and failure occurs at $S_m = S_{ut}$.

**Step 2 — Connect with a Straight Line (Goodman Assumption):**
Plotting these two limiting cases on the $S_m$–$S_a$ plane and joining them with a straight line:

$$
\frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

This is the **Goodman failure locus** — the boundary separating safe (below the line) from unsafe (above the line) operating points.

**Step 3 — Introduce the Factor of Safety $n$:**
In design, we do not operate on the failure line but at a safe distance from it. The factor of safety $n$ scales the operating stress vector so that it must lie within a smaller, homothetic region:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$ in a KTU 2024 ESE Problem:**
Multiplying both sides by $n$:

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}}
$$

This is the **final form** students must quote to obtain full marks in a 14-mark problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Notch Sensitivity and Effective Stress Concentration

**Premise:** The theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t$ is computed assuming perfectly elastic, isotropic, homogeneous material — an idealization. Real materials partially "relax" the local peak through local micro-yielding.

**Step 1 — Define the Fatigue Notch Factor $K_f$:**
$K_f$ is the *effective* stress concentration observed in a *fatigue test* of a notched specimen. It is always less than or equal to $K_t$.

**Step 2 — Empirical Definition of $q$:**
Peterson defined the notch sensitivity index as the *fraction of the theoretical concentration that is actually realized* in fatigue:

$$
q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $K_f$:**

$$
K_f - 1 = q (K_t - 1)
$$

$$
\boxed{K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply in Bending Stress Calculation:**
For a notched shaft with nominal bending stress $\sigma_{nom}$, the effective stress to use in fatigue design is:

$$
\sigma_{eff} = K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Sudden and Shock Load Equation

**Setup:** A vertical bar of length $L$, cross-section area $A$, modulus of elasticity $E$ is fixed at the top. A weight $W$ falls from height $h$ onto a stopper at the bottom.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance:**
The energy stored in the bar at maximum deflection $\delta$ must equal the work done by the falling weight:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \delta^2 = W \cdot (h + \delta)
$$

where $k = AE/L$ is the axial stiffness of the bar.

**Step 2 — Substitute Stiffness and Expand:**

$$
\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^2 = W \cdot h + W \cdot \delta
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply Both Sides by $2L/AE$ to Get a Quadratic in $\delta$:**

$$
\delta^2 = \frac{2WL}{AE} h + \frac{2W L}{AE} \delta
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce Static Deflection $\Delta = WL/(AE)$:**

$$
\delta^2 - 2 \Delta \cdot \delta - 2 h \Delta = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the Quadratic Using the Standard Formula:**

$$
\delta = \frac{2\Delta \pm \sqrt{4\Delta^2 + 8h\Delta}}{2} = \Delta \pm \sqrt{\Delta^2 + 2h\Delta}
$$

Taking the positive root (since $\delta > 0$):

$$
\delta = \Delta + \sqrt{\Delta^2 + 2h\Delta} = \Delta \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Force and Stress:**
Since $P = k \delta$ and $\sigma = P/A = (E/L) \delta$, both scale by the same factor as $\delta$:

$$
\boxed{P_{shock} = P_{static} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta}}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{shock} = \sigma_{static} \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\Delta}}\right)}
$$

**Step 7 — Special Case: Sudden Load ($h = 0$):**

$$
\sigma_{shock} = \sigma_{static} (1 + \sqrt{1 + 0}) = 2 \sigma_{static}
$$

This recovers the famous **factor of 2** for sudden application without initial velocity. ✅

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What (Decision Table for KTU 2024 ESE)

| Given Data in the Question | Failure Criterion to Use | Formula to Quote |
|---|---|---|
| $S_e$, $S_{ut}$, $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_m$, find $n$ | **Goodman** (default) | $n = 1/(\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut})$ |
| $S_y$ given, ductile material, high reliability required | **Soderberg** | $n = 1/(\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y)$ |
| Ductile material, want best fit, less conservative | **Gerber** | $n^2 \sigma_a^2 / S_e^2 + n \sigma_m / S_{ut} = 1$ |
| Notched component, $K_t$ given | **Use $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$** first | $K_f \cdot \sigma_{nom}$ replaces $\sigma_a$ |
| Falling weight from height $h$ | **Shock formula** | $\sigma_{shock} = \sigma_{static}(1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\Delta})$ |
| Load suddenly applied (no fall) | **Multiply static by 2** | $\sigma_{shock} = 2 \sigma_{static}$ |
| S-N curve given, find life for a stress $S_a$ | **Use Basquin's Law** (if in KTU syllabus) | $S_a \cdot N^b = \text{const}$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Define *endurance limit* and *fatigue strength*. Explain why the latter is used for non-ferrous materials.

**Model Answer:**

The **endurance limit** ($S_e'$) is the maximum completely reversed stress that a material can sustain for an *infinite* number of cycles (conventionally $N = 10^6$ to $10^8$) without failure. It exists as a true horizontal asymptote on the S-N curve for **ferrous materials** such as steels and cast irons.

**Fatigue strength**, in contrast, is the stress amplitude corresponding to a *specified* finite life, typically $N = 5 \times 10^8$ cycles. It is used for **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum, copper alloys, magnesium) because these materials do *not* exhibit a true endurance limit — their S-N curves continue to decline with increasing $N$, never flattening out. Hence, the designer must specify a design life and read off the corresponding fatigue strength as the allowable stress. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`** A shaft is subjected to a completely reversed bending stress of 200 MPa and a steady torsional shear stress of 100 MPa. Compute the *amplitude* and *mean* components of the *bending* stress (and reason about the torsion's contribution).

**Model Answer:**

For the **bending stress** (fully reversed):

- $S_{max} = +200$ MPa, $S_{min} = -200$ MPa
- $S_m = (200 + (-200))/2 = 0$ MPa
- $S_a = (200 - (-200))/2 = 200$ MPa

For the **steady torsion** ($S_{min} = S_{max} = 100$ MPa):
- $S_m = 100$ MPa
- $S_a = 0$ MPa

The torsion contributes only to the **mean** component; its amplitude is zero. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

A circular shaft of diameter **40 mm** is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 650$ MPa and $S_y = 400$ MPa. The shaft is subjected to a **fluctuating bending moment** varying from $+250$ N·m to $-150$ N·m. The surface is *ground*, the operating temperature is 50 °C, and a reliability of 90% is required. Assume the loading is bending, with no stress concentration. Compute the factor of safety using the **Goodman criterion**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Section modulus and nominal stresses**

$$
Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40^3}{32} = 62\,832 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{62\,832} = 3.98 \text{ MPa}
$$

Wait — the units don't match expectation. Let me recompute carefully using **N·mm**:

$$
M_{max} = 250 \text{ N·m} = 250 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{62\,832} = 3.98 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Examiner Note:** Always cross-check units. A 40 mm shaft with 250 N·m moment yields only ~4 MPa, which is far below the strength. Such low values indicate a *very high* safety factor. The student must *not* invent bigger numbers.

**Step 2 — Mean and amplitude stress**

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{3.98 + (-2.39)}{2} = 0.795 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{3.98 - (-2.39)}{2} = 3.185 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Uncorrected endurance limit** ($S_{ut} = 650$ MPa $<$ 1400 MPa)

$$
S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 650 = 325 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply correction factors**

| Factor | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| $k_a$ (Ground, $S_{ut} = 650$) | $1.58 \times 650^{-0.085} = 0.978$ | KTU Data Handbook |
| $k_b$ ($d = 40$ mm) | $0.879 \times 40^{-0.107} = 0.6605$ | KTU Data Handbook |
| $k_c$ (Bending) | $1.0$ | Standard |
| $k_d$ ($T = 50^\circ C$) | $1.0$ | Standard |
| $k_e$ (90% reliability) | $0.897$ | Standard |
| $k_f$ | $1.0$ | Standard |

$$
S_e = 0.978 \times 0.6605 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 325 = 188.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Goodman criterion**

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{3.185}{188.5} + \dfrac{0.795}{650}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{1}{0.0169 + 0.00122} = \frac{1}{0.0181} = 55.2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (7 marks sub-parts):**
> A common error is to **omit one or more correction factors** (often $k_b$ or $k_e$), which alone causes a loss of 1–2 marks. Another trap is **forgetting to halve the uncorrected endurance limit** ($S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$). Show every factor in a table for clean valuation. Do not approximate $k_a$ without writing the formula.

**Conclusion:** $n \approx 55$ — an extremely safe design; in practice the shaft would be downsized to save material.

**(Sub-part a = 7 marks: Corrected endurance limit; Sub-part b = 7 marks: Goodman n calculation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`**

A steel rod of length $L = 1.5$ m and diameter $d = 25$ mm ($E = 200$ GPa) is fixed at the upper end. A collar of weight $W = 400$ N is dropped from a height $h = 80$ mm onto a stop at the lower end. Calculate:
**(a)** the maximum stress induced in the rod, and
**(b)** the maximum deflection of the rod.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Static quantities**

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Stiffness of rod:

$$
k = \frac{AE}{L} = \frac{490.87 \times 2 \times 10^5}{1500} = 65\,449 \text{ N/mm}
$$

Static deflection under $W$:

$$
\Delta = \frac{W}{k} = \frac{400}{65\,449} = 0.00611 \text{ mm}
$$

Static stress:

$$
\sigma_{static} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{400}{490.87} = 0.815 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Impact (shock) factor**

$$
I = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\Delta}} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 80}{0.00611}}
$$

$$
I = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 26\,185} = 1 + \sqrt{26\,186} = 1 + 161.82 = 162.82
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum dynamic stress** (Sub-part a)

$$
\sigma_{max} = I \times \sigma_{static} = 162.82 \times 0.815 = 132.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum dynamic deflection** (Sub-part b)

$$
\delta_{max} = I \times \Delta = 162.82 \times 0.00611 = 0.995 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification by direct energy balance**

Work done by falling weight = Strain energy stored in rod:

$$
W(h + \delta) = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^2
$$

$$
400(80 + 0.995) = \frac{1}{2} \times 65\,449 \times 0.995^2
$$

$$
LHS = 32\,398 \text{ N·mm}, \quad RHS = \frac{1}{2} \times 65\,449 \times 0.990 = 32\,397 \text{ N·mm} \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (7 marks sub-parts):**
> A classic error: students compute the *static* deflection and **forget to multiply by the impact factor** in sub-part (b). That single error costs ~3 marks. Also, watch out for **unit conversion** between N, N·m, mm, and MPa — the most common 1-mark deduction in KTU 2024 Part B questions is unit mismatch.

**(Sub-part a = 7 marks: Shock stress calculation; Sub-part b = 7 marks: Shock deflection + verification)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Snapshot:**

- [x] **Endurance limit** = max fully reversed stress for infinite life; *only ferrous metals* have a true one.
- [x] $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for steels with $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa; saturates at 700 MPa above.
- [x] **Five cyclic stress parameters** — $S_m$, $S_a$, $S_r$, $R$, $A$ — all derivable from $S_{max}$, $S_{min}$.
- [x] **Correction chain** — $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ — applied **multiplicatively**.
- [x] **Surface factor $k_a$** = $a S_{ut}^b$ — values of $a, b$ from KTU Data Handbook (Grinding: $a=1.58, b=-0.085$).
- [x] **Size factor $k_b$** = $0.879 d^{-0.107}$ for round shafts in bending/torsion ($2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm).
- [x] **Stress concentration**: $K_t$ (theoretical) $\ge K_f$ (fatigue effective) $\ge 1$.
- [x] **Notch sensitivity** $q$ between 0 and 1: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$.
- [x] **Goodman** is the KTU 2024 default failure criterion: $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$.
- [x] **Soderberg** uses $S_y$ instead of $S_{ut}$ — most conservative; for ductile, safety-critical parts.
- [x] **Gerber** is a parabola — least conservative; best fit to test data for ductile materials.
- [x] **Sudden load** = factor of **2** above static.
- [x] **Shock load** formula: $P_{shock} = P(1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\Delta})$; units must be consistent.
- [x] **E = 200 GPa** for steel; **g = 9.81 m/s²** — memorize these.
- [x] **Factor of safety** typically 1.5 to 4 for well-designed components; use KTU-mandated value.
- [x] When the problem says *"fully corrected endurance limit"*, all six factors must appear in the table.
- [x] Always verify by drawing a **Haigh diagram** mentally to confirm the operating point lies below the failure locus.
- [x] Watch **stress concentration + cyclic loading**: ignore the stress concentration factor in static-only problems, but always include $K_f$ in fatigue problems.
- [x] **Pure torsion** uses $k_c = 0.59$ and the **shear** form of the failure criterion; KTU 2024 sometimes combines bending + torsion in a single question.
- [x] Always quote the **formula** before substituting values in Part B — KTU examiners specifically check for this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eccentric Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eccentric Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Eccentric Loading** is a state of combined loading in which the line of action of an applied load does **not pass through the centroid (geometric axis)** of the cross-section of the machine member. The perpendicular distance between the **line of action of the load** and the **centroidal axis** of the section is called the **eccentricity (e)**.

When a load $P$ acts at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis, it is statically equivalent (by the principle of moments) to a **centric axial load $P$** acting at the centroid plus a **bending moment $M = P \cdot e$** acting about the centroidal axis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Holding a heavy grocery bag at arm's length"**
> 
> Imagine holding a heavy grocery bag — your wrist feels two things simultaneously:
> 1. A **vertical pull** (the weight of the groceries acting straight down through your palm).
> 2. A **rotating twist** (because the bag hangs *away* from your elbow joint, gravity tries to tip your forearm downward, just like a seesaw).
> 
> This is **exactly** what happens in a machine member under eccentric loading — the load produces a *direct stress* (axial) **AND** a *bending stress* (because of the lever-arm distance from the center). Your elbow joint corresponds to the centroid of the section!

### 1.3 Common Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where do we encounter eccentric loading in real machines?**
> - **Cylinder head studs** of an I.C. engine (gas pressure acts at the center of the bore, but the studs are offset → eccentric).
> - **Wall brackets / hangers** carrying machinery loads.
> - **Crane hooks, shackles, and chain links.**
> - **Punch press frames and hammer bases.**
> - **Footstep / pivot bearings** in heavy machinery.
> - **Offset connecting rods and eccentric cams.**

### 1.4 Physical Significance of the Constants

- The **eccentricity $e$** is measured in **mm**.
- For safe design of brittle materials (e.g., cast iron), engineers keep $e$ within the **kern** (core) of the section so that **only compressive stresses** develop — preventing tensile failure.
- For ductile materials (e.g., mild steel), eccentric loading produces **combined tension and compression**, and the section is sized using the **maximum equivalent stress** (principal stress theory).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Equivalent Force System — The Core Logic

The eccentric load $P$ at distance $e$ from the centroidal axis (say, the YY-axis) is replaced by two statically equivalent actions:

1. **A centric axial force** $P$ — produces uniform **direct stress** $\sigma_d = \dfrac{P}{A}$ across the entire cross-section.
2. **A pure bending moment** $M = P \cdot e$ — produces linearly varying **bending stress** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ across the section, with maximum at extreme fibers.

By **superposition**, the combined stress at any fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

The **positive sign** is taken on the side where direct and bending stresses are of the same sense (both tensile or both compressive), and the **negative sign** is taken on the opposite side.

### 2.2 Maximum and Minimum Stresses

Using the **section modulus** $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$, the extreme-fiber stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}
$$

$$
\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}
$$

- $\sigma_{max}$ → governs design (largest magnitude).
- $\sigma_{min}$ → determines whether the stress is fully same-sign or of mixed type.

### 2.3 Concept of KERN (Core of the Section)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kern of a Section** is the region *around the centroid* within which, if the entire load is applied, **the stress across the whole section is of the SAME sign** (no stress reversal). Outside the kern, the section experiences both tensile and compressive stresses.

This is **extremely important** in the design of brittle materials like **brick masonry, cast iron, and concrete**, which are weak in tension.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Cross-Section | Area $A$ | Moment of Inertia $I$ | Section Modulus $Z$ | Maximum Stress $\sigma_{max}$ | Kern Limits |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solid Rectangular** ($b \times h$) | $b \cdot h$ | $\dfrac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{b \cdot h^{2}}{6}$ | $\dfrac{P}{b \cdot h}\left[1 + \dfrac{6e}{h}\right]$ | $\dfrac{h}{6}$ along YY-axis |
| 2 | **Hollow Rectangular** ($B \times H$, $b \times h$) | $B \cdot H - b \cdot h$ | $\dfrac{B \cdot H^{3} - b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ | $\dfrac{B \cdot H^{3} - b \cdot h^{3}}{6H}$ | $\dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P \cdot e}{Z}$ | Depends on geometry |
| 3 | **Solid Circular** (diameter $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4}}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{3}}{32}$ | $\dfrac{4P}{\pi \cdot d^{2}}\left[1 + \dfrac{8e}{d}\right]$ | $\dfrac{d}{8}$ along any diameter |
| 4 | **Hollow Circular** ($d_{o}$, $d_{i}$) | $\dfrac{\pi}{4}(d_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2})$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{64}(d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4})$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{32}\dfrac{(d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4})}{d_{o}}$ | $\dfrac{4P}{\pi(d_{o}^{2}-d_{i}^{2})} + \dfrac{32 P e d_{o}}{\pi(d_{o}^{4}-d_{i}^{4})}$ | $\dfrac{d_{o}}{8}\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{d_{i}}{d_{o}}\right)^{2}\right]$ |
| 5 | **General Equation** | $A$ | $I$ | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$ | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P \cdot e}{Z}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - $P$ → Newtons (N) or kiloNewtons (kN)
> - $e$, $d$, $b$, $h$ → millimeters (mm)
> - Stress $\sigma$ → N/mm² (= MPa)
> - $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$

### 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

- Used in designing **foundation bolts, pipe flanges, manhole covers, and rail joints**.
- Critical for **cylinder-head stud design** where gas pressure $P_g$ acts at the center of the bore but is resisted by studs offset by radius $R$ → the effective eccentricity is roughly $R/2$ (using the "equivalent cone" assumption).
- Forms the **basis of stress concentration analysis** in fillets, keyways, and gear teeth.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Force System for an Eccentric Load

**Given:** A column of cross-sectional area $A$ carries a compressive load $P$ acting at a distance $e$ from the centroidal YY-axis (eccentricity $e$).

**Step 1 — Apply the Principle of Moments:**
Move the load $P$ parallel to itself from its original line of action to the centroid. To keep the system statically equivalent, add a moment equal to the original moment of $P$ about the centroid:

$$
\text{Moment about centroid} = P \cdot e
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Two Internal Actions at Any Section:**
At a transverse section through the column, the internal resisting actions are:
- A **direct axial force** = $P$ (compressive).
- A **bending moment** = $M = P \cdot e$.

**Step 3 — Direct (Axial) Stress:**
The centric force $P$ produces a uniform normal stress:

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(compressive, distributed uniformly)}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Stress:**
The moment $M$ produces a linearly varying bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{b}(y) = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

This is **maximum** at the extreme fibers $y = \pm c$ (where $c$ is the distance from neutral axis to extreme fiber).

**Step 5 — Superpose Both Stresses:**
By the principle of superposition:

$$
\sigma(y) = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b}(y) = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Step 6 — Extreme Fiber Stresses Using Section Modulus:**
At $y = +c$ (side of eccentricity), both stresses add (most compressive if $P$ is compressive):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} \quad \text{(where } Z = I/c\text{)}
$$

At $y = -c$ (opposite side), they subtract:

$$
\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expression for Maximum Stress in a Rectangular Section

**Given:** A short column of rectangular cross-section $b \times h$, loaded eccentrically along the major axis with load $P$ at eccentricity $e$ from the centroid.

**Step 1 — Substitute Geometric Properties:**

$$
A = b \cdot h, \quad I = \frac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}, \quad c = \frac{h}{2}, \quad Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{b \cdot h^{2}}{6}
$$

**Step 2 — Direct Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{b \cdot h}
$$

**Step 3 — Bending Stress (at extreme fiber):**

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot 6}{b \cdot h^{2}} = \frac{6 P e}{b h^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Combined Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{b h} + \frac{6 P e}{b h^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor Out the Common Term:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{b h}\left[1 + \frac{6e}{h}\right]
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for eccentric loading on a rectangular section.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expression for Maximum Stress in a Solid Circular Section

**Given:** Solid circular column of diameter $d$, eccentric load $P$ at distance $e$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Properties:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}, \quad I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}, \quad Z = \frac{I}{(d/2)} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32}
$$

**Step 2 — Direct Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Bending Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{32 P e}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Combined Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}} + \frac{32 P e}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}\left[1 + \frac{8e}{d}\right]
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kern of a Solid Circular Section

**Step 1 — Condition for Zero Stress at Extreme Fiber:**
We want $\sigma = 0$ at the fiber on the opposite side of eccentricity (so the entire section has the same sign of stress).

$$
0 = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e_{max} \cdot c}{I}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Limiting Eccentricity:**

$$
e_{max} = \frac{I}{A \cdot c} = \frac{Z}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for Solid Circle:**

$$
e_{max} = \frac{\pi d^{3}/32}{\pi d^{2}/4} = \frac{d}{8}
$$

This means if the load is applied within a circle of radius $d/8$ around the centroid, the entire cross-section is in **pure compression** — no tension anywhere. This is the **Kern of a solid circle**.

**Step 4 — Similarly for a Hollow Circular Section ($d_{o}$, $d_{i}$):**

$$
e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\pi(d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4})/(32 d_{o})}{\pi(d_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2})/4}
$$

$$
e_{max} = \frac{d_{o}}{8}\left[1 + \left(\frac{d_{i}}{d_{o}}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A short cast iron column of rectangular section $200 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm}$ carries a vertical compressive load of $200 \text{ kN}$ at an eccentricity of $50 \text{ mm}$ along the **major axis (YY)**. Calculate the **maximum and minimum stresses** in the section.

**Given Data:**
- $P = 200 \text{ kN} = 200{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $e = 50 \text{ mm}$
- $b = 150 \text{ mm}$, $h = 200 \text{ mm}$ (major axis = $h$)

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Properties:**

$$
A = b \cdot h = 150 \times 200 = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12} = \frac{150 \times (200)^{3}}{12} = \frac{150 \times 8 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{I}{h/2} = \frac{100 \times 10^{6}}{100} = 1 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Direct (Axial) Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{200{,}000}{30{,}000} = 6.667 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 3 — Bending Moment:**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 200{,}000 \times 50 = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Stress at Extreme Fiber:**

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Combined Stress (on side of eccentricity — both compressive):**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 6.667 + 10 = 16.667 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Combined Stress (opposite side — direct compression − bending tension):**

$$
\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 6.667 - 10 = -3.333 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

| Side of Column | Stress Magnitude | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Side **near** eccentricity | $16.67 \text{ MPa}$ | Compressive |
| Side **far** from eccentricity | $3.33 \text{ MPa}$ | Tensile |

**Step 8 — Verification using KTU Formula:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{b h}\left[1 + \frac{6e}{h}\right] = \frac{200{,}000}{30{,}000}\left[1 + \frac{6 \times 50}{200}\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = 6.667 \times [1 + 1.5] = 6.667 \times 2.5 = 16.667 \text{ MPa} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **tensile stress of 3.33 MPa** on the far side must be less than the tensile strength of cast iron (typically $100$–$200 \text{ MPa}$), otherwise the column will **crack on the tension side**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Equivalent Force System of an Eccentric Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Eccentric Load P at distance e from centroid"] --> B["Apply Principle of Moments"]
    B --> C["Equivalent Force System"]
    C --> D["Axial Load P acting at centroid"]
    C --> E["Bending Moment M = P x e about centroidal axis YY"]
    D --> F["Direct Stress sigma_d = P / A uniform across section"]
    E --> G["Bending Stress sigma_b = M y / I varies linearly across section"]
    F --> H["Superpose Both Stresses"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["sigma_max on near side = P/A + P e / Z"]
    H --> J["sigma_min on far side = P/A minus P e / Z"]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Section under Eccentric Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Cross Section 150 mm x 200 mm"] --> B["Direct Stress sigma_d = 6.667 MPa uniform compression"]
    A --> C["Bending Stress sigma_b linear variation"]
    C --> D["Near side 10 MPa compressive"]
    C --> E["Far side 10 MPa tensile"]
    B --> F["Combined Stress Distribution"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Near extreme fiber sigma_max = 16.667 MPa compressive"]
    F --> H["Neutral Axis shifts upward toward load"]
    F --> I["Far extreme fiber sigma_min = 3.333 MPa tensile"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture: Application in a Cylinder Head

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Gas Pressure P_g acting at bore center"] --> S1["Studs located at radius R from bore center"]
    S1 --> S2["Each stud experiences eccentric tensile load"]
    S2 --> S3["Equivalent system for each stud"]
    S3 --> S4["Tensile load T = Ws / n where Ws = load per stud"]
    S3 --> S5["Bending moment M = T x eccentricity e"]
    S4 --> S6["Combined stress sigma = T/A + M/Z"]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7["Design check against allowable stress"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Concept of KERN (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Apply eccentric load P to section"] --> B{"Where is load applied?"}
    B --> C1["Inside kern region"]
    B --> C2["On kern boundary"]
    B --> C3["Outside kern region"]
    C1 --> D1["Entire section in same sign stress — no reversal"]
    C2 --> D2["Zero stress at one extreme fiber"]
    C3 --> D3["Mixed stresses tensile on one side compressive on other"]
    D1 --> E["Preferred for brittle materials cast iron masonry concrete"]
    D2 --> F["Limiting case design"]
    D3 --> G["Acceptable for ductile materials steel alloys"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define eccentric loading. With a neat sketch, show how an eccentric load is replaced by an equivalent force system.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Eccentric Loading:** A load whose line of action does **not** pass through the centroid (geometric axis) of the cross-section of the member is called an eccentric load. The perpendicular distance between the load line and the centroidal axis is the **eccentricity (e)**.
>
> **Equivalent System:** By the principle of moments, an eccentric load $P$ at eccentricity $e$ is equivalent to:
> - A **centric axial force** $P$ acting at the centroid.
> - A **bending moment** $M = P \cdot e$ acting about the centroidal axis.
>
> The sketch shows the original load $P$ offset by distance $e$ replaced by $P$ at the centroid plus the couple $M = P e$.

---

**Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the kern of a section? State its significance. Find the kern of a solid circular section of diameter $d$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Kern of a Section:** It is the region around the centroid of the cross-section within which, if the entire compressive load is applied, **the stress across the whole section is of the same sign** (i.e., no tension develops on any fiber).
>
> **Significance:**
> - Critical for design of **brittle materials** (cast iron, brick, concrete) which fail in tension.
> - Used to determine **safe eccentricity limits** for column design.
>
> **Kern of Solid Circular Section (diameter $d$):**
>
> The limiting eccentricity is:
>
> $$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\pi d^{3}/32}{\pi d^{2}/4} = \frac{d}{8}$$
>
> Hence, the kern is a **circle of radius $d/8$** centered at the centroid.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **DO NOT** mix up the eccentricity $e$ with the lever arm — it is measured from the **centroid**, not from the near edge.
> - **DO NOT** forget units — $e$ in mm, $P$ in N, and final stress must be in MPa (N/mm²).
> - **Common Error:** Students forget to compute **both** $\sigma_{max}$ AND $\sigma_{min}$. KTU examiners **always check both** for full marks.
> - For **hollow sections**, use $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(d_o^4 - d_i^4)$ — confusing $d^4$ with $d^4 - d_i^4$ loses 4 marks instantly.
> - When load is **compressive** and eccentricity creates extra compression on the same side, **$\sigma_{max}$ is compressive**; the opposite side may show tension.

---

#### 🔹 OPTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A short hollow circular cast iron column has an **outer diameter of 200 mm** and an **inner diameter of 100 mm**. It carries an **eccentric compressive load of 250 kN** at an eccentricity of **50 mm**. Determine the **maximum and minimum stresses** in the section.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d_o = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad d_i = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 250 \text{ kN} = 250{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad e = 50 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(d_o^{2} - d_i^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(200^{2} - 100^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 10000) = 7500\pi \approx 23{,}561.94 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia:**

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(d_o^{4} - d_i^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\left[(200)^{4} - (100)^{4}\right]
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left[1.6 \times 10^{9} - 1 \times 10^{8}\right] = \frac{\pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{9}}{64} \approx 73.63 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{I}{d_o/2} = \frac{73.63 \times 10^{6}}{100} = 736{,}310 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Direct (Axial) Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{250{,}000}{23{,}561.94} = 10.61 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 5 — Bending Moment and Bending Stress:**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 250{,}000 \times 50 = 12.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^{6}}{736{,}310} = 16.98 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Stress (near side of eccentricity, both compressive):**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 10.61 + 16.98 = 27.59 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 7 — Minimum Stress (far side of eccentricity):**

$$
\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 10.61 - 16.98 = -6.37 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Stress | Value | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_{max}$ | $27.59 \text{ MPa}$ | Compressive |
| $\sigma_{min}$ | $6.37 \text{ MPa}$ | Tensile |

---

**(b)** Define the **kern of a section**. Derive an expression for the kern of a **rectangular section** of dimensions $b \times h$. A load is applied with eccentricity $e$ such that it just lies on the boundary of the kern. Sketch the stress distribution.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The kern of a section is the region around the centroid within which a compressive load must be applied if the stress over the entire section is to be of the **same sign** (no tension).

**Step 1 — Stress at extreme fiber (opposite to eccentricity):**
Let the load be applied at distance $e$ on the right of the centroid. To keep the entire section in compression, the **left extreme fiber** must have at least zero stress:

$$
\sigma_{left} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e_{max} \cdot (h/2)}{I} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $e_{max}$:**

$$
e_{max} = \frac{2 I}{A h} = \frac{2 \cdot (bh^{3}/12)}{(bh) \cdot h} = \frac{h}{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Result for Rectangular Section:**

The kern of a $b \times h$ rectangle is a **rhombus** (diamond shape) with:
- Vertices at $\pm h/6$ on the YY-axis (major axis).
- Vertices at $\pm b/6$ on the XX-axis (minor axis).

**Stress Distribution Sketch:**

```
        Linear bending stress (compressive on right, tensile on left)
              |←——— sigma_max (compressive) ———→|
              |                                 |
   ___________|_________________________________|___________
  |     σ_b (tensile)  ←——— P/A (compression) ———→ σ_b (compressive)  |
  |___________|_________________________________|___________|
              |                                 |
              |←——— sigma_min (zero on boundary) ————→|
              0 (neutral axis shifts here, no tension)
```

When $e = h/6$, $\sigma_{min} = 0$ on the left edge; for $e < h/6$, the entire section is in compression.

---

#### 🔹 OPTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **maximum and minimum stresses** in a **solid circular column** subjected to an eccentric compressive load. A solid circular cast iron column of diameter **150 mm** carries an **axial load of 100 kN** with an eccentricity of **25 mm**. Calculate the **stresses at the extreme fibers**.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (already in Section 3.3):**

For a solid circular column of diameter $d$ under eccentric load $P$ at eccentricity $e$:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}\left[1 + \frac{8e}{d}\right] \quad ; \quad \sigma_{min} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}\left[1 - \frac{8e}{d}\right]
$$

**Given:** $d = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 100{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad e = 25 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (150)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 22500}{4} \approx 17{,}671.46 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{\pi \cdot d^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot (150)^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3{,}375{,}000}{32} \approx 331{,}340.7 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Direct Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{d} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{100{,}000}{17{,}671.46} = 5.659 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment and Bending Stress:**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 100{,}000 \times 25 = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{6}}{331{,}340.7} = 7.546 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Stress (compressive, near side):**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{d} + \sigma_{b} = 5.659 + 7.546 = 13.205 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Stress (tensile, far side):**

$$
\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{d} - \sigma_{b} = 5.659 - 7.546 = -1.887 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Verification using compact formula:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{4 \times 100{,}000}{\pi \times (150)^{2}}\left[1 + \frac{8 \times 25}{150}\right] = 5.659 \times [1 + 1.333] = 5.659 \times 2.333 = 13.20 \text{ MPa} \;\checkmark
$$

---

**(b)** A **cylinder head** of an engine is fastened by **8 studs**. The cylinder bore diameter is **120 mm**, and the studs are located on a **bolt circle of diameter 160 mm**. The maximum gas pressure is **4 MPa**. Calculate the **diameter of the studs** assuming permissible tensile stress of **80 MPa**. Use the standard assumption for cylinder head design.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Gas Load on the Cylinder Head:**

$$
W = P_g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (120)^{2}
$$

$$
W = 4 \times 11{,}309.73 = 45{,}238.93 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Load Per Stud:**

$$
P = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{45{,}238.93}{8} = 5{,}654.87 \text{ N (axial load per stud)}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the Eccentricity:**
The gas pressure acts at the **center of the bore (radius = $D/2 = 60$ mm)**, while each stud lies on the bolt circle (radius = $D_b/2 = 80$ mm). The eccentricity is the **mean distance** (cone assumption):

$$
e = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{D_b}{2} - \frac{D}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{160}{2} - \frac{120}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(80 - 60) = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined Stress Equation:**
For a stud of core diameter $d_c$:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{4P}{\pi d_c^{2}} + \frac{32 M}{\pi d_c^{3}}
$$

Where $M = P \cdot e = 5{,}654.87 \times 10 = 56{,}548.7 \text{ N·mm}$.

**Step 5 — Substitute and Equate to Permissible Stress (80 MPa):**

$$
80 = \frac{4 \times 5{,}654.87}{\pi d_c^{2}} + \frac{32 \times 56{,}548.7}{\pi d_c^{3}}
$$

$$
80 = \frac{7{,}200}{d_c^{2}} + \frac{576{,}000}{d_c^{3}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve by Iteration (Trial-and-Error):**

*Try $d_c = 22 \text{ mm}$:*

$$
\frac{7200}{484} + \frac{576{,}000}{10648} = 14.876 + 54.094 = 68.97 \text{ MPa} \; (\text{too low})
$$

*Try $d_c = 21 \text{ mm}$:*

$$
\frac{7200}{441} + \frac{576{,}000}{9261} = 16.327 + 62.196 = 78.52 \text{ MPa} \; (\text{very close})
$$

*Try $d_c = 20.95 \text{ mm}$:*

$$
\frac{7200}{438.9} + \frac{576{,}000}{9192.7} = 16.41 + 62.66 = 79.07 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Adopt $d_c \approx 21 \text{ mm}$ (core diameter).**

Standard M22 stud has core diameter ≈ 18.376 mm — let's check:
$\dfrac{7200}{337.7} + \dfrac{576{,}000}{6202.4} = 21.32 + 92.87 = 114.19$ MPa (over-stressed).

Therefore, an **M24 stud** (core diameter ≈ 19.83 mm) is borderline; a **M27 stud** (core diameter ≈ 23.319 mm) is safe. Recommend **M27 studs**.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 MUST-KNOW POINTS FOR KTU 2024 EXAM**

- ✅ **Eccentric loading** = load $P$ at distance $e$ from centroid = **axial load + bending moment $M = Pe$**.

- ✅ The combined stress equation (memorize!):

$$
\sigma_{max/min} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}
$$

- ✅ **Section modulus** $Z = I/c$ — use this directly to save time.

- ✅ **Kern limits** ($e_{max} = Z/A$):
  - **Solid rectangle:** $h/6$ along major axis, $b/6$ along minor axis.
  - **Solid circle:** $d/8$ along any diameter.
  - **Hollow circle:** $\dfrac{d_o}{8}\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{d_i}{d_o}\right)^{2}\right]$.

- ✅ **Sign convention:**
  - $\sigma_{max}$ is on the **side of eccentricity** (same sign as direct stress).
  - $\sigma_{min}$ is on the **opposite side** (may be tensile if $e$ is large).

- ✅ **Design for brittle materials** (cast iron, masonry): Keep $e$ within the kern → **no tension**.

- ✅ **Design for ductile materials** (mild steel): Both $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ acceptable; check against allowable using **maximum shear stress / principal stress theory**.

- ✅ **Cylinder head design**: Use equivalent eccentricity = $\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{D_b}{2} - \dfrac{D}{2}\right)$ — this is the **standard KTU assumption**.

- ✅ **Common mistakes:**
  - Using $d$ instead of $d^4$ in hollow section formulas.
  - Forgetting to convert kN to N (factor of 1000!).
  - Reporting only $\sigma_{max}$ — examiners want both.

- ✅ **Numerical Practice Strategy:**
  1. Calculate $A$, $I$, $Z$.
  2. Compute $\sigma_d = P/A$.
  3. Compute $M = P \cdot e$, then $\sigma_b = M/Z$.
  4. Add/subtract to get $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$.
  5. Check against allowable stress.

- ✅ **Diagrams to practice drawing in exam:**
  1. Eccentric load + equivalent force-couple system.
  2. Stress distribution block diagram across section.
  3. Kern boundary for rectangular and circular sections.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** Always **box your final numerical answer** and **underline the units** (MPa or N/mm²). Partial marks are awarded even if intermediate steps have errors — so **show every step clearly**!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Screws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Screws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Screw?

A **Power Screw** is a mechanical device that converts **rotary motion into linear motion** (and transmits power) in order to carry an axial load. It is essentially a large, heavily loaded screw-and-nut assembly designed deliberately to **transmit power**, rather than to hold or clamp objects (which is the role of a "fastening screw").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** A power screw (also called a *lead screw* or *transmission screw*) is a screw used to transmit power, as in machine tool lead screws, screw jacks, presses, and similar devices where heavy loads must be moved slowly over considerable distances.

**Core functions of a power screw:**
- **To convert rotation → translation** (raises/lowers a load).
- **To transmit power** from a rotating source to a linearly moving load.
- **To amplify force** (a small torque on the screw can lift very heavy loads).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **car scissor jack**. You turn a small handle (rotary motion) and the car (a massive load) rises smoothly upward. The force you apply at the handle is tiny compared to the weight of the car — this *force amplification* is the magic of a power screw.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it amplifies force:** A small tangential force acting at a large radius (the handle) produces a torque. The screw thread geometry converts that torque into a very large axial force along the screw axis. The trade-off is **speed**: you must rotate the handle many times to lift the car a few centimeters.

**Other everyday examples:**
- **CNC machine lead screw** – moves the cutting tool precisely.
- **Bench vice** spindle – clamps workpieces.
- **Aeroplane wing flaps / landing gear actuators** – hydraulic-assisted or screw-driven.
- **Injection moulding clamp** – generates huge clamping force.
- **C-clamps, G-clamps, toggle clamps** in workshops.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Power Screws

Power screws are classified by the **shape of the thread profile**:

| # | Type of Thread | Profile Sketch Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Square Thread** | Profile is a perfect rectangle (0° thread angle). | Highest efficiency; used in screw jacks, presses, valve stems. |
| 2 | **Acme Thread** | Trapezoidal, **29° included thread angle**. | Machine tool lead screws – easily machinable, robust. |
| 3 | **Buttress Thread** | One flank is perpendicular to the axis, the other is sloped (45°). | Heavy unidirectional loads (e.g., gun breech, heavy presses). |
| 4 | **V-thread (B.S.W. / Metric)** | 60° included angle; ISO metric. | Rarely used as power screw (low efficiency, hard to back-out under load). |
| 5 | **Ball / Recirculating Screw** | Uses steel balls between screw and nut. | Low-friction, high-efficiency applications (CNC, robotics). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why square threads are theoretical favorites but Acme threads are practical favorites:** The square thread is the most efficient and gives the largest bearing area, but it is **impossible to cut with a standard die or single-point tool on a lathe** (no clearance on the flanks). The Acme thread, with its 29° angle, *can* be cut cleanly on a lathe and is therefore the industrial standard. The buttress thread is used when load is one-sided (e.g., a press) and you need both high efficiency and a strong tooth.

---

## 1.4 Thread Terminology (Must-Know Vocabulary)

![Terminology Reference] — these symbols will appear in every KTU question.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $d$ | Nominal (Major) diameter of the screw | mm |
| $d_c$ | Core (Minor / Root) diameter | mm |
| $d_m$ | Mean diameter of the thread $= (d + d_c)/2$ | mm |
| $L$ | **Lead** – distance advanced per revolution | mm |
| $p$ | **Pitch** – distance between adjacent threads | mm |
| $n$ | Number of starts (single = 1, double = 2) | – |
| $\alpha$ | **Helix angle at mean diameter** | rad or ° |
| $\phi$ | **Angle of friction** in the thread, $\tan\phi = \mu$ | rad or ° |
| $\phi_1$ | **Angle of thread friction** $\tan\phi_1 = \mu_1 = \sec\alpha \cdot \mu / \cos\beta_n$ (for Acme) | rad or ° |
| $\beta_n$ | Half the thread angle (Acme: 14.5°, Square: 0°) | rad or ° |
| $W$ | Axial load on the screw | N |
| $F$ | Tangential force at mean radius | N |
| $P_c$ | Collar friction radius / collar mean radius | mm |
| $T$ | Applied torque | N·mm |
| $T_0$ | Torque required to overcome thread friction only | N·mm |
| $T_1$ | Torque required to overcome collar friction only | N·mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial identity:** $L = n \times p$ and $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi\, d_m}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Force Analysis on an Unwrapped Thread (The "Inclined Plane" Model)

The most elegant way to analyze a power screw is to **imagine unwrapping one thread** from the cylindrical screw surface and laying it flat as an **inclined plane** (a right triangle). The base of the triangle equals $\pi d_m$ (one full turn at the mean diameter) and the height equals the **lead $L$**.

**Forces on the thread (raising a load):**
1. **Axial load $W$** – acts down along the screw axis (perpendicular to the base).
2. **Tangential force $F$** – acts horizontally along the base, equal to $2T / d_m$ (the force the user applies at the mean radius).
3. **Normal reaction $R_n$** – perpendicular to the inclined surface.
4. **Friction force $\mu_1 R_n$** – opposite to impending motion, along the inclined surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "unwrapping" works:** A helical thread and an inclined plane share *exactly* the same geometry, the same normal force, and the same friction behaviour. Unwrapping is therefore not an approximation — it is a geometrically exact transformation. The only adjustment needed is the **thread-angle correction** for non-square threads, which modifies the effective friction coefficient.

---

## 2.2 Equilibrium of the Inclined Plane (Square Thread)

Resolving forces **parallel and perpendicular to the inclined plane** (so that friction $\mu_1 R_n$ and normal $R_n$ act along convenient axes):

For **raising the load (motion up the incline):**
$$F = W \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$$

For **lowering the load (motion down the incline):**
$$F = W \tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$$

Multiplying both sides by $d_m/2$ converts tangential force $F$ to applied torque $T$:

$$\boxed{T_R = \frac{W\, d_m}{2}\,\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}$$

$$\boxed{T_L = \frac{W\, d_m}{2}\,\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The minus sign inside $\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$ is the most-forgotten step in KTU papers.** If $\alpha > \phi_1$, the screw will *back-drive* (load will descend uncontrollably) and $T_L$ becomes negative — meaning you must *restrain* the screw (apply a braking torque) rather than help it. This is the **self-locking** phenomenon.

---

## 2.3 Collar (Thrust Bearing) Friction

In addition to thread friction, the **thrust collar** that supports the axial load also carries friction. A separate collar torque is required:

$$T_{collar} = \mu_c\, W\, r_c$$

where $r_c$ is the **mean radius of the collar**, given by $r_c = \dfrac{r_o + r_i}{4}\cdot 2 = \dfrac{r_o + r_i}{2}$ — no, more carefully:

For a flat collar with outer radius $r_o$ and inner radius $r_i$, integrating uniform pressure over the annular area gives:

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{r_o^{\,3} - r_i^{\,3}}{r_o^{\,2} - r_i^{\,2}}}$$

For a **solid collar** ($r_i = 0$): $r_c = \dfrac{2}{3} r_o$.

---

## 2.4 Total Torque, Efficiency, and Self-Locking

| Quantity | Formula (with collar) | Formula (without collar) |
|---|---|---|
| Torque to **raise** | $T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) + \mu_c W r_c$ | $T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$ |
| Torque to **lower** | $T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) + \mu_c W r_c$ | $T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$ |
| **Efficiency** $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{W L}{2\pi T_R}$ (overall) | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}$ (thread only) |
| **Self-locking** | $\alpha \le \phi_1$ (load will not back-drive) | same |

**Derivation of the efficiency formula (without collar):**
Work output per revolution $= W \cdot L$ (the load moves up by one lead).
Work input per revolution $= 2\pi T_R = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) = \pi W d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$.
Since $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$, we have $L = \pi d_m \tan\alpha$, so:

$$\eta = \frac{W L}{\pi W d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum efficiency** of a square-thread screw (with no collar) occurs at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$ and equals $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$. This is rarely achievable in practice because it requires an impractically coarse thread.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet) — Print This!

> All symbols use $\mu = \tan\phi$, $\mu_1 = \sec\alpha \cdot \mu / \cos\beta_n$, and $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$.

| # | Governing Equation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}$ | Find lead angle from geometry. |
| 2 | $\phi_1 = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{\cos\beta_n}\right)$ for Acme; $\phi_1 = \phi$ for square. | Effective friction angle including thread angle. |
| 3 | $T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) + \mu_c W r_c$ | Torque to **raise** load (screw jack lift). |
| 4 | $T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) + \mu_c W r_c$ | Torque to **lower** load. |
| 5 | $\eta_{raise} = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}$ (no collar) | Efficiency of power screw. |
| 6 | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$ | Theoretical peak efficiency. |
| 7 | **Self-locking:** $\alpha \le \phi_1$ | Will the load descend by itself? |
| 8 | $T_{collar} = \mu_c W r_c$ | Torque lost in thrust collar. |
| 9 | $r_c = \dfrac{2}{3}\dfrac{r_o^{\,3}-r_i^{\,3}}{r_o^{\,2}-r_i^{\,2}}$ | Mean collar radius (flat collar). |
| 10 | $F = \dfrac{W}{\cos\beta_n}\left[\cos\alpha - \mu\sin\alpha\right] / \left[\cos\alpha + \mu\sin\alpha\right]$ type | Force on Acme thread flank (for stress). |

---

## 2.6 Design Stresses in a Power Screw

A power screw in service is subjected to:
- **Direct compressive stress** due to axial load $W$.
- **Torsional shear stress** due to applied torque $T$.
- **Shear stress at the thread root** (bending of thread).
- **Bearing (crushing) stress** on the thread flank.
- **Buckling** if the screw is long and slender.

**Nominal shear stress in body of screw (combined torsion + compression):**
$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d_c^{\,3}}$$

**Combined maximum shear (using principal-stress / equivalent-shaft approach):**
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(2\tau)^2 + (4\sigma_c)^2} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + (2\sigma_c)^2}, \quad \sigma_c = \frac{4W}{\pi d_c^{\,2}}$$

**Bearing (crushing) pressure on thread surface** (square thread, $n$ threads in contact):
$$p_b = \frac{W}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}(d^2 - d_c^{\,2})} = \frac{W}{\pi d_m \cdot t \cdot n}$$
where $t = (d - d_c)/2$ is the thread depth, and $n$ is the number of threads in contact (typically take $n = H/p$ where $H$ is nut height).

**Bending stress on the thread (treating the thread as a cantilever):**
$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6\, F_a}{t^{\,2}}$$
where $F_a$ is the axial component of the load on one thread flank, and $t$ is the thread depth.

---

## 2.7 Why Power Screws are Critically Important in Engineering

- **Force amplification:** a 100 N handle force can lift 10 000 N easily → **mechanical advantage 100**.
- **Slow, controlled motion:** ideal for jacking, clamping, pressing.
- **Self-locking capability:** a screw jack will not unwind under load — built-in safety.
- **Back-drive avoidance in vertical motion:** critical for safety in lifts and presses.
- **Positioning accuracy:** every rotation is a precise linear step (used in CNC lead screws).

> [!NOTE]
> **The down-side:** Power screws are **inherently inefficient** (30%–50% typical) due to sliding friction. For high-speed, high-efficiency motion, the **ball screw** is preferred — but ball screws are NOT in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque Required to Raise a Load (Square Thread, with Collar Friction)

### Step 1 — Unwrap the thread

Unwrap one turn of the square thread onto a flat plane. The resulting **inclined plane** has:
- **Base length** $= \pi d_m$ (one revolution at mean diameter).
- **Height** $= L$ (the lead).
- **Slope angle** $= \alpha$ (the lead angle at mean diameter), so $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$.

### Step 2 — Identify all forces on the thread surface

When the screw rotates and the load is being raised, the thread is on the verge of moving **up the incline** (relative to the nut). The forces on a small element of the thread flank are:
- $W$ — axial load, **perpendicular to the base** (pointing down).
- $F$ — tangential force at mean radius, **along the base** (driving motion).
- $R_n$ — normal reaction, **perpendicular to the inclined surface**.
- $\mu_1 R_n$ — friction force, **along the inclined surface, opposing motion** (pointing down the incline).

### Step 3 — Resolve forces into axes parallel and perpendicular to the inclined surface

Let $x$ be along the inclined surface (up-the-slope = +) and $y$ be perpendicular to it (outward = +).

Resolution of $W$ (which is vertical, i.e. along the original screw axis):
- Component along $+x$ (up-slope) $= W \sin\alpha$
- Component along $+y$ (away from surface) $= W \cos\alpha$

Resolution of $F$ (which is horizontal):
- Component along $+x$ (up-slope) $= F \cos\alpha$
- Component along $+y$ (away from surface) $= F \sin\alpha$

(If you prefer, use the equivalent method: sum forces parallel and perpendicular to the plane.)

### Step 4 — Apply equilibrium along the inclined surface

Since the element is on the verge of slipping up the plane, the driving tangential force component must overcome both the load component and friction:

$$F \cos\alpha = W \sin\alpha + \mu_1 R_n$$

### Step 5 — Apply equilibrium perpendicular to the inclined surface

Normal reaction $R_n$ supports the inward components of $W$ and $F$:

$$R_n = W \cos\alpha - F \sin\alpha$$

### Step 6 — Eliminate $R_n$

Substitute Step 5 into Step 4:

$$F \cos\alpha = W \sin\alpha + \mu_1 (W \cos\alpha - F \sin\alpha)$$

Expand:

$$F \cos\alpha = W \sin\alpha + \mu_1 W \cos\alpha - \mu_1 F \sin\alpha$$

Group the $F$ terms on the left:

$$F \cos\alpha + \mu_1 F \sin\alpha = W \sin\alpha + \mu_1 W \cos\alpha$$

$$F (\cos\alpha + \mu_1 \sin\alpha) = W (\sin\alpha + \mu_1 \cos\alpha)$$

$$F = W\,\dfrac{\sin\alpha + \mu_1 \cos\alpha}{\cos\alpha + \mu_1 \sin\alpha}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to tangent-of-sum form

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\alpha$:

$$F = W\,\dfrac{\tan\alpha + \mu_1}{1 - \mu_1 \tan\alpha}$$

Using the identity $\dfrac{a+b}{1-ab} = \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$ where $\mu_1 = \tan\phi_1$:

$$F = W \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$$

### Step 8 — Convert force to torque

The force $F$ acts at the mean radius $d_m/2$, so the **torque on the thread** is:

$$T_0 = F \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} = \dfrac{W\, d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$$

### Step 9 — Add collar friction

The thrust collar experiences sliding friction $\mu_c$ at mean collar radius $r_c$:

$$T_{collar} = \mu_c\, W\, r_c$$

### Step 10 — Total torque to raise the load

$$\boxed{T_R = \dfrac{W\, d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) + \mu_c\, W\, r_c}$$

> **Done!** This is the canonical KTU formula. It carries full marks in ESE whenever correctly derived.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Required to Lower a Load

When lowering, the load tends to **back-drive** the screw (move it down the incline), so friction now **helps** the tangential force.

The only sign change is in the friction term on the inclined plane:
- Friction $\mu_1 R_n$ now points **up the incline** (it opposes the load's back-driving motion).

Re-derive Steps 4–7 with this sign flip:

$$F \cos\alpha + \mu_1 F \sin\alpha = W \sin\alpha - \mu_1 W \cos\alpha$$

Wait — let us redo this carefully. The equilibrium in the $x$ direction (still along inclined surface) now reads:

*Driving force* = $F \cos\alpha$ (component of $F$ up-slope) is partially cancelled by friction, but the load is also trying to push the screw down-slope.

Sum of forces in $x$ = 0 (the screw is in quasi-static equilibrium while lowering):

$$F \cos\alpha + \mu_1 R_n = W \sin\alpha$$

The normal equilibrium (Step 5) is unchanged because $F$ and $W$ are still the same applied forces:

$$R_n = W \cos\alpha - F \sin\alpha$$

Substitute:

$$F \cos\alpha + \mu_1(W \cos\alpha - F \sin\alpha) = W \sin\alpha$$

$$F \cos\alpha + \mu_1 W \cos\alpha - \mu_1 F \sin\alpha = W \sin\alpha$$

$$F(\cos\alpha - \mu_1 \sin\alpha) = W(\sin\alpha - \mu_1 \cos\alpha)$$

$$F = W\,\dfrac{\sin\alpha - \mu_1 \cos\alpha}{\cos\alpha - \mu_1 \sin\alpha} = W \tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T_L = \dfrac{W\, d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) + \mu_c\, W\, r_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The condition $T_L = 0$ gives self-locking:** $\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = \phi_1$. If $\alpha < \phi_1$, the load will not descend by itself (positive $T_L$ means you still have to push it down). If $\alpha > \phi_1$, $T_L < 0$ and the load drives the screw — the system is **non-self-locking**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency of a Power Screw (without collar)

Work output per revolution = load × distance moved per revolution:
$$W_{out} = W \cdot L$$

Work input per revolution = torque × angle turned in one revolution:
$$W_{in} = T_R \cdot 2\pi$$

For the simple case of square thread, no collar:
$$T_R = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$$

So:
$$W_{in} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) = \pi W d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$$

Therefore:
$$\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \frac{W L}{\pi W d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} = \frac{L}{\pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}$$

Since $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$, we have $L = \pi d_m \tan\alpha$. Substituting:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi d_m \tan\alpha}{\pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} = \boxed{\dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Self-Locking Condition

For the load to **not** back-drive, the torque required to lower must be **positive** (you still have to push it down). With zero collar friction and square thread:

$$T_L = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$$

If $\phi_1 - \alpha < 0$, then $\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) < 0$ and $T_L < 0$ → load descends by itself.

If $\phi_1 - \alpha = 0$, $T_L = 0$ → marginal (the screw will not unwind, but a small disturbance will release it).

If $\phi_1 - \alpha > 0$, $T_L > 0$ → **self-locking**.

So:

$$\boxed{\text{Self-locking if} \;\; \alpha \le \phi_1 \;\; \Leftrightarrow \;\; \tan\alpha \le \mu_1 = \tan\phi_1}$$

In terms of geometry (using $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$ and $\mu_1 = \mu/\cos\beta_n$):

$$\frac{L}{\pi d_m} \le \frac{\mu}{\cos\beta_n}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Maximum Efficiency of a Square-Thread Screw

Take $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)}$ and maximize w.r.t. $\alpha$.

Set $\dfrac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = 0$. Using $\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) = \dfrac{\tan\alpha + \tan\phi_1}{1 - \tan\alpha \tan\phi_1}$, write:

$$\eta(\alpha) = \frac{\tan\alpha\,(1 - \tan\alpha \tan\phi_1)}{\tan\alpha + \tan\phi_1}$$

Differentiate and set to zero. The result is:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{1 - \sqrt{\cos\phi_1}\,\sqrt{2 - \cos\phi_1\,\cdot\ldots}}{\sin\phi_1}$$

A more elegant route: take $\dfrac{d}{d\alpha}\ln\eta = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{\sin\alpha\cos\alpha} - \frac{1 + \tan^2(\alpha + \phi_1)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} = 0$$

After trigonometric simplification:

$$\cos(2\alpha + \phi_1) = -\sin\phi_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\alpha + \phi_1 = 90° + \phi_1/2 \quad \text{(principal branch)}$$

Wait — redoing the simplification correctly:

$$\boxed{\alpha_{opt} = 45° - \frac{\phi_1}{2}}$$

Substitute back into the efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\tan(45° - \phi_1/2)}{\tan(45° - \phi_1/2 + \phi_1)} = \frac{\tan(45° - \phi_1/2)}{\tan(45° + \phi_1/2)}$$

Using $\tan(45° \pm \theta) = \dfrac{1 \pm \tan\theta}{1 \mp \tan\theta}$ with $\tan(\phi_1/2) = \dfrac{\sin\phi_1}{1 + \cos\phi_1}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi_1}{1 + \sin\phi_1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design hint (KTU favorite):** A square-thread screw with $\mu = 0.08$ has $\phi \approx 4.6°$ → $\eta_{max} = (1 - 0.08)/(1 + 0.08) \approx 0.852$. So you can never get more than ~85% efficiency in a square-thread screw under realistic friction values.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Bearing (Crushing) Stress in the Threads

The axial load $W$ is transmitted through the **thread flanks** in contact with the nut. For a square thread, the contact area is rectangular:
- Length of contact along the axis $= n \cdot p = H$ (nut height).
- Width of contact = $\pi d_m$ (circumference at mean diameter).

Wait — more carefully, the **projected bearing area** of a single thread is $\pi d_m \cdot t$ where $t = (d - d_c)/2$ is the thread depth. For $n$ threads in contact, total bearing area $A_b = n \cdot \pi d_m \cdot t$.

So:

$$\boxed{p_b = \frac{W}{A_b} = \frac{W}{n \cdot \pi d_m \cdot t} = \frac{4W}{n \pi (d^2 - d_c^{\,2})}}$$

For an Acme thread, the projected area must be reduced by the **cosine of the thread half-angle** $\beta_n$ (because the flank is sloped):

$$A_b = n \cdot \pi d_m \cdot t \cdot \cos\beta_n$$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Torsional and Combined Stresses in the Screw Core

The screw body at the **root diameter $d_c$** is subjected to:
- **Direct compressive stress:** $\sigma_c = \dfrac{W}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d_c^{\,2}} = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d_c^{\,2}}$
- **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau = \dfrac{T_R}{\dfrac{\pi}{16} d_c^{\,3}} = \dfrac{16 T_R}{\pi d_c^{\,3}}$

These combine according to the **maximum shear stress theory** (most common in KTU):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_c^{\,2} + 4\tau^{\,2}}$$

Or in the form used in many KTU textbooks (Khurmi/Gupta, Shigley):

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + \left(\frac{\sigma_c}{2}\right)^2 \cdot 4} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + \sigma_c^{\,2}}$$

Wait — I need to be careful. The two are equivalent in form. The **distortion energy / von Mises** equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_c^{\,2} + 3\tau^2}$$

A common KTU substitution (which yields the same kind of check) is:

$$\tau_{max,\,combined} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_c^{\,2} + 4\tau^2} \le \tau_{allow}$$

---

## 3.8 Work-Kit: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **[Worked Example — typical 14-mark KTU problem]**
> A screw jack is required to lift a load of **60 kN**. The screw is a square thread with **mean diameter 50 mm**, **lead 12 mm**, and the coefficient of thread friction is **0.12**. A collar of outer diameter 80 mm and inner diameter 40 mm supports the load, with collar friction coefficient **0.10**. Find:
> 1. The torque required to lift the load.
> 2. The torque required to lower the load.
> 3. The efficiency.
> 4. Whether the screw is self-locking.

**Given:**
- $W = 60\,000$ N
- $d_m = 50$ mm
- $L = 12$ mm
- $\mu = \mu_1 = 0.12$ (square thread, so $\phi_1 = \phi$, $\beta_n = 0$)
- $r_o = 40$ mm, $r_i = 20$ mm
- $\mu_c = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Helix angle:**
$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{\pi d_m}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{12}{\pi \cdot 50}\right) = \arctan(0.07639) = 4.37°$$

**Step 2 — Friction angle:**
$$\phi_1 = \arctan(0.12) = 6.843°$$

**Step 3 — Collar mean radius:**
$$r_c = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{r_o^{\,3} - r_i^{\,3}}{r_o^{\,2} - r_i^{\,2}} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{40^3 - 20^3}{40^2 - 20^2} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{64\,000 - 8\,000}{1\,600 - 400} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{56\,000}{1\,200} = 31.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Torque to raise (thread part):**
$$T_{0,R} = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) = \frac{60\,000 \times 50}{2}\tan(4.37° + 6.843°)$$
$$= 1\,500\,000 \times \tan(11.213°) = 1\,500\,000 \times 0.1982 = 297\,300 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5 — Torque to raise (collar part):**
$$T_{collar} = \mu_c W r_c = 0.10 \times 60\,000 \times 31.11 = 186\,660 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 6 — Total torque to raise:**
$$T_R = 297\,300 + 186\,660 = 483\,960 \text{ N·mm} \approx 484 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7 — Torque to lower (thread part):**
$$T_{0,L} = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) = 1\,500\,000 \times \tan(6.843° - 4.37°)$$
$$= 1\,500\,000 \times \tan(2.473°) = 1\,500\,000 \times 0.04318 = 64\,770 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 8 — Total torque to lower:**
$$T_L = 64\,770 + 186\,660 = 251\,430 \text{ N·mm} \approx 251 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 9 — Efficiency (overall, including collar):**
$$\eta = \frac{W L}{2\pi T_R} = \frac{60\,000 \times 12}{2\pi \times 483\,960} = \frac{720\,000}{3\,041\,113} = 0.2368 \approx 23.7\%$$

If we exclude the collar (thread efficiency only):
$$\eta_{thread} = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} = \frac{\tan 4.37°}{\tan 11.213°} = \frac{0.07639}{0.1982} = 0.3854 \approx 38.5\%$$

**Step 10 — Self-locking check:**
$$\alpha = 4.37° < \phi_1 = 6.84° \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Self-locking ✓}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note how dramatically the collar destroys efficiency:** thread efficiency is 38.5%, but with the collar included it drops to 23.7%. This is a common "trick" in KTU problems — students forget to include collar friction in the denominator of the efficiency formula and lose 2–3 marks.
