# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map: Components of a Power Screw System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Rotary Motion] --> B[Handle / Torque Source]
    B --> C[Screw Shaft with Threads]
    C --> D[Nut - Translates axially]
    D --> E[Thrust Collar]
    E --> F[Axial Load W - Linear Output]
    C --> G[Friction in Threads]
    E --> H[Friction in Collar]
    G --> I[Energy Loss - Heat]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Mechanical Advantage 100x]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power enters as rotary motion at the handle, the screw's helical threads transform it into axial motion of the nut, the nut pushes the load (via the thrust collar) linearly, and the two friction sites (thread + collar) dissipate energy as heat. The system trades input *speed × torque* for output *force × slow speed*.

---

## 4.2 Unwrapped Thread as an Inclined Plane (Force Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base pi times dm - One full turn] --> B[Slope Angle alpha - Lead helix angle]
    B --> C[Vertical height L - Lead]
    A --> D[F - Tangential force]
    C --> E[W - Axial load]
    D --> F[F cos alpha - Up-slope component]
    E --> G[W sin alpha - Up-slope component]
    F --> H[Friction mu1 Rn - Down-slope]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Net Driving Force equals Friction]
    I --> J[Tan alpha plus phi1 identity]
    J --> K[Result: F equals W tan alpha plus phi1]
```

This block diagram traces the *logical steps* of Section 3.1 — from geometric unwrapping, to force resolution, to the final tangent-of-sum identity.

---

## 4.3 Thread Profile Comparison (Square vs Acme vs Buttress vs V-Thread)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread Profile Selection] --> B{Single or Reversible Load?}
    B -- Unidirectional --> C[Buttress Thread - 45 deg flank]
    B -- Reversible --> D{Manufacturing Priority?}
    D -- Efficiency first --> E[Square Thread - 0 deg - Best efficiency]
    D -- Manufacturability first --> F[Acme Thread - 29 deg - Standard choice]
    B -- Quick clamping only --> G[V-Thread - 60 deg - Low efficiency]
    A --> H[Application Examples]
    H --> I[Screw Jack - Square or Acme]
    H --> J[Press - Buttress]
    H --> K[Lead Screw of Lathe - Acme]
    H --> L[Clamp - V or Acme]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A design engineer first asks whether the load is unidirectional (e.g., a press) or reversible (e.g., a screw jack that must raise and lower). Then they trade off efficiency vs ease of machining. The Acme thread is the "safe default" for almost all general-purpose power screws.

---

## 4.4 Force Resolution on the Inclined Plane (Text-Art Schematic)

```
                     ^ +x (up-slope)
                    /|
                   / |
                  /  | W sin(alpha)   W cos(alpha)
                 /   |    ↓                ↓
                /    |            
               /     |            
              /      |            
             / R_n   |   F cos(alpha)            +y (out of plane)
            /        |    →                  ↗
           /_________|______________________________
           ↑ μ1 R_n acts here, opposing motion (down-slope ←)
            
    The inclined plane is unwrapped: base = π·dm, height = L.
    Normal reaction R_n is perpendicular to the slope.
    Friction μ1·R_n is along the slope, pointing down-slope (since load is rising).
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in an exam:** Draw the inclined-plane free-body diagram in the margin. Mark $W$ vertically downward, $F$ horizontally, $R_n$ perpendicular to the slope, and friction along the slope opposite the direction of motion. Resolve into $x$ and $y$ axes along the plane. The full marks come from the diagram alone, even if the algebra goes wrong — examiners award method marks generously for a clear sketch.

---

## 4.5 Free-Body Diagram of the Threaded Connection (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LOAD
        L1[Axial Load W applied downward on screw head]
    end
    subgraph THREAD
        T1[Threads of mean diameter dm]
        T2[Normal Reaction Rn on flank]
        T3[Friction force mu1 Rn along flank]
    end
    subgraph COLLAR
        C1[Collar of outer radius ro]
        C2[Collar of inner radius ri]
        C3[Friction torque mu_c W rc]
    end
    subgraph SCREW_CORE
        S1[Direct stress sigma_c from W]
        S2[Torsional stress tau from T_R]
        S3[Combined equivalent stress at d_c]
    end
    L1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    T3 -.feeds into.-> S2
    L1 -.feeds into.-> S1
```

This diagram represents the **stress flow path** in a power screw: the load $W$ produces direct stress in the screw core, the thread friction produces torque which produces torsional stress, and the combined stress at the root diameter $d_c$ is what must be compared to allowable values.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper 1]**
**Define a power screw. List any four applications of power screws used in machine design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A power screw is a mechanical device that converts rotary motion into linear motion and transmits power, while carrying a substantial axial load. Unlike fastening screws (which are preloaded once), power screws are designed to operate continuously and to handle very heavy loads slowly.

**Four applications (any four, 0.5 mark each, 2 marks total):**
1. Screw jacks (vehicle lifting).
2. Lead screws of machine tools (lathes, milling machines).
3. C-clamps and G-clamps.
4. Valves in pipe fittings (rising-stem gate valves).
5. Aircraft landing-gear actuators.
6. Injection-moulding clamping units.
7. Coining and embossing presses.

> **Mark-split:** 1 mark for correct definition, 0.5 mark × 4 for applications.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "self-locking" of a power screw? Derive the condition for self-locking of a square-threaded screw.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A power screw is said to be *self-locking* if it does not undergo back-driving — that is, the axial load will not rotate the screw and descend by itself when the applied torque is removed. This is essential for vertical applications such as screw jacks, lifts, and supports.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

For a square-threaded screw, the tangential force to lower the load is:

$$F_L = W \tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$$

For the load to be unable to drive the screw backwards, we need $F_L \ge 0$ (or $T_L \ge 0$), i.e.

$$\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) \ge 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_1 \ge \alpha$$

**Self-locking condition:** $\alpha \le \phi_1$, or equivalently, $\tan\alpha \le \mu_1$.

For a square thread ($\mu_1 = \mu$): $\tan\alpha \le \mu$, i.e. the lead angle must be less than the friction angle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two questions are asked in Part B, and the student answers **any ONE**. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. We provide TWO independent alternatives below so you can practice both.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive an expression for the **torque required to raise a load $W$ in a square-threaded screw jack**, taking into account **both thread friction and collar friction**. State the assumption clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square-threaded screw jack has the following specifications: **mean diameter = 60 mm, lead = 15 mm, coefficient of thread friction = 0.10, coefficient of collar friction = 0.12, collar outer diameter = 100 mm, collar inner diameter = 50 mm.** The screw jack has to lift a load of **80 kN**. Calculate:
1. The torque required to lift the load.
2. The torque required to lower the load.
3. The efficiency of the screw jack (overall).
4. State whether the screw is self-locking. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- (1 mark) **Sketch:** unwrapped inclined plane with $W$ vertically down, $F$ horizontally, $R_n$ perpendicular to slope, $\mu_1 R_n$ along slope opposing motion.
- (1 mark) **Resolve forces** along and perpendicular to the slope.
- (2 marks) **Algebra** to eliminate $R_n$ and reach $F = W \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$.
- (1 mark) **Convert** to torque $T_0 = W d_m/2 \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$.
- (1 mark) **Add collar term** $T_{collar} = \mu_c W r_c$ and state final expression.
- (1 mark) **Assumption** (e.g., uniform pressure, square thread so $\beta_n = 0$, quasi-static motion).

Final expression:
$$T_R = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) + \mu_c W r_c$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute helix angle** (1 mark):
$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{\pi d_m}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{15}{\pi \cdot 60}\right) = \arctan(0.0796) = 4.55°$$

**Step 2: Friction angle** (0.5 mark):
$$\phi_1 = \arctan(0.10) = 5.71°$$

**Step 3: Collar mean radius** (1 mark):
$$r_c = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{50^3 - 25^3}{50^2 - 25^2} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{125\,000 - 15\,625}{2500 - 625} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{109\,375}{1875} = 38.89 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Torque to raise** (1.5 marks):
$$T_R = \frac{80\,000 \times 60}{2}\tan(4.55° + 5.71°) + 0.12 \times 80\,000 \times 38.89$$
$$= 2\,400\,000 \times \tan(10.26°) + 373\,333$$
$$= 2\,400\,000 \times 0.1810 + 373\,333 = 434\,400 + 373\,333 = 807\,733 \text{ N·mm} \approx 808 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Torque to lower** (1 mark):
$$T_L = \frac{80\,000 \times 60}{2}\tan(5.71° - 4.55°) + 0.12 \times 80\,000 \times 38.89$$
$$= 2\,400\,000 \times \tan(1.16°) + 373\,333 = 2\,400\,000 \times 0.02025 + 373\,333$$
$$= 48\,600 + 373\,333 = 421\,933 \text{ N·mm} \approx 422 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Overall efficiency** (1 mark):
$$\eta = \frac{W L}{2\pi T_R} = \frac{80\,000 \times 15}{2\pi \times 807\,733} = \frac{1\,200\,000}{5\,075\,129} = 0.2364 \approx 23.6\%$$

**Step 7: Self-locking** (0.5 mark):
$$\alpha = 4.55° < \phi_1 = 5.71° \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Self-locking ✓}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (7-mark subpart):**
> - **Forgetting the collar term** in $T_R$ and $T_L$ → lose **2 marks**.
> - **Using the wrong collar radius formula** ($r_c = (r_o + r_i)/2$ is WRONG; the correct formula is the $(2/3) \cdot (r_o^3 - r_i^3)/(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ form) → lose **1.5 marks**.
> - **Computing efficiency with the wrong denominator** (using thread torque only instead of total torque) → lose **1 mark**.
> - **Not stating self-locking** explicitly with a numerical comparison → lose **0.5 mark**.
> - **Mixing up the sign** in $\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$ for lowering torque → lose **1.5 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper 2]**

**(a)** What is the **efficiency of a power screw**? Derive the expression for the efficiency of a square-threaded power screw **without collar friction**. Show that the **maximum efficiency** occurs at a helix angle $\alpha = 45° - \phi_1/2$, and that $\eta_{max} = (1 - \sin\phi_1)/(1 + \sin\phi_1)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine-tool lead screw is a **single-start ACME thread** with **nominal (major) diameter 40 mm**, **pitch 8 mm**, **mean diameter 36 mm**, and a **thread half-angle $\beta_n = 14.5°$**. The coefficient of friction between threads is **0.08**, and the axial force to be transmitted is **25 kN**. Compute:
1. The **helix angle** and the **effective friction angle** $\phi_1$.
2. The **torque required to advance** the tool.
3. The **bearing pressure** on the thread flank if **6 threads are in contact** and the thread depth $t = 2$ mm.
4. The **self-locking** status. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- (1 mark) **Definition:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work output per rev}}{\text{Work input per rev}} = \dfrac{W L}{2\pi T_R}$.
- (1.5 marks) **Derivation** of $\eta = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$ (substitute $T_R$ expression and use $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$).
- (1.5 marks) **Differentiation** $d\eta/d\alpha = 0$ leads to $\cos(2\alpha + \phi_1) = -\sin\phi_1$ (or equivalent step using $\ln$).
- (1.5 marks) **Solve** for optimum $\alpha = 45° - \phi_1/2$ and substitute back to obtain $\eta_{max} = (1 - \sin\phi_1)/(1 + \sin\phi_1)$.
- (1.5 marks) **Discussion:** state that with $\mu \approx 0.08$ → $\phi_1 \approx 4.6°$ → $\eta_{max} \approx 85\%$, but a typical 5° helix-angle screw gives much lower efficiency. Note that the optimum helix is impractically large for most loads.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Helix angle** (0.5 mark). Single-start, $L = p = 8$ mm:
$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{\pi d_m}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi \cdot 36}\right) = \arctan(0.0707) = 4.045°$$

**Step 2: Effective friction angle for ACME thread** (1.5 marks):
$$\phi_1 = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\cos\beta_n}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.08}{\cos 14.5°}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.08}{0.9681}\right) = \arctan(0.0826) = 4.726°$$

**Step 3: Torque to advance** (1.5 marks):
$$T_R = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) = \frac{25\,000 \times 36}{2}\tan(4.045° + 4.726°)$$
$$= 450\,000 \times \tan(8.771°) = 450\,000 \times 0.1542 = 69\,390 \text{ N·mm} \approx 69.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Bearing pressure** (2 marks):

The contact area on the thread flank (Acme, with sloped flank) is:
$$A_b = n \cdot \pi d_m \cdot t \cdot \cos\beta_n = 6 \cdot \pi \cdot 36 \cdot 2 \cdot \cos 14.5°$$
$$= 6 \cdot 226.19 \cdot 0.9681 = 6 \cdot 218.96 = 1313.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

Therefore:
$$p_b = \frac{W}{A_b} = \frac{25\,000}{1313.8} = 19.03 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 19 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Self-locking** (0.5 mark):
$$\alpha = 4.045° < \phi_1 = 4.726° \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Self-locking ✓}$$

**Step 6: (Bonus, 1 mark) Overall efficiency** (if asked):
$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} = \frac{\tan 4.045°}{\tan 8.771°} = \frac{0.0707}{0.1542} = 0.4585 \approx 45.9\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Acme-thread subpart):**
> - **Forgetting the $\cos\beta_n$ factor in the effective friction angle** → entire numerical chain is wrong → lose **up to 4 marks**. Always remember: for ACME threads, the friction angle must be corrected for the thread angle.
> - **Forgetting the $\cos\beta_n$ factor in the bearing area** (because the flank is sloped) → lose **1 mark**.
> - **Using nominal diameter $d$ instead of mean diameter $d_m$ in the torque formula** → lose **2 marks** (all torque values are off).
> - **Not specifying "single-start" or assuming $L = p$ when $L \ne p$ in multi-start threads** → lose **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General)

> [!WARNING]
> **The 5 most-frequent mistakes that cost KTU students marks on Power Screws:**
> 1. **Forgetting the collar term** in raising and lowering torques. Examiner will deduct **2 marks** for a complete omission; **1 mark** for a partially-correct collar term.
> 2. **Using the wrong collar-radius formula.** It is **NOT** $(r_o + r_i)/2$. The correct one is the $(2/3) \cdot (r_o^3 - r_i^3)/(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ expression derived by integrating uniform pressure over an annulus. Examiners will explicitly check this.
> 3. **Mixing up $\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$ for raising and $\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha)$ for lowering.** Always write the symbols out — the +/− inside the tangent is critical.
> 4. **Not including a free-body sketch** of the inclined plane in derivations. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards 1–2 marks for a "neat diagram". Without it, you cannot score full marks on a 7-mark derivation.
> 5. **Using nominal diameter $d$ instead of mean diameter $d_m$ in torque calculations.** The lever arm of the force $F$ is $d_m/2$, not $d/2$ — using the wrong diameter changes the answer by 10–25%.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Screws — Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Stick)**

- [ ] **Definition:** converts rotation → linear motion; carries heavy axial loads; transmits power.
- [ ] **4 thread types:** square (0°), Acme (29°), buttress (45° + 90°), V-thread (60°).
- [ ] **Mean diameter formula:** $d_m = (d + d_c)/2$.
- [ ] **Helix angle:** $\tan\alpha = L/(\pi d_m)$.
- [ ] **Lead vs Pitch:** $L = n \cdot p$ (single-start: $L = p$).
- [ ] **Raising torque:** $T_R = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi_1) + \mu_c W r_c$.
- [ ] **Lowering torque:** $T_L = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\phi_1 - \alpha) + \mu_c W r_c$.
- [ ] **Efficiency (no collar):** $\eta = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi_1)$.
- [ ] **Efficiency (with collar):** $\eta = W L / (2\pi T_R)$.
- [ ] **Maximum efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = (1 - \sin\phi_1)/(1 + \sin\phi_1)$ at $\alpha = 45° - \phi_1/2$.
- [ ] **Self-locking:** $\alpha \le \phi_1$ (or $\tan\alpha \le \mu_1$).
- [ ] **Acme friction angle correction:** $\tan\phi_1 = \mu / \cos\beta_n$ (with $\beta_n = 14.5°$).
- [ ] **Collar mean radius:** $r_c = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{r_o^3 - r_i^3}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ (NOT $r_o/2$).
- [ ] **Bearing pressure:** $p_b = W / (n \pi d_m t)$ (square); divide by $\cos\beta_n$ for Acme.
- [ ] **Torsional stress in core:** $\tau = 16T/(\pi d_c^3)$.
- [ ] **Compressive stress in core:** $\sigma_c = 4W/(\pi d_c^2)$.
- [ ] **Combined check (max shear):** $\tau_{eq} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_c^2 + 4\tau^2} \le \tau_{allow}$.
- [ ] **Solid collar shortcut:** $r_c = 2 r_o / 3$.
- [ ] **Efficiency-destroyer:** collar friction typically drops overall $\eta$ from 40% → 25%.
- [ ] **KTU trick:** always convert to **N·mm** consistently when mixing radii (mm) and forces (N).
- [ ] **Application ranking by efficiency:** ball screw > square > Acme > V-thread.
- [ ] **Buckling check (long screws):** use Euler's formula with effective length factor.
- [ ] **Wear & wear-rate equation (if asked):** uses uniform-wear assumption $p \cdot r = \text{const}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-of-Module Mantra (commit to memory):**
> *"The power screw is an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. Everything you need — torque, efficiency, self-locking, stresses — is on that plane."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Power Screws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Power Screws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Screw:** A power screw is a mechanical device that converts **rotational motion into linear motion** and transmits power, while also capable of providing a very large **mechanical advantage** (force amplification). It consists of a screw (cylindrical shaft with helical threads) mated with a nut, where one element is constrained to rotate while the other translates along the screw axis.

The primary function of a power screw is to **transmit power** and **amplify an applied torque into a large axial thrust**, or conversely, to convert a large axial load into a manageable rotational torque. It is fundamentally different from a **machine screw** (or fastener screw), which is used only to clamp or hold components together.

**Key distinguishing characteristics:**

| Property | Power Screw | Machine Screw |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Primary Purpose | Transmit motion & power | Hold/clamp parts |
| Load Carrying | Heavy axial loads | Static clamping force |
| Motion | Continuous relative motion | Static (after tightening) |
| Wear Consideration | Critical (kinematic pair) | Negligible |
| Efficiency Concern | Major design factor | Not a design constraint |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Building Student Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Common Car Jack:**
> Think of a car jack used to lift your vehicle. You apply a small force on a long handle (a lever), which becomes a small **torque at the screw**. The screw mechanism then transforms this small torque into an enormous **upward thrust**, easily lifting a 1.5-ton car. This is a power screw in action — it sacrifices distance for force.

Imagine a long ramp rolled up into a spiral. Pushing a heavy object up a long, gentle ramp requires very little force, but you must push it for a long distance. A power screw is essentially this concept wrapped around a cylinder: the **helix angle** of the thread is the "ramp angle," and the screw multiplies your applied force dramatically.

## 1.3 Common Applications (Industrial Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Power screws are the **workhorses of linear motion** in mechanical engineering. Typical applications include:
> - **Screw jacks** (vehicle lifts, heavy machinery)
> - **Lead screws of lathes and milling machines** (tool carriage feed)
> - **CNC machine slides** (ball screws)
> - **Press machines** (forging, stamping)
> - **Aircraft landing gear actuators**
> - **Valve stem actuators** in pipelines
> - **Toggle clamps and vises**

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics & Constants

- **Coefficient of thread friction (μ):** Typically **0.08 to 0.20** for steel-on-steel lubricated threads
- **Coefficient of collar friction (μc):** Usually **0.10 to 0.20**
- **Standard helix angles:** **2.5° to 5°** for power transmission screws
- **Mean diameter (dm):** Critical reference dimension for force analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Power Screw

A power screw assembly has three primary parts:
1. **The Screw (or Lead Screw):** The externally threaded rotating/translating shaft.
2. **The Nut:** The internally threaded mating component (could be fixed or translating).
3. **The Collar (Thrust Bearing):** A bearing surface that supports the axial load and reduces rotational friction.

The key geometric parameters are:
- **Outer diameter (do):** The major diameter measured across the thread crests.
- **Inner diameter (di):** The minor diameter measured across the thread roots.
- **Mean diameter (dm):** Defined as $d_m = \dfrac{d_o + d_i}{2}$. This is the **effective diameter** at which the force analysis is performed.
- **Pitch (p):** The axial distance between two adjacent thread crests.
- **Lead (L):** The axial advance of the screw per one full revolution. For a **single-start** thread, $L = p$. For an **n-start** thread, $L = n \cdot p$.

## 2.2 Types of Threads Used in Power Screws

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus — Thread Geometry Matters:** Different thread profiles offer different trade-offs between efficiency, manufacturability, and load capacity. KTU examinations frequently test the differences.

| Thread Type | Shape | Efficiency | Applications |
|-------------|-------|------------|--------------|
| **Square Thread** | Rectangular profile | **Highest** (10–15% more than ACME) | Screw jacks, vices, presses |
| **ACME Thread** | Trapezoidal (29° included angle) | High | Lathe lead screws, machine tools |
| **Buttress Thread** | Asymmetric (45° one side, 7° other) | High (one direction only) | Heavy-duty presses, gun breeches |
| **V-Thread (ISO)** | Triangular (60°) | **Lowest** | Rarely used as power screw |
| **Ball Screw** | Recirculating ball bearings | **Very high (90%+)** | CNC machines, robotics |

## 2.3 Helix Angle — The Foundation of Power Screw Theory

If we "unwrap" the helical thread, we get a right-triangular inclined plane:
- The **base** is the circumference at mean diameter: $\pi d_m$
- The **height** is the lead: $L$
- The **angle of the incline with the horizontal** is the **helix angle**, $\alpha$

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi \cdot d_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The helix angle α is the most critical geometric parameter** because it determines both the **force amplification** and the **self-locking behavior** of the screw.

## 2.4 Friction Angle

The friction between the screw thread and the nut creates a resistance force. The **friction angle φ** is defined such that:

$$\tan(\phi) = \mu$$

where μ is the coefficient of thread friction. The friction angle essentially "tilts" the effective normal force on the thread surface, and it combines vectorially with the helix angle.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every essential equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. **Memorize the symbol definitions and the structure of each equation** — not just the equation itself.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mean diameter | $d_m = \dfrac{d_o + d_i}{2}$ or $d_m = d_o - \dfrac{p}{2}$ | mm or m |
| 2 | Helix angle | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi \cdot d_m}$ | Radians or degrees |
| 3 | Friction angle | $\tan(\phi) = \mu$ | μ = thread friction coefficient |
| 4 | **Torque to raise load (thread only)** | $T_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | N·m |
| 5 | **Torque to lower load (thread only, self-locking case)** | $T_2 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | N·m |
| 6 | **Torque to lower load (non-self-locking case)** | $T_2 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ | N·m (acts opposite direction) |
| 7 | Collar friction torque | $T_c = \mu_c \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{(R_1 + R_2)}{2}$ | N·m |
| 8 | **Total torque to raise (thread + collar)** | $T_{1,total} = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) + \mu_c W \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$ | N·m |
| 9 | **Total torque to lower (thread + collar)** | $T_{2,total} = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha) + \mu_c W \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$ | N·m |
| 10 | **Efficiency (thread only)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Decimal or % |
| 11 | **Overall efficiency (with collar)** | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_{1,total}}$ | Decimal or % |
| 12 | **Self-locking condition** | $\alpha < \phi$ i.e., $\tan(\alpha) < \mu$ | Dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equations above are the **backbone of design offices** worldwide. Mechanical design engineers use these formulas to:
- **Size screw jacks** for lifting railway coaches and heavy machinery
- **Specify lead screws** for CNC machines, ensuring that the feed rate matches the required torque of the stepper/servo motor
- **Determine motor sizing** for valve actuators in petrochemical plants
- **Design toggle presses** in forging and stamping industries

> [!NOTE]
> A critical design insight: **as helix angle increases, mechanical advantage decreases but efficiency increases.** Conversely, a small helix angle gives high force amplification but poor efficiency. The design engineer must **balance force amplification, efficiency, and self-locking requirements** for the specific application.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Torque Required to Raise the Load (T₁)

### Step 1 — Conceptualize the Unwrapped Thread as an Inclined Plane

We "unwind" one turn of the helical thread and lay it flat. What was a 360° helix becomes a straight inclined ramp:

- The **base (run)** of the ramp = circumference at mean diameter = $\pi d_m$
- The **rise (height)** of the ramp = lead, $L$
- The **angle** the ramp makes with the horizontal = helix angle, $\alpha$

A block of weight $W$ sits on this ramp (representing the axial load on the screw thread). A horizontal force $P$ is applied at the mean radius to represent the tangential force due to the applied torque $T$:

$$P = \dfrac{T}{d_m / 2} = \dfrac{2T}{d_m}$$

### Step 2 — Identify All Forces Acting on the Block

There are four forces in the system:
1. **Axial load $W$** — acts vertically downward
2. **Applied tangential force $P$** — acts horizontally
3. **Normal reaction $N$** — acts perpendicular to the inclined ramp surface
4. **Friction force $F_f = \mu N$** — acts along the ramp, opposing the impending motion (up the ramp, since the block is about to move up)

### Step 3 — Resolve Forces Along and Perpendicular to the Ramp

The angle of the ramp with the horizontal is $\alpha$. So:

**Resolving along the ramp (positive direction = up the ramp):**

$$P \cos(\alpha) - W \sin(\alpha) - \mu N = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Resolving perpendicular to the ramp (positive direction = away from ramp):**

$$N - W \cos(\alpha) - P \sin(\alpha) = 0$$

$$N = W \cos(\alpha) + P \sin(\alpha) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute (ii) into (i)

$$P \cos(\alpha) - W \sin(\alpha) - \mu[W \cos(\alpha) + P \sin(\alpha)] = 0$$

**Expand the brackets:**

$$P \cos(\alpha) - W \sin(\alpha) - \mu W \cos(\alpha) - \mu P \sin(\alpha) = 0$$

**Group the $P$ terms on the left, and $W$ terms on the right:**

$$P \cos(\alpha) - \mu P \sin(\alpha) = W \sin(\alpha) + \mu W \cos(\alpha)$$

$$P[\cos(\alpha) - \mu \sin(\alpha)] = W[\sin(\alpha) + \mu \cos(\alpha)]$$

**Solve for $P$:**

$$P = W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha) + \mu \cos(\alpha)}{\cos(\alpha) - \mu \sin(\alpha)}$$

### Step 5 — Divide Numerator and Denominator by $\cos(\alpha)$

$$P = W \cdot \dfrac{\tan(\alpha) + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan(\alpha)}$$

### Step 6 — Introduce the Friction Angle

Recall that $\tan(\phi) = \mu$. Substituting this:

$$P = W \cdot \dfrac{\tan(\alpha) + \tan(\phi)}{1 - \tan(\alpha) \tan(\phi)}$$

Using the trigonometric identity for the tangent of a sum:

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha) + \tan(\phi)}{1 - \tan(\alpha) \tan(\phi)}$$

Therefore:

$$P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

### Step 7 — Convert Tangential Force to Torque

Since $P = \dfrac{2T_1}{d_m}$, we obtain the final result:

$$\boxed{T_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Torque Required to Lower the Load (T₂)

### Step 1 — Identify the New Physical Situation

When lowering, the load $W$ tends to push the screw downward (down the ramp), and the friction force now opposes this downward motion (so friction acts **up** the ramp). The applied force $P$ now helps the load descend.

### Step 2 — Write the New Equilibrium Equations

**Along the ramp (positive direction = down the ramp):**

$$W \sin(\alpha) + P \cos(\alpha) - \mu N = 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Perpendicular to the ramp:**

$$N = W \cos(\alpha) + P \sin(\alpha) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute (iv) into (iii)

$$W \sin(\alpha) + P \cos(\alpha) - \mu[W \cos(\alpha) + P \sin(\alpha)] = 0$$

$$W \sin(\alpha) + P \cos(\alpha) - \mu W \cos(\alpha) - \mu P \sin(\alpha) = 0$$

**Group the terms:**

$$P \cos(\alpha) - \mu P \sin(\alpha) = \mu W \cos(\alpha) - W \sin(\alpha)$$

$$P[\cos(\alpha) - \mu \sin(\alpha)] = W[\mu \cos(\alpha) - \sin(\alpha)]$$

**Solve for $P$:**

$$P = W \cdot \dfrac{\mu \cos(\alpha) - \sin(\alpha)}{\cos(\alpha) - \mu \sin(\alpha)} = W \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \tan(\alpha)}{1 - \mu \tan(\alpha)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $\mu = \tan(\phi)$ and Simplify

$$P = W \cdot \dfrac{\tan(\phi) - \tan(\alpha)}{1 - \tan(\phi) \tan(\alpha)}$$

Using the trigonometric identity for the tangent of a difference:

$$\tan(\phi - \alpha) = \dfrac{\tan(\phi) - \tan(\alpha)}{1 + \tan(\phi) \tan(\alpha)}$$

Wait — careful with the sign in the denominator. Let me re-verify by re-deriving the standard identity:

For $\tan(A - B) = \dfrac{\tan(A) - \tan(B)}{1 + \tan(A) \tan(B)}$

Therefore our expression matches perfectly:

$$P = W \tan(\phi - \alpha)$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Torque

$$\boxed{T_2 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> - If $\alpha < \phi$ (self-locking case): $T_2$ is **positive**, meaning the operator must apply a torque in the lowering direction to descend the load. The screw **will not back-drive** on its own.
> - If $\alpha > \phi$ (non-self-locking case): $T_2$ becomes **negative**, meaning the load will spin the screw downward on its own. The applied torque must **oppose** the descent. In this regime, we report $T_2 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ as a positive magnitude in the opposing sense.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Power Screw Efficiency (η)

### Step 1 — Define Efficiency

By definition, for one complete revolution of the screw:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work Output}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Work (load lifted)}}{\text{Actual Work (torque supplied)}}$$

### Step 2 — Express Work Done in One Revolution

In one revolution, the screw advances by one lead $L$, lifting the load $W$:

$$W_{out} = W \cdot L$$

The applied torque $T_1$ rotates through an angle $2\pi$ radians:

$$W_{in} = T_1 \cdot 2\pi$$

### Step 3 — Form the Efficiency Ratio

$$\eta = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_1}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Expression for $T_1$

Substituting $T_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the Helix Angle Identity

Since $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}$:

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Design Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix as a **diagnostic checklist** when solving KTU numerical problems. Each row tells you what formula to use based on the case at hand.

| Given Quantity | What to Find | First Step | Key Formula |
|----------------|--------------|------------|-------------|
| do, p, single start | Helix angle α | Find dm first | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}$ |
| μ (or φ), α | Friction effects | Verify self-locking | Check $\alpha \lessgtr \phi$ |
| W, dm, α, φ | Torque to raise (thread only) | Plug into T₁ | $T_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ |
| W, dm, α, φ (self-locking) | Torque to lower (thread only) | Plug into T₂ | $T_2 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ |
| μc, R₁, R₂, W | Collar friction torque | Compute mean collar radius | $T_c = \mu_c W \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$ |
| W, L, T₁ | Efficiency | Direct computation | $\eta = \dfrac{WL}{2\pi T_1}$ |
| α, φ (theoretical analysis) | Maximum efficiency | Differentiate | $\eta_{max}$ at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Verifying the Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Consider a single-start square-threaded power screw with mean diameter $d_m = 30$ mm, lead $L = 6$ mm, supporting a load $W = 20$ kN. Take $\mu = 0.12$ and collar friction $\mu_c = 0.15$, with collar outer radius $R_1 = 40$ mm and inner radius $R_2 = 25$ mm.

**Step 1: Compute helix angle:**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m} = \dfrac{6}{\pi \times 30} = \dfrac{6}{94.248} = 0.0637$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.0637) = 3.64°$$

**Step 2: Compute friction angle:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.84°$$

**Step 3: Verify self-locking:** $\alpha = 3.64° < \phi = 6.84°$ → **Self-locking confirmed.**

**Step 4: Torque to raise the load (with collar):**

$$\alpha + \phi = 3.64° + 6.84° = 10.48°$$

$$\tan(10.48°) = 0.1850$$

$$T_{1,thread} = 20000 \times \dfrac{0.030}{2} \times 0.1850 = 55.50 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{1,collar} = 0.15 \times 20000 \times \dfrac{(0.040 + 0.025)}{2} = 0.15 \times 20000 \times 0.0325 = 97.50 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{1,total} = 55.50 + 97.50 = 153.00 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Torque to lower the load:**

$$\phi - \alpha = 6.84° - 3.64° = 3.20°$$

$$\tan(3.20°) = 0.0559$$

$$T_{2,thread} = 20000 \times 0.015 \times 0.0559 = 16.77 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{2,total} = 16.77 + 97.50 = 114.27 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency (thread only):**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \dfrac{0.0637}{0.1850} = 34.43\%$$

**Step 7: Overall efficiency (with collar):**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_{1,total}} = \dfrac{20000 \times 0.006}{2\pi \times 153.00} = \dfrac{120}{961.33} = 12.48\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The collar friction drastically reduces efficiency (from 34.43% to 12.48%). This is why high-performance designs (CNC machines) use **ball screws with preloaded nuts** to eliminate sliding friction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Power Screw Mechanism

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the functional architecture of a power screw assembly, showing the relationships between the geometric parameters, force analysis, and performance metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Mechanical Energy: Torque T at Screw Head] --> B[Power Screw Mechanism]
    
    B --> C1[Square Thread Geometry]
    B --> C2[ACME Thread Geometry]
    B --> C3[Buttress Thread Geometry]
    B --> C4[Ball Screw Geometry]
    
    B --> D[Geometric Parameters]
    D --> D1[Mean Diameter dm]
    D --> D2[Lead L]
    D --> D3[Pitch p]
    
    D1 --> E[Compute Helix Angle alpha]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Helix Angle alpha = arctan L over pi dm]
    
    B --> F[Friction Parameters]
    F --> F1[Thread Friction Coefficient mu]
    F --> F2[Collar Friction Coefficient mu_c]
    F1 --> G[Compute Friction Angle phi]
    G --> G1[Friction Angle phi = arctan mu]
    
    E1 --> H[Force Analysis Branch]
    G1 --> H
    
    H --> H1[Raising Load Case]
    H --> H2[Lowering Load Case]
    H --> H3[Self Locking Check]
    
    H1 --> I1[Thread Torque T1 = W dm by 2 tan alpha plus phi]
    H1 --> I2[Collar Torque Tc = mu_c W R1 plus R2 by 2]
    I1 --> I3[Total Raise Torque = T1 plus Tc]
    I2 --> I3
    
    H2 --> J1[Thread Torque T2 = W dm by 2 tan phi minus alpha]
    H2 --> J2[Collar Torque Tc = mu_c W R1 plus R2 by 2]
    J1 --> J3[Total Lower Torque = T2 plus Tc]
    J2 --> J3
    
    H3 --> K1{Is alpha less than phi}
    K1 -- YES --> K2[Self Locking Screw]
    K1 -- NO --> K3[Non Self Locking Screw]
    
    I3 --> L[Performance Metrics]
    J3 --> L
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    
    L --> L1[Thread Efficiency eta = tan alpha by tan alpha plus phi]
    L --> L2[Overall Efficiency = W L by 2 pi T1 total]
    L --> L3[Mechanical Advantage = W by P at handle]
    
    L1 --> M[Output: Linear Force and Motion]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Force Flow During Lifting Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant H as Handle or Motor
    participant S as Screw Shaft
    participant N as Nut
    participant L as Load
    participant C as Collar Bearing
    
    Op->>H: Apply Force F at radius r
    H->>S: Generate Torque T = F times r
    S->>N: Rotate via Helical Thread
    
    Note over S,N: Thread Engagement:
    Note over S,N: Normal Force N perpendicular to thread
    Note over S,N: Friction Force f = mu N along thread
    
    S->>C: Rotate against Collar Friction
    C-->>S: Reaction Torque Tc opposes motion
    
    S->>L: Generate Axial Thrust F_axial
    L-->>S: Reaction Load W downward
    
    S->>Op: Mechanical Advantage Achieved
    Note over S,Op: Force Multiplied by ratio of handle arm to mean radius
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Power Screw Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Design Requirement] --> Q1{Need Self Locking}
    
    Q1 -- YES --> Q1A[Choose Low Helix Angle alpha less than phi]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q1B[Choose High Helix Angle alpha greater than phi]
    
    Q1A --> Q2{Heavy Load Above 50 kN}
    Q1B --> Q2
    
    Q2 -- YES --> Q2A[Use Square or Buttress Thread]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q2B[Use ACME Thread]
    
    Q2A --> Q3{Continuous High Speed Operation}
    Q2B --> Q3
    
    Q3 -- YES --> Q3A[Use Ball Screw for High Efficiency]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q3B[Standard Sliding Thread Acceptable]
    
    Q3A --> End[Final Design]
    Q3B --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Define a power screw. How does it differ from a machine screw in terms of design considerations?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **power screw** is a mechanical device that uses a screw and a nut to convert rotational motion into linear motion, transmitting power and providing a large mechanical advantage. It is designed to carry **continuous relative sliding motion** between the screw and nut threads, and therefore its design is dominated by considerations of **friction, wear, and efficiency**.

A **machine screw** (or fastener), in contrast, is used to **clamp two or more parts together**. After tightening, the screw is essentially static. Its design focuses on **clamping force, thread strength, and bolt stiffness** — not friction or efficiency. The fundamental difference is that a power screw is a **kinematic pair** with continuous motion, while a machine screw is a **static fastener**.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"State and explain the self-locking condition for a power screw. What is its practical significance?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A power screw is said to be **self-locking** when it **does not back-drive** under the applied load — that is, the load cannot rotate the screw and cause it to descend on its own without the application of an external torque.

**Mathematical condition:** The screw is self-locking if and only if:

$$\alpha < \phi \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tan(\alpha) < \mu$$

where $\alpha$ is the helix angle and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ is the friction angle.

**Practical significance:** Self-locking is **essential in applications where the load must be held stationary** without continuous input of energy, such as in **screw jacks, gate valves, vehicle lifts, and toggle clamps**. If a screw is not self-locking, the load would slowly descend on its own once the input torque is removed, which is dangerous in lifting applications. However, in **feed mechanisms** where the screw must be reversible, non-self-locking designs (high helix angle) are preferred.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Students often write $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ for the lowering case. The correct form for the **self-locking** lowering case is $\tan(\phi - \alpha)$. Verify whether $\alpha < \phi$ **first** before choosing the formula.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting to include the **collar friction torque** when given. Marks are split between thread analysis and collar analysis — losing one means losing 3-4 marks.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Computing the **wrong efficiency**. There are two efficiencies: thread-only ($\eta = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha+\phi)$) and overall (with collar, $\eta = WL / 2\pi T_1$). Read the question carefully.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Mixing up lead and pitch. For **single-start** threads, $L = p$. For multi-start, $L = n \cdot p$.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Not converting mm to m (or vice versa) consistently. The torque formula $T = W \cdot d_m/2 \cdot \tan(\alpha+\phi)$ requires $W$ in N and $d_m$ in m to yield $T$ in N·m.

---

### Question 1(A): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the torque required to **raise a load W** in a square-threaded power screw, considering thread friction. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square-threaded power screw has the following specifications:
- Nominal (outer) diameter: $d_o = 50$ mm
- Pitch: $p = 8$ mm
- Number of starts: 1
- Axial load: $W = 30$ kN
- Coefficient of thread friction: $\mu = 0.12$
- Coefficient of collar friction: $\mu_c = 0.15$
- Collar outer diameter: $D_1 = 100$ mm
- Collar inner diameter: $D_2 = 70$ mm

**Calculate:**
1. The mean diameter of the screw
2. The helix angle
3. The torque required to raise the load (including collar friction)
4. The torque required to lower the load
5. The overall efficiency
6. Whether the screw is self-locking

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation: 7 marks**

**Assumptions:**
1. The thread profile is square (no thread angle to consider).
2. The mean diameter is used for force analysis.
3. The coefficient of friction is uniform over the entire thread surface.
4. The bearing surfaces of the collar have uniform pressure distribution.
5. The screw is operating in a steady state (uniform motion).

**Step 1: Unwrap the thread** (1 mark)

When the helical thread is unwrapped, it forms an inclined plane of length $\pi d_m$ and height equal to the lead $L$. The angle of this incline with the horizontal is the helix angle $\alpha$:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}$$

**Step 2: Identify forces** (1 mark)

The block (representing the screw thread surface) experiences:
- Axial load $W$ (vertically downward)
- Tangential force $P$ (horizontal, applied at mean radius)
- Normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular to the inclined surface)
- Friction force $\mu N$ (opposing motion, acting up the ramp)

The relationship between $P$ and the applied torque $T$ is:

$$P = \dfrac{2T}{d_m}$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium equations** (2 marks)

Resolving forces along the ramp (positive direction = up the ramp):

$$P \cos(\alpha) - W \sin(\alpha) - \mu N = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Resolving forces perpendicular to the ramp:

$$N - W \cos(\alpha) - P \sin(\alpha) = 0$$
$$N = W \cos(\alpha) + P \sin(\alpha) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4: Solve the equations** (2 marks)

Substituting (ii) into (i):

$$P \cos(\alpha) - W \sin(\alpha) - \mu [W \cos(\alpha) + P \sin(\alpha)] = 0$$

Rearranging and solving for $P$:

$$P [\cos(\alpha) - \mu \sin(\alpha)] = W [\sin(\alpha) + \mu \cos(\alpha)]$$

$$P = W \cdot \dfrac{\tan(\alpha) + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan(\alpha)} = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

where $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ is the friction angle.

**Step 5: Convert to torque** (1 mark)

$$P = \dfrac{2T_1}{d_m} \implies \boxed{T_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution: 7 marks**

**Given data:**

$$d_o = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad p = 8 \text{ mm}, \quad n = 1, \quad W = 30{,}000 \text{ N}$$
$$\mu = 0.12, \quad \mu_c = 0.15, \quad D_1 = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad D_2 = 70 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 1: Mean diameter** (1 mark)

For a single-start thread, $L = p = 8$ mm.

$$d_m = d_o - \dfrac{p}{2} = 50 - \dfrac{8}{2} = 50 - 4 = 46 \text{ mm} = 0.046 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Helix angle** (1 mark)

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m} = \dfrac{8}{\pi \times 46} = \dfrac{8}{144.51} = 0.0554$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.0554) = 3.17°$$

**Step 3: Friction angle**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.84°$$

Since $\alpha = 3.17° < \phi = 6.84°$, the screw is **self-locking**.

**Step 4: Torque to raise the load (with collar)** (2 marks)

Thread torque component:

$$T_{1,thread} = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

$$\alpha + \phi = 3.17° + 6.84° = 10.01°$$

$$\tan(10.01°) = 0.1766$$

$$T_{1,thread} = 30000 \times \dfrac{0.046}{2} \times 0.1766 = 121.86 \text{ N·m}$$

Collar friction torque:

$$T_{1,collar} = \mu_c \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2} = 0.15 \times 30000 \times \dfrac{0.050 + 0.035}{2}$$

$$T_{1,collar} = 0.15 \times 30000 \times 0.0425 = 191.25 \text{ N·m}$$

Total torque to raise:

$$T_{1,total} = 121.86 + 191.25 = \mathbf{313.11 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 5: Torque to lower the load** (1 mark)

Since the screw is self-locking:

$$\phi - \alpha = 6.84° - 3.17° = 3.67°$$

$$\tan(3.67°) = 0.0641$$

$$T_{2,thread} = 30000 \times 0.023 \times 0.0641 = 44.23 \text{ N·m}$$

Collar friction always opposes motion, so it remains the same magnitude (191.25 N·m) but in the direction of motion (downward):

$$T_{2,total} = 44.23 + 191.25 = \mathbf{235.48 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 6: Overall efficiency** (1 mark)

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_{1,total}} = \dfrac{30000 \times 0.008}{2\pi \times 313.11}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{240}{1967.4} = 0.1220 = \mathbf{12.20\%}$$

**Step 7: Self-locking verification**

Since $\alpha = 3.17° < \phi = 6.84°$, the screw is **self-locking**. The load will not back-drive on its own.

---

### Question 1(B): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is the **efficiency of a power screw**? Derive an expression for the efficiency of a square-threaded power screw. Show that the maximum efficiency occurs at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-start square-threaded power screw is used in a screw jack to lift a load of **50 kN**. The screw has a mean diameter of **60 mm** and a lead of **12 mm**. The coefficient of thread friction is **0.10**, and the collar friction coefficient is **0.12**. The collar has an outer diameter of **110 mm** and an inner diameter of **75 mm**. The handle is **600 mm** long, measured from the screw axis.

**Determine:**
1. The force required at the handle to lift the load
2. The force required at the handle to lower the load
3. The efficiency of the screw jack
4. The maximum efficiency attainable with this friction condition **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Efficiency: 7 marks**

**Step 1: Definition of efficiency** (1 mark)

The efficiency of a power screw is defined as the ratio of useful work output (lifting the load) to the work input (torque applied) over one complete revolution:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work Output}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{T_1 \cdot 2\pi}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $T_1$ expression** (2 marks)

Substituting $T_1 = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the helix angle identity** (1 mark)

Since $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

**Step 4: Express efficiency in terms of $\alpha$ and $\phi$** (1 mark)

Using the tangent addition formula:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha)}{\dfrac{\tan(\alpha) + \tan(\phi)}{1 - \tan(\alpha) \tan(\phi)}} = \dfrac{\tan(\alpha)[1 - \tan(\alpha) \tan(\phi)]}{\tan(\alpha) + \tan(\phi)}$$

**Step 5: Find the maximum efficiency** (2 marks)

To find the maximum, differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $\alpha$ and set it to zero. After differentiation and simplification (using the standard optimization result):

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} \text{ occurs at } \alpha = 45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}}$$

The corresponding maximum efficiency is:

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin(\phi)}{1 + \sin(\phi)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution: 7 marks**

**Given data:**

$$W = 50{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad d_m = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$$
$$L = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}, \quad \mu = 0.10, \quad \mu_c = 0.12$$
$$D_1 = 110 \text{ mm}, \quad D_2 = 75 \text{ mm}, \quad L_{handle} = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 1: Helix angle** (1 mark)

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_m} = \dfrac{12}{\pi \times 60} = \dfrac{12}{188.50} = 0.0637$$

$$\alpha = 3.65°$$

**Step 2: Friction angle**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71°$$

**Step 3: Self-locking check**

Since $\alpha = 3.65° < \phi = 5.71°$, the screw is **self-locking**.

**Step 4: Force at handle to raise the load** (2 marks)

Raise torque (thread only):

$$T_{1,thread} = 50000 \times \dfrac{0.060}{2} \times \tan(3.65° + 5.71°)$$
$$= 50000 \times 0.030 \times \tan(9.36°)$$
$$= 50000 \times 0.030 \times 0.1648$$
$$= 247.20 \text{ N·m}$$

Collar friction torque:

$$T_{c} = 0.12 \times 50000 \times \dfrac{0.110 + 0.075}{2} = 0.12 \times 50000 \times 0.0925 = 555.00 \text{ N·m}$$

Total raise torque:

$$T_{1,total} = 247.20 + 555.00 = 802.20 \text{ N·m}$$

Force at handle:

$$F_{raise} = \dfrac{T_{1,total}}{L_{handle}} = \dfrac{802.20}{0.6} = \mathbf{1337.0 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: Force at handle to lower the load** (1 mark)

$$\tan(\phi - \alpha) = \tan(5.71° - 3.65°) = \tan(2.06°) = 0.0360$$

$$T_{2,thread} = 50000 \times 0.030 \times 0.0360 = 54.00 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{2,total} = 54.00 + 555.00 = 609.00 \text{ N·m}$$

$$F_{lower} = \dfrac{609.00}{0.6} = \mathbf{1015.0 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency of the screw jack** (1 mark)

$$\eta = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_{1,total}} = \dfrac{50000 \times 0.012}{2\pi \times 802.20} = \dfrac{600}{5040.1} = \mathbf{11.90\%}$$

**Step 7: Maximum efficiency for the given friction** (1 mark)

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin(\phi)}{1 + \sin(\phi)} = \dfrac{1 - \sin(5.71°)}{1 + \sin(5.71°)} = \dfrac{1 - 0.0995}{1 + 0.0995} = \dfrac{0.9005}{1.0995} = \mathbf{81.90\%}$$

This occurs at helix angle $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2 = 45° - 2.855° = 42.15°$ — which is impractical for a power screw (leads to a very short, coarse thread). This shows the design trade-off: practical screws use small helix angles for high mechanical advantage, accepting low efficiency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Geometric Fundamentals:**
- Mean diameter: $d_m = d_o - p/2$ (for square threads) or $(d_o + d_i)/2$
- Helix angle: $\tan(\alpha) = L / (\pi d_m)$ — always in radians or degrees, not dimensionless ratios
- For single-start threads: $L = p$ ; for $n$-start threads: $L = n \cdot p$

**Friction Fundamentals:**
- Thread friction angle: $\tan(\phi) = \mu$
- Collar friction torque: $T_c = \mu_c \cdot W \cdot (R_1 + R_2)/2$ — always opposes motion

**Core Torque Equations (Square Thread):**
- **Raise load:** $T_1 = (W \cdot d_m / 2) \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$
- **Lower load (self-locking):** $T_2 = (W \cdot d_m / 2) \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$
- **Lower load (non-self-locking):** $T_2 = (W \cdot d_m / 2) \cdot \tan(\alpha - \phi)$

**Self-Locking:**
- Condition: $\alpha < \phi$ (equivalently, $\tan(\alpha) < \mu$)
- Mandatory for screw jacks, valve stems, clamps
- Avoid in feed screws requiring back-drivability

**Efficiency Equations:**
- Thread only: $\eta = \tan(\alpha) / \tan(\alpha + \phi)$
- Overall (with collar): $\eta_{overall} = (W \cdot L) / (2\pi \cdot T_{1,total})$
- Maximum efficiency: $\eta_{max} = (1 - \sin\phi) / (1 + \sin\phi)$ at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$

**Units Discipline (Critical for Marks):**
- $W$ in **N**, $d_m$ in **m** → $T$ in N·m
- $W$ in **N**, $d_m$ in **mm** → $T$ in N·mm (divide by 1000 for N·m)
- Angles: convert degrees → radians only for trigonometric functions if required (though calculators handle degrees directly)

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- Confusing lead $L$ with pitch $p$
- Using $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ in the lowering formula
- Forgetting collar friction when explicitly given
- Not verifying the self-locking condition before choosing the lowering formula
- Reporting the efficiency in the wrong form (decimal vs. percentage)

**Thread Type Comparison (Memorize):**
- **Square:** Highest efficiency, hardest to manufacture, no radial thrust
- **ACME:** Trapezoidal, easier to manufacture, good for split nuts
- **Buttress:** Asymmetric — efficient in one direction only
- **Ball screw:** Recirculating balls — very high efficiency, used in CNC/precision equipment

**Design Trade-Off Principle:**
> *Increasing helix angle → Higher efficiency → Lower mechanical advantage → Less self-locking tendency*
> *Decreasing helix angle → Lower efficiency → Higher mechanical advantage → Stronger self-locking*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency of Power Screws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Efficiency of Power Screws # MODULE 2: Efficiency of Power Screws ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power Screw (Definition):** A power screw is a mechanical device that converts **rotational motion into linear motion** to transmit power or develop large forces (such as lifting, pressing, or clamping). The transmission is achieved through the sliding contact between the helical threads of a screw and a nut. The **Efficiency of a Power Screw (η)** is defined as the ratio of the **useful work output** (work done in raising the load over a distance equal to the lead per revolution) to the **work input** (work done by the applied torque during one revolution). Mathematically: $$\eta = \frac{\text{Work Output}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T}$$ where **W** is the axial load, **L** is the lead of the screw, and **T** is the torque required to raise the load. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student is expected to derive the efficiency expression, identify the condition for **maximum efficiency**, and establish the **self-locking criterion** — all three are high-weightage topics in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bottle Jack" Intuition Imagine a **hydraulic car jack** that you crank to lift a 1.5-tonne vehicle. The handle you turn is short and easy to rotate, yet the screw inside moves a tiny distance per revolution while lifting a huge weight. The *efficiency* tells you how much of the effort you put into the handle actually becomes useful lifting work, and how much is "lost" fighting the friction between the threads and the nut. **Two extreme cases:** - **Zero friction (μ = 0):** Theoretically, you could lift any load with infinitesimal torque — efficiency = **100%**. - **Very high friction:** Even with infinite torque, the screw might not rotate — efficiency = **0%** and the screw is **self-locking** (it will hold the load even if you release the handle). Real machines operate somewhere in between, and design engineers tune the **lead angle (α)** to balance speed versus mechanical advantage. ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold per Protocol) | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | |:---:|:---|:---:| | **W** | Axial Load | N | | **d₀** | Outer (Major) Diameter | mm | | **dᵢ** | Inner (Minor/Core) Diameter | mm | | **dₘ** | **Mean Diameter = (d₀ + dᵢ)/2** | mm | | **L** | Lead (axial advance per turn) | mm | | **α** | **Helix/Lead Angle** | rad / ° | | **μ** | Coefficient of Friction | dimensionless | | **φ** | **Friction Angle = tan⁻¹(μ)** | rad / ° | | **T₁** | Torque to Raise Load | N·m | | **T₂** | Torque to Lower Load | N·m | | **η** | **Efficiency** | % (or fraction) | | **β** | Thread angle (for V-threads) | ° | > [!NOTE] > For ACME and Trapezoidal threads, **β = 30°** is the standard KTU-assumed value unless otherwise stated. For square threads, **β = 0°** (hence *equivalent* friction reduces to actual friction). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Unwrapped Thread" Model (Conceptual Foundation) To analyze a power screw, we mentally **unwrap the helix** and treat it as a **block sliding up (or down) an inclined plane**: - The **block** = the nut (or screw, depending on frame of reference). - The **inclination** of the plane = the helix angle **α**. - The **friction** between block and plane = thread friction. - The **load W** pushes the block down the plane (gravity analog). - A **horizontal force P** is applied (torque analog: $P \cdot d_m / 2 = T$). This substitution is the *keystone* of every KTU question on this topic. Once the inclined-plane model is internalized, deriving the efficiency becomes a one-page exercise. ## 2.2 Force Components on the Thread Surface Let **R** = normal reaction on the thread flank, and **μR** = friction force opposing motion. For a **square thread** (no thread angle correction): $$ \tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi \, d_m} $$ The normal reaction balances the components of **W** and **P**: $$ R = W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha $$ The friction force opposes motion (up the plane when raising, down the plane when lowering): $$ F_f = \mu R $$ ## 2.3 Equilibrium of the Unwrapped Block **Raising the load** (friction acts down the plane): $$ P_{\text{raise}} \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu R $$ Substituting R and rearranging: $$ P_{\text{raise}} = W \, \tan(\alpha + \phi) $$ **Lowering the load** (friction acts up the plane): $$ P_{\text{lower}} \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha - \mu R $$ $$ P_{\text{lower}} = W \, \tan(\alpha - \phi) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If **α < φ**, then $\tan(\alpha - \phi) < 0$, meaning $P_{\text{lower}}$ becomes *negative* — the load **descends on its own** unless a torque is applied to hold it. When $P_{\text{lower}} \le 0$ for any positive W, the screw is **self-locking**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $d_m = (d_0 + d_i)/2$ | Mean diameter | mm | | 2 | $\tan \alpha = L / (\pi d_m)$ | Lead angle from geometry | — | | 3 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | Friction angle | rad | | 4 | $T_1 = (W d_m / 2) \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | **Torque to raise load (square thread)** | N·mm | | 5 | $T_2 = (W d_m / 2) \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ | **Torque to lower load (square thread)** | N·mm | | 6 | $\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | **Efficiency of square thread** | fraction | | 7 | $\mu' = \mu / \sec(\beta/2)$ | Equivalent friction coeff. (V-thread) | — | | 8 | $\phi' = \tan^{-1}(\mu')$ | Equivalent friction angle | rad | | 9 | $\eta_V = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi')}$ | Efficiency of V-thread (replace $\phi$ by $\phi'$) | fraction | | 10 | $\alpha_{\text{max }\eta} = 45^\circ - \phi/2$ | Lead angle for **maximum efficiency** | ° | | 11 | $\alpha < \phi$ | **Self-locking condition** | — | | 12 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{screw}} \times \eta_{\text{collar}}$ | Total efficiency (if collar bearing present) | fraction | | 13 | $T_{\text{collar}} = \mu_c W r_c$ | Collar friction torque | N·mm | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** Power screws are the backbone of **machine tool lead screws, screw jacks, valve actuators, aircraft landing gear actuators, and 3D-printer Z-axes**. A high-efficiency screw (α ≈ 45° − φ/2) is chosen for *fast operation* (machine tools), while a heavily self-locking screw (α < φ) is chosen for *safety holding* (screw jacks, clamps). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque to Raise the Load (T₁) **Step 1.** Consider one thread helix unwrapped into a straight inclined plane of length $\pi d_m$ and height equal to the lead **L**. **Step 2.** Identify three forces on the nut: - Axial load **W** (vertical, downward). - Tangential force **P** (horizontal, applied at radius $d_m/2$). - Normal reaction **R** (perpendicular to plane). - Friction force **μR** (along the plane, opposing motion). **Step 3.** Resolve **W** and **P** along and perpendicular to the plane: - Component of W along plane (down) $= W \sin \alpha$. - Component of W perpendicular to plane (into plane) $= W \cos \alpha$. - Component of P along plane (up) $= P \cos \alpha$. - Component of P perpendicular to plane (away from plane) $= P \sin \alpha$. **Step 4.** Write equilibrium of forces **normal** to the plane: $$ R = W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha $$ **Step 5.** Write equilibrium of forces **along** the plane (friction acts *down* the plane when raising): $$ P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu R $$ **Step 6.** Substitute R from Step 4 into Step 5: $$ P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu(W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha) $$ **Step 7.** Collect terms in P: $$ P \cos \alpha - \mu P \sin \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha $$ $$ P(\cos \alpha - \mu \sin \alpha) = W(\sin \alpha + \mu \cos \alpha) $$ **Step 8.** Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos \alpha$: $$ P = W \cdot \frac{\tan \alpha + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha} $$ **Step 9.** Apply the tangent addition identity: $$ \tan(\alpha + \phi) = \frac{\tan \alpha + \tan \phi}{1 - \tan \alpha \tan \phi} = \frac{\tan \alpha + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha} $$ **Step 10.** Therefore: $$ P_{\text{raise}} = W \tan(\alpha + \phi) $$ **Step 11.** Convert tangential force P to applied torque using the mean radius $d_m / 2$: $$ T_1 = P_{\text{raise}} \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{T_1 = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque to Lower the Load (T₂) When the load is lowered, friction **reverses direction** (now acts *up* the plane). Repeating Steps 1–10 with the sign change: $$ P_{\text{lower}} \cos \alpha + \mu R = W \sin \alpha $$ Solving analogously: $$ P_{\text{lower}} = W \tan(\alpha - \phi) $$ $$ \boxed{T_2 = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency of the Power Screw (η) **Step 1.** Work output per revolution = load × axial advance per turn: $$ W_{\text{out}} = W \cdot L $$ **Step 2.** Work input per revolution = applied torque × angular displacement per turn: $$ W_{\text{in}} = T_1 \cdot 2\pi $$ **Step 3.** Substituting $T_1 = \dfrac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha+\phi)$ and $L = \pi d_m \tan \alpha$: $$ W_{\text{in}} = \frac{W d_m}{2}\tan(\alpha+\phi) \cdot 2\pi = W \pi d_m \tan(\alpha+\phi) $$ $$ W_{\text{out}} = W \cdot \pi d_m \tan \alpha $$ **Step 4.** Form the ratio: $$ \eta = \frac{W \pi d_m \tan \alpha}{W \pi d_m \tan(\alpha+\phi)} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most important formula** for KTU ESE. Memorize the form: *"efficiency = tan(lead angle) / tan(lead angle + friction angle)"*. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Condition for Maximum Efficiency **Step 1.** Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $\alpha$ and set to zero. Since $\phi$ is a material/interface constant, only $\alpha$ varies. $$ \eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} $$ **Step 2.** Let $u = \tan \alpha$ and $v = \tan(\alpha + \phi)$. Then $\eta = u/v$. $$ \frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = \frac{v (du/d\alpha) - u (dv/d\alpha)}{v^2} = 0 $$ For maximum efficiency: $v(du/d\alpha) = u(dv/d\alpha)$. **Step 3.** Use $du/d\alpha = \sec^2 \alpha$ and $dv/d\alpha = \sec^2(\alpha + \phi)$: $$ \tan(\alpha + \phi) \sec^2 \alpha = \tan \alpha \sec^2(\alpha + \phi) $$ **Step 4.** Cross-multiply and rewrite $\sec^2 = 1 + \tan^2$: $$ \frac{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}{\tan \alpha} = \frac{\sec^2(\alpha + \phi)}{\sec^2 \alpha} = \frac{1 + \tan^2(\alpha + \phi)}{1 + \tan^2 \alpha} $$ **Step 5.** A cleaner route uses the half-angle substitution. After algebraic simplification (a standard result in Shigley/V.B. Bhandari), the optimal condition is: $$ \alpha = 45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2} $$ **Step 6.** Substitute back into the efficiency expression to obtain the **maximum achievable efficiency**: $$ \eta_{\max} = \frac{\tan(45^\circ - \phi/2)}{\tan(45^\circ - \phi/2 + \phi)} = \frac{\tan(45^\circ - \phi/2)}{\tan(45^\circ + \phi/2)} $$ Using the identity $\tan(45^\circ - \theta) \cdot \tan(45^\circ + \theta) = 1/\tan(45^\circ - \theta)/\tan(45^\circ + \theta)$ simplification yields: $$ \boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a problem states *“design the screw for maximum efficiency”*, the steps are: (i) assume thread friction coefficient → find $\phi$, (ii) compute $\alpha_{\text{opt}} = 45^\circ - \phi/2$, (iii) find $L = \pi d_m \tan \alpha_{\text{opt}}$, (iv) choose a standard $L$ from IS/ISO tables and iterate $d_m$. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Self-Locking Condition A screw is **self-locking** if it can hold the axial load **W** without any back-driving tendency. From the lowering-torque expression: $$ T_2 = \frac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi) $$ For self-locking, the load must **not** rotate the screw backwards, i.e., $T_2 \ge 0$ for the screw to *resist* downward motion (equivalently, $T_2 \le 0$ means the load lowers by itself, breaking the holding function). Setting $T_2 = 0$: $$ \tan(\alpha - \phi) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \phi $$ For the screw to be *just* self-locking: $\alpha \le \phi$, i.e., $$ \boxed{\tan \alpha \le \mu} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write "self-locking means $T_2 = 0$" and stop. The full condition is $\alpha \le \phi$ (i.e., lead angle less than friction angle). If $\alpha > \phi$, the screw *back-drives* — it is an **overrunning screw**, used in turbine governor mechanisms and quick-return applications. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Efficiency Variation with α and μ | Friction Coeff. (μ) | Friction Angle (φ) | Optimum α = 45° − φ/2 | η_max = (1−sinφ)/(1+sinφ) | Self-Locking Boundary (α = φ) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0.05 | 2.86° | 43.57° | 90.5% | 2.86° | | 0.10 | 5.71° | 42.14° | 81.5% | 5.71° | | 0.15 | 8.53° | 40.73° | 73.5% | 8.53° | | 0.20 | 11.31° | 39.34° | 66.2% | 11.31° | | 0.30 | 16.70° | 36.65° | 53.5% | 16.70° | | 0.50 | 26.57° | 31.72° | 35.6% | 26.57° | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** As friction increases, the optimum lead angle *decreases* and the maximum achievable efficiency *drops sharply*. This is why **lubricated ball-screw actuators** (with recirculating ball bearings) can reach η > 90%, while **plain sliding screw jacks** typically achieve only 30–50%. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1 — Unwrapped Thread as an Inclined Plane ```mermaid flowchart LR A["W = Axial Load
acting downward on nut"] --> B["Inclined plane
inclination = lead angle alpha"] P["P = Tangential Force
applied at radius dm/2"] --> B B --> C["Normal Reaction R
perpendicular to plane"] B --> D["Friction Force mu R
opposes motion along plane"] C --> E["Equilibrium equations
along and perpendicular to plane"] D --> E E --> F["Result T1 and T2
in N-mm"] F --> G["Efficiency eta
= tan alpha / tan alpha + phi"] ``` ## 4.2 Figure 2 — Force Balance Block Diagram (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUTS["INPUT PARAMETERS"] A1["Axial Load W"] A2["Mean Diameter dm"] A3["Lead L"] A4["Friction Coeff mu"] end subgraph GEOMETRY["GEOMETRY BLOCK"] B1["alpha = arctan L divided by pi dm"] B2["phi = arctan mu"] end subgraph SOLVER["TORQUE & EFFICIENCY SOLVER"] C1["T_raise = W dm/2 times tan alpha + phi"] C2["T_lower = W dm/2 times tan alpha - phi"] C3["eta = tan alpha divided by tan alpha + phi"] end subgraph DESIGN["DESIGN CHECKS"] D1["alpha less than phi equal to SELF LOCKING"] D2["alpha = 45 deg minus phi/2 equal to MAXIMUM EFFICIENCY"] end A1 --> C1 A1 --> C2 A2 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A1 --> D1 A4 --> B2 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B1 --> C3 B2 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B2 --> C3 C1 --> D2 C3 --> D2 ``` ## 4.3 Figure 3 — Decision Tree: Self-Locking vs Back-Driving ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is lead angle alpha greater than friction angle phi?"} Q1 -- "YES alpha greater than phi" --> R1["BACK DRIVING SCREW
Load lowers on its own
Application: quick release, governor"] Q1 -- "NO alpha less than phi" --> R2["SELF LOCKING SCREW
Load holds without brake
Application: screw jack, clamp, lift"] R1 --> S1["eta greater than 50 percent
High speed of operation"] R2 --> S2["eta less than 50 percent
Inherently safe holding"] ``` ## 4.4 Figure 4 — Efficiency vs Lead Angle Curve (Qualitative Shape) ```mermaid flowchart LR X["alpha = 0 deg"] --> Y1["eta = 0"] Y1 --> Y2["eta increases with alpha"] Y2 --> Y3["eta reaches MAX at alpha = 45 deg minus phi/2"] Y3 --> Y4["eta decreases after maximum"] Y4 --> Z["alpha approaches 90 deg, eta approaches 0"] Y3 --> Y5["Maximum Efficiency
eta_max = 1 minus sin phi divided by 1 plus sin phi"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reading of the Curve:** The efficiency is **zero at both extremes** ($\alpha = 0$ — no axial motion; $\alpha = 90°$ — friction dominates). There is a single peak in between, located at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$. Designers typically operate on the *ascending* side of the curve (low α, high mechanical advantage, inherently self-locking) for force applications, and on the *descending* side (high α, faster motion) for kinematic applications like lead screws. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 (Conceptual — Definition)** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Q.1.a]** > *"Define the efficiency of a power screw. State the condition for maximum efficiency of a square-threaded power screw."* **Model Answer:** The efficiency of a power screw is defined as the ratio of work output to work input per revolution: $$ \eta = \frac{W \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot T_1} = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} $$ The condition for **maximum efficiency** is obtained by differentiating $\eta$ with respect to $\alpha$ and setting it to zero: $$ \boxed{\alpha = 45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}} $$ The corresponding maximum efficiency is: $$ \boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi}} $$ ### **Question A2 (Conceptual — Self-Locking)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q.1.b]** > *"What is meant by self-locking in a power screw? Give two practical examples where self-locking is essential."* **Model Answer:** A power screw is said to be **self-locking** if it can hold the axial load **W** without the application of any holding torque, i.e., the load does not cause the screw to rotate backwards. Mathematically, the self-locking condition is: $$ \alpha \le \phi \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tan \alpha \le \mu $$ **Two practical examples requiring self-locking:** 1. **Screw Jack** (used to lift a car) — the jack must not unwind under the vehicle's weight when the handle is released. 2. **Lathe Tailstock Clamp** or **G-Clamp** — the clamp must not loosen under the workpiece reaction force. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question B1.A (Numerical — Torque and Efficiency of Square Thread)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q.2.a/b]** > *"A square-threaded power screw has a nominal diameter of 50 mm and a pitch of 8 mm. The screw is required to lift a load of 50 kN. The coefficient of thread friction is 0.12. Calculate:* > *(a) The torque required to raise the load, and the torque required to lower the load.* > *(b) The efficiency of the screw, and check whether the screw is self-locking."* #### **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Nominal (outer) diameter $d_0 = 50$ mm - Pitch $p = 8$ mm → for single-start square thread, **Lead $L = p = 8$ mm** - Load $W = 50{,}000$ N - Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.12$ **Step 1 — Mean Diameter.** For a square thread, the thread depth equals $p/2$: $$ d_i = d_0 - p = 50 - 8 = 42 \text{ mm} $$ $$ d_m = \frac{d_0 + d_i}{2} = \frac{50 + 42}{2} = 46 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Lead Angle.** $$ \tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d_m} = \frac{8}{\pi \times 46} = \frac{8}{144.51} = 0.05536 $$ $$ \alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05536) = 3.17^\circ $$ **Step 3 — Friction Angle.** $$ \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.84^\circ $$ **Step 4 — Torque to Raise the Load (T₁).** $$ T_1 = \frac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi) $$ $$ \alpha + \phi = 3.17^\circ + 6.84^\circ = 10.01^\circ $$ $$ \tan(10.01^\circ) = 0.1766 $$ $$ T_1 = \frac{50{,}000 \times 46}{2} \times 0.1766 = 1{,}150{,}000 \times 0.1766 = 203{,}090 \text{ N·mm} $$ $$ \boxed{T_1 \approx 203.1 \text{ N·m}} $$ **Step 5 — Torque to Lower the Load (T₂).** $$ T_2 = \frac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi) $$ $$ \alpha - \phi = 3.17^\circ - 6.84^\circ = -3.67^\circ $$ $$ \tan(-3.67^\circ) = -0.0642 $$ $$ T_2 = 1{,}150{,}000 \times (-0.0642) = -73{,}830 \text{ N·mm} $$ $$ \boxed{T_2 \approx -73.8 \text{ N·m}} $$ The **negative sign** indicates that the load tends to *lower itself*; a holding torque of 73.8 N·m must be applied in the *opposite* direction to lower the load gently. **Step 6 — Efficiency.** $$ \eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \frac{0.05536}{0.1766} = 0.3135 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta \approx 31.35\%} $$ **Step 7 — Self-Locking Check.** $$ \alpha = 3.17^\circ < \phi = 6.84^\circ \quad \checkmark $$ The condition $\alpha < \phi$ is satisfied → **the screw is self-locking.** ✓ --- ### **Question B1.B (Alternative Choice — Design for Maximum Efficiency)** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Q.2.a/b Alternative]** > *"A single-start square-threaded power screw of mean diameter 40 mm is to lift a load of 80 kN. The coefficient of thread friction is 0.10.* > *(a) Design the screw (i.e., find the lead) for maximum efficiency. Compute the maximum efficiency and the required torque.* > *(b) If the lead is doubled, calculate the new efficiency and comment on the self-locking property."* #### **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Mean diameter $d_m = 40$ mm - Load $W = 80{,}000$ N - Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.10$ **Step 1 — Friction Angle.** $$ \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71^\circ $$ **Step 2 — Lead Angle for Maximum Efficiency.** $$ \alpha_{\text{opt}} = 45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2} = 45^\circ - 2.855^\circ = 42.145^\circ $$ **Step 3 — Required Lead.** $$ \tan(42.145^\circ) = 0.9054 $$ $$ L = \pi d_m \tan \alpha_{\text{opt}} = \pi \times 40 \times 0.9054 = 113.78 \text{ mm} $$ Round to a standard value: $L \approx 114$ mm (or select nearest from IS 4694 square-thread series). **Step 4 — Maximum Efficiency.** $$ \eta_{\max} = \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi} = \frac{1 - \sin(5.71^\circ)}{1 + \sin(5.71^\circ)} = \frac{1 - 0.0995}{1 + 0.0995} = 0.8182 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{\max} \approx 81.8\%} $$ **Step 5 — Torque at Maximum Efficiency.** $$ T_1 = \frac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi) = \frac{80{,}000 \times 40}{2} \times \tan(47.855^\circ) $$ $$ \tan(47.855^\circ) = 1.1054 $$ $$ T_1 = 1{,}600{,}000 \times 1.1054 = 1{,}768{,}640 \text{ N·mm} \approx 1768.6 \text{ N·m} $$ **Step 6 — If Lead is Doubled (L = 227.56 mm).** New lead angle: $$ \tan \alpha' = \frac{L'}{\pi d_m} = \frac{227.56}{\pi \times 40} = 1.8108 \Rightarrow \alpha' = 61.1^\circ $$ New efficiency: $$ \eta' = \frac{\tan(61.1^\circ)}{\tan(61.1^\circ + 5.71^\circ)} = \frac{1.8108}{\tan(66.81^\circ)} = \frac{1.8108}{2.3396} = 0.7740 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta' \approx 77.4\%} $$ **Step 7 — Self-Locking Check with Doubled Lead.** $$ \alpha' = 61.1^\circ > \phi = 5.71^\circ $$ Condition $\alpha > \phi$ → the screw is **NOT self-locking**. It will back-drive. A **holding brake** is mandatory. **Comment:** Doubling the lead *reduces* efficiency slightly and *destroys* self-locking. The original design (L = 114 mm) is the optimum trade-off. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing pitch and lead:** For a single-start thread, $L = p$, but for double-start threads, $L = 2p$. Many students use $L = p$ even for multi-start screws. **Always check the number of thread starts** from the problem statement or figure. > 2. **Forgetting the thread-angle correction for V-threads:** For ACME/Trapezoidal threads, replace $\mu$ by $\mu' = \mu/\sec(\beta/2)$ *before* computing $\phi$. Using raw $\mu$ in the V-thread formula yields **inflated efficiency** and is a guaranteed mark-deduction. > 3. **Sign error in T₂:** $T_2$ can be negative. A negative value means *holding torque* is required to lower the load. Do not write "T₂ = 0" just to avoid the sign issue — interpret it physically. > 4. **Mean diameter definition:** Some texts use $d_m = d_0 - p/2$ (square thread) or $d_m = d_0 - 0.6495p$ (ACME). Use the **standard KTU convention** given in the problem data. When in doubt, write $d_m = (d_0 + d_i)/2$ explicitly. > 5. **Units:** $T_1$ comes out in **N·mm** if $W$ is in N and $d_m$ in mm.
