Convert to N·m by dividing by 1000 *before* finalising the answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Efficiency formula (square thread):** $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ — memorize verbatim. - ✅ **Torque to raise:** $T_1 = \dfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — friction adds to the lead angle. - ✅ **Torque to lower:** $T_2 = \dfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ — friction opposes the lead angle. - ✅ **Mean diameter:** $d_m = (d_0 + d_i)/2$. - ✅ **Lead angle:** $\tan \alpha = L / (\pi d_m)$. - ✅ **Friction angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$. - ✅ **Maximum efficiency condition:** $\alpha = 45^\circ - \phi/2$. - ✅ **Maximum efficiency value:** $\eta_{\max} = (1 - \sin \phi) / (1 + \sin \phi)$. - ✅ **Self-locking condition:** $\alpha \le \phi$ (i.e., $\tan \alpha \le \mu$). - ✅ **Back-driving condition:** $\alpha > \phi$ (overrunning screw — needs brake). - ✅ **V-thread correction:** $\mu' = \mu / \sec(\beta/2)$ and $\phi' = \tan^{-1}(\mu')$. - ✅ **Overall efficiency** (with collar bearing): $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{screw}} \times \eta_{\text{collar}}$. - ✅ **Practical range of η for sliding screws:** 30–50% (self-locking) to 80–90% (kinematic, lubricated/ball-screw). - ✅ **Rule of thumb:** For minimum friction loss in a *force-applying* screw, $\alpha \approx 3°$ to $5°$ (heavily self-locking). For *motion-transmitting* screws, $\alpha \approx 25°$ to $42°$ (high efficiency). ---

topics.title: Riveted Joint: Stresses in Riveted Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Riveted Joint: Stresses in Riveted Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Riveted Joint?

A **Riveted Joint** is a permanent, non-detachable fastening arrangement in which two or more plates/sheets of metal are held together by means of **metal pins (rivets)** that are plastically deformed (headed) on one or both ends after insertion into pre-formed holes. The deformation creates a mechanical lock that resists both **shear** and **tensile** loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> A riveted joint is classified as a *non-loose joint* under permanent joints, where the load transfer between the connected plates occurs through the rivets in the form of **shear**, **bearing (crushing)**, and the plates themselves experience **tearing (tensile) stress**.

## 1.2 Why Study Stresses in a Riveted Joint?

When a riveted joint is subjected to a tensile load $P$ (acting along the axis of the plate), the following three primary stress systems are developed simultaneously:

1. **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** in the rivet shank.
2. **Bearing / Crushing Stress ($\sigma_b$)** on the projected contact area between rivet and plate hole.
3. **Tearing / Tensile Stress ($\sigma_t$)** in the plate material along the weakest section (net section).

Failure of the joint occurs if **any one** of these stresses exceeds its corresponding permissible value.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stitched Cloth"

Imagine two pieces of thick leather being stitched together with metal nails (rivets):

- The **nails (rivets)** act like little metal pegs that get bent at the back — they try to **slide** apart when you pull the leather. That sliding resistance is **shear**.
- The **holes** in the leather push back against the nails' cylindrical surface — that compressive contact is **bearing (crushing)**.
- The **leather** itself, between the stitch holes, has a reduced cross-section (because the holes are punched out). It is weaker there and can **tear apart** along the line of holes — that is **tearing (tensile) stress**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A well-designed riveted joint must be a **balanced design** — the joint strength should ideally be governed by all three modes of failure simultaneously (uniform strength), so that no single component is overstressed or underutilized.

## 1.4 Assumptions Standard to KTU Board Examination

1. The load $P$ is uniformly distributed among all the rivets of the joint.
2. The load is carried equally by the rivets in a row.
3. The friction between the plates is **neglected**.
4. Shear stress is uniformly distributed over the rivet cross-section.
5. Bearing pressure is uniform on the projected diameter area of the rivet.
6. The plates behave as perfectly elastic, and stress concentration near holes is neglected in basic design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stresses in a Riveted Joint

### A. Shearing Stress in the Rivet ($\tau$)

When the plate tries to slide relative to the rivet, the rivet shank resists this motion through shear across its cross-section.

- **Single Shear**: Occurs in **lap joint** and in **outer plates of butt joint with single cover plate**.
- **Double Shear**: Occurs in **butt joint with double cover plates** (the rivet is sheared in two planes simultaneously).
- **Triple Shear**: Occurs in **butt joint with one cover plate and main plate of double thickness**, or special configurations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Examiner's will most commonly test the difference between single and double shear. Always multiply by the number of shear planes $n_s$ when calculating rivet shear area.

### B. Bearing / Crushing Stress ($\sigma_b$)

The cylindrical surface of the rivet presses against the inner surface of the plate hole. The **projected bearing area** is taken as:

$$A_b = d \times t$$

where $d$ is the rivet diameter and $t$ is the **thinner of the two plates** being joined.

### C. Tearing / Tensile Stress in the Plate ($\sigma_t$)

The plate is weakened by the rivet hole. The **net section** available to resist tension is:

$$A_t = (p - d) \times t$$

where $p$ is the **pitch** of the rivets (centre-to-centre distance between consecutive rivets in a row).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU numerical questions on riveted joints.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Rivet shear area (single) | $A_s = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Lap joint, one shear plane |
| 2 | Rivet shear area (double) | $A_s = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Butt joint, two cover plates |
| 3 | Bearing / crushing area | $A_b = d \cdot t$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | $t$ = thinner plate |
| 4 | Tearing / net tensile area | $A_t = (p - d) \cdot t$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | $p$ = pitch, $d$ = hole dia |
| 5 | Shear strength of rivet | $P_s = n_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau$ | N | $n_s$ = number of shear planes |
| 6 | Bearing strength of rivet | $P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | N | Per rivet per plate thickness |
| 7 | Tearing strength of plate | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Per pitch length |
| 8 | Strength of joint in shear | $P_s = n \cdot n_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau$ | N | $n$ = total number of rivets |
| 9 | Strength of joint in bearing | $P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | N | $n$ = total number of rivets |
| 10 | Strength of joint in tearing | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Per pitch length |
| 11 | Efficiency of joint | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{weakest}}}{P_{\text{solid plate}}} \times 100\,\%$ | \% | Solid plate strength = $p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ |
| 12 | Unwin's formula for pitch | $p = C \cdot d$ | mm | $C = 1.31, 1.41, 1.5$ for steel |
| 13 | Diametral pitch (zig-zag) | $p = 2d + \dots$ | mm | See derivation in §3.6 |

## 2.3 Modes of Failure in a Riveted Joint

| Mode of Failure | Location | Governing Equation | Permissible Stress (Steel) |
|----------------|----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Tearing of plate | Plate net section | $P_t = (p - d)t\sigma_t$ | $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa (structural) |
| Shearing of rivet | Rivet cross-section | $P_s = n_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau$ | $\tau = 70$–$84$ MPa |
| Crushing / bearing | Rivet-hole contact | $P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | $\sigma_b = 126$–$140$ MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Stresses in riveted joints are foundational to the design of **pressure vessels, boiler shells, bridges, railway wagons, and aircraft fuselage panels**. The modern shift to **welding and bolting** has not eliminated riveted joints — they are still preferred where **fatigue resistance, fire safety, or non-sparking requirements** dominate (e.g., chemical plants, ammunition boxes).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shear Strength of a Single Rivet

Consider a rivet of nominal diameter $d$ subjected to a single shear plane under load $P$.

The rivet shank cross-section resists the load through shear. The shear area is the full circular cross-section of the rivet:

$$A_s = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

The average shear stress developed is:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A_s} = \frac{P}{\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

Rearranging for the **shear strength** of one rivet (i.e., the maximum load it can carry before yielding in shear):

$$P_s = A_s \cdot \tau = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau$$

> **Engineering Logic:** This is the **single-shear** condition. For **double shear** (butt joint with two cover plates), the rivet has two shear planes, so the capacity is doubled:
>
> $$P_{s,\text{double}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bearing / Crushing Strength

The rivet bears against the plate hole over a projected area. This projected area is the **rectangle** formed by the rivet diameter $d$ projected onto the plate thickness $t$:

$$A_b = d \cdot t$$

The bearing (crushing) stress is:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}$$

The **bearing strength** per rivet is:

$$P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

> **Engineering Logic:** Since bearing failure appears on **both** sides of the joint (top and bottom plate, or cover plate and main plate), the **thinner of the two plates** is always used in the calculation to ensure the joint is conservatively safe.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tearing / Tensile Strength of the Plate

When a tensile load $P$ passes through a plate carrying rivet holes spaced at pitch $p$, the plate is effectively "perforated" along the line of holes. The cross-section that resists the load is the **net section**:

$$A_{\text{net}} = (p - d) \cdot t$$

The tensile (tearing) stress on this net section is:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_{\text{net}}} = \frac{P}{(p - d) \cdot t}$$

The **tearing strength** per pitch length is:

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

> **Engineering Logic:** This is the **most critical** stress in design because the plate's net section is invariably the weakest link. KTU problems typically ask to find the **pitch** that just makes the joint safe against tearing.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Efficiency of a Riveted Joint

**Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the strength of the riveted joint (at the weakest failure mode) to the strength of the **un-perforated (solid) plate** of the same dimensions.

The strength of a solid plate of width $p$ (one pitch length) and thickness $t$ is:

$$P_{\text{solid}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

The strength of the riveted joint in tearing is:

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

Therefore, the **efficiency** is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_t}{P_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

Simplifying (the $t$ and $\sigma_t$ cancel out):

$$\eta = \frac{p - d}{p} = 1 - \frac{d}{p}$$

In percentage form:

$$\eta_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) \times 100$$

> **Engineering Logic:** Higher efficiency means the riveted joint preserves more of the plate's natural strength. A **100% efficient** joint would require the rivet to occupy **zero diameter** — physically impossible. The realistic upper limit is around **70–80%** for steel structures.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Pitch of the Rivets (Unwin's Formula)

Unwin's empirical formula gives a quick estimate of pitch for steel plates:

$$p = C \cdot d$$

where $C$ is a constant depending on the type of plate and joint:

- $C = 1.31$ for tight riveted joints (best quality, sealed joints).
- $C = 1.41$ for ordinary riveted joints.
- $C = 1.56$ for rough work.

A more **rigorous derivation** from the balance of shear, bearing, and tearing strengths gives the pitch required to make the **tearing strength equal to the rivet shear strength**:

$$(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = n_s \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p - d = \frac{n_s \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$p = d + \frac{n_s \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

> **Engineering Logic:** This is the **balanced design** pitch — at this pitch, the plate would tear just as the rivet shears. Below this pitch, the rivet is overstressed; above this pitch, the plate is overstressed.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Diametral Pitch for Zig-Zag (Staggered) Riveting

In chain riveting, all rivets lie in a single row. In **zig-zag riveting**, the second row is offset by half the pitch. To prevent the net section from falling below the chain-rivet net section, the **diametral pitch** $p_d$ (transverse spacing between rows) is calculated.

Consider a zig-zag arrangement with longitudinal pitch $p$ and diametral pitch $p_d$. The section through the staggered row has a slightly different geometry. By equating the net strength of this diagonal section to the strength of the chain-rivet section, the standard result is:

$$p_d = \sqrt{(p^2 - d^2) - \frac{1}{4}p^2}$$

After algebraic simplification, the commonly tabulated formula becomes:

$$p_d = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{3p^2 - 4d^2}$$

> **Engineering Logic:** This guarantees that the **diagonal (staggered) tearing** path through the plate is at least as strong as the **straight (chain)** path. The staggered arrangement is preferred in pressure vessels and boilers where maximum plate strength retention is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Interaction Map in a Riveted Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applied Tensile Load P on Plate"] --> B["Load Distributes to Rivets"]
    A --> C["Load Distributes in Plate Material"]

    B --> B1["Shear Stress tau in Rivet Shank"]
    B --> B2["Bearing Stress sigma_b at Hole Wall"]

    C --> C1["Tearing Stress sigma_t at Net Section"]
    C --> C2["Shear Lag Near Rivet Hole"]

    B1 --> D{"Strength Check"}
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D

    D --> E["P_s = pi d^2 / 4 times tau"]
    D --> F["P_b = d times t times sigma_b"]
    D --> G["P_t = p minus d times t times sigma_t"]

    E --> H["Compare with Load P"]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I["Design Safe if all three strengths exceed P"]
    H --> J["Design Fails at weakest mode"]

    I --> K["Efficiency eta = P weakest over P solid"]
    J --> L["Revise pitch p or rivet diameter d"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Riveted Joints and Shear Planes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Riveted Joint Classification"] --> B["Lap Joint"]
    A --> C["Butt Joint"]

    B --> B1["Single Riveted Lap"]
    B --> B2["Double Riveted Lap"]
    B --> B3["Chain or Zig-Zag Pattern"]

    C --> C1["Butt with Single Cover Plate"]
    C --> C2["Butt with Double Cover Plates"]

    B1 --> S1["Single Shear Plane per rivet n_s = 1"]
    B2 --> S1

    C1 --> S2["Double Shear Plane per rivet n_s = 2"]
    C2 --> S2

    S1 --> R1["Shear strength = pi d^2 / 4 times tau"]
    S2 --> R2["Shear strength = 2 times pi d^2 / 4 times tau"]

    R1 --> OUT["Governed by weakest of shear, bearing, tearing"]
    R2 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Load P Applied on Riveted Joint"] --> q1{"Rivet shear stress exceeds permissible tau?"}
    q1 -->|Yes| fail1["Failure: Shearing of Rivet"]
    q1 -->|No| q2{"Bearing stress exceeds permissible sigma_b?"}
    q2 -->|Yes| fail2["Failure: Crushing of Plate"]
    q2 -->|No| q3{"Tearing stress exceeds permissible sigma_t?"}
    q3 -->|Yes| fail3["Failure: Tearing of Plate"]
    q3 -->|No| safe["Joint is Safe in all three modes"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In a properly designed KTU problem, you must verify **all three** failure modes. Many students only check one or two — this guarantees marks deduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
> List the **three primary stresses** developed in a riveted joint. State the formula for the **net section area** in each case.

### Model Answer (A1):

The three primary stresses in a riveted joint are:

1. **Shear stress** in the rivet, with shear area:
$$A_s = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \quad \text{(per shear plane)}$$

2. **Bearing / crushing stress** at the rivet-plate interface, with bearing area:
$$A_b = d \cdot t \quad \text{(projected area, } t = \text{thinner plate)}$$

3. **Tearing / tensile stress** in the plate, with net area:
$$A_t = (p - d) \cdot t \quad \text{(pitch length minus hole diameter)}$$

The permissible values for structural steel are typically:
- $\tau = 70$–$84$ MPa
- $\sigma_b = 126$–$140$ MPa
- $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
> Define the **efficiency of a riveted joint**. For a joint with $p = 50$ mm and $d = 20$ mm, compute the efficiency.

### Model Answer (A2):

**Efficiency** is the ratio of the strength of the riveted joint (at the weakest failure mode) to the strength of the corresponding un-perforated (solid) plate, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint, weakest}}}{P_{\text{solid plate}}} \times 100\%$$

For tearing mode (the most common case):

$$\eta = \frac{(p - d)}{p} \times 100\%$$

Substituting $p = 50$ mm, $d = 20$ mm:

$$\eta = \frac{50 - 20}{50} \times 100\% = \frac{30}{50} \times 100\% = 60\%$$

> **Comment:** A 60% efficiency is typical for moderate-quality riveted joints; pressure vessels aim for **70–80%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 2, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> A **double-riveted lap joint** is used to connect two steel plates each of thickness $t = 12$ mm and width $b = 200$ mm. The rivet diameter is $d = 20$ mm, the pitch is $p = 60$ mm, and the permissible stresses are:
> - Shear in rivet: $\tau = 80$ MPa
> - Crushing / bearing: $\sigma_b = 140$ MPa
> - Tearing in plate: $\sigma_t = 90$ MPa
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The **shear strength** of the joint. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** The **bearing strength** of the joint. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Shear Strength

**Given:**
- Lap joint → single shear plane per rivet, so $n_s = 1$
- Number of rivets $n = 2$ (double-riveted)
- $d = 20$ mm, $\tau = 80$ MPa

Shear area per rivet (single shear):

$$A_s = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2$$

Shear strength per rivet:

$$P_{s,\text{per rivet}} = A_s \times \tau = 314.159 \times 80 = 25132.7 \text{ N}$$

Total shear strength of joint:

$$P_s = n \times n_s \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times \tau = 2 \times 1 \times 314.159 \times 80$$

$$\boxed{P_s = 50265.5 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Bearing Strength

**Given:**
- Thinner plate thickness: $t = 12$ mm (both plates equal, so $t = 12$ mm)
- Number of rivets $n = 2$, $d = 20$ mm, $\sigma_b = 140$ MPa

Bearing area per rivet:

$$A_b = d \times t = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ mm}^2$$

Bearing strength per rivet:

$$P_{b,\text{per rivet}} = A_b \times \sigma_b = 240 \times 140 = 33600 \text{ N}$$

Total bearing strength of joint:

$$P_b = n \times d \times t \times \sigma_b = 2 \times 20 \times 12 \times 140$$

$$\boxed{P_b = 67200 \text{ N} = 67.2 \text{ kN}}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $P_s = 50.27$ kN < $P_b = 67.2$ kN, the joint is governed by **shear failure of the rivet** as the weakest mode.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> A **butt joint with two cover plates** connects two main steel plates of thickness $t = 10$ mm. The rivet diameter is $d = 18$ mm, the pitch is $p = 50$ mm, and there are 4 rivets per side in **chain riveting** (so 2 rows of 2 rivets per side, total 4 per side × 2 sides = 8 rivets in the joint, but for **single-row analysis** consider 2 rivets per pitch length). The permissible stresses are:
> - Shear in rivet: $\tau = 75$ MPa
> - Crushing / bearing: $\sigma_b = 130$ MPa
> - Tearing in plate: $\sigma_t = 85$ MPa
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The **tearing strength** of the plate per pitch length. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** The **efficiency** of the joint. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Tearing Strength

**Given:**
- Butt joint with two cover plates → main plate thickness $t = 10$ mm
- $p = 50$ mm, $d = 18$ mm, $\sigma_t = 85$ MPa

Net section area (per pitch length):

$$A_t = (p - d) \times t = (50 - 18) \times 10 = 32 \times 10 = 320 \text{ mm}^2$$

Tearing strength per pitch length:

$$P_t = A_t \times \sigma_t = 320 \times 85 = 27200 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_t = 27200 \text{ N} = 27.2 \text{ kN per pitch length}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Efficiency

Solid plate strength per pitch length:

$$P_{\text{solid}} = p \times t \times \sigma_t = 50 \times 10 \times 85 = 42500 \text{ N} = 42.5 \text{ kN}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_t}{P_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{27200}{42500} = 0.64$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 64\%}$$

> **Comment:** The 64% efficiency falls within the typical range of 60–70% for steel riveted joints.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 3–5 Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing single and double shear:** Students forget to multiply by $n_s = 2$ in butt joints with double cover plates. This **doubles** the rivet shear strength but students leave it out.
> 2. **Using the wrong plate thickness in bearing:** Always use the **thinner** of the two plates (or the cover plate if it's thinner than the main plate) in $A_b = d \times t$.
> 3. **Confusing pitch and diametral pitch:** $p$ is the **longitudinal** pitch (along the load direction); $p_d$ is the **transverse** pitch (between rows). These are **different** quantities.
> 4. **Forgetting to check all three failure modes:** A joint is only as strong as its **weakest** mode. You must compute $P_s$, $P_b$, AND $P_t$ and compare.
> 5. **Sign errors in efficiency:** Efficiency is always between 0 and 1 (or 0% and 100%). A value > 100% indicates a calculation error.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Convert all stresses to N/mm² (MPa) and areas to mm² so that load comes out in Newtons (N).
> 7. **Neglecting the rule "thin plate governs":** In bearing, the thinner plate is always critical. In a butt joint, this is the **cover plate** (typically) since it's usually thinner than the main plate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU Exam:**

- A riveted joint develops **three concurrent stresses**: shear in rivet, bearing at hole, tearing in plate.
- **Shear area** for single shear: $A_s = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$; for double shear: multiply by **2**.
- **Bearing area** is **projected**: $A_b = d \times t$ where $t$ = thinner plate.
- **Tearing area** is the **net section**: $A_t = (p - d) \times t$.
- The joint is **safe** only if all three strengths ($P_s$, $P_b$, $P_t$) exceed the applied load $P$.
- **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{weakest}}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$.
- **Unwin's formula** gives a quick pitch estimate: $p = C \cdot d$ with $C = 1.31$ to $1.56$.
- For **balanced design** (tearing = rivet shear): $p = d + \dfrac{n_s \pi d^2 \tau}{4 t \sigma_t}$.
- For **zig-zag riveting**: diametral pitch $p_d = 0.5\sqrt{3p^2 - 4d^2}$.
- Standard permissible stresses for **structural steel**: $\tau \approx 80$ MPa, $\sigma_b \approx 140$ MPa, $\sigma_t \approx 90$ MPa.
- **Lap joint** → single shear; **Butt joint with two cover plates** → double shear.
- Always verify **all three** failure modes — not just one.
- **Cover plate thickness** in butt joint is typically $\dfrac{5}{8}$ to $\dfrac{6}{8}$ of main plate thickness.
- **Thinner plate governs** in bearing calculations — use the minimum thickness.

---

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Riveted Joint: Stresses in Riveted Joint**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Riveted Joints with Central and Eccentric Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Riveted Joints with Central and Eccentric Loads # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Riveted Joint? A **riveted joint** is a permanent, non-reloadable fastening formed by inserting a ductile metal pin (the *rivet*) through pre-formed holes in overlapping or abutting plates, then plastically deforming the tail end to form a second head. The resulting interference fit, combined with the bearing contact on the hole wall, transfers load between the connected members by **shear in the rivet shank** and **bearing (crushing) on the hole wall**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):** > *A riveted joint is a mechanical fastening in which the connected plates are held together by the plastic deformation of a ductile pin (rivet) whose shear and bearing strength is used to transmit the applied load between members.* --- ## 1.2 Two Categories of Loading — The Governing Question The design procedure for a riveted joint changes drastically depending on **where the line of action of the load passes** relative to the **Centroid (G)** of the rivet group: | Type of Load | Line of Action | Resulting Stresses | Engineering Domain | |---|---|---|---| | **Central Load (Concentric)** | Passes through **G** | Pure direct shear on every rivet | Pressure vessels, boiler shells | | **Eccentric Load** | Passes at distance **e** from **G** | Direct shear **+ Torsional shear** | Bracket connections, crane jibs, machine frames | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Group Hanging a Frame > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy (Central Load):** Imagine four friends of equal strength lifting a heavy painting by holding the four corners of a frame, and the load is hung from a hook **at the exact center** of the frame. Each friend bears an equal share, and the force on each person is *vertically downward*. The math is simply `P/4` per person. > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy (Eccentric Load):** Now move the hanging hook so it is *closer to one corner* of the frame. The friend near the hook feels both a **downward pull (direct shear)** *and* a **twisting pull (torsion)** as the frame tries to rotate. The friend on the diagonally opposite corner feels the *least* twist but still bears direct shear. The *critical* friend is the one on the *load line of action*, farthest from center — they carry the most combined force. This physical intuition is exactly what the **F₁ + F₂ formula** captures in the eccentric-load analysis. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Riveted Joints (Foundation Block) $$ \text{Riveted Joints} = \begin{cases} \text{Lap Joint (single / multi-row)} \\ \text{Butt Joint (single-strap / double-strap)} \end{cases} $$ Standard physical constants used throughout this module: - **Unwin's formula** for rivet diameter: $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ mm, where $t$ = plate thickness in mm - **Pitch (p):** centre-to-centre distance of adjacent rivets - **Back pitch (p_b):** pitch perpendicular to load direction - **Diagonal pitch (p_d):** diagonal distance in zig-zag riveting - **Margin (m):** distance from rivet centre to plate edge - **Standard margin** $m \approx 1.5\,d$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Loewer–Unwin's Efficiency Theorem:** The strength of a riveted joint is the *lowest* of the four failure-loads — shearing of rivet, crushing of rivet, tearing of plate (gross/pitch-section), and tearing of plate at edge. The governing mode controls design. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Modes of Failure of a Riveted Joint A riveted joint can fail in **four** independent ways. The design must satisfy *all* four and the joint strength is governed by the *minimum* of the four. > [!NOTE] > **The Four Failure Modes (Memorise the spelling — this is a frequent 3-mark question):** > 1. **Tearing of plate between rivet hole and edge** (governed by margin) > 2. **Tearing of plate between adjacent rivet holes** (governed by pitch) > 3. **Shearing of the rivet** (governed by rivet cross-section) > 4. **Crushing / Bearing failure of the rivet (or plate hole wall)** (governed by projected area) --- ## 2.2 Derivation of the Four Failure Loads Let the applied tensile load on the joint be **P**, number of rivets **n**, rivet diameter **d**, plate thickness **t**, pitch **p**, margin **m**, allowable shear stress **τ**, allowable crushing stress **σ_c**, allowable tensile stress of plate **σ_t**. ### (i) Shearing Strength of the Rivet (P_s) Each rivet has cross-sectional area $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$. For $n$ rivets, total shear area $= n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$. $$ \boxed{\,P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2 \cdot \tau\,} $$ ### (ii) Crushing (Bearing) Strength (P_c) Bearing area per rivet = projected area $d \times t$. For $n$ rivets: $$ \boxed{\,P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c\,} $$ ### (iii) Tearing of Plate between Adjacent Holes (P_t, pitch section) Net resisting area per pitch length = $(p - d) \cdot t$. For $n$ rivets in single shear plane: $$ \boxed{\,P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t\,} $$ For $n$ rivets in *double* shear (butt joint with double strap), the resisting area is doubled: $$ \boxed{\,P_t = 2\,(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t\,} $$ ### (iv) Tearing of Plate at Edge (P_e) $$ \boxed{\,P_e = (e - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t\,} \quad \text{where } e \text{ is the edge distance} $$ --- ## 2.3 Efficiencies of the Riveted Joint **Efficiency** is the ratio of strength of the *joint* to the strength of the *un-pierced* (solid) plate of the same dimensions. $$ \eta_s = \frac{P_s}{P_p} = \frac{P_s}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \quad \text{(Shearing Efficiency)} $$ $$ \eta_c = \frac{P_c}{P_p} = \frac{n\,d\,\sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t} \quad \text{(Crushing Efficiency)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Exam Trap:** Many students report efficiency only with respect to the *tearing* failure. KTU examiners expect **two distinct efficiencies** — shearing efficiency and crushing efficiency — calculated independently. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Central and Eccentric Loading | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition of Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Unwin's rivet diameter | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | mm | $t$ in mm; structural joints | | 2 | Shear load on rivet | $P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2 \cdot \tau$ | N | Single shear plane | | 3 | Crushing load on rivet | $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | N | All joints | | 4 | Tearing load (pitch) | $P_t = (p-d)\cdot t\cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Single row, single shear | | 5 | Tearing load (butt-double strap) | $P_t = 2(p-d)\cdot t\cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Butt joint, double strap | | 6 | Shearing efficiency | $\eta_s = \dfrac{n\pi d^2 \tau}{4\,p\,t\,\sigma_t}$ | dimensionless | — | | 7 | Crushing efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{n\,d\,\sigma_c}{p\,\sigma_t}$ | dimensionless | — | | 8 | Direct shear per rivet (eccentric) | $F_1 = P/n$ | N | All rivets | | 9 | Torsional shear on rivet at radius $r_i$ | $F_2 = \dfrac{T\cdot r_i}{\sum r_i^{\,2}} = \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot r_i}{\sum r_i^{\,2}}$ | N | Rivet at distance $r_i$ from G | | 10 | Resultant shear on a rivet | $F = \sqrt{F_1^{\,2} + F_2^{\,2} + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta}$ | N | θ = angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$ | | 11 | Maximum resultant (critical rivet, on load line) | $F_{max} = F_1 + F_2$ | N | θ = 0° at the critical rivet | | 12 | Condition for no tension in rivets | $e \leq \dfrac{\sum r_i^{\,2}}{n \cdot y_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{6}$ | — | For bracket-type joints | | 13 | Maximum principal stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$ | MPa | For non-uniform stress rivet groups | > [!NOTE] > **Quick-Reference for $\sum r_i^2$:** > - For a rectangular pattern with $m$ rows × $n$ columns at spacing $a \times b$ centred at G: > $\sum r_i^2 = \dfrac{m\,n\,(m^2 - 1)\,a^2 + m\,n\,(n^2 - 1)\,b^2}{12}$ for even counts; for odd counts an analogous closed form applies. --- ## 2.5 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering | Real Application | Central / Eccentric? | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | Lap joint in boiler shell | **Central** | Hoop stress in cylinder acts axially through G of rivet group | | Bracket-on-column connection (I-beam flange) | **Eccentric** | Bolt/rivet group is offset from line of pull | | Crane pedestal mounting | **Eccentric** | Heavy moment arm produces twist | | Railway track joint splice | **Central** | Axial load through fish-plate rivets | | Lug-type pin connection | **Eccentric** | The pin lies at the load line, but rivet pattern is offset | > [!TIP] > In all eccentric cases the engineer *must* compute $\sum r_i^2$ explicitly. KTU numericals frequently specify only the geometric layout — students must compute radial distances to the **centroid**, not to the corner of the plate. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tearing Resistance of the Plate (Pitch Section) **Starting hypothesis:** Consider a single row of rivets of pitch $p$ joining two plates of thickness $t$. A tensile load $P$ flows through the plate. The plate's load-carrying section is *interrupted* by the rivet hole of diameter $d$. **Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of un-pierced plate per pitch length: $$ A_{gross} = p \cdot t $$ **Step 2:** Cross-sectional area removed by the hole: $$ A_{hole} = d \cdot t $$ **Step 3:** Net resisting area: $$ A_{net} = (p - d) \cdot t $$ **Step 4:** Equate to allowable stress $\sigma_t$: $$ P_t = A_{net} \cdot \sigma_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t $$ > This derivation is the *foundation* of the Loewer efficiency concept. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Eccentric Load on a Rivet Group (the $F_1 + F_2$ proof) **Setup:** A vertical tensile load $P$ is applied at a point on the *load line of action*, which is at a perpendicular distance $e$ from the **centroid G** of the rivet group. There are $n$ rivets. We want the shear force on an arbitrary rivet located at radial distance $r_i$ from G, and on the *critical* rivet. ### 3.2.1 Decomposition of the Eccentric Force A force $P$ acting at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroid is **statically equivalent** to: - A **pure force** $P$ acting *at G* (which produces only *direct* shear), and - A **couple** of moment $T = P \cdot e$ (which produces only *torsional* shear). ``` Direct component → F1 = P/n (uniformly distributed, parallel to P) Torsional component → F2ᵢ = T·rᵢ/Σrᵢ² (perpendicular to rᵢ, magnitude ∝ rᵢ) ``` ### 3.2.2 Magnitude of the Direct Shear (F₁) Force $P$ is shared equally by $n$ rivets: $$ \boxed{\,F_1 = \frac{P}{n}\,} $$ ### 3.2.3 Magnitude of the Torsional Shear (F₂) For a circular-shaft analogy, the shear stress at radius $r_i$ in a shaft of polar moment $J = \sum r_i^2$ (for discrete rivets) subjected to torque $T$ is: $$ \tau_i = \frac{T \cdot r_i}{J} $$ By the discrete analogue, the shear *force* on rivet $i$ is: $$ \boxed{\,F_{2i} = \frac{T \cdot r_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j^{\,2}} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j^{\,2}}\,} $$ ### 3.2.4 Direction of the Torsional Shear (F₂) The torsional shear force is **perpendicular to the radius vector** $r_i$ and oriented so that it is consistent with the direction of rotation of the couple $T$. Concretely, for a downward $P$ on the right of G, the moment $T$ is clockwise, and the torsional shear on every rivet is **clockwise-tangential**. ### 3.2.5 Resultant Force on Rivet $i$ The two components $F_1$ (downward) and $F_{2i}$ (tangential) meet at an angle $\theta_i$ at the rivet centre. The resultant is given by the **Law of Cosines**: $$ \boxed{\,F_i = \sqrt{F_1^{\,2} + F_{2i}^{\,2} + 2 F_1 F_{2i} \cos\theta_i}\,} $$ ### 3.2.6 The Critical Rivet (Maximum F) The critical rivet is the one where $\cos\theta_i$ is **most positive** (i.e. $\theta_i$ is **smallest**). Two important special cases arise in KTU-style problems: | Rivet Position | $\theta_i$ | Resultant | |---|---|---| | Rivet lying **on the load line of action** (farthest from G, on the *same side* as P) | $0°$ | $F_{max} = F_1 + F_{2,max}$ | | Rivet lying **on the load line of action, opposite side** | $180°$ | $F = \vert F_1 - F_2 \vert$ | | General rivet (corner of rectangular pattern) | $0° < \theta < 90°$ | $F = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1F_2\cos\theta}$ | --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Polar Second Moment for a Rectangular Rivet Pattern **Setup:** A rivet group of $m$ rows × $k$ columns, with column pitch $a$ and row pitch $b$, *centred* on the centroid G. For **odd** numbers of rows and columns (say, 3×3 with rivets at $(\pm a, 0), (0, \pm b), (\pm a, \pm b), (0, 0)$): $$ \sum r_i^2 = \sum (x_i^2 + y_i^2) $$ Explicit summation for 3 rows × 3 columns: - 1 rivet at centre: $r^2 = 0$ - 4 rivets on mid-edges: $r^2 = a^2$ (two of them) or $b^2$ (two of them) - 4 corner rivets: $r^2 = a^2 + b^2$ (each) $$ \boxed{\,\sum r_i^2 = 2\,(a^2 + b^2) + (a^2 + a^2) + (b^2 + b^2) = 4a^2 + 4b^2\,} $$ A **generalised closed form** for an $m \times k$ grid centred at G is: $$ \sum r_i^2 = \frac{m k (m^2 - 1)\,a^2 + m k (k^2 - 1)\,b^2}{12} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Joint Design ``` START │ ▼ Is load line through rivet-group centroid G? │ ├── YES → Central loading │ ├── Compute n = P / (allowable shear per rivet) │ ├── Check crushing: Pc = n·d·t·σc ≥ P │ ├── Check tearing: Pt = (p − d)·t·σt ≥ P │ └── Check margin: Pe = (e − d)·t·σt ≥ P │ └── NO → Eccentric loading ├── Compute e, Σrᵢ², ymax ├── Compute F1 = P/n ├── Compute F2max = P·e·ymax / Σrᵢ² ├── Identify critical rivet ├── Compute Fmax = F1 + F2max (if on load line) ├── Check Fmax ≤ allowable shear per rivet ├── Check crushing: Pc per rivet = d·t·σc ≥ Fmax ├── Check tearing: Pt per pitch = (p−d)·t·σt ≥ Fmax └── Check tension criterion: e ≤ Σrᵢ² / (n·ymax) × (1/6) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When the problem says *"design the joint"*, the expected outputs are (in order): (1) rivet diameter, (2) number of rivets $n$, (3) pitch $p$, (4) margin $m$, (5) all four-mode check. Skipping step (1) is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Eccentric Load on a Bracket **Problem:** A bracket carries a vertical load $P = 50\,\text{kN}$ acting at an eccentricity $e = 250\,\text{mm}$ from the centroid G of a rivet group. The group has **6 rivets** arranged in **two columns of three rows** with column pitch $a = 60\,\text{mm}$ and row pitch $b = 80\,\text{mm}$, symmetric about G. Plate thickness $t = 12\,\text{mm}$, rivet diameter $d = 24\,\text{mm}$. Allowable stresses: $\tau = 80\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 160\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_t = 120\,\text{MPa}$. Find the maximum shear force on the most-loaded rivet and check the joint for safety. ### Solution **Step 1: Polar second moment of the rivet group** For 3 rows × 2 columns centred at G, the rivet positions $(x, y)$ in mm are: $$ (0, +80),\ (0, -80),\ (0, 0),\ (+60, +80),\ (+60, -80),\ (-60, +80),\ (-60, -80) $$ Wait — we have 3 rows × 2 columns = 6 rivets. The y-coordinates are $-80, 0, +80$ (3 rows) and the x-coordinates are $\pm 60$ (2 columns). So: $$ \sum r_i^2 = \sum (x_i^2 + y_i^2) = 2\cdot 3 \cdot 60^2 + 6 \cdot \left(\frac{80^2 + 0 + 80^2}{3}\right) $$ Let us do this explicitly. Rivets and their radii squared: - $(-60, +80): r^2 = 3600 + 6400 = 10000$ - $(-60, 0): r^2 = 3600$ - $(-60, -80): r^2 = 10000$ - $(+60, +80): r^2 = 10000$ - $(+60, 0): r^2 = 3600$ - $(+60, -80): r^2 = 10000$ Summing: $$ \sum r_i^2 = 4 \times 10000 + 2 \times 3600 = 40000 + 7200 = 47200\,\text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 2: Direct shear on each rivet** $$ F_1 = \frac{P}{n} = \frac{50000}{6} = 8333.3\,\text{N} $$ **Step 3: Torsional moment** $$ T = P \cdot e = 50000 \times 250 = 1.25 \times 10^7\,\text{N·mm} $$ **Step 4: Identify the critical rivet** The load is vertical and acts at distance $e$ from G. The critical rivet is the one on the load line of action (i.e. lying on the vertical line through P), at the *farthest* distance from G. If the load line is along the y-axis through G, the critical rivets are $(0, +80)$ and $(0, -80)$, at radius $r_{max} = 80\,\text{mm}$. **Step 5: Torsional shear on critical rivet** $$ F_2 = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{\sum r_i^2} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^7 \times 80}{47200} = 21186.4\,\text{N} $$ **Step 6: Resultant on critical rivet (θ = 0° at this rivet)** $$ F_{max} = F_1 + F_2 = 8333.3 + 21186.4 = 29519.7\,\text{N} \approx 29.52\,\text{kN} $$ **Step 7: Check shear strength of one rivet** $$ P_{s,\text{one rivet}} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = \frac{\pi}{4} (24)^2 (80) = 36191.1\,\text{N} \approx 36.19\,\text{kN} $$ Since $F_{max} = 29.52\,\text{kN} < 36.19\,\text{kN}$ — **SAFE in shear**. ✓ **Step 8: Check crushing strength of one rivet** $$ P_{c,\text{one rivet}} = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 24 \times 12 \times 160 = 46080\,\text{N} = 46.08\,\text{kN} $$ Since $F_{max} = 29.52\,\text{kN} < 46.08\,\text{kN}$ — **SAFE in crushing**. ✓ **Step 9: Conclusion** The joint is safe under the given eccentric load. The dominant failure mode is **shear of the critical rivet**, which is the design constraint. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Riveted-Joint Design > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** Physical drawings of lap / butt joints and rivet patterns are reproduced as **functional architecture blocks** below. This single figure is worth 4–5 marks in a typical 7-mark sub-question. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define the term *efficiency of a riveted joint*. How is the *shearing efficiency* different from the *crushing efficiency*?** **Model Answer:** > *Efficiency of a riveted joint* is the ratio of the strength of the **jointed plate** (with rivet holes) to the strength of the **solid un-pierced plate** of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage. > > *Shearing efficiency* is the ratio of the joint strength governed by **rivet shear** to the strength of the solid plate: > $\eta_s = \dfrac{P_s}{P_p} = \dfrac{P_s}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$ > > *Crushing efficiency* is the ratio of the joint strength governed by **rivet crushing (bearing)** to the strength of the solid plate: > $\eta_c = \dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = \dfrac{n \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t}$ > > In general $\eta_s \neq \eta_c$ because they represent two *different* governing failure modes. The **overall joint efficiency** is the smaller of the two. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** **List the different modes of failure of a riveted joint. Which mode usually governs in a well-designed boiler-joint lap arrangement?** **Model Answer:** > The four modes of failure are: > 1. Tearing of the plate between the rivet hole and the plate edge > 2. Tearing of the plate between consecutive rivet holes (pitch section) > 3. Shearing of the rivet(s) > 4. Crushing / bearing failure of the rivet (or hole wall) > > In a **well-designed boiler lap joint**, the failure modes are made **equally strong** by appropriate choice of pitch, diameter, and number of rivets (Unwin's concept of *balanced joint*). Hence, no single mode strictly *governs* — the design is *equi-strong*. However, in practical rolled-steel joints, **rivet shear** is often the slightly governing mode for thin plates, while **plate tearing at pitch** governs for thicker plates. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module 2 Internal Choice > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, the module-2 question is offered with internal choice. You must attempt **one** of the two alternatives below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Central Load with Full Design > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** A double-riveted lap joint of *zig-zag* type is to be designed for a 10 mm thick plate carrying a tensile load of $P = 90\,\text{kN}$. The allowable stresses are: $\tau = 75\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 150\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_t = 110\,\text{MPa}$. Design the joint — i.e. determine (i) rivet diameter, (ii) number of rivets, (iii) pitch, (iv) margin, and (v) verify all four modes of failure. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the four modes of failure of a riveted joint with neat sketches. State **Unwin's formula** for the diameter of the rivet, and justify its use. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1: Rivet diameter using Unwin's formula** $$ d = 6\sqrt{t} = 6\sqrt{10} = 6 \times 3.162 = 18.97 \approx 19\,\text{mm} $$ Adopt $d = 20\,\text{mm}$ (next standard size). **Step 2: Number of rivets $n$ from shear strength** $$ P_{s,\text{one rivet}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau = \frac{\pi}{4} (20)^2 (75) = 23562\,\text{N} \approx 23.56\,\text{kN} $$ $$ n = \frac{P}{P_{s,\text{one rivet}}} = \frac{90}{23.56} = 3.82 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{adopt } n = 4\,\text{rivets (double-riveted)} $$ **Step 3: Pitch of the rivets** For tearing resistance $\geq$ applied load per pitch: $$ P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \geq \frac{P}{n_r} $$ where $n_r$ = number of rivets *per row* (here 2 in zig-zag, but tearing-resisting is per pitch — use 1 rivet per pitch for double-riveted lap, so apply on one rivet): $$ (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = P_{s,\text{one rivet}} $$ $$ (p - 20) \times 10 \times 110 = 23562 $$ $$ p - 20 = \frac{23562}{1100} = 21.42 $$ $$ \boxed{\,p = 41.42 \approx 42\,\text{mm}\,} $$ **Step 4: Margin (edge distance) $m$** $$ m = 1.5 \cdot d = 1.5 \times 20 = 30\,\text{mm} $$ **Step 5: Verify all four modes** - *Shearing:* $P_s = n \cdot P_{s,\text{one}} = 4 \times 23.56 = 94.25\,\text{kN} \geq 90\,\text{kN}$ ✓ - *Crushing:* $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 4 \times 20 \times 10 \times 150 = 120000\,\text{N} = 120\,\text{kN} \geq 90\,\text{kN}$ ✓ - *Tearing at pitch:* $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (42 - 20)(10)(110) = 24200\,\text{N} = 24.2\,\text{kN}$ per pitch; with 2 rivets in series, $2 \times 24.2 = 48.4\,\text{kN}$ per row. Total across 2 rows: $96.8\,\text{kN} \geq 90\,\text{kN}$ ✓ - *Tearing at edge:* $P_e = (m - d/2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (30 - 10) \times 10 \times 110 = 22000\,\text{N} = 22\,\text{kN}$ per rivet × 4 = 88 kN — borderline, so **adopt $m = 35\,\text{mm}$** for safety. Re-checking: $(35 - 10)(10)(110) = 27500$, × 4 = 110 kN ≥ 90 kN ✓ **Design summary:** $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, $n = 4$, $p = 42\,\text{mm}$, $m = 35\,\text{mm}$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) The four modes of failure are presented with their governing equations as shown in Section 3.4 above. **Unwin's formula** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ is empirical, derived from extensive boiler-joint experiments, and gives a rivet diameter that *balances* the shear and bearing stresses when a plate of thickness $t$ is used. The factor 6 is dimensionally adjusted to yield $d$ in mm when $t$ is in mm. It is the standard first-cut in KTU-design problems before refinement by the four-mode check. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Eccentric Load Analysis > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** An L-shaped bracket is connected to a vertical column by **6 rivets** arranged in two vertical columns of three rivets each, as shown below. The column pitch (horizontal) is $60\,\text{mm}$ and the row pitch (vertical) is $50\,\text{mm}$. A vertical load of $P = 40\,\text{kN}$ is applied at a horizontal distance of $200\,\text{mm}$ from the centroid G of the rivet group. Plate thickness $t = 12\,\text{mm}$, rivet diameter $d = 20\,\text{mm}$. Allowable stresses: $\tau = 80\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 150\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_t = 120\,\text{MPa}$. Determine the maximum shear force on the most-loaded rivet and check the joint for safety in shear, crushing, and tearing. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a force diagram, derive an expression for the resultant shear force on a rivet in an *eccentrically loaded* rivet group. Clearly state the assumptions made. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1: Geometry of rivet group and $\sum r_i^2$** Rivet coordinates $(x, y)$ in mm, centred at G: - Row +1 (top): $(-30, +50)$, $(+30, +50)$ - Row 0 (mid): $(-30, 0)$, $(+30, 0)$ - Row −1 (bot): $(-30, -50)$, $(+30, -50)$ Radial distances squared: - Top/bot rows: $r^2 = 30^2 + 50^2 = 900 + 2500 = 3400$ (each), 4 rivets contribute - Mid row: $r^2 = 30^2 = 900$ (each), 2 rivets contribute $$ \sum r_i^2 = 4 \times 3400 + 2 \times 900 = 13600 + 1800 = 15400\,\text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 2: Direct shear per rivet** $$ F_1 = \frac{P}{n} = \frac{40000}{6} = 6666.7\,\text{N} $$ **Step 3: Torsional moment** $$ T = P \cdot e = 40000 \times 200 = 8 \times 10^6\,\text{N·mm} $$ **Step 4: Critical rivet** The load $P$ is vertical; eccentricity $e$ is horizontal. The critical rivet is the one on the load line of action (the horizontal line through G, at maximum radial distance). The two rivets at $(\pm 30, 0)$ lie on this line with $r = 30\,\text{mm}$. However, for the **moment** of $8 \times 10^6\,\text{N·mm}$ to be maximum, we need the rivet at *maximum* $r$ from G — those are at $(\pm 30, \pm 50)$ with $r = \sqrt{3400} = 58.31\,\text{mm}$. **For the critical rivet, $r_{max} = 58.31\,\text{mm}$.** **Step 5: Torsional shear on critical rivet** $$ F_2 = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{\sum r_i^2} = \frac{8 \times 10^6 \times 58.31}{15400} = 30296.1\,\text{N} $$ **Step 6: Resultant on critical rivet** The angle $\theta$ between $F_1$ (downward) and $F_2$ (tangential) at the corner rivet $(\pm 30, \pm 50)$ is not zero. We compute: $$ \tan\alpha = \frac{50}{30} = 1.667 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 59.04°\text{ (angle of } r_i \text{ from x-axis)} $$ $F_2$ is perpendicular to $r_i$, so its direction is $90° - \alpha = 30.96°$ from the y-axis (vertical). Therefore the angle between $F_1$ (vertical) and $F_2$ is $\theta = 30.96°$. $$ F_{max} = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta} $$ $$ \cos(30.96°) = 0.857 $$ $$ F_{max} = \sqrt{(6666.7)^2 + (30296.1)^2 + 2 \times 6666.7 \times 30296.1 \times 0.857} $$ $$ F_{max} = \sqrt{4.444 \times 10^7 + 9.179 \times 10^8 + 3.463 \times 10^8} $$ $$ F_{max} = \sqrt{1.309 \times 10^9} = 36178.6\,\text{N} \approx 36.18\,\text{kN} $$ **Step 7: Shear check on one rivet** $$ P_{s,\text{one rivet}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 (80) = 25132.7\,\text{N} \approx 25.13\,\text{kN} $$ Since $F_{max} = 36.18\,\text{kN} > 25.13\,\text{kN}$ — **NOT SAFE in shear**. ✗ **Step 8: Recommendation** Either (a) **increase the rivet diameter** to $d = 24\,\text{mm}$ (gives $P_s = 36.19\,\text{kN}$, marginal), or (b) **increase the number of rivets** to $n = 8$ (e.g., $4 \times 2$ grid), or (c) **reduce the eccentricity** by repositioning the bracket. A complete design iteration is required. #### Model Solution — Part (b) The derivation follows Section 3.2 above. The two key assumptions are: 1. The plate connecting the rivet group is **rigid** — it transfers load without deforming. 2. The rivets are **equally stiff** in shear — load distributes in proportion to rigidity and geometry. 3. The contact pressure between rivet and plate is **uniformly distributed** over the projected bearing area. 4. The rivets' contribution to the polar moment is **discrete**, treated as a sum: $J = \sum r_i^2$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:** > 1. **Centroid miscalculation:** If the rivet pattern is *not symmetric* about the load line, the centroid G is *not* at the geometric centre of the pattern. Computing $e$ from the wrong reference gives $F_1 + F_2$ values that are completely wrong. **(−3 marks)** > 2. **Using $r$ from the corner of the plate** instead of from G: $r$ must be measured from the centroid of the rivet group, not the edge of the plate. **(−2 marks)** > 3. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$ in the resultant formula:** Many students write $F = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2}$ for all rivets, which is correct *only* when $\theta = 90°$. For the critical rivet on the load line, $\theta = 0°$ and $F = F_1 + F_2$. **(−2 marks)** > 4. **Neglecting crushing check:** KTU mandates checking *all four* modes. Showing only the shear calculation loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing N and kN, mm and m, or omitting units in the final answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Design of Riveted Joints (Central & Eccentric)** - [ ] **Rivet diameter:** Use Unwin's formula $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ mm as the *first* estimate; round up to the nearest standard size. - [ ] **Central load** ⇒ *Direct shear only*; $F_1 = P/n$ is the same on every rivet. - [ ] **Eccentric load** ⇒ $F_1$ (direct) **+** $F_2$ (torsional), where $F_{2i} = P \cdot e \cdot r_i / \sum r_i^2$. - [ ] **Critical rivet in eccentric load:** the one *on the load line of action*, at the *maximum* radial distance from G — *not* the geometrically farthest corner. - [ ] **Resultant on critical rivet:** use $F_{max} = F_1 + F_2$ only when $\theta = 0°$. Otherwise apply the full law of cosines with the correct $\theta$. - [ ] **Polar second moment** of a centred $m \times k$ grid: $\sum r_i^2 = \dfrac{mk(m^2-1)a^2 + mk(k^2-1)b^2}{12}$. - [ ] **Four failure modes** must *all* be checked: rivet shear, rivet crushing, plate tearing at pitch, plate tearing at edge. - [ ] **Efficiencies** $\eta_s$ and $\eta_c$ are reported *separately*, not averaged. - [ ] **Margin** $m \approx 1.5\,d$ is a *minimum*; increase it if edge-tearing becomes the governing mode. - [ ] **Standard margin / pitch nomenclature** (in order along the load): $m$, $p_b$ (back pitch), $p_d$ (diagonal pitch), $p$ (pitch). - [ ] **Assumption set** in eccentric analysis: (i) rigid plate, (ii) equally stiff rivets, (iii) uniformly distributed bearing pressure, (iv) discrete polar moment. - [ ] **Common KTU numerical pattern:** "design the joint" → return $d, n, p, m$ in that order, then verify all four modes. - [ ] **Units in every line:** N (force), N·mm (moment), MPa (stress), mm (length). The examiner *will* dock a mark for missing units. - [ ] **Final sign-off:** Always state whether the joint is **SAFE** or **UNSAFE** explicitly, with the governing mode named. --- > **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2 — DESIGN OF RIVETED JOINTS WITH CENTRAL AND ECCENTRIC LOADS** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education Framework, mapped to CO₂ of PEPET411 Machine Design.*

topics.title: Boiler and Tank Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiler and Tank Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Boiler and Tank Joints?

**Boiler and Tank Joints** are specialized **permanent, leak-proof structural joints** used to fabricate pressure vessels (boilers) and storage containers (tanks) by joining curved or flat plates of mild steel using **hot-driven rivets**. They are designed to safely contain internal pressure (steam in boilers, fluids/gases in tanks) and prevent leakage over long service lives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPET411 – Machine Design, Module 2):**
> *Riveted joints used in the fabrication of boilers, tanks, and similar pressure-retaining structures where leak-tightness, resistance to internal pressure, and long-term reliability are the primary design criteria. The design is governed by the efficiency of the joint against tearing, shearing, and crushing failure modes.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bucket-of-Water" Intuition)

Imagine you are trying to make a **cylindrical bucket** by joining two flat rectangular plates end-to-end so it can hold water without leaking:

- **Plain Bolted / Screwed Joint**: A few discrete screws every 15 cm → water sprays out between the gaps under pressure. ❌
- **Welded Joint (Modern)**: Continuous fused metal → very strong and leak-proof, but in historical/classic machine design syllabus, **riveting** remains the primary topic. ✓
- **Riveted Lap Joint (Boiler/Tank)**: Imagine dozens of **hot cherry-red rivets** hammered continuously along the seam in **zig-zag pattern**, each one forming a metal "plug" that swells and seals the gap. The combined "wall of rivets" acts like a continuous barrier, similar to the studs on a Roman soldier's shield (the *testudo* formation) — together, they are far stronger than the sum of individual parts. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Used:**
> - Permissible Tensile Stress for Mild Steel Plate (**σt = 84 MPa**)
> - Permissible Shear Stress for Mild Steel Rivet (**τ = 70 MPa**)
> - Permissible Crushing Stress for Mild Steel Rivet (**σc = 120 MPa**)
> - **Unwin's Formula** for plate thickness: t = **6.1 × √d** mm (where *d* is rivet diameter in mm)
> - Typical joint types: **Lap Joint (single/double)** and **Butt Joint (with single/double cover straps)**

## 1.3 Why are Boiler and Tank Joints Studied Separately?

Boiler/tank joints are *not* ordinary structural joints. They differ in three critical ways:

1. **Leak-tightness** is the dominant design driver (not just strength).
2. They are usually **longitudinal or circumferential seams** of cylindrical shells under **hoop (circumferential) stress**.
3. The **efficiency of the joint** must be computed considering **all three failure modes simultaneously**, and the **lowest** governs the design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Boiler and Tank Riveted Joints

### (a) Lap Joint
The two plates to be joined are simply **overlapped** and riveted together.

- **Single Riveted Lap Joint** — one row of rivets per pitch.
- **Double Riveted Lap Joint** — two rows of rivets per pitch.

```
    ┌──────────┐
    │ Plate 1  │──┐
    └──────────┘  │ overlap
        ┌──────────┐
        │ Plate 2  │
        └──────────┘
```

### (b) Butt Joint
The two plates are **butted** (placed edge-to-edge) and joined using **cover straps (cover plates)**.

- **Single Riveted Butt Joint with Single Cover Strap** — one cover plate.
- **Double Riveted Butt Joint with Double Cover Strap** — two cover plates (preferred for boilers/tanks).
- **Double Riveted Butt Joint with Single Cover Strap** — used for moderate pressures.

## 2.2 Important Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Pitch | $p$ | Distance between centres of two consecutive rivets in a row |
| Transverse Pitch | $p_t$ | Distance between two consecutive rows of rivets |
| Diagonal Pitch | $p_d$ | Distance between centres of a rivet in one row to the next rivet in the adjacent row |
| Margin | $m$ | Distance between the centre of the rivet hole and the edge of the plate |
| Back Pitch | $p_b$ | Distance between the first and last row of rivets |
| Diametral Pitch | — | Pitch measured along the chain of zig-zag rivets |

## 2.3 Standard Margins (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard margin distances (from edge of plate to centre of rivet):**
> - $m = 1.5d$ for **chain riveting**
> - $m = 1.5d$ to $1.75d$ for **zig-zag riveting**
> - $m = 2d$ for **edges exposed to pressure** (e.g., longitudinal seams of boilers)

## 2.4 Failure Modes of a Riveted Joint (THE 3 CRITICAL MODES)

Every riveted joint fails in **one of the following three modes**. The design must check **all three** and the **lowest strength governs**.

### Mode 1: Tearing Failure of Plate (between rivet holes)
The plate is weakest in tension at the section through the rivet hole, where the cross-sectional area is reduced.

$$\text{Resistance against tearing per pitch length} = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

### Mode 2: Shearing Failure of Rivet
The rivet(s) per pitch length are sheared across their cross-section.

$$\text{Shearing strength per pitch length} = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

where $n$ = number of rivets per pitch length (1 for single, 2 for double riveted).

### Mode 3: Crushing Failure of Rivet
The rivet shank crushes against the plate hole under bearing pressure.

$$\text{Crushing strength per pitch length} = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

## 2.5 Efficiency of a Riveted Joint (The "Big Idea")

The **efficiency** $\eta$ of a joint is defined as the **ratio of the weakest of the three failure strengths to the strength of an un-riveted (solid) plate of the same dimensions**.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Least of } (P_t, P_s, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $P_t$ | Tearing strength of plate per pitch length |
| $P_s$ | Shearing strength of rivet(s) per pitch length |
| $P_c$ | Crushing strength of rivet per pitch length |
| $p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Strength of solid plate of width = $p$, thickness = $t$ |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plate thickness (Unwin) | $t = 6.1\sqrt{d}$ | mm |
| 2 | Tearing strength of plate | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | N |
| 3 | Shearing strength of rivets | $P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ | N |
| 4 | Crushing strength of rivet | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | N |
| 5 | Tearing efficiency | $\eta_t = \dfrac{p - d}{p}$ | — |
| 6 | Shearing efficiency | $\eta_s = \dfrac{n \cdot \pi d^2 \tau}{4 p t \sigma_t}$ | — |
| 7 | Crushing efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{d \sigma_c}{p \sigma_t}$ | — |
| 8 | Overall joint efficiency | $\eta = \min(\eta_t, \eta_s, \eta_c)$ | — |
| 9 | Hoop stress in cylindrical shell | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 t}$ | N/mm² |
| 10 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 t}$ | N/mm² |
| 11 | Maximum pitch (Unwin) | $p_{\max} = C \cdot t + 2.15d$ | mm |
| 12 | Maximum pitch (boilers) | $p_{\max} = 1.31 d \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | mm |

**Where $C$ is a constant** in Unwin's formula:
- $C = 1.31$ for **chain riveting**
- $C = 1.53$ for **zig-zag riveting**

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Shells** (steam boilers, water-tube boilers, fire-tube boilers).
- **Storage tanks** for oil, water, chemicals under atmospheric or moderate pressure.
- **Pressure vessels** in chemical/petrochemical plants (historically; now often welded).
- **Ship hulls** (originally riveted; lap and butt joints along the keel and seams).
- **Bridges and railway girders** (early 20th century structural riveting).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tearing Efficiency of Plate

**Statement:** Show that the efficiency of a plate against tearing between two adjacent rivet holes in a single-riveted lap joint is given by $\eta_t = \dfrac{p - d}{p}$.

**Proof:**

Consider a single-riveted lap joint with **one row** of rivets. The pitch between two consecutive rivets is $p$, and the diameter of each rivet hole is $d$.

**Step 1 — Identify the weakest section.**
The plate is weakened by the presence of the rivet hole. The net cross-section is along a line through the rivet hole, perpendicular to the load.

**Step 2 — Calculate the net (effective) width resisting tension.**
The full pitch length is $p$, but the hole of diameter $d$ removes material. Hence:

$$\text{Net width available} = p - d$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the strength of the un-riveted plate over pitch length $p$.**
The full plate would resist:

$$P_{\text{solid}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the tearing strength of the riveted plate.**

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Step 5 — Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_t &= \frac{P_t}{P_{\text{solid}}} \\
&= \frac{(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \\
&= \frac{p - d}{p} \\
&= 1 - \frac{d}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the **tearing efficiency is**:

$$\boxed{\eta_t = \frac{p - d}{p} = 1 - \frac{d}{p}}$$

**Interpretation:** As $p$ increases (rivets further apart), $\eta_t \to 1$ (stronger plate, but weaker against leakage). As $p$ decreases (rivets closer), $\eta_t \to 0$ (weaker plate).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shearing Efficiency of Rivet

**Statement:** Derive the shearing efficiency of a single-riveted lap joint with $n$ rivets per pitch.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Number of shear planes per rivet.**
A rivet in a **lap joint** has **one shear plane** (single shear). In a **butt joint with double cover strap**, the rivet has **two shear planes** (double shear).

**Step 2 — Shearing strength of one rivet.**

$$P_{s,\text{one rivet}} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — For $n$ rivets per pitch length (e.g., $n = 2$ for double riveted):**

$$P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

**Step 4 — Compare with solid plate strength $p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{P_s}{P_{\text{solid}}} \\
&= \frac{n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\eta_s = \frac{n \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau}{4 \cdot p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}}$$

**Special case — Single riveted lap joint ($n=1$):**

$$\eta_s = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau}{4 \cdot p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Crushing Efficiency of Rivet

**Statement:** Derive the crushing (bearing) efficiency of a riveted joint.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Projected bearing area of rivet on plate.**
The rivet bears against the plate on a **cylindrical projection** of diameter $d$ and depth $t$:

$$A_b = d \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Crushing strength:**

$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_c &= \frac{P_c}{P_{\text{solid}}} \\
&= \frac{d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \\
&= \frac{d \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\eta_c = \frac{d \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Failure-Strength Comparison Table

For a **single-riveted lap joint** with rivet diameter $d$, pitch $p$, plate thickness $t$:

| Failure Mode | Strength per pitch length | Efficiency Expression | Condition for "Safe" |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plate Tearing | $P_t = (p - d) t \sigma_t$ | $\eta_t = \dfrac{p - d}{p}$ | $p \geq \dfrac{d \sigma_c}{\sigma_c - \sigma_t}$ boundary |
| Rivet Shear | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ | $\eta_s = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \tau}{4 p t \sigma_t}$ | $\eta_s \leq \eta_t$ (balanced design) |
| Rivet Crushing | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | $\eta_c = \dfrac{d \sigma_c}{p \sigma_t}$ | $\eta_c \leq \eta_t$ (balanced design) |

**Design rule for an "equally-strong" or "balanced" joint:**

$$\eta_t = \eta_s = \eta_c$$

This gives the **optimum pitch** that maximizes the overall joint efficiency.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style Derivation)

**Problem:** A single-riveted lap joint of a boiler is to be designed. The plate thickness is $t = 12$ mm, rivet diameter $d = 24$ mm. Permissible stresses: $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa, $\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa. Determine the optimum pitch of the rivets.

**Step 1 — Tearing efficiency condition:**

$$\eta_t = 1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{24}{p}$$

**Step 2 — Shearing efficiency condition (equate with tearing):**

$$\frac{\pi d^2 \tau}{4 p t \sigma_t} = 1 - \frac{d}{p}$$

Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi \times 24^2 \times 70}{4 \times p \times 12 \times 84} &= 1 - \frac{24}{p} \\
\frac{\pi \times 576 \times 70}{4032 \, p} &= 1 - \frac{24}{p} \\
\frac{126604.8}{4032 \, p} &= 1 - \frac{24}{p} \\
\frac{31.40}{p} &= 1 - \frac{24}{p} \\
\frac{31.40 + 24}{p} &= 1 \\
p &= 55.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check crushing efficiency at this pitch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_c &= \frac{d \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t} = \frac{24 \times 120}{55.4 \times 84} = \frac{2880}{4653.6} = 0.619
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Tearing efficiency:**

$$\eta_t = 1 - \frac{24}{55.4} = 1 - 0.433 = 0.567$$

**Step 5 — Shearing efficiency (verify):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{\pi \times 24^2 \times 70}{4 \times 55.4 \times 12 \times 84} = \frac{126604.8}{223430.4} = 0.567
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Joint efficiency $\eta = 0.567 = 56.7\%$ (governed by tearing and shear).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Riveted Boiler Joint System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["PRESSURE LOAD CHAIN"]
        P["Internal Pressure p (N/mm2)"] --> HS["Hoop Stress in Shell\n sigma_h = pD/2t"]
        LS["Longitudinal Stress\n sigma_l = pD/4t"]
        P --> LS
    end

    subgraph S2["RIVETED JOINT GEOMETRY"]
        HS --> RT["Riveted Seam\n Longitudinal or Circumferential"]
        LS --> RT
        RT --> T1["Joint Type:\n Lap / Butt with Cover Strap"]
        T1 --> R1["Rivet Pattern:\n Chain / Zig-Zag"]
    end

    subgraph S3["THREE FAILURE MODES CHECK"]
        R1 --> M1["MODE 1: Plate Tearing\n Pt = (p-d).t.sigma_t"]
        R1 --> M2["MODE 2: Rivet Shear\n Ps = n.pi.d^2.tau / 4"]
        R1 --> M3["MODE 3: Rivet Crushing\n Pc = d.t.sigma_c"]
    end

    subgraph S4["DESIGN DECISION"]
        M1 --> C{"Compare P_t, P_s, P_c"}
        M2 --> C
        M3 --> C
        C --> EFF["Efficiency eta = min(P_t, P_s, P_c) / (p.t.sigma_t)"]
        EFF --> SAFE["Acceptable if eta >= Required Efficiency\n Typically 60-70 percent for boilers"]
    end
```

## 4.2 Rivet Pattern Comparison (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHAIN["CHAIN RIVETING (Single Row)"]
        A1["Rivet"] --- A2["Rivet"] --- A3["Rivet"] --- A4["Rivet"]
    end

    subgraph ZIGZAG["ZIG-ZAG RIVETING (Double Row Staggered)"]
        B1["R1"] --- B3["R3"]
        B2["R2"] --- B4["R4"]
        B1 -.-> B2
        B3 -.-> B4
    end

    CHAIN --> USE1["Used in low-pressure tanks\n Simpler alignment, weaker seal"]
    ZIGZAG --> USE2["Used in boilers, pressure vessels\n Better leak-tightness, higher efficiency"]
```

## 4.3 Failure Mode Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOAD["External Tensile Load P"]
        L1["Applied Force P"]
    end

    subgraph FAIL["THREE POSSIBLE FAILURE MODES"]
        L1 --> F1["MODE 1\n Plate Tearing\n Section through rivet hole\n Reduced area: (p-d).t"]
        L1 --> F2["MODE 2\n Rivet Shear\n Single or double shear plane\n Area: n.pi.d^2/4"]
        L1 --> F3["MODE 3\n Rivet Crushing\n Bearing surface: d.t"]
    end

    F1 --> GOV{"LOWEST STRENGTH GOVERNS\n = Joint Capacity"}
    F2 --> GOV
    F3 --> GOV
    GOV --> SAFE["eta = Pmin / (p.t.sigma_t)"]
```

## 4.4 Joint Type Comparison Table

| Feature | Single Riveted Lap | Double Riveted Lap | Double Riveted Butt (Double Strap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strength | Lowest | Higher | Highest |
| Leakage risk | High | Moderate | Lowest |
| Plate material | Same thickness | Same thickness | Cover strap = 0.625t to 0.75t |
| Typical use | Water tanks | Low-pressure boilers | High-pressure boilers |
| Shear planes per rivet | 1 | 1 | 2 (double shear) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "efficiency of a riveted joint". What are the three failure modes considered in its design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **efficiency of a riveted joint** ($\eta$) is defined as the **ratio of the strength of the weakest section of the riveted joint to the strength of the solid (un-riveted) plate of the same dimensions**.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Strength of weakest section of the joint}}{\text{Strength of solid plate of width } p \text{ and thickness } t} = \frac{\min(P_t, P_s, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

The **three failure modes** considered in the design of a riveted joint are:

1. **Tearing of the plate** between consecutive rivet holes along the pitch line.
2. **Shearing of the rivet** along its shear plane(s).
3. **Crushing (bearing) failure** of the rivet shank against the plate hole.

The design is governed by the mode giving the **lowest efficiency**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Unwin's formula for plate thickness. What are the standard values of permissible stresses for mild steel plate and rivets used in boiler joint design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Unwin's Formula** (for plate thickness in terms of rivet diameter):

$$t = 6.1 \sqrt{d}$$

where $t$ is the plate thickness in **mm** and $d$ is the rivet diameter in **mm**.

**Standard permissible stresses for mild steel (KTU Convention):**

- Permissible **tensile** stress for plate: $\sigma_t = 84 \text{ MPa}$
- Permissible **shear** stress for rivet: $\tau = 70 \text{ MPa}$
- Permissible **crushing** stress for rivet: $\sigma_c = 120 \text{ MPa}$

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A double-riveted lap joint with zig-zag riveting is used in a boiler. The plate thickness is $t = 16$ mm, and the rivet diameter is $d = 25$ mm. The permissible stresses are: $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa, $\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa. Determine:
1. The **pitch of the rivets** (equating tearing and shearing strength).
2. The **efficiency of the joint**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **strengths of the joint per pitch length** for all three failure modes. Comment on which mode governs the design and why zig-zag riveting is preferred over chain riveting for boiler joints.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Determining the Pitch and Efficiency

**Given:**
- Double riveted, zig-zag lap joint → $n = 2$ rivets per pitch
- $t = 16$ mm, $d = 25$ mm
- $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa, $\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa

**Step 1 — Equate tearing and shearing strengths for optimum pitch:**

$$P_t = P_s$$

$$(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$(p - 25) \times 16 \times 84 = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25^2 \times 70$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p - 25) \times 1344 &= \frac{\pi}{2} \times 625 \times 70 \\
(p - 25) \times 1344 &= 1.5708 \times 43750 \\
(p - 25) \times 1344 &= 68721.5 \\
p - 25 &= 51.13 \\
p &= 76.13 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check maximum pitch (zig-zag riveting, Unwin):**

$$p_{\max} = 1.53 \, t + 2.15 \, d = 1.53 \times 16 + 2.15 \times 25 = 24.48 + 53.75 = 78.23 \text{ mm}$$

Since $p = 76.13 < 78.23$ mm ✓ — design is safe.

**Step 5 — Calculate joint efficiency:**

Tearing efficiency:

$$\eta_t = \frac{p - d}{p} = \frac{76.13 - 25}{76.13} = \frac{51.13}{76.13} = 0.6716 = 67.16\%$$

**Step 6 — Verify by computing shearing and crushing efficiencies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{n \cdot \pi d^2 \tau}{4 p t \sigma_t} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 625 \times 70}{4 \times 76.13 \times 16 \times 84} \\
&= \frac{274886}{409291} = 0.6716 = 67.16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_c &= \frac{d \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t} = \frac{25 \times 120}{76.13 \times 84} = \frac{3000}{6394.92} = 0.4691 = 46.91\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Overall joint efficiency (minimum):**

$$\boxed{\eta = \min(67.16\%, 67.16\%, 46.91\%) = 46.91\% \approx 47\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this case, **crushing governs** because $\sigma_c / \sigma_t$ ratio is low. For a higher-efficiency joint, the pitch must be **reduced** to balance the crushing and tearing modes.

---

#### Part (b): Comparison of Failure Modes & Chain vs Zig-Zag

**Comparison of strengths per pitch length at $p = 76.13$ mm:**

| Mode | Strength | Value (N) | % of solid plate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tearing | $P_t = (76.13-25) \times 16 \times 84$ | $68{,}735$ N | 67.2% |
| Shearing | $P_s = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 625 \times 70$ | $68{,}722$ N | 67.2% |
| Crushing | $P_c = 25 \times 16 \times 120$ | $48{,}000$ N | 46.9% |

**Solid plate strength:** $p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 76.13 \times 16 \times 84 = 102{,}319$ N

**Governing mode:** **Crushing** — gives the lowest strength.

**Why zig-zag riveting is preferred for boiler joints:**

1. **Tighter seal** — staggered rivets provide more uniform pressure distribution along the seam, reducing leak paths.
2. **Higher efficiency** — for the same pitch $p$, zig-zag gives greater effective bearing area and better shear distribution.
3. **Higher allowable pitch** — Unwin's formula allows $p_{\max} = 1.53t + 2.15d$ (zig-zag) versus $p_{\max} = 1.31t + 2.15d$ (chain).
4. **Reduced stress concentration** — load path is more gradual in zig-zag configuration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A single-riveted butt joint with a **double cover strap** is to be designed for a pressure vessel. The plate thickness is $t = 20$ mm, and the rivet diameter is $d = 30$ mm. The permissible stresses are: $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa, $\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa. Determine:
1. The **optimum pitch** of the rivets.
2. The **efficiency** of the joint.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Show that for a balanced single-riveted lap joint, the **optimum pitch** is given by:

$$p = \frac{d}{4} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{3\pi \tau}{\sigma_t}} \right]$$

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Optimum Pitch for Double-Strap Butt Joint

**Given:**
- Single-riveted butt joint, double cover strap
- $t = 20$ mm, $d = 30$ mm
- $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa, $\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa

**Important — Double cover strap means DOUBLE SHEAR per rivet, so:**

$$P_s = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

**Step 1 — Equate tearing and shearing for optimum pitch:**

$$(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p - 30) \times 20 \times 84 &= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 30^2 \times 70 \\
(p - 30) \times 1680 &= \frac{\pi}{2} \times 900 \times 70 \\
(p - 30) \times 1680 &= 1.5708 \times 63000 \\
(p - 30) \times 1680 &= 98960.4 \\
p - 30 &= 58.90 \\
p &= 88.90 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute joint efficiency:**

Tearing efficiency:

$$\eta_t = \frac{88.90 - 30}{88.90} = 0.6626 = 66.26\%$$

Shearing efficiency (verify):

$$\eta_s = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 30^2 \cdot 70}{4 \times 88.90 \times 20 \times 84} = \frac{395866}{597408} = 0.6626 = 66.26\%$$

Crushing efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \frac{30 \times 120}{88.90 \times 84} = \frac{3600}{7467.6} = 0.4821 = 48.21\%$$

**Result:** Joint efficiency $\eta = 48.21\%$, **governed by crushing**.

---

#### Part (b): Derivation of Optimum Pitch Formula

**Step 1 — For a balanced single-riveted lap joint, equate tearing and crushing efficiencies:**

$$\eta_t = \eta_c$$

$$\frac{p - d}{p} = \frac{d \cdot \sigma_c}{p \cdot \sigma_t}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply:**

$$(p - d) \cdot \sigma_t = d \cdot \sigma_c$$

**Step 3 — Equate tearing and shearing efficiencies:**

$$\eta_t = \eta_s$$
