$$\frac{p - d}{p} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau}{4 \cdot p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$4 (p - d) t \sigma_t = \pi d^2 \tau$$

$$p - d = \frac{\pi d^2 \tau}{4 t \sigma_t}$$

**Step 4 — Use Unwin's formula $t = 6.1\sqrt{d}$:**

For a balanced joint, the **most efficient design** is obtained by equating tearing and shearing only. Substituting $t$ from Unwin's formula and simplifying (this is a KTU-standard manipulation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p - d &= \frac{\pi d^2 \tau}{4 \times 6.1\sqrt{d} \times \sigma_t} = \frac{\pi d^{3/2} \tau}{24.4 \, \sigma_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Squaring both sides to obtain the standard form:**

After algebraic manipulation, the well-known KTU result is:

$$\boxed{p = \frac{d}{4} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{3\pi \tau}{\sigma_t}} \right]}$$

**Verification for our numerical values** ($\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa):

$$p = \frac{30}{4} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{3\pi \times 70}{84}} \right] = 7.5 \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + 7.854} \right] = 7.5 \left[ 1 + 2.975 \right] = 7.5 \times 3.975 = 29.81 \text{ mm}$$

*(This is for a single-riveted lap joint — much smaller pitch than the butt joint example.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Boiler/Tank Joint Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the shear-plane count**: A double-cover-strap butt joint has **2 shear planes per rivet** (double shear), not 1. Many students write $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$ and lose 2–3 marks. **Rule: Lap joint = 1 shear plane, Butt joint with single cover = 1, Butt joint with double cover = 2.**
>
> 2. **Wrong efficiency formula**: The "efficiency" is NOT the ratio of tearing strength to shearing strength. It is **the ratio of the weakest joint strength to the solid plate strength** $p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$. Many students confuse this.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the crushing mode**: Students often check only tearing and shearing, forgetting crushing. The lowest of **all three** governs.
>
> 4. **Not verifying $p \leq p_{\max}$**: Unwin's maximum pitch check is **mandatory**. A pitch that is theoretically optimum but exceeds $p_{\max}$ is unsafe (leakage risk).
>
> 5. **Wrong unit conversion**: 1 MPa = 1 N/mm². Mixing kN and N without conversion loses marks.
>
> 6. **Miscounting rivets per pitch**: In double-riveted joints, $n = 2$ (not 1). In triple-riveted, $n = 3$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Boiler and Tank Joints**
>
> ✅ **Three Failure Modes** — Tearing, Shearing, Crushing (always check all three).
>
> ✅ **Efficiency** = $\min(P_t, P_s, P_c) / (p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t)$ — the **minimum** governs.
>
> ✅ **Tearing strength** = $(p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$
>
> ✅ **Shearing strength** = $n \cdot (\pi/4) \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau$ (use $n \times 2$ for double-cover butt joints).
>
> ✅ **Crushing strength** = $d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$
>
> ✅ **Unwin's plate thickness** — $t = 6.1\sqrt{d}$
>
> ✅ **Unwin's max pitch** — $p_{\max} = C \cdot t + 2.15d$ (C = 1.31 chain, 1.53 zig-zag).
>
> ✅ **Zig-zag riveting preferred for boilers** — better leak-tightness, higher allowable pitch.
>
> ✅ **Standard permissible stresses** (mild steel): $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa, $\tau = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa.
>
> ✅ **Margin** $m = 1.5d$ to $2d$ (use $2d$ for boiler edges).
>
> ✅ **Diagonal pitch** $p_d = \sqrt{(p/2)^2 + p_t^2}$ (for zig-zag pattern design).
>
> ✅ **Hoop stress** in cylindrical shell: $\sigma_h = pD / 2t$ (twice the longitudinal stress).
>
> ✅ **Double-riveted double-strap butt joint** is the **strongest and most leak-proof** joint used in high-pressure boilers.
>
> ✅ **Cover strap thickness** in butt joints: $t_c = 0.625 t$ to $0.75 t$ of main plate.
>
> ✅ **Efficiency of a typical boiler joint** ranges between **45% to 75%** depending on the configuration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Structural Joints?

A **Structural Joint** is a permanent or semi-permanent mechanical connection between two or more machine/structural components that is designed to transmit loads (static, dynamic, or fluctuating) while maintaining the geometric integrity of the assembly. In the context of **Machine Design (PEPET411)**, structural joints encompass **Riveted Joints**, **Welded Joints**, and **Threaded (Screwed) Joints** — each selected based on the nature of load, service environment, cost, and reliability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized Terminology):** "A structural joint is a load-bearing connection, generally permanent, formed between two or more members using mechanical fastening (rivets/bolts), fusion (welding), or form-closure (threads) to satisfy strength, stiffness, and fatigue requirements specified by the design code."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Human Skeleton Metaphor

Think of your own body:
- Your **skull joints** are **welded-like** (fused sutures — they don't move, they're permanent and rigid).
- Your **shoulder joint** is **riveted/pinned** (a ball-and-socket allowing controlled rotation).
- Your **screw thread analogy**: The threads on a screw act like an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder — just as a ramp lets you lift heavy loads with small effort, threads convert rotational motion into linear clamping force.

| Joint Type | Everyday Analogy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Riveted Joint** | Stapler pin joining sheets | Bridges, boilers, sheet metal |
| **Welded Joint** | Fusing two candles together | Machine frames, pressure vessels |
| **Threaded Joint** | Bottle cap and jar | Engine cylinders, couplings, fasteners |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study This?** Approximately **70% of all machine failures** originate at joints, not at the parent members. A robust design must ensure the **joint is stronger than the weakest member** (the "weakest link" principle).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Design Parameters

The following **KTU-recommended standard values** (from IS 800, IS 2062, and Shigley/Black & Mott) are used universally in KTU board examinations:

- **Factor of Safety (FoS) for Rivets:** $n = 4$ to $6$ for structural steel rivets (hot-driven).
- **Factor of Safety for Welds:** $n = 3$ to $5$ for fillet welds in mild steel.
- **Permissible Shear Stress for Rivets ($\tau_p$):** $0.75$ to $0.80$ of ultimate shear stress.
- **Permissible Tensile Stress for Welds:** $0.5$ to $0.6$ of ultimate tensile stress.
- **Efficiency of a Riveted Joint ($\eta$):** Defined as $\eta = \dfrac{P_s}{P} \times 100$, where $P_s$ is the strength of riveted plate and $P$ is the strength of un-riveted plate.
- **Standard Rivet Diameters (Unwin's Formula):** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ mm, where $t$ is the plate thickness in mm.
- **Standard Fillet Weld Size:** $s = 0.7 \times \text{plate thickness}$ (minimum throat condition).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Structural Joints

```
Structural Joints
├── 1. Riveted Joints
│   ├── Based on Loading
│   │   ├── Shear (Lap Joint)
│   │   ├── Tension (Butt Joint with Strap)
│   ├── Based on Number of Rivet Rows
│   │   ├── Single-Row
│   │   ├── Multi-Row (Chain, Zig-Zag/Staggered)
│   └── Based on Rivet Type
│       ├── Hot-Driven
│       └── Cold-Driven
│
├── 2. Welded Joints
│   ├── Fillet Weld
│   │   ├── Transverse (Load perpendicular to weld line)
│   │   ├── Parallel (Load parallel to weld line)
│   │   └── Combined
│   ├── Butt Weld
│   │   ├── Square
│   │   ├── Single-V / Double-V
│   │   └── U-Groove
│   └── Spot / Seam Weld (Resistance Welding)
│
└── 3. Threaded (Screwed) Joints
    ├── Bolt-Nut Assembly
    ├── Power Screws (Lead Screws, Ball Screws)
    └── Studs and Cap Screws
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 RIVETED JOINTS — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Failure Modes (KTU Mandatory)

When a riveted joint is loaded, it can fail in **four distinct modes** — a KTU examiner will always test all four:

1. **Shearing of Rivets** ($\tau$): Rivet shank sheared along the shear plane(s).
2. **Tearing of Plate between Rivets (Tensile Failure)**: Plate cracks through the row of rivets along the load line.
3. **Crushing/Bearing Failure of Rivet or Plate**: The rivet hole is elongated due to compressive stress at the contact surface.
4. **Tearing of Plate at the Edge (Shear Out)**: Plate shears out from the edge of the rivet hole to the edge of the plate.

### 2.1.2 Strength Equations

| Failure Mode | Force (Load) | Stress Quantity | KTU Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shearing of Rivet** | $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau_p$ | Shear Stress | $P_s = n \cdot A \cdot \tau_p$ |
| **Tearing of Plate** | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Tensile Stress | $P_t = (p-d)\,t\,\sigma_t$ |
| **Crushing of Rivet** | $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | Bearing Stress | $P_c = d\,t\,\sigma_c$ |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{plate}}$ | Percentage | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{min}}{(p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t)} \times 100$ |

> **Where:** $n$ = number of rivets, $d$ = rivet diameter, $p$ = pitch (rivet spacing), $t$ = plate thickness, $\tau_p, \sigma_t, \sigma_c$ = permissible stresses.

### 2.1.3 Unwin's Formula and Standard Design Data

**Unwin's Formula (Rivet Diameter):**
$$d = 6\sqrt{t} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

**Staggered Pitch (Zig-Zag):** When rivets are staggered, the diagonal pitch $p_d$ must satisfy:
$$p_d = \sqrt{p^2 + \dfrac{g^2}{4}} \geq 0.33p + 1.5d$$
where $g$ is the gauge (transverse pitch) between rows.

---

## 2.2 WELDED JOINTS — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Types of Fillet Welds

A **fillet weld** is a triangular cross-section weld deposited in the corner formed by two intersecting plates. It has two legs (sides) and a **throat** — the shortest distance from the root to the face.

- **Throat Thickness (effective):** $t_e = 0.707 \times s$ where $s$ is the leg size.
- **For an equal-leg fillet weld of size $s$:** $t_e = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}$.

### 2.2.2 Strength of Fillet Welds

| Weld Type | Shear Failure Plane | Strength Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Transverse Fillet Weld** | Throat (in shear) | $P_{transverse} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_p$ |
| **Parallel Fillet Weld** | Throat (in shear) | $P_{parallel} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_p$ |
| **Combined (Transverse + Parallel)** | Vector Addition | $P = \sqrt{P_T^2 + P_P^2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a transverse weld carry ~30% more load than a parallel weld of the same size?** The transverse weld has a non-uniform stress distribution (stress concentration at the ends), but its **average strength is higher** because the stress vector is aligned with the throat plane at an angle, providing a direct shear path. The parallel weld is essentially being "peeled" along its length.

### 2.2.3 Strength of Butt Welds

For a **complete-penetration butt weld**:
$$P_{butt} = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

where $L$ is the weld length and $t$ is the plate thickness.

For a **partial-penetration butt weld** (depth of penetration $= p_d$):
$$P_{butt} = L \cdot p_d \cdot \sigma_t$$

---

## 2.3 ECCENTRIC LOADING — A Critical KTU Topic

When the **line of action of the applied load does NOT pass through the centroid** of the rivet group or weld group, the joint experiences both:
- **Direct Shear** (from $P$)
- **Torsional/Secondary Shear** (from moment $M = P \cdot e$, where $e$ is the eccentricity)

### 2.3.1 Analysis Steps (Always Follow This Order)

1. Locate the centroid of the rivet/weld group.
2. Compute the **moment** $M = P \cdot e$.
3. Compute the **radial distance** $r$ from centroid to the **farthest rivet** (critical rivet).
4. **Direct shear on farthest rivet:** $F_d = \dfrac{P}{n}$
5. **Torsional shear on farthest rivet:** $F_t = \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot r}{\sum r_i^2}$
6. **Resultant shear:** $F_R = \sqrt{F_d^2 + F_t^2 + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta}$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $F_d$ and $F_t$.

> For a **welded joint** with eccentric loading, the analogous steps apply, but using the **polar moment of inertia** of the weld line about the centroid:
> $$I_x = \sum L_i \cdot y_i^2, \quad I_y = \sum L_i \cdot x_i^2, \quad I_{xy} = \sum L_i \cdot x_i y_i$$
> $$J = I_x + I_y \quad \text{(Polar moment)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Pin This in Your Notebook)

| # | Topic | Formula | Variable Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unwin's Formula | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | $t$ = plate thickness (mm) |
| 2 | Shear Strength of Rivet | $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau_p$ | $n$ = no. of rivets |
| 3 | Tearing Strength | $P_t = (p-d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | $p$ = pitch, $d$ = hole dia |
| 4 | Crushing Strength | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | Bearing on rivet |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{min}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100$ | Strength ratio |
| 6 | Fillet Weld Throat | $t_e = 0.707s$ | $s$ = leg size |
| 7 | Fillet Weld Strength | $P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_p$ | Single transverse |
| 8 | Butt Weld Strength | $P = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Full penetration |
| 9 | Eccentric Rivet Load | $F_R = \sqrt{F_d^2 + F_t^2 + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta}$ | Resultant shear |
| 10 | Polar Moment (Weld) | $J = I_x + I_y$ | For eccentric weld group |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Application:** These formulas are the literal backbone of **boiler design (ASME Section I)**, **pressure vessel codes (PD 5500)**, **bridge construction (IS 800:2007)**, and **automotive chassis design**. KTU examiners frequently tie the numerical problems to industrial contexts (e.g., "a steam boiler plate, 16 mm thick...").

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Joint Selection

| Factor | Riveted | Welded | Threaded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low (labor intensive) | Moderate (skilled welder) | Lowest |
| **Strength-to-Weight** | Moderate (rivet holes weaken plate) | Highest (no holes) | Moderate |
| **Fatigue Resistance** | Poor (stress concentration at holes) | Good for transverse welds | Good (preload benefits) |
| **Disassembly** | Yes (drill out) | No | Yes (unscrew) |
| **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | None | Yes — risk of distortion | None |
| **Typical Use** | Bridges, old boilers | Pressure vessels, ship hulls | Engine, machinery |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Efficiency of a Single-Row Riveted Lap Joint

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a single-riveted lap joint of plate thickness $t$, width $b$, rivet diameter $d$, and pitch $p$ (distance between centers of consecutive rivets in a row).

**Given:** Plate width $b$, thickness $t$, rivet diameter $d$, pitch $p$, permissible shear stress $\tau_p$, permissible tensile stress $\sigma_t$, permissible crushing stress $\sigma_c$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Strength of the un-riveted (solid) plate.**
The full plate, in the absence of rivet holes, can carry a load based on its full cross-sectional area:
$$P = b \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$
This is the **baseline load** against which the joint is compared.

**Step 2: Shearing strength of the rivet (single shear, single rivet).**
The rivet cross-section is circular, and the shear failure occurs on the cross-sectional area:
$$A_{rivet} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$$
For a single rivet in single shear:
$$P_s = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_p$$

**Step 3: Tearing strength of the plate (between the rivet hole and the next rivet).**
The plate, after the rivet hole is drilled, has an effective width of $(p - d)$. The stress acts on this reduced section:
$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

**Step 4: Crushing strength of the rivet (projected area on the plate surface).**
The rivet bears against the plate over the projected area $(d \times t)$:
$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

**Step 5: Identify the weakest mode (minimum of the three).**
The actual strength of the joint is governed by the **smallest** of these three:
$$P_{joint} = \min(P_s, P_t, P_c)$$

**Step 6: Compute the efficiency.**
By definition:
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P} = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{b \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A joint's efficiency can **never exceed 100%** (by definition). A well-designed riveted joint typically has $\eta$ between **60% and 80%**. The remaining 20–40% represents the **strength penalty** for drilling holes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Resultant Shear on a Farthest Rivet Under Eccentric Loading

### 3.2.1 Setup

A bracket is attached to a column with a group of $n$ rivets. A load $P$ is applied at a distance $e$ from the centroid $G$ of the rivet group. Find the resultant shear on the **critical (farthest) rivet**.

### 3.2.2 Full Derivation

**Step 1: Define the moment.**
The load $P$ creates a moment about the centroid $G$:
$$M = P \cdot e$$

**Step 2: Direct shear (uniformly distributed over all rivets).**
By equilibrium of vertical forces, each rivet takes an equal share of the direct shear:
$$F_d = \dfrac{P}{n}$$
The direction of $F_d$ is **parallel** to the applied load $P$.

**Step 3: Torsional shear (proportional to radial distance).**
The moment $M$ creates a torsional shear at any rivet located at radial distance $r$ from the centroid. By the principle of moment distribution:
$$F_t \cdot r = \dfrac{M}{n_r}$$
where $n_r$ is the sum-of-relative-distances, but for a discrete rivet group, we use the polar moment of inertia analogy:
$$F_{t,i} = \dfrac{M \cdot r_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j^2}$$
For the **farthest rivet** at $r = r_{max}$:
$$F_t = \dfrac{M \cdot r_{max}}{\sum r_i^2}$$
The direction of $F_t$ is **perpendicular** to the radial line $r$ from $G$ to the rivet.

**Step 4: Vector addition (resultant).**
The resultant shear on the farthest rivet is found by **vector addition**. If $\theta$ is the angle between $F_d$ and $F_t$ at the rivet location:
$$F_R = \sqrt{F_d^2 + F_t^2 + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta}$$

**Step 5: Verification by dot product.**
Using the dot product form, the same result can be expressed as:
$$F_R^2 = F_d^2 + F_t^2 + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta$$
For **standard cases**:
- If $F_d$ and $F_t$ are **perpendicular** ($\theta = 90°$): $F_R = \sqrt{F_d^2 + F_t^2}$
- If $F_d$ and $F_t$ are **parallel** ($\theta = 0°$): $F_R = F_d + F_t$ (worst case)
- If $F_d$ and $F_t$ are **anti-parallel** ($\theta = 180°$): $F_R = |F_t - F_d|$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Strength of Combined Fillet Welds

### 3.3.1 Setup

A bracket is welded to a column by a **combined weld** — a vertical weld (transverse) of length $L_T$ and a horizontal weld (parallel) of length $L_P$, each with the same leg size $s$. A vertical load $P$ is applied at the end of the bracket.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

**Step 1: Throat thickness.**
For an equal-leg fillet weld of size $s$:
$$t_e = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707s$$

**Step 2: Strength of the transverse weld (per unit length).**
The transverse weld is loaded perpendicular to the weld line. The throat is the failure plane:
$$\tau_T = \dfrac{P_T}{t_e \cdot L_T} \leq \tau_p$$
$$\therefore P_T = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L_T \cdot \tau_p$$

**Step 3: Strength of the parallel weld (per unit length).**
The parallel weld is loaded parallel to the weld line. The throat is again the failure plane:
$$\tau_P = \dfrac{P_P}{t_e \cdot L_P} \leq \tau_p$$
$$\therefore P_P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L_P \cdot \tau_p$$

**Step 4: Total load capacity (using vector addition or direct summation).**
For pure vertical loading on this combination, both welds contribute in the same direction. However, in the **classical method**, since the parallel weld has uneven stress distribution, we use:
$$P_{total} = P_T + P_P \quad \text{(simplified, conservative)}$$
A more rigorous analysis uses the **parallel-axis theorem** for weld groups:
$$P = \dfrac{P_T \cdot L_T + P_P \cdot L_P}{L_T} \quad \text{(weighted by length)}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**
Given $s = 8$ mm, $L_T = 100$ mm, $L_P = 80$ mm, $\tau_p = 70$ MPa:
$$P_T = 0.707 \times 8 \times 100 \times 70 = 39{,}592 \text{ N} \approx 39.6 \text{ kN}$$
$$P_P = 0.707 \times 8 \times 80 \times 70 = 31{,}674 \text{ N} \approx 31.7 \text{ kN}$$
$$P_{total} = 39.6 + 31.7 = 71.3 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Failure Mode Comparison Table

| Joint Type | Failure Mode | Equation | Common KTU Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Riveted | Shear of rivet | $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 \cdot \tau_p$ | Forgetting the $n$ (number of rivets) |
| Riveted | Tearing of plate | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Using $p$ instead of $(p - d)$ |
| Riveted | Crushing | $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | Using circular area $\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2$ instead of $d \cdot t$ |
| Welded | Fillet throat shear | $P = 0.707 s L \tau_p$ | Forgetting the 0.707 factor |
| Welded | Butt in tension | $P = L t \sigma_t$ | Using $s$ (leg) instead of $t$ (plate) |
| Eccentric | Vector sum | $F_R = \sqrt{F_d^2 + F_t^2 + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta}$ | Assuming $\theta = 90°$ without justification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Note on Diagrams:** Structural joint details (rivet cross-sections, weld throats, etc.) require precise engineering drawings. Since ASCII/Mermaid has limitations for such geometric accuracy, the following Mermaid block diagrams provide a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the relationships between load, joint type, and failure analysis — fully compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid safeguards.

---

## 4.1 Functional Flow: Riveted Joint Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Load P] --> B[Determine Plate Thickness t]
    B --> C[Calculate Rivet Dia d using Unwin: d=6sqrt t]
    C --> D[Choose Joint Type: Lap or Butt]
    D --> E[Compute Pitch p from standard tables]
    E --> F{Calculate Failure Strengths}
    F --> F1[Shear: P_s = n pi/4 d^2 tau_p]
    F --> F2[Tearing: P_t = p-d t sigma_t]
    F --> F3[Crushing: P_c = d t sigma_c]
    F1 --> G[Find P_joint = MIN of P_s, P_t, P_c]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Compute Efficiency eta]
    H --> I{Is eta in 60 to 80 percent range}
    I -->|Yes| J[Design is ACCEPTABLE]
    I -->|No| K[Revise: Increase Pitch or Use Multi-Row]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Welded Joint Eccentric Loading Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Load P applied with eccentricity e] --> B[Locate Centroid G of weld group]
    B --> C[Compute Moment M = P times e]
    C --> D[Compute Direct Shear Fd = P divided by n]
    C --> E[Compute Polar Moment J = Ix plus Iy]
    E --> F[Compute Torsional Shear Ft = M r divided by J]
    D --> G[Find Critical Element at r max]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Resultant FR using vector sum]
    H --> I[Check FR less than Permissible]
    I -->|Yes| J[SAFE]
    I -->|No| K[UNSAFE: Increase Weld Size or Add Welds]
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Block Diagram: Structural Joints

```mermaid
graph TD
    SJ[Structural Joints] --> RJ[Riveted Joints]
    SJ --> WJ[Welded Joints]
    SJ --> TJ[Threaded Joints]
    RJ --> R1[Lap Joint: Single Shear]
    RJ --> R2[Butt Joint Double Strap: Double Shear]
    WJ --> W1[Fillet Weld: Transverse or Parallel]
    WJ --> W2[Butt Weld: V Groove or U Groove]
    TJ --> T1[Bolt Nut Assembly]
    TJ --> T2[Power Screws: Lead and Ball]
    R1 --> F1[Failure: Shear, Tearing, Crushing]
    R2 --> F1
    W1 --> F2[Failure: Throat Shear]
    W2 --> F2
    T1 --> F3[Failure: Thread Shear, Tensile]
    T2 --> F3
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Representation: Fillet Weld Geometry (Text-Based)

```
           SIDE VIEW (Cross-Section)
           _______________________
          |                       |     ←  Plate 1 (top)
          |_______________________|
                       *                          *
                      * *                        *
                     *   *  ← Throat (t_e)     *
                    *     *                    *
                   *_______*___________________*  ←  Plate 2 (bottom)

    LEGEND:
    s    = Leg size (equal to plate thickness for full-size weld)
    t_e  = Effective throat = 0.707 × s
    Root = Bottom corner of the triangular weld
    Face = Outer surface (hypotenuse)
```

```
   PLAN VIEW (Looking Down on Weld)
   ╔══════════════════════════════╗
   ║  →→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→   ║   ←  PARALLEL WELD (load along weld)
   ║  →→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→   ║
   ╚══════════════════════════════╝

   vs.

   ╔══════════════════════════════╗
   ║          ┌─┐                 ║
   ║  →→→→→→→│ │← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ║   ←  TRANSVERSE WELD (load across)
   ║          └─┘                 ║
   ╚══════════════════════════════╝
```

---

## 4.5 Schematic: Rivet Failure Modes (Cross-Section)

```
   LAP JOINT CROSS-SECTION (Side View)
   ┌────────────┐         ┌────────────┐
   │            │         │            │
   │  PLATE 1   │←───R───→│  PLATE 2   │
   │            │   ↑↑↑   │            │
   │            │   ↑↑↑   │            │
   └────────────┘         └────────────┘
                  ↑
              RIVET (loaded in SHEAR)

   FAILURE MODES:
   →  Shearing of rivet (vertical cuts across rivet)
   →  Crushing of rivet (ovalization of hole)
   →  Tearing of plate (crack between holes, along line of pitch)
   →  Edge shearing (crack from hole to plate edge)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Define: (i) Throat thickness in a fillet weld, and (ii) Efficiency of a riveted joint. State the standard formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **(i) Throat Thickness of Fillet Weld (1 Mark):** The throat thickness is the shortest distance from the root (intersection point of the two plate surfaces) to the hypotenuse (face) of the triangular fillet weld cross-section. For an equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $s$:
> $$t_e = 0.707 \times s = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}$$
> 
> **(ii) Efficiency of a Riveted Joint (1 Mark):** It is defined as the ratio of the strength of the riveted plate to the strength of the un-riveted (solid) plate, expressed as a percentage.
> $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{plate}} = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{b \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\%$$
> 
> **(iii) Standard Formula (1 Mark):** The riveted joint's actual strength is the minimum of the shear strength, tearing strength, and crushing strength:
> $$P_{joint} = \min\left( n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2\tau_p, \; (p-d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t, \; d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c \right)$$

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a transverse fillet weld and a parallel fillet weld, citing the loading direction and one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Transverse Fillet Weld | Parallel Fillet Weld |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading Direction** | Load acts **perpendicular** to the weld line (axis). | Load acts **parallel** to the weld line (axis). |
| **Stress Distribution** | Non-uniform; maximum at the ends (stress concentration). | Uniform along the length; less stress concentration. |
| **Strength per Unit Length** | Higher (~30% more than parallel). | Lower (acts like a shear lap). |
| **Practical Example** | Weld across the tension face of a crane hook. | Longitudinal welds along the seam of a pressure vessel. |

> **Memory Tip (1 Mark bonus):** "**T**ransverse = **T**ension across, **P**arallel = **P**ulled along."

---

## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A double-riveted lap joint is to be designed for a mild steel plate of thickness $t = 12$ mm. The rivet diameter (Unwin's formula) is $d$, and the joint is in double shear. Take: $\tau_p = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 160$ MPa. The pitch is $p = 50$ mm. Calculate the efficiency of the joint.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **four failure modes** of a riveted joint. State the standard formula for each mode. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Calculate the rivet diameter using Unwin's formula.**
$$d = 6\sqrt{t} = 6\sqrt{12} = 6 \times 3.464 = 20.78 \approx 21 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the shear strength of the rivet (double shear).**
For a double-riveted lap joint, $n = 2$ rivets. For a **lap joint**, it is single shear; but the problem states **double shear**, implying a **butt joint with double straps**. Re-reading: "double-riveted lap joint" — this is double-shear only if it's a butt joint. Assuming **butt joint with double straps** (double shear):
$$A_{rivet} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (21)^2}{4} = 346.36 \text{ mm}^2$$
For double shear, there are 2 shear planes per rivet:
$$P_s = n \times 2 \times A_{rivet} \times \tau_p = 2 \times 2 \times 346.36 \times 80 = 110{,}835 \text{ N} \approx 110.84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the tearing strength of the plate.**
For a double-riveted joint, the tearing occurs across **two holes** (one per row). For chain riveting:
$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (50 - 21) \times 12 \times 120 = 29 \times 12 \times 120 = 41{,}760 \text{ N} \approx 41.76 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the crushing strength of the rivet.**
$$P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 2 \times 21 \times 12 \times 160 = 80{,}640 \text{ N} \approx 80.64 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Find the joint strength (minimum of the three).**
$$P_{joint} = \min(110.84, \; 41.76, \; 80.64) = 41.76 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the strength of the solid (un-riveted) plate.**
Assuming the plate width $b = p + \text{edge margin} = 50 + 30 = 80$ mm (standard assumption, or given in problem):
$$P_{plate} = b \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 80 \times 12 \times 120 = 115{,}200 \text{ N} = 115.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the efficiency.**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{plate}} \times 100 = \dfrac{41.76}{115.2} \times 100 = 36.25\%$$

> **Note for KTU students:** This efficiency is **lower than 60%**, which is unusual. In practice, the pitch would be increased. If pitch = 70 mm: $\eta$ would rise to ~52%. (In board exams, the answer is graded based on the **methodology**, not the absolute efficiency value.)

---

**Solution to (b):**

The **four failure modes** of a riveted joint are:

```
   1. SHEARING OF RIVET
      Force: Ps = (n)(π/4)(d²)(τp)
      Plane: Cross-section of rivet, parallel to load.

   2. TEARING OF PLATE (Tensile Failure)
      Force: Pt = (p - d)(t)(σt)
      Plane: Through the line of rivet holes, perpendicular to load.

   3. CRUSHING OF RIVET (Bearing Failure)
      Force: Pc = (n)(d)(t)(σc)
      Plane: Projected area (d × t) of rivet on plate.

   4. TEARING AT EDGE (Edge Shear)
      Force: Pe = 2 × (edge distance)(t)(τ)
      Plane: From rivet hole to the edge of the plate.
```

> **[Sketch — verbal description for answer sheet]:** Draw a **lap joint** (two overlapping plates with one rivet). Mark the four failure planes with dashed lines:
> - A **horizontal plane** through the rivet (shear).
> - A **vertical plane** through the rivet hole and across the plate (tension).
> - The **circular contact area** between rivet and plate (crushing).
> - The **edge distance** from rivet hole to plate edge (edge shear).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to A): [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A bracket is welded to a column using **two parallel fillet welds** of size $s = 10$ mm and length $L = 150$ mm each. The permissible shear stress is $\tau_p = 90$ MPa. A vertical load $P = 100$ kN is applied at a distance $e = 300$ mm from the centroid of the weld group. Determine whether the welds are safe under **combined direct and torsional shear**. Assume the centroid is at the **midpoint of the weld group** and use the simplified **parallel-axis approximation**.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain **Unwin's formula** for rivet diameter. A plate of thickness 20 mm is to be joined. Calculate the rivet diameter and the **minimum pitch** for single-riveted lap joint if the tearing strength must exceed 35 kN. Take $\sigma_t = 120$ MPa.
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Identify the geometry.**
Two parallel vertical welds, $L = 150$ mm each. The welds are symmetrically placed about the centroid at $y = \pm 75$ mm. The applied load $P$ is vertical, eccentricity $e = 300$ mm.

**Step 2: Compute the direct shear on each weld.**
Total weld length $= 2L = 300$ mm. The load is vertical; the throat area is shared equally:
$$F_d = \dfrac{P}{2L \cdot t_e} \times L = \dfrac{P}{2L} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{2 \times 150} = 333.33 \text{ N/mm}$$
Wait — let me reframe: Direct shear stress on each weld (assuming equal distribution between two welds):
$$\tau_d = \dfrac{P}{2 \cdot t_e \cdot L} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 10 \times 150} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{2121} = 47.15 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute the moment and the torsional shear.**
$$M = P \times e = 100 \times 10^3 \times 300 = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the polar moment of the weld group.**
For two parallel welds of length $L = 150$ mm at distance $c = 75$ mm from the centroid (the welds are vertical, so we treat them as line segments):
$$I_x = 2 \times L \times c^2 = 2 \times 150 \times (75)^2 = 1{,}687{,}500 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$I_y = 2 \times \dfrac{L^3}{12} = 2 \times \dfrac{(150)^3}{12} = 562{,}500 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$J = I_x + I_y = 1{,}687{,}500 + 562{,}500 = 2{,}250{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 5: Compute the torsional shear stress at the critical (farthest) point.**
The farthest point on the weld is at $r = \sqrt{c^2 + (L/2)^2} = \sqrt{75^2 + 75^2} = 75\sqrt{2} = 106.07$ mm.
But for the **simplified parallel-axis method**, we use the **farthest point on the weld** (at the extreme corner of the weld line, distance $r = 106.07$ mm from centroid):
$$\tau_t = \dfrac{M \cdot r}{J \cdot t_e} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^6 \times 106.07}{2{,}250{,}000 \times 0.707 \times 10}$$
$$\tau_t = \dfrac{3.182 \times 10^9}{15{,}907{,}500} = 200.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Combine direct and torsional shear.**
Since the load is vertical and the welds are vertical, the direct shear is vertical and the torsional shear is horizontal at the extreme point. They are **perpendicular**:
$$\tau_R = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2} = \sqrt{(47.15)^2 + (200.06)^2} = \sqrt{2223 + 40024} = \sqrt{42247} \approx 205.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7: Check the safety.**
$$\tau_R = 205.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \tau_p = 90 \text{ MPa}$$
Since $\tau_R > \tau_p$, **the welds are UNSAFE**. To make them safe, the leg size $s$ must be increased. Solving for $s$:
$$205.5 = 90 \times \dfrac{0.707 \cdot s}{0.707 \cdot 10} \quad \text{(incorrect scaling)}$$
Re-derive: $\tau \propto 1/s$, so $s_{new} = s_{old} \times \dfrac{205.5}{90} = 10 \times 2.28 = 22.8$ mm. Use $s = 24$ mm.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: State Unwin's formula.**
Unwin's formula gives the standard rivet diameter as a function of plate thickness:
$$\boxed{d = 6\sqrt{t} \text{ (in mm)}}$$
where $t$ is the plate thickness in mm. This formula is empirical and is valid for structural steel plates with thicknesses in the range 6–25 mm.

**Step 2: Calculate the rivet diameter for $t = 20$ mm.**
$$d = 6\sqrt{20} = 6 \times 4.472 = 26.83 \approx 27 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Apply the tearing condition.**
For a single-riveted lap joint, the tearing strength is:
$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \geq 35 \text{ kN} = 35{,}000 \text{ N}$$
$$(p - d) \geq \dfrac{35{,}000}{t \cdot \sigma_t} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{20 \times 120} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{2400} = 14.58 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the minimum pitch.**
$$p_{min} = d + 14.58 = 27 + 14.58 = 41.58 \approx 42 \text{ mm}$$

> **Note:** For practical purposes, $p$ is also bounded below by the standard minimum pitch of $2.5d$ (or $\approx 3d$ for water-tight joints). Here, $2.5d = 67.5$ mm, so the **governing minimum pitch is $67.5$ mm**, not $42$ mm. The student must mention this practical bound.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Joint Design Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $n$ (number of rivets):** A common slip is to write $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 \tau_p$ without the $n$. Always state clearly: "for $n$ rivets in single shear..."
> 2. **Confusing $p$ with $(p - d)$:** When calculating tearing strength, the **net section** is $(p - d)$, not $p$. This is a 2-mark deduction in board exams.
> 3. **Crushing area mistake:** Use $d \times t$ (projected rectangular area), NOT $\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2$ (the rivet's actual cross-section). The crushing/bearing is on the **plate's hole edge**, which is a rectangle.
> 4. **Forgetting the 0.707 factor in fillet welds:** The throat is $0.707s$, and the strength equation $P = 0.707 s L \tau_p$ **must** carry this factor. A 1-mark deduction if missing.
> 5. **Eccentric load — assuming $\theta = 90°$ without justification:** In the resultant shear equation, $\theta$ is the angle between $F_d$ and $F_t$ **at the specific rivet location**. For the **farthest rivet directly in line with the load**, $\theta$ is often 0° (worst case) — not 90°!
> 6. **Unwin's formula units:** Always express $t$ in **mm** to get $d$ in **mm**. Mixing units (e.g., $t$ in meters) is a guaranteed zero.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 KTU 2024 High-Yield Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

- [ ] **Unwin's Formula:** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ — Always in mm; for $t$ in mm.
- [ ] **Three failure modes of a riveted joint:** Shear ($n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 \tau_p$), Tearing ($(p-d) t \sigma_t$), Crushing ($d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$).
- [ ] **Efficiency Definition:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{plate}} \times 100$ — Always expressed as a percentage.
- [ ] **Fillet Weld Throat:** $t_e = 0.707 s$ — This is the failure plane.
- [ ] **Fillet Weld Strength:** $P = 0.707 s L \tau_p$ — One equation for both transverse and parallel (in the conservative case).
- [ ] **Butt Weld Strength:** $P = L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ — Full penetration; uses plate thickness $t$, not leg size $s$.
- [ ] **Eccentric Loading Vector Sum:** $F_R = \sqrt{F_d^2 + F_t^2 + 2 F_d F_t \cos\theta}$ — The angle $\theta$ depends on the rivet location, NOT a fixed 90°.
- [ ] **Pitch Limits:** $p \geq 2.5d$ (standard), $p \geq 3d$ (water/airtight).
- [ ] **Edge Distance:** $e \geq 1.5d$ (typical), $e \geq 2d$ (for severe loads).
- [ ] **Transverse vs. Parallel Weld:** Transverse carries higher load per unit length (~30% more) but has stress concentration at the ends.
- [ ] **Permissible Stress Defaults (for board exams):** $\tau_p = 0.75 \sigma_t$, $\sigma_c = 1.5 \sigma_t$ (or values given in the problem).
- [ ] **Margin Distance:** Distance from the center of the rivet hole to the edge of the plate; standard value is $1.5d$ to $2d$.
- [ ] **Numbering of Shear Planes:** Lap joint = 1 shear plane; Butt joint with single strap = 1 shear plane (note: not 2!); Butt joint with **double** straps = 2 shear planes (true double shear).

> **Final Tip for KTU Board Exam:** Always start your answer with a **labeled diagram** (even if rough). Examiners award 1–2 marks for a proper sketch. State **assumptions** clearly (e.g., "Assume $\tau_p = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 120$ MPa as per IS 800"). Conclude with a **safety verdict** ("The joint is SAFE / UNSAFE because..."). This professional framing wins half-marks that students often lose to incomplete answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welded Joints: Types of Welded Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Welded Joints: Types of Welded Joints # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **welded joint** is a permanent, non-detachable connection between two or more machine/structural components formed by localised fusion of the base metals (with or without the addition of a filler material) under the application of heat, pressure, or both. In the context of **PEPET411 – Machine Design (Module 2)**, welded joints are analysed as load-transferring discontinuities in a machine assembly, where the designer must ensure that the **weld metal (deposited zone)** and the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** sustain the operational static and dynamic loads without failure. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight** > Per the KTU Machine Design syllabus, welded joints are classified by: > 1. **Weld geometry** (fillet, butt, edge, plug, slot, spot, seam) > 2. **Loading orientation** (parallel/transverse/oblique) > 3. **Welding process** (arc, gas, resistance, forge, thermit) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Metal Glue That Melts" Imagine two steel plates that you cannot bolt together because the joint is inaccessible from the back. You take an electric arc (≈ 4000 °C) and **melt the edges of the two plates together with a filler rod**, just like melting two ends of a chocolate bar with a candle and pressing them. As the molten pool cools, it solidifies into a single continuous metallic crystal — this is a **weld bead**. **Real-world parallel:** A bicycle frame, a pressure cooker body, a ship's hull, an automobile chassis — almost every mass-produced metallic structure you see uses welded joints because they are: - **Cheaper** than castings for complex shapes - **Lighter** than riveted/bolted joints - **Stronger** (≈ 90–100 % of parent metal strength) than mechanical fasteners - **Airtight/watertight** (no gaskets required) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in Machine Design:** A welder is **not** a machine element. The *weld bead geometry* itself is the machine element. The designer's job is to size the **leg size (s)**, **throat thickness (t)**, and **weld length (l)** so that the induced shear stress in the throat plane does not exceed the **permissible shear stress of the weld metal (τ_perm)**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Welded Joint Design | Design Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | |---|---|---| | Permissible shear stress of weld | $\tau_{perm}$ | **55–110 N/mm²** for structural steel | | Permissible tensile stress of weld | $\sigma_{pt}$ | **0.5 × ultimate tensile strength** | | Ultimate tensile strength of weld | $S_{ut}$ | **410–480 MPa** for E60/E70 electrodes | | Weld leg size | $s$ | **mm** | | Throat thickness | $t$ | $= 0.707 \, s$ (for equal-leg fillet) | | Effective weld length | $l$ | **mm** (actual length minus 2 × s for end crater) | | Electrode factor | $E$ | **0.707 (for fillet)**, **1.0 (for butt)** | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Master Classification Tree of Welded Joints ``` WELDED JOINTS │ ├── A. FUSION WELDS (heat-based) │ ├── 1. Fillet Weld (triangular cross-section) │ │ ├── Transverse (load ⊥ to weld axis) │ │ ├── Parallel (load ∥ to weld axis) │ │ └── Combined (both above) │ │ │ ├── 2. Butt Weld (in-plane fusion, plates edge-to-edge) │ │ ├── Square Butt (no edge preparation, t ≤ 8 mm) │ │ ├── Single-V Butt (8 < t ≤ 20 mm) │ │ ├── Double-V Butt (t > 20 mm) │ │ ├── Single-U / Double-U │ │ ├── Single-J / Double-J │ │ └── Single-Bevel / Double-Bevel │ │ │ ├── 3. Edge Weld (overlap on edges of plates, t ≤ 3 mm) │ │ │ ├── 4. Corner Weld (L-shaped plate edges) │ │ │ └── 5. Plug & Slot Weld (circular/elongated hole filled with weld) │ ├── B. RESISTANCE WELDS (heat + pressure, no filler) │ ├── Spot Weld (localised nugget) │ ├── Seam Weld (continuous row of spots, leak-proof) │ └── Projection Weld (preformed dimple concentrates current) │ └── C. SPECIAL PROCESSES ├── Flash Butt (arc + upset, for bars/rails) ├── Upset Butt ├── Thermit Welding (rail joint, exothermic Al + Fe₂O₃) └── Forge / Friction Welding ``` ## 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Weld Type ### A. Fillet Weld - **Geometry:** Triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at right angles (or at an angle between 60°–120°; ideally 90°). - **Leg size (s):** Length of each side of the triangle. - **Throat thickness (t):** Shortest distance from root to face = $0.707 \, s$ for a 90° equal-leg fillet. - **Failure plane:** Always assumed to fail in **shear along the throat** at 45° — this is the *weakest* plane. - **Common use:** Lap joints, T-joints in tanks, brackets, gusset plates. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule:** Always take **$t = 0.707 \, s$** for a 90° equal-leg fillet. For unequal legs or oblique angle θ, use $t = h \sin(\theta/2)$ where $h$ is the shorter leg. ### B. Butt Weld - **Geometry:** Two plates placed edge-to-edge in the same plane; full penetration weld. - **Edge preparation:** Necessary for thickness > 6–8 mm to ensure full fusion. - **Effective stress area:** Full plate cross-section → **stronger than fillet** of equal throat. - **Loading:** Can sustain direct tension/compression with no eccentricity (unlike fillet). - **Common use:** Boiler shells, pressure vessels, ship hulls, railway wagon tanks. ### C. Edge Weld - Used for thin sheets (t ≤ 3 mm) where the overlap is on the edges of plates folded together. Carries very low loads. ### D. Plug & Slot Weld - Holes (or elongated slots) are cut in the top plate and filled with weld metal to transmit shear. - Used to **supplement** fillet welds, or to join a flat plate to a curved surface (e.g., a dished end). ### E. Spot Weld - A localised nugget (~ 4–8 mm diameter) formed by resistance heating + forge pressure. Used in **sheet metal assemblies** (car bodies, refrigerator cabinets, washing-machine drums). - Spots arranged in rows: each spot carries a shear load $P_{spot} = \tau_{perm} \times \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2}$. ### F. Seam Weld - A continuous row of overlapping spot welds made by **roller electrodes**. Produces a **leak-proof** joint. Used in fuel tanks, milk cans, pressure vessels. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **No vertical pipe `|` characters used — set notation replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` for markdown safety.** | # | Joint Type | Throat / Effective Area | Shear / Normal Stress | Permissible Load | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fillet (90° equal leg) | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, s \, l}$ | $P = 0.707 \, s \, l \, \tau_{perm}$ | | 2 | Fillet (parallel + transverse) | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, s \, (l_{p} + 1.414 \, l_{t})}$ | $P = 0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm} \, (l_{p} + 1.414 \, l_{t})$ | | 3 | Butt (square/V) | $A = t_{plate} \times l$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{t_{plate} \, l}$ | $P = \sigma_{pt} \, t_{plate} \, l$ | | 4 | Spot weld (single row) | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{n \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}}$ | $P = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \tau_{perm}$ | | 5 | Plug weld | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ (plug area) | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{A_{plug}}$ | $P = A_{plug} \cdot \tau_{perm}$ | | 6 | Combined direct + bending | $A = 0.707 \, s \, l$ | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} + \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | $I = \dfrac{0.707 \, s \, l^{3}}{12}$ | | 7 | Oblique fillet (angle θ) | $t = h \sin(\theta/2)$ | — | $P = h \sin(\theta/2) \cdot l \cdot \tau_{perm}$ | **Symbols:** - $s$ = leg size of fillet (mm) - $l$ = effective weld length (mm); effective $= l_{actual} - 2s$ for end craters - $t$ = throat thickness (mm) - $P$ = applied load (N) - $\tau_{perm}$ = permissible shear stress in weld (N/mm²) - $\sigma_{pt}$ = permissible tensile stress in weld (N/mm²) - $d$ = spot-weld nugget diameter (mm) - $n$ = number of spots per row ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Structural Engineering:** Welded plate girders in bridges, gantry cranes, transmission towers. - **Pressure Vessel Design (ASME BPVC Sec VIII):** Butt welds are mandatory for circumferential and longitudinal joints of pressure vessels. - **Automotive:** Spot-welded monocoque chassis (typically 4000–5000 spot welds per car). - **Aerospace:** Friction-stir welded fuselage panels, laser-welded titanium compressor blades. - **Shipbuilding:** Submerged-arc welded (SAW) double-V butt joints for hull plates. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Throat Thickness of a 90° Fillet Weld **Statement:** For a 90° equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $s$, derive the expression for the throat thickness $t$. **Step 1 — Geometry setup.** Consider a fillet weld of leg size $s$ on each side. The cross-section is an **isosceles right triangle** with two equal legs of length $s$ meeting at 90°. **Step 2 — Identify the throat plane.** The throat is the **shortest distance from the root (right-angle corner) to the hypotenuse (outer face)**. By symmetry, the throat bisects the 90° root angle, so it lies at 45° to each leg. **Step 3 — Apply trigonometry.** $$ \begin{aligned} t &= s \cdot \sin(45^\circ) \\ &= s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ &= \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} \\ &= 0.707 \, s \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The throat thickness of a 90° equal-leg fillet weld is $t = 0.707 \, s$. This is the dimension that **carries the shear load** in design. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strength of a Parallel (Longitudinal) Fillet Weld **Statement:** A lap joint of two plates of thickness $t_p$ is connected by **two parallel fillet welds** (one on each side of the overlap). Derive the load $P$ that the joint can sustain in tension. **Step 1 — Assume failure plane.** The weld fails in **shear along the throat plane** (45° plane), with area $= t \times l$ per weld. **Step 2 — Substitute throat.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{shear} &= t \times l = 0.707 \, s \times l \quad \text{(for one weld)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply shear-stress limit.** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{induced} &= \frac{P}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, l} \leq \tau_{perm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for the safe load.** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= 2 \times 0.707 \, s \, l \times \tau_{perm} \\ &= 1.414 \, s \, l \, \tau_{perm} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Two parallel fillet welds of leg $s$ and length $l$ can carry $P = 1.414 \, s \, l \, \tau_{perm}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strength of a Transverse Fillet Weld **Statement:** A lap joint uses **two transverse fillet welds** (load is perpendicular to the weld axis). Show why a transverse weld is **stronger** than a parallel weld of the same dimensions. **Step 1 — Shear area is the same** ($0.707 \, s \cdot l$ per weld), so the **shear strength** is identical to the parallel case if only shear is considered. **Step 2 — But the transverse weld has an extra benefit:** The load direction is **normal to the weld axis**, creating a direct compression on the throat face plus a small frictional component. IS 800 and AWS codes empirically assign a **strength multiplier of 1.414** to the transverse weld over the parallel weld. **Step 3 — Effective length concept (KTU method).** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{transverse} &= 2 \times 0.707 \, s \, l \times \tau_{perm} \times 1.414 \\ &= 2 \times s \, l \, \tau_{perm} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** A transverse weld is ~ **41.4 % stronger** than a parallel weld because the failure plane is oriented favourably relative to the load. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Parallel + Transverse Fillet Weld (Un-symmetric Loading) **Statement:** A bracket is welded to a column using **two parallel fillet welds of length $l_p$ each** and **one transverse fillet of length $l_t$**. The bracket carries an eccentric load $P$ that produces a direct shear $P$ and a moment $M = P \times e$. **Step 1 — Primary shear stress (uniform).** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_1 &= \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, (2 l_p + 1.414 \, l_t)} \end{aligned} $$ > The transverse weld is given a **relative weight of 1.414** because it is more effective per unit length. **Step 2 — Secondary shear stress due to moment $M$.** The polar moment of weld group is computed with the parallel + weighted transverse legs: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{xx} &= \frac{0.707 \, s \cdot l_p^{3}}{12} + 0.707 \, s \, l_p \left(\frac{l_p}{2}\right)^{2} \quad \text{(for one parallel pair)}\\ J &= I_{xx} + I_{yy} \quad \text{(about the C.G. of the weld group)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum secondary shear (at the farthest point, distance $r_{max}$ from C.G.).** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_2 &= \frac{M \cdot r_{max}}{J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Vector sum of $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \sqrt{\tau_1^{2} + \tau_2^{2} + 2\tau_1\tau_2 \cos\phi} \end{aligned} $$ where $\phi$ is the angle between the directions of $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ at the critical point. **Step 5 — Design condition.** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &\leq \tau_{perm} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — At-a-Glance Comparison | Property | Fillet Weld | Butt Weld | Spot Weld | Plug Weld | |---|---|---|---|---| | Effective area | $0.707 \, s \, l$ | $t_p \, l$ | $\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | | Stress type | Shear in throat | Direct normal | Shear in nugget | Shear in plug | | Strength efficiency | 70.7 % | ≈ 100 % | depends on $d$ | depends on $d$ | | Eccentricity of load | Yes (creates bending) | No | Yes | Yes | | Thickness range | any | any, needs prep > 6 mm | thin sheets | thin sheets | | Cost | Low | Moderate–High | Very low (mass prod.) | Low | | Failure mode | Throat shear | Parent metal or HAZ | Nugget shear/pull-out | Plug shear | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Welded Joint Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Two components to be joined] --> B{Service load type?} B -- Tension only --> C{Plate thickness t_p} B -- Shear only --> D{Access for arc?} B -- Combined load --> E[Use Butt or Transverse Fillet] C -- t_p less than 6 mm --> F[Square Butt Weld] C -- 6 to 20 mm --> G[Single V Butt Weld] C -- greater than 20 mm --> H[Double V Butt Weld] D -- Yes --> I{Loading direction vs weld axis} D -- No --> J[Use Spot or Plug Weld] I -- Parallel --> K[Parallel Fillet Weld] I -- Perpendicular --> L[Transverse Fillet Weld] I -- Both --> M[Combined Fillet Weld] F --> N[Validate: sigma less than sigma_pt] G --> N H --> N K --> O[Validate: tau less than tau_perm] L --> O M --> O J --> P[Validate: tau less than tau_perm for nugget] N --> Q[DESIGN COMPLETE] O --> Q P --> Q classDef decision fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef terminal fill:#b6f5b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class B,C,D,I decision class F,G,H,K,L,M,J,N,O,P process class A,Q terminal ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Weld Geometry Cross-Sections ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Fillet Weld] -- leg s, leg s --> A2[Throat equals 0.707 s] B1[Square Butt] -- no prep --> B2[t less than 6 mm] C1[Single V Butt] -- 60 to 90 deg included --> C2[8 to 20 mm] D1[Double V Butt] -- both sides bevelled --> D2[t greater than 20 mm] E1[Spot Weld] -- resistance nugget --> E2[Dia d equals 4 to 8 mm] F1[Seam Weld] -- continuous spots --> F2[Leak proof joint] G1[Plug Weld] -- circular fill --> G2[Hole in top plate] H1[Edge Weld] -- thin sheets --> H2[t less than 3 mm] classDef geom fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1.5px classDef note fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:1px class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,G1,H1 geom class A2,B2,C2,D2,E2,F2,G2,H2 note ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Failure Plane Identification in a Fillet Weld ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant PlateA as Plate A participant Weld as Weld Throat participant PlateB as Plate B participant Load as Applied Load P Load->>PlateA: Pull outward PlateA->>Weld: Tensile pull along 45 deg plane Weld->>PlateB: Transfers force Note over Weld: Critical plane = 45 deg throat
Area = 0.707 s x l
Shear stress = P divided by 0.707 s l Weld-->>Load: If tau exceeds tau_perm, fracture along throat ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid — Spot-Welded Lap Joint Layout (Top View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Sheet1[Top Sheet] S1[Spot 1] --- S2[Spot 2] --- S3[Spot 3] end subgraph Sheet2[Bottom Sheet] B1[Spot 1] --- B2[Spot 2] --- B3[Spot 3] end S1 === B1 S2 === B2 S3 === B3 Sheet1 --- Sheet2 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a transverse fillet weld and a parallel fillet weld. State which one is stronger and why.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **transverse fillet weld** is one in which the direction of the load is **perpendicular** to the axis (length) of the weld, while in a **parallel (longitudinal) fillet weld** the load is **parallel** to the weld axis. For the same leg size and length, a transverse fillet weld is **stronger** (by a factor of ≈ 1.414) because the failure plane (throat) is oriented such that the load creates a combination of shear and compression on the throat face, whereas in a parallel weld the throat is loaded in pure shear only. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for justification of strength.) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any three types of butt-weld edge preparations. State the typical plate-thickness range for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Three common butt-weld edge preparations with their typical thickness ranges are: 1. **Square butt (no edge preparation):** Used for thin plates, $t_p \leq 6$ mm. 2. **Single-V butt:** Used for medium plates, $8 \text{ mm} < t_p \leq 20$ mm. 3. **Double-V butt:** Used for thick plates, $t_p > 20$ mm; reduces weld volume and distortion by ~ 50 %. (3 marks: 1 mark each.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two full alternative questions are provided below; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, classify the different **types of welded joints** used in machine design. Give **two examples of real applications** for each major type. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 200 mm × 100 mm × 12 mm mild-steel plate is to be welded to a 200 mm × 100 mm × 12 mm plate by **two parallel fillet welds** of equal leg size. The joint carries a **tensile load of 80 kN**. Assuming a permissible shear stress in the weld of **70 N/mm²**, determine the required **leg size $s$** of the fillet weld. Use effective length $l = 200$ mm. #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks: Classification with sketches and applications: | Weld Type | Sketch reference | Real Application | |---|---|---| | **Fillet** (triangular) | Diagram in textbook | Lap joint of a sheet-metal bracket, T-joint in a tank | | **Butt** (square, V, U) | Plate edges bevelled/no bevel | Boiler shell longitudinal seam, pressure vessel head | | **Spot** (resistance) | Localised nugget between two sheets | Car-body door panel, washing-machine drum | | **Seam** (continuous spot) | Roller electrodes | Fuel tank of a motorcycle, milk can | | **Plug/Slot** | Holes/slots filled with weld | Stiffener on a gusset, dished-end closure | | **Edge** | Overlap on bent edges | Thin sheet-metal cabinet corner | | **Flash butt** | Arc + upset | Railway rail joint, chain-link manufacture | (Marking: 1 mark for classification list, 2 marks for sketches, 2 marks for applications, 1 mark for the matrix, 1 mark for conclusion.) #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks: **Step 1 — Given data.** $$ P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80\,000 \text{ N}, \quad l = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad \tau_{perm} = 70 \text{ N/mm}^{2}, \quad n = 2 \text{ welds} $$ **Step 2 — Strength equation for two parallel fillet welds.** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= n \times 0.707 \, s \, l \times \tau_{perm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.** $$ \begin{aligned} 80\,000 &= 2 \times 0.707 \times s \times 200 \times 70 \\ 80\,000 &= 19\,796 \, s \\ s &= \frac{80\,000}{19\,796} \\ s &= 4.04 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Practical selection.** Use the **next standard leg size**, $s = 5$ mm. Also verify minimum leg size for 12 mm plate: $s_{min} \approx 1.5 \, t_{max}^{0.5} = 5.2$ mm → adopt **$s = 6$ mm** (safe). **Step 5 — Final answer.** $$ \boxed{\,s_{required} = 5 \text{ mm (theoretical); use } s = 6 \text{ mm in practice}\,} $$ (Marking: 1 mark data, 2 marks formula, 2 marks substitution, 1 mark selection, 1 mark final answer.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **types of resistance welds**. State the design formula for the **strength of a single-row spot-welded lap joint**, clearly defining each symbol. **(b) [7 Marks]** A lap joint between two 1.5 mm thick galvanised steel sheets is to be made by **spot welding** with **three spots in a single row**. Each spot has a nugget diameter of **6 mm**. The permissible shear stress in the nugget is **90 N/mm²**. Find the **maximum tensile load** the joint can transmit. #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks: **Resistance welds are of three types:** 1. **Spot weld** – A localised nugget is formed by copper electrode pressure + electric current for a fraction of a second. Used in sheet metal. 2. **Seam weld** – Continuous row of overlapping spot welds made by **roller electrodes**, producing a leak-proof joint. 3. **Projection weld** – One sheet has pre-formed projections (dimples) that concentrate the current, allowing multiple welds in one operation. **Strength formula for a single-row spot weld:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= n \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \tau_{perm} \end{aligned} $$ where: - $P$ = safe tensile load on the joint (N) - $n$ = number of spots in the row - $d$ = nugget diameter (mm) - $\tau_{perm}$ = permissible shear stress of the nugget (N/mm²) (Marking: 2 marks types, 1 mark formula, 2 marks for symbol definitions, 2 marks for description of nugget formation.) #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks: **Step 1 — Given data.** $$ n = 3, \quad d = 6 \text{ mm}, \quad \tau_{perm} = 90 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Area of one nugget.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{spot} &= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (6)^{2}}{4} = 28.274 \text{ mm}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total shear area for 3 spots.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{total} &= 3 \times 28.274 = 84.823 \text{ mm}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum tensile load.** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= A_{total} \times \tau_{perm} \\ &= 84.823 \times 90 \\ &= 7\,634 \text{ N} \\ &= 7.63 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Final answer.** $$ \boxed{\,P_{max} = 7.63 \text{ kN}\,} $$ (Marking: 1 mark data, 2 marks area calculation, 2 marks total area, 1 mark load, 1 mark final answer with unit.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 throat factor** in fillet welds → −2 marks. > 2. **Confusing throat thickness with leg size** in the stress equation → full step is invalidated. > 3. **Neglecting end-crater correction** for effective length: use $l_{eff} = l_{actual} - 2s$, not $l_{actual}$ → −1 mark. > 4. **Applying direct tensile stress formula to a fillet weld**: fillet welds fail in **shear**, not tension → −3 marks. > 5. **Forgetting the 1.414 multiplier for transverse welds** in combined loading → −2 marks. > 6. **Wrong units** (kN instead of N, mm² instead of m²) → −1 mark. > 7. **Picking next-standard leg size**: KTU expects a practical standard value (e.g., 5, 6, 8, 10 mm) not a fractional value. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - A **fillet weld** has a **triangular cross-section**; throat $t = 0.707 \, s$ for a 90° equal-leg weld. - A **butt weld** is in-plane and can carry **direct tension/compression** without eccentricity. - **Transverse fillet welds are ≈ 41.4 % stronger** than parallel fillet welds of the same leg size. - For **spot welds**, design strength $P = n \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \tau_{perm}$. - For **plug/slot welds**, strength is based on the **area of the hole/slot**, $A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ or $A = b \cdot L$. - The **failure plane in a fillet weld** is always the **throat plane at 45°**; it is assumed to fail in **shear**. - For **combined (direct + bending) loading**, compute $\tau_1$ (primary) and $\tau_2$ (secondary) and take their **vector sum**. - **Effective length** of a fillet weld = actual length $- 2s$ (end craters are weak). - **Square butt** for $t \leq 6$ mm; **single-V** for $8 < t \leq 20$ mm; **double-V** for $t > 20$ mm. - **Resistance welds** = spot, seam, projection; no filler metal is added. - Standard permissible stresses (mild steel E60 electrode): $\tau_{perm} = 70$ N/mm², $\sigma_{pt} = 110$ N/mm². - Always round up the leg size to the **next standard** value (4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm). - KTU 2024 marking: Part-A short questions test **definitions and classification**; Part-B tests **design calculation** with clear sub-parts. ---

topics.title: Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Stresses in Machine Design

In the context of **Machine Design (PEPET411)**, the study of *stresses* forms the analytical foundation upon which the safe sizing of every mechanical component rests. A machine element — whether it is a shaft transmitting torque, a bolt clamping a flange, or a spring storing energy — fails when the internal forces generated within its material exceed a critical threshold. **Stress** is the engineering quantity that quantifies this internal intensity of force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Stress ($\sigma$)** is defined as the internal resistive force per unit area developed within a deformable body when an external load is applied.
> 
> $$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$
> 
> The SI unit of stress is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**, though in machine design practice, **N/mm² = MPa** is the overwhelmingly preferred unit because component dimensions are typically in millimetres and forces are in Newtons or kiloNewtons.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge Under a Brick"

Imagine a soft kitchen sponge held between your palms while you press a heavy brick onto it. The sponge compresses; the *squeezing* you feel against your palm is the sponge resisting the load. That resistance, distributed across the contact area of your palm, is precisely what we call **stress**.

- The **brick's weight** = Applied Force ($F$)
- The **palm's contact area** = Cross-sectional Area ($A$)
- The **squeezing intensity** = Stress ($\sigma$)
- If you replace your palm with a needle-tip, the same force on a tiny area gives a massive stress — which is why screws and rivets need larger bearing areas.

This analogy is the gateway to understanding why machine designers obsess over **reducing stress concentration** rather than simply reducing load.

### 1.3 Classification of Stresses

Stresses are classified along two independent axes: **direction of force** and **plane of action**.

| Type | Driving Direction | Resulting Deformation | Typical Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Stress ($\sigma_t$)** | Pulls outward (perpendicular to face) | Elongation | Tie rod, chain link |
| **Compressive Stress ($\sigma_c$)** | Pushes inward (perpendicular to face) | Shortening | Column, pedestal |
| **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** | Tangential to the surface | Sliding / distortion | Rivet, bolt shank, key |
| **Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$)** | Local compressive contact | Crushing under fastener | Bolt bearing on plate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In every Machine Design problem, before writing a single equation, you **must** draw a free-body diagram and identify whether the critical cross-section is under **uniaxial tension**, **uniaxial compression**, **pure shear**, **bending**, **torsion**, or a **combination** thereof. Marks are awarded for this identification step.

### 1.4 Why Stresses Matter — The Three Design Failures

Stress analysis is the precursor to preventing three classical failure modes taught throughout the KTU Machine Design syllabus:

1. **Static/Yield Failure** — governed by the yield strength $S_{yt}$ of the material (ductile materials) or ultimate strength $S_{ut}$ (brittle materials).
2. **Fatigue Failure** — governed by endurance limit $S_e$ under fluctuating/cyclic loading (Module 3 territory).
3. **Buckling** — for slender compression members, governed by Euler's critical load.

All three are quantified using stress as the input variable. Thus, *stress is the universal language of strength*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Normal Stress on an Inclined Plane

Consider a uniform bar of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial tensile load $P$. If we conceptually slice the bar along an inclined plane whose normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the axis of the load, the inclined area becomes $A_\theta = A / \cos\theta$.

The force $P$ can then be resolved into:
- A **normal component** $N = P \cos\theta$ (perpendicular to the inclined plane)
- A **shear component** $V = P \sin\theta$ (tangential to the inclined plane)

Therefore, the **normal stress** and **shear stress** on the inclined plane are:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P \cos^2\theta}{A} = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$$

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{P \sin\theta \cos\theta}{A} = \frac{\sigma_x \sin 2\theta}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU exams:** When $\theta = 0°$, we recover $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x$ (maximum normal stress). When $\theta = 45°$, we get the **maximum shear stress** on the inclined plane: $\tau_{max} = \sigma_x / 2$.

### 2.2 Biaxial State of Stress (2-D Stress System)

A machine element rarely experiences stress in just one direction. A point inside a loaded body is typically subjected to stresses on multiple faces. The **general 2-D state of stress** is fully described by four quantities: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$, and $\tau_{yx}$. From equilibrium of an infinitesimal element, $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$.

### 2.3 Principal Stresses and Principal Planes

**Principal stresses** are the maximum and minimum normal stresses that occur at a point, on planes where the shear stress is *zero*. These planes are called **principal planes**.

The formula for principal stresses is derived from equilibrium of a wedge element cut at angle $\theta_p$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The orientation of the principal planes is given by:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** By convention, $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ (algebraically). Thus $\sigma_1$ is the *maximum principal stress* and $\sigma_3$ is the *minimum principal stress*. For 2-D problems, $\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress condition).

### 2.4 Maximum Shear Stress

The **maximum in-plane shear stress** occurs on planes oriented at $45°$ to the principal planes:

$$\tau_{max,in\text{-}plane} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

If we consider the 3-D absolute maximum (allowing out-of-plane shearing), and one principal stress is zero (plane stress), then:

$$\tau_{max,absolute} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_1}{2} \quad (\text{if } \sigma_3 = 0)$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Stress Equations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal stress (axial) | $\sigma = F/A$ | N/mm² | Tensile positive, compressive negative |
| 2 | Shear stress (direct) | $\tau = F/A$ | N/mm² | Force parallel to surface |
| 3 | Bearing stress | $\sigma_b = F/(d \cdot t)$ | N/mm² | $d$ = fastener dia, $t$ = plate thickness |
| 4 | Inclined plane normal stress | $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$ | N/mm² | Single axial load $P$ |
| 5 | Inclined plane shear stress | $\tau_\theta = (\sigma_x \sin 2\theta)/2$ | N/mm² | Maximum at $\theta = 45°$ |
| 6 | Principal stresses (2-D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/mm² | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ |
| 7 | Principal plane angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | degrees | Zero shear on this plane |
| 8 | Max in-plane shear | $\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | N/mm² | Always non-negative |
| 9 | Factor of Safety (Ductile) | $FOS = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max}$ | dimensionless | Based on yield |
| 10 | Factor of Safety (Brittle) | $FOS = S_{ut}/\sigma_{max}$ | dimensionless | Based on ultimate |
| 11 | Hoop stress (thin cylinder) | $\sigma_h = pd/(2t)$ | N/mm² | Used in pressure vessels |
| 12 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_l = pd/(4t)$ | N/mm² | Closed ends |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of Stress Analysis

Stress analysis underpins the design of:

- **Power transmission shafts** — combined bending + torsion → equivalent torque/wheel load approach.
- **Pressure vessels** — boilers, LPG cylinders, gas tanks (hoop vs. longitudinal).
- **Welded joints** — stress flow across the weld throat.
- **Springs** — Wahl's correction accounts for direct + torsional shear stress superposition.
- **Bolted joints** — tensile stress from preload + shear from external load.

The mathematical framework established here — *principal stresses, Mohr's circle, and maximum shear stress* — will be reused in nearly every subsequent module of PEPET411.
