## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Normal and Shear Stresses on an Inclined Plane

Let us rigorously derive the equations stated in Section 2.1.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the inclined cut.**
Consider a bar of original cross-sectional area $A$, cut by a plane whose outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the bar's longitudinal axis. The new (inclined) surface area is:

$$A_\theta = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}$$

The justification is purely geometric: the inclined face is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose base is the original area $A$.

**Step 2 — Resolution of the axial load $P$.**
The axial force $P$ acting along the bar axis can be decomposed into a component normal to the inclined plane and a component tangential (shear) to it.

$$N = P \cos\theta \quad \text{(normal component)}$$

$$V = P \sin\theta \quad \text{(shear component)}$$

**Step 3 — Computing the stresses.**
Dividing these force components by the inclined area $A_\theta = A / \cos\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{N}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \cos\theta}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{P \cos^2\theta}{A}$$

Using $\sigma_x = P/A$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$$

Similarly for shear:

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{V}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \sin\theta}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{P \sin\theta \cos\theta}{A} = \frac{\sigma_x \sin 2\theta}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of limiting cases.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta = 0° &: \quad \sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cdot 1 = \sigma_x \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_\theta = 0 \\
\theta = 90° &: \quad \sigma_\theta = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_\theta = 0 \\
\theta = 45° &: \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms our intuition: the inclined plane at $45°$ experiences equal normal and shear stresses.

### 3.2 Derivation of Principal Stresses

Consider a 2-D rectangular element with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on its faces. Cut a wedge at angle $\theta$ and enforce force equilibrium along the inclined face.

**Step 1 — Inclined area.**
If the inclined face has area $dA$, then the vertical and horizontal faces of the wedge have areas $dA \cos\theta$ and $dA \sin\theta$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Force balance perpendicular to inclined face.**
Summing forces perpendicular to the inclined plane:

$$\sigma_\theta \cdot dA = \sigma_x (dA \cos\theta)\cos\theta + \sigma_y (dA \sin\theta)\sin\theta + 2\tau_{xy}(dA \cos\theta)\sin\theta$$

Note: the factor of 2 on $\tau_{xy}$ arises because both vertical and horizontal faces contribute equal shear components along the inclined normal.

**Step 3 — Simplification.**
Dividing through by $dA$ and using double-angle identities ($\cos^2\theta = (1+\cos 2\theta)/2$, etc.):

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 4 — Maximisation.**
To find the angle at which $\sigma_\theta$ is maximum, set $d\sigma_\theta / d\theta = 0$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

Solving:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 5 — Substitution back.**
Substituting the optimal angle back into the $\sigma_\theta$ expression and using the identity $\cos 2\theta_p = (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum shear derivation.**
Following identical procedure but balancing forces *along* the inclined face instead of perpendicular to it yields:

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

Setting $d\tau_\theta / d\theta = 0$ gives $\tan 2\theta_s = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/(2\tau_{xy})$, which is rotated $45°$ from $\theta_p$. Substituting yields:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Combined Loading Scenarios

Machine elements under complex loading can be reduced to combined normal + shear stresses at a critical point. The KTU syllabus frequently tests the following combinations:

| Loading Case | $\sigma_x$ | $\sigma_y$ | $\tau_{xy}$ | $\sigma_1$ | Critical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure axial tension $P$ | $P/A$ | $0$ | $0$ | $P/A$ | Tie rods, bolts in tension |
| Pure bending moment $M$ | $Mc/I$ | $0$ | $0$ | $Mc/I$ | Beam under flexure |
| Pure torsion $T$ | $0$ | $0$ | $T \cdot r / J$ | $T \cdot r / J$ | Shaft under twisting |
| Axial + Bending | $\sigma_a + \sigma_b$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\sigma_a + \sigma_b$ | Eccentrically loaded column |
| Bending + Torsion | $\sigma_b$ | $0$ | $\tau_t$ | $\sigma_b/2 + \sqrt{(\sigma_b/2)^2 + \tau_t^2}$ | Crankshaft |
| Internal pressure (thin cylinder) | $\sigma_h = pd/2t$ | $\sigma_l = pd/4t$ | $0$ | $\sigma_h$ | Pressure vessel wall |
| Shrink-fit assembly | $\sigma_t$ (tangential) | $\sigma_r$ (radial) | $0$ | — | Compound cylinders |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The bending + torsion case ($\sigma_b$, $0$, $\tau_t$) is the **most frequently appearing combined-loading problem** in KTU Machine Design. It is directly used in shaft design (Module 4 of PEPET411).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Principal Stress Calculation

**Problem:** At a point in a loaded steel member, the stresses on the $x$-face and $y$-face are:
$\sigma_x = 80$ N/mm² (tensile), $\sigma_y = -40$ N/mm² (compressive), and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ N/mm².

Find: (a) Principal stresses and their planes, (b) Maximum shear stress.

**Solution:**

**(a) Principal stresses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2} \\
&= \frac{40}{2} \pm \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2} \\
&= 20 \pm \sqrt{3600 + 900} \\
&= 20 \pm \sqrt{4500} \\
&= 20 \pm 67.08 \\
\sigma_1 &= 87.08 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma_2 &= -47.08 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Principal plane angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5 \\
2\theta_p &= 26.57° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 13.28°
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{87.08 - (-47.08)}{2} = \frac{134.16}{2} = 67.08 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This matches the square-root term computed above, confirming the identity $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{((\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the terms: (i) Stress, (ii) Principal stress, and (iii) Factor of safety. State their units.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Stress** is the internal resistive force per unit area developed inside a material when external loads are applied. It is mathematically expressed as $\sigma = F/A$. **Unit:** N/mm² or MPa.

(ii) **Principal stress** is the maximum or minimum normal stress occurring at a point, on a plane where the shear stress is zero. There are three principal stresses, $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$, for a general 3-D state. **Unit:** N/mm².

(iii) **Factor of Safety (FOS)** is the ratio of the material's strength (yield or ultimate) to the working stress in the component. Mathematically, $\text{FOS} = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max}$ for ductile materials. It is a **dimensionless** quantity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (Part A):** Students often confuse *principal stress* with *maximum shear stress*. Remember: principal stress is a **normal stress**; max shear stress is a **shear stress**. They are perpendicular concepts. Also, do not forget to mention **units** — 1 mark is reserved for units in every Part A question.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Derive the expression for normal stress on an inclined plane in a member subjected to an axial tensile load $P$.*

**Model Answer:**

Consider a bar of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial tensile load $P$. Let a plane be cut such that its normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the axis of loading. The inclined area becomes $A_\theta = A/\cos\theta$.

The force $P$ can be resolved into:
- Normal to the plane: $N = P\cos\theta$
- Tangential to the plane: $V = P\sin\theta$

Therefore, the normal stress on the inclined plane is:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{N}{A_\theta} = \frac{P\cos\theta}{A/\cos\theta} = \frac{P\cos^2\theta}{A} = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta$$

This is maximum at $\theta = 0°$ giving $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x$ and zero at $\theta = 90°$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A circular bar of diameter 40 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN and a torque of 600 N·m simultaneously. Determine:
(i) The normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes 30° with the axis of the bar.
(ii) The principal stresses and maximum shear stress at the surface of the bar.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft transmits 75 kW at 200 rpm. It is supported on two bearings and carries a central pulley with a belt drive producing a bending moment of 1500 N·m at the pulley location. Determine the equivalent bending moment and equivalent torque using the maximum shear stress theory. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute axial stress $\sigma_x$.**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(40)^2 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{1256.64} = 63.66 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute torsional shear stress $\tau_{xy}$.**

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}d^4 = \frac{\pi}{32}(40)^4 = 2.513 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{(600 \times 10^3)(20)}{2.513 \times 10^5} = 47.75 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Stresses on the inclined plane ($\theta = 30°$).**

Using transformation equations with $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 63.66 \cos^2 30° + 47.75 \sin 60°$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 63.66 (0.75) + 47.75 (0.866) = 47.75 + 41.35 = 89.10 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

(Note sign convention — depends on shear direction)

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{63.66}{2}\sin 60° - 47.75\cos 60° = 31.83(0.866) - 47.75(0.5) = 27.57 - 23.88 = 3.69 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Principal stresses.**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{63.66 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{63.66 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (47.75)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 31.83 \pm \sqrt{(31.83)^2 + (47.75)^2} = 31.83 \pm \sqrt{1013.15 + 2280.06}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 31.83 \pm \sqrt{3293.21} = 31.83 \pm 57.39$$

$$\sigma_1 = 89.22 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_2 = -25.56 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Maximum shear stress.**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{89.22 - (-25.56)}{2} = 57.39 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Torque transmitted by the shaft.**

$$T = \frac{60P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 75 \times 10^3}{2\pi \times 200} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{1256.64} = 3581 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca).**

For combined bending and torsion, the equivalent bending moment and equivalent torque are:

$$M_e = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2} \quad \text{(often approximated as } M_e \approx M + 0.35T \text{ for quick estimates)}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute equivalent quantities.**

$$T_e = \sqrt{(1500)^2 + (3581)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^6 + 12.82 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{15.07 \times 10^6} = 3882 \text{ N·m}$$

For the conservative equivalent bending moment (using maximum shear stress theory):

$$M_e = \frac{M + T_e}{2} = \frac{1500 + 3882}{2} = 2691 \text{ N·m}$$

These values are then used to size the shaft diameter in subsequent design steps (covered in Module 4).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (Part B — Combined Loading):**
> The single most common mistake in KTU valuation is using $M_e = M$ and $T_e = T$ instead of the equivalent combined loading values. **Always** combine $M$ and $T$ through the appropriate theory-of-failure equation. Also, ensure the units are consistent — convert N·m to N·mm by multiplying by 1000 before computing stress.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 600 mm and a wall thickness of 12 mm. It is subjected to an internal pressure of 2.5 MPa. Calculate:
(i) Hoop stress, (ii) Longitudinal stress, (iii) Maximum shear stress in the wall.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Maximum Shear Stress Theory and Distortion Energy Theory of failure. Compare their predictions for a uniaxial tensile loading case and show that the von Mises yield criterion predicts yielding at a stress $1.155$ times lower than Tresca for the same material. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** $d = 600$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $p = 2.5$ MPa $= 2.5$ N/mm².

**(i) Hoop (circumferential) stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t} = \frac{2.5 \times 600}{2 \times 12} = \frac{1500}{24} = 62.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(ii) Longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{62.5}{2} = 31.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(iii) Maximum shear stress in the wall:**

For plane stress with $\sigma_1 = 62.5$, $\sigma_2 = 31.25$, $\sigma_3 = 0$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{62.5 - 0}{2} = 31.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST / Tresca Criterion):**
A ductile material yields when the maximum shear stress in the complex state of stress equals the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test.

Mathematically:

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\right) = \frac{S_{yt}}{2} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = S_{yt}$$

**Distortion Energy Theory (DET / Von Mises Criterion):**
Yielding begins when the distortion energy per unit volume reaches the same value as in a simple tension test at yield. The equivalent (von Mises) stress is:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

Yielding occurs when $\sigma_{vm} = S_{yt}$.

**Comparison for uniaxial tension ($P/A$ only):**

For uniaxial tension: $\sigma_1 = \sigma$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$.

- **MSST predicts yield at:** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma = S_{yt}$, so $\sigma_{yield} = S_{yt}$.
- **DET predicts yield at:**

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma - 0)^2 + (0 - 0)^2 + (0 - \sigma)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\sigma^2}{2}} = \sigma = S_{yt}$$

So for **pure uniaxial tension**, both theories predict the same yield stress — there is no difference in this simple case.

**The 1.155 factor emerges in pure shear:**
For pure shear: $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_3 = -\tau$, $\sigma_2 = 0$.

- **MSST:** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2\tau = S_{yt} \Rightarrow \tau_{yield} = S_{yt}/2 = 0.5 \, S_{yt}$.
- **DET:**

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{(\tau - 0)^2 + (0+\tau)^2 + (-\tau - \tau)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\tau^2 + \tau^2 + 4\tau^2}{2}} = \sqrt{3\tau^2} = \tau\sqrt{3} = S_{yt}$$

So $\tau_{yield} = S_{yt}/\sqrt{3} = 0.577 \, S_{yt}$.

**The ratio is therefore:**

$$\frac{\tau_{yield,DET}}{\tau_{yield,MSST}} = \frac{0.577}{0.500} = 1.155$$

This shows that von Mises (DET) predicts yielding at a stress **15.5% higher** than Tresca for pure shear, which is experimentally more accurate for ductile metals. Hence, von Mises is the preferred criterion in modern machine design codes (AGMA, ASME, ISO).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (Theory Comparison):**
> Many students misattribute the 1.15 factor to *uniaxial tension* — it is actually the **pure shear** case where the two theories diverge. In tension, they are identical. Always state the loading case explicitly before comparing theories.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Stress** = Force/Area; always quote units as **N/mm² = MPa** in KTU exams.
- [x] **Tensile stress** is positive; **compressive** is negative; **shear** is denoted $\tau$.
- [x] On an inclined plane under axial load: $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x\cos^2\theta$ and $\tau_\theta = (\sigma_x\sin 2\theta)/2$.
- [x] **Principal stresses** are normal stresses on planes of zero shear; $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.
- [x] Principal stress formula: $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2})^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- [x] **Max in-plane shear** $= (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$; **absolute max shear** $= (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$.
- [x] For **thin cylinders**: $\sigma_h = pd/2t$ and $\sigma_l = pd/4t$ — hoop stress is always twice the longitudinal.
- [x] **Combined loading (bending + torsion)** is the most common KTU Machine Design problem; always combine using MSST or DET.
- [x] **Factor of Safety**: $FOS = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max}$ for ductile, $FOS = S_{ut}/\sigma_{max}$ for brittle.
- [x] **Von Mises (DET)** is more accurate than Tresca (MSST) for ductile materials; both agree in uniaxial tension but differ by 15.5% in pure shear.
- [x] Always **draw the stress element** showing $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$ with correct directions before solving — examiners award 2 marks for this step alone.
- [x] In KTU valuation, **missing units** in the final answer costs 0.5 to 1 mark per answer — never skip them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Welded Joints Subjected to Axial, Torsional and Bending Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Welded Joints Subjected to Axial, Torsional and Bending Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **welded joint** is a permanent joint formed by localized fusion of parent metal with or without filler metal, wherein coalescence is achieved by heating the mating surfaces to a suitable temperature with or without the application of pressure. In the context of **Machine Design (PEPET411)**, we deal predominantly with **arc-welded joints** governed by the provisions of **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel – Code of Practice)** and AWS D1.1 standards.

For design purposes, welded joints in machine components are classified into two fundamental geometric categories:

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Butt Weld** | The two plates lie in the same plane and are joined edge-to-edge; the weld metal fills the gap between them. |
| **Fillet Weld** | The two plates lie in different planes (typically at right angles) and the weld metal is deposited in the triangular section formed at the corner. |

The two principal **orientations** of a fillet weld relative to the direction of applied load are:

- **Transverse Fillet Weld**: Weld axis is *perpendicular* to the line of action of the load. Higher strength.
- **Parallel (Longitudinal) Fillet Weld**: Weld axis is *parallel* to the line of action of the load. Lower strength due to non-uniform shear distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A fillet weld is *assumed to fail in shear through the throat*. The **throat thickness** is therefore the *effective* dimension of the weld, NOT the leg size. This is the single most important conceptual point in welded joint design.

## 1.2 The Throat Thickness — The Heart of the Problem

For a **right-angled (90°) fillet weld** with equal legs of size `s`:

$$t = s \cos(45°) = 0.707 \, s$$

This is the **effective throat thickness**. The area resisting shear per unit length of weld is therefore:

$$A_{eff} = 0.707 \times s \times 1 \text{ (per unit length)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedge of Metal":** Imagine a fillet weld as a triangular wedge of solidified molten metal glued into the corner of two plates. If you try to *slide one plate past the other*, the wedge resists this by failing along its *shortest perpendicular cross-section* — the throat. A knife placed on the throat and pushed sideways is the easiest way to rip the weld apart. That knife's depth is `0.707s`, and the force the knife must apply is what we use to size the weld.

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview of Loading Conditions

In real machine design (bracket-type supports, gear-base connections, motor mounting lugs, crane jib attachments, etc.), the weld rarely sees a pure axial pull. Instead, the load is almost always **eccentric**, producing a combination of:

1. **Direct (Primary) Shear** — from the translational component of the load.
2. **Torsional Shear** — from the moment of the load about the centroid of the weld group.
3. **Bending Stress (Tension/Compression)** — from the bending moment of the load about the relevant axis of the weld group (in some configurations).

The design task is to find the **resultant shear stress** at the *most critical point* of the weld group and ensure it does not exceed the **permissible shear stress in the weld metal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permissible Shear Stress in Weld (τ_perm):** As per IS 800:2007, for fillet welds made with E41 electrodes, the permissible shear stress is **τ_perm = 102 MPa** (≈ 102 N/mm²). For higher-grade electrodes (E50, E60), this value increases. This number is a **design constant** you must memorize for the KTU exam.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permissible shear stress in weld (E41 electrode):** τ_perm = **102 N/mm²**
- **Permissible shear stress in weld (E43 electrode):** τ_perm ≈ **110 N/mm²**
- **Throat factor for equal-leg 90° fillet weld:** **0.707**
- **Weld leg size limits (IS 800):** s_min = 3 mm; s_max = (t_thinner plate) − 1.5 mm
- **Effective length of fillet weld:** Actual length minus **2 × s** (for weld crater effects)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Failure Assumption and Design Philosophy

The classical **Filonenko-Borodich / Timoshenko assumption** used in KTU textbooks (Shigley, V.B. Bhandari, Khurmi-Gupta) for fillet welds is:

> The fillet weld is treated as a line of *zero width* lying along the *root* of the weld. The cross-section resisting the load is the **throat area**, and the failure occurs as **shear** on the throat plane (inclined at 45° to the weld legs).

The shear stress is **assumed to be uniformly distributed** along the throat — this is a simplifying assumption that yields conservative, easy-to-compute results and is what KTU examiners expect.

## 2.2 Pure Axial (Direct) Loading on a Fillet Weld

When a load `P` acts along the centroidal axis of a weld group of **total effective length** `L_e`:

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \times s \times L_e}$$

The design condition is:

$$\tau_d \leq \tau_{perm}$$

For a *single parallel* fillet weld of length `L` and leg `s`, the strength is:

$$P = 0.707 \, s \, L_e \, \tau_{perm}$$

## 2.3 Eccentric Loading — The General Case

When a load `P` is applied at an eccentricity `e` from the **centroid of the weld group**, the effect is twofold:

### 2.3.1 Direct (Primary) Shear
A uniform shear stress parallel to the load direction:

$$\tau_{direct} = \frac{P}{0.707 \times s \times L_{total}}$$

where `L_total` is the sum of all effective lengths of welds in the group.

### 2.3.2 Torsional Shear
The eccentric load creates a **torque** `T = P × e` about the centroid of the weld group. This torque produces a shear stress at any point `r` from the centroid:

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \times r}{J_w}$$

where `J_w` is the **polar second moment of area of the weld group about its centroid** (also called the "polar moment of inertia" of the weld pattern). The direction of this torsional shear is *perpendicular to the radius vector* `r`.

### 2.3.3 Resultant Shear (Vector Sum)
The two shear stresses `τ_direct` and `τ_t` are **vector quantities** acting in different directions at the critical point. The resultant is:

$$\tau_{res} = \sqrt{\tau_{direct}^2 + \tau_t^2 + 2 \, \tau_{direct} \, \tau_t \cos\theta}$$

where `θ` is the **angle between the two stress vectors** at the point of interest. In the most common configuration (load parallel to one of the welds, eccentricity measured from centroid), `θ = 90°` at the critical point, simplifying to:

$$\tau_{res} = \sqrt{\tau_{direct}^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When the resultant is at 90°:** Many KTU textbook problems (V.B. Bhandari, Shigley) state that the critical point is the one *farthest* from the centroid, and at that point the angle between `τ_direct` and `τ_t` is 90°. Verify by drawing the vectors — at a corner of a rectangular weld pattern, this is almost always the case. Always draw the vector diagram to confirm!

## 2.4 Polar Moment of Inertia for Common Weld Patterns

The value of `J_w` depends on the geometry. For the most common patterns:

### 2.4.1 Rectangular Fillet Weld Group (b × d, where `b` is the dimension parallel to load, `d` perpendicular)

$$J_w = \frac{b \, d}{12} \left( b^2 + d^2 \right)$$

This comes from: `J_w = I_x + I_y = (b·d³)/12 + (d·b³)/12`.

> **Caveat:** This formula assumes the *centroid* lies at the geometric center. For an **unsymmetric** weld group (e.g., C-shape, L-shape, or unbalanced rectangular), the centroid must be located first using `x̄ = ΣA_i x_i / ΣA_i`, where the area of each weld line is `l_i × 1` (unit depth).

### 2.4.2 C-Shaped (U-Shaped) Weld Around Three Sides
For a weld of total horizontal length `b` (top and bottom flanges) and two vertical sides of height `d`, with a gap `g` at the bottom center:

$$J_w = \frac{b \, d^2}{2} \left( \frac{b^2}{6} + d^2 \right) + \frac{g \, d^3}{6}$$

> **Note:** A complete derivation of this is shown in Section 3.

### 2.4.3 Circular Weld Group (Annular Weld)
For a weld on a circle of diameter `D` (e.g., flange of a pipe):

$$J_w = \frac{\pi D^3}{32} \times t_{weld}$$ — but for unit throat (line weld):

$$J_w = \frac{\pi D^3}{8}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip — This is the master reference for any numerical problem in KTU on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Throat thickness (90° fillet) | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | mm |
| 2 | Effective length of weld | $L_e = L_{actual} - 2s$ | mm |
| 3 | Direct shear stress | $\tau_d = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{total}}$ | N/mm² |
| 4 | Torsional shear at point r | $\tau_t = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J_w}$ | N/mm² |
| 5 | Torque from eccentric load | $T = P \times e$ | N·mm |
| 6 | Polar moment (rectangular pattern, b×d) | $J_w = \dfrac{b \, d}{12}(b^2 + d^2)$ | mm⁴ |
| 7 | Polar moment (line weld, unit width) — generic | $J_w = I_x + I_y$ | mm⁴ |
| 8 | Resultant (θ = 90° case) | $\tau_{res} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2}$ | N/mm² |
| 9 | Resultant (general angle θ) | $\tau_{res} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2 + 2\tau_d \tau_t \cos\theta}$ | N/mm² |
| 10 | Bending stress (rare, for fillet welds in bending) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I_w}$ | N/mm² |
| 11 | Design strength (axial) | $P = 0.707 \, s \, L_e \, \tau_{perm}$ | N |
| 12 | Permissible shear (E41 electrode) | $\tau_{perm} = 102$ | N/mm² |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

These principles govern the design of every bracket-mounted gearbox, motor pillow block, crane column-to-base connection, off-highway vehicle chassis joint, and pressure vessel flange. In high-cycle fatigue applications (railway bogies, wind turbine hubs), the *stress concentration* at the weld toe becomes the limiting factor and additional fatigue checks (per IS 800:2007) are performed — but the static design taught in KTU Module 2 is the foundational starting point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Throat Thickness of a 90° Equal-Leg Fillet Weld

Consider a fillet weld joining two plates at 90°, with equal legs of size `s`. The cross-section of the weld is an isosceles right triangle with the two legs of length `s` along the two plates. The throat is the **perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse**.

By geometry, the hypotenuse has length `s√2`, and the altitude of an isosceles right triangle to the hypotenuse is half the hypotenuse:

$$t = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \, s$$

For an **unequal-leg fillet weld** with legs `s₁` and `s₂` at angle `α` between the plates, the general expression is:

$$t = \frac{s_1 s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2 - 2 s_1 s_2 \cos\alpha}}$$

For `s₁ = s₂ = s` and `α = 90°`, this reduces to `t = 0.707s`. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Direct Shear Stress in an Axially Loaded Fillet Weld

Let a parallel fillet weld of leg `s` and effective length `L_e` carry an axial load `P` (tensile). The load is transmitted to the parent plates through the throat area. Per unit length, the throat area is `0.707s × 1`. Over the total length:

$$A_{throat} = 0.707 \, s \, L_e$$

The assumption of *uniform shear distribution* along the throat yields:

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{throat}} = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_e}$$

Rearranging for the design problem (find `s` or `L_e` given `P`):

$$s = \frac{P}{0.707 \, L_e \, \tau_{perm}} \quad \text{or} \quad L_e = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Polar Moment of Inertia of a Rectangular Weld Group (b × d)

A line weld forming the perimeter of a rectangle of width `b` (along x-axis) and depth `d` (along y-axis), all unit-width strips, is modelled as a system of infinitesimal lines. Each element of the weld at coordinates `(x, y)` is at distance `r = √(x² + y²)` from the centroid.

The **second moment of area** of the weld group about the x-axis (with unit throat width) is:

$$I_x = \int y^2 \, dA = \int_{-d/2}^{d/2} y^2 \cdot b \, dy = b \cdot \frac{y^3}{3}\bigg\vert_{-d/2}^{d/2} = b \cdot \frac{2(d/2)^3}{1} = \frac{b \, d^3}{12}$$

Wait — this assumes the weld is a *filled* rectangle. For a *perimeter weld* (which is the actual KTU case), the integration is only over the weld *lines*. For a weld of unit throat width and length along the perimeter:

$$I_x = \int y^2 \, dL = 2 \int_{-d/2}^{d/2} y^2 \, dy \quad \text{(for two horizontal welds, each of length b)}$$

But this double-counts. The correct KTU formula for a **rectangular perimeter weld of width b and depth d** is derived as follows.

For each weld segment of length `L` at distance `y` from the centroid, its contribution to `I_x` is `L × y²`. For the two horizontal welds of length `b` at `y = ±d/2`:

$$I_{x,horiz} = 2 \times b \times (d/2)^2 = \frac{b \, d^2}{2}$$

For the two vertical welds of length `d` spanning `y` from `-d/2` to `+d/2`:

$$I_{x,vert} = 2 \int_{-d/2}^{d/2} y^2 \, dy = 2 \cdot \frac{2(d/2)^3}{3} = \frac{d^3}{6}$$

Total: `I_x = b·d²/2 + d³/6 = (3b·d² + d³)/6 = d²(3b + d)/6`

Similarly, by symmetry: `I_y = b²(3d + b)/6`

And the polar moment is `J_w = I_x + I_y`. Substituting:

$$J_w = \frac{d^2(3b + d)}{6} + \frac{b^2(3d + b)}{6} = \frac{3bd^2 + d^3 + 3b^2d + b^3}{6} = \frac{bd(b + d)}{2} + \frac{b^3 + d^3}{6}$$

This is the **exact** KTU formula. However, **most textbooks (Bhandari, Shigley)** use the simpler **filled-rectangle approximation**:

$$J_w \approx \frac{bd}{12}(b^2 + d^2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** The KTU exam accepts the **filled-rectangle approximation** `J_w = (bd/12)(b² + d²)` for rectangular weld groups because the difference is small and the assumption of uniform shear at the throat is itself an approximation. *However*, for **C-shaped or unsymmetric** welds, you must derive `J_w` rigorously by the `Σ Lᵢ rᵢ²` method. This is a frequent mark-loser if you blindly apply the rectangular formula.

## 3.4 Derivation: Torsional Shear Stress at the Critical Point

Given a weld group with centroid `G`, and an eccentric load `P` at distance `e` from `G`. The torque about `G` is:

$$T = P \times e$$

At a point `P_i` on the weld, at radial distance `r_i` from `G`, the shear stress in the weld is:

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r_i}{J_w}$$

The maximum value is at the point **farthest from the centroid**:

$$\tau_{t,max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J_w}$$

The *direction* of this shear stress is **perpendicular to the radius vector** `r` (the line from `G` to `P_i`), and it is in the sense of rotation caused by the torque.

## 3.5 Full Step-by-Step Worked Procedure for Eccentric Loading

This is the **canonical KTU exam procedure** for an eccentrically loaded fillet weld (e.g., a bracket bolted to a column, with the load hanging from the bracket arm).

**Step 1:** Identify the weld group geometry. Draw a clean, dimensioned sketch showing the centroid `G` and the load point.

**Step 2:** Calculate `J_w` about the centroid. For a rectangular perimeter:
- Use the **exact** perimeter formula: `J_w = d²(3b + d)/6 + b²(3d + b)/6`
- OR the **textbook approximation**: `J_w = (bd/12)(b² + d²)`

**Step 3:** Identify the **critical point** — usually the point on the weld *farthest from the centroid*.

**Step 4:** Calculate the direct shear stress:
$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{total}}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the maximum torsional shear at the critical point:
$$\tau_t = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{max}}{J_w}$$

**Step 6:** Determine the **angle θ** between `τ_d` and `τ_t` at the critical point by drawing the vector diagram. For a corner point of a rectangular weld with load parallel to one side, `θ = 90°`.

**Step 7:** Calculate the resultant:
$$\tau_{res} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2 + 2 \, \tau_d \, \tau_t \, \cos\theta}$$

For `θ = 90°`: `τ_res = √(τ_d² + τ_t²)`.

**Step 8:** Compare with allowable:
$$\tau_{res} \leq \tau_{perm} = 102 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

If exceeded, **increase the leg size `s`** and re-check (since `τ_d` is inversely proportional to `s`, but `τ_t` is *independent* of `s` — this is a subtle but important point!).

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (For Internal Mastery)

**Problem:** A bracket carries an eccentric vertical load of `P = 50 kN` at `e = 200 mm` from the centroid of a rectangular weld pattern of size `200 mm × 150 mm` (width × depth). The weld leg is `s = 10 mm`. Permissible shear stress `τ_perm = 102 N/mm²`. Check the design.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

Step 1: Total perimeter weld length:
$$L_{total} = 2(b + d) = 2(200 + 150) = 700 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Polar moment (textbook approximation):
$$J_w = \frac{b \, d}{12}(b^2 + d^2) = \frac{200 \times 150}{12}(200^2 + 150^2) = 2500 \times (40000 + 22500) = 2500 \times 62500 = 1.5625 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

Step 3: Critical point — corner farthest from centroid:
$$r_{max} = \sqrt{(b/2)^2 + (d/2)^2} = \sqrt{100^2 + 75^2} = \sqrt{10000 + 5625} = \sqrt{15625} = 125 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4: Direct shear:
$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{total}} = \frac{50000}{0.707 \times 10 \times 700} = \frac{50000}{4949} \approx 10.10 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Step 5: Torsional shear at the critical point:
$$T = P \times e = 50000 \times 200 = 1.0 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J_w} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^7 \times 125}{1.5625 \times 10^8} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^9}{1.5625 \times 10^8} = 8.00 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Step 6: At the corner of a rectangle with load parallel to `b`, the angle between `τ_d` (vertical) and `τ_t` (perpendicular to radius) is `90°`.

Step 7: Resultant:
$$\tau_{res} = \sqrt{10.10^2 + 8.00^2} = \sqrt{102.01 + 64.00} = \sqrt{166.01} \approx 12.88 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Step 8: Check:
$$\tau_{res} = 12.88 \text{ N/mm}^2 \ll 102 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ Design is SAFE.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The direct shear is by far the dominant stress in this case. The weld is grossly under-stressed, which means the leg size could be reduced or a more economical layout chosen. In a real KTU problem, you may be asked to find the *minimum* leg size — solve `√(τ_d² + τ_t²) = 102` for `s`. Note that `τ_t` does NOT depend on `s`, so this is a quadratic in `s`:
> $$\left(\frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{total}}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2 = 102^2 \implies s = \frac{P}{0.707 \, L_{total} \, \sqrt{102^2 - \tau_t^2}}$$

## 3.7 Combined Axial + Bending + Torsion — The General Case

For a **cantilever-type** weld group where the eccentric load creates both bending moment `M` and torque `T` (e.g., load applied in a plane parallel to the weld face, at an offset *and* height), the stresses are:

- **Bending normal stress:** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I_w}$
- **Bending shear stress** (if applicable): $\tau_b = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I_w \cdot b_w}$
- **Direct shear:** $\tau_d = \dfrac{V}{A_w}$
- **Torsional shear:** $\tau_t = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J_w}$

The **principal stresses** at the critical point are then computed from combined `σ` and `τ`, and compared against **allowable normal and shear stresses** of the weld metal (typically `σ_perm ≈ 1.5 × τ_perm`).

This generalized case appears in advanced KTU problems involving **welded I-beam to column flange** connections or **overhead crane girders** mounted via welded brackets.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Flow in an Eccentrically Loaded Welded Bracket

> The figure below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping how a single eccentric load `P` decomposes into multiple stress components at every point of the weld group, culminating in the safety check.

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputLoad["Applied Eccentric Load P at distance e from centroid G"] --> Decompose{"Decompose into components"}
    Decompose --> DirectShear["Direct Shear: tau_d = P / 0.707 s L_total"]
    Decompose --> Torsion["Torsional Shear: tau_t = T r / J_w, where T = P e"]
    DirectShear --> VectorAdd{"Vector Addition at critical point r_max"}
    Torsion --> VectorAdd
    VectorAdd --> AngleCheck{"Angle theta between vectors at critical point?"}
    AngleCheck -->|theta = 90| Resultant90["tau_res = sqrt tau_d squared + tau_t squared"]
    AngleCheck -->|theta general| ResultantGen["tau_res = sqrt tau_d squared + tau_t squared + 2 tau_d tau_t cos theta"]
    Resultant90 --> Compare["Compare tau_res with tau_perm = 102 N per mm squared"]
    ResultantGen --> Compare
    Compare -->|tau_res less than tau_perm| Safe["DESIGN IS SAFE: Record s and L_total"]
    Compare -->|tau_res greater than tau_perm| Unsafe["INCREASE LEG SIZE s and RECOMPUTE"]
    Unsafe --> DirectShear
    Safe --> End(["Output: Final leg size s and weld length L"])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Weld Group Geometry Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start(["Identify Weld Pattern"]) --> Type{"Type of Weld Group?"}
    Type -->|Rectangular Perimeter b by d| Rect["J_w equals bd by 12 times b squared plus d squared"]
    Type -->|C-Shaped or U-Shaped| CShape["Compute centroid x_bar first, then J_w = sum of L_i times r_i squared"]
    Type -->|Circular Annular| Circle["J_w equals pi D cubed by 8 for line weld"]
    Type -->|L-Shaped Unsymmetric| LShape["Locate centroid by area moments, then J_w about that centroid"]
    Rect --> Output(["Use J_w in torsional stress equation"])
    CShape --> Output
    Circle --> Output
    LShape --> Output
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Combined Loading Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start(["Load Applied to Weld Group"]) --> Q1{"Is the load concentric or eccentric?"}
    Q1 -->|Concentric| Axial["Pure Direct Shear: tau_d = P divided by 0.707 s L_total"]
    Q1 -->|Eccentric| Ecc{"Is load in plane of weld or perpendicular to it?"}
    Ecc -->|In Plane| TorsionOnly["Torsion plus Direct Shear combination"]
    Ecc -->|Perpendicular Plane| TorsionBend["Torsion plus Bending plus Direct Shear: Full combined state"]
    Axial --> Check1["Check tau_d less than tau_perm"]
    TorsionOnly --> Check2["Compute tau_res vector sum and check against tau_perm"]
    TorsionBend --> Check3["Compute principal stresses and check against sigma_perm and tau_perm"]
    Check1 --> Result(["Design Verified"])
    Check2 --> Result
    Check3 --> Result
```

## 4.4 Schematic Notation Summary Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical KTU Unit |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| P | Applied load (axial component) | N |
| T | Torque (= P × e) | N·mm |
| M | Bending moment | N·mm |
| e | Eccentricity of load from centroid | mm |
| s | Leg size of fillet weld | mm |
| t | Throat thickness (= 0.707 s) | mm |
| L_total | Sum of all weld segment lengths | mm |
| L_e | Effective length (L_actual − 2s) | mm |
| J_w | Polar moment of weld group about centroid | mm⁴ |
| I_w | Second moment of weld group (bending axis) | mm⁴ |
| r | Radial distance from centroid to point of interest | mm |
| r_max | Radial distance to farthest point of weld | mm |
| τ_d | Direct (primary) shear stress | N/mm² |
| τ_t | Torsional shear stress | N/mm² |
| τ_res | Resultant (vector-summed) shear stress | N/mm² |
| τ_perm | Permissible shear stress in weld | N/mm² = **102** |
| θ | Angle between τ_d and τ_t at the critical point | degrees / radians |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Physical Drawings:** A Mermaid-rendered schematic cannot faithfully reproduce the geometric reality of a fillet weld (a 3D triangular section with two parent plates at 90°). The block diagrams above abstract the **force-flow logic** of the design, which is what KTU examiners test. For physical sketches, you are expected to draw a clean 2D side-view (two rectangles at 90° with a triangular weld bead) and a plan-view of the weld pattern showing dimensions `b`, `d`, load point, and centroid `G` — directly in your answer booklet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q:** Define *throat thickness* of a fillet weld. Why is it considered the *effective* dimension rather than the leg size for design purposes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **throat thickness** of a fillet weld is the **shortest distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse face** of the triangular weld cross-section. For a 90° equal-leg fillet weld of leg size `s`, the throat thickness is `t = 0.707s`.

It is considered the **effective dimension** because the failure of a fillet weld is assumed to occur by **shear along the throat plane**, which is the *weakest cross-section* of the weld metal. The leg size is merely a geometric descriptor of how the weld sits on the parent plates; it is the throat area that physically resists the load. Hence, the permissible load on the weld is calculated as `P = 0.707 s L_e τ_perm`, where the **throat area = 0.707 s × L_e** governs the design.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: formula; 1 mark: justification of failure mode)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** State the two principal orientations of a fillet weld and compare their strength under axial loading.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two principal orientations are:

1. **Parallel (Longitudinal) Fillet Weld:** The weld axis is *parallel* to the direction of the applied load. Stress distribution along the weld is **non-uniform** (high at the ends, low in the middle). The strength per unit length is `0.707 s τ_perm`, but actual strength is **lower** due to the non-uniform distribution.

2. **Transverse Fillet Weld:** The weld axis is *perpendicular* to the load direction. Stress is **more uniformly distributed** along the throat, and the throat is loaded in a more direct manner.

**Comparison:** For the *same* leg size and length, a transverse fillet weld is **stronger** than a parallel fillet weld, typically by a factor of approximately **1.3 to 1.5** for ductile failure. However, for KTU design problems, both are conservatively assumed to have the same strength `0.707 s τ_perm` per unit length.

*(1 mark each for definitions; 1 mark for comparison)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 60 mm wide and 100 mm deep rectangular bar is welded to a support by means of fillet welds on all four sides, as shown in Fig. Q-A. The bar carries a steady tensile load of 90 kN, applied at the centroid of the weld group, along the longitudinal axis of the bar. Assuming a leg size of 8 mm and permissible shear stress in the weld as 102 N/mm², determine the *effective length* of the weld required. Also find the *throat thickness* and verify whether the proposed leg size is safe.

**(b) [7 Marks)]** If the same bar in part (a) is instead subjected to an **eccentric** tensile load of 90 kN applied at a distance of 150 mm from the centroid of the weld group (in the plane of the weld), determine the **maximum resultant shear stress** in the weld and check the design. Comment on whether the eccentric load causes a critical increase in the stress.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Concentric Axial Load

**Given:**
- Weld pattern: rectangular perimeter, b = 60 mm (along load), d = 100 mm
- Load: P = 90,000 N (concentric, axial)
- Leg size: s = 8 mm
- Permissible shear: τ_perm = 102 N/mm²

**Step 1: Throat thickness**
$$t = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Direct shear stress for a unit effective length of weld**
For a total effective length `L_e`, the shear stress is:
$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_e} \leq \tau_{perm}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the required effective length**
$$L_e = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm}} = \frac{90000}{0.707 \times 8 \times 102}$$

$$L_e = \frac{90000}{576.91} = 156.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Verification of proposed design**
Total geometric perimeter = 2(b + d) = 2(60 + 100) = 320 mm. The actual physical length of weld on all four sides is 320 mm. Since `L_e = 156.0 mm` is the *required* effective length and 320 mm (after subtracting 2s = 16 mm = 304 mm) is available, the design is **safe**.

Available effective length = 320 − 2(8) = **304 mm > 156.0 mm** ✓

Verification of stress at the design effective length:
$$\tau_d = \frac{90000}{0.707 \times 8 \times 304} = \frac{90000}{1719.4} = 52.34 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 102 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Result (Part a):** Required `L_e = 156 mm`; Throat `t = 5.656 mm`; Leg size `s = 8 mm` is **SAFE**.

#### Part (b) — Eccentric Load (Torque + Direct Shear)

**Given (new):** P = 90,000 N; e = 150 mm; same weld geometry.

**Step 1: Direct shear**
$$L_{total} = 2(60 + 100) = 320 \text{ mm}$$
$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{total}} = \frac{90000}{0.707 \times 8 \times 320} = \frac{90000}{1809.92} = 49.72 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Torque**
$$T = P \times e = 90000 \times 150 = 1.35 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Polar moment of inertia (rectangular approximation)**
$$J_w = \frac{b \, d}{12}(b^2 + d^2) = \frac{60 \times 100}{12}(60^2 + 100^2) = 500 \times (3600 + 10000) = 500 \times 13600 = 6.8 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Critical point — farthest corner**
$$r_{max} = \sqrt{(b/2)^2 + (d/2)^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 50^2} = \sqrt{900 + 2500} = \sqrt{3400} \approx 58.31 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Torsional shear at the critical point**
$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J_w} = \frac{1.35 \times 10^7 \times 58.31}{6.8 \times 10^6} = \frac{7.872 \times 10^8}{6.8 \times 10^6} = 115.76 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Angle between vectors**
At the corner, the direct shear `τ_d` acts along the bar axis (say, horizontal). The torsional shear `τ_t` is perpendicular to the radius vector. At the corner of a rectangle where the radius is at angle `α = arctan(50/30) = 59.04°` from the bar axis, the torsional shear is at `90° - 59.04° = 30.96°` from the bar axis. The angle between the two is therefore:
$$\theta = 90° \text{ (since direct shear is along the bar axis, and torsional shear is perpendicular to the radius)}$$

**Step 7: Resultant shear (using θ = 90° for KTU simplicity)**
$$\tau_{res} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2} = \sqrt{49.72^2 + 115.76^2} = \sqrt{2472.1 + 13400.4} = \sqrt{15872.5} = 126.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 8: Design check**
$$\tau_{res} = 126.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 > \tau_{perm} = 102 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \textbf{UNSAFE} \quad \times$$

**Comment:** The eccentric load increases the maximum stress by a factor of **2.53×** (from 49.72 to 126.0 N/mm²) compared to the concentric case. The torsional component (115.76) is dominant. The design must be revised — either increase leg size `s` (note: `τ_t` is independent of `s`, only `τ_d` reduces — so this is a partial fix), or increase the weld pattern dimensions, or add stiffeners. The new leg size can be found by solving:

$$s = \frac{P}{0.707 \, L_{total} \sqrt{\tau_{perm}^2 - \tau_t^2}} = \frac{90000}{0.707 \times 320 \times \sqrt{102^2 - 115.76^2}}$$

Since `115.76 > 102`, even an infinite leg size will not save the design. **The weld geometry must be changed** — for example, by moving the load to reduce eccentricity, or by enlarging `b` and `d`.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two flat steel plates, each 12 mm thick, are joined by a **transverse fillet weld** of leg size 10 mm. The joint is required to transmit a steady load of 80 kN. Determine:
(i) the throat thickness,
(ii) the effective length of weld required, and
(iii) the strength of the joint per cm of weld length.
Use τ_perm = 102 N/mm².

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular flange of diameter 200 mm is welded to a shaft by means of an **annular fillet weld**. A torque of 5 kN·m is applied to the shaft. Determine the **required leg size** of the weld, given τ_perm = 102 N/mm². Assume the weld to be a line weld at radius R = 100 mm.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Transverse Fillet Weld Strength

**Given:**
- Plate thickness = 12 mm (only relevant for max leg check)
- Leg size s = 10 mm
- Load P = 80,000 N
- τ_perm = 102 N/mm²

**Step 1: Verify leg size is within limits**
IS 800:2007 limits: `s_max = t_thinner − 1.5 = 12 − 1.5 = 10.5 mm`. So s = 10 mm is **acceptable** ✓

**Step 2: Throat thickness**
$$t = 0.707 \, s = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Effective length required**
$$L_e = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm}} = \frac{80000}{0.707 \times 10 \times 102} = \frac{80000}{721.14} = 110.94 \text{ mm}$$

Round up to L_e = **111 mm** for design safety.

**Step 4: Strength per cm of weld length**
$$P_{per\,cm} = 0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 0.707 \times 10 \times 102 \times 10 = 7211.4 \text{ N/cm} \approx 7.21 \text{ kN/cm}$$

**Results (Part a):**
- (i) Throat t = **7.07 mm**
- (ii) Required effective length L_e ≈ **111 mm**
- (iii) Strength per cm of weld ≈ **7.21 kN/cm**

#### Part (b) — Annular Weld Under Torque

**Given:**
- Weld diameter D = 200 mm, hence R = 100 mm
- Torque T = 5 kN·m = 5 × 10⁶ N·mm
- τ_perm = 102 N/mm²

**Step 1: Polar moment of inertia of the weld pattern**
For an annular line weld at radius R, modelled as unit-width strip:
$$J_w = \frac{\pi D^3}{8} = \frac{\pi \times 200^3}{8} = \frac{\pi \times 8 \times 10^6}{8} = \pi \times 10^6 = 3.1416 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Maximum torsional shear stress**
At any point on the weld, `r = R = 100 mm`. The shear stress is:
$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot R}{J_w} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 100}{3.1416 \times 10^6} = \frac{5 \times 10^8}{3.1416 \times 10^6} = 159.15 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Set up the strength equation**
The strength of a fillet weld in shear is `0.707 s τ_perm`. This must equal or exceed the applied torsional shear `τ_t`:
$$0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm} \geq \tau_t$$
$$s \geq \frac{\tau_t}{0.707 \, \tau_{perm}} = \frac{159.15}{0.707 \times 102} = \frac{159.15}{72.114} = 2.207 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply practical design rule**
IS 800:2007 specifies `s_min = 3 mm` for a fillet weld (regardless of calculation). Therefore, adopt:
$$s = \max(s_{calculated}, s_{min}) = \max(2.21, 3) = \textbf{3 mm}$$

**Result (Part b):** Required leg size s = **3 mm** (governed by the minimum-size rule, not by the strength calculation).

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in KTU Welded Joint Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 2s from the actual weld length** to get the effective length. If the problem states "weld of 100 mm length with s = 8 mm", the *effective* length is 100 − 16 = 84 mm, NOT 100 mm. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Using the wrong J_w formula for C-shaped or L-shaped welds.** The rectangular formula `bd(b² + d²)/12` is ONLY valid for closed symmetric rectangular perimeters. For an L or C, you must locate the centroid first using `x̄ = ΣL_i x_i / ΣL_i` and then compute `J_w = Σ L_i × r_i²` where `r_i` is the distance from the *centroid* to each weld line. **(−3 marks, often a full-question killer)**
>
> 3. **Adding τ_d and τ_t arithmetically** instead of vectorially. The formula is the **square root of the sum of squares** (when θ = 90°), NOT `τ_d + τ_t`. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting that τ_t is independent of s.** If the design is failing because `τ_res > τ_perm`, increasing `s` only helps with `τ_d`, not `τ_t`. Students often double the leg size, redo the calculation, and are surprised the result barely changes — then assume the textbook is wrong. The textbook is correct; the geometry (b, d) needs changing.
>
> 5. **Confusing the angle θ.** At the farthest corner of a rectangle, the angle between `τ_d` (along the load axis) and `τ_t` (perpendicular to the radius vector) is **90°**, not the angle of the radius itself. Draw the vectors carefully on the figure.
>
> 6. **Using τ_perm = 110 N/mm²** for E41 electrode (the value 110 is for the parent plate, not the weld). The KTU-accepted value for the weld is **102 N/mm²**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Quick-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- ☐ **Throat thickness** `t = 0.707s` is the *design dimension*, not the leg size `s`. The factor **0.707** comes from `1/√2 = cos 45°`.
- ☐ **Failure plane of a fillet weld** is the **throat**, assumed in **shear**.
- ☐ **Permissible shear stress in weld** = **102 N/mm²** (E41 electrode, IS 800:2007).
- ☐ **Effective length** = **Actual length − 2s** (weld crater correction at start and end).
- ☐ **Concentric axial load** → only direct shear: `τ_d = P / (0.707 s L_total)`.
- ☐ **Eccentric in-plane load** → direct shear + torsional shear, combined as vectors.
- ☐ **Critical point** = point of weld *farthest from the centroid* of the weld group.
- ☐ **Polar moment of inertia J_w** is the analogue of J for a weld group; for a symmetric rectangular perimeter, `J_w = (bd/12)(b² + d²)`.
- ☐ **Resultant shear** for the most common case (`θ = 90°`): `τ_res = √(τ_d² + τ_t²)`.
- ☐ **Torsional shear τ_t is independent of leg size s**; only `τ_d` is affected by `s`.
- ☐ **Min leg size** by IS 800:2007 is **3 mm**; max leg size ≤ thinner plate thickness − **1.5 mm**.
- ☐ For an **annular weld** at diameter D, `J_w = πD³/8` (line weld at unit width).
- ☐ For an **unsymmetric (C- or L-shaped) weld**, locate the **centroid first**, then compute `J_w` using the parallel-axis theorem if necessary.
- ☐ In **combined bending + torsion** (out-of-plane load), check **principal stresses** at the critical point, not just resultant shear.
- ☐ When `τ_res > τ_perm` and increasing `s` does not help, the **geometry (b, d, or pattern)** must be redesigned — this is the *correct* engineering conclusion, not an error.
- ☐ Always **draw the vector diagram** at the critical point to determine `θ`; never assume `θ = 90°` blindly.

> **Final Note:** This topic is a **high-weightage, high-scoring** item in the KTU PEPET411 ESE and is also tested in **Module 1 of GATE Mechanical** and **Assistant Engineer (Mechanical) Kerala PSC** exams. Master the eccentric-load procedure, the `J_w` formula, and the resultant-shear vector sum — and you can solve ~80% of the problem variants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welds Subjected to Fluctuating Loads
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Welds Subjected to Fluctuating Loads

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Machine Design (PEPET411)**, a **fluctuating load** on a welded joint is defined as a time-varying load whose magnitude (and sometimes direction) cycles between a maximum and a minimum value. Welded joints subjected to such cyclic loading experience **fatigue failure**, which occurs at stress levels well below the ultimate static strength of the weld metal or the base metal.

The three primary stress parameters used to characterize fluctuating loads on welds are:

- **Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$)** — the largest tensile (or compressive) stress in the cycle.
- **Minimum Stress ($\sigma_{min}$)** — the smallest stress in the cycle.
- **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$)** — the average of the maximum and minimum stresses.
- **Stress Amplitude / Alternating Stress ($\sigma_a$)** — half the algebraic difference between maximum and minimum stresses.
- **Stress Range ($\sigma_r$)** — the algebraic difference between maximum and minimum stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Welded joints are *inherently* stress-raisers because of the geometric discontinuities (toe, root, underbead) and metallurgical discontinuities (weld metal vs. heat-affected zone). Even "perfect" welds exhibit a stress concentration factor (SCF) of $K_t \geq 1.0$ due to micro-notches at the weld toe. This is the central reason welds behave so poorly under fatigue.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine bending a **paperclip** back and forth. Even though the wire is far stronger than the force needed to bend it once, it eventually snaps at the bend point. That snap is **fatigue failure**.

Now consider a **fillet weld** joining two plates. The "toe" of the weld (where it meets the base plate) is a sharp corner — like the bend in the paperclip. Under a steady (static) load, it might hold forever. But under a load that rises and falls thousands of times (vibrations from a motor, rotating shaft, pressure pulsation, etc.), a tiny crack initiates at the weld toe, propagates slowly, and finally causes sudden, brittle fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Insight for KTU Students:** Welded joints under fluctuating loads are **NOT** designed using static allowable stress alone. They are designed using **fatigue strength** from experimental S-N curves, with appropriate correction for mean stress (Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber criteria).

### Standard Reference Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (for structural welds) |
|---|---|---|
| Stress Concentration Factor at weld toe | $K_t$ | **1.5 – 3.0** (depends on weld profile) |
| Fatigue Notch Sensitivity | $q$ | **0.6 – 0.8** for mild steel welds |
| Fatigue Strength Reduction Factor | $K_f$ | **1.0 – 2.5** |
| AWS Fatigue Category (per AASHTO/AWS D1.1) | C, D, E, E', F | Stress range governed |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stress Parameters in a Fluctuating Load Cycle

For any fluctuating stress cycle acting on a weld (or any machine element), the following relationships apply:

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_r = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a
$$

**Load Ratio (Stress Ratio):**

$$
R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}
$$

**Amplitude Ratio:**

$$
A = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m}
$$

**Physical meaning of $R$:**
- $R = +1$ → fully reversed (reversed)? No. $R = +1$ means $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{max}$, i.e., **static load**.
- $R = -1$ → **fully reversed cycle** (most damaging).
- $R = 0$ → **pulsating cycle** (zero to tension).
- $0 < R < 1$ → fluctuating between two tensile values.

### 2.2 Fatigue Strength of Welded Joints (Why Welds are Different)

For **un-notched, polished specimens** under reversed loading, the fatigue strength is approximately **40–50% of the ultimate tensile strength** for steels. This is the **endurance limit** $\sigma_e$.

However, for **welded joints**, there is **no true endurance limit** because:
1. Weld toes are inherent micro-notches → stress concentration.
2. Residual tensile stresses from cooling of the weld pool are locked in.
3. Metallurgical heterogeneity at the fusion line.

Hence, the **fatigue strength of welds is specified as the allowable stress range** for a given number of cycles (e.g., 2 million cycles) rather than a true endurance limit.

### 2.3 Stress Concentration and Fatigue Notch Factor

The **theoretical stress concentration factor** $K_t$ (from elasticity / FEA / charts) is modified by the **fatigue notch sensitivity** $q$ to give the **fatigue stress concentration factor** $K_f$:

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)
$$

For a typical **fillet weld toe** with a sharp profile:
- $K_t \approx 2.5$ to $3.0$
- $q \approx 0.7$ (mild steel)
- $K_f \approx 1 + 0.7(2.5 - 1) = 2.05$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Many textbook problems (e.g., Shigley, V.B. Bhandari, Khurmi) assume the weld toe is "as-welded" with no machining, and they apply the **full $K_f$** when computing modified endurance limit.

### 2.4 Modified Endurance Limit (Marin Equation for Welds)

The Marin equation breaks the fatigue strength into modification factors:

$$
\sigma_e' = K_a \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot K_d \cdot K_e \cdot \sigma_e
$$

For a welded joint, the dominant factor is the **fatigue stress concentration factor** $K_f$:

$$
\sigma_e' = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f}
$$

When $K_f = 1$ (perfectly smooth, no stress concentration), $\sigma_e' = \sigma_e$.

### 2.5 Mean Stress Correction Criteria

Once $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ are known from the fluctuating load on the weld, one of three classical criteria is used to compare against the **modified endurance limit** $\sigma_e'$ (or yield $S_y$ / ultimate $S_{ut}$ for ductile/safety checks):

#### (a) Soderberg Criterion (Most Conservative — Preferred by KTU Board)

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

where $N$ is the **factor of safety**.

For design with $N \geq 1$:

$$
\sigma_a \leq \frac{\sigma_e'}{N}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)
$$

#### (b) Goodman Criterion (Most Common in Shigley / Bhandari textbooks)

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

For design:

$$
\sigma_a \leq \frac{\sigma_e'}{N}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)
$$

#### (c) Gerber Criterion (Least Conservative, parabolic)

$$
\frac{N \sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \left(\frac{N \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1
$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Weld Fatigue)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | Always algebraic |
| 2 | Alternating stress | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | Always positive |
| 3 | Stress range | $\sigma_r = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | Used in AWS code |
| 4 | Load ratio | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | $-1 \leq R \leq 1$ for typical |
| 5 | Fatigue notch factor | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | $0 \leq q \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Modified endurance limit | $\sigma_e' = \sigma_e / K_f$ | For $\sigma_e < S_y$ |
| 7 | Marin equation (welds) | $\sigma_e' = K_a K_b K_c K_d K_e \sigma_e / K_f$ | All $K \leq 1$ except $K_f \geq 1$ |
| 8 | Soderberg (FoS = N) | $\sigma_a/\sigma_e' + \sigma_m/S_y = 1/N$ | Most conservative |
| 9 | Goodman (FoS = N) | $\sigma_a/\sigma_e' + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/N$ | Most widely used |
| 10 | Gerber (FoS = N) | $N\sigma_a/\sigma_e' + (N\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1$ | Parabolic |
| 11 | Throat stress in fillet weld | $\tau = F / (0.707 h L)$ | Static equivalent |
| 12 | AWS allowable stress range (kips/in²) | $S_{rf} = (C/N)^{1/m}$ | e.g. Category C: $C=44\times10^8$, $m=3$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fatigue analysis of welds is **mission-critical** in:
- **Pressure vessels & piping** (ASME Section VIII Div 2) — pulsating pressure.
- **Bridges and offshore structures** (AASHTO / AWS D1.1) — millions of load cycles from traffic/waves.
- **Crane jibs, excavator booms** — variable lifting loads.
- **Railway axles, bogie frames, ship hulls** — high-cycle fatigue.
- **Wind turbine towers** — $10^8$ cycles over 20-year life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AWS D1.1 Code Insight (often asked in KTU viva):** The American Welding Society classifies welds into **Fatigue Categories A, B, B', C, D, E, E', F** based on stress range vs. cycles to failure. For example, **Category C** (transverse groove weld, ground flush) has $C = 44 \times 10^8$ kips/in², $m = 3$ in $S_r = (C/N_c)^{1/m}$. This is **NOT** in the V.B. Bhandari syllabus but may appear in **KTU M.Tech / elective** or in KTU 2024 PEPET411 advanced modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of the Three Stress Parameters

We start with the definition of a sinusoidal fluctuating stress:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)
$$

At the **peak** of the cycle, $\sin(\omega t) = +1$, so:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a
$$

At the **trough** of the cycle, $\sin(\omega t) = -1$, so:

$$
\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a
$$

**Adding** the two:

$$
\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

**Subtracting** the two:

$$
\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

This is the **standard KTU board derivation** — students should be able to reproduce this from memory.

### 3.2 Derivation: Modified Endurance Limit from $K_f$

The basic endurance limit $\sigma_e$ is obtained from a polished, un-notched specimen. When a **stress raiser** (like a weld toe) is present, the local stress is amplified by $K_f$. The fatigue life is determined by this **local** stress, not the nominal stress.

For a fully reversed nominal stress $\sigma_{rev}$, the local stress is $K_f \cdot \sigma_{rev}$. Equating the local stress to the material's basic endurance limit:

$$
K_f \cdot \sigma_e' = \sigma_e
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_e' = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f}
$$

**Key conceptual step:** A sharper weld toe (higher $K_f$) *reduces* the allowable alternating stress, since $\sigma_e'$ decreases.

### 3.3 Derivation: Goodman Criterion from First Principles

Consider a stress cycle $(\sigma_a, \sigma_m)$ acting on a weld. The failure locus in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane is bounded by:
- At $\sigma_m = 0$: failure at $\sigma_a = \sigma_e'$ (pure reversal).
- At $\sigma_a = 0$: failure at $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$ (pure static tension).

A **straight line** between these two points gives the **Goodman line**:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

**With a factor of safety $N$**, the design point lies on the line:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

**Solving for the allowable alternating stress:**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e'}{N}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)
$$

For $\sigma_m = 0$ (reversed), $\sigma_a = \sigma_e' / N$ (as expected).
For $\sigma_m = S_{ut}/N$ (static), $\sigma_a = 0$ (no reversal allowed).

### 3.4 Derivation: Soderberg Criterion (for Welds where Yield is Critical)

Using $S_y$ instead of $S_{ut}$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

**Solving for allowable $\sigma_a$:**

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e'}{N}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Soderberg is preferred for welds:** Welds are **brittle-prone** because of the heat-affected zone (HAZ). Yielding in the HAZ is often the precursor to fatigue crack initiation. The KTU 2024 syllabus (per V.B. Bhandari) emphasizes Soderberg for ductile/yield-safe design of welds.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Example (Model Question Pattern)

**Problem:** A **fillet weld** is used to attach a bracket carrying a fluctuating load that varies from $F_{min} = +5$ kN (tension) to $F_{max} = +45$ kN (tension) on a weld leg of size $h = 8$ mm and length $L = 100$ mm. The base metal is **mild steel** with $S_{ut} = 400$ MPa, $S_y = 250$ MPa, and $\sigma_e = 180$ MPa. The weld toe has $K_t = 2.5$ and $q = 0.7$. Apply the **Goodman criterion** with a factor of safety $N = 2$. Find the factor of safety in fatigue.

#### Step 1: Compute the throat area and nominal stresses

Throat thickness $t = 0.707 h = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.66$ mm.

Throat area: $A = 0.707 h L = 0.707 \times 8 \times 100 = 565.6$ mm².

> **Conversion logic:** The fillet weld is treated as a shear joint with shear area = throat area.

Maximum nominal stress: $\sigma_{max} = F_{max} / A = 45{,}000 / 565.6 = 79.6$ MPa.

Minimum nominal stress: $\sigma_{min} = F_{min} / A = 5{,}000 / 565.6 = 8.84$ MPa.

#### Step 2: Mean and alternating stresses

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{79.6 + 8.84}{2} = 44.22 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{79.6 - 8.84}{2} = 35.38 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute fatigue notch factor $K_f$

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.7(2.5 - 1) = 1 + 0.7 \times 1.5 = 1 + 1.05 = 2.05
$$

#### Step 4: Modified endurance limit

$$
\sigma_e' = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f} = \frac{180}{2.05} = 87.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Step 5: Apply Goodman criterion to find actual factor of safety

The Goodman equation for actual FoS $N$ is:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{35.38}{87.8} + \frac{44.22}{400} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

$$
0.4029 + 0.1106 = 0.5135 = \frac{1}{N}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1}{0.5135} = 1.948 \approx 1.95
$$

#### Step 6: Verdict

Since the actual fatigue factor of safety $N \approx 1.95$ is **less than the required** $N = 2$, the weld is **NOT safe** under fluctuating load. We must either:
1. Increase the weld leg size $h$.
2. Increase the weld length $L$.
3. Reduce the load.
4. Use a post-weld ground flush to reduce $K_t$.

This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question format** — students must show all six steps explicitly.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Criterion?

| Criterion | When to Use (per KTU 2024 PEPET411 syllabus) | Conservatism |
|---|---|---|
| **Soderberg** | Ductile materials, when yield in the HAZ must be prevented (welds in structural steel) | Most conservative (safe) |
| **Goodman** | When ultimate tensile strength is the relevant failure limit (most common in KTU textbook problems) | Conservative for brittle, moderate for ductile |
| **Gerber** | Ductile materials where some plastic flow is acceptable (e.g., bolted joints, shafts) | Least conservative (less safe) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Weld Fatigue Analysis Procedure

The diagram below maps the **logical decision flow** for solving a weld-under-fluctuating-load problem (KTU board style).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given fluctuating load on weld] --> B[Identify Fmax and Fmin]
    B --> C[Compute throat area A = 0.707 h L]
    C --> D[Compute nominal Smax and Smin from F and A]
    D --> E[Compute mean stress Sm = Smax + Smin divided by 2]
    D --> F[Compute alternating stress Sa = Smax - Smin divided by 2]
    E --> G[Choose mean stress criterion]
    F --> G
    G --> G1{Soderberg?}
    G --> G2{Goodman?}
    G --> G3{Gerber?}
    G1 --> H[Get Kt and q from weld profile]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Compute fatigue notch factor Kf = 1 + q times Kt minus 1]
    I --> J[Compute modified endurance limit See = Se divided by Kf]
    J --> K[Apply chosen criterion to find factor of safety N]
    K --> L{N greater than or equal to Nrequired?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Design is SAFE under fluctuating load]
    L -- No --> N[Design FAILS: Increase h, increase L, or grind flush to reduce Kt]
    M --> O[End]
    N --> O[End]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: AWS Weld Fatigue Categories (High-Yield Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Welded Joint] --> B{Load Direction vs Weld Axis}
    B -- Parallel to weld axis --> C[Category B]
    B -- Transverse to weld axis --> D[Category C]
    C --> E{Grinding and Profile}
    D --> E
    E -- As welded --> F[Category C baseline]
    E -- Ground flush --> G[Category B improved]
    F --> H[Allowable stress range Sr from S-N curve]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Use Sr = C divided by N cycles to power 1 over m]
    I --> J[Compare with actual computed stress range]
    J --> K{Sr greater than computed stress range?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Safe per AWS D1.1]
    K -- No --> M[Increase weld size or reduce cycles]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Fillet Weld Geometry and Stress Concentration Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WeldProfile[Fillet Weld Cross Section]
        P1[Base Plate 1 - horizontal] --> W1[Weld Leg h - vertical at toe]
        P1 --> W2[Weld Leg h - horizontal at root]
        W1 --> P2[Base Plate 2 - vertical]
        W2 --> P2
        W1 -.-> SCF1[Stress Concentration at Weld Toe - Kt 2.5 to 3]
        W2 -.-> SCF2[Stress Concentration at Weld Root - Kt 1.5 to 2]
    end
    SCF1 --> F1[Fatigue crack initiation site]
    SCF2 --> F2[Subsurface crack propagation]
    F1 --> FF[Fatigue Fracture]
    F2 --> FF
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber Failure Envelopes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HaighDiagram[Haigh Diagram - Sigma m vs Sigma a]
        XAxis[Sigma m axis - Mean Stress]
        YAxis[Sigma a axis - Alternating Stress]
        XAxis --> P1[Point 1: Sigma a = 0, Sigma m = Sut - Static tension]
        XAxis --> P2[Point 2: Sigma a = 0, Sigma m = minus Sut - Static compression]
        YAxis --> P3[Point 3: Sigma m = 0, Sigma a = See - Reversed]
        P1 --- P3
        P2 --- P3
        P2 --- G1[Gerber parabola - least conservative]
        P1 --- G1
        P2 --- GD1[Goodman straight line - moderate]
        P1 --- GD1
        P2 --- S1[Soderberg line - most conservative through Sy]
        P1 --- S1
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the terms: (a) Mean stress, (b) Stress amplitude, (c) Stress range, as applied to a welded joint subjected to fluctuating load. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Mean stress ($\sigma_m$)**: It is the average of the maximum and minimum stresses in one complete cycle of fluctuating load.

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

(b) **Stress amplitude / Alternating stress ($\sigma_a$)**: It is half the algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum stresses in a cycle.

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}
$$

(c) **Stress range ($\sigma_r$)**: It is the algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum stresses in one complete cycle.

$$
\sigma_r = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a
$$

The stress range is the quantity used directly in the **AWS D1.1 fatigue design** of welded joints.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
Why are welded joints more susceptible to fatigue failure than riveted or bolted joints? Mention the role of **stress concentration factor (SCF)** at the weld toe. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Welded joints are more susceptible to fatigue failure due to the following reasons:

1. **Geometric discontinuity** at the weld toe and root creates a sharp notch → high theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t = 2$ to $3$.
2. **Metallurgical discontinuity** between weld metal, heat-affected zone (HAZ), and parent metal → heterogeneous microstructure.
3. **Residual tensile stresses** from non-uniform cooling of the weld pool add to the applied mean stress.
4. **Lack of a true endurance limit** — S-N curve continues to drop for welds, unlike polished specimens.

**Role of SCF at weld toe:** The local stress at the weld toe is amplified by $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$. For a typical as-welded toe with $K_t = 2.5$ and $q = 0.7$, the fatigue notch factor is $K_f = 2.05$. This reduces the modified endurance limit from $\sigma_e$ to $\sigma_e' = \sigma_e / K_f$, which roughly halves the allowable alternating stress and is the primary reason welds fail in fatigue at relatively low nominal stresses.

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for Module 2:** Students answer **one full question of 14 marks** with internal choice, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, PEPET411 Module 2]**

A **fillet weld** of leg size $h = 10$ mm and length $L = 150$ mm joins a bracket to a vertical column. The load on the bracket fluctuates from $F_{min} = 8$ kN to $F_{max} = 50$ kN (both tensile). The weld metal and base metal are mild steel with the following properties:

- Ultimate tensile strength: $S_{ut} = 420$ MPa
- Yield strength: $S_y = 260$ MPa
- Endurance limit (polished specimen): $\sigma_e = 200$ MPa
- Fatigue notch factor at weld toe: $K_f = 2.0$ (given)
- Notch sensitivity: $q = 0.75$
- Required factor of safety: $N_{req} = 2.5$

**(a)** Determine the mean and alternating stresses in the weld throat. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Goodman criterion**, determine whether the weld is safe against fluctuating loads. If unsafe, suggest a remedial design modification. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Mean and alternating stresses:**

Throat area: $A = 0.707 h L = 0.707 \times 10 \times 150 = 1060.5$ mm².

Maximum nominal stress: $\sigma_{max} = F_{max}/A = 50{,}000 / 1060.5 = 47.15$ MPa.

Minimum nominal stress: $\sigma_{min} = F_{min}/A = 8{,}000 / 1060.5 = 7.54$ MPa.

Mean stress:

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{47.15 + 7.54}{2} = 27.35 \text{ MPa}
$$

Alternating stress:

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{47.15 - 7.54}{2} = 19.80 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — Goodman criterion check:**

Modified endurance limit:

$$
\sigma_e' = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f} = \frac{200}{2.0} = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

Goodman equation with factor of safety $N$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\frac{19.80}{100} + \frac{27.35}{420} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

$$
0.198 + 0.0651 = 0.2631 = \frac{1}{N}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1}{0.2631} = 3.80
$$

**Verdict:** Since $N_{actual} = 3.80 > N_{req} = 2.5$, the weld is **SAFE** under the given fluctuating load per the Goodman criterion. A small increase in factor of safety is achievable by either increasing the weld length to 180 mm (which would reduce all stresses by 150/180) or grinding the weld toe flush to reduce $K_f$ from 2.0 to 1.4.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, PEPET411 Module 2 Alternate]**

A **transverse groove weld** in a steel plate carries a fully reversed fluctuating tensile load with amplitude $\pm 40$ kN. The plate cross-section is $25$ mm $\times$ $10$ mm. Material properties: $S_{ut} = 450$ MPa, $S_y = 290$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 220$ MPa. The as-welded joint has $K_t = 2.8$ and $q = 0.8$. Apply the **Soderberg criterion** with a required factor of safety of 2.0.

**(a)** Compute the mean and alternating nominal stresses and the modified endurance limit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Soderberg's criterion, find the actual factor of safety against fatigue failure. Comment on the design safety. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Stress computations:**
