Cross-sectional area: $A = 25 \times 10 = 250$ mm².

For a fully reversed load, $\sigma_{max} = +40{,}000/250 = 160$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -40{,}000/250 = -160$ MPa.

Mean stress: $\sigma_m = (160 + (-160))/2 = 0$ MPa.

Alternating stress: $\sigma_a = (160 - (-160))/2 = 160$ MPa.

**Fatigue notch factor:**

$$
K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.8(2.8 - 1) = 1 + 0.8 \times 1.8 = 1 + 1.44 = 2.44
$$

**Modified endurance limit:**

$$
\sigma_e' = \frac{\sigma_e}{K_f} = \frac{220}{2.44} = 90.16 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — Soderberg criterion check:**

Soderberg equation:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e'} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

Substituting ($\sigma_m = 0$):

$$
\frac{160}{90.16} + \frac{0}{290} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

$$
1.7744 = \frac{1}{N}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1}{1.7744} = 0.564
$$

**Verdict:** Since $N_{actual} = 0.564 \ll N_{req} = 2.0$, the weld is **NOT SAFE** under fully reversed load. The design must be modified by:
1. **Grinding the weld flush** to reduce $K_t$ to ~1.5, giving $K_f = 1.4$ and $\sigma_e' = 157$ MPa. Then $N = 157/160 \approx 0.98$ (still inadequate).
2. **Replacing the transverse groove with a parallel-side fillet weld** (lower $K_t \approx 1.5$).
3. **Reducing the applied load** to $F_{max} = 25$ kN to keep $N \geq 2$.

This problem demonstrates why fully reversed loading is the **most critical** case for welded joints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using static throat stress formula for fluctuating load.** Many students compute $\tau = F/(0.707 h L)$ once with $F_{max}$ and check against an allowable stress, ignoring $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$ splitting. **You will lose 5–7 marks** for this in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $K_f$.** If you use $\sigma_e$ directly (180 or 200 MPa) without dividing by $K_f$, your factor of safety will be 2× too optimistic. The board will award **partial credit at best**.
> 3. **Mixing $S_{ut}$ and $S_y$ between Goodman and Soderberg.** Goodman uses $S_{ut}$ in the denominator; Soderberg uses $S_y$. **Swapping them is a 1-mark error** in the formula and will propagate to the wrong answer.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\sigma_{min}$.** A compressive $\sigma_{min}$ (e.g., $-40$ MPa) is negative, not its absolute value. Use **algebraic** addition in $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$.
> 5. **Not stating units** (MPa vs. N/mm²). KTU board deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in a 14-mark answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Welds under fluctuating load** are designed for **fatigue**, not static strength. The dominant failure mode is crack initiation at the **weld toe** due to high $K_t$.
- The **three stress parameters** are: $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$, $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$, $\sigma_r = 2\sigma_a$.
- **Throat area** of a fillet weld = $0.707 h L$ (always, no exception).
- **Fatigue notch factor**: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$. For an as-welded toe, $K_f \approx 2$.
- **Modified endurance limit**: $\sigma_e' = \sigma_e / K_f$. This is the fatigue strength at the notch.
- **Mean stress correction** (use one):
   - **Soderberg**: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e' + \sigma_m/S_y = 1/N$ (most conservative, preferred for welds).
   - **Goodman**: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e' + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/N$ (widely used in textbooks).
   - **Gerber**: $N\sigma_a/\sigma_e' + (N\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 = 1$ (parabolic, least conservative).
- **Always compute actual $N$** and compare with $N_{req}$. If $N_{actual} < N_{req}$, the design fails.
- **Remedies** for failing designs: increase $h$, increase $L$, grind weld toe flush (reduces $K_t$), use parallel-side welds, reduce applied load.
- **AWS D1.1 (advanced)**: welds are categorized A through F; allowable stress range is $S_r = (C/N_c)^{1/m}$ (e.g., Category C: $C=44\times 10^8$, $m=3$ in ksi units).
- **Key real-world applications**: pressure vessels, bridges, crane jibs, ship hulls, wind towers, rail bogies.
- **In a KTU exam, always show units** (MPa for stress, mm for length, mm² for area) and **state which criterion** you are using.
- **Memorize the formulas** in the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.6) — they appear verbatim in nearly every ESE question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Classification" in Machine Design?

In the context of Machine Design (PEPET411), **Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of **Fits and Tolerances** between mating parts. A *fit* is the degree of looseness or tightness between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) of the same nominal size. The classification system is governed by the international standard **ISO 286-1:2010**, which provides a unified framework for interchangeable manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *fit* is the **relationship** that exists between two mating parts (hole and shaft) of the same nominal size, characterized by the **difference between their dimensions** before assembly. Classification of fits is the systematic arrangement of these relationships into standardized categories for design, manufacturing, and inspection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **ring and a finger**. 

*   If the ring is **larger** than your finger → it slides off easily (this is a **Clearance Fit** — the shaft moves freely inside the hole, just like a drain plug in a bathtub).
*   If the ring is **smaller** than your finger → you cannot get it on at all (this is an **Interference Fit** — you need force or heat to assemble, like pressing a bearing onto a shaft).
*   If the ring is **just right** → you can push it on with effort, and it stays (this is a **Transition Fit** — could go either way, like a precision gear on a shaft).

In machine design, we need to mathematically define this "just right-ness" using three pillars: **Tolerances** (the permissible variation in size), **Allowances** (the minimum intentional gap or interference), and **Fundamental Deviations** (the position of the tolerance zone relative to the nominal size).

### 1.3 Why Do We Classify Fits?

Without a classification system, every workshop would interpret "make a tight fit" differently, leading to non-interchangeable parts. Classification ensures:
*   **Interchangeability** — any part from any factory can be assembled.
*   **Functional selection** — engineers can quickly pick the right fit for the job (sliding, rotating, stationary).
*   **Cost optimization** — tighter fits cost more; classification helps avoid over-specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must master three categories of fits, the **Hole-Basis vs. Shaft-Basis** systems, and the calculation of **maximum/minimum clearance or interference** for a given designation (e.g., H7/g6).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Building Blocks

Before classifying fits, every student must internalize these three definitions:

1.  **Nominal Size (Basic Size, D):** The size of the part as identified by the designer (e.g., 50 mm). It is the same for both hole and shaft.
2.  **Actual Size:** The measured size of the manufactured part.
3.  **Limits of Size:** The two extreme permissible sizes (maximum and minimum) between which the actual size must lie.

From these limits, we derive:
*   **Upper Deviation:** The algebraic difference between the *maximum* size and the nominal size.
*   **Lower Deviation:** The algebraic difference between the *minimum* size and the nominal size.
*   **Tolerance:** The algebraic difference between the *maximum* and *minimum* sizes. It is **always positive**.
*   **Allowance:** The *intentional* (minimum) difference between the hole and shaft dimensions designed into the fit.

### 2.2 Classification of Fits (The Heart of the Topic)

The ISO system classifies fits into three primary categories:

#### A. Clearance Fit
*   The hole is **always larger** than the shaft.
*   Clearance is **always positive** (from minimum to maximum).
*   Parts can always be assembled by hand or with light force.
*   **Examples:** H11/a11, H9/d9, H8/f7, H7/h6.
*   **Use Cases:** Sliding gears, bearing inner races, hinges, pulley hubs.

#### B. Interference Fit (Force Fit)
*   The shaft is **always larger** than the hole.
*   Interference (negative clearance) is **always negative**.
*   Assembly requires **force, heating the outer part, or cooling the inner part**.
*   **Examples:** H7/s6, H7/p6, H8/u8.
*   **Use Cases:** Permanent couplings, railway wheel hubs, bushings in castings.

#### C. Transition Fit
*   The hole may be slightly larger **or** smaller than the shaft.
*   Clearance and interference are both possible, depending on the actual manufactured dimensions.
*   Assembly requires **light hammer blows or hand pressure**.
*   **Examples:** H7/k6, H7/js6, H7/m6.
*   **Use Cases:** Gear and pulley mountings requiring accurate centring.

### 2.3 Hole-Basis vs. Shaft-Basis Systems

This is a **guaranteed 3- to 7-mark question** in KTU exams.

*   **Hole-Basis System:** The **hole** is kept constant (H) as the reference, and the shaft tolerance is varied to obtain different fits. The lower deviation of the hole is **zero** (EI = 0 for H).
    *   **Advantages:** Preferred in industry because drills and reamers are standardized to a single hole size; reducing the number of tools.
*   **Shaft-Basis System:** The **shaft** is kept constant (h) as the reference, and the hole tolerance is varied. The upper deviation of the shaft is **zero** (es = 0 for h).
    *   **Advantages:** Used when a long shaft has multiple mating parts; only one shaft diameter is machined.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table as your **first-page reference** during numerical problems. Note: All values are in **mm** unless stated.

| Symbol / Term | Description | Governing Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_h$ | Hole Tolerance | $T_h = ES - EI$ |
| $T_s$ | Shaft Tolerance | $T_s = es - ei$ |
| $H_{max}$ | Maximum Hole Size | $D + ES$ |
| $H_{min}$ | Minimum Hole Size | $D + EI$ |
| $S_{max}$ | Maximum Shaft Size | $D + es$ |
| $S_{min}$ | Minimum Shaft Size | $D + ei$ |
| $C_{max}$ | Maximum Clearance | $H_{max} - S_{min}$ |
| $C_{min}$ | Minimum Clearance | $H_{min} - S_{max}$ |
| $I_{max}$ | Maximum Interference | $S_{max} - H_{min}$ |
| $I_{min}$ | Minimum Interference | $S_{min} - H_{max}$ |
| Allowance | Min. Intended Gap / Press | $\vert H_{min} - S_{max} \vert$ |
| IT Grade | Standard Tolerance Grade | $i \cdot$ (factor from ISO 286) |

**Real-World Utility:** This classification system is used in the design of every mass-produced mechanical assembly — from the **crankshaft journals in your car engine** (clearance fit for oil film) to the **cylinder liner in a marine engine** (interference fit for heat transfer).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Relationships of a Clearance Fit

A clearance fit is defined as one where the **minimum clearance is always $\geq 0$**. Let us derive the working equations.

**Given:**
*   Hole of size $D$ with deviations $EI$ (lower) and $ES$ (upper).
*   Shaft of size $D$ with deviations $ei$ (lower) and $es$ (upper).
*   The clearance at any instant is $C = H - S$ (Hole minus Shaft).

**Step 1: Maximum Clearance occurs when...**
The hole is at its **largest** and the shaft is at its **smallest**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} \\
C_{max} &= (D + ES) - (D + ei) \\
C_{max} &= ES - ei
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Logic:* The "biggest gap" is created by the "biggest hole" minus the "smallest shaft."

**Step 2: Minimum Clearance occurs when...**
The hole is at its **smallest** and the shaft is at its **largest**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} \\
C_{min} &= (D + EI) - (D + es) \\
C_{min} &= EI - es
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Logic:* The "smallest gap" is created by the "smallest hole" minus the "biggest shaft."

**Step 3: Allowance (Minimum Intentional Clearance)**
For a clearance fit, allowance $= C_{min}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowance} &= EI - es
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Logic:* Allowance is the *designed* smallest gap, assuming worst-case tolerances. For an H/h fit, allowance $= 0$ (running fit).

### 3.2 Derivation: Interference Fit (Mathematical Mirror)

For an interference fit, the shaft is always larger. We take Interference $I = S - H$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max} &= S_{max} - H_{min} = es - EI \\
I_{min} &= S_{min} - H_{max} = ei - ES
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Logic:* This is the **exact mirror image** of the clearance formula. The "tightest squeeze" is the largest shaft minus the smallest hole.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IT Grade Values (ISO 286-1:2010)

The International Tolerance (IT) grade is the size of the tolerance zone. For KTU problems, you are expected to use the following standard values:

| Nominal Size Range (mm) | IT6 ($\mu m$) | IT7 ($\mu m$) | IT8 ($\mu m$) | IT9 ($\mu m$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $18 - 30$ | 13 | 21 | 33 | 52 |
| $30 - 50$ | 16 | 25 | 39 | 62 |
| $50 - 80$ | 19 | 30 | 46 | 74 |
| $80 - 120$ | 22 | 35 | 54 | 87 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip:** The tolerance value $i$ (the tolerance unit) in $\mu m$ is roughly:
> $$i \approx 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$$
> where $D$ is the geometric mean of the size range in mm. IT$k$ = $k \cdot i$ approximately for grades 5-8.

### 3.4 Numerical Demonstration: H7/h6 Fit on 50 mm Shaft

**Problem:** For a nominal size of 50 mm with a H7/h6 fit, determine all limits and clearances.

**Step 1: Identify IT grade tolerances.**
From the matrix: 50 mm falls in the $30-50$ range.
*   IT7 = $25\ \mu m = 0.025\ mm$
*   IT6 = $16\ \mu m = 0.016\ mm$

**Step 2: Calculate Hole (H7) Limits.**
For H (hole basis), lower deviation $EI = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{min} &= D + EI = 50 + 0 = 50.000\ mm \\
H_{max} &= D + ES = D + (EI + T_h) = 50 + (0 + 0.025) = 50.025\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Shaft (h6) Limits.**
For h (shaft), upper deviation $es = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= D + es = 50 + 0 = 50.000\ mm \\
S_{min} &= D + ei = D + (es - T_s) = 50 + (0 - 0.016) = 49.984\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Clearances.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 50.025 - 49.984 = 0.041\ mm \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 50.000 - 50.000 = 0.000\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The H7/h6 fit is a **sliding clearance fit** with zero allowance — the shaft can just barely slide into the hole. This is a classic "running fit" used for centring collars and slow-speed spindles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Classification of Fits (Mermaid Tree)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete ISO classification hierarchy, including typical designations and applications for KTU exam reference.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[FITS CLASSIFICATION] --> nodeB[Clearance Fit]
    rootA --> nodeC[Interference Fit]
    rootA --> nodeD[Transition Fit]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Hole ALWAYS greater than Shaft]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Clearance is ALWAYS positive]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Example designations]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB3a[H11 slash a11 Loose]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB3b[H8 slash f7 Easy Sliding]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB3c[H7 slash h6 Sliding Running]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Use in Bearings and Hinges]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Shaft ALWAYS greater than Hole]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Interference is ALWAYS negative]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Example designations]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3a[H7 slash p6 Light Press]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3b[H7 slash s6 Medium Press]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3c[H8 slash u8 Heavy Force]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Use in Couplings and Wheel Hubs]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Clearance AND Interference both possible]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Result depends on actual size]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Example designations]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD3a[H7 slash js6 Pure Transition]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD3b[H7 slash k6 Light Transition]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD3c[H7 slash m6 Heavy Transition]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Use in Gears and Pulley Mounts]
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Block Architecture of the Hole-Basis vs. Shaft-Basis System

This block diagram contrasts the two reference systems for fit selection, highlighting the invariant reference dimension in each.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sysTop[FIT REFERENCE SYSTEMS] --> sysH[Hole Basis System]
    sysTop --> sysS[Shaft Basis System]

    sysH --> sysH1[Reference Hole H is CONSTANT]
    sysH1 --> sysH1a[Lower Deviation EI = 0]
    sysH1 --> sysH1b[Hole Tolerance Zone fixed]

    sysH --> sysH2[SHAFT tolerance is VARIED]
    sysH2 --> sysH2a[Shaft a to zc to create fits]
    sysH2 --> sysH2b[Different shaft tools needed]

    sysH --> sysH3[Industry Preferred for Drilled Holes]

    sysS --> sysS1[Reference Shaft h is CONSTANT]
    sysS1 --> sysS1a[Upper Deviation es = 0]
    sysS1 --> sysS1b[Shaft Tolerance Zone fixed]

    sysS --> sysS2[HOLE tolerance is VARIED]
    sysS2 --> sysS2a[Hole A to ZC to create fits]
    sysS2 --> sysS2b[Different reamers needed]

    sysS --> sysS3[Used for Long Shafts with Multiple Mates]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use the alphanumeric prefix convention (rootA, nodeB, etc.) and labels are kept as clean uppercase text to ensure zero syntax errors when pasted into Mermaid Live Editor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following terms: (a) Tolerance, (b) Allowance, (c) Fundamental Deviation.**

**Model Answer:**
*   **(a) Tolerance:** It is the algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum permissible sizes of a part. Tolerance $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$. It represents the permissible variation in manufacturing.
*   **(b) Allowance:** It is the intentional, minimum difference in dimensions of two mating parts (hole and shaft) before assembly. For a clearance fit, allowance $= C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$.
*   **(c) Fundamental Deviation:** It is the deviation (upper or lower) of the tolerance zone closest to the basic size. It defines the position of the tolerance zone relative to the nominal line. For example, for the H-h pair, the fundamental deviation of H (EI = 0) and h (es = 0) both touch the basic size.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Hole-Basis and Shaft-Basis systems of fits.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hole-Basis System | Shaft-Basis System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference Part** | The **hole (H)** is fixed. | The **shaft (h)** is fixed. |
| **Invariant Deviation** | Lower deviation of hole: $EI = 0$. | Upper deviation of shaft: $es = 0$. |
| **Variable Part** | The **shaft tolerance** is shifted to obtain various fits. | The **hole tolerance** is shifted to obtain various fits. |
| **Tooling Implication** | Requires **different shafts** (broaches, reamers remain the same). | Requires **different holes** (re-tools needed for each fit). |
| **Industrial Preference** | **Most preferred** worldwide, as standard drills/reamers produce the H hole. | Used only for long shafts mating with multiple components. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Model)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> *   **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Forgetting to convert micrometers ($\mu m$) to millimeters ($mm$). $1\ \mu m = 0.001\ mm$. **Always check units first.**
> *   **Mark Loss Zone 2:** Confusing $es$ with $ei$ for shafts from **a to h** (where $es$ is the fundamental deviation) versus shafts from **j to zc** (where $ei$ is the fundamental deviation). A sign error here costs **3-4 marks**.
> *   **Mark Loss Zone 3:** Failing to draw the **tolerance zone sketch** in descriptive answers. A well-labeled diagram is worth at least **2 marks**.
> *   **Mark Loss Zone 4:** For transition fits, calculating both $C_{min}$ (which may be negative) AND $I_{max}$ — examiners want to see you identified it is a **transition** fit, not just one or the other.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **three types of fits** (clearance, interference, transition) as per the ISO classification. State one industrial application for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hole and shaft assembly has a nominal diameter of **80 mm**. The hole is designated as **H8** and the shaft as **f7**. Determine:
    (i) The tolerance for the hole and shaft.
    (ii) The maximum and minimum clearance.
    (iii) The allowance of the fit.
    *(Given: For 80 mm size, IT7 = 30 $\mu m$, IT8 = 46 $\mu m$. For shaft 'f', the fundamental deviation es = -30 $\mu m$.)* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**
*   Basic size $D = 80\ mm$.
*   Hole: H8 → $T_h = IT8 = 46\ \mu m = 0.046\ mm$, $EI = 0$.
*   Shaft: f7 → $T_s = IT7 = 30\ \mu m = 0.030\ mm$, $es = -30\ \mu m = -0.030\ mm$.

**Step 2: Calculate Hole (H8) Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{min} &= D + EI = 80 + 0 = 80.000\ mm \\
H_{max} &= D + ES = 80 + 0.046 = 80.046\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Shaft (f7) Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= D + es = 80 - 0.030 = 79.970\ mm \\
S_{min} &= D + ei = 80 + (es - T_s) = 80 + (-0.030 - 0.030) = 79.940\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Clearance and Allowance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 80.046 - 79.940 = 0.106\ mm \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 80.000 - 79.970 = 0.030\ mm \\
\text{Allowance} &= C_{min} = 0.030\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** It is a **clearance fit** with a running allowance of 30 $\mu m$ — ideal for loose-running pulleys or bearing housings.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Hole-Basis** and **Shaft-Basis** systems. State **two advantages and one disadvantage** of the Hole-Basis system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of nominal diameter **50 mm** is designated as **h6** and the mating hole is **H7**. Determine:
    (i) The maximum and minimum size of the hole and shaft.
    (ii) The maximum and minimum clearance.
    (iii) State the type of fit with justification.
    *(Given: For 50 mm size, IT6 = 16 $\mu m$, IT7 = 25 $\mu m$.)* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**
*   $D = 50\ mm$, Hole H7, Shaft h6.
*   $T_h = IT7 = 0.025\ mm$, $EI = 0$.
*   $T_s = IT6 = 0.016\ mm$, $es = 0$.

**Step 2: Calculate Hole (H7) Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{min} &= 50 + 0 = 50.000\ mm \\
H_{max} &= 50 + 0.025 = 50.025\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Shaft (h6) Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= 50 + 0 = 50.000\ mm \\
S_{min} &= 50 - 0.016 = 49.984\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Clearances**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 50.025 - 49.984 = 0.041\ mm \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 50.000 - 50.000 = 0.000\ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Classify the Fit**
Since $C_{min} = 0$ and $C_{max} > 0$, the fit lies exactly on the boundary of a clearance and transition fit. Per ISO 286, H7/h6 is classified as a **Sliding Clearance Fit** (zero allowance running fit), used for slow-speed sliding members and centring collars.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

*   **Three Fit Types:** Clearance ($C_{min} \geq 0$), Interference ($I_{max} \leq 0$ in algebraic sign), Transition (sign of clearance can flip).
*   **Hole is the reference in 95% of designs:** Remember $EI = 0$ for H — this is the foundation of hole-basis fits.
*   **Sign Convention is King:** Upper deviation ($es$, $ES$) is always algebraically **greater than** lower deviation ($ei$, $EI$). For shafts a–h, $es$ is the fundamental deviation. For shafts j–zc, $ei$ is the fundamental deviation.
*   **ISO 286 Standard:** Standard IT grades (IT01 to IT18) define tolerance magnitude. For KTU problems, memorize IT6, IT7, IT8 values for the 30–50 and 50–80 mm ranges.
*   **The "Zero" Allowance Fits:** H/h fits have **zero allowance** — they are the tightest clearance fits, often called "sliding fits."
*   **Statistical Tip:** When you see "H7/g6" in a problem, you do **not** need to memorize $g$'s value — it is usually given in the problem data.
*   **Always draw the tolerance zone diagram** in descriptive answers: a 3-line sketch showing nominal line, hole zone, and shaft zone saves you from losing descriptive marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Spring Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Spring Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **spring material** is a specially engineered metallic or non-metallic substance selected for its ability to undergo large elastic deformations under applied load and to recover its original shape upon unloading, without undergoing plastic deformation or fatigue failure. In the context of KTU Machine Design (PEPET411), spring materials are classified based on their **shear modulus (G)**, **tensile strength (Sut)**, **allowable shear stress (τ_allow)**, **endurance limit under cyclic torsion**, **creep resistance**, and **corrosion resistance**.

According to **Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design** and the **IS 7906 / ASTM standards** adopted under the KTU 2024 scheme, common spring materials are wires, rods, and flat strips used in helical compression, helical extension, helical torsion, and leaf springs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Selection of spring material depends on service conditions: **Static vs. Cyclic Loading**, **Operating Temperature**, **Corrosive Environment**, **Cost**, and **Required Fatigue Life (N cycles)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Trampoline Effect"

Imagine a **trampoline fabric stretched across a metal frame**. When you jump on it, the fabric stretches (deformation) and bounces back to its original shape (recovery) repeatedly for years. Now imagine if the fabric were made of cheap plastic — it would sag permanently after a few jumps (plastic deformation) and eventually tear (fatigue failure).

Spring materials work the same way:
- **Music wire** = like premium trampoline fabric — high strength, long life, expensive.
- **Hard-drawn carbon steel** = like mid-grade fabric — adequate for moderate use, cheaper.
- **Phosphor bronze** = like a fabric coated for outdoor weather — corrosion-resistant but slightly weaker.

The "memory" of a material to return to its original shape is governed by its **Elastic Limit** and **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Material Properties Used in KTU Spring Design Calculations:**
>
> - **Shear Modulus (G)** for steel ≈ **77.2 GPa (11.2 × 10⁶ psi)**
> - **Shear Modulus (G)** for stainless steel ≈ **69 GPa (10 × 10⁶ psi)**
> - **Shear Modulus (G)** for phosphor bronze ≈ **41.4 GPa (6 × 10⁶ psi)**
> - **Shear Modulus (G)** for beryllium copper ≈ **48.3 GPa (7 × 10⁶ psi)**
> - **Density of steel** ≈ **7850 kg/m³**
> - **Wahl's Factor (Ks)** accounts for direct shear stress correction in helical springs

---

## 1.4 Why Material Selection is Critical

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Material Property Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Sagging** (loss of load capacity) | Low elastic limit | High **Elastic Limit** |
| **Fatigue fracture** | Repeated cycling | High **Endurance Limit (Se)** |
| **Stress relaxation** | High temperature operation | High **Creep Resistance** |
| **Pitting/Rust** | Moist environment | High **Corrosion Resistance** |
| **Surge failure** | Sudden loading at natural frequency | Adequate **Damping Capacity** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Common Spring Materials

Spring materials fall into four broad categories:

### A. Carbon & Alloy Steels (Most Common — ~80% of industrial springs)

1. **Hard-Drawn Carbon Steel Wire (ASTM A227 / IS 7906 Grade-1)**
   - Cheapest general-purpose spring wire.
   - Tensile Strength: **Sut = 1030–2380 MPa** depending on wire diameter.
   - Used for static or low-cycle springs (e.g., mattresses, screen springs).

2. **Oil-Tempered Carbon Steel Wire (ASTM A229)**
   - Heat-treated via austempering for uniform properties.
   - Tensile Strength: **Sut = 1255–1830 MPa**.
   - Used for helical springs subjected to moderate cyclic loading.

3. **Music Wire (ASTM A228 / EN 10270-1)**
   - Highest tensile strength among carbon steels.
   - Tensile Strength: **Sut = 1800–4000 MPa** (highest for small diameters).
   - Used for highly loaded cyclic springs — automotive clutches, valve springs.
   - Cannot be used above **~120 °C** (loses hardness due to tempering).

4. **Chrome-Vanadium Steel (ASTM A231, A232)**
   - Alloy steel with Cr and V for high-temperature performance.
   - Used up to **220 °C**.
   - Excellent for **automotive valve springs** and **aircraft springs**.

5. **Silicon-Manganese Steel (ASTM A401)**
   - Used for **helical torsion bars** and large helical springs.
   - Good shock-load capacity.

### B. Stainless Steels (Corrosion-Resistant)

- **ASTM A313 (Austenitic SS 302, 304, 316)** — used for food, medical, marine springs.
- Tensile Strength: **Sut = 1100–2400 MPa** (varies by grade and cold work).
- Operating temperature up to **260 °C**.

### C. Non-Ferrous Alloys (Special Applications)

1. **Phosphor Bronze (UNS C51000, C52100)** — excellent electrical conductivity + corrosion resistance; used in **electrical switch contacts**.
2. **Beryllium Copper (UNS C17200)** — high strength + non-magnetic; used in **aerospace, precision instruments**.
3. **Silicon Bronze (UNS C65500)** — good for marine applications.
4. **Monel (Nickel-Copper)** — high corrosion resistance in chemical/sea-water environments.

### D. Composite & Special Materials

- **GFRP / CFRP (Fiber-Reinforced Polymers)** — used in lightweight automotive springs.
- **Elastomers (rubber)** — used in torsion/suspension bushings (not classical wire springs).

---

## 2.2 Why Material Properties Matter — The Engineering Logic

When designing a helical compression spring, the **spring index (C = D/d)** and **wire diameter (d)** determine the **Wahl's Factor (Ks)**:

$$K_s = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

The **allowable shear stress** for a given material is:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{S_{sy}}{n \cdot K_s}$$

where:
- **S_sy** = Shear Yield Strength (≈ 0.577 × Sut for ductile steels)
- **n** = Factor of safety
- **K_s** = Wahl's correction factor

The **spring rate (k)** is governed by **Shear Modulus G**:

$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N_a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If we change the material from steel (G = 77.2 GPa) to phosphor bronze (G = 41.4 GPa), the spring rate **drops by ~46%** for the same geometry. This is why material selection directly affects spring stiffness.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Spring Materials & Design)

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wahl's Factor** | $K_s = \dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ | Dimensionless; C = D/d |
| 2 | **Spring Index** | $C = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | Recommended: **4 ≤ C ≤ 12** |
| 3 | **Shear Yield Strength (Steels)** | $S_{sy} \approx 0.577 \, S_{ut}$ | Valid for Sut ≤ 2070 MPa |
| 4 | **Shear Endurance Limit** | $S_{se} \approx 0.22 \, S_{ut}$ to $0.30 \, S_{ut}$ | For hard-drawn & oil-tempered wires |
| 5 | **Spring Rate (k)** | $k = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N_a}$ | N/m |
| 6 | **Critical Frequency Check** | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz; avoid resonance |
| 7 | **Bergsträsser Factor (Surging)** | $K_B = \dfrac{4C+2}{4C-3}$ | Used for active coil correction |
| 8 | **Tensile Strength vs. Diameter (Music Wire ASTM A228)** | $S_{ut} = \dfrac{A}{d^m}$ | A = 2211 MPa·mmᵐ, m = 0.145 |
| 9 | **Tensile Strength (Hard-drawn ASTM A227)** | $S_{ut} = \dfrac{A}{d^m}$ | A = 1783 MPa·mmᵐ, m = 0.190 |
| 10 | **Tensile Strength (Oil-tempered A229)** | $S_{ut} = \dfrac{A}{d^m}$ | A = 1857 MPa·mmᵐ, m = 0.087 |

> [!NOTE]
> The empirical relation **Sut = A / dᵐ** (from ASTM standards) is a **highly tested concept** in KTU Module 3. The constants **A** and **m** are unique to each material and must be remembered.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Material | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive suspension** | Chrome-vanadium, Si-Mn steel | High fatigue life, shock loading |
| **Valve springs (IC engines)** | Music wire, Cr-V steel | High cycle life (10⁸+ cycles) |
| **Aerospace** | Beryllium copper, titanium alloys | High strength-to-weight, non-magnetic |
| **Medical devices** | Stainless steel 316L | Biocompatibility, corrosion resistance |
| **Marine hardware** | Monel, Phosphor bronze | Salt-water corrosion resistance |
| **Electrical switches** | Phosphor bronze, Be-Cu | Electrical conductivity + spring action |
| **Furniture / Mattress** | Hard-drawn carbon steel | Low cost, adequate performance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wahl's Factor for Helical Springs

### Step 1 — Identify the Stress Components

A helical spring under axial load **W** develops:
- **Direct shear stress** (τ_d) due to the load itself.
- **Torsional shear stress** (τ_t) due to the moment **M = W × R**, where **R = D/2**.

### Step 2 — Direct Shear Stress

$$\tau_d = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{W}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

$$\tau_d = \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

### Step 3 — Torsional Shear Stress

$$\tau_t = \frac{M \cdot c}{J} = \frac{(W \cdot R) \cdot (d/2)}{\frac{\pi d^4}{32}}$$

$$\tau_t = \frac{16 W R}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}$$

### Step 4 — Total Maximum Shear Stress (Combined by Superposition)

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_t + \tau_d = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

Factor out the common term:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}\left[1 + \frac{d}{2D}\right]$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Spring Index C = D / d

Let **d/(2D) = 1/(2C)**:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}\left[1 + \frac{1}{2C}\right]$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}\left[\frac{2C + 1}{2C}\right]$$

### Step 6 — Compare with Pure Torsion Formula

The basic torsion-only stress is:

$$\tau_{torsion} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}$$

The correction factor (Wahl's Factor) is:

$$K_s = \frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{torsion}} = \frac{2C + 1}{2C} = \frac{4C + 2}{4C}$$

Adding **curvature correction** (because wire curvature changes stress distribution):

$$K_s = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is Wahl's Factor — the most critical formula in KTU Module 3 spring design.**

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Material Selection for a Valve Spring

**Given:**
- Axial operating load: **W_op = 400 N**
- Maximum load: **W_max = 600 N**
- Spring index: **C = 6**
- Wire diameter: **d = 5 mm**
- Factor of safety: **n = 1.5**
- Material: **Music wire (ASTM A228)**

**Find:** Suitability and the allowable stress.

### Step A — Calculate Wahl's Factor

$$K_s = \frac{4(6) - 1}{4(6) - 4} + \frac{0.615}{6}$$

$$K_s = \frac{23}{20} + \frac{0.615}{6}$$

$$K_s = 1.15 + 0.1025$$

$$K_s = 1.2525$$

### Step B — Maximum Shear Stress in the Spring

$$\tau_{max} = K_s \cdot \frac{8 W_{max} D}{\pi d^3}$$

Mean coil diameter **D = C × d = 6 × 5 = 30 mm = 0.030 m**:

$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \cdot \frac{8 \times 600 \times 0.030}{\pi \times (0.005)^3}$$

Compute numerator:

$$8 \times 600 \times 0.030 = 144 \text{ N·m}$$

Compute denominator:

$$\pi \times (0.005)^3 = \pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 3.927 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \times \frac{144}{3.927 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \times 3.666 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\tau_{max} \approx 4.59 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 459 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step C — Allowable Shear Stress from Material Properties

For music wire at **d = 5 mm**, use ASTM A228:

$$S_{ut} = \frac{2211}{(5)^{0.145}}$$

$$(5)^{0.145} = e^{0.145 \ln 5} = e^{0.145 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.2334} \approx 1.2630$$

$$S_{ut} = \frac{2211}{1.2630} \approx 1750 \text{ MPa}$$

Shear Yield Strength:

$$S_{sy} \approx 0.577 \times 1750 = 1010 \text{ MPa}$$

Allowable shear stress:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{S_{sy}}{n \cdot K_s} = \frac{1010}{1.5 \times 1.2525} = \frac{1010}{1.879}$$

$$\tau_{allow} \approx 537 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step D — Verification

Since **τ_max = 459 MPa < τ_allow = 537 MPa** ✅

The music wire spring is **SAFE** with a margin of ~14.5%.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Comparison Table (For Quick Exam Reference)

| Material | ASTM | Sut Range (MPa) | G (GPa) | Max Temp (°C) | Relative Cost | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Music Wire** | A228 | 1800 – 4000 | 77.2 | 120 | High | High-cycle valve springs |
| **Hard-Drawn Carbon** | A227 | 1030 – 2380 | 77.2 | 120 | Low | Static / low-cycle |
| **Oil-Tempered Carbon** | A229 | 1255 – 1830 | 77.2 | 120 | Medium | Helical springs, general |
| **Chrome-Vanadium** | A231 | 1550 – 2070 | 77.2 | 220 | High | Automotive, aircraft |
| **Silicon-Manganese** | A401 | 1550 – 2070 | 77.2 | 220 | Medium | Torsion bars, large springs |
| **Stainless 302/304** | A313 | 1100 – 2400 | 69.0 | 260 | High | Corrosive / medical |
| **Phosphor Bronze** | B159 | 410 – 1400 | 41.4 | 95 | Medium | Electrical contacts |
| **Beryllium Copper** | B197 | 1100 – 1500 | 48.3 | 200 | Very High | Aerospace, instruments |
| **Monel** | — | 690 – 1100 | 66.0 | 230 | Very High | Marine / chemical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** List any four commonly used spring materials and state one specific application of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (4 × 1 = 4 marks, 3-mark question with one extra point):**

1. **Music Wire (ASTM A228)** — Used in internal combustion engine valve springs due to its very high tensile strength and excellent fatigue resistance.
2. **Hard-Drawn Carbon Steel (ASTM A227)** — Used in mattresses, screen springs, and general-purpose static springs due to its low cost.
3. **Stainless Steel 302 (ASTM A313)** — Used in medical devices, food-processing equipment, and marine springs due to corrosion resistance.
4. **Phosphor Bronze** — Used in electrical switch contacts and relays due to good electrical conductivity combined with spring action.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only the *name* and forget the *application*. Always pair **material + specific use** to score full marks.

---

### **Q2.** What is the significance of the Wahl factor in spring design? Why is it needed? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Wahl factor (K_s)** is a stress-correction factor used in helical spring design to account for two effects:
1. **Combined direct shear stress and torsional shear stress** acting simultaneously on the wire cross-section due to the axial load.
2. **Curvature of the wire**, which causes non-uniform stress distribution across the wire cross-section.

$$K_s = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

It is **needed** because if we ignore these effects, the calculated shear stress will be **underestimated**, leading to unsafe designs. The Wahl factor multiplies the simple torsional stress to give the **actual maximum shear stress** in the wire, ensuring safe design under the **allowable shear stress limit**.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the desirable properties of spring materials. Discuss in detail the factors affecting the selection of spring materials for engineering applications.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A helical compression spring is made of **oil-tempered carbon steel wire (ASTM A229)** with wire diameter **d = 6 mm**, mean coil diameter **D = 36 mm**, and total number of coils **Nt = 10**. If the spring is subjected to a **maximum axial load of 500 N**, determine:
- (i) Wahl's factor
- (ii) Maximum shear stress in the wire
- (iii) Whether the spring is safe if the allowable shear stress is **480 MPa**.

---

### ✅ **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Desirable Properties of Spring Materials:**

1. **High Elastic Limit / Yield Strength** — to withstand high stress without permanent deformation.
2. **High Fatigue Strength (Endurance Limit)** — for springs subjected to cyclic loading (e.g., valve springs).
3. **High Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — to provide adequate stiffness and predictable spring rate.
4. **Creep Resistance** — to maintain load capacity at elevated temperatures.
5. **Corrosion Resistance** — for springs used in marine, chemical, or humid environments.
6. **Good Ductility** — to allow cold coiling without cracking.
7. **Low Cost & Availability** — for mass production (e.g., automotive, furniture).

**Factors Affecting Spring Material Selection:**

1. **Type of Loading** — Static (hard-drawn) vs. cyclic (music wire).
2. **Operating Temperature** — Music wire fails above 120 °C → use Cr-V or stainless steel.
3. **Corrosive Environment** — Marine applications → stainless steel or Monel.
4. **Required Fatigue Life** — 10⁶ cycles vs. 10⁸ cycles.
5. **Cost Constraints** — Budget vs. performance.
6. **Spring Index (C = D/d)** — Affects manufacturing feasibility.
7. **Weight / Size Constraints** — Aerospace → Be-Cu, Ti alloys.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Wire diameter, **d = 6 mm**
- Mean coil diameter, **D = 36 mm**
- Total coils, **Nt = 10**
- Maximum axial load, **W_max = 500 N**
- Allowable shear stress, **τ_allow = 480 MPa**
- Material: **Oil-Tempered Carbon Steel (ASTM A229)**

**Step 1 — Spring Index**

$$C = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{36}{6} = 6$$

**Step 2 — Wahl's Factor**

$$K_s = \frac{4(6) - 1}{4(6) - 4} + \frac{0.615}{6}$$

$$K_s = \frac{23}{20} + \frac{0.615}{6}$$

$$K_s = 1.15 + 0.1025 = 1.2525$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{max} = K_s \cdot \frac{8 W_{max} D}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \times \frac{8 \times 500 \times 0.036}{\pi \times (0.006)^3}$$

Numerator:

$$8 \times 500 \times 0.036 = 144 \text{ N·m}$$

Denominator:

$$\pi \times (0.006)^3 = \pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7} = 6.786 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \times \frac{144}{6.786 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \times 2.122 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\tau_{max} \approx 2.658 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 265.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Safety Verification**

Since **τ_max = 265.8 MPa < τ_allow = 480 MPa** ✅

The spring is **SAFE**, with significant margin (FoS ≈ 1.8).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert mm to m** before substituting into formulas. Also, students often compute **Wahl's Factor incorrectly** by using the wrong formula (e.g., $K_s = (4C+2)/(4C)$ without curvature correction). Always show **both terms** of the Wahl factor equation.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the properties and applications of the following spring materials:
- (i) Music Wire (ASTM A228)
- (ii) Hard-Drawn Carbon Steel (ASTM A227)
- (iii) Chrome-Vanadium Steel (ASTM A231)
- (iv) Phosphor Bronze

**(b)** [7 Marks] A helical compression spring made of **music wire (ASTM A228)** has a wire diameter of **4 mm** and is subjected to a fluctuating load between **200 N and 500 N**. The spring index is **C = 8**. Determine:
- (i) The mean coil diameter D
- (ii) Wahl's factor K_s
- (iii) The factor of safety against fatigue using the **Gerber criterion** (given Ssy = 0.577 × Sut).

---

### ✅ **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Comparison Table

| Property | Music Wire A228 | Hard-Drawn A227 | Chrome-Vanadium A231 | Phosphor Bronze |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 1800 – 4000 | 1030 – 2380 | 1550 – 2070 | 410 – 1400 |
| **Shear Modulus G (GPa)** | 77.2 | 77.2 | 77.2 | 41.4 |
| **Max Operating Temp (°C)** | 120 | 120 | 220 | 95 |
| **Cost** | High | Low | High | Medium |
| **Fatigue Resistance** | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Poor | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Typical Applications** | Valve springs, clutch springs | Mattresses, screen springs | Automotive valve springs, aircraft | Electrical contacts, marine hardware |

**Key observations:**
- **Music wire** has the **highest tensile strength** but poor corrosion resistance.
- **Hard-drawn carbon steel** is the **cheapest** but has lower fatigue life.
- **Chrome-vanadium** is suitable for **high-temperature** applications.
- **Phosphor bronze** is preferred when **electrical conductivity + corrosion resistance** is needed.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Wire diameter, **d = 4 mm**
- Fluctuating load: **W_min = 200 N**, **W_max = 500 N**
- Spring index, **C = 8**
- Material: **Music Wire (ASTM A228)**

**Step 1 — Mean Coil Diameter**

$$D = C \times d = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ mm} = 0.032 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Wahl's Factor**

$$K_s = \frac{4(8) - 1}{4(8) - 4} + \frac{0.615}{8}$$

$$K_s = \frac{31}{28} + \frac{0.615}{8}$$

$$K_s = 1.1071 + 0.0769 = 1.1840$$

**Step 3 — Material Tensile Strength (ASTM A228 Empirical Equation)**

$$S_{ut} = \frac{A}{d^m} = \frac{2211}{(4)^{0.145}}$$

$$(4)^{0.145} = e^{0.145 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.2010} \approx 1.2226$$

$$S_{ut} = \frac{2211}{1.2226} \approx 1808 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Shear Yield Strength**

$$S_{sy} = 0.577 \times S_{ut} = 0.577 \times 1808 = 1043 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Shear Endurance Limit**

$$S_{se} = 0.30 \times S_{ut} = 0.30 \times 1808 = 542 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Stress Amplitudes**

$$\tau_{max} = K_s \cdot \frac{8 W_{max} D}{\pi d^3} = 1.1840 \times \frac{8 \times 500 \times 0.032}{\pi \times (0.004)^3}$$

Numerator:

$$8 \times 500 \times 0.032 = 128 \text{ N·m}$$

Denominator:

$$\pi \times (0.004)^3 = \pi \times 6.4 \times 10^{-8} = 2.011 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.1840 \times \frac{128}{2.011 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.1840 \times 6.365 \times 10^{8} = 753.6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{min} = 1.1840 \times \frac{8 \times 200 \times 0.032}{\pi \times (0.004)^3} = \frac{200}{500} \times 753.6 = 301.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Stress Amplitudes (Mean and Alternating)**

$$\tau_m = \frac{\tau_{max} + \tau_{min}}{2} = \frac{753.6 + 301.4}{2} = 527.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_a = \frac{\tau_{max} - \tau_{min}}{2} = \frac{753.6 - 301.4}{2} = 226.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8 — Gerber Criterion for Factor of Safety**

$$n = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{S_{sy}}{\tau_m} \right)^2 \frac{\tau_a}{S_{se}} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{2 S_{se} \tau_m}{S_{sy} \tau_a} \right)^2 } \right]$$

Calculate ratios:

$$\frac{S_{sy}}{\tau_m} = \frac{1043}{527.5} = 1.977$$

$$\left(\frac{S_{sy}}{\tau_m}\right)^2 = 3.909$$

$$\frac{2 S_{se} \tau_m}{S_{sy} \tau_a} = \frac{2 \times 542 \times 527.5}{1043 \times 226.1} = \frac{571810}{235830} = 2.425$$

$$\left(\frac{2 S_{se} \tau_m}{S_{sy} \tau_a}\right)^2 = 5.881$$

Inside the bracket:

$$-1 + \sqrt{1 + 5.881} = -1 + \sqrt{6.881} = -1 + 2.623 = 1.623$$

Now compute n:

$$n = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.909 \times \frac{226.1}{542} \times 1.623$$

$$n = 0.5 \times 3.909 \times 0.4172 \times 1.623$$

$$n = 0.5 \times 3.909 \times 0.6772$$

$$n = 0.5 \times 2.647 = 1.324$$

**Factor of Safety n ≈ 1.32**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Gerber vs. Goodman vs. Soderberg:**
> - **Gerber** (used for **ductile** materials) → parabolic curve.
> - **Goodman** (conservative) → linear curve.
> - **Soderberg** (most conservative, includes yield) → linear curve.
> - Using the **wrong criterion** will lose you 2–3 marks. For music wire (ductile), **Gerber is preferred**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Music wire (ASTM A228)** is the **strongest** carbon steel spring wire; A = 2211, m = 0.145.
- ✅ **Hard-drawn (A227)**: A = 1783, m = 0.190.
- ✅ **Oil-tempered (A229)**: A = 1857, m = 0.087.
- ✅ **Chrome-vanadium (A231)**: A = 2005, m = 0.168.
- ✅ **Stainless steel (A313)**: A = 1867, m = 0.146.
- ✅ **Wahl's Factor formula** $K_s = \dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ must always be applied.
- ✅ **Spring Index C = D/d**; recommended range is **4 ≤ C ≤ 12**.
- ✅ **Shear Yield Strength** ≈ **0.577 × Sut** for steels.
- ✅ **Endurance Limit** for spring wire is **0.22 to 0.30 × Sut** (not the standard 0.5 × Sut for steels — a classic KTU trap).
- ✅ **Material Selection depends on**: Load type, Temperature, Corrosion, Cost, Fatigue life.
- ✅ **Operating Temperature Limits**: Music wire < 120 °C; Cr-V < 220 °C; SS 17-7PH < 260 °C.
- ✅ **Gerber Criterion** is used for **ductile** spring materials; **Soderberg** for **brittle/safety-critical**; **Goodman** as conservative compromise.
- ✅ **Empirical Sut = A/dᵐ** equation is the most tested KTU formula in Module 3.
- ✅ **Practical KTU trick**: Always convert **mm to meters** before substituting into SI formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress and Deflection in Helical Compression Spring with Axial Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stress and Deflection in Helical Compression Spring with Axial Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Helical Compression Spring** is a flexible elastic machine element, made of a wire coiled into a helix, designed to store mechanical energy and resist an **axial compressive load**. The load is applied along the longitudinal axis of the spring, causing the coils to **close together** (reduce pitch) while developing internal **torsional shear stress** in the wire cross-section.

Under axial loading **W** (Newtons), two principal responses must be quantified for design:

| Response | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | Torsional + direct shear stress in wire, $\mathbf{N/mm^2}$ |
| **Deflection** | $\delta$ | Axial compression of spring, $\mathbf{mm}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The stress developed in a helical spring is **NOT pure torsion**. It is a **combined state of direct shear + torsional shear** caused by the axial load. Both must be combined, and then a **Wahl's correction factor K** is applied to account for direct shear and curvature.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Door Mat Spring"

Imagine a tightly wound Slinky toy standing on a table. Now press it straight down from the top:
- The top coil pushes the coil below it, and so on — **this is axial compressive loading**.
- The whole spring shortens — **this is deflection (δ)**.
- Try twisting the wire of a coat hanger — the spring wires are also being **twisted (torsion)** even though you only pushed down.
- Now bend the wire slightly while pushing — this is the **curvature correction** (Wahl's factor K).

> [!NOTE]
> The wire of a helical compression spring is subjected to a **torque T = W·R** AND a **direct shear force W** because the load does not pass through the centroid of the wire cross-section.

## 1.3 Spring Geometry — Key Parameters

- **Wire diameter** = $d$ (mm)
- **Mean coil diameter** = $D$ (mm) — measured from centre-to-centre of wire
- **Outer coil diameter** = $D_o = D + d$
- **Inner coil diameter** = $D_i = D - d$
- **Number of active coils** = $n$
- **Total coils** = $n_t = n + n_i$ (for squared and ground ends add 2)
- **Free length** = $L_f$
- **Solid length** = $L_s = n_t \cdot d$
- **Pitch** = $p = L_f / n$ (for close-coiled, $L_f = nd + $ end allowances)
- **Spring Index** = $C = D/d$ (a critical design ratio; **recommended: 4 ≤ C ≤ 12**)

## 1.4 Sign Convention for Axial Load

- **Compression load W** is taken as **positive**.
- It produces **clockwise torque** on the back face of wire (looking from the loaded end).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Combined Loading Occurs

When an axial load **W** is applied at the tip of a helical spring wire, two distinct internal reactions develop in every cross-section of the wire:

1. **Torsional Moment (T):** The line of action of W is offset from the wire axis by the mean radius **R = D/2**. This creates a torque that tries to twist the wire.
   $$T = W \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

2. **Direct Shear Force (F):** The vertical component (load W) itself acts as a transverse shear on the wire cross-section.
   $$F = W$$

3. **Bending Moment (M):** Strictly, there is also a small bending moment $M = W \cdot \frac{d}{2}$, but for a tightly coiled spring where $d \ll D$, this bending stress is **negligibly small** and is **ignored** in standard KTU design (per IS 7906 / Shigley's approach).

## 2.2 Shear Stress Derivation — Torsion Component

The torsional shear stress in a solid circular wire of diameter $d$ is given by the torsion formula:

$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

Substituting $T = W D/2$:

$$\tau_t = \frac{16 \cdot (WD/2)}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

## 2.3 Shear Stress — Direct Shear Component

The average direct (transverse) shear stress across the wire cross-section:

$$\tau_d = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{W}{\frac{\pi}{4}d^2} = \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

## 2.4 Combined Maximum Shear Stress

Both stresses are tangential on the cross-section, so the **maximum shear stress** at the inner fibre of the wire is the **algebraic sum**:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_t + \tau_d = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

Factorising and substituting $C = D/d$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8W}{\pi d^2}\left(\frac{D}{d} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{8W}{\pi d^2}\left(C + 0.5\right)$$

## 2.5 Wahl's Correction Factor (K)

Because helical springs have **curved wire** and the load has both shear components, a stress concentration arises at the **inner fibre** of the coil. **Wahl** (and independently later **Bach**) proposed an empirical correction factor:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}}$$

The **corrected maximum shear stress** used in design is:

$$\tau_{max} = K \cdot \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} = K \cdot \frac{8WC}{\pi d^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **helical spring with SQUARED & GROUND ENDS**, KTU 2024 syllabus uses the **modified Wahl factor**:
> $$K_s = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C} + \frac{0.5}{C^2}$$ (rarely asked; standard K is acceptable in most answers)

## 2.6 Deflection of Helical Compression Spring

The axial deflection comes from the **angle of twist** $\theta$ of the wire along its mean helix length. Using the torsion equation:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L_{wire}}$$

where $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ and mean wire length $L_{wire} = \pi D n$.

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{TL_{wire}}{GJ} = \frac{(WD/2)(\pi D n)}{G(\pi d^4/32)} = \frac{16WD^2 n}{Gd^4}$$

Axial deflection $\delta = R \cdot \theta = (D/2)\theta$:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} = \frac{8WC^3 n W}{Gd}}$$

## 2.7 Spring Stiffness (Rate)

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{Gd^4}{8D^3 n} = \frac{Gd}{8C^3 n} \quad (\text{N/mm})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Spring stiffness $k \propto d$ and $k \propto 1/D^3$. Halving the mean diameter increases stiffness by **8 times** — the spring becomes drastically stiffer.

## 2.8 Strain Energy Stored

The strain energy stored in a helical spring under axial load W with deflection δ is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta = \frac{4W^2 D^3 n}{Gd^4} \quad (\text{N·mm} = \text{mJ})$$

In terms of **stress and volume**:

$$U = \frac{\tau^2 V}{2Gd \cdot \text{constant}} \implies \text{Volume} \; V = \frac{\pi^2 d^2 D n}{4}$$

## 2.9 Failure Theories in Spring Design

KTU 2024 expects students to know **two failure criteria**:

| Theory | Condition | Use When |
|---|---|---|
| **Static (Yield) Failure** | $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{perm} = \dfrac{0.3 \, \sigma_{ut}}{FOS}$ | Brittle wire, low-cycle loading |
| **Fatigue Failure** | $\tau_{max} \le \tau_f$ (from Soderberg/Goodman) | Cyclic / dynamic loading |

> [!WARNING]
> For **statically loaded** springs (occasional shock), use **0.3 to 0.4 times UTS** as the allowable shear stress per IS 7906.

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spring Index | $C = D/d$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Wahl's Factor | $K = \dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 4 | Direct Shear Stress | $\tau_d = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | Max Shear Stress (uncorrected) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8W}{\pi d^2}\left(C + 0.5\right)$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | Corrected Max Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = K \cdot \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 7 | Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4}$ | $\text{mm}$ |
| 8 | Spring Rate | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{Gd^4}{8D^3 n}$ | $\text{N/mm}$ |
| 9 | Strain Energy | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}W\delta = \dfrac{4W^2 D^3 n}{Gd^4}$ | $\text{N·mm}$ |
| 10 | Solid Length | $L_s = n_t \cdot d$ | $\text{mm}$ |
| 11 | Wire Length | $L_w = \pi D n$ | $\text{mm}$ |
| 12 | Vol. of Wire | $V = \dfrac{\pi d^2 L_w}{4}$ | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 13 | Shear Modulus (Steel) | $G = 0.77 \times 10^5$ (typical) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 14 | Allowable Shear Stress | $\tau_{allow} = 0.3\,\sigma_{ut}$ (statically) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive suspension:** Helical compression springs (coil springs) absorb road shocks — designed for max stress and desired ride height.
- **Valve springs in IC engines:** Operate under high cyclic loading — **fatigue-based design** dominates.
- **Railway buffers:** Heavy-duty compression springs.
- **Die springs (jigs & fixtures):** Pre-stressed springs in press tools.
- **Clutch and brake assemblies:** Energy storage and release.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Shear Stress in a Helical Spring

### Step 1: Free Body of Wire
A short segment of the spring wire is subjected to:
- An axial compressive force $W$
- A moment arm equal to the mean coil radius $R = D/2$

The force $W$ is offset from the wire axis, so the equivalent loading on the wire cross-section is:

$$\text{Torque, } T = W \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

$$\text{Direct shear force, } F_s = W$$

### Step 2: Apply the Torsion Formula
For a solid circular cross-section, the polar section modulus is:

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$$

The torsional shear stress at the outer fibre:

$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{W \cdot D/2}{\pi d^3 / 16} = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Direct Shear Formula
Area of wire cross-section: $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$

$$\tau_d = \frac{F_s}{A} = \frac{W}{\pi d^2/4} = \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

### Step 4: Superposition
Both shear stresses act in the **same direction** at the inner fibre of the coil, so they add:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_t + \tau_d = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of Spring Index
Factorising the common term $\dfrac{8W}{\pi d^2}$ and dividing by $d/d$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8W}{\pi d^2}\left(\frac{D}{d} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{8W}{\pi d^2}\left(C + 0.5\right)$$

> This form is **critical** for KTU — many questions give only $C$ and $W$ and ask to find $\tau_{max}$.

## 3.2 Derivation — Wahl's Correction Factor

### Step 1: Why Correction is Needed
Two effects are *not* yet captured:
1. **Curvature of wire** — the inner fibre has higher stress than the simple torsion formula predicts (similar to curved beam theory).
2. **Direct shear** — we already added it, but in reality it acts on a curved surface.

### Step 2: Wahl's Empirical Equation
Through photoelastic experiments and curved-beam analysis, Wahl proposed:

$$K = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

For the **theoretical case** (no curvature, no direct shear): $K = 1$ when $C \to \infty$ (straight wire).

### Step 3: Final Design Equation
Multiply uncorrected stress by K:

$$\boxed{\tau_{design} = K \cdot \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Range of K

| C | K |
|---|---|
| 3 | 1.945 |
| 4 | 1.498 |
| 5 | 1.310 |
| 6 | 1.213 |
| 7 | 1.156 |
| 8 | 1.119 |
| 10 | 1.077 |
| 12 | 1.053 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $C = 8$ (a typical design value), $K \approx 1.119$. For low C (strong curvature), K grows rapidly. This is why **C ≥ 4 is recommended** — to keep stress correction reasonable.

## 3.3 Derivation — Deflection (Angle of Twist Method)

### Step 1: Wire Geometry
The mean length of wire in $n$ active coils is the circumference of the mean helix times the number of turns:

$$L_w = \pi D n$$

### Step 2: Angle of Twist Per Unit Length
Using the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L_w}$:

$$\theta = \frac{T L_w}{G J}$$

Substituting $T = WD/2$, $J = \pi d^4/32$, and $L_w = \pi D n$:

$$\theta = \frac{(WD/2) \cdot (\pi D n)}{G \cdot (\pi d^4/32)} = \frac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4}$$

### Step 3: Relate Angle of Twist to Axial Deflection
Each coil, when twisted by $\theta$, moves axially by the arc-length:

$$\delta = R \cdot \theta = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \theta = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4} = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $C = D/d$

$$\delta = \frac{8 W C^3 n d^3}{G d^4} = \frac{8 W C^3 n}{G d}$$

This shows: **larger index C → larger deflection** (softer spring).

## 3.4 Derivation — Strain Energy (Castigliano / Direct Method)

### Method 1: From Deflection
$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta = \frac{1}{2} W \cdot \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} = \frac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

### Method 2: From Torsion in Wire
Strain energy in pure torsion per unit length:

$$u = \frac{T^2}{2GJ} \quad \text{(N·mm/mm)}$$

Total energy over the wire length $L_w = \pi D n$:

$$U = \frac{T^2}{2GJ} \cdot L_w = \frac{(WD/2)^2 \cdot (\pi D n)}{2 G (\pi d^4/32)} = \frac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4} \;\;\checkmark$$

Both methods match — confirming the derivation.

## 3.5 Analytical Design Matrix — Procedure for Solving a KTU Problem

> [!NOTE]
> Whenever KTU gives data like $W$, $d$, $D$, $n$, $G$, $\sigma_{ut}$ and asks to "design the spring" or "check for safety" — follow this **5-step template**:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compute $C = D/d$. Check $4 \le C \le 12$. | Validate geometry |
| 2 | Compute $K = \dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ | Stress factor |
| 3 | Compute $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8KWD}{\pi d^3}$ | Actual stress |
| 4 | Compute $\tau_{allow} = 0.3 \sigma_{ut}$ (or 0.4 for static shock) | Allowable stress |
| 5 | Compare: if $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$, **safe** | Safety verdict |

For **deflection problems**, also compute $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4}$ and check against allowable deflection.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (For Self-Study)

> **Problem:** A helical compression spring has wire diameter $d = 6$ mm, mean coil diameter $D = 60$ mm, and 8 active coils. It carries an axial load of $W = 200$ N. Take $G = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm². Find (a) max shear stress, (b) deflection, (c) stiffness, (d) strain energy.

**Solution:**

$C = D/d = 60/6 = 10$

$$K = \frac{4(10)-1}{4(10)-4} + \frac{0.615}{10} = \frac{39}{36} + 0.0615 = 1.0833 + 0.0615 = 1.1448$$

$$\tau_{max} = K \cdot \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} = 1.1448 \cdot \frac{8 \times 200 \times 60}{\pi \times 216} = 1.1448 \times 141.47 \approx 161.96 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\delta = \frac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 60^3 \times 8}{80{,}000 \times 6^4} = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 216{,}000 \times 8}{80{,}000 \times 1296} = \frac{2.7648 \times 10^9}{1.0368 \times 10^8} \approx 26.67 \; \text{mm}$$

$$k = W/\delta = 200/26.67 \approx 7.5 \; \text{N/mm}$$

$$U = \tfrac{1}{2} W \delta = 0.5 \times 200 \times 26.67 = 2666.7 \; \text{N·mm} = 2.67 \; \text{J}$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Helical Compression Spring — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Load W applied] --> B[Force Offset by D/2 from wire axis]
    B --> C[Torque T = W * D/2 acts on wire section]
    B --> D[Direct Shear Fs = W acts on wire section]
    C --> E[Torsional Shear Stress tau_t = 8WD/pi d^3]
    D --> F[Direct Shear Stress tau_d = 4W/pi d^2]
    E --> G[Superpose: tau = tau_t + tau_d]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Curvature Correction Factor K]
    H --> I[Design Shear Stress tau_design = K * 8WD/pi d^3]
    I --> J{Compare with tau_allow = 0.3 sigma_ut}
    J -->|tau_design <= tau_allow| K[SPRING IS SAFE]
    J -->|tau_design > tau_allow| L[REDESIGN: increase d or D]
    
    A2[Same Load W] --> M[Wire twists by angle theta]
    M --> N[Axial Deflection delta = 8 W D3 n / G d4]
    N --> O[Spring Stiffness k = G d4 / 8 D3 n]
    N --> P[Strain Energy U = 0.5 W delta]
```

## 4.2 Spring Index and Wahl's Factor — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Wire diameter d] --> B[Input: Mean coil diameter D]
    B --> C[Compute C = D/d]
    C --> D{C between 4 and 12?}
    D -->|No| E[REJECT - redesign geometry]
    D -->|Yes| F[Compute K from Wahl equation]
    F --> G[K = 4C-1 over 4C-4 + 0.615/C]
    G --> H[Use K in tau_design formula]
    H --> I[Output: Corrected Max Shear Stress]
```

## 4.3 Spring with Combined Loading — Force Resolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Applied Axial Load W] --> Q[Resolve at wire cross-section]
    Q --> R1[Torque T = W x D/2]
    Q --> R2[Direct Shear Fs = W]
    Q --> R3[Bending M = W x d/2 - neglected]
    R1 --> S1[Torsional Stress tau_t = 16T/pi d3]
    R2 --> S2[Direct Shear tau_d = 4W/pi d2]
    S1 --> T[Sum at inner fibre]
    S2 --> T
    T --> U[Apply Wahl Factor K]
    U --> V[tau_max = K x 8WD/pi d3]
```

## 4.4 Design Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Given: W, d, D, n, G, sigma_ut]) --> Step1[Step 1: Compute C = D/d]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Compute K]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Compute tau_max]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Compute tau_allow = 0.3 sigma_ut]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Compute deflection delta]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Compute stiffness k]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Compute strain energy U]
    Step7 --> Verify{tau_max less than or equal to tau_allow?}
    Verify -->|Yes| Pass([DESIGN IS SAFE])
    Verify -->|No| Fail([FAIL - increase d or reduce W])
```
