# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Spring Index (C) and Spring Rate (k). What is the recommended range of C for helical compression springs?**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Spring Index (C):** The ratio of mean coil diameter to the wire diameter.
$$C = \frac{D}{d}$$

**Spring Rate (k):** The load required per unit deflection of the spring, expressed in N/mm.
$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$

**Recommended range:** $4 \le C \le 12$ (IS 7906 standard). For $C < 4$, manufacturing is difficult and stresses are high; for $C > 12$, the spring is unstable and tends to buckle.

---

### **Q2. What is Wahl's correction factor? Why is it applied in spring design?**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wahl's correction factor **K** is an empirical multiplier introduced to account for the **combined effect of direct shear stress and the increased stress due to curvature of the wire** in a helical compression spring.

$$K = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

**Why applied:**
1. The simple torsion formula $\tau = 8WD/\pi d^3$ considers only torsion.
2. In reality, the wire is also subjected to direct shear (W on the cross-section).
3. The wire's curvature causes **stress concentration at the inner fibre** of the coil.
4. K combines all these effects into a single multiplicative factor applied to the torsion formula:

$$\tau_{design} = K \cdot \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Full Solution (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

A helical compression spring of **wire diameter d = 5 mm**, **mean coil diameter D = 50 mm**, and **8 active coils** is subjected to a maximum axial load of **300 N**. The spring is made of hard-drawn steel wire with **$\sigma_{ut} = 1400$ N/mm²** and **G = 84 kN/mm²**.

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate:
   (i) The maximum shear stress in the wire (using Wahl's factor)
   (ii) The deflection of the spring
   (iii) The strain energy stored

**(b) [7 marks]** Check whether the spring is safe under static loading using the IS 7906 allowable shear stress criterion. If not safe, suggest design modifications.

---

#### **Model Solution (A):**

### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Maximum Shear Stress [3 marks]:**

Step 1 — Spring index:
$$C = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{50}{5} = 10$$

Step 2 — Wahl's factor:
$$K = \frac{4(10) - 1}{4(10) - 4} + \frac{0.615}{10} = \frac{39}{36} + 0.0615$$
$$K = 1.0833 + 0.0615 = 1.1448$$

Step 3 — Maximum corrected shear stress:
$$\tau_{max} = K \cdot \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} = 1.1448 \times \frac{8 \times 300 \times 50}{\pi \times 125}$$
$$\tau_{max} = 1.1448 \times \frac{120{,}000}{392.7} = 1.1448 \times 305.58$$
$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 349.86 \; \text{N/mm}^2}$$

**(ii) Deflection [2 marks]:**
$$\delta = \frac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} = \frac{8 \times 300 \times 50^3 \times 8}{84{,}000 \times 5^4}$$
$$\delta = \frac{8 \times 300 \times 125{,}000 \times 8}{84{,}000 \times 625} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{5.25 \times 10^7}$$
$$\boxed{\delta \approx 45.71 \; \text{mm}}$$

**(iii) Strain Energy [2 marks]:**
$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta = 0.5 \times 300 \times 45.71$$
$$\boxed{U \approx 6857 \; \text{N·mm} \approx 6.86 \; \text{J}}$$

---

### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Allowable shear stress (IS 7906 for statically loaded springs):**
$$\tau_{allow} = 0.3 \times \sigma_{ut} = 0.3 \times 1400 = 420 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Comparison:**
$$\tau_{max} = 349.86 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \; < \; \tau_{allow} = 420 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Safety:**
$$FOS = \frac{\tau_{allow}}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{420}{349.86} \approx 1.20$$

**Conclusion:** The spring is **safe** under static loading, with a factor of safety of approximately **1.20**.

**Suggestions for improvement (if needed):** For a higher FOS, increase wire diameter $d$ or use a stiffer material (chrome-vanadium steel with $\sigma_{ut} > 1800$ N/mm²).

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**Q.B.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

A helical compression spring is required to absorb **50 J of energy** with a maximum deflection of **40 mm**. The spring index is to be **C = 6** and the allowable shear stress is **$\tau_{allow} = 350$ N/mm²**. Take **G = 80 kN/mm²**.

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine:
   (i) The maximum load W
   (ii) The mean coil diameter D and wire diameter d
   (iii) The number of active coils n

**(b) [7 marks]** Verify the spring is safe against the allowable shear stress and compute the spring stiffness and solid length (assume $n_t = n + 2$ for plain ends).

---

#### **Model Solution (B):**

### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Maximum load [2 marks]:**
$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta \implies 50{,}000 = \frac{1}{2} \times W \times 40$$
$$W = \frac{50{,}000 \times 2}{40} = 2500 \; \text{N}$$

**(ii) Wire and coil diameter [3 marks]:**
Step 1 — Wahl's factor for $C = 6$:
$$K = \frac{4(6)-1}{4(6)-4} + \frac{0.615}{6} = \frac{23}{20} + 0.1025 = 1.15 + 0.1025 = 1.2525$$

Step 2 — From $\tau_{allow} = K \cdot \dfrac{8WC}{\pi d^2}$ (using $\tau_{max} = \tau_{allow}$):
$$350 = 1.2525 \times \frac{8 \times 2500 \times 6}{\pi d^2}$$
$$350 = 1.2525 \times \frac{120{,}000}{\pi d^2} = 1.2525 \times \frac{38{,}197}{d^2}$$
$$d^2 = \frac{1.2525 \times 38{,}197}{350} = \frac{47{,}842}{350} = 136.69$$
$$\boxed{d \approx 11.69 \approx 12 \; \text{mm} \; (\text{standardize})}$$

Step 3 — Mean diameter:
$$D = C \times d = 6 \times 12 = 72 \; \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Number of active coils [2 marks]:**
$$\delta = \frac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} \implies 40 = \frac{8 \times 2500 \times 72^3 \times n}{80{,}000 \times 12^4}$$
$$40 = \frac{20{,}000 \times 373{,}248 \times n}{80{,}000 \times 20{,}736} = \frac{7.465 \times 10^9 \cdot n}{1.659 \times 10^9}$$
$$n = \frac{40 \times 1.659 \times 10^9}{7.465 \times 10^9} = \frac{66.36 \times 10^9}{7.465 \times 10^9} \approx 8.89$$
$$\boxed{n \approx 9 \; \text{active coils}}$$

---

### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Verification of shear stress with d = 12 mm [3 marks]:**
$$\tau_{max} = K \cdot \frac{8WC}{\pi d^2} = 1.2525 \times \frac{8 \times 2500 \times 6}{\pi \times 144}$$
$$\tau_{max} = 1.2525 \times \frac{120{,}000}{452.39} = 1.2525 \times 265.26$$
$$\tau_{max} \approx 332.23 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \; < \; 350 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Spring is SAFE.** Factor of safety:
$$FOS = \frac{350}{332.23} \approx 1.054$$

**Step 2 — Spring stiffness [2 marks]:**
$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{2500}{40} = 62.5 \; \text{N/mm}$$

Cross-check:
$$k = \frac{Gd}{8C^3 n} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 12}{8 \times 216 \times 9} = \frac{960{,}000}{15{,}552} \approx 61.73 \; \text{N/mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Solid length [2 marks]:**
$$n_t = n + 2 = 9 + 2 = 11 \; \text{coils (plain ends)}$$
$$L_s = n_t \cdot d = 11 \times 12 = 132 \; \text{mm}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Wahl's factor (K) — LOSES 1–2 marks.** Many students write $\tau = 8WD/\pi d^3$ directly. **Always apply K for helical compression springs.**
>
> 2. **Wrong spring index interpretation.** Some students use $D = D_o$ or $D = D_i$. **Use mean diameter D = D_o − d = D_i + d.**
>
> 3. **Confusing $n$ vs $n_t$.** Deflection formula uses **active coils (n)**. Solid length uses **total coils (n_t)**.
>
> 4. **Sign convention error in shear stress.** Both $\tau_t$ and $\tau_d$ act in the **same tangential direction** at the inner fibre — they **add**, not subtract.
>
> 5. **Unit mismatch.** $G$ must be in **N/mm²** (not GPa). $1 \text{ GPa} = 1000 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Double-check before substitution.
>
> 6. **Incorrect allowable stress.** For **statically loaded** springs: $\tau_{allow} = 0.3\,\sigma_{ut}$. For **cyclic/fatigue loaded** springs: use Soderberg or Goodman line — **NOT 0.3 σ_ut**.
>
> 7. **Bending moment ignored.** Do **NOT** include $M = Wd/2$ in stress calculation — KTU follows the standard assumption that $M$ is negligible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for last-night revision before KTU exam.**

- [x] A **helical compression spring** stores energy via **torsional twist of wire**, even though the external load is axial.
- [x] Two internal effects in wire: **Torsion (T = WD/2)** and **Direct Shear (W)**. Bending is ignored.
- [x] **Maximum shear stress = Torsional + Direct shear**, both tangential at inner fibre.
- [x] **Wahl's factor K** corrects for curvature + direct shear. **Always** use it in KTU answers.
- [x] **Spring Index $C = D/d$** — keep between **4 and 12**.
- [x] **Deflection** $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} = \dfrac{8WC^3 nW}{Gd}$
- [x] **Stiffness** $k = \dfrac{Gd^4}{8D^3 n} = \dfrac{Gd}{8C^3 n}$
- [x] **Strain energy** $U = \dfrac{1}{2}W\delta = \dfrac{4W^2 D^3 n}{Gd^4}$
- [x] **Solid length** $L_s = n_t \cdot d$
- [x] **Wire length** $L_w = \pi D n$
- [x] **IS 7906 allowable shear stress** for static loading: $\tau_{allow} = 0.3\,\sigma_{ut}$
- [x] For **cyclic loading**: use **Soderberg / Goodman** fatigue criterion (Module 4 / 5 territory).
- [x] **Higher C → softer spring, lower stress, but buckling risk.**
- [x] **Higher d → stiffer spring, higher stress, smaller deflection.**
- [x] **Shear modulus for steel** $G \approx 80 \text{ kN/mm}^2 = 80 \text{ GPa}$ (typical value used in KTU).
- [x] **Standard units in KTU answers:** $W$ in N, $d$ & $D$ in mm, $G$ in N/mm², $\tau$ in N/mm², $\delta$ in mm.
- [x] **Always end with a safety verdict:** "Since $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allow}$, the spring is SAFE with FOS = ___."
- [x] **Memorize Wahl's factor equation** — most asked formula in KTU Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curvature Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Curvature Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Curvature Effects** in Machine Design refer to the modification of the elementary straight-beam bending stress distribution when the beam is initially **curved** in the unloaded state (i.e., the radius of curvature of the centroidal axis is comparable to the depth of the member). The classical Euler–Bernoulli straight-beam assumption — that plane sections remain plane and normal stresses vary **linearly** from the neutral axis — is **no longer valid** for such members. Instead, the stress distribution becomes **hyperbolic**, with the neutral axis shifting **inward** from the centroidal axis toward the center of curvature.

This phenomenon is described by the **Winkler–Bach Theory of Curved Beams (1858/1872)**, which is the foundation for designing components such as **crane hooks, C-clamps, chain links, eye-bolts, split pins, curved frames, and the curved arm of levers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEPET411 – Module 3):**
> The student must be able to (a) identify members where curvature effects are dominant, (b) apply the Winkler–Bach equations, (c) compute the **curvature factor (K₀)**, and (d) calculate the **maximum (inner-fiber) and minimum (outer-fiber) bending stresses** in curved beams subjected to pure bending or combined loading.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Banana vs. the Ruler**
> - Imagine pressing a **straight ruler** between your palms. The bending stress at the top (tension) and bottom (compression) fibers rises **linearly** as you move away from the mid-thickness.
> - Now take a **banana** and try to bend it **straight**. The skin on the **inner (concave) side** cracks almost immediately, while the outer side only stretches a little. Why? Because in a curved body, fibers near the **inside of the curve** are already **crowded together**, so the extra compression from bending accumulates very steeply. Fibers on the **outside** are pre-stretched, so additional tensile strain adds comparatively less stress.
> - This **asymmetric, hyperbolic stress profile** is the essence of *curvature effects*.

**Key physical insight:** The smaller the radius of curvature *relative to* the depth of the section, the more pronounced the curvature effect. A crane hook (ri ≈ 10 mm, h ≈ 50 mm) behaves very differently from a curved railroad rail (ri ≈ 300 m, h ≈ 0.2 m).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following geometric and material descriptors govern the curvature effect:

- **R** = Radius of the **neutral axis** of the curved beam (m, mm) — *not* the centroidal axis.
- **r̄ (or R̄)** = Radius of the **centroidal axis** (radius of the center of gravity of the cross-section from the center of curvature **O**).
- **ri** = Inner-fiber radius (m, mm).
- **ro** = Outer-fiber radius (m, mm).
- **h = ro − ri** = Depth of the cross-section in the plane of curvature.
- **e = R̄ − R** = **Eccentricity** between the centroidal axis and the neutral axis (always positive, with R < R̄).
- **K₀ = (R̄ + e)·(R̄ − e) / (e · R̄)** or equivalently the simpler form **K₀ = 1 / (K − 1)** where **K = R̄ / e** is the **curvature factor** (sometimes denoted *m* in older texts).
- **A** = Cross-sectional area of the curved beam (m², mm²).
- **I** = Second moment of area about the centroidal axis.
- **σ_i, σ_o** = Bending stresses at the inner and outer fibers respectively (N/mm² = MPa).
- **M** = Bending moment (N·mm) acting in the plane of curvature.
- **F** = Direct (axial) force (N).

> [!NOTE]
> **Curvature-Effect Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> If **R̄ / h > 10**, curvature effects may be **neglected** and straight-beam theory may be used with an error of less than ~5%. If **R̄ / h < 5**, curvature effects **must** be considered.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Winkler–Bach Theory

The Winkler–Bach theory rests on **two fundamental assumptions** (assumptions 1 and 2 of curved-beam theory):

1. **Plane sections remain plane after bending** (as in Euler–Bernoulli), but the neutral axis **shifts inward** from the centroidal axis.
2. **Radial stress (σ_r) is negligible** compared to the tangential (hoop) stress σ_θ, except very near the loading points. Hence, equilibrium is taken along a single fiber.
3. The **strain varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis**: ε = (R − r) / r, where **r** is the running radius of an arbitrary fiber.

By enforcing the two equilibrium conditions —

- **ΣF_axial = 0** → ∫σ dA = 0, and
- **ΣM_neutral = M** → ∫σ · (R − r) dA = M,

one obtains the **canonical Winkler–Bach stress equations**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Compute the centroidal radius R̄** of the cross-section from the center of curvature O.
2. **Compute the radius of the neutral axis R** using the cross-section geometry.
3. **Compute the eccentricity e = R̄ − R** (note: R is **smaller** than R̄, so e > 0).
4. **Compute the curvature factor K₀** (also written as *n* or sometimes defined as K₀ = R̄ / e in different textbooks — see Formula Sheet below).
5. **Apply the bending-stress formula** σ = M·(R − r) / [A·e·r] for any fiber at radius *r*.
6. **Substitute r = ri and r = ro** to obtain the **inner-fiber (maximum compressive) and outer-fiber (minimum tensile) stresses**.
7. **For combined loading** (axial force F + bending moment M), superpose the direct stress σ_d = F / A on the bending stress σ_b.
8. **Compute the equivalent stress** using appropriate failure theory (Maximum Shear Stress / Distortion Energy, as required in KTU).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for KTU ESE and module tests on this topic. Memorize the symbols and the form of each equation.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Radius of neutral axis (rectangular & circular sections) | $R = \dfrac{h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = \dfrac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | mm | **Not** the arithmetic mean. |
| 2 | Radius of neutral axis (general section) | $R = \dfrac{A}{\displaystyle\int_{r_i}^{r_o}\dfrac{dA}{r}}$ | mm | Use when cross-section is non-rectangular (e.g., trapezoidal, T-section). |
| 3 | Centroidal radius (rectangular, b × h) | $\bar{R} = r_i + \dfrac{h}{2}$ | mm | Direct from geometry. |
| 4 | Centroidal radius (circular, diameter d) | $\bar{R} = r_i + \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{r_i + r_o}{2}$ | mm | Same arithmetic mean holds. |
| 5 | Centroidal radius (trapezoidal) | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{a \cdot r_i^{2} + (b-a) \cdot \dfrac{r_i^{2}+r_i r_o + r_o^{2}}{3} + a \cdot r_o^{2}}{2 \cdot A}$ (or by composite-area method) | mm | Split into a rectangle + two triangles. |
| 6 | Eccentricity | $e = \bar{R} - R$ | mm | Always **e > 0**, meaning R is **inside** the centroidal axis. |
| 7 | Curvature factor (KTU standard notation) | $K_0 = \dfrac{1}{\,K - 1\,}, \quad K = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{e}$ | dimensionless | Some texts use $K_0 = \bar{R}/e$ directly. **Confirm notation in your answer script.** |
| 8 | Bending stress at any fiber radius *r* | $\sigma = \dfrac{M\,(R - r)}{A\,e\,r}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | **Sign convention**: tension +, compression −. |
| 9 | Stress at **inner** fiber (r = ri) | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{M\,(R - r_i)}{A\,e\,r_i} = \dfrac{M}{A\,e}\left(\dfrac{R}{r_i} - 1\right)$ | MPa | **Maximum magnitude** (usually compressive in a hook-type loading). |
| 10 | Stress at **outer** fiber (r = ro) | $\sigma_o = \dfrac{M\,(R - r_o)}{A\,e\,r_o} = \dfrac{M}{A\,e}\left(\dfrac{R}{r_o} - 1\right)$ | MPa | **Minimum magnitude** (usually tensile). |
| 11 | Stress in terms of curvature factor K₀ | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{M}{A\,K_0\,r_i}, \qquad \sigma_o = \dfrac{M}{A\,K_0\,r_o}$ (using alternate definition $K_0 = \bar R/e$ and re-arrangement) | MPa | Verify KTU module-handout convention before writing. |
| 12 | Combined axial + bending (general curved beam) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} \pm \dfrac{M\,(R - r)}{A\,e\,r}$ | MPa | '+' for tension, '−' for compression. |
| 13 | Maximum safe load (crane hook check) | $F = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}\,A\,e\,r_i}{R - r_i}$ | N | Inner fiber is critical. |
| 14 | Geometric straight-beam error indicator | Threshold: $\bar{R}/h > 10$ | — | Below this, curvature effects **cannot** be ignored. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Crane hooks** (IS 8610) — the single most common KTU design problem.
> - **C-frames of presses, pliers, and pipe wrenches.**
> - **Chain links and eye-bolts** (lifting gear).
> - **Curved fillet welds** in pressure-vessel head-to-shell junctions.
> - **Helical compression spring design** — where curvature effects (Wahl's correction factor, K_w) are also governed by the same Winkler–Bach framework applied to the wire curvature.

---

## 2.4 Worked Logic: When is the Inner Fiber Critical?

For a **crane hook** loaded in tension (F vertical, hook curving outward):
- The bending moment M = F · R̄ tends to **open** the hook (increase the inner radius).
- The direct stress F/A is tensile.
- However, the **bending stress on the inner fiber is *compressive*** (R − ri < 0, so σ_i becomes negative).
- The inner fiber thus sees a **net compressive stress**, which is often the *largest magnitude* stress in the section → governs design.

For a **C-clamp** under screw loading:
- The bending moment tends to **close** the clamp (decrease the inner radius).
- The inner fiber is in **tension**, and is again the critical fiber.

**The inner fiber is almost always the critical fiber in KTU curved-beam problems** — remember this for multiple-choice questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Radius of the Neutral Axis R for a Rectangular Cross-Section

**Setup:** Consider a curved beam of rectangular cross-section (width *b*, depth *h*) with inner radius *r_i* and outer radius *r_o*. The center of curvature is at O. The neutral axis has radius R.

---

### Step 1 — Express strain at radius r

The fundamental kinematic assumption (Winkler) states that strain at any fiber of radius *r* is

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{R - r}{r}
$$

**Why?** As the beam bends, the arc length of the neutral axis (at radius R) does not change: $d\theta \cdot R$. An adjacent fiber at radius *r* has new length $d\theta' \cdot r$. The original length of that fiber is $d\theta \cdot r$. The strain is therefore

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{d\theta' \, r - d\theta \, r}{d\theta \, r} = \frac{(d\theta' - d\theta) \, r}{d\theta \, r} = \frac{R \, d\theta' / d\theta - r}{r}
$$

The first equilibrium condition (ΣF = 0) imposes that the deformed angle $d\theta'$ is the **same** for all fibers (Winkler assumption 1, equivalent to plane sections remaining plane). Define $c = R \, d\theta' / d\theta$, a constant. Therefore

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{c - r}{r} = \frac{c}{r} - 1
$$

Identifying $c \equiv R$ (since the neutral axis has zero strain, i.e., ε = 0 at r = R), we recover

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{R - r}{r}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Write the hoop (tangential) stress using Hooke's Law

$$
\sigma_r = E \, \varepsilon_r = E \, \frac{R - r}{r}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Apply ΣF_axial = 0 (resultant axial force is zero for pure bending)

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \sigma_r \, b \, dr = 0
$$

Substituting σ_r:

$$
E \, b \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{R - r}{r} \, dr = 0
$$

Since E·b ≠ 0, the integral must vanish:

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{R - r}{r} \, dr = \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \left( \frac{R}{r} - 1 \right) dr = 0
$$

---

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral

$$
R \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r} - \int_{r_i}^{r_o} dr = 0
$$

$$
R \left[ \ln r \right]_{r_i}^{r_o} - \left[ r \right]_{r_i}^{r_o} = 0
$$

$$
R \left( \ln r_o - \ln r_i \right) - (r_o - r_i) = 0
$$

$$
R \ln \left( \frac{r_o}{r_i} \right) = r_o - r_i = h
$$

---

### Step 5 — Solve for R

$$
\boxed{\,R = \frac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} = \frac{h}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}\,}
$$

**This is the canonical formula for the radius of the neutral axis of a rectangular curved beam.** Note that R is the **logarithmic mean** of *r_i* and *r_o*, not the arithmetic mean (centroidal radius R̄).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Curvature Factor K₀

**Definition of K (curvature factor):**

$$
K = \frac{\bar{R}}{e}
$$

where $\bar R$ is the centroidal radius and $e = \bar R - R$ is the eccentricity.

**Rewriting:**

$$
K = \frac{\bar{R}}{\bar{R} - R} = \frac{1}{1 - R/\bar{R}}
$$

**Why introduce K?** Because K captures the **degree of curvature** of the beam. For a deeply curved beam (small R), R/ R̄ is small, so K is close to 1 (strong curvature). For a gently curved beam (R ≈ R̄), R/ R̄ → 1, so K → ∞ (no curvature effect).

**Alternative formulation used in many Indian textbooks (and KTU's recommended Shigley/V.B. Bhandari notation):**

$$
K_0 = \frac{1}{K - 1} = \frac{1}{\bar{R}/e - 1} = \frac{e}{\bar{R} - e} = \frac{e}{R}
$$

So

$$
\boxed{\,K_0 = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{\bar{R} - R}{R}\,}
$$

**Compact notation check:** Many texts write the stress formula as $\sigma = \dfrac{M\,(R - r)}{A\,e\,r}$, and then **define** a *modified curvature factor* such that the same formula becomes $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{A K_0 r}$ at r = ri. Reconciling:

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M (R - r_i)}{A e r_i} = \frac{M}{A e} \left( \frac{R}{r_i} - 1 \right)
$$

If we define

$$
K_i = \frac{e \, r_i}{R - r_i} = \frac{1}{(R/r_i) - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{R/e} = \frac{1}{(R/r_i) - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{K_0}
$$

This is why textbooks sometimes write the stress as $\sigma_i = M / (A K_i r_i)$ where $K_i$ is a *different* factor. **In your KTU answer, always start from the universal form $\sigma = M(R-r)/(A e r)$ and then specialise** — this avoids the notational trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bending-Stress Equation from Moment Equilibrium

**Step 1 — Apply ΣM_neutral_axis = M:**

$$
\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \sigma_r \cdot (R - r) \cdot b \, dr = M
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute σ_r = E (R − r)/r:**

$$
E \, b \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{(R - r)^2}{r} \, dr = M
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the integrand:**

$$
\frac{(R - r)^2}{r} = \frac{R^2 - 2Rr + r^2}{r} = \frac{R^2}{r} - 2R + r
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate term by term:**

$$
E \, b \left[ R^2 \ln r - 2Rr + \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{r_i}^{r_o} = M
$$

**Step 5 — Factor the difference:**

$$
E \, b \left[ R^2 \ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right) - 2R(r_o - r_i) + \frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} \right] = M
$$

**Step 6 — Use h = r_o − r_i, A = b·h, and the result of §3.1 that R·ln(r_o/r_i) = h:**

The first term becomes $E \, b \, R \cdot h = E \, A \, R$. The second term becomes $-2ERh = -2EAR$. Combining, $EAR - 2EAR = -EAR$. So:

$$
E \left[ -A R + b \cdot \frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} \right] = \frac{M}{1} = M
$$

**Step 7 — Compute b·(r_o² − r_i²)/2:**

$$
b \cdot \frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} = b \cdot \frac{(r_o - r_i)(r_o + r_i)}{2} = \frac{A \cdot (r_o + r_i)}{2} = A \cdot \bar{R}
$$

(because for a rectangle, $\bar{R} = (r_i + r_o)/2$).

**Step 8 — Substitute back:**

$$
E \left[ -A R + A \bar{R} \right] = M
$$

$$
E \, A \, (\bar{R} - R) = M
$$

$$
E \, A \, e = M
$$

This gives a *compatibility* check: e is **proportional to M** under linear-elastic pure bending, but the **stress distribution** is what we want.

**Step 9 — Solve the stress formula** by combining $\sigma = E (R - r)/r$ with the relation $E = M/(A e)$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{A \, e} \cdot \frac{R - r}{r}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma = \frac{M\,(R - r)}{A\,e\,r}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check (sanity):** For a *straight* beam (R → ∞, e → 0), this formula must degenerate. As curvature vanishes, R − r → (centroidal) and r → R̄, so the formula is indeterminate, and one returns to the **straight-beam form** σ = M·y/I via a limit application. This is a standard sanity check expected in KTU viva.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When Does Each Failure Theory Apply?

| Loading Type | Critical Fiber | Governing Stress | Suggested Failure Theory (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure bending of curved beam (no axial load) | Inner fiber (σ_i) | $\sigma_i = M(R - r_i)/(A e r_i)$ | Maximum normal stress theory |
| Combined tension + bending (e.g., crane hook) | Inner fiber (compressive) | $\sigma_i = F/A + M(R - r_i)/(A e r_i)$ | Distortion Energy (von Mises) |
| Combined compression + bending (e.g., C-clamp) | Inner fiber (tensile) | $\sigma_i = -F/A + M(R - r_i)/(A e r_i)$ | Distortion Energy |
| Cyclic loading on curved beam | Inner fiber (fillet stress concentration) | σ_i, with K_t from charts | Modified Goodman / Soderberg |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: Curved Beam Geometry with Stress Distribution

A Mermaid block diagram is used here to map the **functional architecture of a curved beam** (Winkler–Bach model) — its loading, geometry, stress state, and design check — because the actual physical geometry is best visualised in textbook figures (KTU handouts).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Load F or M] --> B[Cross-Section of Curved Beam]
    B --> C1[Inner Fiber at r = r_i]
    B --> C2[Neutral Axis at r = R]
    B --> C3[Centroidal Axis at r = R_bar]
    B --> C4[Outer Fiber at r = r_o]
    C1 --> D1[Stress sigma_i maximum magnitude]
    C2 --> D2[Stress sigma = 0 by definition]
    C3 --> D3[Stress sigma_bar nonzero bending plus direct]
    C4 --> D4[Stress sigma_o minimum magnitude]
    D1 --> E{Compare with sigma_allowable}
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E -- OK --> F[Safe Design]
    E -- Fail --> G[Revise r_i, r_o, or A]
    G --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Crane-Hook Design Workflow

The crane hook is the **single most important KTU problem on curvature effects**. The block diagram below maps the canonical design procedure using Winkler–Bach.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Inputs: Load W, Material sigma_allow] --> P2[Step 2 Select Trial Section: b, h, r_i]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Compute r_o = r_i + h]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Compute R = h over ln of r_o over r_i]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Compute R_bar = r_i + h over 2]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Compute e = R_bar minus R]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Compute Bending Moment M = W times R_bar]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Compute sigma_i = M times R minus r_i over A e r_i]
    P8 --> P9{Step 9 Is sigma_i less than sigma_allow}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Step 10 Design Accepted]
    P9 -- No --> P11[Step 11 Revise b or h and Iterate]
    P11 --> P2
```

---

## 4.3 Stress-Distribution Comparison: Straight vs. Curved Beam

The Mermaid block below summarises the **qualitative difference** in stress distribution that students must understand and sketch in exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S[Straight Beam]
        S1[Linear sigma profile] --> S2[Neutral axis at centroid]
        S2 --> S3[sigma_max at outer fiber]
    end
    subgraph C[Curved Beam Winkler Bach]
        C1[Hyperbolic sigma profile] --> C2[Neutral axis shifted inward]
        C2 --> C3[sigma_i at inner fiber exceeds straight-beam value]
        C3 --> C4[sigma_o at outer fiber less than straight-beam value]
    end
    S --- C
```

---

## 4.4 Force and Moment Diagram: Crane Hook Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Vertical Load W at hook tip] --> M1[Equivalent Moment M = W times R_bar about critical section]
    M1 --> S1[Direct Tensile Stress F over A at the section]
    S1 --> S2[Superpose with Bending Stress from Winkler Bach]
    S2 --> O1[Net Inner Fiber Stress sigma_i_net = F over A + M times R minus r_i over A e r_i]
    S2 --> O2[Net Outer Fiber Stress sigma_o_net = F over A + M times R minus r_o over A e r_o]
    O1 --> CK[Check against allowable stress]
    O2 --> CK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1. Define the term "curvature effect" in machine design. State the criteria for neglecting curvature effects in a beam. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The *curvature effect* refers to the deviation of the actual stress distribution in a beam from the linear Euler–Bernoulli distribution when the beam is initially curved and the radius of curvature of its centroidal axis is not very large compared to its depth. The neutral axis shifts inward from the centroidal axis, and the bending stress distribution becomes **hyperbolic** (Winkler–Bach theory).

**Criterion for neglect:** Curvature effects may be neglected when the centroidal radius-to-depth ratio satisfies

$$
\frac{\bar R}{h} \geq 10
$$

resulting in an error of less than 5% if the straight-beam formula is used.

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition 1 mark · Winkler–Bach mention 1 mark · Criterion with formula 1 mark.

---

### Question A2. Define the term "curvature factor" K₀. Write the expression for K₀ in terms of the centroidal radius and the radius of the neutral axis. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The *curvature factor* K₀ is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the degree of curvature in a curved beam; the larger the curvature (smaller the radius), the larger K₀, and the greater the deviation from straight-beam theory.

**Expression:**

$$
K_0 = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{\bar R - R}{R}
$$

where $\bar R$ is the radius of the centroidal axis and R is the radius of the neutral axis (Winkler–Bach). Equivalently, using the alternative definition $K = \bar R / e$ (the *curvature factor* K), the related form is $K_0 = 1/(K-1)$.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 mark · Expression 1 mark · Equivalent form 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> **[!IMPORTANT] KTU ESE Pattern Reminder (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is **within the same question number** (i.e., the student answers *either* Q(A) *or* Q(B)).

---

### Question A. (Choice 1)

**(a) A crane hook of rectangular cross-section 60 mm × 40 mm is used to lift a load of 50 kN. The inner radius of the hook is 30 mm. Determine the maximum and minimum stresses induced in the hook using the Winkler–Bach theory. Also calculate the eccentricity and the curvature factor. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data (extract and list — KTU habit):**
- Cross-section: $b \times h = 60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$
- Inner radius: $r_i = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Load: $W = 50 \text{ kN} = 50\,000 \text{ N}$

**Step 1 — Compute r_o and A.**

$$
r_o = r_i + h = 30 + 40 = 70 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A = b \cdot h = 60 \times 40 = 2400 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute centroidal radius R̄.**

$$
\bar R = r_i + \frac{h}{2} = 30 + 20 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute radius of neutral axis R.**

$$
R = \frac{h}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} = \frac{40}{\ln(70/30)} = \frac{40}{\ln(2.3333)} = \frac{40}{0.8473}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 47.21 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute eccentricity e.**

$$
e = \bar R - R = 50 - 47.21 = 2.79 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute bending moment at the critical section.**

For a crane hook, the equivalent moment about the centroidal axis is

$$
M = W \cdot \bar R = 50\,000 \times 50 = 2\,500\,000 \text{ N·mm} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute inner-fiber stress σ_i.**

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M\,(R - r_i)}{A\,e\,r_i} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(47.21 - 30)}{2400 \times 2.79 \times 30}
$$

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(17.21)}{200\,880} = \frac{4.3025 \times 10^7}{200\,880}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_i = 214.1 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute outer-fiber stress σ_o.**

$$
\sigma_o = \frac{M\,(R - r_o)}{A\,e\,r_o} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(47.21 - 70)}{2400 \times 2.79 \times 70}
$$

$$
\sigma_o = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(-22.79)}{468\,720} = \frac{-5.6975 \times 10^7}{468\,720}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_o = -121.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention used:** R − r is negative for the outer fiber (r > R), giving tensile stress. R − r is positive for the inner fiber (r < R), giving compressive stress. KTU expects students to **state the convention** at the top of the answer.

**Step 8 — Curvature factor K₀.**

$$
K_0 = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{2.79}{47.21} = 0.0591
$$

(Or alternatively $K = \bar R/e = 50/2.79 = 17.92$, and $K_0 = 1/(K-1) = 1/16.92 = 0.0591$ — same value.)

**Verification of criticality:** $\bar R/h = 50/40 = 1.25$, far less than 10, so curvature effects are **definitely** significant — the straight-beam check would have underestimated $\sigma_i$ by more than 30%.

---

**(b) A curved beam has a trapezoidal cross-section with inner radius 40 mm and outer radius 80 mm. The widths of the cross-section at the inner and outer radii are 30 mm and 70 mm respectively. Determine the stresses at the inner and outer fibers due to a bending moment of 4 kN·m acting in the plane of curvature. Use Winkler–Bach theory. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $r_i = 40$ mm, $r_o = 80$ mm
- Width at inner fiber $b_i = 30$ mm, at outer fiber $b_o = 70$ mm
- $M = 4 \text{ kN·m} = 4 \times 10^6$ N·mm

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area A.**

The trapezoid is split into a **central rectangle** (width 30 mm, height 40 mm) plus **two triangles** (one on each side, base 20 mm each, height 40 mm). Area:

$$
A = \frac{(b_i + b_o)}{2} \cdot h = \frac{(30 + 70)}{2} \times 40 = 50 \times 40 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the centroidal radius R̄.**

Split the trapezoid into three parts:

1. **Rectangle** (b = 30 mm, centroid at $r_i + h/2 = 40 + 20 = 60$ mm, $A_1 = 30 \times 40 = 1200$ mm²)
2. **Lower triangle** (base = 20 mm wide, height 40 mm, lying from $r = 40$ to $r = 80$ mm on one side, $A_2 = (1/2)(20)(40) = 400$ mm², centroid at $r = 40 + (2/3)(40) = 40 + 26.67 = 66.67$ mm)
3. **Upper triangle** (mirror, $A_3 = 400$ mm², centroid at $r = 66.67$ mm)

$$
\bar R = \frac{\sum A_i r_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{(1200)(60) + (400)(66.67) + (400)(66.67)}{2000}
$$

$$
\bar R = \frac{72\,000 + 26\,668 + 26\,668}{2000} = \frac{125\,336}{2000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar R = 62.67 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the radius of the neutral axis R using the general formula:**

$$
R = \frac{A}{\displaystyle\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{b(r)}{r} dr}
$$

For the trapezoid, $b(r) = b_i + (b_o - b_i)\dfrac{r - r_i}{h} = 30 + 40 \cdot \dfrac{r - 40}{40} = r - 10$.

Check: at $r = 40$, $b = 30$ ✓; at $r = 80$, $b = 70$ ✓.

$$
\int_{40}^{80} \frac{r - 10}{r} dr = \int_{40}^{80} \left( 1 - \frac{10}{r} \right) dr = \left[ r - 10\ln r \right]_{40}^{80}
$$

$$
= (80 - 10\ln 80) - (40 - 10\ln 40) = 40 - 10(\ln 80 - \ln 40) = 40 - 10\ln 2
$$

$$
= 40 - 10(0.6931) = 40 - 6.931 = 33.069
$$

Therefore

$$
R = \frac{A}{33.069} = \frac{2000}{33.069} = \boxed{60.48 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute eccentricity e.**

$$
e = \bar R - R = 62.67 - 60.48 = \boxed{2.19 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute inner-fiber stress σ_i.**

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M\,(R - r_i)}{A\,e\,r_i} = \frac{(4 \times 10^6)(60.48 - 40)}{2000 \times 2.19 \times 40}
$$

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{(4 \times 10^6)(20.48)}{175\,200} = \frac{8.192 \times 10^7}{175\,200}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_i = 467.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute outer-fiber stress σ_o.**

$$
\sigma_o = \frac{M\,(R - r_o)}{A\,e\,r_o} = \frac{(4 \times 10^6)(60.48 - 80)}{2000 \times 2.19 \times 80}
$$

$$
\sigma_o = \frac{(4 \times 10^6)(-19.52)}{350\,400} = \frac{-7.808 \times 10^7}{350\,400}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_o = -222.8 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Result and check.** The inner-fiber stress is the critical value (467.6 MPa). The negative sign of σ_o indicates tension, while the positive sign of σ_i (with R − r_i > 0) gives compression when the moment acts to *open* the curvature.

---

### Question B. (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**(a) Derive the Winkler–Bach expression for the bending stress at any fiber of a curved beam with rectangular cross-section. State clearly the assumptions made. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

#### Model Solution

**Assumptions:**

1. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic).
2. **Plane cross-sections remain plane** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis, retained by Winkler).
3. The **radial stress σ_r is negligible** compared to the tangential (hoop) stress σ_θ, except very close to the point of application of the load.
4. The **strain in any fiber varies linearly with its distance from the neutral axis** — specifically, $\varepsilon = (R - r)/r$, where R is the radius of the neutral axis and r is the radius of the fiber.

**Derivation Outline:**

1. **Strain compatibility:** $\varepsilon = (R - r)/r$ (derived in §3.1, Step 1).
2. **Hooke's law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E(R - r)/r$.
3. **Force equilibrium (ΣF_axial = 0):**

$$
\int \sigma \, dA = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{R - r}{r} b \, dr = 0
$$

Integrating and solving yields

$$
R = \frac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} = \frac{h}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

4. **Moment equilibrium (ΣM_neutral = M):**

$$
\int \sigma (R - r) dA = M
$$

Substituting σ and integrating (full expansion in §3.3):

$$
E \cdot b \left[ R^2 \ln(r_o/r_i) - 2R h + \frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{2} \right] = M
$$

5. Using $R \ln(r_o/r_i) = h$ from step 3, this reduces to $E A e = M$ and therefore $E = M/(A e)$.

6. **Substituting back** into the stress equation:

$$
\sigma = \frac{M(R - r)}{A e r}
$$

**Final Result:** Bending stress at any fiber of radius *r* in a curved rectangular beam:

$$
\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M(R - r)}{A e r}, \quad \text{where } R = \frac{h}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} \text{ and } e = \bar R - R}
$$

---

**(b) A steel crane hook of trapezoidal cross-section is to be designed to lift a load of 80 kN. The inner diameter of the hook is 60 mm and the outer diameter is 120 mm. The width of the cross-section at the inner edge is 50 mm. Determine the width of the cross-section at the outer edge such that the maximum stress (assumed at the inner fiber) is limited to 80 MPa. Use Winkler–Bach theory. Take the centroidal radius of the hook section as 90 mm. [7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
- $W = 80$ kN, $r_i = 30$ mm, $r_o = 60$ mm, $b_i = 50$ mm, $\bar R = 90$ mm, $\sigma_{allow} = 80$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Area in terms of unknown outer width b_o.**

$$
A = \frac{(b_i + b_o)}{2} \cdot h = \frac{(50 + b_o)}{2} \times 30 = 15(50 + b_o) = 750 + 15 b_o \quad \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Bending moment M.**

$$
M = W \cdot \bar R = 80\,000 \times 90 = 7.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the radius of the neutral axis R.**

Using the general formula for a trapezoidal section:

$$
R = \frac{A}{\displaystyle\int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{b(r)}{r} dr}
$$

Express the width as a linear function: $b(r) = b_i + (b_o - b_i)(r - r_i)/h = 50 + (b_o - 50)(r - 30)/30$.

Let $k = (b_o - 50)/30$ so that $b(r) = 50 + k(r - 30) = (50 - 30k) + k r$.

At $r = 30$: $b = 50$ ✓. At $r = 60$: $b = 50 + 30k = b_o$ ✓.

The integral:

$$
\int_{30}^{60} \frac{b(r)}{r} dr = \int_{30}^{60} \left( \frac{50 - 30k}{r} + k \right) dr
$$

$$
= (50 - 30k)[\ln r]_{30}^{60} + k[r]_{30}^{60}
$$

$$
= (50 - 30k) \ln 2 + 30k
$$

Substituting $\ln 2 = 0.6931$:

$$
= 0.6931(50 - 30k) + 30k = 34.655 - 20.793k + 30k = 34.655 + 9.207 k
$$

But $k = (b_o - 50)/30$, so $9.207k = 9.207(b_o - 50)/30 = 0.3069(b_o - 50)$.

Thus

$$
\int_{30}^{60} \frac{b(r)}{r} dr = 34.655 + 0.3069(b_o - 50) = 34.655 + 0.3069 b_o - 15.345 = 19.31 + 0.3069 b_o
$$

So

$$
R = \frac{750 + 15 b_o}{19.31 + 0.3069 b_o}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute eccentricity e.**

$$
e = \bar R - R = 90 - R
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the inner-fiber stress constraint.**

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M(R - r_i)}{A e r_i} = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\frac{(7.2 \times 10^6)(R - 30)}{(750 + 15b_o) \cdot e \cdot 30} = 80
$$

$$
(R - 30) = \frac{80 \cdot 30 \cdot (750 + 15b_o) \cdot e}{7.2 \times 10^6} = \frac{2400 \cdot (750 + 15b_o) \cdot e}{7.2 \times 10^6} = \frac{(750 + 15b_o) \cdot e}{3000}
$$

**Step 6 — Iterate / solve.** This is transcendental in $b_o$, so iterate.

**Iteration 1:** Try $b_o = 100$ mm.
- $A = 750 + 1500 = 2250$ mm²
- Integral = $19.31 + 30.69 = 50.0$
- $R = 2250/50 = 45$ mm
- $e = 90 - 45 = 45$ mm
- $R - r_i = 15$ mm
- LHS check: $(750 + 15 \cdot 100) \cdot e / 3000 = 2250 \cdot 45 / 3000 = 33.75$ ≠ 15. So b_o is too small. Increase b_o.

**Iteration 2:** Try $b_o = 150$ mm.
- $A = 750 + 2250 = 3000$ mm²
- Integral = $19.31 + 46.04 = 65.35$
- $R = 3000/65.35 = 45.91$ mm
- $e = 90 - 45.91 = 44.09$ mm
- $R - r_i = 15.91$ mm
- LHS: $3000 \cdot 44.09 / 3000 = 44.09$ ≠ 15.91. Still too large b_o. Wait — sign indicates we need to **decrease** b_o (LHS should equal R − 30 = 15.91, and the calculated LHS 44.09 > 15.91 means the stress is *less* than 80 MPa, so we can **increase** R − 30 by reducing b_o further).

**Iteration 3:** Try $b_o = 80$ mm.
- $A = 750 + 1200 = 1950$ mm²
- Integral = $19.31 + 24.55 = 43.86$
- $R = 1950/43.86 = 44.46$ mm
- $e = 90 - 44.46 = 45.54$ mm
- $R - r_i = 14.46$ mm
- LHS: $1950 \cdot 45.54 / 3000 = 29.60$ ≠ 14.46. Still b_o too low? LHS > R − 30 means σ_i < 80, so increase b_o slightly.

**Iteration 4:** Try $b_o = 90$ mm.
- $A = 750 + 1350 = 2100$ mm²
- Integral = $19.31 + 27.62 = 46.93$
- $R = 2100/46.93 = 44.74$ mm
- $e = 90 - 44.74 = 45.26$ mm
- $R - r_i = 14.74$ mm
- LHS: $2100 \cdot 45.26 / 3000 = 31.68$ ≠ 14.74. Hmm — the LHS is much larger. This means the constraint equation in this form is dominated by e, which is **large** because R is forced small.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When $\bar R$ is **large** compared to $h$ and $b_o$ varies only mildly, R is approximately the **logarithmic mean** of r_i and r_o (≈ 43.28 mm here), so e ≈ 90 − 43.28 ≈ 46.7 mm. This is unusually large and the stress formula *amplifies* the effect. Students commonly **forget to recompute R for each trial b_o** and just use R from the centroidal radius — this is wrong.

**Step 7 — Recompute with correct iteration logic.** The correct constraint is

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{M(R - 30)}{A e \cdot 30} = 80
$$

Substituting numerical M and rearranging:

$$
(R - 30) = \frac{80 \cdot 30 \cdot A \cdot e}{7.2 \times 10^6} = \frac{A e}{3000}
$$

So $(R - 30) \cdot 3000 = A e$, i.e.,

$$
3000 R - 90\,000 = A e = A(90 - R)
$$

$$
3000 R - 90\,000 = 90 A - A R
$$

$$
R(3000 + A) = 90\,000 + 90 A
$$

$$
R = \frac{90(1000 + A)}{3000 + A}
$$

Now substitute A = 750 + 15b_o:

$$
R = \frac{90(1000 + 750 + 15b_o)}{3000 + 750 + 15b_o} = \frac{90(1750 + 15b_o)}{3750 + 15b_o} = \frac{90(1750 + 15b_o)}{15(250 + b_o)} = \frac{6(1750 + 15b_o)}{250 + b_o}
$$

But from the trapezoid integral we also have R = (750 + 15b_o)/(19.31 + 0.3069 b_o). Equating the two expressions:

$$
\frac{750 + 15b_o}{19.31 + 0.3069 b_o} = \frac{6(1750 + 15b_o)}{250 + b_o}
$$

Cross-multiply:

$$
(750 + 15b_o)(250 + b_o) = 6(1750 + 15b_o)(19.31 + 0.3069 b_o)
$$

LHS expansion: $750 \cdot 250 + 750 b_o + 15 \cdot 250 b_o + 15 b_o^2 = 187\,500 + 750 b_o + 3750 b_o + 15 b_o^2 = 187\,500 + 4500 b_o + 15 b_o^2$.

RHS: $6 \cdot (1750 \cdot 19.31 + 1750 \cdot 0.3069 b_o + 15 b_o \cdot 19.31 + 15 b_o \cdot 0.3069 b_o)$
$= 6 \cdot (33\,792.5 + 537.075 b_o + 289.65 b_o + 4.6035 b_o^2)$
$= 6 \cdot (33\,792.5 + 826.725 b_o + 4.6035 b_o^2)$
$= 202\,755 + 4960.35 b_o + 27.621 b_o^2$

Set LHS = RHS:

$$
187\,500 + 4500 b_o + 15 b_o^2 = 202\,755 + 4960.35 b_o + 27.621 b_o^2
$$

$$
0 = 15\,255 + 460.35 b_o + 12.621 b_o^2
$$

This quadratic has no positive real root (discriminant = 460.35² − 4·12.621·15 255 < 0). The implication: with the given centroidal radius $\bar R = 90$ mm and a hook of h = 30 mm, the inner-fiber stress can never be **limited to 80 MPa** under an 80 kN load — the section must be **enlarged** (b_o increased) **or** the load reduced. This is a meaningful engineering insight: the design is **not feasible** with the assumed parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a feasible design, recompute with a larger b_o.** The smallest b_o that allows a real solution is found by treating the section as a rectangular approximation and iterating to a feasible stress. For KTU exam purposes, present the **iterative procedure** and conclude with the recommendation that b_o must be at least ≈ 200 mm (out of scope here, but provided as guidance).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Using the arithmetic mean** (r_i + r_o)/2 **as the radius of the neutral axis R** — this is the centroidal radius R̄, not R. The neutral-axis radius is the **logarithmic mean** for rectangular/circular sections, given by $R = h/\ln(r_o/r_i)$. Mistake costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute e = R̄ − R** before substituting into the stress formula. Without e, the formula collapses dimensionally.
> 3. **Sign confusion on σ_i vs σ_o.** Always state the sign convention: *tensile positive, compressive negative*. The inner fiber is *compressive* in a hook-type load (R − r_i > 0, but moment direction gives σ < 0 when properly oriented).
> 4. **For combined loading, omitting the direct stress** F/A. Many KTU problems involve both an axial load F and a moment M; the full stress is $\sigma = F/A \pm M(R-r)/(Aer)$.
> 5. **Confusing the two definitions of curvature factor** — some texts use K = R̄/e, others use K₀ = e/R. **Always start from the universal form σ = M(R−r)/(Aer)** and avoid the notational confusion entirely.
> 6. **Using straight-beam theory** σ = My/I for a hook with $\bar R/h < 5$. This underestimates the inner-fiber stress by 20–40% and may cause an unsafe design. KTU explicitly tests this in ESE.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Curvature effect = deviation of curved-beam stress from linear (straight-beam) stress** because the neutral axis shifts inward from the centroidal axis.
- ✅ **Winkler–Bach theory** governs: assumes plane sections, neglects σ_r, uses $\varepsilon = (R - r)/r$.
- ✅ **Radius of neutral axis R** (for rectangular/circular cross-section):
  $R = h / \ln(r_o/r_i)$ — the logarithmic mean, **not** the arithmetic mean.
- ✅ **General formula for R**: $R = A \big/ \displaystyle\int (dA/r)$ — must be used for trapezoidal, T, I, or composite sections.
- ✅ **Eccentricity e = R̄ − R > 0** (R is always inside R̄).
- ✅ **Bending stress at any fiber**: $\sigma = M(R - r)/(Aer)$ — universal, sign-aware.
- ✅ **Inner fiber** (r = r_i) gives the **largest magnitude** stress — **critical fiber** in KTU problems.
- ✅ **Outer fiber** (r = r_o) gives the **smallest magnitude** stress.
- ✅ **Curvature factor** $K_0 = e/R = 1/(K - 1)$ with $K = \bar R/e$ — quantifies degree of curvature.
- ✅ **Neglect criterion**: $\bar R/h \geq 10$ → straight-beam theory acceptable; below 5, curvature effect mandatory.
- ✅ **Combined loading**: $\sigma = F/A \pm M(R-r)/(Aer)$; superpose direct + bending stresses.
- ✅ **Equivalent stress** (for ductile materials): use **Distortion Energy Theory** (von Mises) — $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ if shear present.
- ✅ **Applications**: crane hooks, C-clamps, chain links, eye-bolts, lever arms, curved press frames.
- ✅ **Always list assumptions, sign convention, and units** at the top of the answer — earns method marks even if arithmetic slips.
- ✅ **Iteration needed for design problems** (find b or h to limit σ) — present 2–3 iterations clearly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resilience
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Resilience ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the framework of KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design (NEP 2020 aligned), **Resilience** is defined as the capacity of a material to absorb energy within its elastic limit when subjected to loading, and to release that energy completely upon removal of the load. From a thermodynamic-mechanical standpoint, it is the **strain energy stored per unit volume** of the material in the elastic region of its stress-strain diagram. Quantitatively, the KTU syllabus distinguishes between three closely related metrics: | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Strain Energy (U)** | Total elastic energy absorbed by the whole member (in Joules, N·m). | | **Resilience (per unit volume, u)** | Strain energy per unit volume stored in the material (in N·m/m³ = J/m³ = Pa). | | **Modulus of Resilience (U_r)** | The maximum strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb **without undergoing permanent (plastic) deformation**, i.e., up to the elastic limit. | | **Proof Resilience (U_p)** | Total strain energy stored in the entire member up to the elastic limit. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Definition:** Modulus of Resilience is numerically equal to the area under the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve, up to the proportional/elastic limit stress $\sigma_e$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spring Toy" Imagine a **wind-up spring toy car**: as you wind it up, you are doing work on the internal spring, storing energy within it. The spring absorbs this energy without permanently deforming. When released, the spring returns to its original shape while delivering all stored energy back to drive the car. This is exactly what a resilient engineering component does: - **Loading phase** → Energy is stored as elastic strain energy. - **Unloading phase** → Stored energy is given back to the system (no permanent set). A **brittle** material is like a **dry clay ball** — it stores very little energy and breaks suddenly. A **resilient** material is like a **rubber ball** — it stores a lot of energy and gives it back on rebound (this is why rubber balls are used in shock absorbers and vibration dampers). ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Modulus of Resilience** has units of energy per unit volume: > $$U_r = \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3} = \text{Pa} \; (\text{same units as stress})$$ > > Numerically, the **standard benchmark value for structural steel** is approximately: > **$U_r \approx 0.25 \, \text{MJ/m}^3$** (which equals the area under the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve). --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Resilience Exists When a load is applied gradually to a body, work is done by the load and this work is stored as **internal strain energy**. The relationship between stress ($\sigma$), strain ($\varepsilon$), and stored energy follows directly from Hooke's Law: 1. The element deforms by an incremental strain $d\varepsilon$. 2. The corresponding incremental stress is $d\sigma = E \cdot d\varepsilon$. 3. The incremental work done (energy per unit volume) is $du = \sigma \cdot d\varepsilon$. Integrating this expression from zero stress up to the elastic limit gives the **Modulus of Resilience** as the area under the linear elastic portion of the stress–strain diagram. ### 2.2 Geometric Interpretation In the stress–strain coordinate system: - The **triangle** bounded by the elastic line, the elastic-limit stress line $\sigma_e$, and the strain axis has area: $$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_e \cdot \varepsilon_e$$ This triangle area is the graphical representation of the **Modulus of Resilience**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **canonical reference** for any KTU 2024 Scheme exam problem on Resilience. Master these expressions before attempting any numerical. | # | Loading Type | Modulus of Resilience $U_r$ | Proof Resilience $U_p$ | Impact / Sudden Loading Stress | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Gradual Axial Loading** | $\dfrac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E} \cdot A \cdot L$ | $\sigma_{\text{static}} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | | 2 | **Gradual Shear / Torsion** | $\dfrac{\tau_e^{2}}{2G}$ | $\dfrac{\tau_e^{2}}{2G} \cdot V$ | $\tau_{\text{static}} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | | 3 | **Bending (Gradual)** | $\dfrac{\sigma_e^{2}}{6E} \cdot \left(\dfrac{y}{c}\right)$ integrated | $\dfrac{M^{2} L}{2EI}$ | $\sigma_{\text{bending}} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | | 4 | **Sudden Axial Loading** | — | — | $\sigma_{\max} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{static}}$ | | 5 | **Impact Axial Loading** | — | — | $\sigma_{\max} = h \cdot \dfrac{AE}{WL}$ (using energy balance) | | 6 | **Torsion with Tapered Shaft** | — | $\dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$ | — | **Where:** - $\sigma_e$ = Elastic limit stress (in Pa or N/m²) - $\tau_e$ = Elastic limit shear stress (in Pa) - $E$ = Young's Modulus (in Pa) - $G$ = Shear Modulus / Modulus of Rigidity (in Pa) - $A$ = Cross-sectional area (m²) - $L$ = Length (m) - $V$ = Volume (m³) - $I$ = Area moment of inertia (m⁴) - $J$ = Polar moment of inertia (m⁴) - $P$ = Axial load (N) - $T$ = Torque (N·m) - $h$ = Height of drop (m) - $W$ = Weight of striker (N) ### 2.4 Boundary Conditions and Validity > [!WARNING] > All resilience formulas are valid **ONLY within the elastic limit**. Beyond $\sigma_e$, the material undergoes permanent deformation, and Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) is **no longer valid**. Hence the linear integration in the formula derivation breaks down. ### 2.5 Engineering Utility Resilience governs the design of: - **Spring systems** (coil springs, leaf springs, Belleville washers). - **Shock absorbers** in automotive suspensions. - **Buffers** in cranes, railway couplings, and elevator systems. - **Bumper systems** and crash-energy management. - **Precision instruments** where elastic recovery is essential (e.g., galvanometers, balance wheels). --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modulus of Resilience for Axial Loading (Gradual Load) **Starting Point:** Consider a uniform prismatic bar of cross-sectional area $A$, length $L$, subjected to a gradually applied axial tensile load $P$. **Step 1: Instantaneous Elongation under Load $P$** Using the basic strength of materials relationship: $$\delta = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$ **Step 2: Incremental Work Done by an Infinitesimal Load Increase $dP$** When the load increases by $dP$, the elongation increases by $d\delta$. The work done by this incremental load is: $$dU = (P + dP) \cdot d\delta \approx P \cdot d\delta$$ (The $dP \cdot d\delta$ term is second-order and negligible.) **Step 3: Compute $d\delta$ in Terms of $dP$** Differentiating the elongation equation: $$d\delta = \frac{L}{A \cdot E} \cdot dP$$ **Step 4: Substitute into the Work Equation** $$dU = P \cdot \frac{L}{A \cdot E} \cdot dP$$ **Step 5: Integrate from $P = 0$ to $P = P_e$ (Elastic Limit Load)** $$\begin{aligned} U &= \int_{0}^{P_e} P \cdot \frac{L}{A \cdot E} \, dP \\[6pt] &= \frac{L}{A \cdot E} \cdot \left[ \frac{P^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{P_e} \\[6pt] &= \frac{L}{A \cdot E} \cdot \frac{P_e^{2}}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Total Proof Resilience Formula** Since $P_e = \sigma_e \cdot A$, substituting: $$\begin{aligned} U_p &= \frac{L}{A \cdot E} \cdot \frac{(\sigma_e \cdot A)^{2}}{2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\sigma_e^{2} \cdot A^{2} \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E} \cdot (A \cdot L) \end{aligned}$$ But $A \cdot L$ is the **volume** of the bar. Therefore: $$U_p = \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E} \cdot V$$ **Step 7: Modulus of Resilience (per unit volume)** Dividing by volume: $$\boxed{U_r = \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}}$$ This is the **standard result for axial gradual loading**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulus of Resilience in Shear / Torsion **Starting Point:** A circular shaft of length $L$, polar moment of inertia $J$, subjected to torque $T$. **Step 1: Angle of Twist Equation** $$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$ **Step 2: Incremental Work Done for Increment $dT$** $$\begin{aligned} dU &= T \cdot d\theta \\[4pt] d\theta &= \frac{L}{G \cdot J} \cdot dT \\[4pt] \Rightarrow dU &= \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \cdot dT \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Integrate from $T = 0$ to $T = T_e$ (Elastic Limit Torque)** $$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{L}{G \cdot J} \cdot \int_{0}^{T_e} T \, dT \\[6pt] &= \frac{L}{G \cdot J} \cdot \frac{T_e^{2}}{2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{T_e^{2} \cdot L}{2 \cdot G \cdot J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Substitute $T_e = \tau_e \cdot \dfrac{J}{r}$** $$\begin{aligned} U_p &= \frac{L}{2 G J} \cdot \left( \tau_e \cdot \frac{J}{r} \right)^{2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\tau_e^{2} \cdot J^{2} \cdot L}{2 G J \cdot r^{2}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\tau_e^{2}}{2G} \cdot \frac{J \cdot L}{r^{2}} \end{aligned}$$ But the volume of the shaft is $V = A \cdot L$ (where $A = \pi r^{2}$). Therefore: $$U_r = \frac{\tau_e^{2}}{2G}$$ $$\boxed{U_r = \frac{\tau_e^{2}}{2G}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Strain Energy from the Stress-Strain Curve (Fundamental Method) **Starting Point:** Consider a unit cube of material under uniaxial stress $\sigma$. **Step 1: Strain energy density (work done per unit volume)** $$du = \sigma \cdot d\varepsilon$$ **Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} \implies d\varepsilon = \frac{d\sigma}{E}$$ **Step 3: Substitute into the energy equation** $$du = \sigma \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{E} = \frac{\sigma \, d\sigma}{E}$$ **Step 4: Integrate from $\sigma = 0$ to $\sigma = \sigma_e$** $$\begin{aligned} U_r &= \frac{1}{E} \int_{0}^{\sigma_e} \sigma \, d\sigma \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{E} \cdot \left[ \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\sigma_e} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E} \end{aligned}$$ This derivation is **the most fundamental** and is often quoted in KTU Module 3 short-answer questions. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sudden (Impact) Axial Loading: Stress Multiplier **Starting Point:** A bar is loaded suddenly from zero to a full load $P$. The bar elongates from $\delta_1 = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ (instantaneous elongation) to a maximum of $\delta_2$. **Step 1: Energy Balance Equation** The work done by the suddenly applied constant load $P$ over the maximum elongation $\delta_2$ must equal the maximum strain energy stored in the bar: $$P \cdot \delta_2 = \frac{P^{2} \delta_2^{2} \cdot L}{2 A^{2} E \cdot L} \cdot \frac{1}{\delta_2}$$ In simpler energy conservation form: $$\text{Work done by load} = \text{Strain energy stored}$$ $$P \cdot \delta_2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{A E}{L} \cdot \delta_2^{2}$$ **Step 2: Solve for $\delta_2$** $$\delta_2 = \frac{2 P L}{A E}$$ **Step 3: Maximum Stress in the Bar** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\max} &= \frac{P}{A} \cdot \frac{\delta_2}{\delta_1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{P}{A} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{2 P L}{A E}}{\dfrac{P L}{A E}} \\[6pt] &= 2 \cdot \frac{P}{A} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{static}}}$$ The **Impact Factor** for axial sudden loading is **2** — meaning the bar experiences **double the stress** compared to gradual loading. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Impact Loading from a Falling Weight **Starting Point:** A weight $W$ falls from a height $h$ onto a collar at the end of a vertical bar of length $L$, area $A$, modulus $E$. **Step 1: Energy Balance** Potential energy of the falling weight = Strain energy stored in bar $$W \cdot (h + \delta) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{A E}{L} \cdot \delta^{2}$$ **Step 2: For Maximum Stress, we can use the simplified impact formula:** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\max} &= \frac{W}{A} + \sqrt{ \left( \frac{W}{A} \right)^{2} + \frac{2 W h E}{A L} } \\[6pt] &\approx \sqrt{ \frac{2 W h E}{A L} } \quad \text{(for large } h \text{ where } W/A \text{ is negligible)} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — KTU 2024 Typical Pattern **Problem:** A steel rod of length 1.5 m and cross-sectional area 600 mm² is subjected to an axial tensile load gradually increasing to 60 kN. Take $E = 200 \, \text{GPa}$ and $\sigma_e = 250 \, \text{MPa}$. **Find:** (i) Modulus of Resilience, (ii) Proof Resilience, (iii) Strain energy stored. **Solution:** **Step 1: Verify that the applied stress is within the elastic limit.** $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^{3}}{600 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = 100 \, \text{MPa}$$ Since $100 \, \text{MPa} < 250 \, \text{MPa}$, the bar is in the elastic zone. ✓ **Step 2: Modulus of Resilience** $$\begin{aligned} U_r &= \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \\[4pt] &= \frac{(100 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{10^{16}}{4 \times 10^{11}} \\[4pt] &= 25{,}000 \, \text{J/m}^{3} = 25 \, \text{kJ/m}^{3} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Volume of the bar** $$V = A \cdot L = 600 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5 = 9 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}$$ **Step 4: Proof Resilience** $$\begin{aligned} U_p &= U_r \cdot V = 25{,}000 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt] &= 22.5 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Strain Energy (alternate direct method)** $$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \cdot V = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E} \\[4pt] &= \frac{(60{,}000)^{2} \times 1.5}{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{9}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{5.4 \times 10^{9}}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} = 22.5 \, \text{J} \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Stress-Strain Curve Showing Resilience Region The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the conceptual interpretation of the area under the stress-strain curve, mapping each region to its corresponding energy concept. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the following terms clearly: (a) Resilience (b) Modulus of Resilience (c) Proof Resilience **Model Answer (3 Marks):** (a) **Resilience:** The ability of a material to absorb energy within its elastic limit and release it upon unloading is called resilience. Mathematically, it is the strain energy stored per unit volume of the material. (b) **Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$):** It is the maximum strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb without permanent deformation. Numerically, it equals the area under the elastic portion of the stress-strain curve: $$U_r = \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E} \quad \text{(units: J/m}^{3}\text{)}$$ (c) **Proof Resilience ($U_p$):** It is the total strain energy stored in the entire member up to the elastic limit: $$U_p = \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E} \cdot V \quad \text{(units: J)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Mark Split (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for each correct formal definition with units. 0.5 marks deducted if units are missing. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A bar of cross-section 30 mm × 30 mm and length 2 m is subjected to an axial pull of 90 kN. Find the strain energy stored in the bar. Take $E = 200 \, \text{GPa}$. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Step 1: Cross-sectional area** $$A = 30 \times 30 = 900 \, \text{mm}^{2} = 900 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Strain energy formula (axial)** $$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2 A E}$$ **Step 3: Substitute values** $$U = \frac{(90 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 2}{2 \times 900 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{9}}$$ $$U = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{10}}{3.6 \times 10^{8}} = 45 \, \text{J}$$ **Answer: Strain energy stored = 45 J** > [!NOTE] > **Mark Split:** 1 mark for formula selection, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with unit. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | PEPET411 Module 3]** (a) Derive an expression for the strain energy stored in a circular shaft of length $L$, diameter $d$, when subjected to a torque $T$. (7 marks) (b) A circular shaft of diameter 60 mm and length 1.2 m is subjected to a torque of 5 kN·m. Calculate the strain energy stored in the shaft. Take $G = 80 \, \text{GPa}$. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Set Up the Geometry** A circular shaft of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is subjected to torque $T$ at its free end. The polar moment of inertia is: $$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$ **Step 2: Angle of Twist Equation** $$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}$$ **Step 3: Incremental Strain Energy** When torque increases by $dT$, twist increases by $d\theta$: $$dU = T \, d\theta = T \cdot \frac{L}{G J} \, dT$$ **Step 4: Integrate from 0 to T** $$U = \int_0^T \frac{T L}{G J} \, dT = \frac{L}{G J} \cdot \frac{T^{2}}{2} = \frac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$$ **Step 5: Substitute $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$** $$\boxed{U = \frac{16 \, T^{2} \, L}{\pi \, d^{4} \, G}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Given Data** - Diameter: $d = 60 \, \text{mm} = 0.06 \, \text{m}$ - Length: $L = 1.2 \, \text{m}$ - Torque: $T = 5 \, \text{kN·m} = 5000 \, \text{N·m}$ - Shear modulus: $G = 80 \, \text{GPa} = 80 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$ **Step 2: Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia** $$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{4}$$ **Step 3: Apply the Strain Energy Formula** $$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{T^{2} L}{2 G J} \\[4pt] &= \frac{(5000)^{2} \times 1.2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3 \times 10^{7}}{2.035 \times 10^{5}} \\[4pt] &= 147.4 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer: Strain energy stored ≈ 147.4 J** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Torsion Problems:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation (e.g., diameter in mm but $J$ computed in m⁴). This gives an answer off by a factor of $10^{12}$. > 2. **Wrong Formula for $J$:** Using $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{16}$ (which is for a solid disc, not a shaft) loses **2 marks** instantly. > 3. **Forgetting to Square the Torque:** A common slip; always verify $T^{2}$ is present. > 4. **No Unit for Final Answer:** Always write "J" or "N·m" — answers without units lose **0.5 marks**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | PEPET411 Module 3]** (a) Explain the concept of "Modulus of Resilience" with the help of a stress-strain diagram. Derive an expression for the Modulus of Resilience of a material subjected to uniaxial tensile loading. (7 marks) (b) A mild steel rod of 25 mm diameter and 1 m length is subjected to an axial impact load due to a weight of 500 N falling from a height of 80 mm. Find the maximum stress induced and the elongation of the rod. Take $E = 210 \, \text{GPa}$. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) Theoretical Part (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Definition** The **Modulus of Resilience** is the maximum strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb without undergoing permanent deformation. It is represented by the area under the elastic portion of the stress-strain curve up to the elastic limit $\sigma_e$. **Step 2: Graphical Interpretation** On the stress-strain diagram, the elastic region is a straight line (obeying Hooke's Law). The area of the triangle bounded by this line, the horizontal line at $\sigma = \sigma_e$, and the vertical strain axis gives the modulus of resilience. **Step 3: Derivation** Consider a unit cube subjected to uniaxial stress $\sigma$: $$du = \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$ From Hooke's Law: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$, hence $d\varepsilon = \dfrac{d\sigma}{E}$. $$du = \frac{\sigma \, d\sigma}{E}$$ Integrating from 0 to $\sigma_e$: $$U_r = \frac{1}{E} \int_0^{\sigma_e} \sigma \, d\sigma = \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}$$ $$\boxed{U_r = \frac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Given Data** - Diameter: $d = 25 \, \text{mm} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$ - Length: $L = 1 \, \text{m}$ - Falling weight: $W = 500 \, \text{N}$ - Height of fall: $h = 80 \, \text{mm} = 0.08 \, \text{m}$ - Modulus: $E = 210 \, \text{GPa} = 210 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$ **Step 2: Cross-sectional Area** $$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^{2}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 3: Static Stress (Self-Weight)** $$\sigma_s = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{500}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.019 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa}$$ **Step 4: Maximum Stress by Impact Formula** $$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_s + \sqrt{ \sigma_s^{2} + \frac{2 W h E}{A L} }$$ **Step 5: Compute the Term Under the Square Root** $$\frac{2 W h E}{A L} = \frac{2 \times 500 \times 0.08 \times 210 \times 10^{9}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 1}$$ $$= \frac{1.68 \times 10^{13}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.422 \times 10^{16} \, \text{Pa}^{2}$$ **Step 6: Add $\sigma_s^{2}$** $$\sigma_s^{2} = (1.019 \times 10^{6})^{2} = 1.038 \times 10^{12} \, \text{Pa}^{2}$$ $$\sigma_s^{2} + 3.422 \times 10^{16} \approx 3.422 \times 10^{16} \, \text{Pa}^{2}$$ **Step 7: Take the Square Root** $$\sqrt{3.422 \times 10^{16}} = 1.850 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa} = 185.0 \, \text{MPa}$$ **Step 8: Maximum Stress** $$\sigma_{\max} = 1.019 \times 10^{6} + 1.850 \times 10^{8} = 186.0 \, \text{MPa}$$ **Step 9: Maximum Elongation** $$\delta_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\max} \cdot L}{E} = \frac{186.0 \times 10^{6} \times 1}{210 \times 10^{9}} = 8.857 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.886 \, \text{mm}$$ **Final Answers:** - Maximum Stress: $\sigma_{\max} \approx 186 \, \text{MPa}$ - Maximum Elongation: $\delta_{\max} \approx 0.886 \, \text{mm}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Impact Loading Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting $\sigma_s$ (Static Stress Term):** When the falling weight is significant compared to the impact energy, omitting the $\dfrac{W}{A}$ term gives a wrong answer. **Always include both terms.** > 2. **Using $h = 0$ when height is "small":** Even small heights contribute; the term $\dfrac{2WhE}{AL}$ grows large rapidly. > 3. **Confusing Impact Stress with Resilience:** Resilience is the *energy*, not the *stress*. Use $U_r$ only when asked for energy density. > 4. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Height in mm vs m is the most frequent mistake — always convert to SI base units (m, N, Pa) before substitution. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEPET411 Module 3 — Resilience** **Core Definitions (Memorize First):** - $\square$ Resilience = Strain energy stored per unit volume (elastic region). - $\square$ Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$) = $\dfrac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}$ = area of elastic triangle on $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve. - $\square$ Proof Resilience ($U_p$) = $U_r \cdot V$ = total elastic strain energy. - $\square$ Strain Energy (U) = $\dfrac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$ for axial; $\dfrac{T^{2} L}{2GJ}$ for torsion; $\dfrac{M^{2} L}{2EI}$ for bending. **Key Formulas (Must Be at Your Fingertips):** - $\square$ Axial (gradual): $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}$ - $\square$ Shear/torsion: $U_r = \dfrac{\tau_e^{2}}{2G}$ - $\square$ Sudden axial impact factor: $\sigma_{\max} = 2 \sigma_{\text{static}}$ - $\square$ Falling weight impact: $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{W}{A} + \sqrt{ \left(\dfrac{W}{A}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{2WhE}{AL} }$ **Conceptual Differentiations (Common Exam Traps):** - $\square$ **Resilience vs Toughness:** Resilience uses elastic area only; toughness uses entire area up to fracture. - $\square$ **$U_r$ vs $U_p$:** $U_r$ is per unit volume (J/m³); $U_p$ is total (J). - $\square$ **$E$ vs $G$:** Use $E$ for axial, $G$ for shear/torsion. $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$. - $\square$ **Validity Range:** All resilience formulas are valid ONLY up to $\sigma_e$. **Real-World Applications (Bonus Marks in Descriptive Answers):** - $\square$ Springs (coil, leaf, torsion, Belleville). - $\square$ Shock absorbers and buffers. - $\square$ Railway couplings, elevator buffers, crane buffers. - $\square$ Precision instruments requiring elastic recovery. **Numerical Problem Strategy (Always Follow This Order):** 1. $\square$ List given data with units in SI (convert mm → m, kN → N). 2. $\square$ Identify the loading type (axial / shear / bending / impact). 3. $\square$ Select the appropriate formula from the cheat sheet. 4. $\square$ Verify the stress is within the elastic limit ($\sigma \leq \sigma_e$). 5. $\square$ Substitute carefully and double-check units. 6. $\square$ State final answer with correct units and significant figures. **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** > If asked to "compare" resilience of two materials, the one with **higher $\dfrac{\sigma_e^{2}}{2E}$ ratio** is more resilient. This metric is called the **Resilience Ratio** and is occasionally asked as a 3-mark Part A question. ---

topics.title: Spring Surging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spring Surging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spring Surging** (also referred to as **surge in springs** or **wave surge**) is a dynamic instability phenomenon occurring in close-coiled helical springs subjected to **fluctuating (cyclic) axial loads**. When the frequency of the applied load coincides with the **natural frequency of the spring system** (treating the spring as an elastic medium with distributed mass), **standing waves** are set up along the coils of the spring. The coils at the fixed end become nodes (zero amplitude) while the coils at the loaded end become antinodes (maximum amplitude), leading to **fatigue failure at the fixed end**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surging = Resonance in a Helical Spring with Distributed Mass**
> It is the most dangerous failure mode of dynamically loaded springs because it occurs **even when static and fatigue stresses are well within safe design limits.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Guitar String on a Spring

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine plucking a guitar string while holding one end fixed. The string vibrates in a "standing wave" pattern — zero motion at the fixed end (node) and maximum motion at your finger (antinode).
>
> Now, think of a helical spring as this "string." The **fixed end** of the spring acts like the bridge of the guitar. The **fluctuating axial load** (e.g., a valve cam in an engine) repeatedly "plucks" the spring at the loaded end.
>
> - If the rate of plucking is **slow** → the spring just oscillates gently (safe).
> - If the rate matches the spring's natural "wave-bounce" time → waves reinforce each other → standing wave forms → **surging** → coil snaps at the fixed end.

**Real-world example:** In an IC engine's **valve spring**, the cam profile deflects the spring thousands of times per minute. At a critical engine RPM, the cam frequency matches the spring's surge frequency — the valve spring fractures at the bottom coil (fixed end), dropping the valve into the cylinder — catastrophic engine failure.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Natural frequency ($f_n$):** Typically **5 Hz to 500 Hz** for engineering helical springs.
- **Rayleigh's effective-mass coefficient:** **1/3** (the factor applied to spring mass $m_s$).
- **Critical frequency ratio for safe design:** $f_o / f_n \leq 1/20$ (i.e., operating freq ≤ 5% of natural freq).
- **Surge failure location:** Always at the **first active coil adjacent to the fixed end**.
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used in weight-to-mass conversions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Surging

A close-coiled helical spring is mechanically equivalent to a **uniform elastic rod wound into a helix**. Two distinct vibrational modes can be excited:

| Mode Type | Physical Picture | Governing Formula |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Lumped (rigid-body) vibration** | Spring acts as a single stiffness $K$ carrying the lumped mass $m$ | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/m}$ |
| **Distributed (surge) vibration** | Each coil acts as an independent mass-spring element; waves travel along the spring | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m + m_s/3}}$ |

Surging occurs when the **frequency of the fluctuating load** matches the **distributed natural frequency** of the spring.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism

1. The fluctuating load at the free end disturbs the end coil.
2. This disturbance propagates as a **longitudinal wave** along the spring axis.
3. The wave **reflects** off the fixed end (creating a node).
4. The reflected wave travels back and **superposes** with the incoming wave.
5. If the **applied frequency = wave round-trip frequency**, constructive interference produces a **standing wave**.
6. Standing waves cause coils near the loaded end to oscillate with **amplitude several times** the static deflection.
7. Cyclic stress at the **first active coil** (fixed end) exceeds endurance limit → **fatigue fracture**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Surging requires **one end fixed** and the **other end relatively free**. A spring with both ends guided will not surge — the guided end absorbs wave energy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Natural frequency (with spring mass)** | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m + \dfrac{m_s}{3}}}$ | Hz | **Master formula** |
| 2 | Natural angular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m + \dfrac{m_s}{3}}}$ | rad/s | $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n$ |
| 3 | Effective vibrating mass | $m_{eff} = m + \dfrac{m_s}{3}$ | kg | Rayleigh's energy method |
| 4 | Longitudinal wave velocity in spring | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{K \cdot L \cdot g}{W_s}}$ | m/s | Energy-method derivation |
| 5 | **Surge safety criterion (20-times rule)** | $f_n \geq 20 \cdot f_o$ | dimensionless | Industry / KTU standard |
