\sigma_h^{max} &= A + \dfrac{B}{r_i^2} = -6.67 + \dfrac{-5.33 \times 10^5}{10000} = -6.67 - 53.33 = -60 \text{ MPa (compressive)} \\
\sigma_r &= A - \dfrac{B}{r_i^2} = -6.67 - (-53.33) = -6.67 + 53.33 = 46.67 \text{ MPa (tensile — wait, this is wrong sign)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** In Lamé's equations, the **boundary condition at the inner wall** is $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive). If your $\sigma_r$ at $r = r_i$ comes out **positive**, you have a sign error. Re-check your $A$ and $B$ computation. With the convention used in the standard textbook, the radial stress at the inner wall should equal $-p_i$ (i.e., $-60$ MPa when $p_o = 0$, and $-60$ MPa with both pressures since the inner boundary still feels $-p_i$ from inside).

**Final Correct Values at Inner Surface (with $p_i = 60$ MPa, $p_o = 20$ MPa):**

$$
\sigma_r \big\vert_{r = r_i} = -60 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_h \big\vert_{r = r_i} = 60 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall #3:**
> In Lamé's equation problems, the **sign convention is the #1 source of lost marks**. Always verify that your computed $\sigma_r$ at $r = r_i$ equals $-p_i$ (the inner pressure) as a sanity check. If not, re-derive $A$ and $B$ with consistent signs. Also, students frequently forget that with **both internal and external pressure**, the constants $A$ and $B$ **must be recomputed** for the new boundary conditions — they do **not** scale linearly.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Thin Vessel Definition:** $t/D \le 0.05$ or $t/r \le 1/20$. Always verify this assumption at the start of any numerical problem.
- ✅ **Golden Ratio:** $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ for thin cylindrical vessels. This single equation is asked in nearly every KTU exam.
- ✅ **Spherical Vessel:** $\sigma = pD/(4t)$ — same value for both hoop and longitudinal stress (more efficient material usage).
- ✅ **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca):** For thin vessels, $\tau_{max} = \sigma_h/2 = pD/(4t)$.
- ✅ **Lamé's Equation Triggers:** Use only when $t/D > 0.05$, i.e., for **thick** cylinders. Equations are functions of radial position $r$.
- ✅ **Hoop Stress Maximum:** Always at the **inner wall** ($r = r_i$) in a thick cylinder.
- ✅ **Radial Stress:** Always **compressive**, ranging from $-p_i$ at inner wall to $-p_o$ at outer wall.
- ✅ **Lamé Constant A:** $\dfrac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ — same in both $\sigma_r$ and $\sigma_h$ expressions.
- ✅ **Lamé Constant B:** $\dfrac{r_i^2 r_o^2 (p_o - p_i)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ — sign flips between $\sigma_r$ (subtracted) and $\sigma_h$ (added).
- ✅ **Solid Cylinder Note:** Lamé's equation does **not** apply to solid cylinders ($r_i = 0$ leads to singularity). The stress distribution is uniform.
- ✅ **Composite / Shrink-Fit Cylinders:** A special case where an outer sleeve is heated and shrunk onto an inner cylinder to introduce **compressive pre-stress**, offsetting the tensile operational stress (used in gun barrels, autoclaves, hydraulic accumulators).
- ✅ **Design Codes:** IS 2825, ASME Section VIII Div. 1 & 2 govern industrial pressure vessel design. KTU expects awareness, not detailed code memorization.
- ✅ **Joint Efficiency:** For riveted/welded seams, $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Strength of joint}}{\text{Strength of solid plate}}$. Minimum efficiency is used to multiply the allowable stress in design thickness calculations.
- ✅ **Common Numerical Trap:** The wall thickness formula is $t = \dfrac{pD}{2\sigma_{allow} - p}$ (not just $pD/2\sigma_{allow}$) — the denominator correction accounts for the inner pressure acting on the resisting cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For Part A, memorize the four formula tables and key definitions. For Part B, *always draw the free-body diagram first*, then do the force balance — even if the question is purely a derivation. A labeled FBD alone is worth 2-3 marks out of 7. For Lamé's numerical problems, **sign-check your result** against the boundary condition $\sigma_r = -p_i$ at $r = r_i$ before submitting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thin Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **thin cylinder** is defined as a cylindrical pressure vessel in which the **ratio of wall thickness ($t$) to internal diameter ($D$) is less than or equal to $\dfrac{1}{20}$** (i.e., $\dfrac{t}{D} \le \dfrac{1}{20}$). Under this geometric condition, the stress distribution across the wall thickness is assumed to be **uniform**, and the radial stress component ($\sigma_{radial} \approx p$) is neglected because it is negligibly small compared to the tangential (hoop) and longitudinal stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Thin cylinders are pressure vessels where wall thickness is small compared to diameter. The stresses developed are:
> 1. **Hoop stress / Circumferential stress ($\sigma_h$)** — acts along the circumference.
> 2. **Longitudinal stress / Axial stress ($\sigma_l$)** — acts along the axis of the cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Convention (IS 2825 / ASME Code):**
> - If $\dfrac{t}{D} \le \dfrac{1}{20}$ → **Thin Cylinder**
> - If $\dfrac{t}{D} > \dfrac{1}{20}$ → **Thick Cylinder** (Lamé's equations applicable)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Think of a **pressure cooker** or a **LPG gas cylinder** in your kitchen. The metal wall of the cylinder is quite thin compared to its diameter, but it must safely contain high-pressure gas inside.

Imagine slicing the cylinder along its length into two half-cylinders (like cutting a sausage lengthwise). The internal pressure tries to **burst the cylinder open** (pull the two halves apart). The **hoop stress** is the metal's resistance to this bursting — like the tension in a rubber band stretched around a balloon.

Now imagine pulling the cylinder apart from both ends (like stretching a spring). The internal pressure also pushes against the **end caps**, trying to push them outward. The **longitudinal stress** is the metal's resistance to this — like a chain holding the two ends together.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples of Thin Cylinders:**
> - LPG domestic gas cylinders
> - Compressed air receivers
> - Water supply pipelines
> - Fire extinguishers
> - Hydraulic accumulators
> - Boiler shells (low-pressure)
> - Penstock pipes in hydroelectric plants

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Internal pressure | $p$ | MPa or N/mm² |
| Internal diameter | $D$ | mm |
| Wall thickness | $t$ | mm |
| Length of cylinder | $L$ | mm |
| Young's Modulus (Steel) | $E$ | **2 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)** |
| Poisson's ratio (Steel) | $\mu$ or $\nu$ | **0.3** |
| Design safety factor (typical) | $FOS$ | **3 to 5** |
| Allowable stress (mild steel) | $\sigma_{allow}$ | **80 – 130 N/mm²** |

## 1.4 Fundamental Assumptions in Thin Cylinder Theory

1. The material of the cylinder is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
2. The wall thickness is **uniform** throughout.
3. The internal pressure is **uniformly distributed**.
4. The **radial stress is negligible** (since $t \ll D$).
5. The stresses are **uniformly distributed** across the thickness (plane stress condition).
6. The cylinder is **sealed** at both ends, so longitudinal stress is fully developed.
7. End caps are **flat or spherical** (affects longitudinal stress calculation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Stress Development

When an internal pressure $p$ is applied to a thin cylindrical shell, **two principal stresses** develop simultaneously:

### Step 1: Hoop Stress (Circumferential Stress) — $\sigma_h$

- The internal pressure acts **radially outward** on the cylinder wall.
- This force tends to **increase the diameter** of the cylinder.
- To resist this, the wall material develops a **tensile stress in the circumferential direction**.
- **Why it is the largest:** The bursting force acts over the entire projected length, but the resisting cross-sectional area is only **2 × t × L** (two walls of the cut section).

### Step 2: Longitudinal Stress (Axial Stress) — $\sigma_l$

- The internal pressure acts on the **end caps** of the cylinder.
- This force tends to **pull the cylinder apart** along its axis.
- To resist this, the wall material develops a **tensile stress in the longitudinal direction**.
- **Why it is half of hoop stress:** The bursting force acts over the **circular area** ($\dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$), and the resisting cross-sectional area is the full **annular ring** ($\pi D t$).

### Step 3: Radial Stress — $\sigma_r$

- Acts perpendicular to the wall (inward and outward).
- On the **inner surface** = $-p$ (compressive).
- On the **outer surface** = $0$.
- Since $t \ll D$, the average radial stress is **negligibly small** and is **neglected** in thin cylinder analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Relationship (Always Remember!):**
> $$\sigma_h = 2 \, \sigma_l$$
> The hoop stress is **always twice** the longitudinal stress. This is a high-yield KTU exam fact.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:----:|:--------|
| 1 | Hoop stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | N/mm² | Largest principal stress |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Half of hoop stress |
| 3 | Ratio check | $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ | — | Always verify |
| 4 | Change in diameter | $\Delta D = \dfrac{pD^2}{2tE}(1-\tfrac{1}{m})$ or $\dfrac{pD^2}{2tE}(1-\mu)$ | mm | For thin cylinder |
| 5 | Change in length | $\Delta L = \dfrac{pDL}{4tE}(1-2\mu)$ | mm | Shortening if $0.5 \le \mu$ |
| 6 | Change in volume | $\Delta V = \dfrac{p\pi D^2 L}{4tE}(2.5 - \mu)$ | mm³ | For thin cylinder |
| 7 | Thickness (hoop basis) | $t = \dfrac{pD}{2\sigma_t \cdot \eta_l}$ | mm | Design formula |
| 8 | Thickness (long. basis) | $t = \dfrac{pD}{4\sigma_t \cdot \eta_c}$ | mm | Design formula |
| 9 | Spherical shell stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Uniform all directions |
| 10 | Design pressure | $p_d = $ max of $(1.5 p_w)$ or $(p_w + 0.5)$ | MPa | IS 2825 rule |
| 11 | Strain in hoop direction | $\epsilon_h = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{E} - \dfrac{\mu \sigma_l}{E}$ | — | Bi-axial strain |
| 12 | Strain in long. direction | $\epsilon_l = \dfrac{\sigma_l}{E} - \dfrac{\mu \sigma_h}{E}$ | — | Bi-axial strain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:** $p$ = internal pressure, $D$ = internal diameter, $t$ = thickness, $E$ = Young's Modulus, $\mu$ or $m$ = Poisson's ratio, $\eta_l$ = longitudinal joint efficiency, $\eta_c$ = circumferential joint efficiency, $\sigma_t$ = tensile allowable stress.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Boiler Design (Steam Power Plants):** Thin cylinder formulas are used to design the shell of fire-tube boilers and the tube sheets.
2. **Pipeline Engineering:** Oil and gas transmission pipelines are designed as thin cylinders.
3. **Aerospace Industry:** Aircraft fuselages (cylindrical body) and rocket motor casings use thin-walled pressure vessel theory.
4. **Process Industries:** Reactors, separators, and distillation columns in refineries and chemical plants.
5. **Hydraulic Systems:** Hydraulic cylinders, accumulators, and intensifiers.
6. **Domestic Appliances:** LPG cylinders (14.2 kg), pressure cookers, and aerosol cans.
7. **Compressors and Receivers:** Storage tanks for compressed air and gases.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Because $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$, most thin cylinders **fail first along a longitudinal seam** (where hoop stress is the acting stress). This is why **longitudinal joints are made stronger** (e.g., double-riveted) than circumferential joints (single-riveted) in riveted pressure vessels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop (Circumferential) Stress ($\sigma_h$)

### Setup
Consider a thin cylindrical shell of:
- Internal diameter = $D$
- Wall thickness = $t$
- Length = $L$
- Internal pressure = $p$

### Step 1: Section the Cylinder
Imagine cutting the cylinder along a **longitudinal (axial) plane** to obtain two halves. The internal pressure acts on the **projected area** of the cut section.

### Step 2: Identify the Bursting Force
The bursting force acts on the **projected rectangular area** of the cut:
$$F_{burst} = p \times A_{projected} = p \times D \times L$$

### Step 3: Identify the Resisting Force
The resisting force is provided by the **tensile stress in the wall material** acting on the two cut sections (both walls):
$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \times (2 \times t \times L) = 2 \sigma_h t L$$

### Step 4: Apply Equilibrium
For equilibrium, bursting force = resisting force:
$$p \times D \times L = 2 \sigma_h t L$$

### Step 5: Simplify
Cancelling $L$ on both sides:
$$p D = 2 \sigma_h t$$

### Step 6: Solve for Hoop Stress
$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Hoop stress is **tensile** (positive) when internal pressure is positive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal (Axial) Stress ($\sigma_l$)

### Setup
Consider the **end cap** of the cylindrical shell. The internal pressure acts on the closed end.

### Step 1: Identify the Bursting Force on End Cap
The force on the end cap due to internal pressure acts on the **circular cross-section**:
$$F_{burst} = p \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

### Step 2: Identify the Resisting Force
The resisting force is provided by the longitudinal stress in the **cylindrical wall** (annular ring):
$$F_{resist} = \sigma_l \times \pi D t$$

### Step 3: Apply Equilibrium
$$p \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \sigma_l \times \pi D t$$

### Step 4: Simplify
$$\frac{p D}{4} = \sigma_l t$$

### Step 5: Solve for Longitudinal Stress
$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t}}$$

### Step 6: Establish the Key Ratio
Dividing the two stresses:
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{pD / 2t}{pD / 4t} = 2$$
$$\therefore \quad \sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Change in Diameter ($\Delta D$)

### Step 1: Identify Principal Stresses
For a thin cylinder, the two principal stresses in the wall are:
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ (circumferential)
- $\sigma_2 = \sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ (longitudinal)
- $\sigma_3 \approx 0$ (radial, neglected)

### Step 2: Apply Generalized Hooke's Law (Plane Stress)
The circumferential strain is:
$$\epsilon_h = \frac{\sigma_h}{E} - \frac{\mu \sigma_l}{E} - \frac{\mu \sigma_r}{E}$$

Since $\sigma_r \approx 0$:
$$\epsilon_h = \frac{\sigma_h}{E} - \frac{\mu \sigma_l}{E}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Stress Values
$$\epsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{pD}{2t} - \frac{\mu \, pD}{4t}\right) = \frac{pD}{2tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Express Change in Diameter
Since $\epsilon_h = \dfrac{\Delta D}{D}$:
$$\frac{\Delta D}{D} = \frac{pD}{2tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right)$$

### Step 5: Final Formula
$$\boxed{\Delta D = \frac{pD^2}{2tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Change in Length ($\Delta L$)

### Step 1: Longitudinal Strain
$$\epsilon_l = \frac{\sigma_l}{E} - \frac{\mu \sigma_h}{E} = \frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{pD}{4t} - \frac{\mu pD}{2t}\right)$$

### Step 2: Simplify
$$\epsilon_l = \frac{pD}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$$

### Step 3: Express Change in Length
$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{pD}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$$

### Step 4: Final Formula
$$\boxed{\Delta L = \frac{pDL}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $(1 - 2\mu)$ for $\Delta D$ — that is **WRONG**. For diameter, the formula is $(1 - \mu/2)$. For length, it is $(1 - 2\mu)$. **Do not interchange!**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Change in Volume ($\Delta V$)

### Step 1: Original Volume
$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$$

### Step 2: Differential Change in Volume
$$\Delta V = V \left(\frac{2\Delta D}{D} + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Strains
$$\Delta V = V \left[ 2 \cdot \frac{pD}{2tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right) + \frac{pD}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu) \right]$$

### Step 4: Simplify
$$\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^3 L}{8tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right) + \frac{p \pi D^3 L}{16tE}(1 - 2\mu)$$

### Step 5: Combine
$$\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^3 L}{16tE}\left[2\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right) + (1 - 2\mu)\right]$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^3 L}{16tE}\left[2 - \mu + 1 - 2\mu\right]$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^3 L}{16tE}\left[2.5 - \mu\right] \times 2 = \frac{p \pi D^3 L}{8tE}(2.5 - \mu)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^2 L}{4tE}\left(\frac{5}{2} - \mu\right)}$$

---

## 3.6 Complete Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:**
A thin cylindrical shell has internal diameter **500 mm**, wall thickness **10 mm**, and length **1500 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **2.5 MPa**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $\mu = 0.3$.

**Find:**
1. Hoop stress and longitudinal stress
2. Change in diameter
3. Change in length
4. Change in volume

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $D = 500$ mm
- $t = 10$ mm
- $L = 1500$ mm
- $p = 2.5$ N/mm²
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $\mu = 0.3$

**Step 1: Hoop Stress**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{2.5 \times 500}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1250}{20} = 62.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal Stress**
$$\sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{2.5 \times 500}{4 \times 10} = \frac{1250}{40} = 31.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Verify Ratio**
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{62.5}{31.25} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Change in Diameter**
$$\Delta D = \frac{pD^2}{2tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right) = \frac{2.5 \times 500^2}{2 \times 10 \times 2 \times 10^5}\left(1 - \frac{0.3}{2}\right)$$

$$\Delta D = \frac{2.5 \times 250000}{4 \times 10^6} \times (1 - 0.15)$$

$$\Delta D = \frac{625000}{4000000} \times 0.85 = 0.15625 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{\Delta D = 0.1328 \text{ mm (increase)}}$$

**Step 5: Change in Length**
$$\Delta L = \frac{pDL}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu) = \frac{2.5 \times 500 \times 1500}{4 \times 10 \times 2 \times 10^5}(1 - 0.6)$$

$$\Delta L = \frac{1875000}{8 \times 10^6} \times 0.4 = 0.234375 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L = 0.09375 \text{ mm (increase)}}$$

**Step 6: Change in Volume**
$$\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^2 L}{4tE}\left(\frac{5}{2} - \mu\right) = \frac{2.5 \times \pi \times 250000 \times 1500}{4 \times 10 \times 2 \times 10^5}(2.5 - 0.3)$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{2.5 \times \pi \times 3.75 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^6} \times 2.2$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{2.5 \times 3.75 \times 2.2 \times \pi \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^6}$$

$$\Delta V = 2.5781 \times \pi \times 10^2 \approx 809.8 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx 809.83 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ (increase)}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Distribution in a Thin Cylinder

This diagram illustrates the force balance and stress distribution when internal pressure acts on a thin cylindrical shell.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thin Cylinder Subjected to Internal Pressure p] --> B[Cut Section Method 1 Longitudinal Cut]
    A --> C[Cut Section Method 2 End Cap Section]
    B --> D[Projected Area: D x L]
    C --> E[Projected Area: pi by 4 D squared]
    D --> F[Bursting Force F1: p times D times L]
    E --> G[Bursting Force F2: p times pi by 4 D squared]
    F --> H[Resisting Force: 2 sigma_h times t times L]
    G --> I[Resisting Force: sigma_l times pi times D times t]
    H --> J[Equilibrium F1 equals F2]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Hoop Stress: sigma_h = pD over 2t]
    J --> L[Longitudinal Stress: sigma_l = pD over 4t]
    K --> M[Result: sigma_h is Twice sigma_l]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Failure Mode: Longitudinal Seam Fails First]
    M --> O[Design Implication: Stronger Longitudinal Joint Needed]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Design Procedure for Thin Pressure Vessels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Input Data] --> S2[Internal Pressure p, Diameter D, Material sigma_t]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Determine Design Pressure p_d]
    S3 --> S4[p_d = max of 1.5 p_w or p_w plus 0.5 MPa]
    S4 --> S5[Step 3: Select Joint Efficiencies eta_l and eta_c]
    S5 --> S6[Step 4: Calculate Thickness from Hoop Stress]
    S6 --> S7[t_h = p_d D over 2 sigma_t eta_l]
    S7 --> S8[Step 5: Calculate Thickness from Longitudinal Stress]
    S8 --> S9[t_l = p_d D over 4 sigma_t eta_c]
    S9 --> S10[Step 6: Select the Larger Thickness]
    S10 --> S11[t_design = max of t_h and t_l]
    S11 --> S12[Step 7: Add Corrosion Allowance 1 to 3 mm]
    S12 --> S13[Step 8: Final Thickness t_final = t_design plus c]
    S13 --> S14[Step 9: Verify t over D less than 1 by 20]
    S14 --> S15{Is thin cylinder assumption valid?}
    S15 -->|Yes| R1[Design Complete]
    S15 -->|No| R2[Use Thick Cylinder Theory Lamé Equations]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Components in the Cylinder Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Internal Pressure p] --> A[Hoop Stress sigma_h]
    P --> B[Longitudinal Stress sigma_l]
    P --> C[Radial Stress sigma_r negligible]
    A --> D[Acts Tangentially on Cylinder Wall]
    B --> E[Acts Axially along Cylinder Length]
    C --> F[Acts Radially inward to outward]
    D --> G[Principal Stress sigma_1: pD over 2t]
    E --> H[Principal Stress sigma_2: pD over 4t]
    F --> I[sigma_3 approximately 0]
    G --> J[Maximum Stress Component]
    H --> K[Half of Hoop Stress]
    I --> L[Negligible in Thin Cylinder Analysis]
    J --> M[Failure Plane: Longitudinal Direction]
    K --> M
```

## 4.4 Visual Representation of Cylinder Geometry

| Element | Symbol | Description |
|:--------|:------:|:------------|
| Cylinder Diameter | $D$ | Internal diameter of the shell |
| Wall Thickness | $t$ | Thickness of the cylinder wall |
| Cylinder Length | $L$ | Total length of the shell |
| Internal Pressure | $p$ | Pressure acting on inner wall |
| Hoop Direction | — | Direction along the circumference |
| Longitudinal Direction | — | Direction along the axis |

```
        ←————————— D —————————→
       ↑                        ↑
       |                        |
       |      p  (Pressure)     |
       |   → → → → → → → → → → |
       |                        |   ↑
       |   Thin Cylinder Wall   |   |
       |                        |   t  (Thickness)
       |   ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← |   |
       |                        |   ↓
       ↓                        ↓
        ←——————————————————————→
                (Longitudinal direction)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The arrows pointing outward ($\rightarrow \rightarrow$) represent the internal pressure acting on the wall.
> - The cylinder wall resists this pressure through hoop stress (circumferentially) and longitudinal stress (axially).
> - Wall thickness $t$ is **much smaller** than diameter $D$ — the defining feature of a thin cylinder.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a thin cylinder. State the conditions under which a pressure vessel is classified as a thin cylinder.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A thin cylinder is a pressure vessel in which the **wall thickness is very small compared to its internal diameter**, such that the stress distribution across the wall thickness can be assumed to be uniform.

**Conditions for thin cylinder classification:**
1. The ratio of wall thickness to internal diameter is **$t/D \le 1/20$**.
2. The radial stress ($\sigma_r$) is **negligible** compared to hoop and longitudinal stresses.
3. The stresses are assumed **uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness.

(1 Mark for definition + 2 Marks for conditions)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Prove that the hoop stress in a thin cylindrical shell is twice the longitudinal stress.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hoop stress (from longitudinal cut equilibrium):
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}$$

Longitudinal stress (from end cap equilibrium):
$$\sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t}$$

Dividing:
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{pD/2t}{pD/4t} = \frac{4t}{2t} = 2$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l \quad \text{(Hence proved.)}$$

(1 Mark for each formula + 1 Mark for final ratio and conclusion)

---

## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

A thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **1.2 m** and length **3 m** is subjected to an internal pressure of **3 MPa**. The thickness of the shell is **20 mm**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.3$.

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**Calculate the:**
- (i) Hoop stress
- (ii) Longitudinal stress
- (iii) Verify the relationship $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**Determine the:**
- (i) Change in diameter
- (ii) Change in length
- (iii) Change in volume

---

#### ✅ **Complete Model Solution for Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 1.2$ m = **1200 mm**
- $L = 3$ m = **3000 mm**
- $p = 3$ MPa = **3 N/mm²**
- $t = 20$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $\mu = 0.3$

### Part (a) Solution:

**(i) Hoop Stress:**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{3 \times 1200}{2 \times 20} = \frac{3600}{40}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 90 \text{ N/mm}^2} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Stress:**
$$\sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{3 \times 1200}{4 \times 20} = \frac{3600}{80}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = 45 \text{ N/mm}^2} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Verification:**
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{90}{45} = 2$$

$$\therefore \sigma_h = 2\sigma_l \quad \text{(Verified)} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

### Part (b) Solution:

**(i) Change in Diameter:**
$$\Delta D = \frac{pD^2}{2tE}\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{2}\right) = \frac{3 \times 1200^2}{2 \times 20 \times 2 \times 10^5}\left(1 - \frac{0.3}{2}\right)$$

$$= \frac{3 \times 1440000}{8 \times 10^6} \times (1 - 0.15)$$

$$= \frac{4320000}{8000000} \times 0.85 = 0.54 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{\Delta D = 0.459 \text{ mm (increase)}} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Change in Length:**
$$\Delta L = \frac{pDL}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu) = \frac{3 \times 1200 \times 3000}{4 \times 20 \times 2 \times 10^5}(1 - 0.6)$$

$$= \frac{10800000}{16 \times 10^6} \times 0.4 = 0.675 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L = 0.27 \text{ mm (increase)}} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Change in Volume:**
$$\Delta V = \frac{p \pi D^2 L}{4tE}\left(\frac{5}{2} - \mu\right) = \frac{3 \times \pi \times 1440000 \times 3000}{4 \times 20 \times 2 \times 10^5}(2.5 - 0.3)$$

$$= \frac{3 \times \pi \times 4.32 \times 10^9}{1.6 \times 10^7} \times 2.2$$

$$= \pi \times 810 \times 2.2 = 1782\pi$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 5598.5 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ (increase)}} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

A thin spherical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **2 m** and thickness **15 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **2.5 MPa**. The material has an allowable tensile stress of **120 N/mm²** and joint efficiency of **85%**.

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**Calculate:**
- (i) Hoop stress in the sphere
- (ii) Factor of safety against bursting

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**If the same material is used to redesign this as a thin cylindrical vessel of the same diameter and pressure, determine:**
- (i) The required thickness based on hoop stress
- (ii) The required thickness based on longitudinal stress
- (iii) The design thickness (with 2 mm corrosion allowance)

---

#### ✅ **Complete Model Solution for Question B:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 2$ m = **2000 mm**
- $t = 15$ mm
- $p = 2.5$ MPa = **2.5 N/mm²**
- $\sigma_t = 120$ N/mm²
- $\eta = 0.85$
- Corrosion allowance $c = 2$ mm

### Part (a) Solution:

**(i) Hoop Stress in Spherical Shell:**
For a thin spherical shell, stress is uniform in all directions:
$$\sigma = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{2.5 \times 2000}{4 \times 15} = \frac{5000}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 83.33 \text{ N/mm}^2} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Factor of Safety:**
$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_{actual}} = \frac{120}{83.33}$$

$$\boxed{FOS = 1.44} \quad \text{(4 Marks)}$$

### Part (b) Solution (Redesign as Cylinder):

**(i) Thickness from Hoop Stress (using longitudinal joint):**
$$t_h = \frac{pD}{2\sigma_t \cdot \eta_l} = \frac{2.5 \times 2000}{2 \times 120 \times 0.85} = \frac{5000}{204}$$

$$\boxed{t_h = 24.51 \text{ mm}} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Thickness from Longitudinal Stress (using circumferential joint):**
$$t_l = \frac{pD}{4\sigma_t \cdot \eta_c} = \frac{2.5 \times 2000}{4 \times 120 \times 0.85} = \frac{5000}{408}$$

$$\boxed{t_l = 12.25 \text{ mm}} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Design Thickness:**
$$t_{design} = \max(t_h, t_l) = 24.51 \text{ mm}$$

Adding corrosion allowance:
$$t_{final} = 24.51 + 2 = 26.51 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{t_{final} \approx 27 \text{ mm (adopt standard value)}} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $D$ and $d$:** Use **internal diameter** $D$ in formulas. Some students mistakenly use outer diameter and lose 2-3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Wrong formula for $\Delta D$ vs $\Delta L$:** The factor is $(1 - \mu/2)$ for diameter and $(1 - 2\mu)$ for length. Interchanging them is a **fatal error**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Pressure in MPa must be converted to N/mm² (1 MPa = 1 N/mm²), diameter in meters must be converted to mm.
> 
> 4. **Missing the $1/2$ factor:** $\sigma_l = pD/(4t)$, NOT $pD/(2t)$. The factor is **4**, not 2.
> 
> 5. **Not adding corrosion allowance** in design problems: 2 mm is typical and examiners specifically check this.
> 
> 6. **Sign of $\Delta L$:** For $\mu > 0.5$, $\Delta L$ becomes negative (length decreases). This is a tricky case often tested.
> 
> 7. **Joint efficiency values:** In design, $\eta_l$ (longitudinal) and $\eta_c$ (circumferential) are different and must be specified separately.
> 
> 8. **Thin cylinder assumption check:** Always verify $t/D \le 1/20$ at the end. If not, the question requires thick cylinder theory (Lamé's equations).

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — MEMORIZE THESE!**

- ✅ A cylinder is **thin** if $\dfrac{t}{D} \le \dfrac{1}{20}$.

- ✅ **Hoop stress (circumferential):** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ — **LARGEST** stress in the cylinder.

- ✅ **Longitudinal stress (axial):** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ — **HALF** of hoop stress.

- ✅ **Key Ratio:** $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ — **Always true** for a closed-end thin cylinder.

- ✅ **Failure plane:** Longitudinal seam fails first because hoop stress acts across it.

- ✅ **Design rule:** Longitudinal joints are made **stronger** (double-riveted) than circumferential joints.

- ✅ **Change in diameter:** $\Delta D = \dfrac{pD^2}{2tE}\left(1 - \dfrac{\mu}{2}\right)$ — note the $(1 - \mu/2)$ factor.

- ✅ **Change in length:** $\Delta L = \dfrac{pDL}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$ — note the $(1 - 2\mu)$ factor.

- ✅ **Change in volume:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{p\pi D^2 L}{4tE}\left(\dfrac{5}{2} - \mu\right)$.

- ✅ **Spherical shell stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ — uniform in all directions; **more efficient** than cylinders (50% less stress).

- ✅ **Design pressure:** $p_d = \max(1.5 p_w, \; p_w + 0.5 \text{ MPa})$ per IS 2825.

- ✅ **Standard values:** $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\mu_{steel} = 0.3$.

- ✅ **Radial stress** is **neglected** in thin cylinder analysis.

- ✅ **Plane stress** condition exists in the cylinder wall (stress in the third direction is zero).

- ✅ **Sign convention:** All stresses are **tensile** (positive) for internal pressure.

- ✅ **Joint efficiency symbols:** $\eta_l$ = longitudinal joint, $\eta_c$ = circumferential joint.

- ✅ **Corrosion allowance:** 1 to 3 mm is added to the design thickness.

- ✅ **For a thin sphere:** Only **one** principal stress exists; it is **half** of the hoop stress in a cylinder of the same diameter and pressure.

- ✅ **Volumetric strain:** $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{pD}{4tE}\left(\dfrac{5}{2} - \mu\right)$ — useful in fluid mechanics applications.

- ✅ **Units to remember:** $1$ MPa $= 1$ N/mm² $= 10^6$ N/m² $= 10$ bar.

---

> **🎓 KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In KTU Part B questions, always draw a **neat free-body diagram** of the sectioned cylinder. This fetches **1-2 grace marks** from the examiner even if your final answer is slightly off. Also, **box the final answer** clearly with proper units — examiners reward readability!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thick Cylinder Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thick Cylinder Equation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **thick cylinder** is defined as a cylindrical pressure vessel in which the **wall thickness** is greater than **one-tenth (1/10) of its internal diameter** (i.e., $t > D_i/10$ or equivalently $t/r_i > 0.1$). Under such geometric proportions, the stress distribution across the wall thickness is no longer uniform, and the simplifying assumption used for thin cylinders (uniform hoop stress) becomes invalid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** *"A cylinder is classified as 'thick' when its ratio of wall thickness to internal diameter (t/D) exceeds 0.1. For such cylinders, the stresses (radial and hoop) vary significantly from the inner to the outer surface, and Lamé's equations must be applied to determine the actual stress distribution."* The **Thick Cylinder Equations**, derived by **Gabriel Lamé (1833)**, give the radial and circumferential (hoop) stress at any radius $r$ within the wall of a thick cylindrical shell subjected to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$. The two fundamental equations are: $$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}$$ $$\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$ where $\sigma_r$ is the **radial stress** (compressive, taken negative), $\sigma_h$ is the **hoop/circumferential stress** (tensile, taken positive), and $A$ and $B$ are **Lamé's constants** determined from boundary conditions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Inflated Garden Hose Analogy** > Imagine pressing your thumb against the open end of a thick rubber garden hose while water flows inside. Now compare two cases: > - **Thin hose wall (e.g., cheap balloon):** The wall is so thin that the stress inside the rubber is nearly the same throughout — it's as if every particle of rubber is being "pulled" with the same force. This is the *thin cylinder assumption*. > - **Thick hose wall (e.g., hydraulic pipe):** The rubber closest to the water (inner surface) bears a much higher pulling force than the rubber on the outside. The stress **decreases** as you move from the inside to the outside. This is exactly what **Lamé's equations** describe — a *gradient* of stress across the wall. **Why does this happen?** Because the inner layers of material must support not only the pressure acting on them but also "carry" the load of all the outer material around them. The outer layers only need to contain what's left of the pressure after the inner layers have absorbed their share. The pressure itself drops from $p_i$ at the inner wall to $p_o$ at the outer wall — and the stresses follow this pressure drop. **Industrial Relevance:** - Hydraulic cylinders in earth-moving equipment (JCB, Caterpillar) - Gun barrels (subjected to extreme internal pressures) - High-pressure boiler tubes and steam pipes - Nuclear reactor pressure vessels - Submarine hulls (external hydrostatic pressure) - Shrink-fit assemblies (gear-on-shaft, railway wheels on axles) --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Maximum allowable hoop stress** for ductile materials is often taken as the yield strength divided by a factor of safety: $\sigma_{h,allow} = S_{yt}/N$ (per ASME BPVC). - **Outer-to-Inner Radius Ratio:** $K = r_o/r_i$ — a key dimensionless design parameter. - **Standard value of wall thickness ratio** for thick cylinder classification: $t/r_i \geq 0.1$ (or $K \geq 1.2$). - **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$ for typical engineering steels: **0.3**; for cast iron: **0.25**; for copper alloys: **0.33**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** If a problem says *"diameter = 200 mm, thickness = 25 mm"*, immediately check: $t/D_i = 25/200 = 0.125 > 0.1$ → **THICK CYLINDER** applies. Always verify this in your first step during the exam — examiners award marks for correct classification. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Theory: A Three-Point Logical Breakdown ### Step 1: The Equilibrium Condition (Radial Force Balance) Consider a small element of the cylinder wall at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$ and unit length. Two forces act on it radially: - The pressure from inside pushes outward - The pressure from outside pushes inward - Plus the **radial stress** $\sigma_r$ acting on the curved surfaces Summing forces in the radial direction and taking the limit as $dr \to 0$ yields the **equilibrium equation**: $$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_h}{r} = 0$$ ### Step 2: The Compatibility Condition (Plane Stress Assumption) Because the cylinder is long and the ends are far away, we assume **plane stress** (axial stress may exist but is independent of $r$ for a closed-end cylinder). The radial and hoop strains must be kinematically compatible. For a small element: $$\varepsilon_h = \frac{u}{r}, \quad \varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}$$ where $u$ is the **radial displacement** of a point originally at radius $r$. ### Step 3: Stress-Strain Relationship (Generalized Hooke's Law) Applying Hooke's law for plane stress (radial-hoop plane): $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu\sigma_h)$$ $$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu\sigma_r)$$ Substituting the strain-displacement relations into Hooke's law and integrating, we get the **general solution** of the differential equation. Two integration constants ($A$ and $B$) appear, giving us Lamé's two equations. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition / Application | |---|---------|-------------|----------------------------------| | 1 | $\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | Radial stress at radius $r$ | General Lamé equation (radial) | | 2 | $\sigma_h = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress at radius $r$ | General Lamé equation (hoop) | | 3 | $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Lamé constant $A$ | Standard pressure vessel | | 4 | $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Lamé constant $B$ | Standard pressure vessel | | 5 | $r = r_i$ | Inner radius | $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive, BC) | | 6 | $r = r_o$ | Outer radius | $\sigma_r = -p_o$ (compressive, BC) | | 7 | $K = r_o / r_i$ | Radius ratio | $K > 1.2 \Rightarrow$ thick cylinder | | 8 | $\sigma_{h,i} = A + \dfrac{B}{r_i^2} = \dfrac{p_i(r_i^2 + r_o^2) - 2 p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Hoop stress at **inner surface** | Critical for pressure vessels | | 9 | $\sigma_{h,o} = A + \dfrac{B}{r_o^2} = \dfrac{2 p_i r_i^2 - p_o(r_i^2 + r_o^2)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Hoop stress at **outer surface** | Useful for shrink fits | | 10 | $\sigma_{h,max} = \sigma_{h,i}$ | Maximum hoop stress | Always at inner surface (for $p_i > p_o$) | | 11 | $\sigma_{r,max} = -p_i$ | Maximum radial stress (magnitude) | At inner surface, compressive | | 12 | $u = \dfrac{r}{E}\big[(1-\nu)A + (1+\nu)\dfrac{B}{r^2}\big]$ | Radial displacement at radius $r$ | Used in shrink-fit problems | | 13 | $p_o = 0$: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Hoop stress (no external pressure) | Open cylinder with $p_o = 0$ | | 14 | $p_i = 0$: $\sigma_h = -\dfrac{2 p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Uniform hoop stress (external only) | Submarine hulls | | 15 | $K = r_o / r_i$ | Radius ratio | Used in tables and design charts | | 16 | $\sigma_{h,avg} = \dfrac{p_i d}{2t}$ | Thin cylinder hoop stress (for comparison) | Valid only when $t < D_i/10$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard):** > - **Tensile stress** = **positive (+)** → e.g., hoop stress in a pressurized cylinder > - **Compressive stress** = **negative (−)** → e.g., radial stress (because pressure pushes inward on the material) > - Internal pressure $p_i$ always acts **inward** on the inner wall → $\sigma_r(r_i) = -p_i$ > - External pressure $p_o$ always acts **inward** on the outer wall → $\sigma_r(r_o) = -p_o$ --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The thick cylinder equations are foundational to: 1. **Design of High-Pressure Vessels** — In oil refineries, chemical plants, and power stations, pressures often exceed 100 bar. A thin-cylinder design would be unsafe. Lamé's equations give the **true stress distribution**, allowing accurate material selection. 2. **Shrink-Fit Assemblies** — When two cylinders (inner and outer) are joined by heating the outer and cooling the inner, they develop **interference pressure** $p$ at the interface. The **contact pressure** is calculated using the *compatibility of radial displacements* at the common radius, applying Lamé's equations to both cylinders. 3. **Autofrettage Process** — Gun barrels and high-pressure cylinders are intentionally over-pressurized to induce beneficial **compressive residual stresses** at the inner wall. The Lamé equations are solved for plastic deformation, then "unloaded" to find residual stresses. 4. **Hydraulic Press Cylinders and Accumulators** — Where the wall thickness is comparable to the bore diameter. 5. **Burst Pressure Calculation** — The theoretical burst pressure of a thick-walled ductile cylinder is found by setting $\sigma_{h,i} = S_{yt}$ (yield strength at the inner wall — the critical point). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Lamé's Equations ### Step 1: Free Body Diagram of a Cylindrical Element Consider a small element of the cylinder wall at radius $r$ with: - Radial thickness $dr$ - Circumferential angle $d\theta$ - Unit axial length Forces acting on the element (in the radial direction, taking outward as positive): - $\sigma_r$ on the **inner curved face** (area $= r \, d\theta \cdot 1$) - $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)$ on the **outer curved face** (area $= (r + dr) \, d\theta \cdot 1$) - $\sigma_h$ on the **two radial flat faces** (each of area $dr \cdot 1$), acting **inward** with a component $\sigma_h \sin(d\theta/2) \approx \sigma_h \cdot d\theta/2$ ### Step 2: Equilibrium Equation Sum of forces in the radial direction = 0: $$(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr) d\theta - \sigma_r \cdot r \, d\theta - 2 \sigma_h \cdot dr \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = 0$$ Expanding and dropping second-order infinitesimals ($d\sigma_r \cdot dr$ and $dr^2$ terms): $$\sigma_r \, dr + r \, d\sigma_r - \sigma_h \, dr = 0$$ Dividing through by $r \, dr$: $$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_h}{r} = 0 \quad \text{...(Equation of Equilibrium)}$$ ### Step 3: Compatibility (Plane Strain Compatibility) The radial and circumferential strains are related through the radial displacement $u(r)$: $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_h = \frac{u}{r}$$ For the radial and hoop strains to be kinematically consistent, they must satisfy (from elasticity theory, plane stress): $$\frac{d\varepsilon_h}{dr} = \frac{\varepsilon_r - \varepsilon_h}{r}$$ ### Step 4: Hooke's Law (Plane Stress) For a long cylinder with free axial ends (plane stress condition): $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu \sigma_h)$$ $$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_h - \nu \sigma_r)$$ ### Step 5: Substituting and Combining Substituting the strain-stress relations into the compatibility equation and simplifying (long algebraic process), we arrive at: $$\frac{d}{dr}\left[\frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{du}{dr} - \nu \frac{u}{r}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{u}{r} - \nu \frac{du}{dr}\right) - \frac{1}{E}\left(\frac{du}{dr} - \nu \frac{u}{r}\right)\right]$$ After simplification: $$r^2 \frac{d^2u}{dr^2} + r \frac{du}{dr} - u = 0$$ ### Step 6: Solving the ODE This is an **Euler-Cauchy equation** with the trial solution $u = r^n$: $$r^2 \cdot n(n-1)r^{n-2} + r \cdot n r^{n-1} - r^n = 0$$ $$n(n-1) + n - 1 = 0$$ $$n^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow n = \pm 1$$ Hence, the general solution: $$u(r) = C_1 r + \frac{C_2}{r}$$ ### Step 7: Recovering Stresses from Displacement Substitute $u(r)$ back into Hooke's law: $$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)\frac{du}{dr} + \nu \frac{u}{r}\right]$$ Computing the derivatives: $$\frac{du}{dr} = C_1 - \frac{C_2}{r^2}, \quad \frac{u}{r} = C_1 + \frac{C_2}{r^2}$$ After substitution and rearrangement, we get the famous **Lamé's equations** in terms of constants $A$ and $B$: $$\boxed{\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r^2}}$$ where $A$ and $B$ are new constants related to $C_1$ and $C_2$ by: $A = \dfrac{E C_1}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu) + \nu\right] = \dfrac{E C_1}{1+\nu}$ and $B = \dfrac{E C_2}{1+\nu}$. ### Step 8: Applying Boundary Conditions **Boundary Condition 1:** At $r = r_i$, $\sigma_r = -p_i$ $$-p_i = A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} \quad \text{...(BC-1)}$$ **Boundary Condition 2:** At $r = r_o$, $\sigma_r = -p_o$ $$-p_o = A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} \quad \text{...(BC-2)}$$ ### Step 9: Solving for A and B Subtracting BC-2 from BC-1: $$-p_i + p_o = -\frac{B}{r_i^2} + \frac{B}{r_o^2}$$ $$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \quad \text{...(Lamé constant B)}$$ Adding (BC-1)$\cdot r_i^2$ and (BC-2)$\cdot r_o^2$: $$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \quad \text{...(Lamé constant A)}$$ ### Step 10: Substituting Back The **final Lamé equations** for a thick cylinder with internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$ are: $$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \cdot \frac{1}{r^2}$$ $$\sigma_h = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \cdot \frac{1}{r^2}$$ ### Step 11: Special Case — Cylinder with No External Pressure ($p_o = 0$) Setting $p_o = 0$: $$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 - \frac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$$ $$\sigma_h = \frac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 + \frac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$$ At $r = r_i$ (inner surface): $$\sigma_{h,i} = \frac{p_i r_i^2 (r_i^2 + r_o^2)}{r_i^2(r_o^2 - r_i^2)} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_i^2 + r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$ This can be written in terms of the radius ratio $K = r_o/r_i$: $$\sigma_{h,i} = p_i \cdot \frac{K^2 + 1}{K^2 - 1}$$ This is the **most-asked formula** in KTU exams for thick cylinders. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Stresses at Different Locations | Location | Radial Stress $\sigma_r$ | Hoop Stress $\sigma_h$ | Magnitude (assuming $p_o=0$, $p_i > 0$) | |----------|--------------------------|-------------------------|----| | $r = r_i$ (inner wall) | $-p_i$ (max compressive) | $\sigma_{h,i} = p_i \dfrac{K^2+1}{K^2-1}$ (max tensile) | Highest | | $r = r$ (intermediate) | $\sigma_r(r) = p_i\dfrac{r_i^2}{r_o^2-r_i^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$ | $\sigma_h(r) = p_i\dfrac{r_i^2}{r_o^2-r_i^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Intermediate | | $r = r_o$ (outer wall) | $0$ (no external pressure) | $\sigma_{h,o} = \dfrac{2 p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \dfrac{2p_i}{K^2-1}$ | Lowest | **Key Observation for KTU Exam:** The hoop stress is **always maximum at the inner surface** and decreases outward. This is the most critical design location. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Compound Cylinder (Shrink Fit) Equations Consider two cylinders joined by interference fit: - **Inner cylinder (core):** inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_c$ (common interface) - **Outer cylinder (jacket):** inner radius $r_c$, outer radius $r_o$ - **Interface pressure:** $p$ (induced by interference) For the **inner cylinder** (subjected to external pressure $p$ at $r = r_c$, $p_i = 0$): $$\sigma_{h,\text{inner}}(r_c) = -\frac{2 p r_c^2}{r_c^2 - r_i^2}$$ For the **outer cylinder** (subjected to internal pressure $p$ at $r = r_c$, $p_o = 0$): $$\sigma_{h,\text{outer}}(r_c) = p \cdot \frac{r_c^2 + r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_c^2}$$ **Compatibility condition:** The radial displacements at $r = r_c$ must be equal for both cylinders (otherwise there would be a gap or overlap): $$u_{\text{inner}}(r_c) = u_{\text{outer}}(r_c)$$ This yields the **interface (contact) pressure**: $$p = \frac{\delta \cdot E}{r_c} \cdot \frac{(r_c^2 - r_i^2)(r_o^2 - r_c^2)}{2 r_c^2(r_o^2 - r_i^2)}$$ where $\delta$ is the **radial interference** (difference in radii before assembly). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Thick Cylinder Stress Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Parameters] --> B[Internal Pressure pi] A --> C[External Pressure po] A --> D[Inner Radius ri] A --> E[Outer Radius ro] A --> F[Young Modulus E] A --> G[Poisson Ratio nu] B --> H[Classify Cylinder] C --> H D --> H E --> H H --> I{Is t/Di greater than 0.1?} I -->|Yes| J[Apply Lame Equations] I -->|No| K[Apply Thin Cylinder Formula] J --> L[Compute Lame Constant A] J --> M[Compute Lame Constant B] L --> N[Radial Stress sigma r] M --> N L --> O[Hoop Stress sigma h] M --> O N --> P[Stress Distribution Plot] O --> P P --> Q[Identify Critical Point at ri] Q --> R[Compare with Allowable Stress] R --> S{Is sigma h less than Syt by N?} S -->|Yes| T[Design is Safe] S -->|No| U[Increase Thickness or Material] ``` ## 4.2 Stress Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CYL[Thick Cylinder Wall] P1[Inner Surface ri
sigma r equals minus pi
sigma h equals max] --> P2[Mid Wall r equals sqrt of ri ro
Intermediate Stress] --> P3[Outer Surface ro
sigma r equals minus po
sigma h equals min] end P1 -->|Decreases Outward| P2 P2 -->|Decreases Further| P3 P1 --> Q1[Most Critical Location
Check Yield Here] Q1 --> R1[Design Factor of Safety] R1 --> S1[Final Material Selection] ``` ## 4.3 Shrink Fit Assembly Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Inner Cylinder ri to rc] --> B[Heat Outer Cylinder] C[Outer Cylinder rc to ro] --> B B --> D[Cool Inner Cylinder with Liquid N2] D --> E[Slide Inner into Outer] E --> F[Both Reach Room Temperature] F --> G[Radial Interference delta Develops] G --> H[Contact Pressure p at rc] H --> I[Inner Cylinder: External Pressure Case] H --> J[Outer Cylinder: Internal Pressure Case] I --> K[Compatibility of Displacements at rc] J --> K K --> L[Calculate Interface Pressure p] L --> M[Check Hoop Stresses in Both] M --> N{All Stresses within Allowable?} N -->|Yes| O[Shrink Fit Design Validated] N -->|No| P[Modify Interference or Geometry] ``` ## 4.4 Boundary Condition Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB BC1[At r equals ri
Radial Stress equals minus pi
Compressive Due to Internal Pressure] --> RES[Stress Field] BC2[At r equals ro
Radial Stress equals minus po
Compressive Due to External Pressure] --> RES RES --> EQ1[sigma r equals A minus B over r squared] RES --> EQ2[sigma h equals A plus B over r squared] EQ1 --> SOL[Lamé Solution] EQ2 --> SOL SOL --> APPS[Design of Pressure Vessels
Hydraulic Cylinders
Gun Barrels
Nuclear Reactors] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define a thick cylinder. How is it distinguished from a thin cylinder?** **Model Answer:** A thick cylinder is a cylindrical pressure vessel in which the **wall thickness is more than 1/10th of its internal diameter** (i.e., $t > D_i/10$ or $t/r_i > 0.1$). **Distinction from Thin Cylinder:** | Feature | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder | |---------|---------------|----------------| | Wall thickness ratio | $t/D_i < 0.1$ | $t/D_i > 0.1$ | | Stress across wall | Assumed uniform | Varies (gradient exists) | | Governing equation | $\sigma_h = pd/2t$ | Lamé's equations | | Radial stress | Neglected | Significant, equal to $-p$ at inner wall | | Accuracy | Approximate | Exact (within elasticity theory) | In a thin cylinder, the radial stress is negligible compared to the hoop stress, so the hoop stress is assumed uniform. In a thick cylinder, the radial stress is significant and changes from $-p_i$ at the inner wall to $-p_o$ at the outer wall, causing the hoop stress to also vary considerably. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2023] **State Lamé's equations for a thick cylinder and explain the terms.** **Model Answer:** Lamé's equations for a thick cylinder are: $$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}$$ $$\sigma_h = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$ **Explanation of Terms:** - $\sigma_r$ = **Radial stress** at any radius $r$ (compressive, taken negative) - $\sigma_h$ = **Hoop (circumferential) stress** at any radius $r$ (tensile, taken positive) - $r$ = Radius of the point where stress is to be calculated (variable) - $A$ and $B$ = **Lamé's constants**, determined by the boundary conditions - $p_i$ = Internal pressure applied at $r = r_i$ - $p_o$ = External pressure applied at $r = r_o$ The constants are: $$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}, \quad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$ --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **A thick cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 200 mm and an outer diameter of 320 mm. It is subjected to an internal pressure of 60 MPa. Find:** **(a)** The hoop and radial stresses at the inner and outer surfaces of the cylinder. **[7 marks]** **(b)** The maximum shear stress and the radius at which it occurs. **[7 marks]** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks] **Given Data:** - Internal diameter $D_i = 200$ mm → $r_i = 100$ mm - Outer diameter $D_o = 320$ mm → $r_o = 160$ mm - Internal pressure $p_i = 60$ MPa - External pressure $p_o = 0$ MPa (assumed) **Step 1:** Verify that it is a thick cylinder. $$\frac{t}{D_i} = \frac{(320-200)/2}{200} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30 > 0.1 \quad \checkmark \text{ Thick cylinder}$$ **Step 2:** Compute Lamé's constants. $$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{60 \times 100^2 - 0}{160^2 - 100^2} = \frac{60 \times 10000}{25600 - 10000} = \frac{600000}{15600}$$ $$A = 38.46 \text{ MPa}$$ $$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{60 \times 100^2 \times 160^2}{15600} = \frac{60 \times 10000 \times 25600}{15600}$$ $$B = \frac{1.536 \times 10^{10}}{15600} = 9.846 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa·mm}^2$$ **Step 3:** Compute stresses at the **inner surface** ($r = r_i = 100$ mm). $$\sigma_{r,i} = A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} = 38.46 - \frac{9.846 \times 10^5}{100^2} = 38.46 - 98.46 = -60 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$ $$\sigma_{h,i} = A + \frac{B}{r_i^2} = 38.46 + 98.46 = 136.92 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4:** Compute stresses at the **outer surface** ($r = r_o = 160$ mm). $$\sigma_{r,o} = A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} = 38.46 - \frac{9.846 \times 10^5}{160^2} = 38.46 - 38.46 = 0 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$ $$\sigma_{h,o} = A + \frac{B}{r_o^2} = 38.46 + 38.46 = 76.92 \text{ MPa}$$ **Final Answers for Part (a):** - Inner surface: $\sigma_{r,i} = -60$ MPa, $\sigma_{h,i} = 136.92$ MPa - Outer surface: $\sigma_{r,o} = 0$ MPa, $\sigma_{h,o} = 76.92$ MPa --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks] **Step 1:** Identify the principal stresses at any point. In a thick cylinder under internal pressure (no axial load assumed), the three principal stresses are: - $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$ (hoop, maximum) - $\sigma_2 = \sigma_r$ (radial) - $\sigma_3 = 0$ (axial, neglected for plane stress open-ended vessel) **Step 2:** Express principal stresses in terms of $r$. $$\sigma_h(r) = 38.46 + \frac{9.846 \times 10^5}{r^2}$$ $$\sigma_r(r) = 38.46 - \frac{9.846 \times 10^5}{r^2}$$ **Step 3:** Maximum shear stress at any radius $r$ is: $$\tau_{max}(r) = \frac{\sigma_h(r) - \sigma_r(r)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{2B}{r^2}\right] = \frac{B}{r^2}$$ **Step 4:** To find the **maximum value of $\tau_{max}$**, observe that $\tau_{max}$ is **inversely proportional to $r^2$**, so it is maximum at the **smallest radius**, i.e., at $r = r_i = 100$ mm. $$\tau_{max,max} = \frac{B}{r_i^2} = \frac{9.846 \times 10^5}{100^2} = 98.46 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 98.46 \text{ MPa at } r = r_i = 100 \text{ mm}}$$ **Verification:** Using principal stress difference at $r = r_i$: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{h,i} - \sigma_{r,i}}{2} = \frac{136.92 - (-60)}{2} = \frac{196.92}{2} = 98.46 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — Alternative Choice **A compound cylinder consists of an inner cylinder of outer diameter 240 mm and an outer cylinder of inner diameter 240 mm and outer diameter 360 mm. The radial interference is 0.08 mm. Take $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$.** **(a)** Determine the interface (contact) pressure developed at the common surface after assembly. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Find the maximum hoop stress in the inner and outer cylinders. **[7 marks]** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks] **Given Data:** - Inner cylinder: $r_i = ?$ (not needed directly), $r_c = 120$ mm (common interface) - Outer cylinder: $r_c = 120$ mm, $r_o = 180$ mm - Radial interference $\delta = 0.08$ mm - $E = 200$ GPa = $200 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$ **Step 1:** The compatibility condition (radial displacement equality at $r_c$) gives: $$u_{inner}(r_c) + u_{outer}(r_c) = \delta$$ The radial displacement formula (Lamé): $$u(r) = \frac{r}{E}\left[(1-\nu)A + (1+\nu)\frac{B}{r^2}\right]$$ **For the inner cylinder** (subjected to external pressure $p$ at $r_c$ with $p_i = 0$ at $r_i$): - $A_i = \dfrac{-p \cdot r_c^2}{r_c^2 - r_i^2}$ ... (using external pressure convention) - $B_i = \dfrac{(-p) r_i^2 r_c^2}{r_c^2 - r_i^2}$ For the inner cylinder with $r_i$ to be determined — we need it. Let's assume the inner cylinder is solid ($r_i = 0$ is the limit case; but practically the inner cylinder is also hollow with some small bore). **Assuming the inner cylinder is a solid shaft** ($r_i = 0$) is a common simplification. For a **solid inner cylinder** (or $r_i \to 0$), subjected to external pressure $p$: - $A_i = -p$, $B_i = 0$ - The radial displacement at $r_c$: $u_{inner}(r_c) = -\dfrac{p r_c}{E}(1-\nu)$ (negative means inward contraction) **For the outer cylinder** (subjected to internal pressure $p$ at $r_c$ with $p_o = 0$ at $r_o$): - $A_o = \dfrac{p r_c^2}{r_o^2 - r_c^2}$, $B_o = \dfrac{p r_c^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_c^2}$ - The radial displacement at $r_c$: $u_{outer}(r_c) = \dfrac{p r_c}{E(r_o^2 - r_c^2)}\left[(1-\nu)r_c^2 + (1+\nu)r_o^2\right]$ (positive means outward expansion) **Step 2:** Compatibility equation: $$|u_{outer}(r_c)| + |u_{inner}(r_c)| = \delta$$ $$\frac{p r_c}{E}\left[\frac{(1-\nu)r_c^2 + (1+\nu)r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_c^2}\right] + \frac{p r_c (1-\nu)}{E} = \delta$$ **Step 3:** Substituting values: - $r_c = 120$ mm, $r_o = 180$ mm, $\delta = 0.08$ mm, $E = 200 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$ - $r_o^2 - r_c^2 = 32400 - 14400 = 18000$ mm² - $r_o^2 = 32400$, $r_c^2 = 14400$ Numerator of first term: $(1-0.3)(14400) + (1+0.3)(32400) = 0.7 \times 14400 + 1.3 \times 32400 = 10080 + 42120 = 52200$ $$\frac{p \times 120}{200000} \times \frac{52200}{18000} + \frac{p \times 120 \times 0.7}{200000} = 0.08$$ $$p \times \frac{120 \times 52200}{200000 \times 18000} + p \times \frac{120 \times 0.7}{200000} = 0.08$$ $$p \times 0.00174 + p \times 0.00042 = 0.08$$ $$p \times 0.00216 = 0.08$$ $$p = \frac{0.08}{0.00216} = 37.04 \text{ MPa}$$ **Final Answer for Part (a):** The interface contact pressure $p \approx 37.04$ MPa. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks] **Step 1: Maximum hoop stress in the INNER cylinder** (solid shaft with external pressure $p = 37.04$ MPa). For a solid cylinder under external pressure, the hoop stress is **uniform throughout** (no $r$ dependence for the $B$ term vanishes): $$\sigma_{h,\text{inner}} = -p = -37.04 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$ **Step 2: Maximum hoop stress in the OUTER cylinder** (with internal pressure $p = 37.04$ MPa at $r = r_c = 120$ mm, $p_o = 0$ at $r_o = 180$ mm). Maximum hoop stress occurs at the **inner surface** ($r = r_c$): $$\sigma_{h,\text{outer},max} = p \cdot \frac{r_c^2 + r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_c^2} = 37.04 \times \frac{14400 + 32400}{32400 - 14400} = 37.04 \times \frac{46800}{18000}$$ $$\sigma_{h,\text{outer},max} = 37.04 \times 2.6 = 96.30 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$ **Final Answers for Part (b):** - Inner cylinder maximum hoop stress: $37.04$ MPa (compressive) - Outer cylinder maximum hoop stress: $96.30$ MPa (tensile) at $r = 120$ mm --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Marks are deducted (often 1-2 marks) if students write radial stress as **positive** at the inner surface. The correct sign is **negative** because pressure acts inward on the material. > 2. **Forgetting the Outer Pressure:** Many students assume $p_o = 0$ even when the problem states otherwise. Always re-read the problem carefully. > 3. **Thin vs Thick Cylinder Classification:** Students often directly apply the thin cylinder formula $pd/2t$ to a problem that actually requires Lamé's equations. This gives incorrect answers. **Always check $t/D_i > 0.1$ first.** > 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** When $B$ has units of MPa·mm², you must use $r$ in **mm** (not m). Mixed units = full marks deduction in numerical problems. > 5. **Maximum Shear Stress Calculation:** Use the **principal stress difference divided by 2**, not just the hoop stress. Don't forget that the radial stress is also a principal stress. > 6. **In Shrink-Fit Problems:** Don't forget that the **interference is radial**, so the diametral interference is $2\delta$. Also, the compatibility equation requires the **sum of magnitudes** of radial displacements, not the algebraic difference, because both cylinders move toward each other. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Classification Rule:** $t/D_i > 0.1 \Rightarrow$ Thick Cylinder. Always check this **first** in any problem. - **Lamé's Equations:** - Radial: $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$ - Hoop: $\sigma_h = A + B/r^2$ - **Constants:** - $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ - $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ - **Boundary Conditions (must remember):** - At $r = r_i$: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive) - At $r = r_o$: $\sigma_r = -p_o$ (compressive) - **Critical Design Location:** Maximum hoop stress always occurs at the **inner surface** (when $p_i > p_o$). This is the **first check** for yield. - **Maximum Shear Stress Location:** Always at $r = r_i$ for internally pressurized cylinders. - **Key Formula (most asked):** $\sigma_{h,i} = p_i \cdot \dfrac{K^2 + 1}{K^2 - 1}$ where $K = r_o/r_i$ - **Sign Convention:** Tensile = (+), Compressive = (−). Pressure is always compressive on the wall. - **Thin Cylinder Comparison:** $\sigma_{h,avg} = pd/2t$ vs. Thick Cylinder's $\sigma_{h,i} = p(K^2+1)/(K^2-1)$. As $K \to \infty$, the latter approaches $p$ while the former approaches $pK$. - **Shrink Fit Key Idea:** Compatibility of radial displacements at the common interface gives the contact pressure. - **Auto-frettage Application:** Uses the elastic-plastic version of Lamé's equations to induce beneficial compressive residual stresses. - **Poisson's Ratio Values:** Steel = 0.3, Cast Iron = 0.25, Copper = 0.33 (use as given in problem). - **Plane Stress vs. Plane Strain:** For open-ended cylinders → plane stress; for closed-end cylinders → axial stress = $p_i r_i / (2t)$ must be added to the analysis.

topics.title: Hydraulic Accumulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Accumulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Accumulator:** A hydraulic accumulator is a pressure-storage reservoir in which a non-compressible hydraulic fluid is held under pressure by an external source of energy (gravity, spring force, or compressed gas). It is used in hydraulic systems to store potential energy (pressure-energy), supplement pump flow during peak demand, absorb pressure shocks/pulsations, compensate for leakage, and maintain constant system pressure.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design syllabus (Module 3)**, the study of hydraulic accumulators is mandatory under fluid power systems, focusing primarily on **gas-charged (hydro-pneumatic) accumulators** because they are the industry standard (used in >90% of modern hydraulic circuits).

**Standard Metric / Symbol Convention:**
- Pre-charge (gas) pressure: $P_1$ — typically **0.6 × Maximum system pressure ($P_2$) to 0.7 × $P_2$**
- Maximum system (hydraulic) pressure: $P_2$
- Total gas volume of accumulator: $V_A$ (litres)
- Gas volume at $P_2$: $V_2$
- Effective (usable / discharge) volume: $V_d = V_A - V_2$
- Polytropic index: $n$ = **1.4** (adiabatic, fast cycles) or **1.0** (isothermal, slow cycles)

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Hydraulic Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Think of it as a "Rechargeable Battery":**
> Just as a battery stores **electrical energy chemically** and releases it when current demand exceeds what the source can supply, a hydraulic accumulator stores **fluid pressure-energy** and releases pressurized fluid when the actuator demand exceeds the pump's delivery capacity.
>
> - **Pump** = Charger / Power Supply
> - **Accumulator** = Battery (energy storage)
> - **Hydraulic Actuator** = Load (motor, cylinder)
> - **Gas pre-charge** = Initial stored chemical energy
>
> Without a battery, peak loads would require an oversized, expensive pump running continuously. Without an accumulator, peak hydraulic loads would require an oversized pump.

### 1.3 Why We Need Accumulators in Machine Design

1. **Energy Storage** — Stores pressurized fluid to handle intermittent high-demand cycles.
2. **Pump Sizing Economy** — Allows a smaller pump to meet peak loads.
3. **Pressure Shock Absorption** — Damps water-hammer / pressure spikes.
4. **Leakage Compensation** — Maintains pressure during pump-off periods.
5. **Emergency Power Source** — Supplies fluid if pump fails.
6. **Pulsation Damping** — Smoothens pulsating flow from piston pumps.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic Accumulators

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask a 3-mark question: *"Classify hydraulic accumulators with sketches."* Memorise this hierarchy.

**Step-by-step classification logic:**

- **A. Weight-Loaded (Gravity) Accumulator**
  - Uses dead weight on a piston/plunger to pressurize oil.
  - Pressure = Weight / Piston Area = (constant).
  - Pros: Constant pressure regardless of volume.
  - Cons: Very large, heavy (impractical for mobile systems).
  - Modern use: Rare; mostly historical and educational.

- **B. Spring-Loaded Accumulator**
  - Uses a coiled compression spring on the fluid piston.
  - Pressure varies linearly with fluid volume (spring deflection).
  - Pros: Compact, simple, low cost.
  - Cons: Pressure varies widely; springs fatigue at high pressure (>200 bar).
  - Modern use: Low-pressure circuits, vehicle suspensions.

- **C. Gas-Loaded (Hydro-Pneumatic) Accumulator** ★ Industry Standard
  - Uses an inert gas (typically **Nitrogen — $N_2$**) pre-charged on the separated side.
  - Energy stored in compressed gas (Boyle's Law / Polytropic Law).
  - Pros: Compact, lightweight, high pressure capability, fast response.
  - Cons: Requires pre-charge monitoring, gas permeation over time.
  - Sub-types based on separation element:
    - **(i) Bladder-type** — Flexible rubber bladder; fastest response; most common.
    - **(ii) Piston-type** — Floating piston with seals; for high capacity; slower response.
    - **(iii) Diaphragm-type** — Welded metal diaphragm; compact; for smaller volumes.

### 2.2 Operating Cycle of a Gas-Charged Accumulator

**The Why:** The gas (pre-charged to $P_1$) compresses when oil enters, then expands back to drive oil out — converting gas potential energy into hydraulic work.

- **Step 1 — Pre-charge:** Nitrogen filled at $P_1$ (≈ 0.6 $P_2$) with oil port closed.
- **Step 2 — Charging (Pump ON):** Oil enters from pump; gas compresses from $P_1$ to $P_2$ (max).
- **Step 3 — Holding:** Pump stops; check valve holds pressure in system.
- **Step 4 — Discharging (Pump OFF / Peak Demand):** Gas expands, pushing oil out to actuators.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — these formulas appear in 14-mark derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$ | Polytropic gas compression (Boyle's generalized) | $n = 1$ isothermal, $n = 1.4$ adiabatic |
| 2 | $V_d = V_A - V_2$ | Effective / discharge volume definition | Litres |
| 3 | $V_d = V_A \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n} \right]$ | Effective discharge volume (from Polytropic law) | Litres |
| 4 | $V_A = \dfrac{V_d}{\left[ 1 - (P_1/P_2)^{1/n} \right]}$ | Required accumulator total volume | Litres |
| 5 | $V_2 = V_A \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n}$ | Gas volume at max system pressure | Litres |
| 6 | $Q_p = \dfrac{V_A \, N \, k}{60 \, t}$ | Pump capacity (discharge time approach) | L/min; $N$ = cycles/min, $k$ = usage fraction |
| 7 | $P_{rec} = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{2} + P_1$ | Recommended pre-charge pressure (rule of thumb) | bar |
| 8 | $E = \int_{V_2}^{V_A} P \, dV$ | Energy stored in accumulator | Joules |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_A}{n - 1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | Energy stored (final, in Joules) | $V_A$ in m³, $P$ in Pa |
| 10 | $V_{rate} = Q_d \, t$ | Flow-rate-discharge relation | Volume delivered |

**Notation key:** $P_1$ = pre-charge (gas) pressure, $P_2$ = max hydraulic pressure, $V_A$ = total geometric volume of accumulator, $V_2$ = gas volume at $P_2$, $V_d$ = effective/usable volume, $n$ = polytropic index, $Q_p$ = pump flow, $N$ = cycles/min, $t$ = time per cycle, $k$ = fraction of cycle in which pump alone runs.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Injection moulding machines** — Accumulators drive fast clamp/unclamp cylinders.
- **Mobile hydraulics (excavators, loaders)** — Accumulator-assisted braking, ride control, emergency steering.
- **Aircraft hydraulic systems** — Multiple accumulators provide redundancy.
- **Ship steering gear** — Meets SOLAS emergency steering rule.
- **Pulp & paper mills** — Damps pulsations from reciprocating pumps.
- **Machine tool hydraulics** — Smoothens pressure for precision motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Discharge Volume $V_d$

**Objective:** Derive the expression for the effective (usable) volume of oil a gas-charged accumulator can deliver when gas pressure falls from $P_2$ (max) to $P_1$ (pre-charge).

**Given:** Polytropic compression relation for nitrogen.

$$
P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify states:**
At pre-charge (initial gas state): pressure = $P_1$, gas volume = $V_1 = V_A$ (entire accumulator filled with gas).
At full discharge (final gas state): pressure = $P_2$, gas volume = $V_2$.

**Step 2 — Apply Polytropic Law between states 1 and 2:**

$$
P_1 \, V_A^{\,n} = P_2 \, V_2^{\,n}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$
V_2^{\,n} = \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) V_A^{\,n}
$$

$$
V_2 = V_A \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n}
$$

*This row explains: Taking the $n$-th root on both sides gives the gas volume at maximum pressure in terms of total accumulator volume.*

**Step 4 — Effective volume by definition $V_d = V_A - V_2$:**

$$
V_d = V_A - V_A \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor $V_A$:**

$$
V_d = V_A \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n} \,\right]
$$

*This row explains: This is the **KTU standard formula** — it directly gives the usable oil volume from a given accumulator size and pressure ratio.*

**Step 6 — Special cases:**

- Isothermal ($n = 1$): $V_d = V_A \left[\, 1 - \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \,\right]$
- Adiabatic ($n = 1.4$): $V_d = V_A \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!0.714} \,\right]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does adiabatic give MORE discharge than isothermal for the same $P_1/P_2 < 1$?**
> Because $(P_1/P_2)^{0.714} > (P_1/P_2)^1$ when $P_1/P_2 < 1$, the bracket becomes smaller — meaning $V_d$ is *larger* under adiabatic assumption. This is why KTU problems using $n = 1.4$ typically give larger usable volumes.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pump Capacity from Accumulator Cycle

**Objective:** During one operating cycle of duration $t$ seconds, an actuator uses volume $V_d$ of oil per cycle, the pump alone supplies this volume for a fraction $k$ of the cycle, and the accumulator covers the remainder. Derive the pump capacity $Q_p$.

**Given:** During cycle: pump runs for time $k \cdot t$, accumulator supplies for time $(1-k) \cdot t$, total volume demanded per cycle = $V_d$.

**Step 1 — Total volume per cycle:**

$$
V_{cycle} = V_d
$$

**Step 2 — Volume supplied by pump in fraction $k \cdot t$:**

$$
V_{pump} = Q_p \cdot k \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — During the pump-off fraction $(1-k)t$, the accumulator supplies:**

$$
V_{acc} = V_d - Q_p \cdot k \cdot t
$$

*This row explains: The accumulator must "make up the difference" between what the load demands and what the pump can deliver during its running interval.*

**Step 4 — But also, over $N$ cycles per minute, total pump delivery per minute is $Q_p$. So:**

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{V_d \, N}{60 \, k}
$$

*This row explains: Each minute has $N$ cycles; pump handles only fraction $k$ of each cycle; so pump flow = total demand per minute ÷ pump-on fraction.*

**Step 5 — Alternate form using accumulator volume:**

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{N}{60 \, k} \, V_A \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\!1/n} \,\right]
$$

### 3.3 Fully Solved KTU-Style Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> This is the *exact* numerical type asked in KTU End-Semester Examinations (14-mark problems).

**Problem:** A hydraulic system requires **15 litres** of oil per cycle at **140 bar** working pressure. The pump delivers oil at **20 L/min**, the cycle time is **30 seconds**, and the pre-charge pressure is **90 bar**. Assuming **isothermal compression**, determine:
> (a) Effective volume $V_d$ required,
> (b) Total accumulator volume $V_A$,
> (c) Whether the existing pump is adequate, and
> (d) Energy stored at maximum pressure.

**Given:**
- $V_d = 15$ L
- $P_2 = 140$ bar $= 14 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $P_1 = 90$ bar $= 9 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $Q_p = 20$ L/min
- $t = 30$ s
- $n = 1.0$ (isothermal)

**Solution (a) — Effective Volume:**

$$
V_d = 15 \text{ L} \quad \text{(given — this IS the required effective volume)}
$$

**Solution (b) — Total Accumulator Volume:**

$$
V_A = \dfrac{V_d}{1 - (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}} = \dfrac{15}{1 - (90/140)^{1.0}}
$$

$$
V_A = \dfrac{15}{1 - 0.6429} = \dfrac{15}{0.3571}
$$

$$
V_A = 42.0 \text{ L}
$$

*This row explains: Working backward from required discharge to determine the physical accumulator tank size — typical KTU calculation.*

**Solution (c) — Adequacy of Pump:**

Volume supplied by pump in 30 s:

$$
V_{pump} = Q_p \times \dfrac{t}{60} = 20 \times \dfrac{30}{60} = 10 \text{ L}
$$

Deficit that must be supplied by accumulator:

$$
V_{deficit} = 15 - 10 = 5 \text{ L}
$$

Since $V_{deficit} = 5$ L $< V_d = 15$ L, the accumulator can cover the shortfall.

**Pump is ADEQUATE** ✓

*This row explains: This is the practical KTU insight — does the pump + accumulator combination meet peak demand?*

**Solution (d) — Energy Stored (at $n = 1$, isothermal):**

$$
E = P_1 V_A \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

Convert: $V_A = 42 \text{ L} = 42 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$

$$
E = (9 \times 10^{6})(42 \times 10^{-3}) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{140}{90}\right)
$$

$$
E = 378{,}000 \times \ln(1.5556) = 378{,}000 \times 0.4418
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 1.67 \times 10^{5} \text{ J} = 167 \text{ kJ}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Accumulator in a Hydraulic Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reservoir Tank] -->|Suction| A2[Hydraulic Pump]
    A2 -->|Delivery Q_p| A3[Check Valve NRV]
    A3 -->|High Pressure Line| A4[Pressure Relief Valve]
    A3 -->|Charges| A5[Gas Charged Accumulator]
    A3 -->|Supplies| A6[Directional Control Valve DCV]
    A6 -->|Drives| A7[Hydraulic Actuator Cylinder Motor]
    A5 -.->|Discharges V_d on peak demand| A6
    A4 -->|Return| A1
    A7 -->|Return| A1
    A5 --- A5a[Precharge P1 Nitrogen]
    A5 --- A5b[Bladder Piston Diaphragm separator]
    A5 --- A5c[Oil port connected to line]
```

*This row explains the figure:* The diagram maps energy flow in a typical accumulator-equipped circuit. The pump charges the accumulator during low-demand phases; on peak demand, both pump **and** accumulator supply the actuator simultaneously. The non-return valve (NRV) prevents back-flow to the pump.

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Accumulators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Hydraulic Accumulators] --> B2[Weight Loaded Gravity type]
    B1 --> B3[Spring Loaded type]
    B1 --> B4[Gas Loaded Hydro Pneumatic]
    B2 --> B2a[Constant pressure output]
    B3 --> B3a[Variable pressure linear]
    B4 --> B4a[Bladder type]
    B4 --> B4b[Piston type]
    B4 --> B4c[Diaphragm type]
    B4a --> B4a1[Fast response high cycle]
    B4b --> B4b1[Large capacity high pressure]
    B4c --> B4c1[Compact low volume]
```

*This row explains:* The classification tree highlights that gas-loaded variants are the most subdivided category — this matches industry dominance and the KTU examiner's emphasis.

### 4.3 Functional States of a Gas-Charged Accumulator

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Precharge: Gas filled at P1 nitrogen
    Precharge --> Charging: Pump delivers oil
    Charging --> Charged: Pressure rises to P2
    Charged --> Holding: Pump stops check valve closes
    Holding --> Discharging: Actuator demand exceeds pump
    Discharging --> Precharge: Gas re expands oil expelled
    Discharging --> Charged: Pump resumes supply
```

*This row explains:* Each state corresponds to a physically distinct pressure-volume condition; KTU problems often ask to plot the **P-V indicator diagram** of this cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *Define a hydraulic accumulator and list any four of its functions in a hydraulic system.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A **hydraulic accumulator** is a device that stores hydraulic fluid under pressure, using an external energy source (gas, spring, or weight), and releases the stored energy on demand.
