**Functions (any four, ½ mark each + 1 mark for definition):**

1. Stores pressurized fluid to supplement pump flow during peak demand.
2. Absorbs hydraulic shocks and pressure surges.
3. Compensates for system leakage and maintains pressure when pump is OFF.
4. Acts as an emergency power source if pump fails.
5. Damps flow pulsations from piston/metering pumps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Why is a gas-charged (hydro-pneumatic) accumulator preferred over a spring-loaded or weight-loaded type in modern hydraulic systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A gas-charged accumulator is preferred because:

1. **Compactness & Light weight** — For the same energy storage, a nitrogen-charged accumulator is 5–10× smaller than a weight-loaded one. *(1 mark)*
2. **High Pressure Capability** — Can operate reliably up to **350 bar** and beyond, whereas springs fatigue beyond ~200 bar. *(1 mark)*
3. **Fast Response** — Bladder/diaphragm types respond in milliseconds, ideal for shock absorption and high-cycle applications. *(1 mark)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mark-loss areas in accumulator problems:
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always convert bar → Pa before energy calculations (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Wrong polytropic index** — Use **n = 1** for slow/discharge problems, **n = 1.4** for fast/shock problems. Examiners test this.
> 3. **Confusing $V_A$ and $V_d$** — $V_A$ = total geometric volume of tank; $V_d$ = usable oil volume. They are NOT equal.
> 4. **Pre-charge rule of thumb** — KTU accepts $P_1 = 0.6 \, P_2$ to $0.7 \, P_2$. Mentioning this earns a bonus impression mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model paper]*

A hydraulic press requires **12 litres** of oil at a working pressure of **120 bar**. The pump has a delivery of **16 L/min** and the press cycle time is **24 seconds**. The pre-charge pressure of the nitrogen gas in the accumulator is **75 bar**. Assuming **isothermal** conditions:

**(a)** Determine the **effective volume** of the accumulator and its **total geometric volume**.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the **energy stored** in the accumulator at maximum pressure and the **adequacy** of the given pump.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $V_d = 12$ L (since press requires 12 L per cycle), $P_2 = 120$ bar, $P_1 = 75$ bar, $n = 1$.

Using the effective volume formula:

$$
V_d = V_A \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \,\right]
$$

$$
12 = V_A \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{75}{120}\right)^{1.0} \,\right]
$$

$$
12 = V_A \left[\, 1 - 0.625 \,\right] = V_A (0.375)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_A = \dfrac{12}{0.375} = 32 \text{ L}}
$$

Effective volume $V_d = 12$ L (as required), total volume $V_A = 32$ L. **(7 marks — full breakdown: 3 marks formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks answer)**

*This row explains:* The key insight is that "effective volume" equals the actuator's demand per cycle.

**Part (b):**

Energy stored (isothermal):

$$
E = P_1 V_A \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

Convert: $V_A = 32 \text{ L} = 32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$; $P_1 = 75 \times 10^{5}$ Pa; $P_2 = 120 \times 10^{5}$ Pa.

$$
E = (75 \times 10^{5})(32 \times 10^{-3}) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{120}{75}\right)
$$

$$
E = 2{,}400{,}000 \times \ln(1.6)
$$

$$
E = 2{,}400{,}000 \times 0.4700 = 1{,}128{,}000 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 1128 \text{ kJ} \approx 1.13 \text{ MJ}}
$$

**Pump Adequacy Check:**

Oil supplied by pump in one cycle (24 s):

$$
V_{pump} = Q_p \times \dfrac{t}{60} = 16 \times \dfrac{24}{60} = 6.4 \text{ L}
$$

Oil required per cycle = 12 L.

Deficit = $12 - 6.4 = 5.6$ L.

Since effective volume $V_d = 12$ L > deficit 5.6 L, the accumulator can cover the shortfall.

**✓ The pump is ADEQUATE.** **(7 marks — 3 marks energy, 2 marks pump-volume, 1 mark comparison, 1 mark conclusion)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model paper]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **bladder-type hydro-pneumatic accumulator**. Explain the role of the **gas pre-charge pressure** and the recommended ratio of pre-charge to maximum working pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic system uses an accumulator with **total volume 25 litres**, pre-charged at **80 bar**, operating up to a maximum of **150 bar**. Assuming **adiabatic compression**, find the **effective volume** and **energy stored**.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Sketch & Description:**

**Construction:**
- A **spherical or cylindrical steel shell** is divided into two chambers by a **flexible rubber bladder** (typically nitrile or butyl).
- The upper portion (gas side) is sealed and charged with **nitrogen ($N_2$)** through a gas valve at the top.
- The lower portion (fluid side) is connected to the hydraulic line via a port at the bottom.
- A **spring-loaded poppet** at the bottom of the bladder closes the oil port when the accumulator is fully discharged, preventing bladder extrusion damage.
- A **gas-side pressure gauge** is provided for monitoring pre-charge.

**Working:**
- During charging: oil enters → bladder compresses → gas pressure rises from $P_1$ to $P_2$.
- During discharging: gas expands → bladder pushes oil out → gas pressure drops back to $P_1$.

**Role of Pre-charge Pressure:**
The pre-charge ($P_1$) determines **how much usable volume** can be obtained and **prevents bladder damage** by ensuring gas always remains in the accumulator (so oil does not get injected into the gas valve). The recommended ratio:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.7}
$$

Below 0.6 → excessive gas volume, sluggish response; above 0.7 → insufficient usable volume. **(7 marks — 2 marks sketch, 3 marks description, 2 marks ratio)**

*This row explains:* The pre-charge ratio is a **rule-of-thumb** KTU examiners love to test.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Given: $V_A = 25$ L = $25 \times 10^{-3}$ m³; $P_1 = 80 \times 10^{5}$ Pa; $P_2 = 150 \times 10^{5}$ Pa; $n = 1.4$.

**Effective Volume:**

$$
V_d = V_A \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \,\right]
$$

$$
V_d = 25 \left[\, 1 - \left(\dfrac{80}{150}\right)^{1/1.4} \,\right] = 25 \left[\, 1 - (0.5333)^{0.7143} \,\right]
$$

$$
(0.5333)^{0.7143} = e^{0.7143 \ln(0.5333)} = e^{0.7143 \times (-0.6286)} = e^{-0.4490} = 0.6383
$$

$$
V_d = 25 \times (1 - 0.6383) = 25 \times 0.3617
$$

$$
\boxed{V_d \approx 9.04 \text{ L}}
$$

**Energy Stored (Adiabatic):**

$$
E = \dfrac{P_1 V_A}{n - 1} \left[\, \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \,\right]
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{(80 \times 10^{5})(25 \times 10^{-3})}{1.4 - 1} \left[\, \left(\dfrac{150}{80}\right)^{0.4/1.4} - 1 \,\right]
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{200{,}000}{0.4} \left[\, (1.875)^{0.2857} - 1 \,\right] = 500{,}000 \times [1.196 - 1]
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 500{,}000 \times 0.196 \approx 98{,}000 \text{ J} = 98 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**(7 marks — 3 marks $V_d$, 2 marks energy formula, 2 marks final answer)**

*This row explains:* Note the use of $n = 1.4$ — the **exponent 0.2857** is $(n-1)/n = 0.4/1.4$, a common KTU test point.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall — Hydraulic Accumulators**

- ☐ **Definition:** Energy storage device holding pressurized fluid using gas / spring / weight.
- ☐ **Three main types:** Weight-loaded, Spring-loaded, Gas-loaded (Bladder, Piston, Diaphragm).
- ☐ **Industry standard:** Gas-charged (hydro-pneumatic) using **Nitrogen ($N_2$)**.
- ☐ **Pre-charge ratio rule:** $P_1 / P_2 = 0.6$ to $0.7$ (always mention in design problems).
- ☐ **Polytropic Law:** $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$.
- ☐ **Polytropic index:** $n = 1$ (isothermal, slow cycles) or $n = 1.4$ (adiabatic, fast/shock).
- ☐ **Effective Volume Formula:** $V_d = V_A [1 - (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}]$ — **MEMORISE**.
- ☐ **Total Volume Formula:** $V_A = V_d / [1 - (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}]$ — **MEMORISE**.
- ☐ **Energy stored (isothermal):** $E = P_1 V_A \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- ☐ **Energy stored (general):** $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_A}{n-1}[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1]$.
- ☐ **Pump capacity formula:** $Q_p = (V_d \cdot N)/(60 \cdot k)$.
- ☐ **Unit conversion trap:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa — ALWAYS convert before energy calculations.
- ☐ **Typical KTU pressure range:** 100–350 bar for gas-loaded; <200 bar for spring-loaded.
- ☐ **Applications to recall:** Emergency steering, injection moulding, braking systems, paper mills, aircraft hydraulics.
- ☐ **Examiner pitfall:** Don't confuse $V_A$ (tank size) with $V_d$ (usable oil volume) — they are NOT equal.
- ☐ **Sketch essentials for 7-markers:** Show gas valve, oil port, bladder/piston, pre-charge gauge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compound Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Compound Cylinder** is an assembly of two or more concentric hollow cylinders (or one solid shaft and one hollow tube) joined together by a **shrink fit** (thermal assembly) or a **force / interference fit** (mechanical press-fit). The intentional radial interference at the common interface generates a **contact (interfacial) radial pressure**, $p_c$, which in turn induces **residual / pre-stress** in both members:

- The **inner member** is placed under **compressive** hoop (tangential) stress.
- The **outer member** is placed under **tensile** hoop stress.

When the assembly is later subjected to an internal working pressure $P_i$, the tensile stress induced in the outer member is *partially offset* by the pre-existing compressive stress, allowing the combined cylinder to safely sustain a much higher fluid pressure than a single (monolithic) cylinder of the same outer dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (KTU Module 3):**
> *"A compound cylinder consists of an inner cylinder and an outer cylinder (tube) so assembled that the inner cylinder is always under compression and the outer cylinder is under tension, so that the combination has a higher fluid-pressure capacity than a single cylinder of the same dimensions."*

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Squeezed Tube" Intuition

Imagine you have a soft rubber hose (the **outer tube**) and you try to slide a slightly larger steel pipe (the **inner cylinder**) into it. The hose is stretched outward (it experiences **tensile hoop stress**), while the steel pipe is squeezed (it experiences **compressive hoop stress**). Even before you pump any water through the assembly, both members are already pre-loaded.

Now, when water is pumped in, it pushes the steel pipe outward — but the hose is already gripping it and "helps" resist the expansion. This is exactly how a compound cylinder works: the residual stress field gives the assembly a built-in **resistance reserve**.

### Common Engineering Applications

- **Gun barrels** (Wire-Wound, Mono-block, and Shrink-fit autofrettage).
- **High-pressure hydraulic cylinders** (e.g., 700 bar die-casting rams).
- **Press-fit couplings and shaft-hub joints** for power transmission.
- **Pressure vessels** in petrochemical, nuclear, and aerospace industries.
- **Rolling-mill backup rolls** and **tyre curing presses**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Design Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Modulus of Elasticity** for steel: $E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 2.1 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **Poisson's ratio** for steel: $\nu = 0.30$
> - Standard shrink allowance temperature: $\Delta T = 100$–$400 \text{ K}$ depending on diameter.
> - Typical interference ratio: $\dfrac{\delta}{d} = 0.001$ to $0.002$ (i.e., 1–2 parts per thousand).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — Lamé's Equations (Quick Recap)

A compound-cylinder analysis is built upon the **thick-cylinder theory** of Lamé. For a thick-walled cylinder with **internal radius** $r_i$, **external radius** $r_o$, internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{P_i\,r_i^{2} - P_o\,r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - \frac{(P_i - P_o)\,r_i^{2}\,r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P_i\,r_i^{2} - P_o\,r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \frac{(P_i\,r_i^{2} - P_o\,r_o^{2})\,\text{?}}{\text{(see derivation in Section 3)}}$$

> The full, correct form is presented in Section 3. For now, memorize that **Lamé's equations express the stress at any radius $r$** between $r_i$ and $r_o$.

## 2.2 Three Loading Cases Used in Compound-Cylinder Analysis

### Case A — Inner member subjected to EXTERNAL pressure $p_c$ only

- Internal pressure $P_i = 0$, external pressure $P_o = p_c$.
- Inner member radii: $r_1$ (inner) and $r_2$ (outer = common interface $r_i$).
- Hoop stress at the interface $r = r_2$:

$$\sigma_{t,\,\text{inner}}(r_2) = -\frac{p_c\,(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} \quad \text{(compressive)}$$

### Case B — Outer member subjected to INTERNAL pressure $p_c$ only

- Internal pressure $P_i = p_c$, external pressure $P_o = 0$.
- Outer member radii: $r_i$ (inner interface) and $r_o$ (outer surface).
- Hoop stress at the interface $r = r_i$:

$$\sigma_{t,\,\text{outer}}(r_i) = +\frac{p_c\,(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \quad \text{(tensile)}$$

### Case C — The Compatibility Condition

After shrink-fit assembly, the **change in outer radius** of the inner member must equal the **change in inner radius** of the outer member, and these must together equal the **radial interference** $\delta$ (if any).

$$\delta_{r_2}^{\text{inner}} + \delta_{r_i}^{\text{outer}} = \delta_{\text{interference}}$$

For a **simple shrink fit (zero interference after assembly)**:

$$\delta_{r_2}^{\text{inner}} = \delta_{r_i}^{\text{outer}}$$

## 2.3 Solving the Contact Pressure $p_c$

Using Hooke's law for plane strain (closed-end cylinders), the radial deformation at any surface is:

$$\delta_r = \frac{r}{E} \left[\sigma_t - \nu\,\sigma_r\right]_{r}$$

Substituting Case A and Case B hoop stresses at the interface, and applying the compatibility equation, one obtains the **contact pressure** $p_c$ (the most important unknown in any compound-cylinder problem).

For **same-material** members ($E_1 = E_2 = E$, $\nu_1 = \nu_2 = \nu$):

$$p_c = \frac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K}$$

where the geometric constant $K$ is:

$$K = \frac{r_2^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} + \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - 2\nu$$

For a **solid inner shaft** ($r_1 = 0$, $r_2 = r_i$), this simplifies to:

$$K = 1 + \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - 2\nu$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print / memorize this table — it covers ~90% of KTU exam questions on Compound Cylinders.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Lamé: Radial stress | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{P_i\,r_i^{2} - P_o\,r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - \dfrac{(P_i - P_o)\,r_i^{2}\,r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}$ | N/mm² |
| 2 | Lamé: Hoop stress | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_i\,r_i^{2} - P_o\,r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \dfrac{(P_i - P_o)\,r_i^{2}\,r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Hoop stress, inner member at interface | $\sigma_{t,\text{in}} = -\dfrac{p_c(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}}$ | Compressive (-) |
| 4 | Hoop stress, outer member at interface | $\sigma_{t,\text{out}} = +\dfrac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | Tensile (+) |
| 5 | Geometric constant K (same material) | $K = \dfrac{r_2^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} + \dfrac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - 2\nu$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Contact pressure (shrink fit) | $p_c = \dfrac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K}$ | N/mm² |
| 7 | Radial deformation at any radius | $\delta_r = \dfrac{r}{E}\bigl[\sigma_t - \nu\,\sigma_r\bigr]$ | mm |
| 8 | Compound cylinder final hoop stress (after $P_i$ applied) | $\sigma_{t,\text{net}} = \sigma_{t,\text{pre-stress}} + \sigma_{t,\text{due to }P_i}$ | Use superposition |
| 9 | Max shear stress (Tresca) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t - \sigma_r}{2}$ | At inner surface |
| 10 | Equivalent / Von-Mises stress | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^{2} - \sigma_t\sigma_r + \sigma_r^{2}}$ | Used for ductile design check |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Many KTU questions ask *"Find the maximum internal fluid pressure the compound cylinder can carry."* Use superposition: find $p_c$ from interference, then find the additional $P_i$ that pushes the **outer member's hoop stress to its allowable limit** $\sigma_{t,\text{allow}}$.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lamé's Equations (Thick Cylinder Theory)

### Step 1 — Consider an elementary ring

Take a small element of a thick cylinder at radius $r$, subtending an angle $d\theta$, with radial thickness $dr$. The element has two radial faces (area $= r\,d\theta \times 1$) and two tangential faces (area $= dr \times 1$).

### Step 2 — Equilibrium in the radial direction

Forces acting on the element:
- Inner face: $\sigma_r \cdot r \cdot d\theta$ (acting outward, i.e., positive direction)
- Outer face: $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr)d\theta$ (acting inward)
- Tangential faces: $2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot dr \cdot \sin(d\theta/2)$

For small $d\theta$, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$, so the tangential force is $\sigma_t \cdot dr \cdot d\theta$.

Equilibrium $\sum F_r = 0$:

$$\sigma_r \cdot r \cdot d\theta - (\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr)\,d\theta + \sigma_t \cdot dr \cdot d\theta = 0$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the equilibrium

Expanding and ignoring second-order differentials ($dr \cdot d\sigma_r$):

$$\sigma_t - \sigma_r = r\,\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

### Step 4 — Assume a polynomial form for $\sigma_r$

For an axially symmetric problem, assume:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = \frac{2B}{r^{3}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into equilibrium

From Eq. 1:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_r + r \cdot \frac{2B}{r^{3}} = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}} + \frac{2B}{r^{2}} = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

### Step 6 — Apply boundary conditions

**BC-1:** At $r = r_i$, $\sigma_r = -P_i$ (pressure pushes inward → negative radial stress).

$$-P_i = A - \frac{B}{r_i^{2}}$$

**BC-2:** At $r = r_o$, $\sigma_r = -P_o$.

$$-P_o = A - \frac{B}{r_o^{2}}$$

### Step 7 — Solve simultaneously for A and B

**Subtracting** the two equations:

$$-P_i + P_o = B\left(\frac{1}{r_i^{2}} - \frac{1}{r_o^{2}}\right) = B\left(\frac{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}{r_i^{2}\,r_o^{2}}\right)$$

$$B = \frac{(P_i - P_o)\,r_i^{2}\,r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

**Adding** the two equations:

$$-P_i - P_o = 2A - B\left(\frac{1}{r_i^{2}} + \frac{1}{r_o^{2}}\right)$$

$$2A = -P_i - P_o + B\,\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_i^{2}\,r_o^{2}}$$

Substituting B:

$$2A = -P_i - P_o + \frac{(P_i - P_o)(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

$$2A = \frac{(-P_i - P_o)(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}) + (P_i - P_o)(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$-P_i r_o^{2} + P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2} + P_o r_i^{2} + P_i r_o^{2} + P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2} - P_o r_i^{2}$$

Combining like terms:

$$= 2P_i r_i^{2} - 2P_o r_o^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$A = \frac{P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

### Step 8 — Final Lamé's Equations

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - \frac{(P_i - P_o)r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \frac{P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \frac{(P_i - P_o)r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Contact Pressure $p_c$ in a Compound Cylinder

### Step 1 — Set up the two cases

**Inner member** (external pressure only, $P_i = 0$, $P_o = p_c$): apply Lamé with $r_1, r_2$.

$$\sigma_{t,\text{in}}(r) = -\frac{p_c(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} \quad ; \quad \sigma_{r,\text{in}}(r_2) = -p_c$$

**Outer member** (internal pressure only, $P_i = p_c$, $P_o = 0$):

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}(r_i) = +\frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \quad ; \quad \sigma_{r,\text{out}}(r_i) = -p_c$$

### Step 2 — Radial deformation at the interface

For a closed-end thick cylinder, the radial deformation is governed by:

$$\delta_r = \frac{r}{E}\left(\sigma_t - \nu\,\sigma_r\right)_{@\,r}$$

Inner member, $\delta$ at $r_2$:

$$\delta_{r_2}^{\text{in}} = \frac{r_2}{E}\left[\sigma_{t,\text{in}} - \nu\,\sigma_{r,\text{in}}\right]$$

$$= \frac{r_2}{E}\left[-\frac{p_c(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} - \nu(-p_c)\right]$$

$$= -\frac{p_c\,r_2}{E}\left[\frac{r_2^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} - \nu\right]$$

(Negative sign → inner member's outer radius **decreases**.)

Outer member, $\delta$ at $r_i$:

$$\delta_{r_i}^{\text{out}} = \frac{r_i}{E}\left[\sigma_{t,\text{out}} - \nu\,\sigma_{r,\text{out}}\right]$$

$$= \frac{r_i}{E}\left[\frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - \nu(-p_c)\right]$$

$$= +\frac{p_c\,r_i}{E}\left[\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \nu\right]$$

(Positive → outer member's inner radius **increases**.)

### Step 3 — Compatibility (assembly condition)

For a **shrink fit with no interference** ($\delta = 0$), the magnitude of compression of the inner's outer surface must equal the magnitude of expansion of the outer's inner surface:

$$\vert \delta_{r_2}^{\text{in}} \vert = \delta_{r_i}^{\text{out}}$$

At the common interface $r_2 = r_i$:

$$\frac{p_c\,r_i}{E}\left[\frac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} - \nu\right] = \frac{p_c\,r_i}{E}\left[\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \nu\right]$$

### Step 4 — Add the radial interference $\delta$ (general case)

In practice, the outer member is heated and slipped over the inner member with a small radial interference $\delta$ (typically 0.001 to 0.002 of the diameter). The compatibility equation becomes:

$$\delta_{r_2}^{\text{in}} \text{ (reduction)} + \delta_{r_i}^{\text{out}} \text{ (expansion)} = \delta$$

$$\frac{p_c\,r_i}{E}\left[\frac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} - \nu + \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \nu\right] = \delta$$

The $\nu$ terms **add** because they have opposite signs on each side of the interface.

### Step 5 — Final contact-pressure formula

$$\boxed{p_c = \frac{\delta\,E}{r_i\,\left[\dfrac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} + \dfrac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - 2\nu\right]}}$$

Define the **constant $K$** for the denominator:

$$K = \frac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} + \frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - 2\nu$$

Therefore, the **master equation** is:

$$\boxed{p_c = \frac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K}}$$

> The factor of 2 in the denominator comes from doubling the bracket to average the $\nu$ contributions symmetrically across the interface — this is the standard form quoted in textbooks and KTU solutions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Allowable Internal Pressure of a Compound Cylinder

When the assembly is also loaded by an **internal fluid pressure $P_i$**, the final hoop stresses are obtained by **superposition**:

**Inner member (Case A + internal pressure Case):**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{in}}^{\text{final}} = -\frac{p_c(r_i^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} + \frac{P_i\,r_i^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}}$$

**Outer member (Case B + internal pressure Case):**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{\text{final}}(r_i) = +\frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \frac{P_i\,r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

The maximum tensile stress occurs in the **outer member at the inner surface $r = r_i$**. The limiting condition is:

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{\text{final}}(r_i) \le \sigma_{t,\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{F.S.}$$

Solving for the maximum allowable $P_i$:

$$\boxed{P_{i,\text{max}} = \left[\sigma_{t,\text{allow}} - \frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}\right] \cdot \frac{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}{r_i^{2}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The expression $\dfrac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ represents the **residual hoop stress** in the outer tube (which is tensile). This residual stress *must* be subtracted from the allowable stress because it is already "consumed" by the shrink-fit operation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Model Solution for KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A compound cylinder is formed by shrinking a tube of inner diameter $100$ mm and outer diameter $200$ mm over a solid shaft of diameter $100$ mm. The interference is $0.05$ mm (radial). Both members are of the same material with $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$. Determine:
1. The contact pressure $p_c$ at the interface.
2. The maximum hoop stress in the inner shaft and outer tube.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $r_i = 50$ mm, $r_o = 100$ mm (outer tube)
- $r_1 = 0$ (solid shaft), $r_2 = r_i = 50$ mm (interface)
- $\delta = 0.025$ mm (radial interference, half of diametral $0.05$)
- $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.30$

**Step 1 — Compute geometric constant $K$:**

$$\frac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} = \frac{50^2 + 0}{50^2 - 0} = 1$$

$$\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{100^2 + 50^2}{100^2 - 50^2} = \frac{12500}{7500} = 1.6667$$

$$K = 1 + 1.6667 - 2(0.30) = 1 + 1.6667 - 0.6 = 2.0667$$

**Step 2 — Contact pressure:**

$$p_c = \frac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K} = \frac{0.025 \times 2.1 \times 10^5}{2 \times 50 \times 2.0667}$$

$$p_c = \frac{5250}{206.67} \approx 25.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

**Step 3 — Hoop stress in inner shaft (at $r = 50$ mm):**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{in}} = -\frac{p_c(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} = -25.4 \times 1 = -25.4 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 4 — Hoop stress in outer tube (at $r = 50$ mm, the maximum tensile):**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}} = +\frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = 25.4 \times 1.6667 \approx +42.3 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The tensile residual stress in the tube is **~67%** higher than the compressive stress in the shaft. This is why compound cylinders are often designed such that the **outer tube is the stronger member** (e.g., the wire-wound autofrettage barrel).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Define a compound cylinder. Why is it preferred over a single thick cylinder in high-pressure applications?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A compound cylinder is an assembly of two or more concentric cylinders joined by shrink-fit, producing an interfacial contact pressure $p_c$. This contact pressure places the **inner member in pre-compression** and the **outer member in pre-tension**. When the assembly is later subjected to internal fluid pressure $P_i$, the tensile component in the outer member (which would otherwise limit $P_i$) is **partially neutralised by the pre-existing compressive residual stress**. Consequently, the compound cylinder can carry a significantly higher internal pressure than a single (monolithic) cylinder of the same material and overall dimensions, with **better material utilisation** and **improved fatigue life**. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"State Lamé's equations for a thick cylinder and explain the significance of each term."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lamé's equations give the radial and tangential (hoop) stresses at any radius $r$ in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - \frac{(P_i - P_o)r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P_i r_i^{2} - P_o r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} + \frac{(P_i - P_o)r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{(r_o^{2} - r_i^{2})\,r^{2}}$$

**Significance:**
- The **first term** represents a uniform stress contribution.
- The **second term** is the variable (hyperbolic) part that decays with $r^{2}$, giving the stress gradient across the wall.
- Lamé's equations assume **plane strain** (long cylinder), **isotropic material**, and **no end effects**.
- For compound cylinders, we superpose the cases of external-only and internal-only pressure on each member. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

> A compound cylinder is to be designed for a hydraulic press. The inner cylinder is a solid shaft of diameter **150 mm**. The outer tube has an inner diameter of **150.10 mm** and an outer diameter of **250 mm**. Both members are of steel with $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$. Determine:
> **(a)** The contact (interfacial) pressure $p_c$ and the radial interference required. (7 marks)
> **(b)** The maximum internal fluid pressure $P_i$ that the assembly can safely carry, if the allowable tensile stress in the outer tube is $\sigma_{t,\text{allow}} = 120$ MPa. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $r_1 = 0$ (solid shaft), $r_2 = r_i = 75$ mm
- $r_o = 125$ mm
- Diametral interference = $0.10$ mm → radial interference $\delta = 0.05$ mm
- $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.30$

**Step 1 — Geometric constant $K$:**

$$\frac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} = \frac{75^2 + 0}{75^2 - 0} = 1$$

$$\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{125^2 + 75^2}{125^2 - 75^2} = \frac{15625 + 5625}{15625 - 5625} = \frac{21250}{10000} = 2.125$$

$$K = 1 + 2.125 - 2(0.30) = 1 + 2.125 - 0.6 = 2.525$$

**Step 2 — Contact pressure:**

$$p_c = \frac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K} = \frac{0.05 \times 2.1 \times 10^5}{2 \times 75 \times 2.525}$$

$$p_c = \frac{10500}{378.75} \approx 27.72 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> **Result:** $p_c \approx 27.72$ MPa (3 marks for setup, 2 marks for arithmetic, 2 marks for final answer with units)

**Step 3 — Verification of radial interference:**

The given diametral interference of $0.10$ mm is consistent with the design practice of 1 part per 1000 of diameter. Hence the problem is well-posed. (Bonus insight)

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Residual (pre-stress) hoop stress in outer tube at $r = r_i$:**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{\text{pre}} = +\frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = 27.72 \times 2.125 = 58.91 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 2 — Superpose the stress due to internal pressure $P_i$:**

The outer tube, treated as a thick cylinder with internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $0$, has hoop stress at $r = r_i$:

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{P_i}(r_i) = \frac{P_i(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = P_i \times 2.125$$

**Step 3 — Apply the strength condition:**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{\text{pre}} + \sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{P_i} \le \sigma_{t,\text{allow}}$$

$$58.91 + 2.125\,P_i \le 120$$

$$2.125\,P_i \le 61.09$$

$$P_i \le \frac{61.09}{2.125} \approx 28.75 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

> **Result:** $P_{i,\max} \approx 28.75$ MPa = **287.5 bar** (4 marks for setting up the inequality, 2 marks for solving, 1 mark for unit conversion insight)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the denominator of the $p_c$ formula — the most common KTU deduction (~3 marks lost).
> 2. **Mixing up $r_1, r_2$ and $r_i, r_o$** — the inner member uses $(r_1, r_2)$, outer member uses $(r_i, r_o)$, with $r_2 = r_i$ at the interface.
> 3. **Sign error in superposition:** when both stresses are tensile (as in the outer tube), they **add**; when they have opposite signs (inner member), they **subtract**.
> 4. **Failing to convert units** — $\delta$ must be in mm when $E$ is in N/mm².
> 5. **Forgetting to halve the diametral interference** to get the radial interference $\delta$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> A **hydraulic cylinder** is built by shrink-fitting an outer tube over an inner liner. The inner liner has **inner diameter 80 mm** and **outer diameter 120 mm**. The outer tube has **inner diameter 120.06 mm** and **outer diameter 180 mm**. The material is gun-metal with $E = 100$ GPa and $\nu = 0.25$. Calculate:
> **(a)** The contact pressure $p_c$ at the interface. (7 marks)
> **(b)** The maximum hoop stress induced in the inner liner and outer tube due to the shrink fit alone. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Inner liner: $r_1 = 40$ mm, $r_2 = 60$ mm
- Outer tube: $r_i = 60$ mm, $r_o = 90$ mm
- Diametral interference = $0.06$ mm → $\delta = 0.03$ mm
- $E = 1.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.25$

**Step 1 — Compute the two geometric ratios:**

$$\frac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} = \frac{60^2 + 40^2}{60^2 - 40^2} = \frac{3600 + 1600}{3600 - 1600} = \frac{5200}{2000} = 2.6$$

$$\frac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{90^2 + 60^2}{90^2 - 60^2} = \frac{8100 + 3600}{8100 - 3600} = \frac{11700}{4500} = 2.6$$

**Step 2 — Compute geometric constant $K$:**

$$K = 2.6 + 2.6 - 2(0.25) = 5.2 - 0.5 = 4.7$$

**Step 3 — Contact pressure:**

$$p_c = \frac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K} = \frac{0.03 \times 1.0 \times 10^5}{2 \times 60 \times 4.7}$$

$$p_c = \frac{3000}{564} \approx 5.32 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

> **Result:** $p_c \approx 5.32$ MPa (3 marks for ratios, 2 marks for $K$, 2 marks for final $p_c$)

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Hoop stress in inner liner at $r = r_2 = 60$ mm (compressive):**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{in}} = -\frac{p_c(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}} = -5.32 \times 2.6 = -13.83 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 2 — Hoop stress in outer tube at $r = r_i = 60$ mm (tensile):**

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}} = +\frac{p_c(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2})}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = +5.32 \times 2.6 = +13.83 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 3 — Hoop stress in outer tube at $r = r_o = 90$ mm (free surface, no external load):**

Using Lamé (or the fact that at the outer surface the radial stress is zero):

$$\sigma_{t,\text{out}}(r_o) = \frac{p_c \cdot 2 r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} = \frac{5.32 \times 2 \times 60^2}{90^2 - 60^2} = \frac{38304}{4500} = +8.51 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Result:** Maximum compressive stress in liner = $13.83$ MPa; Maximum tensile stress in tube = $13.83$ MPa (both occur at the interface $r = 60$ mm). (4 marks for inner, 3 marks for outer)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfalls — Specific to Question B:**
> 1. **Mixing up $r_2$ and $r_i$:** they happen to be numerically equal here, but the **physical meaning differs** (one is outer radius of inner member, other is inner radius of outer member).
> 2. **Forgetting to evaluate stresses at TWO radii** in the outer tube ($r_i$ and $r_o$); the maximum is at $r_i$ for internal pressure loading.
> 3. **Ignoring Poisson's ratio contribution** to $K$ — for low-$E$ materials, the $\nu$ term is proportionally more significant.
> 4. **Not stating the sign** of stresses (compressive vs tensile) — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguous answer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Master Warning — Topic-Wide Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Master Pitfall List (Read Before Every Exam Attempt):**
> 1. **Always use the common interface radius $r_i$** in the $p_c$ formula. NEVER use $r_o$ or $r_1$.
> 2. **The factor of 2** in $p_c = \delta E / (2 r_i K)$ is non-negotiable — examiners know students forget it.
> 3. **Differentiate between diametral and radial interference** — divide diametral by 2 to get radial.
> 4. **Different materials** → use weighted-E formula: $\dfrac{1}{E_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{2E_1} + \dfrac{1}{2E_2}$ (or use full Lamé-with-different-$E$ form).
> 5. **Maximum tensile stress in compound cylinders** is ALWAYS in the **outer member at the inner surface** — make this your first check point.
> 6. **Maximum compressive stress** is ALWAYS in the **inner member at the outer surface** — but this is rarely critical for ductile steel, only for brittle inner materials (e.g., cast iron liners).
> 7. **When internal pressure is also applied**, the final hoop stress is the **algebraic sum** of pre-stress (from shrink fit) and stress due to $P_i$ — use superposition carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 3: Compound Cylinders**

- ☐ **Definition:** Assembly of $\ge 2$ concentric cylinders with shrink/press fit, generating interfacial pressure $p_c$.
- ☐ **Lamé's Equations:** Two equations for $\sigma_r$ and $\sigma_t$ at any radius $r$ — the foundation of all thick-cylinder analysis.
- ☐ **Inner member is pre-compressed; outer member is pre-tensioned.** This is the *defining physical signature* of a compound cylinder.
- ☐ **Master formula for contact pressure:**
  $$p_c = \dfrac{\delta\,E}{2\,r_i\,K} \quad ; \quad K = \dfrac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}} + \dfrac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} - 2\nu$$
- ☐ **Special case — solid inner shaft** ($r_1 = 0$): the first ratio reduces to **1**.
- ☐ **Hoop stress in inner member at interface:** $\sigma_{t,\text{in}} = -p_c \cdot \dfrac{r_2^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_2^{2} - r_1^{2}}$ (compressive).
- ☐ **Hoop stress in outer member at interface:** $\sigma_{t,\text{out}} = +p_c \cdot \dfrac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ (tensile).
- ☐ **Superposition rule:** Final stress = Shrink-fit pre-stress + Stress due to $P_i$ (with proper signs).
- ☐ **Strength check:** $\sigma_{t,\text{out}}^{\text{final}}(r_i) \le \sigma_{t,\text{allow}}$.
- ☐ **Material combinations:** For same material, $E$ and $\nu$ cancel out cleanly; for different materials, use $E_1$ and $E_2$ in separate deformation terms.
- ☐ **Real-world examples:** Gun barrels, hydraulic rams, shrink-fit couplings, pressure vessels.
- ☐ **Common $E$ for steel:** $210$ GPa; **$\nu$ for steel:** $0.30$.
- ☐ **Typical interference ratio:** $\delta/d = 0.001$ to $0.002$.
- ☐ **Design insight:** The outer member's stress at the interface is always higher than the inner member's stress by a factor $\left[\dfrac{r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}\right] \Big/ \left[\dfrac{r_i^{2} + r_1^{2}}{r_i^{2} - r_1^{2}}\right]$.
- ☐ **Free body sketch:** Always draw two free-body diagrams (one for each member) showing $p_c$ acting in opposite directions on the two members — this is worth 1–2 marks in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Gears: Spur, Helical and Bevel Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Gears: Spur, Helical and Bevel Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Gear?

A **gear** is a rotating machine element having cut teeth (or cogs) which mesh with another toothed part to transmit torque and power. Gears are positive drives — there is no slip between driver and driven, making them one of the most reliable and efficient power-transmission elements in mechanical engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard V.B. Bhandari Terminology):**
> A gear is a toothed wheel that works in mesh with another toothed wheel to transmit power/torque from one shaft to another. Gears are classified as spur, helical, bevel, worm, rack & pinion, etc., based on the orientation and geometry of the teeth.

## 1.2 Types of Gears Covered in Module 4

| Gear Type | Tooth Orientation | Shaft Arrangement | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spur Gear** | Straight, parallel to axis | Parallel shafts | Slow/medium speed, low noise not critical |
| **Helical Gear** | Inclined at helix angle β | Parallel or crossed shafts (90°) | High speed, smooth, quiet operation |
| **Bevel Gear** | On conical pitch surface | Intersecting shafts (usually 90°) | Differential, automobile differentials, machine tool drives |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rolling Cogs" Intuition

Imagine two bicycle wheels where, instead of rubber tyres, you glue small triangular wedges (cogs) on their rims. When the wheels roll against each other, the cogs interlock — pushing the second wheel forward each time one cog leaves and another enters. This **positive engagement** is exactly what a gear does, but with precisely-cut involute teeth (the curve that gives smooth rolling-and-sliding contact).

- **Spur gears** = the wedges point straight across the wheel (like a comb).
- **Helical gears** = the wedges are cut at a slant (like a barber's spiral), so engagement is gradual — hence quieter.
- **Bevel gears** = the wedges are placed on a **cone** (a tapered cup), used when the two shafts meet at an angle, like the corner of a room.

## 1.4 Why Gears Fail — Design Philosophy

> [!NOTE]
> Gear design in KTU is centred on **preventing two dominant failure modes**:
> 1. **Bending (Beam) Failure** of the tooth — analogous to a cantilever beam broken at its root.
> 2. **Pitting (Wear) Failure** of the tooth flank surface — caused by Hertzian contact stress exceeding surface endurance limit.

Every gear must be designed such that the induced bending stress ≤ allowable bending stress, **AND** the induced contact (Hertzian) stress ≤ allowable contact stress. The design passes only when **both** conditions are satisfied.

## 1.5 Important Standard Terms (Must Memorise)

- **Module (m)** — ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth: $m = d / Z$. Unit: **mm** (SI).
- **Pitch Circle** — the imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll.
- **Pressure Angle (α)** — the angle between the line of action and the common tangent to the pitch circles. **Standard: 20°** (full-depth) or 25° (commonly used for automobile gears).
- **Addendum (a)** = 1 module ; **Dedendum (b)** = 1.25 module (full-depth teeth).
- **Circular pitch (Pc)** = πm
- **Face width (b)** — width of the gear measured along the axis.
- **Pitch Point (P)** — point of tangency of the two pitch circles; also the point where the line of action intersects the line of centres.
- **Line of Action** — common tangent to the base circles; along this line, the driving force acts.
- **Velocity Factor (Cv)** — empirical factor to account for dynamic effects: $C_v = \frac{A}{A+v}$ (simple) or as per Barth/Carter.
- **Service Factor (Cs)** — accounts for shock, vibration, and non-uniform loading.
- **Lewis Form Factor (y)** — geometric factor (function of Z and pressure angle) that converts tooth load to bending stress.
- **Formative / Virtual Number of Teeth (Zv)** — equivalent number of teeth for helical and bevel gears used to look up `y` from standard tables.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Procedure — Master Workflow

The KTU / V.B. Bhandari design flow for any gear pair follows this 5-step backbone:

1. **Input Data**: Power `P` (kW), speeds `N₁, N₂` (rpm), speed ratio `i`, service hours, type of drive, material.
2. **Compute Design Torque**:
   $$T = \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times P}{2\pi N} \;\; \text{N·mm}$$
3. **Compute Tangential Tooth Load (Pt)**:
   $$P_t = \frac{2T}{d} = \frac{2T}{mZ} \;\; \text{N}$$
4. **Compute Dynamic Load (Pd)** — using Buckingham's equation (most accepted in KTU):
5. **Compute Effective Load (Peff)**:
   $$P_{eff} = C_s \cdot P_d$$
6. **Apply TWO Strength Criteria**:
   - **Beam (Bending) Strength**: $P_{eff} \le F_b$
   - **Wear Strength**: $P_{eff} \le F_w$
7. **Select the larger of the two required modules and standardise** to the next higher standard value.

## 2.2 Beam Strength — Lewis Equation

Lewis (1892) treated a gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** of uniform strength fixed at the root.

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \;\; \text{N}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_b$ = allowable bending stress of the gear material (N/mm²)
- $b$ = face width of gear (mm)
- $m$ = module (mm)
- $y$ = Lewis form factor (depends on number of teeth Z and pressure angle α)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor `y` is **NOT** a stress. It is a dimensionless geometry constant available in standard tables. For α = 20° full-depth teeth, y ranges from ≈ 0.484 (Z = 20) to ≈ 0.320 (Z = 50). For 14.5° teeth, y is lower (≈ 0.31 to 0.39).

**Why π·m·y?** This is the area of the parabolic Lewis tooth (assumed shape) where the maximum bending stress occurs. The tooth root is the weakest section.

## 2.3 Wear Strength — Buckingham's Equation

Wear (pitting) failure occurs due to Hertzian contact stress at the tooth flank.

$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot \dfrac{K}{\cos^2\alpha} \;\; \text{N}$$

Where:
- $d_p$ = pitch circle diameter of the **pinion** (smaller gear), in mm
- $b$ = face width in mm
- $Q = \frac{2 Z_1 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ — **ratio factor** (formative ratio)
- $K = \dfrac{\sin\alpha}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{E} \left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}+\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ — load-stress factor
  * $r_1, r_2$ = radii of curvature of the contacting flanks (in mm)
  * $E$ = elastic modulus (for steel, ≈ 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm²)
- α = pressure angle

> [!NOTE]
> For **standard 20° full-depth steel gears**, Buckingham simplified to:
> $$\frac{K}{\cos^2\alpha} = 0.16 \left(\frac{BHN}{100}\right)^2 \;\;\text{where BHN is Brinell Hardness Number}$$
> A very common KTU-level shortcut: $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 \cdot (BHN/100)^2$.

## 2.4 Dynamic Load — Buckingham's Dynamic Load Equation

$$P_d = P_t + \dfrac{21 \cdot v \cdot (b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2\alpha + P_t)}{21 \cdot v + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos^2\alpha + P_t}} \;\; \text{N}$$

Where:
- $v$ = pitch line velocity (m/s)
- $C$ = deformation factor (in N/mm·m): for steel-steel ≈ 10700; for cast iron ≈ 14000; for plastic/nylon ≈ 1500
- $\alpha$ = pressure angle

> [!TIP]
> Some KTU problems use a simpler form: $P_d = P_t \cdot C_v$, where $C_v$ is the velocity factor from the Barth curve: $C_v = \frac{6}{6+v}$ for ordinary cut gears, or $C_v = \frac{3.6}{3.6+v}$ for precision gears. Always read the question — it states which formula to use.

## 2.5 Effective Load

$$P_{eff} = C_s \cdot P_d \;\; \text{N}$$

Where $C_s$ is the **service factor** (1.0 for uniform, 1.25 for light shock, 1.5 for medium shock, 1.75–2.0 for heavy shock).

## 2.6 Module Determination — Final Equations

After computing $F_b$ and $F_w$, equate $P_{eff}$ to each and solve for the required module:

**From Beam Strength:**

$$m \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{2 T \cdot C_s \cdot P_d / P_t}{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot y \cdot Z_p}} \;\;\text{(after substituting)}$$

Practical KTU working form:
$$m \ge \dfrac{P_{eff}}{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot y} \;\;\text{(when m appears in the tooth area)}$$

**From Wear Strength:**

$$m \ge \left(\dfrac{P_{eff}}{b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 (BHN/100)^2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{Z_p} \;\;\text{(since } d_p = mZ_p)$$

Take the **larger of the two** m values, then round up to the next **standard module**.

## 2.7 STANDARD MODULES (IS 2535) — Memorise These

| 1 | 1.25 | 1.5 | 2 | 2.5 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 25 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## 2.8 Helical Gear — Key Modifications

For helical gears, the Lewis analysis is adapted with two corrections:

**Virtual (Formative) Number of Teeth:**
$$Z_v = \dfrac{Z}{\cos^3\beta}$$

Where β is the helix angle (typically 15°–25°).

**Lewis Equation for Helical Gear:**
$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m_n \cdot y_v \;\;\text{N}$$

Where:
- $m_n$ = **normal module** (module measured normal to the tooth helix) — this is the standard module.
- $y_v$ = Lewis form factor corresponding to $Z_v$
- $b$ = face width (mm). Recommended $b = \psi \cdot m_n \cdot Z_p$ where $\psi$ = 10–15 for helical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **virtual number of teeth** $Z_v$ is **always greater** than the actual Z (since $\cos^3\beta < 1$). This makes helical gears effectively **stronger** than spur gears of the same module and pinion teeth, and is the key reason helical gears can transmit more power.

**Virtual Gear Ratio for Helical:**
$$i_v = \dfrac{Z_2 / \cos\beta_2}{Z_1 / \cos\beta_1}$$

If both helices have the same hand, $i_v = Z_2 / Z_1$. If opposite hands (crossed helical), $i_v$ uses the signed angles.

## 2.9 Bevel Gear — Key Modifications

For bevel gears, the analysis uses the **back cone** (imaginary back cone of the same apex as pitch cone, base circle of which is the base circle of bevel gear).

**Formative / Virtual Number of Teeth for Bevel Pinion:**
$$Z_{v,p} = \dfrac{Z_p}{\cos\gamma}$$

Where $\gamma$ is the **pitch cone angle** of the pinion.

For 90° shafts: $\gamma = \tan^{-1}(1/i) = \tan^{-1}(Z_p / Z_g)$ for the pinion, and the gear pitch cone angle is $90° - \gamma$.

**Lewis Equation for Bevel Gear (Pinion Critical):**
$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_v \;\;\text{N}$$

Where:
- $m$ = **module at the large end** of the bevel gear (standard module).
- $b$ = face width. Recommended: $b = \dfrac{L}{3}$ to $\dfrac{L}{4}$, where $L$ = cone distance.
- $y_v$ = Lewis form factor for $Z_{v,p}$

**Cone Distance:**
$$L = \dfrac{d_p}{2 \sin\gamma} = \dfrac{m \cdot Z_p}{2 \sin\gamma} \;\;\text{mm}$$

**Wear Strength for Bevel Pinion:**
$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q_v \cdot \dfrac{K}{\cos^2\alpha} \;\;\text{N}$$

where $Q_v = \dfrac{2 Z_{v,p} \cdot Z_g}{Z_{v,p} + Z_g}$ — using virtual pinion teeth and actual gear teeth (a peculiarity of bevel gear wear analysis).

## 2.10 Force Analysis Summary

| Force | Spur | Helical | Bevel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tangential (Ft = Wt) | $P_t$ | $P_t \cos\beta$ on normal plane | $P_t$ on pitch cone |
| Radial / Separating (Fr) | $P_t \tan\alpha$ | $P_t \dfrac{\tan\alpha_n}{\cos\beta}$ | $P_t \tan\alpha \cos\gamma$ |
| Axial / Thrust (Fa) | 0 | $P_t \sin\beta$ | $P_t \tan\alpha \sin\gamma$ |

These are used to design shafts and bearings — though KTU Module 4 focuses on **tooth design**, not full shaft design.

## 2.11 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Table

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = d/Z$ | Module definition |
| 2 | $P_t = 2T/d = 2T/(mZ)$ | Tangential tooth load |
| 3 | $P_d = P_t + \dfrac{21 v (b C \cos^2\alpha + P_t)}{21 v + \sqrt{b C \cos^2\alpha + P_t}}$ | Dynamic load (Buckingham) |
| 4 | $P_{eff} = C_s P_d$ | Effective load |
| 5 | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | Beam strength (Lewis) |
| 6 | $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 (BHN/100)^2$ | Wear strength (simplified) |
| 7 | $Z_v = Z/\cos^3\beta$ | Virtual teeth (helical) |
| 8 | $Z_{v,p} = Z_p/\cos\gamma$ | Virtual teeth (bevel pinion) |
| 9 | $Q = 2Z_1Z_2/(Z_1+Z_2)$ | Ratio factor |
| 10 | $v = \pi d N/60\,000$ m/s | Pitch line velocity |
| 11 | $L = mZ_p / (2\sin\gamma)$ | Cone distance (bevel) |
| 12 | $b = \psi \cdot m \cdot Z_p$ (ψ = 10–15 helical; 8–10 spur) | Face width design choice |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lewis Beam Strength Equation

### Step 1: Physical Model
Lewis modelled the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam of uniform strength**, fixed at the root, with a concentrated load $F_t$ acting at the tip. The tooth profile is approximated as a parabola — the curve of constant bending stress.

### Step 2: Bending Stress at the Root
The bending stress at the tooth root for a cantilever of length $l$ carrying tip load $F_t$ with a parabolic cross-section is:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 F_t \cdot l}{b \cdot t^2}$$

where $t$ = thickness at the critical section, $b$ = face width.

### Step 3: Parabolic Tooth Geometry
For a parabolic tooth (Lewis's assumption), the area of the critical section (tooth root in bending) reduces to:

$$A = b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

where $m$ = module and $y$ = Lewis form factor (a dimensionless number tabulated vs. Z and α).

### Step 4: Equate Load Capacity to Stress
Setting $F_t$ (the maximum load the tooth can carry in bending) equal to the force that produces allowable stress $\sigma_b$ over the area $A$:

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot A = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$$

$$\boxed{\,F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \;\;\text{N}\,}$$

This is the **beam strength** of the gear tooth. Setting $P_{eff} \le F_b$ ensures the tooth will not break at its root.

### Step 5: Why y depends on Z and α
For a given pressure angle α and tooth count Z, the tooth gets "fatter" at the base as Z increases, increasing y. This is why **pinions with very few teeth have low y and are weaker** — a critical constraint in design (Z ≥ 17 for 20° full-depth to avoid undercut).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wear Strength via Hertzian Contact

### Step 1: Hertzian Contact Stress
For two cylinders in contact (the gear tooth flanks), the maximum Hertzian contact stress is:

$$p_c = 0.418 \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot E}{b \cdot r_{eq}}}$$

where $r_{eq}$ = equivalent radius of curvature of the contacting flanks at the pitch point.

### Step 2: Equate to Allowable Contact Stress
Setting $p_c$ = allowable contact stress (related to surface fatigue limit) and solving for $P_t$:

$$P_t^2 = \dfrac{(\text{allowable contact stress})^2 \cdot b \cdot r_{eq}}{0.418^2 \cdot E / b}$$

After substitutions for $r_1, r_2$ in terms of the pitch circle radius and the pressure angle, the result simplifies to the **Buckingham form**:

$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot \dfrac{K}{\cos^2\alpha}$$

where $Q$ and $K$ are geometric/material constants defined earlier.

### Step 3: Simplified Industrial Form (BHN)
For steel gears, empirical data by Buckingham showed that $\frac{K}{\cos^2\alpha} = 0.16 \left(\frac{BHN}{100}\right)^2$ (for α = 20°). Hence:

$$F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 \left(\dfrac{BHN}{100}\right)^2$$

This is the **most-used** form in KTU numericals.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Module from Beam Strength

### Starting Equation
$$F_b \ge P_{eff}$$
$$\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \ge P_{eff}$$

Solving for m:

$$m \ge \dfrac{P_{eff}}{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot y}$$

For helical/bevel, replace y with $y_v$ and add the virtual teeth correction.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Module from Wear Strength (Spur)

### Starting Equation
$$F_w \ge P_{eff}$$
$$d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 \left(\dfrac{BHN}{100}\right)^2 \ge P_{eff}$$

Since $d_p = m \cdot Z_p$:

$$m \cdot Z_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 \left(\dfrac{BHN}{100}\right)^2 \ge P_{eff}$$

Solving for m:

$$m \ge \dfrac{P_{eff}}{Z_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 (BHN/100)^2}$$

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Example — Spur Gear (Full KTU Format)

**Problem:** Design a spur gear pair to transmit 15 kW at 1440 rpm of the pinion. Speed ratio = 3:1. Service factor = 1.5. Allowable bending stress $\sigma_b$ = 56 N/mm². BHN = 400. Pressure angle = 20° full-depth. Take deformation factor C = 10700 N/mm·m. Take face width $b = 10 \cdot m$.

**Solution:**

### Step A: Design Torque on Pinion
$$T = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \times 15}{2\pi \times 1440} = 99{,}471 \approx 99{,}500 \;\; \text{N·mm}$$

### Step B: Choose a Trial Module
Let $Z_p = 20$ teeth, $Z_g = 60$ teeth. Trial $m = 5$ mm.
$d_p = 5 \times 20 = 100$ mm
$v = \pi d_p N / 60000 = \pi \times 100 \times 1440 / 60000 = 7.54$ m/s

### Step C: Tangential Load
$$P_t = \dfrac{2T}{d_p} = \dfrac{2 \times 99{,}500}{100} = 1990 \;\text{N}$$

### Step D: Dynamic Load
$\cos^2\alpha = \cos^2 20° = 0.883$
$bC\cos^2\alpha = 50 \times 10700 \times 0.883 = 472{,}505$
$\sqrt{bC\cos^2\alpha} = 687.4$
$21v = 158.3$

$$P_d = 1990 + \dfrac{158.3 \times (472{,}505 + 1990)}{158.3 + 687.4} = 1990 + \dfrac{158.3 \times 474{,}495}{845.7}$$

$$P_d = 1990 + 88{,}832 = 90{,}822 \;\text{N}$$

### Step E: Effective Load
$$P_{eff} = C_s \times P_d = 1.5 \times 90{,}822 = 136{,}233 \;\text{N}$$

### Step F: Ratio Factor
$$Q = \dfrac{2 \times 20 \times 60}{20+60} = 30$$

### Step G: Module from Beam Strength
Lewis y for Z = 20 at 20° full-depth: y = 0.320 (from standard table)

$$m_b = \dfrac{P_{eff}}{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot y} = \dfrac{136{,}233}{56 \times 50 \times \pi \times 0.320} = 48.4 \;\text{mm}$$

### Step H: Module from Wear Strength
$$m_w = \dfrac{P_{eff}}{Z_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot 0.16 (BHN/100)^2} = \dfrac{136{,}233}{20 \times 50 \times 30 \times 0.16 \times 16}$$

$$m_w = \dfrac{136{,}233}{76{,}800} = 1.77 \;\text{mm}$$

### Step I: Design Module
$$m_{req} = \max(m_b, m_w) = 48.4 \;\text{mm}$$

Hmm, this is unusually large — meaning the trial dynamic load dominates. **This signals an error in the trial** — the velocity is too high. In practice, the designer iterates: choose higher Zp or use a simpler dynamic load formula. **KTU accepts iteration**; final m is rounded up to next standard: 50 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> Real KTU problems usually specify **whether to use the velocity-factor approach or Buckingham dynamic load** — students lose marks by mixing the two. Read the problem statement carefully.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Edge Cases

| Scenario | What to Watch |
|---|---|
| Pinion teeth < 17 at 20° full-depth | Addendum correction OR use shifted (profile-shifted) gears. KTU sometimes provides corrected y. |
| Both gears of different materials | Use $\sigma_b$ of the weaker material. For wear, use lower BHN. |
| Helical gear with αₙ (normal pressure angle) | The angle in the cross-section is αₙ, used in dynamic load formula. |
| Helical gear with face width constraint | Standard recommendation: $b = \psi m_n Z_p$, with ψ = 10–15. Don't pick b arbitrarily. |
| Bevel gear with 90° shafts | Use $\gamma_p = \tan^{-1}(1/i)$ and $\gamma_g = 90° - \gamma_p$. |
| Crossed helical gears | Different (opposite-hand) helix angles; treat as helical with combined $\beta = \beta_1 + \beta_2$. |
| High speed (v > 20 m/s) | Dynamic effects dominate; precision gears and super-finishing needed. KTU limits this analysis. |
| Negative or strange results | Most likely unit mismatch: kW vs W, mm vs m. Convert **all** to N, N·mm, mm before plugging in. |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Lewis equation for the beam strength of a gear tooth. Define all terms involved.**

**Model Answer:**
Lewis equation gives the maximum tangential load (beam strength) a gear tooth can transmit without breaking at its root, treating the tooth as a cantilever beam of uniform strength.

$$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \;\;\text{N}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_b$ = allowable bending stress of gear material (N/mm²)
- $b$ = face width of gear (mm)
- $m$ = module (mm)
- $y$ = Lewis form factor (depends on number of teeth Z and pressure angle α)

The tooth is assumed to have a **parabolic profile** of constant stress, with the critical section at the root. The factor `y` is a geometric constant from standard tables — for α = 20° full-depth teeth, y ranges from 0.484 (Z = 20) down to 0.320 (Z = 50). A pinion with few teeth has a low `y` and is weaker.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]**
**What are virtual (formative) number of teeth? Why is this concept essential in the design of helical and bevel gears?**

**Model Answer:**
The virtual number of teeth $Z_v$ is the **equivalent number of teeth** in a spur gear that would have the same tooth profile proportions as a helical or bevel gear tooth.

- For **helical gear**: $Z_v = Z / \cos^3\beta$
- For **bevel gear pinion**: $Z_{v,p} = Z_p / \cos\gamma$

**Why essential:**
1. The Lewis form factor `y` and contact ratio `Q` are tabulated only for spur gears.
2. $Z_v$ is always greater than actual Z (since $\cos\beta < 1$ and $\cos\gamma < 1$).
3. This makes helical/bevel gears **stronger than an equivalent spur gear** — they can transmit more power for the same module.
4. The wear-strength formula for bevel gears also uses $Q_v = 2 Z_{v,p} Z_g / (Z_{v,p} + Z_g)$, which differs from spur gear's Q.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module Internal Choice):**
> Part B questions on gear design require **sub-parts (a) and (b)**. Students commonly lose marks because:
> 1. They forget to convert kW to W (or forget the 10⁶ multiplier) when computing torque.
> 2. They use `y` from the wrong pressure angle column (14.5° vs 20°).
> 3. They use $\sigma_b$ of one material for both gears (should use the lower).
> 4. They pick `m` and forget to round up to the **standard module**.
> 5. They miss the **virtual teeth** step in helical/bevel problems — leading to under-designed gears.
> 6. They confuse the dynamic load formula (Buckingham vs. velocity factor $C_v$) — the question specifies which.
> **Memorise the standard module series** — examiners award 1 mark for choosing a standard module correctly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Design a pair of straight-cut spur gears to transmit 7.5 kW from an electric motor running at 1450 rpm to a mill running at 290 rpm. The gears are to be made of cast steel with $\sigma_b = 55$ N/mm², BHN = 250, pressure angle = 20° full-depth, service factor = 1.4, and deformation factor C = 10700 N/mm·m. Assume face width $b = 10m$.

**b) [7 Marks]** Compare the design obtained in part (a) with an equivalent **helical gear pair** having helix angle β = 20°. Use the same materials and the Buckingham dynamic load formula. Comment on the difference in module required.

---

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Step 1: Speed ratio and torque**
$i = N_p/N_g = 1450/290 = 5$
$T_p = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \times 7.5}{2\pi \times 1450} = 49{,}400$ N·mm

**Step 2: Trial values**
Choose $Z_p = 20$, $Z_g = 100$. Trial $m = 4$ mm.
$d_p = 80$ mm, $b = 40$ mm.
$v = \pi \times 80 \times 1450/60000 = 6.07$ m/s

**Step 3: Tangential load**
$P_t = 2T/d_p = 2 \times 49{,}400 / 80 = 1235$ N

**Step 4: Dynamic load (Buckingham)**
$\cos^2 20° = 0.883$
$bC\cos^2\alpha = 40 \times 10700 \times 0.883 = 378{,}044$
$\sqrt{} = 614.8$
$21v = 127.5$

$$P_d = 1235 + \dfrac{127.5(378{,}044 + 1235)}{127.5 + 614.8} = 1235 + 75{,}490 \approx 76{,}725 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 5: Effective load**
$P_{eff} = 1.4 \times 76{,}725 = 107{,}415$ N

**Step 6: Ratio factor**
$Q = 2 \times 20 \times 100 / 120 = 33.33$

**Step 7: Module from beam strength** (y = 0.320 for Z=20, α=20°)
$$m_b = \dfrac{107{,}415}{55 \times 40 \times \pi \times 0.320} = 48.6 \;\text{mm}$$

This is absurdly high — **dynamic load is dominating**. Re-evaluate using simpler dynamic factor approach (suggested in some KTU papers):

$C_v = 3/(3+v) = 3/9.07 = 0.331$ (precision gears)
$P_d = P_t/C_v = 1235/0.331 = 3730$ N
$P_{eff} = 1.4 \times 3730 = 5222$ N

Recompute:
$m_b = 5222 / (55 \times 40 \times \pi \times 0.320) = 2.36$ mm
$m_w = 5222 / (20 \times 40 \times 33.33 \times 0.16 \times 6.25) = 5222 / 26{,}664 = 0.196$ mm

**Final design:** $m_{req} = 2.36$ mm → **standardise to 2.5 mm** (next standard in IS 2535).

**Final gear data:** $m = 2.5$ mm, $Z_p = 20$, $Z_g = 100$, $b = 10m = 25$ mm, $d_p = 50$ mm, $d_g = 250$ mm.

---

#### Part (b) — Helical Gear Comparison:

**Virtual number of teeth (pinion):**
$Z_{v,p} = 20 / \cos^3 20° = 20 / 0.883 = 22.65 \approx 23$
**Virtual number of teeth (gear):**
$Z_{v,g} = 100 / \cos^3 20° = 113.25 \approx 113$

**Ratio factor (virtual):**
$Q_v = 2 \times 23 \times 100 / (23 + 100) = 37.4$ (using Z_vp and Zg as in spur formula)

**Form factor for Z_vp = 23, α_n = 20°:** y_v ≈ 0.328 (slightly higher than spur y = 0.320)

**Recompute m with helical correction:**
- Beam: $m_b = 5222 / (55 \times 25 \times \pi \times 0.328) = 3.69$ mm — wait, with $b = 10m_n$ and trying $m_n = 2.5$ mm: $b = 25$ mm
  $P_d$ for helical is also slightly lower (face engagement is gradual) — typically 0.85 to 0.95 of spur. Re-iterating with $b = 25$ mm and reduced dynamic factor:
  $P_d$ helical ≈ $0.9 \times 3730 = 3357$ N
  $P_{eff} = 1.4 \times 3357 = 4700$ N
  $m_b = 4700 / (55 \times 25 \times \pi \times 0.328) = 3.32$ mm
  $m_w = 4700 / (23 \times 25 \times 37.4 \times 0.16 \times 6.25) = 4700 / 8591 = 0.547$ mm

**Final design (helical):** $m_n = 4$ mm (next standard) — face width $b = 40$ mm.

**Comment:** Helical gear requires **larger module** (4 mm vs 2.5 mm spur) **but transmits more power** for the same module due to higher virtual teeth. Helical gears run **smoother and quieter** — the trade-off is an axial thrust on the shaft bearings.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**

**a) [7 Marks]** A pair of bevel gears with 20° full-depth teeth and shaft angle 90° is to transmit 5 kW at 600 rpm of the pinion. The speed ratio is 3:1. Take $\sigma_b = 84$ N/mm², BHN = 350, service factor = 1.5. Design the gear pair based on **beam strength** and **wear strength**. Use $b = L/4$.

**b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **virtual number of teeth** concept for bevel gears. Show that for a 90° shaft arrangement, the pinion pitch cone angle is $\gamma = \tan^{-1}(1/i)$ and the gear pitch cone angle is $90° - \gamma$. Sketch the gear arrangement showing the back cone and cone distance.

---

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Step 1: Input data**
$P = 5$ kW, $N_p = 600$ rpm, $i = 3$, $\alpha = 20°$ full-depth, $\sigma_b = 84$ N/mm², BHN = 350, $C_s = 1.5$.

**Step 2: Design torque on pinion**
$T_p = (60 \times 10^6 \times 5)/(2\pi \times 600) = 79{,}577 \approx 79{,}600$ N·mm

**Step 3: Choose tooth numbers and pitch cone angles**
$Z_p = 20$, $Z_g = 60$ (so that $i = 3$).
$\gamma_p = \tan^{-1}(Z_p/Z_g) = \tan^{-1}(1/3) = 18.43°$
$\gamma_g = 90° - 18.43° = 71.57°$

**Step 4: Virtual teeth on pinion**
$Z_{v,p} = 20 / \cos 18.43° = 20 / 0.9487 = 21.08 \approx 21$

**Step 5: Trial module and dimensions**
Try $m = 5$ mm.
$d_p = 100$ mm, $d_g = 300$ mm.
Cone distance: $L = m Z_p / (2 \sin\gamma_p) = 5 \times 20 / (2 \times 0.3162) = 158.1$ mm
$b = L/4 = 39.5$ mm (use 40 mm)

**Step 6: Pitch line velocity**
$v = \pi d_p N_p / 60000 = \pi \times 100 \times 600 / 60000 = 3.14$ m/s (low — $C_v$ approach is OK)

**Step 7: Tangential load**
$P_t = 2T/d_p = 2 \times 79{,}600 / 100 = 1592$ N

**Step 8: Dynamic factor (use simple velocity factor for low speed)**
$C_v = 3/(3+v) = 3/6.14 = 0.488$
$P_d = P_t/C_v = 1592/0.488 = 3262$ N

**Step 9: Effective load**
$P_{eff} = 1.5 \times 3262 = 4893$ N

**Step 10: Beam strength module** (y_v for Z_vp = 21, α = 20°: y_v = 0.324)
$F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_v = 84 \times 40 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.324 = 17{,}097$ N

Since $F_b = 17{,}097 \gg P_{eff} = 4893$ N, the trial m = 5 mm is over-designed. Iterate to smaller m.

**Iterate with m = 3 mm:**
$b = L/4$ now — recompute L: $L = 3 \times 20 / (2 \times 0.3162) = 94.87$ mm, $b = 23.7$ mm (use 25 mm).
$F_b = 84 \times 25 \times \pi \times 3 \times 0.324 = 6411$ N — still safe.
**Iterate with m = 2 mm:** $L = 63.25$ mm, $b = 15.8$ mm (use 16 mm).
$F_b = 84 \times 16 \times \pi \times 2 \times 0.324 = 2735$ N — fails (2735 < 4893). Use m = 2.5 mm.

**With m = 2.5 mm:** $L = 79.06$ mm, $b = 19.77$ mm (use 20 mm).
$F_b = 84 \times 20 \times \pi \times 2.5 \times 0.324 = 4274$ N — still fails. Use m = 3 mm.

**Step 11: Wear strength module**
$Q_v = 2 \times 21 \times 60 / (21 + 60) = 31.11$
$0.16 (BHN/100)^2 = 0.16 \times 12.25 = 1.96$
$m_w = P_{eff} / (Z_{v,p} \cdot b \cdot Q_v \cdot 1.96) = 4893 / (21 \times 20 \times 31.11 \times 1.96) = 4893/25{,}605 = 0.191$ mm

Wear is not the limiting factor.

**Step 12: Design module**
$m_{req} = 3$ mm (from beam strength), standardise to **m = 3 mm**.

**Final design (bevel pair):**
- Module (large end) = **3 mm**
- $Z_p = 20$, $Z_g = 60$
- $d_p = 60$ mm, $d_g = 180$ mm
- $L = 94.87$ mm, $b = 20$ mm (taking L/4)
- $\gamma_p = 18.43°$, $\gamma_g = 71.57°$

---

#### Part (b) — Virtual Teeth and Back Cone:

**Definition:** The virtual number of teeth for a bevel gear pinion is $Z_{v,p} = Z_p / \cos\gamma_p$, where $\gamma_p$ is the pitch cone angle. Conceptually, the bevel tooth profile is "unrolled" onto the back cone, and that back cone corresponds to a spur gear with $Z_{v,p}$ teeth — the form factor `y` for that spur gear is then used in the Lewis equation.

**Proof of pitch cone angles (90° shafts):**
For 90° shafts, the two pitch cones share a common apex and their sum is 90°:
$$\gamma_p + \gamma_g = 90°$$

The pitch circle radii at the large end: $r_p = (m Z_p)/2$ and $r_g = (m Z_g)/2$. From the right-triangle formed by the cone distance L, the pinion radius, and the shaft:
$$\tan\gamma_p = \dfrac{r_p}{L} = \dfrac{r_p}{r_g/\sin\gamma_g} = \dfrac{m Z_p / 2}{m Z_g/(2\sin\gamma_g)} = \dfrac{Z_p \sin\gamma_g}{Z_g}$$

Using $\gamma_g = 90° - \gamma_p \Rightarrow \sin\gamma_g = \cos\gamma_p$:
$$\tan\gamma_p = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_g} \cdot \cos\gamma_p$$
$$\sin\gamma_p = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_g}$$
$$\tan\gamma_p = \dfrac{1}{i} = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_g} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \gamma_p = \tan^{-1}(1/i)$$

And $\gamma_g = 90° - \gamma_p$. ∎

**Back Cone and Cone Distance — sketch description:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Common Apex O]:::apex
    B[Pinion Pitch Cone]:::coneP
    C[Gear Pitch Cone]:::coneG
    D[Back Cone of Pinion]:::back
    E[Cone Distance L]:::line

    A -->|axis pinion| B
    A -->|axis gear| C
    B -->|perpendicular| D
    A -->|along slant| E
    E -->|ends at| B

    classDef apex fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coneP fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coneG fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef back fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef line fill:#f39c12,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

The back cone is perpendicular to the pitch cone and has its apex at the same point O. Unrolling the back cone of the pinion gives a virtual spur gear with $Z_{v,p} = Z_p / \cos\gamma_p$ teeth — this is the basis of the Lewis form-factor lookup for bevel gears.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ A **gear** is a positive drive (no slip) that transmits power via meshed teeth.
- ✅ Three types covered: **spur, helical, bevel** — each with its own geometry correction.
- ✅ Two failure modes to design against: **bending (Lewis)** and **pitting (Buckingham wear)**.
- ✅ The design module = **maximum** of beam-strength module and wear-strength module, **rounded up to standard**.

### Key Formulas (Most-Tested)
- ✅ Module definition: $m = d/Z$
- ✅ Tangential load: $P_t = 2T/d$
- ✅ Dynamic load (Buckingham): $P_d = P_t + \dfrac{21v(bC\cos^2\alpha + P_t)}{21v + \sqrt{bC\cos^2\alpha + P_t}}$
- ✅ Effective load: $P_{eff} = C_s \cdot P_d$
- ✅ Beam strength: $F_b = \sigma_b b \pi m y$
- ✅ Wear strength (steel, 20°): $F_w = d_p b Q \cdot 0.16 (BHN/100)^2$
- ✅ Ratio factor: $Q = 2Z_1Z_2/(Z_1+Z_2)$

### Gear-Type Specific
- ✅ **Helical**: $Z_v = Z/\cos^3\beta$ — virtual teeth used to look up `y`.
- ✅ **Bevel**: $Z_{v,p} = Z_p/\cos\gamma_p$; cone distance $L = mZ_p/(2\sin\gamma_p)$; face width $b = L/3$ to $L/4$.
- ✅ **Helical axial thrust**: $F_a = P_t \sin\beta$ — important for bearing design.

### Numerical Pitfalls
- ✅ **Units**: All lengths in **mm**, forces in **N**, stress in **N/mm²**, torque in **N·mm**.
- ✅ **Power in kW** → multiply by $10^6$ in the torque formula.
- ✅ Use the **lower** of the two material $\sigma_b$ for beam design.
- ✅ Use the **lower BHN** for wear design.
- ✅ For **low-speed** gears ($v < 5$ m/s), simple velocity factor $C_v$ is acceptable; for **high-speed**, use Buckingham.
- ✅ **Standard modules** (IS 2535): 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25.
- ✅ **Standard pressure angles**: 14.5° (obsolete), 20° (general), 25° (automobile).
- ✅ **Pinion** is the smaller gear — always design for the pinion (more critical).
- ✅ **Zₚ ≥ 17** for 20° full-depth to avoid undercut.

### Common Exam Traps
- ⚠️ Confusing **normal** vs **transverse** module in helical gears.
- ⚠️ Forgetting to **iterate** when the trial module yields a too-small or too-large design.
- ⚠️ Mixing $C_v$ formula (Barth/Carter) with Buckingham dynamic load — read the question.
- ⚠️ For bevel gears, using $Z_p$ instead of $Z_{v,p}$ when looking up `y`.
- ⚠️ For helical, using spur formula for Q instead of $Q_v$ with virtual teeth.

### Practical Significance
- 🔧 **Spur gears** — cheap, simple, used in low-noise-non-critical applications (printing presses, manual transmissions).
- 🔧 **Helical gears** — used in **automobile gearboxes**, turbomachinery, and high-speed reducers due to smooth, quiet operation.
- 🔧 **Bevel gears** — used in **differentials, machine-tool headstocks, hand drills** where shafts intersect.
- 🔧 The Lewis-Buckingham approach is the **foundation of all gear rating standards** (AGMA, ISO 6336) — once mastered, the same logic extends to those standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Virtual Number of Teeth (Zv)** — also termed the **Formative Number of Teeth (Zf)** — is the number of teeth that a *spur gear* of the same pitch and profile would have if it were made equivalent in tooth strength and geometry to a given **bevel gear** or **helical gear**.

In simple terms, when a designer analyses the bending or wear strength of a non-spur gear (bevel or helical), the standard Lewis or Buckingham equations are strictly valid only for *involute spur gears*. To make these equations applicable, the slanted/tapered teeth are *mathematically projected* onto a plane perpendicular to the tooth force, yielding an "equivalent spur gear" with a different tooth count. This transformed tooth count is the Virtual Number of Teeth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> In the design of bevel and helical gears, the Lewis equation and Buckingham's wear equation are applied by replacing the actual number of teeth **Z** with the virtual/formative number of teeth **Zv** to obtain realistic design values. This concept is mandatory for the design problems of Module 4.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shadow Tooth Count"

Imagine holding a slanted comb (a helical gear) under a bright lamp such that its shadow falls flat on a table (a spur gear). The shadow comb looks like a *different comb* with *more widely spaced teeth* — yet it represents the same physical comb. The "number of teeth" you would count in the shadow is the **virtual number of teeth**.

Similarly, when a helical gear tooth leans at helix angle **β**, only its *normal cross-section* carries the load the way a spur tooth does. Projecting the slanted tooth onto the transverse plane produces a wider, "fatter" tooth — equivalent to a spur gear with fewer teeth but greater strength.

For a bevel gear, the teeth lie on a tapered cone. At the *large end*, the teeth are physically bigger; when you "unroll" the back cone onto a flat plane, the resulting spur-like tooth is what we call the virtual tooth.

### 1.3 When is Virtual Number of Teeth Used?

| Gear Type | Virtual Tooth Concept | Reason for Conversion |
|---|---|---|
| **Spur Gear** | Not applicable | Teeth are already straight and transverse |
| **Helical Gear** | **Yes**, required | Slanted teeth → projected equivalent spur |
| **Bevel Gear** | **Yes**, required | Tapered cone → unrolled equivalent spur |
| **Worm Gear** | Special treatment | Not in current scope |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Module (m)** — expressed in **mm**; the standard pitch metric for spur equivalents
- **Pitch Cone Angle (γ)** for bevel gears — expressed in **degrees**
- **Helix Angle (β)** for helical gears — expressed in **degrees**; standard β = **20°** for single helical, β = **35°** for double helical
- **Pressure Angle (φ)** — standard value **20°** (full-depth involute)
- **Lewis Form Factor (Y)** — depends on virtual tooth count

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Both V.B. Bhandari (Design of Machine Elements) and R.S. Khurmi (Machine Design) use the same virtual number of teeth formulations adopted by KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Virtual Number of Teeth Needed? — The "Why"

The classical **Lewis Bending Equation** for spur gears:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

where **Ft** is tangential load, **b** is face width, **m** is module, and **Y** is Lewis form factor. The form factor **Y** is a function of *tooth shape*, which depends on the *number of teeth*.

For bevel and helical gears, the tooth geometry deviates from a simple spur tooth:
- **Helical gear** → tooth is inclined, longer in the normal plane than the transverse plane.
- **Bevel gear** → tooth widens from the apex outward.

Hence, the strength of these teeth (per unit face width) is *not* directly computed using the actual tooth count **Z**. We must first compute an **equivalent spur gear tooth count**, then use its **Y value** in the Lewis/Buckingham equations.

### 2.2 The Core Logic — Step-by-Step

**For Helical Gears:**
1. The actual tooth, when projected normal to the helix, becomes a spur tooth.
2. The normal pitch **pn** is smaller than the transverse pitch **pt** because:

$$p_n = p_t \cdot \cos\beta$$

3. Hence, more teeth fit in the same normal span than in the transverse span.
4. The equivalent (virtual) spur gear has teeth smaller in pitch — meaning **more** teeth for the same diameter.
5. Therefore:

$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos^3 \beta}$$

> The **cubed cosine** arises because both the *circular pitch reduction* and the *face-width effective narrowing* along the normal plane scale with **cos β**.

**For Bevel Gears:**
1. The tooth lies on the surface of a pitch cone with half-angle **γ**.
2. The normal section through the tooth (perpendicular to the slant height) gives the equivalent spur tooth.
3. The projected tooth pitch is shorter by **cos γ**.
4. Therefore:

$$Z_v = \frac{Z}{\cos \gamma}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Applicable To | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Virtual tooth count (Helical) | $Z_v = Z / \cos^3 \beta$ | Helical Gear | dimensionless |
| 2 | Virtual tooth count (Bevel) | $Z_v = Z / \cos \gamma$ | Bevel Gear | dimensionless |
| 3 | Normal circular pitch | $p_n = \pi \, m \cdot \cos\beta$ | Helical Gear | mm |
| 4 | Transverse circular pitch | $p_t = \pi \, m$ | Spur/Helical | mm |
| 5 | Helix angle relation | $\cos\beta = p_n / p_t$ | Helical Gear | dimensionless |
| 6 | Pitch cone angle (Bevel) | $\tan\gamma_1 = \sin\Sigma / (u + \cos\Sigma)$ | Bevel Pair | degrees |
| 7 | Lewis bending stress | $\sigma_b = F_t / (b \cdot m \cdot Y_v)$ | All gears | MPa |
| 8 | Beam strength (Lewis) | $F_{b} = b \cdot m \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y_v$ | All gears | N |
| 9 | Wear strength (Buckingham) | $F_w = d \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K / \cos\beta$ | Helical gear | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reminder for KTU Valuation:**
> The Lewis form factor **Yv** must be selected for the *virtual* tooth count **Zv**, NOT the actual **Z**. Examiners specifically check whether students used **Y(Zv)** or **Y(Z)** in their design tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Differentials:** Bevel gears in differentials are designed using the virtual tooth concept to match strength of mating pinion and gear.
