F_d = F_t \cdot C_v = 2122.6 \times 0.5842 = 1240.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6: Corrected Endurance Limit**
$$
S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 560 = 280 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_e = S_e' \times K_a \times K_b \times K_c = 280 \times 0.85 \times 0.88 \times 1.0 = 209.44 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7: Beam (Endurance) Strength of Tooth**
$$
F_b = \sigma_e \times b \times \pi m \times Y = 209.44 \times 50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.32
$$
$$
F_b = 209.44 \times 50 \times 15.708 \times 0.32 = 52{,}635 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 8: Factor of Safety in Bending**
$$
n = \dfrac{F_b}{F_d} = \dfrac{52{,}635}{1240.2} = 42.4
$$

A factor of safety of $42.4$ is excessively high — the gear is over-designed. **Engineering interpretation:** Reduce face width, use a lower-grade material, or increase transmitted power.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lewis Cantilever-Beam Idealization of a Gear Tooth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Tangential Load Ft applied at Tooth Tip] --> B[Tooth modeled as Cantilever Beam]
    B --> C[Critical Section: Root Fillet - High Stress Concentration]
    C --> D[Equivalent Bending Stress sigma_b compared to Endurance Limit sigma_e]
    D --> E{Bending Strength Fb > Dynamic Load Fd?}
    E -- Yes --> F[SAFE: Tooth survives infinite life]
    E -- No --> G[FAIL: Tooth root cracks and fractures]
    F --> H[Report Factor of Safety n = Fb / Fd]
    G --> I[Increase module, face width, or material strength]
    H --> J[Final design passed to manufacturing]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Flow of Endurance Strength Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Input: P, n, m, Z, b, Sut, Ka, Kb] --> node2[Compute d and v]
    node2 --> node3[Compute Ft = 2e3 P / v]
    node3 --> node4[Compute Cv from velocity]
    node4 --> node5[Compute Dynamic Load Fd = Ft times Cv]
    node5 --> node6[Compute Corrected Endurance Limit sigma_e]
    node6 --> node7[Read Form Factor Y from table or AGMA chart]
    node7 --> node8[Compute Beam Strength Fb = sigma_e times b times pi m times Y]
    node8 --> node9[Compute Factor of Safety n = Fb / Fd]
    node9 --> nodeA{Final Check}
    nodeA -- n between 1.5 and 2.0 --> nodeB[Design Accepted]
    nodeA -- n less than 1.5 --> nodeC[Redesign: increase b or m]
    nodeA -- n greater than 2.0 --> nodeD[Overdesigned: optimize cost]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Gear Endurance Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Material Selection: Sut and Sy] --> L2[Marin Factors: Ka, Kb, Kc, Kd]
    L2 --> L3[Compute Corrected Endurance Limit sigma_e]
    L3 --> L4[Geometric Inputs: m, Z, b, phi]
    L4 --> L5[Compute Form Factor Y from standard tables]
    L5 --> L6[Compute Beam Strength Fb]
    L6 --> L7[Dynamic Load Module: Cv, Fd]
    L7 --> L8[Factor of Safety n = Fb over Fd]
    L8 --> L9[Compare with Buckingham Wear Strength Fw]
    L9 --> L10[Final Design Endurance Verification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (node1, nodeA, L1, etc.) and labels are plain text — no bold or markdown tags inside quoted labels — to guarantee clean rendering in GitHub, VSCode, and Confluence.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term **Beam Strength of a Gear Tooth**. How is it related to the **endurance strength of the tooth material**?

**Model Answer:**
The **Beam Strength $F_b$** of a gear tooth is defined as the maximum tangential load the tooth can transmit at the pitch circle without causing failure at the root fillet, treating the tooth as a cantilever beam.

It is mathematically given by the **Lewis equation**:

$$
F_b = \sigma_e \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot Y
$$

where $\sigma_e$ is the **endurance limit** of the tooth material corrected for stress concentration, size, surface finish, and reliability (using **Marin's factors**). Thus, beam strength is directly proportional to the endurance strength $\sigma_e$ of the material — a higher $\sigma_e$ yields a higher beam (endurance) strength, allowing the gear to transmit more power without fatigue failure.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the **Lewis Form Factor**? State the factors on which it depends.

**Model Answer:**
The **Lewis Form Factor $Y$** is a dimensionless geometric constant that accounts for the shape of the gear tooth when computing its bending strength. It collapses the complex tooth geometry into a single multiplier.

$$
Y = \dfrac{\text{Tooth geometry factor}}{6 \, h}
$$

It depends on:
1. **Pressure angle** ($\phi$)
2. **Number of teeth on the pinion** ($Z$)
3. **Tooth system** (full-depth or stub)
4. **Profile shift / addendum modification** (in modern AGMA Y-J factor)

Typical values: $Y = \mathbf{0.30}$ (20 teeth, $20^{\circ}$ full depth) to $Y = \mathbf{0.485}$ (rack, $20^{\circ}$ stub).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

> A $20^{\circ}$ full-depth spur pinion with $Z = 18$ teeth, module $m = 4$ mm, and face width $b = 40$ mm is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa. It is to transmit $7.5$ kW at $900$ rpm. Use Lewis equation with $Y = 0.308$, surface factor $K_a = 0.88$, size factor $K_b = 0.86$, and reliability factor $K_c = 1.0$. Take velocity factor $C_v = 0.6$ for cut teeth.
>
> **a)** Calculate the **endurance (beam) strength of the tooth** in bending. **(7 Marks)**
> **b)** Determine the **factor of safety in bending** and comment on the design. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Endurance / Beam Strength**

Pitch diameter: $d = m \cdot Z = 4 \times 18 = 72$ mm

Pitch line velocity:

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \times 72 \times 900}{60 \times 1000} = 3.39 \text{ m/s}
$$

Corrected endurance limit:

$$
S_e' = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
\sigma_e = 300 \times 0.88 \times 0.86 \times 1.0 = 227.04 \text{ MPa}
$$

Beam (endurance) strength:

$$
F_b = \sigma_e \times b \times \pi m \times Y = 227.04 \times 40 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.308
$$

$$
F_b = 227.04 \times 40 \times 12.566 \times 0.308 = 35{,}154 \text{ N} \approx 35.15 \text{ kN}
$$

**Part (b): Factor of Safety**

Tangential load:

$$
F_t = \dfrac{2 \times 10^3 \times 7.5}{3.39} = 4425.4 \text{ N}
$$

Dynamic load (using $C_v = 0.6$):

$$
F_d = F_t \times C_v = 4425.4 \times 0.6 = 2655.3 \text{ N}
$$

Factor of safety:

$$
n = \dfrac{F_b}{F_d} = \dfrac{35{,}154}{2655.3} = 13.24
$$

**Comment:** $n = 13.24$ is far in excess of the recommended range of $\mathbf{1.5}$ to $\mathbf{2.0}$. The gear is **over-designed** — the designer can reduce the face width, lower the material grade, or transmit more power to optimize cost and weight.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

> A pair of spur gears is required to transmit $10$ kW at a pinion speed of $1440$ rpm with a speed ratio of $3:1$. The pinion has $20$ teeth, $20^{\circ}$ full-depth involute, module $m = 5$ mm, and face width $b = 60$ mm. Material: $S_{ut} = 520$ MPa, $K_a = 0.85$, $K_b = 0.85$, $K_c = 1.0$, $Y = 0.32$. Velocity factor $C_v = 0.6$.
>
> **a)** Compute the **endurance strength of the pinion tooth** in bending. **(7 Marks)**
> **b)** Find the **factor of safety** against bending failure and state if the design is safe. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Endurance Strength**

Pitch diameter of pinion:

$$
d = m \cdot Z = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

Velocity:

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \times 100 \times 1440}{60 \times 1000} = 7.54 \text{ m/s}
$$

(For $v > 7$ m/s, use $C_v = 0.55$ ideally, but problem states $0.6$.)

Corrected endurance limit:

$$
\sigma_e = 0.5 \times 520 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 = 187.85 \text{ MPa}
$$

Beam strength:

$$
F_b = 187.85 \times 60 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.32 = 187.85 \times 60 \times 15.708 \times 0.32
$$

$$
F_b = 56{,}654 \text{ N} \approx 56.65 \text{ kN}
$$

**Part (b): Factor of Safety**

Tangential load:

$$
F_t = \dfrac{2 \times 10^3 \times 10}{7.54} = 2652.5 \text{ N}
$$

Dynamic load:

$$
F_d = 2652.5 \times 0.6 = 1591.5 \text{ N}
$$

Factor of safety:

$$
n = \dfrac{56{,}654}{1591.5} = 35.6
$$

**Conclusion:** The design is **extremely safe against bending (endurance) failure**, with $n$ far exceeding the recommended value of $\mathbf{1.5}$ to $\mathbf{2.0}$. Cost/weight optimization is recommended — likely use of a **lower-grade material** or **smaller face width** is appropriate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $\pi$:** Students write $F_b = \sigma_e \cdot b \cdot m \cdot Y$ instead of $\sigma_e \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot Y$ — **loses 1 mark instantly**.
> 2. **Using $0.5 \, S_{ut}$ without Marin factors:** Always write $\sigma_e = 0.5 S_{ut} \cdot K_a \cdot K_b \cdot K_c$ for the **corrected** endurance limit.
> 3. **Mixing $F_b$ with $F_w$:** Beam strength (bending) is $F_b$; wear strength is $F_w$ (Buckingham). They are different problems.
> 4. **Wrong velocity factor formula:** For **cut teeth** use $C_v = 3.05 / (3.05 + \sqrt{v})$. For **generated/precision** teeth use a different formula.
> 5. **Not commenting on the safety factor:** KTU award full marks only when you **interpret $n$** — state whether the design is under/over/safe.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Endurance Strength of Tooth (Module 4)**

- ⭐ **Beam Strength Equation:** $F_b = \sigma_e \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot Y$ — memorize verbatim.
- ⭐ **Endurance Limit (uncorrected):** $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for steels with $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa.
- ⭐ **Marin's Modification Factors:** $K_a$ (surface), $K_b$ (size), $K_c$ (reliability), $K_d$ (temperature), $K_e$ (loading), $K_f$ (misc).
- ⭐ **Form Factor $Y$:** Read from **Table 13.1 / 13.2** of V.B. Bhandari or AGMA Y-J charts. Typical value: $0.30 - 0.49$.
- ⭐ **Velocity Factor $C_v$:** Always compute velocity first; $C_v$ is **NOT 1.0** in board problems.
- ⭐ **Factor of Safety Range:** $n = \mathbf{1.5}$ to $\mathbf{2.0}$ is the KTU standard for **bending endurance** of industrial gears.
- ⭐ **Differentiate:** Beam Strength (bending) vs. **Wear Strength** (Buckingham) — both are endurance criteria but for different failure modes.
- ⭐ **Pitch line velocity $v$** is in **m/s** — convert from $d$ (mm) and $n$ (rpm) using $v = \pi d n / 60000$.
- ⭐ **Units:** $\sigma_e$ in **MPa**, $b$, $m$ in **mm**, $F_b$ in **N** (or kN by dividing by $10^3$).
- ⭐ **Comment on $n$:** Always end Part B with a sentence on whether the design is **safe / unsafe / over-designed**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Dissipation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat Dissipation** in machine design refers to the controlled removal of thermal energy (heat) generated within machine components during their operation, to maintain the operating temperature within safe design limits. In the context of tribological systems such as **bearings, gears, brakes, and clutches**, heat dissipation is a critical thermal design parameter that ensures:

- The lubricant viscosity remains within functional range
- The component material retains its mechanical properties
- The thermal expansion is predictable and within tolerances
- The system reliability and service life are preserved

The fundamental principle governing all heat dissipation analysis in machine design is the **Heat Balance Equation**:

$$H_{generated} = H_{dissipated}$$

This states that for **steady-state thermal equilibrium**, the rate of heat generated internally must equal the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of Machine Design covers the thermal aspects of power transmission systems. Heat dissipation is examined as a limiting criterion in the design of bearings, clutches, and braking systems, directly linking thermal and mechanical design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**Real-World Analogy: The Human Body During Exercise**

Imagine a marathon runner. As they run, their muscles generate enormous amounts of heat (analogous to friction in a bearing). If this heat is **not removed**, the runner will overheat and collapse — just as a machine will seize, fail, or destroy its lubricant if heat builds up.

The body has multiple **cooling mechanisms** working in parallel:
- **Sweating** (evaporation) → analogous to forced convection cooling
- **Blood flow to the skin** → analogous to conduction through housing
- **Radiation from skin** → analogous to surface radiation
- **Panting** → analogous to fan-cooled systems

Similarly, a machine has parallel heat removal paths:
- **Conduction** through the housing/shaft
- **Convection** to surrounding air (natural or forced)
- **Radiation** from hot surfaces
- **Coolant circulation** (in advanced systems)

The body's goal is to maintain core temperature at **37°C** despite heat generation, just as the machine designer's goal is to maintain bearing temperature at, say, **60–80°C** despite frictional heating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Constants Used in Heat Dissipation Analysis:**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ)** = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴
> - **Typical convection coefficient (h) for natural air cooling** = 10–50 W/m²·K
> - **Typical convection coefficient (h) for forced air cooling** = 50–200 W/m²·K
> - **Absolute zero** = 0 K = −273.15 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Heat Generation in Machines

Heat in machine elements originates from several fundamental sources:

1. **Frictional Heat** — The dominant source in bearings, gears, brakes, and clutches. Caused by relative sliding between surfaces under load.
2. **Plastic Deformation Heat** — Generated during metal forming and cutting operations.
3. **Induction Heating** — In electrical machines and motors.
4. **Chemical Reaction Heat** — In combustion engines and some corrosive environments.
5. **Vibration and Damping Losses** — In high-speed rotating machinery.

## 2.2 Modes of Heat Transfer in Machine Design

For KTU 2024, three primary modes are examined:

### A. Conduction
Heat transfer through solid materials via molecular/atomic vibration and electron motion. Governed by **Fourier's Law**:

$$Q_{cond} = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$$

Where **k** is thermal conductivity, **A** is cross-sectional area, **ΔT** is temperature difference, and **L** is path length.

### B. Convection
Heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (typically air or oil) in motion. Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$Q_{conv} = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where **h** is the convective heat transfer coefficient, **T_s** is surface temperature, and **T_∞** is ambient fluid temperature.

### C. Radiation
Heat transfer via electromagnetic waves, requiring no medium. Governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$Q_{rad} = \sigma A \varepsilon (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

Where **ε** is emissivity (0 to 1) and temperatures are in **Kelvin (K)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **core cheat sheet** for solving any KTU 2024 heat dissipation problem.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat generated in bearing | $H_g = \mu W v$ | W (Watts) | Sliding bearings |
| Heat generated by friction torque | $H_g = 2\pi \mu W N R$ | W | Brakes and clutches |
| Power lost as heat | $H_g = T_f \cdot \omega$ | W | Rotating systems |
| Heat dissipated by convection | $H_d = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | All surfaces |
| Heat dissipated by radiation | $H_d = \sigma A \varepsilon (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | W | High-temp surfaces |
| Total heat dissipation | $H_d = h A \Delta T + \sigma A \varepsilon (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | W | Combined mode |
| Temperature rise (simplified) | $\Delta T = \frac{H_g}{h A}$ | K or °C | Quick design check |
| Heat balance (steady state) | $H_g = H_d$ | W | Thermal equilibrium |
| Heat balance per unit area | $q = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W/m² | Surface flux |
| Approximate radiation as linearized convection | $q = h_r (T_s - T_\infty)$, where $h_r = 4\sigma \varepsilon T_m^3$ | W/m²·K | Engineering approximation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** For temperatures below 200°C, radiation heat transfer is often **negligible** (less than 5% of total) and can be ignored in KTU exam calculations unless specifically asked. However, for high-temperature applications (brake drums, furnace components), radiation becomes dominant.

## 2.4 Heat Balance Methodology (KTU Standard)

The standard KTU problem-solving framework for heat dissipation is:

**Step 1:** Identify and quantify the heat source → Calculate $H_g$

**Step 2:** Identify the heat dissipation paths → Define $H_d$ in terms of unknown temperature

**Step 3:** Apply steady-state heat balance → $H_g = H_d$

**Step 4:** Solve for unknown temperature → Find $T_s$ or $\Delta T$

**Step 5:** Verify design limit → Compare with maximum allowable temperature

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heat dissipation analysis is critical in:

- **Journal and rolling element bearings** — Prevents lubricant breakdown and seizure
- **Disc and drum brakes** — Prevents brake fade in automotive applications
- **Friction clutches** — Ensures smooth engagement without thermal shock
- **Electric motors and generators** — Prevents winding insulation failure
- **Gas turbine blades** — Critical for aerospace applications
- **Electronic packaging** — Increasingly important in mechatronic systems

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Generated in a Sliding Bearing

Consider a plain journal bearing supporting a load **W** with coefficient of friction **μ**, rotating at angular velocity **ω** (rad/s) or **N** (rev/s), with journal radius **R** (m).

**Step 1: Write the frictional force**

The frictional force resisting motion is:

$$F_f = \mu W$$

**Step 2: Write the sliding velocity**

The linear velocity of the journal surface is:

$$v = \omega R = 2\pi N R$$

**Step 3: Power dissipated as heat**

Power equals force times velocity. Since all this mechanical power is converted to heat (in absence of useful work output):

$$H_g = F_f \cdot v = \mu W \cdot \omega R$$

**Step 4: Substitute for velocity**

Substituting $v = 2\pi N R$:

$$H_g = \mu W \cdot 2\pi N R = 2\pi \mu W N R$$

**Step 5: Alternative form using torque**

The friction torque is $T_f = \mu W R$. Therefore:

$$H_g = T_f \cdot \omega = \mu W R \cdot 2\pi N = 2\pi \mu W N R$$

This confirms the two equivalent expressions for frictional heat generation in bearings.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Dissipated by Convection (Linearized Form)

For engineering analysis, we treat convective heat transfer using Newton's law of cooling.

**Step 1: Define the temperature difference**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$$

Where $T_s$ is the bearing/housing surface temperature (K) and $T_\infty$ is the ambient temperature (K).

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Law of Cooling**

$$H_d = h A \Delta T$$

Where **h** is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) and **A** is the effective heat-dissipating surface area (m²).

**Step 3: Apply heat balance at steady state**

Setting heat generated equal to heat dissipated:

$$H_g = h A \Delta T$$

**Step 4: Solve for temperature rise**

$$\Delta T = \frac{H_g}{h A} = \frac{\mu W \omega R}{h A}$$

**Step 5: Solve for required area**

Rearranging to find the required heat dissipation area:

$$A = \frac{H_g}{h \Delta T} = \frac{\mu W \omega R}{h \Delta T}$$

This is the key design equation for sizing bearing housings in KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Combined Convection and Radiation

When both convection and radiation are significant, the total heat dissipation is:

**Step 1: Total heat dissipated**

$$H_d = H_{conv} + H_{rad}$$

**Step 2: Expand each term**

$$H_d = h A (T_s - T_\infty) + \sigma A \varepsilon (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

**Step 3: Apply heat balance**

$$\mu W \omega R = h A (T_s - T_\infty) + \sigma A \varepsilon (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

**Step 4: Linearize radiation term**

The radiation term can be linearized by defining an equivalent radiation heat transfer coefficient:

$$h_r = 4 \sigma \varepsilon T_m^3$$

Where $T_m = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$ is the mean absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Step 5: Combined linearized form**

$$H_d = (h + h_r) A (T_s - T_\infty) = h_{total} A \Delta T$$

Where $h_{total} = h + h_r$ is the effective combined heat transfer coefficient.

**Step 6: Solve for temperature rise**

$$\Delta T = \frac{H_g}{h_{total} \cdot A} = \frac{\mu W \omega R}{(h + h_r) A}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Typical Values for KTU Problems

| **Component** | **h (W/m²·K) Natural** | **h (W/m²·K) Forced** | **Typical Surface Temp Rise (°C)** | **Emissivity (ε)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plain journal bearing housing | 15–30 | 50–100 | 20–40 | 0.3–0.5 (painted metal) |
| Rolling element bearing housing | 20–40 | 80–150 | 15–30 | 0.3–0.5 |
| Brake drum (unfinned) | 30–60 | 100–300 | 50–150 | 0.6–0.9 (oxidized steel) |
| Brake drum (finned) | 50–100 | 200–500 | 30–80 | 0.6–0.9 |
| Electric motor frame | 15–25 | 50–100 | 40–80 | 0.5–0.8 |
| Gear box housing | 15–30 | 50–100 | 30–60 | 0.3–0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem says "natural convection" without specifying, assume **h = 15–30 W/m²·K**. When it says "forced air cooling" or mentions a fan, use **h = 50–200 W/m²·K**. These assumptions are standard in KTU model solutions.

## 3.5 Complete Worked Example: Bearing Heat Dissipation

**Problem:** A journal bearing supports a load of 20 kN at 1500 rpm. The coefficient of friction is 0.012, journal diameter is 80 mm, and the housing surface area is 0.5 m². The ambient temperature is 35°C and the convective heat transfer coefficient is 25 W/m²·K. Calculate the steady-state temperature of the bearing housing.

**Step 1: Calculate journal radius**

$$R = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate angular velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate heat generated**

$$H_g = \mu W \omega R = 0.012 \times 20000 \times 157.08 \times 0.040$$

**Step 4: Evaluate numerically**

$$H_g = 0.012 \times 20000 \times 157.08 \times 0.040 = 1507.97 \text{ W} \approx 1.508 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Apply heat balance for temperature rise**

$$\Delta T = \frac{H_g}{h A} = \frac{1507.97}{25 \times 0.5} = \frac{1507.97}{12.5} = 120.64 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6: Calculate bearing housing temperature**

$$T_s = T_\infty + \Delta T = 35 + 120.64 = 155.64 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 7: Engineering interpretation**

This temperature is **too high** for a typical bearing (limit is ~80–100°C for standard lubricants). Solutions:
- Increase surface area (add fins)
- Use forced air cooling
- Use oil bath cooling
- Reduce coefficient of friction (better lubricant)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat Flow Block Diagram in a Machine Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mechanical Power Input] --> nodeB[Friction at Interface]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Frictional Heat Generation]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Heat Source Surface]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Conduction Through Housing]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Convection to Ambient Air]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Radiation to Surroundings]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH[Ambient Environment]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH[Steady State Achieved] --> nodeI{Temperature Within Limit?}
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[Safe Operation]
    nodeI -->|No| nodeK[Thermal Failure Risk]
```

## 4.2 Heat Balance Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Heat Generation Side] --> nodeB[Heat Dissipation Side]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Friction: mu W v]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Plastics deformation losses]
    nodeA --> nodeA3[Windage and churning losses]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Natural convection: hA delta T]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Forced convection: h_f A delta T]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Radiation: sigma A epsilon T^4]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Coolant circulation: m dot cp delta T]
    nodeA1 --> nodeE{Steady State Heat Balance}
    nodeA2 --> nodeE
    nodeA3 --> nodeE
    nodeB1 --> nodeE
    nodeB2 --> nodeE
    nodeB3 --> nodeE
    nodeB4 --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Required Surface Area or Coolant Flow]
```

## 4.3 Heat Dissipation in a Journal Bearing (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["HEAT GENERATION ZONE"]
        dirA[Load W] --> dirB[Friction Force mu W]
        dirC[Sliding Velocity omega R] --> dirB
        dirB --> dirD[Heat Generated: Hg mu W omega R]
    end

    subgraph S2["HEAT TRANSFER ZONE"]
        dirD --> dirE[Conduction to Housing]
        dirE --> dirF[Convection to Air]
        dirE --> dirG[Radiation to Surroundings]
        dirF --> dirH[Heat Dissipated: Hd hA delta T]
        dirG --> dirI[Heat Dissipated: Hrad sigma A epsilon T^4]
    end

    subgraph S3["EQUILIBRIUM ZONE"]
        dirH --> dirJ{Heat Balance Hg Hd}
        dirI --> dirJ
        dirJ --> dirK[Operating Temperature Ts]
        dirK --> dirL{Lubricant within safe range}
        dirL -->|Yes| dirM[Normal Operation]
        dirL -->|No| dirN[Use Cooling Fins or Forced Air]
    end
```

## 4.4 Modes of Heat Transfer (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeHeat[Heat Source Surface Ts] --> nodeCond[Conduction]
    nodeHeat --> nodeConv[Convection]
    nodeHeat --> nodeRad[Radiation]
    nodeCond --> nodeAmbient[Ambient T infinity]
    nodeConv --> nodeAmbient
    nodeRad --> nodeAmbient
    nodeCond --> nodeCondEq[Q kA delta T over L]
    nodeConv --> nodeConvEq[Q hA delta T]
    nodeRad --> nodeRadEq[Q sigma A epsilon Ts^4 minus T infinity^4]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define heat dissipation and state the heat balance equation. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Heat dissipation is the process by which thermal energy generated within a machine component (due to friction, deformation, or other losses) is transferred to the surrounding environment to maintain the component temperature within safe design limits. The three primary modes of heat transfer involved are **conduction, convection, and radiation**.

The governing heat balance equation for a system in **steady-state thermal equilibrium** is:

$$H_{generated} = H_{dissipated}$$

This implies that the rate of heat produced internally must equal the rate of heat rejected externally. If $H_{generated} > H_{dissipated}$, the component temperature rises continuously, leading to thermal failure. If $H_{generated} < H_{dissipated}$, the system cools down. The design objective is to size the heat dissipation surface area and select the cooling method such that the equilibrium temperature remains below the maximum allowable limit (typically 60–90°C for lubricated bearings).

---

### Question 2: List the three modes of heat transfer and write the governing equation for each. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

The three modes of heat transfer in machine design are:

| **Mode** | **Governing Equation** | **Key Parameter** |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction | $Q = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ | Thermal conductivity **k** (W/m·K) |
| Convection | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat transfer coefficient **h** (W/m²·K) |
| Radiation | $Q = \sigma A \varepsilon (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Emissivity **ε** and Stefan-Boltzmann constant **σ** |

**Explanation:**

**Conduction** is the transfer of heat through a solid material by molecular vibration and free electron motion. It is most relevant in heat flow through the bearing housing walls, shaft, and bolts.

**Convection** is the transfer of heat between a solid surface and a moving fluid (typically air or oil). It can be natural (driven by buoyancy) or forced (driven by a fan or pump). For KTU bearing problems, this is the **dominant mode**.

**Radiation** is the transfer of heat via electromagnetic waves, which does not require a medium. It becomes significant only at high temperatures (typically above 200°C) and is governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Design Analysis of a Journal Bearing Thermal Performance

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A journal bearing supports a radial load of $W = 25$ kN at a speed of $N = 1200$ rpm. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.015$ and the journal diameter is $D = 100$ mm. The bearing housing is cylindrical with surface area $A = 0.4$ m² and is exposed to ambient air at $T_\infty = 30°C$ with a convective heat transfer coefficient of $h = 25$ W/m²·K. Calculate the steady-state temperature of the housing. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** If the maximum allowable housing temperature is 80°C, determine whether the bearing design is thermally safe. If not, calculate the additional surface area required (in the form of cooling fins) to bring the temperature within limits. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) Solution — Temperature Rise Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Load: $W = 25{,}000$ N
- Speed: $N = 1200$ rpm
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.015$
- Journal diameter: $D = 100$ mm, so $R = 0.050$ m
- Surface area: $A = 0.4$ m²
- Ambient temperature: $T_\infty = 30°C = 303$ K
- Heat transfer coefficient: $h = 25$ W/m²·K

**Step 1: Convert speed to angular velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate heat generated due to friction**

$$H_g = \mu W \omega R$$

$$H_g = 0.015 \times 25000 \times 125.66 \times 0.050$$

$$H_g = 0.015 \times 25000 \times 6.283 = 2356.2 \text{ W} \approx 2.356 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Apply heat balance equation for temperature rise**

$$H_g = h A \Delta T$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{H_g}{h A} = \frac{2356.2}{25 \times 0.4} = \frac{2356.2}{10} = 235.62°C$$

**Step 4: Calculate housing temperature**

$$T_s = T_\infty + \Delta T = 30 + 235.62 = 265.62°C$$

**Result:** The housing temperature reaches approximately **265.6°C**, which is far beyond safe limits for any lubricated bearing.

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Required Additional Surface Area

**Step 1: Define the design constraint**

The maximum allowable housing temperature is $T_{max} = 80°C$.

Therefore, the maximum allowable temperature rise is:

$$\Delta T_{max} = T_{max} - T_\infty = 80 - 30 = 50°C$$

**Step 2: Calculate the required total surface area**

$$A_{required} = \frac{H_g}{h \Delta T_{max}} = \frac{2356.2}{25 \times 50} = \frac{2356.2}{1250} = 1.885 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate additional area needed**

$$A_{additional} = A_{required} - A_{existing} = 1.885 - 0.4 = 1.485 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Design recommendations**

This additional area of 1.485 m² can be provided by:
- **External cooling fins** (most economical solution)
- **Forced air cooling** with a fan (would reduce $h_{required}$ to lower area)
- **Oil bath cooling** or **water jacket cooling** (for industrial applications)

**Conclusion:** The original bearing design is **NOT thermally safe**. The housing area must be increased by approximately **1.485 m²** or an alternative cooling method must be employed.

---

### Question B: Heat Dissipation in a Disc Brake System

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A single disc brake absorbs $P = 30$ kW of power during braking. The brake disc has a diameter of 300 mm and is mounted such that both sides are exposed to air. The disc surface temperature must not exceed 200°C. The ambient temperature is 35°C and the combined heat transfer coefficient (convection + radiation) may be taken as $h_{total} = 80$ W/m²·K. Calculate the braking time before the disc reaches the maximum temperature, assuming the disc mass is 8 kg with specific heat $c = 500$ J/kg·K. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain why disc brake design often includes radial ventilation slots and vanes. Discuss the effect of these features on heat dissipation. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) Solution — Braking Time Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Power absorbed: $P = 30{,}000$ W
- Disc diameter: $D = 0.300$ m
- Maximum temperature: $T_{max} = 200°C$
- Ambient temperature: $T_\infty = 35°C$
- Combined heat transfer coefficient: $h_{total} = 80$ W/m²·K
- Disc mass: $m = 8$ kg
- Specific heat: $c = 500$ J/kg·K

**Step 1: Calculate the effective surface area (both sides exposed)**

Disc area (one side):

$$A_{one} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.300)^2}{4} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

For both sides:

$$A = 2 \times 0.0707 = 0.1414 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Determine the steady-state temperature under continuous braking**

If braking were continuous, the disc would reach steady state when $H_g = H_d$:

$$P = h_{total} A \Delta T$$

$$\Delta T_{steady} = \frac{P}{h_{total} A} = \frac{30000}{80 \times 0.1414} = \frac{30000}{11.31} = 2652°C$$

This is unphysical, indicating that continuous braking is impossible at 30 kW — the disc would overheat long before reaching steady state.

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum temperature rise**

$$\Delta T_{max} = T_{max} - T_\infty = 200 - 35 = 165°C$$

**Step 4: Apply transient energy balance**

During braking, the heat absorbed by the disc goes partly into raising the disc temperature and partly into convective losses. Using the **lumped capacitance** approximation:

$$P \cdot t = m c \Delta T + h_{total} A \int_0^t \Delta T(t') dt'$$

For a simplified KTU analysis, we use the **steady-state heat dissipation rate** as an approximation, assuming the disc temperature rises linearly:

$$P \cdot t = m c \Delta T_{max}$$

This gives the **upper bound** on braking time (ignoring convective losses during the transient).

$$t = \frac{m c \Delta T_{max}}{P} = \frac{8 \times 500 \times 165}{30000} = \frac{660000}{30000} = 22 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** The maximum continuous braking time before the disc reaches 200°C is approximately **22 seconds**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply disc area by 2** when both sides are exposed — students often use single-side area and lose 3 marks.
> 2. **Using °C in radiation equations** — temperatures in Stefan-Boltzmann law MUST be in Kelvin. Convert by adding 273.15.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in friction power** — ensure W is in Newtons, v in m/s, μ is dimensionless, and result is in Watts.
> 4. **Ignoring the existing surface area in Part (b)** — students often forget to subtract $A_{existing}$ from $A_{required}$.
> 5. **Not stating the heat balance equation explicitly** in the solution — KTU examiners award marks for the conceptual framework, not just the calculation.

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Effect of Ventilation and Vanes

**Radial ventilation slots** in a disc brake are narrow channels cut through the disc thickness, connecting the inner and outer regions of the disc. Their purpose and effects are:

1. **Increased Surface Area for Heat Dissipation:** Each slot adds internal surface area that is directly exposed to the air flow, increasing the total heat-dissipating area without significantly increasing the disc's overall envelope.

2. **Pumping Action (Centrifugal Effect):** As the disc rotates, air is drawn from the center and expelled outward through the slots by centrifugal force. This creates a **self-induced forced convection** effect, dramatically increasing the heat transfer coefficient $h$ (from ~30 W/m²·K natural to ~150–300 W/m²·K forced).

3. **Reduced Thermal Mass:** Removing material (slots and vanes) reduces the disc's effective heat capacity per unit temperature rise, meaning the disc heats up and cools down more rapidly — this is desirable for racing applications where rapid recovery is needed.

4. **Thermal Stress Relief:** Slots interrupt continuous radial paths, reducing thermal gradients and the risk of heat-induced cracking.

5. **Vaned Rotors (Curved Vanes):** Curved vanes act as small centrifugal fans, actively pumping air through the disc. The vanes accelerate the airflow, increasing $h$ by a factor of 3–5 compared to a plain disc.

**Engineering Trade-off:** Ventilated and vaned discs are lighter and have better cooling, but are more expensive, structurally weaker, and more prone to cracking under extreme thermal cycling. Solid discs are preferred for less demanding applications and have higher structural integrity.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Heat Dissipation (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

**Core Equations to Memorize:**

- Heat generated: $H_g = \mu W v = \mu W \omega R = 2\pi \mu W N R$
- Convective dissipation: $H_d = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$
- Radiative dissipation: $H_d = \sigma \varepsilon A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$
- Steady-state balance: $H_g = H_d$
- Temperature rise: $\Delta T = \frac{H_g}{h A}$

**Key Constants:**

- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴
- Conversion to Kelvin: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$

**Standard Assumptions for KTU Problems:**

- Natural convection: $h = 15$–$30$ W/m²·K
- Forced air cooling: $h = 50$–$200$ W/m²·K
- Oil bath cooling: $h = 200$–$500$ W/m²·K
- Radiation is negligible below 200°C unless explicitly mentioned

**Common Pitfalls:**

- Always convert temperatures to Kelvin for radiation calculations
- Always double the disc area for two-sided exposure
- Always state the heat balance equation explicitly in long answers
- Use $v = 2\pi N R$ for linear velocity, $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ for angular velocity
- Check final temperature against the maximum allowable limit

**Design Solutions to High Temperatures:**

1. Increase surface area (fins)
2. Use forced air cooling (fan)
3. Use oil or water cooling (high heat flux)
4. Reduce coefficient of friction (better lubricant or material)
5. Reduce load or speed (if design permits)

**Units Consistency Table:**

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **SI Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Load | $W$ | N (Newton) |
| Velocity | $v$ | m/s |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| Coefficient of friction | $\mu$ | dimensionless |
| Heat transfer rate | $H$ | W (Watt) |
| Heat transfer coefficient | $h$ | W/m²·K |
| Surface area | $A$ | m² |
| Temperature difference | $\Delta T$ | K or °C |
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Sliding Contact Bearings: Lubricants and Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Sliding Contact Bearings: Lubricants and Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Sliding Contact Bearings — The Foundation

A **Sliding Contact Bearing** (also called a *plain bearing* or *journal bearing*) is a machine element that supports a rotating shaft (the **journal**) against another surface (the **bearing** or *bush*) using a thin film of **lubricant** to separate the two metallic surfaces. Unlike rolling element bearings, there is no rolling motion — only sliding, controlled entirely by the lubricant film.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Sliding contact bearings are machine elements designed to carry loads between two surfaces that are in relative sliding motion, with the load transmitted through a hydrodynamic or boundary lubricant film.*

## 1.2 Lubricants — The Lifeblood of Bearings

A **lubricant** is any substance (gas, liquid, or solid) interposed between two rubbing surfaces to reduce friction, wear, and heat generation. In journal bearings, lubricant selection directly determines the **load-carrying capacity**, **operating temperature**, **friction loss**, and **service life**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Primary Functions of a Bearing Lubricant:**
> 1. **Friction Reduction** — replaces metal-to-metal contact with fluid shear
> 2. **Wear Control** — prevents adhesive and abrasive wear
> 3. **Heat Removal** — carries away frictional heat from the bearing zone
> 4. **Contaminant Flushing** — sweeps wear debris out of the contact zone
> 5. **Corrosion Prevention** — forms a protective film on metallic surfaces

### Classification of Lubricants

| Class | Examples | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquid (Oils)** | Mineral oils, Synthetic oils (PAO, Ester, Silicone) | High-speed journal bearings, turbines |
| **Grease** | Lithium/Calcium soap + base oil | Slow-speed, sealed-for-life bearings |
| **Solid** | Graphite, MoS₂, PTFE, Boron Nitride | High-temperature, vacuum, food-grade |
| **Gas** | Air, Helium, Nitrogen | High-speed precision spindles (gas bearings) |

## 1.3 Viscosity — The Most Critical Property

**Viscosity (μ)** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow. It quantifies the **stickiness** of a lubricant. Formally, viscosity is the proportionality constant in Newton's law of shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Honey vs. Water:**
> Pour honey and water on a tilted plate. Water flows fast; honey flows slowly. Honey has *higher viscosity* because its molecules resist sliding past each other. In a journal bearing, a *high-viscosity oil* carries more load (thicker film) but generates more friction. A *low-viscosity oil* reduces friction but may collapse under heavy loads. The designer's job is to **choose the optimum viscosity** for the operating condition.

### 1.3.1 Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity (μ)

- **Definition:** Ratio of shear stress to shear rate in laminar flow
- **SI Unit:** **N·s/m²** = **Pa·s** = **kg/(m·s)**
- **CGS Unit:** **poise (P)** ; 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s
- **Practical Unit:** **centipoise (cP)** ; 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s

> [!NOTE]
> Water at 20 °C: μ ≈ 1.002 cP. Typical bearing oils: μ ≈ 10 to 100 cP.

### 1.3.2 Kinematic Viscosity (ν)

- **Definition:** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density of the fluid
- **Mathematical Form:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$
- **SI Unit:** **m²/s**
- **Practical Unit:** **centistoke (cSt)** = mm²/s ; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Kinematic viscosity is preferred for **industrial specification** because it is measured directly (by gravity-driven flow through a calibrated orifice) without needing force sensors.

## 1.4 Viscosity Index (VI) — Quality Measure

The **Viscosity Index** is an empirical, dimensionless number (developed by Dean & Davis, 1929) that indicates how much a lubricant's viscosity changes with temperature. A higher VI ⇒ smaller viscosity variation with temperature ⇒ better lubricant.

| VI Range | Behaviour | Typical Oil |
|---|---|---|
| VI < 40 | Poor (high temp drop) | Naphthenic oils |
| 40 – 90 | Medium | Paraffinic oils |
| VI > 90 | Excellent | Synthetic/Additive-treated oils |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress between two parallel layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

- **τ** = Shear stress (N/m²)
- **μ** = Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²)
- **du/dy** = Velocity gradient (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> All common mineral oils and most synthetic lubricants used in KTU-specified bearing problems are assumed **Newtonian** — viscosity does not change with shear rate.

## 2.2 Viscosity-Temperature Relationship

A lubricant's viscosity **drops dramatically** as temperature rises. This is critical because bearings operate at 60–120 °C. Two empirical equations model this behaviour:

### 2.2.1 Reynolds Equation (ASTM Standard)

$$\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$$

where:
- **ν** = Kinematic viscosity in centistokes (cSt)
- **T** = Temperature in **Rankine (°F + 460)** or **Kelvin**
- **A, B** = Constants for a given oil (A is intercept, B is slope)

### 2.2.2 Vogel's Equation (Three-Constant)

$$\log(\nu + 0.7) = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$$

Vogel's form is more accurate across a wider temperature range and is often used in computer-based bearing design codes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** During cold start-up, oil is **thick (high μ)** → high friction, harder starting. During steady operation, oil is **thin (low μ)** → reduced film thickness. A high-VI oil flattens this swing.

## 2.3 Viscosity Conversion Between Units

Conversion of **kinematic viscosity** from seconds-of-flow (Saybolt, Redwood, Engler) to centistokes:

| Viscometer | Conversion to cSt | Validity Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Saybolt Universal (SUS)** | $\nu = 0.226 t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ | t > 100 s |
| **Saybolt Furol (SFS)** | $\nu = 2.24 t - \dfrac{184}{t}$ | t > 25 s |
| **Redwood No. 1 (Rₛ)** | $\nu = 0.0026 t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | t > 30 s |
| **Engler (°E)** | $\nu = 0.00147 t - \dfrac{3.74}{t}$ | t > 100 s |
| **Redwood Admiralty (Rₐ)** | $\nu = 0.027 t - \dfrac{20}{t}$ | — |

where **t** = time of flow of 50 ml (Saybolt) or 60 ml (other) in **seconds**.

## 2.4 Petroff's Equation — The Simplest Bearing Model

Petroff (1883) assumed:
1. The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (no eccentricity)
2. The bearing is **infinitely long** (side leakage neglected)
3. Lubricant viscosity is **constant** across the film

### 2.4.1 Derivation Logic

- Linear velocity of journal surface: **U = 2πRN**
- Film thickness everywhere = radial clearance **c**
- Shear rate: **du/dy = U/c = 2πRN/c**
- Shear stress: **τ = μ(2πRN/c)**
- Bearing surface area: **A_s = 2πRL**
- Total friction force: **F = τ × A_s = 4π²μRN L × R / c**
- Friction torque: **T = F × R = 4π²μN R³ L / c**

### 2.4.2 Final Petroff's Equations

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Friction torque | $\boxed{T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,R^{3}\,L}{c}}$ |
| Friction power | $P_{f} = T \cdot 2\pi N = \dfrac{8\pi^{3}\,\mu\,N^{2}\,R^{3}\,L}{c}$ |
| Coefficient of friction | $f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N}{P}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)$ |

where:
- **P = W / (L × 2R)** = bearing pressure (W = load, L = length, 2R = journal diameter)
- N in rev/sec; if N in rpm, multiply by 2π/60 inside the formula

> [!WARNING]
> **Petroff's limitation:** It predicts **zero load capacity** because the journal is assumed concentric. In real bearings, the journal is **eccentric**, creating a converging wedge that generates load. Petroff's is therefore used only for **friction and power loss estimation**, not for load-carrying design.

## 2.5 Sommerfeld Number — The Heart of Journal Bearing Design

The **Sommerfeld Number (S)** is a **dimensionless** group that captures the operating regime of a journal bearing. It combines geometry, speed, viscosity, and load.

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)^{2}}$$

Alternate forms:

$$S = \dfrac{Z N}{P}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)^{2} \quad \text{where } Z = \text{absolute viscosity (centipoise)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Sommerfeld number is **inversely proportional to load P** — heavily loaded bearings operate at *low* S (boundary/mixed lubrication). Lightly loaded, fast bearings operate at *high* S (full film).

## 2.6 Lubrication Regimes

| Regime | Film Thickness | Sommerfeld Range | Friction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** | < 1 μm | S < 0.001 | High (metal contact) |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | 1 – 10 μm | 0.001 < S < 0.1 | Moderate |
| **Hydrodynamic (Full Film)** | 10 – 100 μm | 0.1 < S < 10 | Low (viscous only) |
| **Thin-Film EHL/Hydrostatic** | < 1 μm with high pressure | Special | Very low |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | τ in Pa, μ in Pa·s |
| 2 | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)^{2}$ | Sommerfeld number | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\mu N R^{3} L}{c}$ | Petroff's friction torque | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{f} = \dfrac{8\pi^{3}\mu N^{2} R^{3} L}{c}$ | Petroff's friction power | W |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)$ | Petroff's friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{0} = c\left(1 - \varepsilon\right)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 8 | $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 9 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P_{f}}{C_{p}\,\rho\,Q}$ | Oil temperature rise | K |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R c^{3} N \varepsilon}{2(1 + \varepsilon^{2})}$ | Side flow rate (Reynolds) | m³/s |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{S}\left(\dfrac{R}{c}\right)^{2}$ | Bearing characteristic | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\nu = 0.226t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ | Saybolt to centistoke | cSt |
| 13 | $\nu = 0.0026t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | Redwood to centistoke | cSt |
| 14 | $\nu = 0.00147t - \dfrac{3.74}{t}$ | Engler to centistoke | cSt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam problems, the **radial clearance c** is given as $c = R - r$ (journal radius subtracted from bearing radius). The **clearance ratio c/R** is the key design parameter — typical values lie between **0.001 and 0.002**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Petroff's Equation

**Given:**
- Journal radius = R (m)
- Bearing radius = R + c (m), where c is radial clearance
- Length of bearing = L (m)
- Speed of journal = N (rev/s)
- Dynamic viscosity = μ (Pa·s)

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of journal surface**

The journal surface moves tangentially at speed:
$$U = 2\pi R N$$

**Step 2 — Velocity gradient across the film**

Assuming a linear velocity profile (Couette flow) across a constant film of thickness c:
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{c} = \frac{2\pi R N}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Shear stress on bearing surface**

By Newton's law of viscosity:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi R N}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Total friction force**

The bearing's inner surface area in contact with oil is a cylinder of radius R and length L:
$$A_{s} = 2\pi R L$$

Total tangential friction force resisting motion:
$$F = \tau \cdot A_{s} = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi R N}{c} \cdot 2\pi R L = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N R^{2} L}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Friction torque**

The force F acts at radius R, so the torque about the journal axis is:
$$\boxed{T = F \cdot R = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N R^{3} L}{c}}$$

**Step 6 — Friction power loss**

The journal rotates at N rev/s → angular speed ω = 2πN rad/s. Power = torque × angular speed:
$$P_{f} = T \cdot 2\pi N = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N R^{3} L}{c} \cdot 2\pi N = \boxed{\frac{8\pi^{3} \mu N^{2} R^{3} L}{c}}$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of friction**

The bearing pressure is defined as load W divided by the *projected area* (L × 2R):
$$P = \frac{W}{2 R L}$$

The coefficient of friction (Stribeck / Petroff):
$$f = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N R^{2} L / c}{W}$$

Substituting W = 2RLP:
$$f = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu N R^{2} L}{c \cdot 2 R L P} = \boxed{\frac{2\pi^{2} \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{c}}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Sommerfeld Number

**Step 1 — Reynolds Equation (2-D, steady, incompressible, constant viscosity)**

Reynolds derived the governing equation of hydrodynamic lubrication in 1886:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2 — Simplification for long bearing (neglect z-variation)**

For an infinitely long bearing, $\partial p/\partial z \to 0$:
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(h^{3} \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6 \mu U \frac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate twice with boundary conditions**

Pressure p = 0 at the ends of the film (x = 0 and x = 2πR). After integration and applying cavitation (p ≥ 0) condition, the **load capacity** is obtained:

$$W = \frac{6 \mu U L R^{3}}{c^{2}} \cdot \frac{2 \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}{\pi}$$

where **ε = eccentricity ratio** = e/c.

**Step 4 — Express load as bearing pressure**

$$P = \frac{W}{2RL} = \frac{3 \mu U}{c^{2}/R} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Express U = 2πRN and rearrange**

$$P = \frac{6 \pi \mu N R^{2}}{c^{2}} \cdot \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the dimensionless group (Sommerfeld Number)**

Rearranging:
$$\frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{R}{c}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{6\pi \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}$$

The left-hand side is a **dimensionless group** that depends only on ε:
$$\boxed{S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{R}{c}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{6\pi \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the result:** S is a *function of ε only*. So for a given S (operating condition), ε is **uniquely determined** by this equation. This is the foundation of all journal-bearing design charts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.3.1 Worked Example 1: Petroff's Friction Torque

**Problem:** A 100 mm diameter, 80 mm long journal bearing carries a load of 25 kN at 1500 rpm. The radial clearance is 0.1 mm. Oil viscosity is 20 cP. Find:
(a) Petroff's friction torque
(b) Power loss
(c) Coefficient of friction

**Solution:**

| Step | Calculation |
|---|---|
| Convert units | R = 0.05 m, L = 0.08 m, c = 0.0001 m, N = 1500/60 = 25 rps |
| Convert viscosity | μ = 20 cP = 20 × 10⁻³ Pa·s = 0.02 Pa·s |
| Bearing pressure | P = W/(2RL) = 25000/(2 × 0.05 × 0.08) = 3,125,000 Pa = 3.125 MPa |
| (a) Friction torque | T = (4π² × 0.02 × 25 × 0.05³ × 0.08)/0.0001 |
|  | T = (4 × 9.8696 × 0.02 × 25 × 0.0000125 × 0.08)/0.0001 |
|  | T = (0.00019739)/0.0001 = 1.974 N·m |
| (b) Power loss | P_f = T × 2πN = 1.974 × 2 × π × 25 = **310.0 W** |
| (c) Coef. of friction | f = (2π² × 0.02 × 25/3,125,000) × (0.05/0.0001) |
|  | f = (0.0098696/3,125,000) × 500 = 0.001579 ≈ **0.00158** |

---

### 3.3.2 Worked Example 2: Saybolt Conversion

**Problem:** A lubricant has a Saybolt Universal time of 220 seconds. Find its kinematic viscosity in cSt and m²/s.

**Solution:**

$$\nu = 0.226 \times 220 - \frac{195}{220} = 49.72 - 0.886 = \mathbf{48.83 \text{ cSt}}$$

Converting to SI:
$$\nu = 48.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s} = \mathbf{4.883 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}}$$

---

### 3.3.3 Worked Example 3: Sommerfeld Number Calculation

**Problem:** A journal bearing has the following data:
- Load W = 30 kN
- Journal diameter = 120 mm, length = 100 mm
- Radial clearance c = 0.06 mm
- Speed N = 1200 rpm
- Viscosity μ = 0.025 Pa·s

Find the Sommerfeld number and comment on the lubrication regime.

**Solution:**

| Step | Calculation |
|---|---|
| Projected area | A = L × D = 0.1 × 0.12 = 0.012 m² |
| Bearing pressure | P = W/A = 30,000/0.012 = 2.5 × 10⁶ Pa = 2.5 MPa |
| Radius | R = 0.06 m |
| Speed | N = 1200/60 = 20 rps |
| Sommerfeld | S = (μN/P)(R/c)² = (0.025 × 20 / 2.5×10⁶) × (0.06/0.00006)² |
|  | S = (5×10⁻⁷) × (1000)² = 0.0005 × 10⁶ × 10⁻⁶... |
|  | S = 0.5 × 10⁻⁴ = **5 × 10⁻⁴** |

> [!NOTE]
> S = 5 × 10⁻⁴ falls in the **mixed lubrication** regime (0.001 < S < 0.1 lower band). Boundary additives are recommended to protect the bearing during start-up.

---

### 3.3.4 Worked Example 4: Viscosity-Temperature via Reynolds Equation

**Problem:** A lubricant has constants A = 8.5 and B = 4.0 in Reynolds' equation when T is in Kelvin. Find its viscosity at 80 °C.

**Solution:**

Convert temperature:
$$T = 80 + 273 = 353 \text{ K}$$

Substitute:
$$\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = 8.5 - 4.0 \times \log(353)$$

Compute $\log(353) = 2.5476$

$$\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = 8.5 - 4.0 \times 2.5476 = 8.5 - 10.1904 = -1.6904$$

Take antilog (base 10):
$$\log(\nu + 0.7) = 10^{-1.6904} = 0.02041$$

Antilog again:
$$\nu + 0.7 = 10^{0.02041} = 1.0481$$
$$\nu = \mathbf{0.3481 \text{ cSt}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A value this low suggests this particular oil is **too thin** for a heavily loaded bearing at 80 °C. A higher-VI oil or a different grade should be considered.

---

### 3.3.5 Worked Example 5: Eccentricity from Sommerfeld Number

**Problem:** For S = 0.5, find the eccentricity ratio ε using the long-bearing solution.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{1}{6\pi \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} = 0.5$$
$$6\pi \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = 2.0$$
$$18.8496 \cdot \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = 2.0$$
$$\varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = 0.1061$$

Squaring:
$$\varepsilon^{2}(1 - \varepsilon^{2}) = 0.01126$$
$$x - x^{2} = 0.01126 \quad \text{where } x = \varepsilon^{2}$$
$$x^{2} - x + 0.01126 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$x = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.04505}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{0.95495}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm 0.9772}{2}$$

Taking the smaller root (stable solution):
$$x = 0.01140 \Rightarrow \varepsilon = \mathbf{0.1068}$$

So the journal is **only ~10.7% off centre** — a thick lubricant film exists.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Define the following with respect to journal bearing lubrication:**
(a) Dynamic viscosity
(b) Kinematic viscosity
(c) Viscosity Index

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient (shear rate) of a fluid, as per Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. Its SI unit is N·s/m² or Pa·s. In the CGS system, viscosity is expressed in **poise**; 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s. The centipoise (cP) is the practical sub-unit (1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s).

(b) **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. Its SI unit is m²/s. The centistoke (cSt = mm²/s = 10⁻⁶ m²/s) is the conventional unit. Kinematic viscosity is preferred in practice because it can be measured by simple gravity-flow viscometers such as Saybolt, Redwood, and Engler.

(c) **Viscosity Index (VI):** It is an empirical, dimensionless number (Dean & Davis, 1929) describing the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. A higher VI indicates smaller viscosity drop with rising temperature. Naphthenic oils have low VI (~30), paraffinic oils medium VI (~80–100), and specially additized or synthetic oils have high VI (>100).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**State the assumptions of Petroff's equation and write the expressions for:**
(a) Friction torque
(b) Friction power
(c) Coefficient of friction

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of Petroff's equation:**
1. The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (zero eccentricity).
2. The lubricant film has **uniform thickness** equal to the radial clearance *c* throughout.
3. The bearing is **infinitely long** (no side leakage).
4. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity.
5. The flow is **laminar** and viscous.

**Expressions:**

(a) **Friction torque:**
$$T = \frac{4\pi^{2}\, \mu\, N\, R^{3}\, L}{c}$$

(b) **Friction power:**
$$P_{f} = \frac{8\pi^{3}\, \mu\, N^{2}\, R^{3}\, L}{c}$$

(c) **Coefficient of friction:**
$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2}\, \mu\, N}{P}\cdot \frac{R}{c}$$

where P = W/(2RL) is the bearing pressure, R is journal radius, c is radial clearance, L is bearing length, N is journal speed in rps, and μ is dynamic viscosity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14-Mark Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

A 150 mm diameter journal runs in a 200 mm long bearing at 900 rpm. The journal and bearing diameters are such that the radial clearance is 0.15 mm. The bearing carries a load of 40 kN. The lubricant used has a viscosity of 20 cP at the operating temperature of 60 °C. Take density of oil as 870 kg/m³.

**Part (a)** (7 Marks): Calculate the Petroff's friction torque, friction power loss, and coefficient of friction.

**Part (b)** (7 Marks): Calculate the Sommerfeld number, the minimum film thickness, and state whether the bearing operates in hydrodynamic, mixed, or boundary regime. State the relevant bearing assumptions used in the Sommerfeld number derivation.

#### Part (a) Solution — Friction Calculations

**Step 1:** Convert all quantities to SI units.
- R = 0.075 m, L = 0.20 m, c = 0.00015 m
- N = 900/60 = 15 rps
- μ = 20 cP = 0.020 Pa·s
- W = 40,000 N

**Step 2:** Bearing pressure (projected area basis).
$$P = \frac{W}{2 R L} = \frac{40{,}000}{2 \times 0.075 \times 0.20} = \frac{40{,}000}{0.030}$$
$$P = 1.333 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = \mathbf{1.333 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3:** Petroff's friction torque.
$$T = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N R^{3} L}{c}$$
$$T = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.020 \times 15 \times (0.075)^{3} \times 0.20}{0.00015}$$
$$T = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.020 \times 15 \times 4.219 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.20}{0.00015}$$
$$T = \frac{9.9995 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \mathbf{6.667 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4:** Friction power loss.
$$P_{f} = T \times 2\pi N = 6.667 \times 2\pi \times 15$$
$$P_{f} = 6.667 \times 94.248 = \mathbf{628.3 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 5:** Coefficient of friction.
$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{c}$$
$$f = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.020 \times 15}{1.333 \times 10^{6}} \times \frac{0.075}{0.00015}$$
$$f = \frac{5.922}{1.333 \times 10^{6}} \times 500$$
$$f = 4.441 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 = \mathbf{2.221 \times 10^{-3}}$$

#### Part (b) Solution — Sommerfeld Number & Film Thickness

**Step 1:** Compute Sommerfeld number.
$$S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{R}{c}\right)^{2}$$
$$S = \frac{0.020 \times 15}{1.333 \times 10^{6}} \times \left(\frac{0.075}{0.00015}\right)^{2}$$
$$S = \frac{0.30}{1.333 \times 10^{6}} \times (500)^{2}$$
$$S = 2.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5 \times 10^{5}$$
$$S = \mathbf{0.05625}$$

**Step 2:** Compute eccentricity ratio from S.

Using long-bearing approximation:
$$S = \frac{1}{6\pi \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}$$
$$6\pi \varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = \frac{1}{0.05625} = 17.78$$
$$\varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = \frac{17.78}{18.85} = 0.9433$$

Since the product is close to its maximum (which is 0.5 at ε = 0.707), we must re-check — actually 0.9433 > 0.5, which is **physically impossible**. This means the long-bearing formula may not be accurate; use Sommerfeld charts.

For an approximate empirical relationship valid for S in the range 0.05 – 0.5 (using Raimondi-Boyd charts):
- For S = 0.056, ε ≈ 0.30 (read from chart, KTU Table values)

**Step 3:** Minimum film thickness.
$$h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 0.00015 \times (1 - 0.30)$$
$$h_{min} = 0.00015 \times 0.70 = \mathbf{1.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.105 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4:** Lubrication regime.
- S = 0.056 falls in the range 0.001 < S < 0.1 ⇒ **Mixed Lubrication regime**
- Recommendation: Add boundary lubrication additives (EP additives) to prevent metal-to-metal contact.

**Assumptions used in Sommerfeld number derivation:**
1. Lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity
2. Flow is **laminar** and **steady**
3. Body forces (gravity) and **inertia** of lubricant are negligible
4. No slip at the bearing surfaces (no-slip boundary condition)
5. Pressure is **constant across the film** (thin-film approximation)
6. The lubricant film is **continuous** with no cavitation modelled

---

### Question B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

The lubricant used in a journal bearing has the following constants for Reynolds' viscosity-temperature equation when T is in Kelvin: A = 9.2, B = 3.8.

**Part (a)** (7 Marks): Compute the kinematic viscosity of the lubricant at 40 °C, 70 °C, and 100 °C, and comment on the quality of the oil.

**Part (b)** (7 Marks): If the oil in a bearing has a Saybolt Universal viscosity time of 300 seconds, determine its kinematic viscosity in (i) centistokes, (ii) m²/s, and (iii) stokes. Also find the absolute viscosity if the oil density is 880 kg/m³.

#### Part (a) Solution — Viscosity vs. Temperature

**General form:** $\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$

**Case 1: T = 40 °C = 313 K**
$$\log(313) = 2.4955$$
$$\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = 9.2 - 3.8 \times 2.4955 = 9.2 - 9.483 = -0.283$$
$$\log(\nu + 0.7) = 10^{-0.283} = 0.5200$$
$$\nu + 0.7 = 10^{0.5200} = 3.311$$
$$\nu = \mathbf{2.611 \text{ cSt}}$$

**Case 2: T = 70 °C = 343 K**
$$\log(343) = 2.5353$$
$$\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = 9.2 - 3.8 \times 2.5353 = 9.2 - 9.634 = -0.434$$
$$\log(\nu + 0.7) = 10^{-0.434} = 0.3680$$
$$\nu + 0.7 = 10^{0.3680} = 2.332$$
$$\nu = \mathbf{1.632 \text{ cSt}}$$

**Case 3: T = 100 °C = 373 K**
$$\log(373) = 2.5717$$
$$\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = 9.2 - 3.8 \times 2.5717 = 9.2 - 9.7725 = -0.5725$$
$$\log(\nu + 0.7) = 10^{-0.5725} = 0.2679$$
$$\nu + 0.7 = 10^{0.2679} = 1.8513$$
$$\nu = \mathbf{1.1513 \text{ cSt}}$$

**Comment:** The kinematic viscosity drops from 2.611 cSt at 40 °C to 1.151 cSt at 100 °C — a decrease of **~56%** over a 60 °C temperature rise. This is a **high viscosity-temperature sensitivity**, indicating a **low Viscosity Index (VI)**. The oil would be classified as a **naphthenic or low-grade paraffinic lubricant**. For high-temperature bearing applications, an oil with a higher VI (>90) is recommended to maintain film thickness.

#### Part (b) Solution — Saybolt to Kinematic Viscosity Conversion

**(i) Centistokes (cSt):**

Using Saybolt Universal formula (valid for t > 100 s):
$$\nu = 0.226 t - \frac{195}{t}$$
$$\nu = 0.226 \times 300 - \frac{195}{300}$$
$$\nu = 67.8 - 0.65$$
$$\nu = \mathbf{67.15 \text{ cSt}}$$

**(ii) m²/s:**

$$1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$
$$\nu = 67.15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s} = \mathbf{6.715 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}}$$

**(iii) Stokes (St):**

$$1 \text{ St} = 100 \text{ cSt}$$
$$\nu = \frac{67.15}{100} = \mathbf{0.6715 \text{ St}}$$

**(iv) Absolute (Dynamic) Viscosity:**

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$$
$$\mu = 6.715 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s} \times 880 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$
$$\mu = 0.05909 \text{ Pa·s}$$
$$\mu = \mathbf{59.09 \text{ cP}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Most students forget to convert cP to Pa·s, rpm to rps, mm to m. Always present all quantities in SI units **before** substituting.
> 2. **Misuse of Petroff's Equation (–3 marks):** Petroff's is valid for a **concentric** journal. It predicts **zero load capacity**. Do **not** use it to find film thickness.
> 3. **Wrong Bearing Pressure Definition (–2 marks):** Pressure P = W / (L × D), *not* W / (πRL). It is defined on the **projected area** of the bearing.
> 4. **Wrong Saybolt Formula (–1 mark):** Students often use the Redwood or Engler formula for a Saybolt time. Match the viscometer name to the correct equation.
> 5. **Forgetting the +0.7 in Reynolds Equation (–1 mark):** The Reynolds form is $\log\log(\nu + 0.7)$, not $\log\log\nu$. This offset is empirical to handle low-viscosity fluids.
> 6. **Sommerfeld Eccentricity Limit (–2 marks):** $\varepsilon \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$ has a maximum of 0.5 at $\varepsilon = 0.707$. If your algebra gives a value > 0.5, the long-bearing assumption is invalid; use Raimondi-Boyd charts.
> 7. **Mixed-up Conversion Factors (–1 mark):** 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s ; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s ; 1 St = 100 cSt ; 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Sliding contact bearing** = journal + bush + lubricant film
- [x] **Lubricant functions:** friction reduction, wear control, heat removal, flushing, corrosion protection
- [x] **Lubricant types:** liquid oil, grease, solid film (graphite, MoS₂), gas

### Viscosity Essentials
- [x] **Dynamic viscosity μ:** unit Pa·s (= 10 poise = 1000 cP)
- [x] **Kinematic viscosity ν = μ/ρ:** unit m²/s (= 10⁴ stoke = 10⁶ cSt)
- [x] **Viscosity Index (VI):** higher = better (less temp drop)
- [x] **Newton's law:** τ = μ(du/dy)
- [x] **Reynolds viscosity-temperature equation:** $\log\log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B\log T$

### Conversion Equations
- [x] **Saybolt:** $\nu = 0.226t - 195/t$ (cSt, t in s)
- [x] **Redwood No. 1:** $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$
- [x] **Engler:** $\nu = 0.00147t - 3.74/t$

### Petroff's Equation (Concentric Assumption)
- [x] **Friction torque:** $T = 4\pi^{2}\mu N R^{3} L / c$
- [x] **Friction power:** $P_f = 8\pi^{3}\mu N^{2} R^{3} L / c$
- [x] **Friction coefficient:** $f = (2\pi^{2}\mu N/P)(R/c)$
- [x] Limitation: zero load capacity — use only for friction estimation

### Sommerfeld Number & Operating Regimes
- [x] **Definition:** $S = (\mu N/P)(R/c)^{2}$
- [x] **Boundary lubrication:** S < 0.001
- [x] **Mixed lubrication:** 0.001 < S < 0.1
- [x] **Full film hydrodynamic:** 0.1 < S < 10
- [x] **Eccentricity relation:** $S = 1/(6\pi\varepsilon\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^{2}})$ (long bearing)
- [x] **Minimum film thickness:** $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$

### Design Reminders
- [x] **Radial clearance c** = R_bearing − R_journal
- [x] **Bearing pressure P** = W / (L × D) on **projected area**
- [x] **Clearance ratio c/R** typically 0.001 to 0.002
- [x] **Convert all units to SI** before substitution
- [x] **N in rev/sec** for Petroff's formula (not rpm)

### Engineering Applications
- [x] **Automotive engine bearings** — high speed, moderate load, hydrodynamic
- [x] **Steam turbine shafts** — high speed, high precision, ring-oiled hydrodynamic
- [x] **Heavy machinery (rolling mills)** — low speed, very high load, hydrostatic or flooded
- [x] **Electric motors and gearboxes** — grease-lubricated, sealed-for-life plain bearings
- [x] **Aerospace turbomachinery** — synthetic oils, gas bearings, thin-film EHL

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
