topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Journal Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: JOURNAL BEARINGS — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Journal Bearing** is a cylindrical sleeve bearing that supports a rotating shaft (the *journal*) through a thin film of lubricant, where the load is transmitted from the journal to the bearing surface through a pressure developed in the lubricant film due to its hydrodynamic action. Under ideal operating conditions, the journal and bearing surfaces are completely separated by a continuous hydrodynamic oil film — a state known as **Full Fluid Film Lubrication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):** "A journal bearing is a machine element that provides a sliding contact support to a rotating shaft, wherein the load is carried by a thin pressurized lubricant film generated by the relative motion between the journal and the bearing, eliminating direct metal-to-metal contact."

Mathematically, a journal bearing is described by the geometry of two concentric (or eccentric) circles:

$$D = 2r = \text{Journal diameter (m)}$$

$$R = \text{Bearing (sleeve) radius (m)}$$

$$c = R - r = \text{Radial clearance (m)}$$

$$\psi = \frac{c}{r} = \text{Relative clearance (dimensionless)}$$

$$L = \text{Bearing axial length (m)}$$

$$\frac{L}{D} = \text{Length-to-diameter ratio (typically 0.5 to 2.0)}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Skier"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a water skier being pulled behind a speedboat. The skier does NOT touch the water surface — they *plane* on top of the water, supported by a wedge-shaped ramp of water that builds up in front of them. The faster the boat goes, the higher and more supportive the water ramp becomes.
>
> In a **journal bearing**, the rotating journal is the skier, the bearing is the boat's wake-zone, and the **lubricant wedge** is the water ramp. As the journal spins, it drags oil into the converging wedge-shaped gap (where the gap thickness $h$ decreases in the direction of motion). The oil cannot escape fast enough, so the pressure rises — and this pressure supports the load. **No oil pressure → no support → metal-to-metal contact → seizure.**

## 1.3 Classification of Journal Bearings

| Class | Type | Description | Load Support Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **By Lubrication** | Hydrodynamic | Self-generating pressure from motion | Fluid film pressure (Reynolds) |
| | Hydrostatic | External pump supplies oil under pressure | External pressurized supply |
| | Boundary | Asperities contact; soap-like film | Molecular adhesion |
| **By Load Direction** | Radial Journal | Load perpendicular to shaft axis | Most common type |
| | Thrust Collar | Load parallel to shaft axis | End-thrust collars |
| **By Geometry** | Full Journal | Bearing surrounds 360° | $L/D$ governs side leakage |
| | Partial Journal | Bearing surrounds $< 360°$ | Used for oscillating motion |
| **By Film State** | Thick Film ($h_{min} >$ composite roughness) | $h_{min} > 5\,\mu m$ typical | Safe hydrodynamic regime |
| | Thin Film | $h_{min} \approx$ composite roughness | Mixed lubrication risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The course strictly emphasizes **Hydrodynamic (Thick Film) Radial Journal Bearings** operating under *steady-state, isothermal, laminar, incompressible* conditions. Design parameters revolve around the **Sommerfeld Number**, **Petroff's equation**, and the **Reynolds equation** in two dimensions.

## 1.4 Standard Bearing Materials & Lubricants

| Component | Common Material Choices | Key Property Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Bearing Liner (Sleeve)** | Babbitt (Sn/Pb-based), Bronze, Cast Iron, Whitemetal | Embeddability, Conformability, Soft |
| **Journal (Shaft)** | Hardened & Ground Steel (e.g., EN31, 40C8) | Hardness $\geq 60$ HRC, Surface finish $\leq 0.4\,\mu m$ Ra |
| **Lubricant** | Mineral oils (ISO VG 32, 46, 68, 100, 220) | Viscosity $\mu$ at operating temperature |
| **Additives** | EP, anti-wear (ZDDP), anti-foam | Boundary protection during start/stop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Lubricant Viscosity Reference:** At $40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, mineral oil grades typically exhibit: **VG 32 → 32 cP**, **VG 46 → 46 cP**, **VG 68 → 68 cP**, **VG 100 → 100 cP**. At $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, viscosity drops by a factor of approximately 5 to 10×.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4: JOURNAL BEARINGS — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Hydrodynamic Wedge

The journal does NOT sit at the geometric center of the bearing. Under load, it shifts off-center by a distance $e$ called the **eccentricity**. This shift creates a non-uniform oil gap whose thickness varies around the circumference as:

$$h(\theta) = c + e\cos(\theta - \phi) = c\left[1 + \varepsilon\cos(\theta - \phi)\right]$$

where:
- $\theta$ = angular coordinate measured from line of centers
- $\phi$ = attitude angle (angle between load line and minimum film thickness line)
- $\varepsilon = e/c$ = **eccentricity ratio** ($0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The minimum film thickness $h_{min}$ occurs where the gap is narrowest, on the line of centers opposite the load direction.
> $$h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$$
> $$h_{max} = c(1 + \varepsilon)$$

## 2.2 Petroff's Equation — Friction in a Concentric (Unloaded) Bearing

Petroff assumed a concentric journal ($\varepsilon = 0$) with simple shear Couette flow (no pressure gradient), and derived the friction torque and friction coefficient for a lightly-loaded, high-speed bearing.

**Friction Torque (Petroff):**

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$

**Friction Coefficient (Petroff):**

$$f = \frac{T_f}{W r} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

**Heat Generated (Petroff):**

$$H_g = T_f \cdot \omega = 4\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L / c$$

> [!NOTE]
> Petroff's equation is used to estimate **frictional power loss** in lightly loaded bearings (e.g., electric motor shafts) and to compute the **viscous heat generation** that must be dissipated by the lubricant/oil cooler.

## 2.3 The Reynolds Equation in 2D

For a journal bearing, the 2D Reynolds equation in cylindrical coordinates (neglecting inertia, body forces, and side flow in the axial direction $x$) reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

For an **infinitely long bearing** (no side leakage, $L \to \infty$), this further simplifies to the 1D form:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = 6\mu U \frac{h - h^*}{h^3}$$

where $h^*$ is the constant of integration (the film thickness at which $dp/dx = 0$). Solving and integrating gives the classic Sommerfeld pressure distribution.

## 2.4 The Sommerfeld Number

The **Sommerfeld Number** $S$ is the single most important dimensionless group in hydrodynamic bearing design. It couples the operating variables (load, speed, viscosity) with the geometry (clearance, radius).

$$\boxed{\,S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}\,}$$

where:
- $r/c$ = inverse relative clearance
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $N$ = journal speed (rev/s or rev/min — consistency required)
- $P$ = bearing pressure ($= W / (2rL)$, in Pa)

**Equivalent forms:**

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu \omega}{P} \quad \text{where } \omega = 2\pi N$$

**Range for safe hydrodynamic operation:** $0.001 \le S \le 0.1$ (typical); bearings outside this range are either overloaded (S too small) or underloaded (S too large → thin film, risk of metal contact).

## 2.5 Load Capacity from Sommerfeld Number

For a long bearing ($\pi \le L/D \le 2\pi$, i.e., $L/D \ge 1$ approx), the dimensionless load is related to Sommerfeld number by:

$$\frac{W}{2 r L P} = \pi S \quad \text{(Short, infinitely-long approximation)}$$

More refined (from Sommerfeld-von Mises charts):

$$\frac{W}{2 r L P} = f(\varepsilon, L/D, S)$$

For the **infinitely long bearing** limit, the load is given by the closed-form:

$$W = \frac{4\mu \omega r^3 L}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\left(2 + \varepsilon^2\right)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}$$

## 2.6 Coefficient of Friction (Sommerfeld-Based)

The dimensionless friction torque (Sommerfeld) is:

$$\frac{T_f}{\mu \omega r^3 L / c} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\left(2 + \varepsilon^2\right)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}} \cdot \left(\frac{L}{D}\right) + \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1 + \frac{3}{2}\varepsilon^2\right) \cdot \frac{1}{L/D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first term represents the **load-carrying friction** (due to film pressure). The second term represents the **viscous shear friction** (Petroff-type). For long bearings, the second term dominates. For short bearings, the first term dominates.

The friction coefficient:

$$f = \frac{T_f}{W r} = \frac{r}{c} \cdot 2\pi^2 S \cdot \left(\frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{1}{K_2} + \frac{K_1}{L/D}\right)$$

where $K_1(\varepsilon)$ and $K_2(\varepsilon)$ are tabulated functions.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** formula required to solve Part A and Part B journal bearing problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize and internalize this sheet.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = (r/c)^2 \cdot \mu N / P$ | Sommerfeld Number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $f = 2\pi^2 (\mu N / P) (r/c) = 2\pi^2 S \cdot c/r$ | Friction coefficient (Petroff / long-bearing) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_f = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L / c$ | Petroff friction torque | N·m |
| 4 | $H_g = 4\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L / c$ | Heat generated | W (J/s) |
| 5 | $H_d = h_{conv} A_s (T_{oil} - T_{amb})$ | Heat dissipated (convection) | W |
| 6 | $W = 2\pi \mu \omega L (r/c)^2 S r^2 / \pi$ | Load capacity (long bearing) | N |
| 7 | $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 8 | $h_0 = c(1 + \varepsilon)$ | Maximum film thickness | m |
| 9 | $Q = r\omega c L \varepsilon / 2$ | Side-flow oil rate (long bearing) | m³/s |
| 10 | $\phi = $ attitude angle | Angle between load line and min-film line | rad/deg |
| 11 | $P = W / (2rL) = W / (DL)$ | Bearing pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| 12 | $\eta_{min} = h_{min} / (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)$ | Specific film thickness | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical units consistency check:** If $\mu$ is in **Pa·s** (SI), then $N$ MUST be in **rev/s** (not rpm). If $N$ is in rpm, convert: $N_{rps} = N_{rpm} / 60$. Similarly, $P$ must be in Pa = N/m², not bar or MPa. **Common KTU mistake: mixing rpm with Pa·s and getting $S$ off by a factor of 60!**

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Turbo-machinery (steam/gas turbines):** High-speed journal bearings in turbogenerators operate at $S \sim 0.01$ to $0.05$ with $L/D \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$. The hydrodynamic film supports rotors weighing several tonnes.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Crankshaft main bearings and connecting-rod big-end bearings are journal bearings operating under cyclic, dynamically-loaded conditions. Modern engines use hydrodynamic bearings with $\varepsilon \approx 0.6$ to $0.85$.
- **Electric Motors & Pumps:** Petroff's equation predicts viscous losses in lightly loaded high-speed shafts.
- **Hydroelectric Generators:** Massive thrust + journal bearings support vertical-axis runners under combined axial (weight of rotor) and radial (guide bearing) loading.
- **Aerospace Turbopumps:** Cryogenic fluid-lubricated journal bearings (e.g., liquid oxygen, liquid hydrogen) require precise Sommerfeld analysis to ensure sub-millimeter film gaps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 4: JOURNAL BEARINGS — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Petroff's Equation for a Concentric Journal

### Step 1 — Geometry & Assumptions

Consider a concentric journal of radius $r$ rotating inside a bearing of radius $R = r + c$ with constant angular velocity $\omega$. The gap is uniform, equal to $c$ everywhere. A Newtonian lubricant of viscosity $\mu$ fills the gap.

### Step 2 — Velocity Profile (Pure Couette Flow)

With no pressure gradient (concentric, no load), the Navier-Stokes equation in the thin gap reduces to a linear shear:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Integrating from the stationary bearing surface (at $y = 0$, where $u = 0$) to the moving journal surface (at $y = c$, where $u = U = r\omega$):

$$\int_0^{U} du = \frac{1}{\mu}\int_0^c \tau\, dy \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{c} = \frac{r\omega}{c}$$

Therefore the velocity profile is linear:

$$u(y) = \frac{U \cdot y}{c} = \frac{r\omega\, y}{c}$$

### Step 3 — Shear Stress on the Journal

$$\tau = \mu \frac{r\omega}{c}$$

### Step 4 — Friction Force on the Journal Surface

The shear acts tangentially on the journal of projected area $2rL$ (the cylindrical surface area divided by radius to get force):

$$F_f = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{2\pi \mu r^2 \omega L}{c}$$

### Step 5 — Friction Torque

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{2\pi \mu r^3 \omega L}{c}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N$ (N in rev/s)

$$T_f = 2\pi \mu r^3 (2\pi N) L / c = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$

**This is the Petroff friction torque.** ✓

### Step 7 — Friction Coefficient

In a concentric (unloaded) bearing, Petroff's analysis gives no load capacity (the pressure is symmetric and integrates to zero). However, formally defining $f = T_f / (Wr)$ with the assumption that a small load $W = P \cdot 2rL$ is supported somehow:

$$f = \frac{T_f}{W r} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L / c}{P \cdot 2rL \cdot r} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

$$\boxed{\,f = 2\pi^2 S \cdot \frac{c}{r} = 2\pi^2 \left(\frac{\mu N}{P}\right)\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Reynolds Equation for a 2D Thin Film

### Step 1 — Continuity Equation (Incompressible, Steady, Laminar)

For an elemental control volume in the lubricant film:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Navier-Stokes Equations (Thin-Film Simplification)

Neglecting inertia and body forces, the $x$-momentum equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Similarly, the $z$-momentum equation:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Twice Across the Film Thickness

Integrating the first equation with respect to $y$ twice and applying boundary conditions $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ and $u = U$ at $y = h$:

$$u(x,y,z) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(y^2 - yh) + U\frac{y}{h}$$

Similarly for $w(x,y,z)$:

$$w(x,y,z) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}(y^2 - yh)$$

(assuming no sliding in the $z$-direction, $W = 0$).

### Step 4 — Substituting into Continuity and Integrating Across $y$

The volumetric flow rates per unit width are:

$$q_x = \int_0^h u\, dy = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}$$

$$q_z = \int_0^h w\, dy = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}$$

Applying continuity to the mass flow in the $x$-$z$ plane:

$$\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Step 5 — The General Reynolds Equation

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\,}$$

This is the **Reynolds equation of hydrodynamic lubrication**, applicable to slider bearings, journal bearings, and thrust bearings alike.

## 3.3 Derivation: Sommerfeld Number from Infinitely-Long Bearing

### Step 1 — Geometry

Wrap the journal into a flat slider of length $\pi r$ (half-circumference) and width $L$. The film thickness varies as:

$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into 1D Reynolds Equation

For infinite length, $\partial p / \partial z = 0$. The 1D form:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = 6\mu U \frac{h - h^*}{h^3}$$

Substituting $x = r\theta$, $dx = r\, d\theta$, $U = r\omega$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{dp}{d\theta} = 6\mu r\omega \frac{c(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta) - h^*}{\left[c(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)\right]^3}$$

### Step 3 — Determine $h^*$

Apply the boundary condition $p(0) = p(\pi) = 0$ (cavitation / atmospheric pressure at film rupture):

$$\int_0^{\pi} \frac{dp}{d\theta}\, d\theta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_0^{\pi} \frac{c(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta) - h^*}{[c(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)]^3}\, d\theta = 0$$

Evaluating the integral yields:

$$h^* = c \cdot \frac{2 + \varepsilon^2}{3}$$

### Step 4 — Pressure Distribution

$$p(\theta) = \frac{6\mu r^2 \omega}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \sin\theta (2 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)^2}$$

The pressure is positive over the converging half (load-carrying side) and negative (sub-atmospheric, leading to cavitation) over the diverging half.

### Step 5 — Load Capacity by Integration

$$W = L \int_0^{\pi} p(\theta) \cos\theta \cdot r\, d\theta - L \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} p(\theta) \cos\theta \cdot r\, d\theta$$

After the integration (cancelling the negative half):

$$W = \frac{4\pi \mu \omega L r^3}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\left(2 + \varepsilon^2\right)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}$$

### Step 6 — Introduce Bearing Pressure $P = W / (2rL)$

$$\frac{W}{2rL P} = S = \frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{2\pi \mu \omega r^2}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\left(2 + \varepsilon^2\right)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu \omega}{P} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon \cdot 2\pi}{\left(2 + \varepsilon^2\right)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu \omega}{P} \cdot F(\varepsilon)\,}$$

where $F(\varepsilon) = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^2}}$ is the dimensionless load function (ranges from 0 at $\varepsilon = 0$ to $\infty$ as $\varepsilon \to 1$).

## 3.4 Derivation: Heat Balance & Thermal Equilibrium

### Step 1 — Heat Generated by Viscous Shear & Pressure Friction

The total heat generated in the bearing combines the shear-friction heat (Petroff term) and the load-friction heat (sommerfeld term):

$$H_g = T_f \cdot \omega \quad \text{[W]}$$

### Step 2 — Heat Dissipated by Convection

For a bearing in still air with surface area $A_s$ and ambient temperature $T_{amb}$:

$$H_d = h_{conv} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{oil} - T_{amb})$$

Typical convection coefficient for oil on a housing: $h_{conv} \approx 10$ to $50\,\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$.

### Step 3 — Thermal Equilibrium

At steady-state, $H_g = H_d$:

$$4\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L / c = h_{conv} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{oil} - T_{amb})$$

Solving for the oil-temperature rise $\Delta T = T_{oil} - T_{amb}$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{c \cdot h_{conv} \cdot A_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is critical for **thermal design** of journal bearings. $\Delta T$ should NOT exceed **20 to 30 K** above ambient for safe mineral oil operation (to prevent oxidation, viscosity breakdown, and seizure). The viscosity $\mu$ used in design must be evaluated at the **mean operating temperature** $T_{oil} = T_{amb} + \Delta T / 2$, which usually requires iterative computation.

## 3.5 Analytical Design Matrix — Decision Tree

| Given | Find | Use This Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $W, N, \mu, P, L/D$ | $r/c$ | $S = (r/c)^2 \mu N / P$ → solve for $c$ |
| $r/c, \varepsilon$ | $h_{min}$ | $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ |
| $h_{min}, \sigma_1, \sigma_2$ | Specific film $\eta_{min}$ | $\eta_{min} = h_{min} / (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) \ge 1$ |
| $r/c, \mu, N, P$ | $f$ (friction coeff.) | $f = 2\pi^2 (\mu N / P)(r/c)$ |
| $f, W, N$ | $T_f$ | $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$ |
| $T_f, N$ | $H_g$ | $H_g = T_f \cdot \omega = 2\pi N T_f$ |
| $H_g, h_{conv}, A_s, T_{amb}$ | $T_{oil}$ | $T_{oil} = T_{amb} + H_g / (h_{conv} A_s)$ |
| $Q_{supply}$ (oil flow) | $T_{oil}$ | $T_{oil} = T_{in} + H_g / (\rho C_p Q)$ |
| $r, c, \omega, L$ | $Q$ (side flow) | $Q = r\omega c L \varepsilon / 2$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 4: JOURNAL BEARINGS — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Shaft Journal<br/>Radius r, Speed N, Load W] --> B[Converging Oil Wedge<br/>h varies from h_max to h_min]
    B --> C[Hydrodynamic Pressure<br/>Field p theta]
    C --> D[Pressure Force Vector<br/>Integrates to W vertically]
    D --> E[Load Carried by<br/>Fluid Film - No Metal Contact]
    E --> F[Bearing Sleeve<br/>Radius R = r + c]
    F --> G[Sump or Oil Bath<br/>Temperature T_oil]
    G --> H[Recirculated Lubricant<br/>via feed holes]
    H --> B
    C --> I[Shear Stress<br/>tau = mu du/dy]
    I --> J[Friction Torque T_f]
    J --> K[Heat Generation H_g = T_f omega]
    K --> L[Convection to Housing<br/>A_s, h_conv]
    L --> M[Steady-State Oil<br/>Temperature T_oil]
    M --> N[Viscosity mu T_oil]
    N --> C

    classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef therm fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315,color:#000
    class A,F input
    class B,C,D,E,I,J phys
    class G,H,K,L,M,N therm
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Design Procedure for a Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Identify Load W and Speed N from application] --> S2[Step 2: Choose material, target eta_min ge 1.5 to 3]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Assume L by D ratio between 0.5 and 2.0]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate bearing pressure P equals W by D L]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Select lubricant, assume T_oil estimate 70 to 80 C]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Read viscosity mu at T_oil]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Assume Sommerfeld S from charts 0.001 to 0.1]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Calculate radial clearance c equals r times sqrt mu N by P S]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute eccentricity ratio epsilon from S and L by D]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Calculate h_min equals c times 1 minus epsilon]
    S10 --> S11{Check eta_min ge target?}
    S11 -- No --> S7
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Step 12: Compute friction f and heat H_g]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Thermal check delta T le 20 to 30 K]
    S13 --> S14{Thermal OK?}
    S14 -- No --> S5
    S14 -- Yes --> S15[Step 14: Finalize dimensions, document, conclude]

    classDef step fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0,color:#fff
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S12,S13 step
    class S11,S14 decision
    class S15 final
```

## 4.3 Geometry and Pressure Field of a Journal Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph geomBearing [Bearing Geometry Cross Section]
        G1[Bearing Inner Surface<br/>Radius R<br/>Stationary]
        G2[Journal Outer Surface<br/>Radius r<br/>Rotating at omega]
        G3[Center of Bearing O]
        G4[Center of Journal O prime<br/>Offset by eccentricity e]
        G5[Load W applied vertically downward]
        G6[Minimum Film Thickness h_min on left side]
        G7[Maximum Film Thickness h_max on right side]
        G8[Attitude Angle phi between O O prime and h_min direction]
    end

    G3 --- G4
    G5 --> G4
    G2 --> G1

    subgraph pressureField [Pressure Distribution around Journal]
        P1[At theta equals 0<br/>h equals h_max<br/>p equals 0 ambient]
        P2[At theta equals phi<br/>h equals h_min<br/>p equals 0 sub atmospheric]
        P3[At theta equals pi minus phi<br/>h equals h_max<br/>p equals 0 sub atmospheric]
        P4[At theta equals pi plus phi to 2 pi minus phi<br/>p equals 0 cavitation zone]
        P5[Pressure Peak<br/>at theta equals phi to pi minus phi<br/>Load Carrying Zone]
    end

    P5 --> W[Load W equals integral of p cos theta dA over carrying zone]
    W --> S[Sommerfeld Number S couples W N mu r c]

    classDef geom fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000

```

## 4.4 Lubrication Regime Stability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Boundary Lubrication<br/>eta less than 1<br/>Direct asperity contact] -->|Increase Speed or Viscosity| R2[Mixed Lubrication<br/>eta 1 to 3<br/>Partial film and asperity contact]
    R2 -->|Increase Speed or Viscosity further| R3[Full Fluid Film Hydrodynamic<br/>eta greater than 3<br/>Complete separation]
    R3 -->|Increase Load or Reduce Viscosity| R2
    R2 -->|Increase Load or Reduce Viscosity| R1
    R1 -->|Loss of Lubricant| R4[Seizure / Failure]
    R3 -->|Excessive Heat| R4
    R3 -->|Loss of Lubricant| R4
    R2 -->|Whirl Instability| R5[Oil Whip / Half Frequency Whirl<br/>Requires Stabilizing Design]
    R5 -->|Uncontrolled| R4

    classDef danger fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    class R2,R5 warn

```

# MODULE 4: JOURNAL BEARINGS — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the term "Sommerfeld number" and state its significance in the design of hydrodynamic journal bearings.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Sommerfeld number** is a dimensionless parameter that groups the operating variables of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It is defined as:

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

where $r/c$ is the inverse relative clearance, $\mu$ is the lubricant dynamic viscosity, $N$ is the journal speed in rev/s, and $P$ is the bearing pressure $W/(2rL)$.

**Significance:** (i) $S$ determines the operating **eccentricity ratio** $\varepsilon$ of the journal, which in turn fixes the **minimum film thickness** $h_{min} = c(1-\varepsilon)$ and hence the safety of the bearing. (ii) $S$ controls the **friction coefficient** $f = 2\pi^2 S (c/r)$ and thus the **heat generated**. (iii) For a given $L/D$ ratio, $S$ uniquely specifies the bearing's performance point on the Sommerfeld chart. (iv) Typical safe range is $0.001 \le S \le 0.1$ for full-film hydrodynamic operation. ✓

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is meant by "attitude angle" in a journal bearing? Sketch the pressure distribution around the journal and show the attitude angle on it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **attitude angle** $\phi$ is the angle between the **line of action of the external load** $W$ and the **line of minimum film thickness** $h_{min}$ (joining the bearing center $O$ to the journal center $O'$), measured in the direction of journal rotation.

**Physical meaning:** As the journal rotates and drags oil into the converging wedge, the pressure peak does NOT occur directly below the load line — it is **shifted** ahead of the load line in the direction of rotation. The angle of this shift is the attitude angle.

**Typical values:** $\phi \approx 50°$ to $90°$ for lightly loaded bearings (low $\varepsilon$, high $S$); $\phi \approx 10°$ to $20°$ for heavily loaded bearings (high $\varepsilon$, low $S$).

**Pressure distribution sketch (described in words):**
- A curve starting at $p = 0$ (atmospheric) at the maximum film thickness.
- Rising smoothly to a peak at an angle $\phi$ *ahead* of the load line (in the direction of rotation).
- Falling to zero (sub-atmospheric, leading to cavitation) at the minimum film thickness.
- Remaining at zero over the diverging half of the wedge (cavitation zone).

$\boxed{\phi = \text{attitude angle, between load line and } h_{min}\text{ line}}$

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]

A full journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter: $D = 100\,\mathrm{mm}$
- Bearing length: $L = 100\,\mathrm{mm}$
- Radial clearance: $c = 0.1\,\mathrm{mm}$
- Speed: $N = 1500\,\mathrm{rpm}$
- Lubricant viscosity: $\mu = 0.05\,\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$
- Load on bearing: $W = 25\,\mathrm{kN}$

#### (a) Calculate the Sommerfeld number, the coefficient of friction, and the heat generated. (7 marks)

#### (b) Determine the minimum film thickness and check whether the bearing operates in the hydrodynamic regime. Surface roughness of the journal is $0.4\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ and of the bearing is $0.8\,\mu\mathrm{m}$. (7 marks)

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute the journal radius and bearing pressure.**

$$r = D/2 = 0.1/2 = 0.05\,\mathrm{m}$$

$$P = \frac{W}{D \cdot L} = \frac{25\,000}{0.1 \times 0.1} = 2.5 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{Pa} = 2.5\,\mathrm{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Convert rotational speed to rev/s.**

$$N = 1500 / 60 = 25\,\mathrm{rev/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Sommerfeld number.**

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.05}{0.0001} = 500$$

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P} = (500)^2 \times \frac{0.05 \times 25}{2.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$S = 250\,000 \times \frac{1.25}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 250\,000 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 0.125$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.125}$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of friction (long bearing / Petroff approximation).**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot S \cdot \frac{c}{r} = 2\pi^2 \times 0.125 \times \frac{1}{500}$$

$$f = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.125 \times 0.002 = 0.00493$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.00493}$$

**Step 5: Heat generated.**

$$H_g = f \cdot W \cdot r \cdot \omega = 0.00493 \times 25\,000 \times 0.05 \times 2\pi \times 25$$

$$H_g = 0.00493 \times 25\,000 \times 0.05 \times 157.08$$

$$H_g = 0.00493 \times 196\,350 = 967.6\,\mathrm{W}$$

$$\boxed{H_g \approx 968\,\mathrm{W}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Eccentricity ratio from Sommerfeld number.**

For an infinitely-long bearing, the relationship between $S$ and $\varepsilon$ is:

$$S = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^2}}$$

Letting $K = (r/c)^2 (\mu N / P) = 0.125 / F(\varepsilon)$. Iterating with $S = 0.125$, $L/D = 1.0$, and using Sommerfeld chart interpolation, we get $\varepsilon \approx 0.55$.

**Step 2: Minimum film thickness.**

$$h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 0.0001 \times (1 - 0.55) = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m} = 45\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$

**Step 3: Composite surface roughness.**

$$\sigma_{composite} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (0.8)^2}\,\mu\mathrm{m} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.64} = \sqrt{0.80} = 0.894\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$

**Step 4: Specific film thickness.**

$$\eta_{min} = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma_{composite}} = \frac{45}{0.894} \approx 50.3$$

**Step 5: Verification of hydrodynamic regime.**

Since $\eta_{min} \gg 1$ (specifically, $\eta_{min} > 3$ to 5 is the safe hydrodynamic criterion), the bearing is **operating well within the full hydrodynamic regime** with no risk of asperity contact. ✓

$$\boxed{\eta_{min} \approx 50.3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Hydrodynamic operation confirmed.}}$$

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper — Alternative Choice]

A hydrodynamic journal bearing is to be designed for a centrifugal pump. The design requirements are:
- Load: $W = 50\,\mathrm{kN}$
- Speed: $N = 3000\,\mathrm{rpm}$
- Bearing length-to-diameter ratio: $L/D = 1.0$
- Lubricant: SAE 30 oil with viscosity $\mu = 0.025\,\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$ at the operating temperature
- Desired eccentricity ratio: $\varepsilon = 0.7$
- Maximum surface roughness: $0.6\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ for both journal and bearing

#### (a) Determine the journal diameter $D$, the radial clearance $c$, and the minimum film thickness $h_{min}$. (7 marks)

#### (b) Calculate the coefficient of friction, the heat generated, and check if the temperature rise is within permissible limits. Take the housing surface area $A_s = 0.5\,\mathrm{m^2}$, convection coefficient $h_{conv} = 25\,\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$, and ambient temperature $T_{amb} = 35°\mathrm{C}$. Permissible temperature rise: $\Delta T_{max} = 30\,\mathrm{K}$. (7 marks)

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Convert speed.**

$$N = 3000 / 60 = 50\,\mathrm{rev/s}$$

**Step 2: Assume a typical relative clearance and use the Sommerfeld relationship.**

For an infinitely-long bearing at $\varepsilon = 0.7$:

$$F(\varepsilon) = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1-\varepsilon^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.7}{(2 + 0.49)\sqrt{0.51}} = \frac{4.398}{2.49 \times 0.7141} = \frac{4.398}{1.778} = 2.474$$

**Step 3: The long-bearing load equation.**

$$S = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot F(\varepsilon)$$

For $\varepsilon = 0.7$, $L/D = 1$, $S \approx 0.04$ (from Sommerfeld charts). Rearranging to find $r/c$:

$$\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{2\pi S P}{\mu N F(\varepsilon)} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.04 \times P}{0.025 \times 50 \times 2.474} = \frac{0.2513 P}{3.0925} = 0.0813 P$$

**Step 4: Assume a trial diameter and iterate.**

Try $D = 0.1\,\mathrm{m}$: $r = 0.05\,\mathrm{m}$, $P = W/(DL) = 50\,000 / (0.1 \times 0.1) = 5\,\mathrm{MPa}$.

$$(r/c)^2 = 0.0813 \times 5 \times 10^6 = 4.064 \times 10^5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r/c = 637.5$$

$$c = 0.05 / 637.5 = 7.84 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m} = 0.0784\,\mathrm{mm}$$

This is reasonable (typical $c \approx 0.05$ to $0.15\,\mathrm{mm}$ for a 100 mm journal).

**Step 5: Verify $L/D$ and compute $h_{min}$.**

$$L/D = 0.1 / 0.1 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 7.84 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3 = 2.35 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m} = 23.5\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$

**Step 6: Verify hydrodynamic regime.**

$$\sigma_{composite} = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.6^2} = 0.849\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$

$$\eta_{min} = h_{min} / \sigma_{composite} = 23.5 / 0.849 = 27.7 \gg 3 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{D = 100\,\mathrm{mm}, \quad c = 78.4\,\mu\mathrm{m}, \quad h_{min} = 23.5\,\mu\mathrm{m}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Coefficient of friction (long bearing form).**

$$f = 2\pi^2 S \cdot \frac{c}{r} = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.04 \times \frac{1}{637.5} = 0.00124$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.00124}$$

**Step 2: Friction torque.**

$$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 0.00124 \times 50\,000 \times 0.05 = 3.10\,\mathrm{N \cdot m}$$

**Step 3: Heat generated.**

$$H_g = T_f \cdot \omega = 3.10 \times 2\pi \times 50 = 3.10 \times 314.16 = 973.9\,\mathrm{W}$$

$$\boxed{H_g \approx 974\,\mathrm{W}}$$

**Step 4: Temperature rise check.**

$$H_d = h_{conv} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{oil} - T_{amb}) = 25 \times 0.5 \times \Delta T = 12.5 \cdot \Delta T$$

Setting $H_g = H_d$:

$$\Delta T = H_g / 12.5 = 974 / 12.5 = 77.9\,\mathrm{K}$$

**Step 5: Comparison with permissible limit.**

Since $\Delta T = 77.9\,\mathrm{K} > \Delta T_{max} = 30\,\mathrm{K}$, the bearing **fails the thermal check**.

**Remedial actions:** (i) Increase oil supply rate $Q$ to carry away more heat (forced circulation), (ii) Add an oil cooler in the circuit, (iii) Use a lubricant with higher viscosity index and better thermal stability, (iv) Increase $L/D$ ratio to spread the load over a larger area, reducing $P$ and $H_g$, (v) Consider a **tilting-pad bearing** for improved thermal stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Journal Bearing Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Catastrophe:** Mixing $N$ in rpm with $\mu$ in Pa·s gives an $S$ value off by a factor of 60. Always convert $N$ to rev/s FIRST. This is the **single most common** mark-loss error.
> 2. **Forgetting $L/D = 1$ Adjustment:** Using pure Petroff's equation ($f = 2\pi^2 S c/r$) for short bearings ($L/D < 1$) over-predicts friction. The full Sommerfeld equation with $K_1$ and $K_2$ factors must be used, or Sommerfeld chart interpolation is required.
> 3. **Iterative Viscosity at Operating Temperature:** $\mu$ depends on $T_{oil}$, which depends on $H_g$, which depends on $\mu$. KTU students must explicitly state this is an **iterative** calculation and either iterate 2-3 times or note the assumption $T_{oil} = 70$ to $80°\mathrm{C}$ at the start.
> 4. **Specific Film Thickness $\eta_{min}$ Check Omission:** Many students compute $h_{min}$ but forget to verify $\eta_{min} = h_{min}/\sigma_{composite} \ge 1$ (or $\ge 3$ for safe design). KTU examiners allocate **at least 2 marks** for this final safety check.
> 5. **Pressure Formula Confusion:** $P$ in the Sommerfeld equation is bearing pressure $W/(DL)$, NOT hydrodynamic peak pressure and NOT inlet oil pressure.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and Pin This Checklist Above Your Desk Before the ESE**

**Core Definitions (Part A Favourites):**
- [ ] Journal bearing = sliding cylindrical support with hydrodynamic oil film
- [ ] Hydrodynamic lubrication = pressure self-generated by motion through a converging wedge
- [ ] Sommerfeld number $S = (r/c)^2 (\mu N / P)$ — dimensionless
- [ ] Eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = e/c$ — 0 (concentric) to 1 (full contact)
- [ ] Minimum film thickness $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$
- [ ] Maximum film thickness $h_{max} = c(1 + \varepsilon)$
- [ ] Attitude angle $\phi$ = angle between load line and $h_{min}$ line
- [ ] Specific film thickness $\eta_{min} = h_{min} / (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2) \ge 1$
- [ ] Petroff's equation (concentric, no-load friction): $f = 2\pi^2 (\mu N / P)(r/c)$
- [ ] Reynolds equation (2D thin film): couples pressure to film geometry

**Mandatory Calculation Steps (Part B Workflow):**
- [ ] Convert all units to SI before computing $S$
- [ ] Compute bearing pressure $P = W/(DL)$
- [ ] Compute $S$ from $S = (r/c)^2 (\mu N / P)$
- [ ] Find $\varepsilon$ from $S$ and $L/D$ (Sommerfeld chart or formula)
- [ ] Compute $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ and $\eta_{min}$
- [ ] Compute $f$, $T_f$, and $H_g$
- [ ] Perform thermal check: $\Delta T = H_g / (h_{conv} A_s)$ or $H_g / (\rho C_p Q)$
- [ ] Iterate on $\mu$ at $T_{oil} = T_{amb} + \Delta T / 2$ if needed

**Critical Numerical Ranges to Memorize:**
- [ ] $L/D$ typical: 0.5 to 2.0
- [ ] $r/c$ typical: 400 to 1000
- [ ] $S$ typical: 0.001 to 0.1
- [ ] $\varepsilon$ typical: 0.3 to 0.85
- [ ] $h_{min}$ target: $\ge 5$ to $10\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ for normal industrial bearings
- [ ] $\eta_{min}$ target: $\ge 1.5$ to 3.0 for full-film hydrodynamic
- [ ] $\Delta T$ typical: $\le 20$ to $30\,\mathrm{K}$
- [ ] Bearing pressure $P$ typical: 0.5 to $5\,\mathrm{MPa}$ (industrial)

**Engineering Judgement Tips:**
- [ ] High load → high $\varepsilon$ → high friction → check thermal limits
- [ ] High speed → low $\varepsilon$ → thin film near line of centers → check $\eta_{min}$
- [ ] Cold start → highest $\mu$ → thickest film → safe
- [ ] Hot running → lowest $\mu$ → thinnest film → risk zone
- [ ] Whirl instability (oil whip) appears at very high speeds in lightly loaded bearings

> **End of Module 4 — Journal Bearings Notes. Best of luck in the KTU 2024 ESE!**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HYDRODYNAMIC THEORY OF LUBRICATION**
> Hydrodynamic theory of lubrication is the branch of tribology that mathematically describes the formation, maintenance, and load-carrying capacity of a **continuous fluid film** between two relatively moving surfaces, where the pressure required to support the external load is generated solely by the **viscous shear of the lubricant** and the **geometry of the converging gap** formed between the surfaces.

The theory was pioneered by **Osborne Reynolds (1886)** through his seminal paper on the bearing of a rotating shaft. The governing partial differential equation, now universally known as the **Reynolds Equation**, forms the mathematical backbone of all hydrodynamic bearing analysis.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Viscosity (μ)** | The internal resistance of a fluid to flow; the proportionality constant between shear stress and velocity gradient. |
| **Boundary Surfaces** | The two surfaces (e.g., journal and bearing) that enclose the lubricant film. |
| **Converging Wedge** | A geometry where the lubricant gap decreases in the direction of relative motion, generating pressure. |
| **Sommerfeld Number (S)** | A dimensionless number characterizing the operating regime of a hydrodynamic bearing. |
| **Eccentricity (e)** | The radial distance between the bearing center and the journal center. |
| **Attitude (Attitude Angle φ)** | The angle between the line of centers and the direction of the applied load. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Log Flume" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **THINK OF A CANOE ENTERING A NARROW RIVER BEND**

Imagine pushing a flat-bottomed canoe (the **journal**) into a gently narrowing channel (the **bearing**). As the canoe moves forward, water rushes into the narrowing gap and, having nowhere else to go, is forced to push back against the canoe's hull. This lateral "push-back" is precisely what we call **fluid pressure**.

In hydrodynamic lubrication:
- The **canoe** = rotating journal
- The **narrowing river channel** = converging clearance space
- The **water** = lubricant (oil)
- The **lateral push** = hydrodynamic pressure that supports the load

If the gap were perfectly parallel (no convergence), no pressure would build up — just as a canoe in a perfectly uniform river encounters no sideways force. This is the essence of **Reynolds' convergence theorem**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FUNDAMENTAL CONSTANTS USED IN HYDRODYNAMIC ANALYSIS**
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** SI units = **N·s/m²** (or Pa·s); CGS units = **poise** (1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s)
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** SI units = **m²/s** = μ/ρ
> - **Absolute Viscosity Range (Machinery Oils):** 0.001 – 1.0 Pa·s
> - **Typical Sommerfeld Number (S):** 10⁻³ to 10⁰ for full-film journal bearings
> - **Clearance Ratio (c/r):** Usually between **0.001 and 0.002** for precision bearings

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Cornerstone

The entire hydrodynamic theory rests upon **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, which states that for a Newtonian fluid, the **shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient** perpendicular to the direction of flow:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- **τ** = Shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- **μ** = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- **du/dy** = Velocity gradient (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY THIS MATTERS:** In a journal bearing, the lubricant film is dragged by the rotating journal (top layer moving at surface velocity U), while the stationary bearing holds the bottom layer at zero velocity. This velocity difference across a microscopic film thickness (h) creates a shear stress that not only drags the lubricant but also generates the pressure needed to support the load.

### 2.2 Assumptions of Classical Hydrodynamic Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE 7 STANDARD ASSUMPTIONS (Reynolds, 1886)**
> 
> 1. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** (viscosity independent of shear rate).
> 2. The flow is **laminar** (Reynolds number of the film flow < 2000).
> 3. **Inertia forces are negligible** compared with viscous forces.
> 4. The fluid film is **thin** compared with the bearing dimensions (h << r).
> 5. **Body forces** (gravity) are neglected.
> 6. The pressure is **constant across the film thickness** (no variation in the z/y direction).
> 7. The lubricant and bearing surfaces are **in continuous contact** — no rupture of the film.

### 2.3 Petroff's Friction Theory — A Starting Point

**Petroff** (1883) developed the simplest form of hydrodynamic analysis, assuming **concentric journal and bearing** with a uniform clearance gap.

For a concentric cylindrical journal rotating at angular velocity ω inside a stationary bushing with radius clearance *c* and length *L*:

$$\text{Coefficient of Friction, } f = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega r}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

A more elegant form:

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{60}{60}$$

Where **N** is in rps. The Petroff equation gives a **lower bound** on friction — actual bearings have friction slightly higher due to load and eccentricity.

### 2.4 Sommerfeld Number — The Master Dimensionless Group

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOMMERFELD NUMBER (S)**
> The Sommerfeld Number is the single most important dimensionless parameter in hydrodynamic bearing design. It encapsulates operating conditions, lubricant properties, and geometry into one dimensionless group.

$$S = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

Where:
- **N** = Journal rotational speed (rps)
- **P** = Bearing pressure (load per unit projected area) = W/(L·D)
- **r/c** = Reciprocal of clearance ratio

### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER EQUATION REFERENCE TABLE — Memorize these for KTU ESE!**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Shear Stress (Newton) | τ = μ (du/dy) | N/m² | Foundation of all viscous flow |
| 2 | Petroff Friction | $f = 2\pi^2 \frac{\mu N}{P} \frac{r}{c}$ | dimensionless | Lower-bound friction estimate |
| 3 | Petroff Power Loss | $\mu_{power} = f \cdot W \cdot v$ | W | Quick thermal check |
| 4 | Reynolds Equation (1D) | $\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6U\frac{dh}{dx}$ | Pa/m | Pressure distribution in film |
| 5 | Sommerfeld Number | $S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Bearing regime identification |
| 6 | Eccentricity Ratio | $\varepsilon = e/c$ | dimensionless | Operating position of journal |
| 7 | Load Capacity | $W = \frac{\mu N L r^3}{c^2} \cdot f(\varepsilon)$ | N | Primary design output |
| 8 | Minimum Film Thickness | $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | m | Lubrication reliability check |
| 9 | Coefficient of Friction (Sommerfeld) | $\frac{r}{c}f = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot 2\pi \left[ \frac{\varepsilon}{\sqrt{(1-\varepsilon^2)} \cdot \tan^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}}} \right]$ | dimensionless | Actual friction in loaded bearing |
| 10 | Heat Dissipation | $\Delta T = \frac{\mu_{power}}{C_p \cdot Q}$ | °C | Thermal balance check |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PIPE-SYMBOL NOTE:** In tables above, the term $(r/c)$ uses a forward slash. Never write absolute values using the vertical bar `|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hydrodynamic theory underpins the design of every high-speed rotating machine:
- **Turbochargers and gas turbines** (operating at 30,000+ rpm)
- **Internal combustion engine crankshafts**
- **Electric motor and generator bearings**
- **Aircraft gas turbine mainshaft bearings**
- **Rolling mill roll-neck bearings**
- **Marine propeller shaft bearings**

> [!NOTE]
> Without the predictive power of Reynolds' equation, engineers would still rely on empirical "trial-and-error" bearing design — a costly and time-consuming practice unsuited to modern high-performance machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION OF REYNOLDS EQUATION (1-Dimensional, Plane Slider)

This derivation is the **single most asked derivation** in KTU ESE. We will perform every step explicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **SETUP — Consider a flat slider block of length L moving at velocity U over a stationary inclined surface. The lubricant film thickness varies linearly from h₁ at inlet to h₂ at exit.**

#### STEP 1 — Coordinate System and Geometry

Let the **x-axis** be along the direction of motion, and the **y-axis** normal to the lower (stationary) surface. The upper surface moves at velocity U, while the lower surface is stationary. The film thickness h is a function of x only: h = h(x).

#### STEP 2 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

The shear stress at any depth *y* within the film is:

$$\tau_{xy} = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

#### STEP 3 — Force Balance on a Fluid Element

Consider an infinitesimal fluid element of dimensions **dx × dy × unit width (z)**. For **steady-state** flow with **no body forces**, Newton's second law (sum of forces = 0 in the x-direction) gives:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}dx\,dy = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}dx\,dy$$

Dividing both sides by dx·dy:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

#### STEP 4 — Integrate Twice with Respect to y

**First Integration:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}y + C_1$$

**Second Integration:**

$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

#### STEP 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At y = 0 (stationary lower surface), u = 0
$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = 0$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At y = h (moving upper surface), u = U
$$U = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}h^2 + C_1 h$$

Solving for C₁:

$$C_1 = \frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}h$$

#### STEP 6 — Substitute Constants Back to Obtain Velocity Profile

$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}y(y - h) + \frac{U}{h}y$$

This is the famous **parabolic velocity profile** of combined Couette-Poiseuille flow in a thin film.

#### STEP 7 — Volume Flow Rate per Unit Width (q)

$$q = \int_0^h u \, dy$$

Compute the integral step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \int_0^h \left[ \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}y(y - h) + \frac{U}{h}y \right] dy \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\int_0^h (y^2 - hy)\,dy + \frac{U}{h}\int_0^h y\,dy \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left[\frac{y^3}{3} - \frac{hy^2}{2}\right]_0^h + \frac{U}{h}\left[\frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^h \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left[\frac{h^3}{3} - \frac{h^3}{2}\right] + \frac{U \cdot h}{2} \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\cdot\frac{h^3}{6} + \frac{Uh}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

#### STEP 8 — Apply Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For incompressible steady flow, the volume flow rate entering the control volume must equal that leaving. Therefore:

$$\frac{dq}{dx} = 0$$

Differentiating the expression for q:

$$\frac{dq}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left[-\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}\right] = 0$$

Since U is a constant (rigid body motion of upper plate):

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{Uh}{2}\right) = \frac{U}{2}\frac{dh}{dx}$$

#### STEP 9 — Final Form of Reynolds Equation (1-D, Plane Slider)

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6U\frac{dh}{dx}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THIS IS THE REYNOLDS EQUATION FOR 1-D PLANE SLIDER BEARING** — It is the foundational equation students are expected to derive in the KTU university examination.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION OF PETROFF'S EQUATION

Petroff's case assumes **concentric journal and bearing** with no load (W = 0). Therefore, pressure is constant: dp/dx = 0.

The velocity profile reduces to pure **Couette (simple shear) flow**:

$$u = \frac{U}{h}y$$

The shear stress at the journal surface (y = h) is:

$$\tau_0 = \mu \left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=h} = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

The **friction force** on the journal (projected area = 2πr·L):

$$F_f = \tau_0 \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{\mu U}{h} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

Since U = 2πrN, and h = c (radial clearance for concentric case):

$$F_f = \frac{\mu (2\pi r N)}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2}{c}$$

The **load** in Petroff's case is the bearing pressure P times the projected area:

$$W = P \cdot (2rL)$$

Therefore the **coefficient of friction**:

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N L r^2 / c}{2r L P} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{c \cdot P}
\end{aligned}$$

Rewriting with r/c:

$$\boxed{f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION OF SOMMERFELD NUMBER AND ITS RELATIONSHIP TO ECCENTRICITY

For a journal bearing, the dimensionless form of the Reynolds equation in polar coordinates leads to an integral relationship between load capacity and eccentricity. The result (after careful integration — Shigley/V.B. Bhandari treatment) is:

$$\frac{W c^2}{2 \mu N L r^3} = \pi \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}$$

A more rigorous form, valid for **lightly loaded bearings**:

$$S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

Comparing with the load equation, the **Sommerfeld number** is directly proportional to the eccentricity ratio function:

$$S = \pi \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \frac{r}{c} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{load term}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KEY INSIGHT:** A **smaller Sommerfeld number** → **higher eccentricity** → thinner minimum film thickness → bearing operates closer to failure.

### 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — WHEN TO USE WHAT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PROBLEM-SOLVING DECISION MATRIX**

| Scenario Given | Governing Equation | Required Output |
|----------------|--------------------|-----------------| 
| Concentric journal, no load | Petroff's equation | Friction coefficient, Power loss |
| Eccentric journal, load W given | Sommerfeld relation | Eccentricity ratio, attitude angle |
| Film thickness at angle θ | h = c(1 + ε cos θ) | Minimum film thickness h₀ |
| Power loss given | μ = power loss / friction work | Viscosity requirement |
| Heat balance | ΔT = Q·η / (ρ·C_p·q) | Oil outlet temperature |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 — 3 Marks]**
> **Define hydrodynamic lubrication and explain how pressure is developed in a converging film.**

**Model Answer:**
> Hydrodynamic lubrication is a regime of lubrication in which the load-carrying capacity between two relatively moving surfaces is provided entirely by the pressure generated within a viscous fluid film due to its motion and the geometry of the converging gap. Pressure is developed when a viscous lubricant is forced into a converging wedge between the surfaces; the fluid cannot escape laterally and is therefore compressed, creating a pressure distribution that supports the external load. This is governed by the Reynolds equation: $\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6U\frac{dh}{dx}$. A converging gap (dh/dx < 0 in the direction of motion for a slider moving into a narrowing space) produces a positive pressure gradient. **Maximum pressure occurs at the location where the gap stops converging**, after which pressure decays in the diverging region.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — 3 Marks]**
> **State Newton's law of viscosity and give its significance in hydrodynamic lubrication.**

**Model Answer:**
> Newton's law of viscosity states that for a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient across the fluid: **τ = μ (du/dy)**. Here μ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), and du/dy is the rate of change of velocity in the direction perpendicular to flow. **Significance in hydrodynamic lubrication:** This law is the foundation of all hydrodynamic theory. It establishes that (i) the viscous drag of the lubricant creates the shear stresses that oppose relative motion, generating friction and heat, and (ii) when combined with the continuity equation, it leads to the Reynolds equation which predicts pressure build-up in a converging film. Without Newton's law, mathematical modeling of journal bearings, thrust bearings, and gears would not be possible.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — 7 Marks]**
> Derive Reynolds equation for a one-dimensional plane slider bearing starting from Newton's law of viscosity. State all the assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Newtonian fluid, constant viscosity μ.
2. Steady, laminar flow.
3. Thin film (h << L, no variation in z).
4. Inertia and body forces neglected.
5. No slip at boundaries.
6. Pressure constant across film thickness.

**Derivation:**

Consider a flat slider of length L, inclined upper surface moving at velocity U. The lower surface is stationary. Film thickness is h(x).

By Newton's law of viscosity:
$$\tau_{xy} = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Navier-Stokes (x-momentum, simplified):
$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Integrating twice w.r.t. y:
$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

Boundary conditions: u(0) = 0 → C₂ = 0; u(h) = U → C₁ = U/h − (1/2μ)(∂p/∂x)h

Therefore:
$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}y(y-h) + \frac{U}{h}y$$

Volume flow rate per unit width:
$$q = \int_0^h u\,dy = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}$$

Applying continuity (dq/dx = 0):
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6U\frac{dh}{dx}$$

This is the **Reynolds equation** for a 1-D plane slider.

---

> **Q3(b). [7 Marks]**
> A 60 mm diameter journal bearing runs at 1500 rpm. The radial clearance is 0.06 mm and the length of the bearing is 60 mm. The lubricant has a viscosity of 0.020 kg/(m·s). Calculate the Petroff's coefficient of friction and the power loss if the bearing load is 12 kN.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter D = 60 mm → r = 0.030 m
- Speed N = 1500 rpm = 25 rps
- Clearance c = 0.06 mm = 0.00006 m
- Length L = 60 mm = 0.060 m
- μ = 0.020 Pa·s
- Load W = 12 kN = 12,000 N

**Step 1: Bearing pressure P**

Projected area = 2rL = 2 × 0.030 × 0.060 = 0.0036 m²

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{12{,}000}{0.0036} = 3.333 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Petroff's coefficient of friction**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

$$f = 2 \times (3.1416)^2 \cdot \frac{0.020 \times 25}{3.333 \times 10^6} \cdot \frac{0.030}{0.00006}$$

$$f = 19.739 \times \frac{0.5}{3.333 \times 10^6} \times 500$$

$$f = 19.739 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 500 = 19.739 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{f = 0.00148}$$

**Step 3: Sliding velocity v**

$$v = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.060 \times 25 = 4.712 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Friction force**

$$F_f = f \cdot W = 0.00148 \times 12{,}000 = 17.78 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Power loss**

$$P_{loss} = F_f \cdot v = 17.78 \times 4.712 = 83.78 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} \approx 83.8 \text{ W}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **Q4(a). [7 Marks]**
> Explain Petroff's equation of friction. What are its limitations?

**Model Solution:**

Petroff's equation gives the coefficient of friction for a concentric journal bearing (no load):

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- Assumes perfectly concentric journal and bearing.
- Uniform clearance film of thickness c.
- Pure Couette flow (no pressure-driven flow since no load).
- Shear stress τ = μU/c at the journal surface.
- Friction force = τ × (2πrL); Load = P × (2rL).

**Limitations of Petroff's Equation:**

1. **No-load assumption:** Assumes concentric journal — actual bearings operate with significant eccentricity.
2. **No pressure generation:** Ignores the hydrodynamic pressure that develops in a converging film.
3. **No side leakage:** Assumes infinitely long bearing — actual bearings have axial flow.
4. **Lower-bound estimate:** Predicted friction is lower than actual because it ignores the side flow due to pressure gradients.
5. **No account of journal whipping:** Real bearings have dynamic loads and vibration that change eccentricity.

> The Petroff equation is useful only for very lightly loaded bearings at high speeds (where ε → 0) and serves as a sanity-check for more sophisticated hydrodynamic analyses.

---

> **Q4(b). [7 Marks]**
> A full journal bearing has a diameter of 100 mm, length 100 mm, with a radial clearance of 0.1 mm. It supports a load of 25 kN at 1440 rpm. The viscosity of the oil is 0.030 Pa·s. Calculate: (i) the Sommerfeld number, (ii) the coefficient of friction using Petroff's equation, and (iii) the power loss.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- D = 0.100 m → r = 0.050 m
- L = 0.100 m
- c = 0.1 mm = 0.0001 m
- W = 25,000 N
- N = 1440 rpm = 24 rps
- μ = 0.030 Pa·s

**Step 1: Bearing pressure P**

Projected area = 2 × 0.050 × 0.100 = 0.010 m²

$$P = \frac{25{,}000}{0.010} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Sommerfeld number S**

$$S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{0.030 \times 24}{2.5 \times 10^6} \times \left(\frac{0.050}{0.0001}\right)^2$$

$$S = \frac{0.720}{2.5 \times 10^6} \times (500)^2 = 2.88 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5 \times 10^5$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.072}$$

**Step 3: Petroff's coefficient of friction**

$$f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} = 19.739 \times \frac{0.030 \times 24}{2.5 \times 10^6} \times 500$$

$$f = 19.739 \times 2.88 \times 10^{-7} \times 500 = 19.739 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{f = 0.00284}$$

**Step 4: Sliding velocity**

$$v = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.100 \times 24 = 7.540 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Power loss**

$$P_{loss} = f \cdot W \cdot v = 0.00284 \times 25{,}000 \times 7.540$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} = 535.3 \text{ W}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN KTU ESE**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing N in rpm with N in rps. **Always convert N to rps** by dividing rpm by 60 before applying Petroff's or Sommerfeld's formulas.
>
> 2. **Projected Area vs. Liner Area:** Bearing pressure **P = W/(2rL)** uses the **projected area**, NOT the inner cylindrical surface area 2πrL. Confusing these gives wrong P, wrong S, and wrong friction — cascading errors lose 4-5 marks per question.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Square on (r/c):** In the Sommerfeld number, $(r/c)^2$ is **squared**. Writing $(r/c)$ alone reduces S by a factor of c/r = 1/500, leading to ridiculous answers.
>
> 4. **Petroff is a Lower Bound:** Do NOT use Petroff's equation for a heavily loaded bearing — it will under-predict friction. The examiner will deduct 1-2 marks if you don't state this limitation.
>
> 5. **Direction of Convergence:** In a plane slider, dh/dx is **negative** in the converging zone. A sign error here propagates through the Reynolds equation and gives negative pressures — always check physical intuition.
>
> 6. **Power Loss Formula:** Many students write P_loss = F_f × v² (wrong). The correct formula is **P_loss = F_f × v** = f × W × v.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **HYDRODYNAMIC THEORY — QUICK REVISION BULLETS**
>
> - **Newton's Law:** τ = μ(du/dy) — the foundation of all hydrodynamic analysis.
> - **Petroff's Equation** is for the **concentric (unloaded)** case; gives a **lower bound** on friction.
> - **Reynolds Equation** is the **master PDE** governing pressure build-up in converging films; derived from Navier-Stokes + continuity.
> - **Converging gap** = **positive pressure**; **diverging gap** = **negative pressure** (or sub-atmospheric).
> - **Sommerfeld Number** S = (μN/P)(r/c)² — dimensionless parameter; **smaller S → thinner film → higher risk of metal-to-metal contact**.
> - **Eccentricity ratio** ε = e/c lies between 0 (concentric) and 1 (metal-to-metal contact).
> - **Minimum film thickness** h₀ = c(1 − ε); must exceed **3 × composite surface roughness** (σ) for full-film lubrication.
> - **Real bearings** operate with **ε between 0.6 and 0.95** for optimal load capacity and friction balance.
> - **Power loss** P = f · W · v = f · W · πDN.
> - **Heat balance** is critical: viscosity decreases with temperature → thinner film → potential failure. **Thermal feedback loop!**
> - **Always convert N to rps** (rps = rpm/60) when using Petroff's or Sommerfeld's formulas.
> - **Always use projected area** (2rL) when computing bearing pressure P — NOT 2πrL.
> - **Friction circle (r/c)f** is plotted against ε — used for chart-based solutions in KTU exam.
> - **Cracking the problem-solving mindset:** Identify given quantities → choose the right formula (Petroff vs. Sommerfeld) → convert all units to SI → plug in carefully → sanity check the answer physically.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL EXAM ADVICE FOR KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDENTS**
> - For 14-mark derivation questions, **write each assumption explicitly** — this earns 2 free marks.
> - In numerical problems, always show the **substitution step** — partial credit is awarded for correct formula usage even if the final answer is wrong.
> - Memorize the **Reynolds equation in both Cartesian (1-D slider) and polar (journal) forms**.
> - Practice at least **3 numerical problems on Petroff** and **2 on Sommerfeld** before the ESE.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Bearing Characteristic Number** (also called the **Sommerfeld Number**, $S_o$) is a **dimensionless number** that characterises the operating condition of a **hydrodynamically lubricated journal bearing**. It relates the lubricant viscosity, journal speed, bearing load, and radial clearance into a single design parameter that uniquely determines the **eccentricity ratio ($\epsilon$)** of the journal inside the bearing.

Mathematically, the Sommerfeld Number is expressed as:

$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = absolute (dynamic) viscosity of the lubricant (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $N$ = journal speed in **revolutions per second** (rps)
- $P$ = bearing pressure $= W / (L \cdot D)$ (N/m²)
- $r$ = journal radius (m)
- $c$ = radial clearance (m)

In the engineering units commonly adopted by **V.B. Bhandari (the primary KTU reference)**, the same parameter is written using kinematic–like notation as:

$$C_n = \frac{Z \cdot N_s}{P}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = absolute viscosity in **centipoise (cP)**
- $N_s$ = journal speed in **rev/sec (Hz)**
- $P$ = bearing pressure in **kgf/cm²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Convention:** Use the form $C_n = \dfrac{Z N_s}{P}$ when working in **kgf–cm–s** units (Bhandari convention). Use the form $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ when working in **SI** units. Do **NOT** mix the two.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Surfing Duck Analogy"

Imagine a small rubber duck floating on the surface of a slowly moving river. The duck naturally drifts toward the **inside bank** (where the water is slowest) but never quite touches it — a thin film of water always separates the duck from the bank. The faster the river flows (high $N$) and the thicker the water (high $\mu$), the more strongly the duck is pushed sideways, and the more *centred* it floats. The heavier the duck (high load $W$) and the wider the river (high $c/r$), the closer it sinks to the bank.

The **Bearing Characteristic Number** is simply a *single number* that captures this balance of forces:
- **Numerator** → forces trying to *centre* the journal (viscosity × speed × inverse-clearance²)
- **Denominator** → forces trying to *push the journal off-centre* (load → pressure)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Once you compute $C_n$ or $S_o$, you can look up design charts (Trumpler's or Bhandari's Fig. 13.x) to immediately read off:
> 1. The eccentricity ratio $\epsilon = e/c$
> 2. The minimum film thickness $h_{min} = c(1 - \epsilon)$
> 3. The coefficient of friction $f$ (or friction variable $\mu_{fr} \cdot c/r$)
> 4. The position of the minimum film thickness (angle $\phi$)

This single number, therefore, *completely characterises* the hydrodynamic bearing — hence the name.

## 1.3 Why the Bearing Characteristic Number Matters

| Quantity | What it tells the designer |
|----------|---------------------------|
| $\epsilon$ | How off-centre the journal rides (1 = concentric, 0 = touching) |
| $h_{min}$ | Safety against metal-to-metal contact (must exceed surface roughness) |
| $f$ | Power loss as heat — drives thermal design of bearing |
| $\phi$ | Where the pressure peak lies — important for load direction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing **Sommerfeld Number** ($S_o$, dimensionless) with **Bearing Modulus**. They are related by a factor of $\pi$ and side-leakage corrections for finite bearings. KTU typically uses the Bhandari convention which folds all constants into the empirical design charts.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Development: From Petroff to Sommerfeld

### Step 1 — Petroff's Friction Equation (1883)
Petroff assumed the journal to be **perfectly concentric** ($\epsilon = 0$) with the bearing. He derived the frictional torque as:

$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N^2 L r^3}{c}$$

Coefficient of friction:

$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right) = \frac{2\pi^2}{C_n}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Petroff's Paradox:** The equation predicts that *friction increases linearly with load*. Tower's experiments (1883–1885) showed the opposite — friction *decreased* with load. The discrepancy arises because Petroff ignored the **load-carrying wedge** of lubricant that forms when the journal is eccentric.

### Step 2 — Reynolds Equation (1886)
Osborne Reynolds showed that the **pressure build-up** in the converging wedge of oil is what supports the load. The 2-D Reynolds equation for a journal bearing (neglecting squeeze and side flow in first analysis):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

The film thickness at angle $\theta$ (measured from line of centres):

$$h = c + e\cos\theta = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\theta)$$

### Step 3 — Sommerfeld Solution (1904)
For an **infinitely long bearing** (no side leakage, $\partial p/\partial z = 0$):

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{dh}{dx}$$

Integrating with boundary conditions $p = p_a$ at $h_{max}$ and $h_{min}$:

$$p - p_a = \frac{6\mu U}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\epsilon(\cos\theta - \epsilon\sin\theta - 2\epsilon)}{(1 + \epsilon\cos\theta)^2(1 - \epsilon^2)}$$

> *(simplified form — see Bhandari Section 13.4 for full expression)*

Integrating pressure over the projected area gives the load capacity, which yields the **Sommerfeld Equation**:

$$\frac{W}{L D \mu N_s} = \frac{2(r/c)^2 \epsilon \sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2}}{[2 + \epsilon^2] \cdot \pi}$$

Rearranged:

$$\boxed{\,S_o = \frac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(1 - \epsilon^2)}{2\pi\epsilon\sqrt{1-\epsilon^2}}\,[\text{geometric correction}]\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **finite-length** bearing used in real machines ($l/d = 0.25$ to $2.0$), Sommerfeld's exact solution is *not* in closed form. **Trumpler (1935)** used a semi-empirical correction factor $K(l/d)$ to scale the result, which is what the standard Bhandari design charts tabulate.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units (SI) | Units (Bhandari) |
|---|---------|---------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld Number (SI) | dimensionless | — |
| 2 | $C_n = \dfrac{Z \cdot N_s}{P}$ | Bearing Characteristic Number | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{Z}{9810} \times 9.81 = \dfrac{Z}{10^6}$ when $Z$ in cP | Viscosity conversion | Pa·s | kgf·s/m² |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | Bearing pressure | Pa (N/m²) | kgf/cm² |
| 5 | $h_{min} = c(1 - \epsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m | cm |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{T_f}{W \cdot r}$ | Coefficient of friction | — | — |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi^3 D^3 N_s f W}{4 \times 10^5}$ (Bhandari eq. 13.43) | Heat generated in bearing | — | kcal/s |
| 8 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{C_p \cdot \rho \cdot Q_oil}$ | Temperature rise | — | °C |
| 9 | Petroff: $f = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{C_n}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)$ | Petroff friction (concentric) | — | — |
| 10 | Trumpler correction: $S_o^{finite} = \dfrac{S_o^{\infty}}{K(l/d)}$ | Side-leakage correction | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-character trap in markdown tables:** For absolute value or ratio use `\vert` (e.g., $\mu N_s / P$) — never a raw $\vert$ which breaks table formatting.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The Sommerfeld Number is the **cornerstone of hydrodynamic bearing design** because it allows a designer to:
1. Predict **eccentricity** and therefore **minimum film thickness** — the most important safety parameter.
2. Estimate **friction and power loss** — drives thermal balance and lubricant selection.
3. Compare **different lubricants or operating conditions** on a single, normalised basis.
4. Scale **model test data** to full-size bearings (Reynolds similarity).

It is used in KTU Module 4 in the design of:
- IC engine crankshaft bearings
- Turbine and gearbox support bearings
- Rolling-mill roll-neck bearings
- Turbocharger floating-ring bearings
