For bearings advertised to take "heavy thrust" (like "max type" 6 bearings) $f$ is higher. 3. **Effect on misalignment:** - Higher $f$ → ball can slide axially in the groove → bearing can accommodate small angular misalignment between shaft and housing. 4. **Industrial implication:** - SKF, FAG, NSK publish conformity ratio and optimized groove profiles (e.g., "logarithmic profile") to maximize both life and load. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students often confuse conformity with the **Poisson's ratio** (also $f$ in some texts). KTU uses $f = r/D_w$. State the definition clearly in the first sentence. > 2. Many write only the formula but do not explain the **trade-off** — which is the heart of the 7-mark answer. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Classification of Rolling Contact Bearings"** - ✅ Rolling bearings convert sliding friction to rolling friction; they are **catalogue-selected** in the KTU curriculum. - ✅ **Two major classes by rolling element:** Ball (point contact, $p = 3$) and Roller (line contact, $p = 10/3$). - ✅ **Three classes by load direction:** Radial only, Thrust only, Combined. - ✅ **Type code 6 = Deep Groove Ball**, 7 = Angular Contact, 3 = Tapered Roller, 1/2 = Self-aligning, 8 = Cylindrical Roller, 5 = Thrust Ball. - ✅ **Conformity ratio** $f = r / D_w \approx 0.52$–$0.58$ for deep groove balls — controls trade-off between stress and thrust capacity. - ✅ **ISO bore code:** codes 04–96 → bore = code × 5 mm. - ✅ **Width series** is the **2nd digit**; **diameter series** is the **3rd digit** in the ISO designation (don't swap them). - ✅ **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ — $X$ and $Y$ depend on bearing type and the ratio $F_a / F_r$. - ✅ **Self-aligning bearings** (ball or spherical roller) are the only types that tolerate more than a few minutes of misalignment. - ✅ **Tapered roller bearings** must always be used in **pairs** (or sets) — they are separable and one bearing alone cannot locate the shaft axially. - ✅ **Needle bearings** have length-to-diameter ratio ≥ 4:1 — for limited radial space, high radial load. - ✅ **Hybrid ceramic bearings** (Si₃N₄ balls on steel races) — for very high speed, low friction, electric insulation, and high temperature.

topics.title: Bearing Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bearing Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bearing Life** is the defined number of revolutions (or hours of operation at a constant speed) that a bearing can complete before the first evidence of fatigue develops on the rolling elements (balls or rollers) or on the raceways. Because rolling-element fatigue is fundamentally a **statistical phenomenon**, bearing life is always expressed in probabilistic terms tied to a stated **reliability level**.

The most commonly used metric in KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design is the **L10 Life** (also called the **Rating Life** or **Basic Rating Life**), defined as:

> The number of revolutions (or hours at constant speed) at which **90% of a group of apparently identical bearings** will still be operating without any evidence of fatigue failure (i.e., **10% of the bearings have failed**).

Two reference load ratings govern bearing life calculations:
- **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** — The constant, stationary radial load which a bearing can theoretically endure for a basic rating life of **one million revolutions** with **90% reliability**.
- **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀)** — The maximum load a non-rotating bearing can sustain without permanent plastic deformation exceeding **0.0001 of the ball/raceway contact diameter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The rating life is **always** based on a 90% reliability level unless explicitly stated otherwise. Any other life (say L1, L5, L50) corresponds to a different failure percentage and uses a different reliability factor.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Marathon of the Steel Balls"

Imagine you place **100 identical steel marbles** on a long, rough metal racetrack and ask them to roll continuously. Because each marble hits microscopic surface imperfections slightly differently, the marbles will fail at different times — some quickly, some much later. If you plot the time of failure of all 100 marbles on a graph, you get a **Weibull distribution** curve.

The **L10 life** is simply the time on the x-axis at which the **first 10 marbles (10%)** have cracked. If you want to use the bearing more conservatively (e.g., aircraft application, 99% reliability), you must select a *longer* life point on the same curve, which is why the **reliability factor a₂** is introduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** "Bearing life" is **not** a guarantee that the bearing will fail at exactly that hour — it is a **statistical promise** that 90% of bearings in a batch will last *at least* this long under the given load.

## 1.3 The Physical Constants

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $p$ | Load-life exponent (balls) | **10/9 ≈ 1.111** |
| $p$ | Load-life exponent (rollers) | **3 (i.e., 10/3)** |
| $10^6$ | Reference revolutions in the basic rating | **1 million revolutions** |
| $e$ | Limiting value of $F_a/F_r$ for X, Y selection | Catalogue value |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Load–Life Relationship

The cornerstone of bearing design is the **load–life relationship** derived from the Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue theory. The basic dynamic load rating $C$ is the load that a bearing can carry for $L = 10^6$ revolutions at 90% reliability. The relationship between load and life is:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

where $L_{10}$ is expressed in **millions of revolutions**. The exponent $p$ depends on the bearing type:
- **Ball bearings:** $p = 10/9$
- **Roller bearings:** $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$

If life is required in **hours** rather than revolutions:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot N} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \frac{16666}{N} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \text{ hours}$$

where $N$ is the speed in **rpm**.

## 2.2 The Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)

Bearings are rarely loaded with **pure radial** or **pure thrust** loads. To handle combined loading, manufacturers define the **equivalent dynamic load** $P$ as the constant radial load that, if applied, would give the same life as the actual combined load:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

- $F_r$ = Radial component of actual load (N)
- $F_a$ = Axial (thrust) component of actual load (N)
- $X$ = Radial load factor (depends on $F_a/F_r$ ratio)
- $Y$ = Thrust load factor (depends on $F_a/F_r$ ratio)
- The values of $X$ and $Y$ are read from the bearing catalogue based on the ratio $F_a / C_0$ and the limiting factor $e$.

## 2.3 Modified Bearing Life Equation

For real engineering, the basic equation is modified by correction factors accounting for reliability, material, lubrication, and operating conditions:

$$L_{na} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot a_4 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

| Factor | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application factor | $a_1$ | Shock and vibration beyond normal | **1.0 – 3.0** |
| Reliability factor | $a_2$ | Required reliability $> 90\%$ | **0.21 – 1.0** |
| Material factor | $a_3$ | Improved steel/process | **0.8 – 1.0** |
| Temperature factor | $a_4$ | High operating temperature | **1.0 (at 150°C) – 0.5 (at 250°C)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse logic of $a_1$:** Notice that $a_1$ appears in the **numerator** of the *life* expression. This means a **harsher** application (high $a_1$) actually *increases* the rating — because it forces the designer to specify a stronger bearing. It is a **penalty on selection**, not on life.

## 2.4 Equivalent Dynamic Load for Variable Loading

When the load varies (as in gear drives, presses, automotive axles), we use the **Palmgren–Miner cumulative damage rule**. The equivalent constant load that produces the same fatigue damage is:

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{\sum (P_i^{\,p} \cdot n_i)}{\sum n_i}\right)^{1/p}$$

- $P_i$ = constant load during the $i$-th interval
- $n_i$ = number of revolutions at load $P_i$
- $p$ = load-life exponent (10/9 or 10/3)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Basic rating life in millions of revolutions | millions of revs |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Rating life in hours | hours |
| 3 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic radial load | N |
| 4 | $L_{na} = a_1 a_2 a_3 a_4 \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Modified life equation | millions of revs |
| 5 | $P_{eq} = \left(\dfrac{\sum P_i^{\,p} n_i}{\sum n_i}\right)^{1/p}$ | Equivalent load for variable loading | N |
| 6 | $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$ | Required dynamic load rating | N |
| 7 | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | Equivalent static load | N |
| 8 | $C_0 \geq S_0 \cdot P_0$ | Static load selection with safety factor $S_0$ | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bearing-life equations are used in **every rotating machine** — from tiny dental drills (life in millions of revs) to wind turbine main shafts (life often specified as L10h > 175,000 hours ≈ 20 years continuous). KTU frequently tests the variable-load case because it directly mirrors **automotive, gearbox, and rolling-mill** duty cycles.

## 2.6 Why $p = 10/9$ for Balls and $p = 10/3$ for Rollers?

This arises from Hertzian contact stress theory:
- A **ball-on-raceway** contact produces a **point contact** with a stress field whose volume scales with the **3.33 power** of load. The number of stress cycles to fatigue scales as the **inverse 9th power of shear stress** ⇒ $p = 3.33 \times 3 \approx 10/9$ per revolution.
- A **roller-on-raceway** contact is a **line contact** with a stress field that scales with the **first power** of load. The cycles-to-failure scales as the **inverse 9th power of stress** ⇒ $p = 1 \times 3 = 10/3$ per revolution.

Thus, **roller bearings are far more sensitive to load** than ball bearings — a 10% increase in roller-bearing load reduces life by **~24%**, while for a ball bearing, life drops by only **~11%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Load–Life Equation

### Starting Hypothesis (Lundberg–Palmgren, 1947)

The probability of survival of a single stressed volume element $V$ at depth $z$ under alternating shear stress $\tau_0$ is:

$$\ln \frac{1}{S} \propto \frac{\tau_0^{\,c} \cdot V^{e_1} \cdot N^{e_2}}{z^{e_3}}$$

where:
- $S$ = survival probability
- $N$ = number of stress cycles
- $c, e_1, e_2, e_3$ = empirically determined exponents

For ball bearings, the experimentally determined exponent on $\tau_0$ is **$c = 9$** (this is the famous "9th power rule" of rolling fatigue). The Hertzian contact theory gives a load–stress relationship: $\tau_0 \propto P^{1/3}$ for a point contact.

Substituting and simplifying (the algebra is long, but standard):

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{9/e_2} \cdot \text{constant}$$

Calibrating the constant so that $L = 1$ (i.e., $10^6$ revs) when $P = C$ gives the **clean form**:

$$\boxed{L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p}$$

The exponent $p$ comes from combining the **9th-power stress-life slope** with the **stress–load scaling**:
- Point contact (ball): $\tau \propto P^{1/3}$ ⇒ $p = 9 / 3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} = 10/9$
- Line contact (roller): $\tau \propto P^{1/2}$ (different stress field) ⇒ $p = 10/3$

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU exam**, you do **not** need to reproduce the full Lundberg–Palmgren derivation. You must, however, **state the load–life equation, identify the exponent, and know the basic assumption (90% reliability)**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Basic Life Calculation

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU question):**

A deep-groove ball bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 35{,}000$ N and carries an equivalent dynamic radial load $P = 7{,}000$ N. The shaft runs at $N = 1{,}440$ rpm. Calculate:
1. The rating life in millions of revolutions.
2. The rating life in hours.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bearing type and exponent**

The bearing is a **ball** bearing, therefore $p = 10/9$.

**Step 2 — Apply the load–life equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \left(\frac{35{,}000}{7{,}000}\right)^{10/9} \\
&= (5.0)^{10/9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{10}{9} &= 1.1111 \\
(5.0)^{1.1111} &= e^{1.1111 \cdot \ln(5.0)} = e^{1.1111 \cdot 1.6094} = e^{1.7883} \\
&\approx 5.98 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot N} = \frac{10^6 \times 5.98}{60 \times 1{,}440} \\
&= \frac{5.98 \times 10^6}{86{,}400} \approx 69.2 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** $L_{10} = 5.98 \times 10^6$ revolutions, $L_{10h} \approx 69.2$ hours.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Modified Life with Correction Factors

**Problem:**

A cylindrical roller bearing operates under the following conditions:
- $C = 80{,}000$ N, $P = 12{,}000$ N, $N = 900$ rpm
- Reliability required: $99\%$ ⇒ $a_2 = 0.21$
- Application: heavy shock loads ⇒ $a_1 = 1.5$
- Operating temperature: $180^\circ$C ⇒ $a_4 = 0.90$
- Premium steel ⇒ $a_3 = 1.0$

Calculate the modified life in hours.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the modified life equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{na} &= a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot a_4 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} \\
&= 1.5 \times 0.21 \times 1.0 \times 0.90 \times \left(\frac{80{,}000}{12{,}000}\right)^{10/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the load term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{P} &= 6.6667 \\
\left(6.6667\right)^{10/3} &= e^{(10/3) \cdot \ln(6.6667)} = e^{3.3333 \times 1.8971} = e^{6.3237} \\
&\approx 558.5 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the correction factors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{na} &= 0.2835 \times 558.5 = 158.3 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{nah} &= \frac{158.3 \times 10^6}{60 \times 900} = \frac{158.3 \times 10^6}{54{,}000} \approx 2{,}932 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The combined factor $a_1 a_2 a_3 a_4 = 0.2835$ is much less than 1 because the high reliability demand (99%) drastically shortens life. This is why **aerospace and safety-critical** systems use bearings with $C$ several times higher than the equivalent load.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Variable Loading (Cumulative Damage)

**Problem:**

A tapered roller bearing on a rolling-mill pinion experiences the following duty cycle:

| Interval | Load $P_i$ (kN) | Revs $n_i$ (millions) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25 | 4 |
| 2 | 40 | 8 |
| 3 | 60 | 3 |

Determine the equivalent constant load $P_{eq}$. Take $p = 10/3$ (roller bearing).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the cumulative-damage formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} = \left(\frac{\sum P_i^{\,p} n_i}{\sum n_i}\right)^{1/p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator term-by-term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1^{\,p} n_1 &= (25{,}000)^{10/3} \times 4 \times 10^6 \\
P_2^{\,p} n_2 &= (40{,}000)^{10/3} \times 8 \times 10^6 \\
P_3^{\,p} n_3 &= (60{,}000)^{10/3} \times 3 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation**

First, calculate the cube-roots (so that we raise to the 10th power cleanly):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(25{,}000)^{1/3} &\approx 29.24 \\
(40{,}000)^{1/3} &\approx 34.20 \\
(60{,}000)^{1/3} &\approx 39.15
\end{aligned}
$$

Then raise each to the 10th power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(25{,}000)^{10/3} &= (29.24)^{10} \approx 1.62 \times 10^{14} \\
(40{,}000)^{10/3} &= (34.20)^{10} \approx 7.18 \times 10^{14} \\
(60{,}000)^{10/3} &= (39.15)^{10} \approx 2.30 \times 10^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the weighted terms**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_i^{\,p} n_i &= (1.62 \times 10^{14})(4) + (7.18 \times 10^{14})(8) + (2.30 \times 10^{15})(3) \\
&= 6.48 \times 10^{14} + 5.74 \times 10^{15} + 6.90 \times 10^{15} \\
&= 1.33 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum of revolutions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum n_i &= 4 + 8 + 3 = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $P_{eq}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= \left(\frac{1.33 \times 10^{16}}{15 \times 10^6}\right)^{3/10} \\
&= (8.87 \times 10^8)^{0.3} \\
&= e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(8.87 \times 10^8)} \\
&= e^{0.3 \times 20.604} \\
&= e^{6.181} \\
&\approx 4.84 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 48.4 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The equivalent constant load (48.4 kN) is **between** the maximum (60 kN) and the second-load (40 kN), but is **not** a simple arithmetic mean. Because $p = 10/3$ is large, the *highest* load dominates the damage — this is the famous **"high-load dominance"** of fatigue.

## 3.5 Reverse Problem — Required Dynamic Load Rating

**Problem:**

An electric motor bearing must achieve $L_{10h} = 20{,}000$ hours at $N = 1{,}500$ rpm under $P = 5{,}000$ N radial load. Find the required basic dynamic load rating $C$ (ball bearing).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Invert the life equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert hours to revolutions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \frac{20{,}000 \times 60 \times 1{,}500}{10^6} = 1{,}800 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse load-life relation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 5{,}000 \times (1{,}800)^{1/(10/9)} \\
&= 5{,}000 \times (1{,}800)^{9/10} \\
&= 5{,}000 \times (1{,}800)^{0.9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1{,}800)^{0.9} &= e^{0.9 \cdot \ln(1{,}800)} = e^{0.9 \times 7.4955} = e^{6.7460} \approx 846.3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\approx 5{,}000 \times 846.3 = 4.23 \times 10^6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **huge** required $C$ value — illustrating that long-life specifications (20,000 hours) for moderate loads demand **very large bearings**. Real-world solution: increase the shaft speed, use a roller bearing, or relax the life requirement.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Life to Load

The table below shows the **percentage reduction in L10 life** for a **10% increase in load**, illustrating the $p$ exponent's effect:

| Bearing Type | $p$ | $L_{new} / L_{old}$ | % Life Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ball | $10/9$ | $(1/1.1)^{1.111} = 0.886$ | **−11.4%** |
| Roller | $10/3$ | $(1/1.1)^{3.333} = 0.756$ | **−24.4%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** A common student mistake is to use $p = 3$ for **all** bearings. This underestimates life for ball bearings by a factor of nearly 2 and is a guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bearing Life Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **logical flow** of a bearing-life design problem from input to output. It captures the cascading decisions a designer must make.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Application, Load, Speed, Reliability] --> B{Is Load Combined or Pure Radial?}
    B -- Pure Radial --> C[Set P = Fr]
    B -- Combined --> D[Read X and Y from Catalogue using Fa/Fr ratio]
    D --> E[Compute P = X Fr + Y Fa]
    C --> F{Is Load Constant or Variable?}
    E --> F
    F -- Constant --> G[Compute L10 = C by P power p]
    F -- Variable --> H[Apply Palmgren-Miner Rule to find P_eq]
    H --> G
    G --> I{Is L10 Satisfactory?}
    I -- No --> J[Upsize Bearing: Increase C, repeat]
    I -- Yes --> K{Environmental Adjustments Needed?}
    K -- None --> L[Final L10 and L10h reported]
    K -- Yes --> M[Apply a1, a2, a3, a4 factors]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Output: Selected Bearing, L10h, Safety Factor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each block represents a *decision* or *calculation* node. The arrows show the sequential logic that a KTU examiner expects you to write out in your answer.

## 4.2 Bearing Life Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Start: Define Fr, Fa, N, L10h target] --> T2{Determine Bearing Type}
    T2 -- Ball --> T3a[p = 10/9]
    T2 -- Roller --> T3b[p = 10/3]
    T3a --> T4[Equivalent Load P]
    T3b --> T4
    T4 --> T5{Catalogue Available?}
    T5 -- Yes --> T6[Pick trial C, compute L10]
    T5 -- No --> T7[Compute C from L10, then pick from catalogue]
    T6 --> T8{Modification needed?}
    T7 --> T8
    T8 -- Yes --> T9[Multiply by a1 a2 a3 a4]
    T8 -- No --> T10[Report Final Life]
    T9 --> T10
```

## 4.3 Weibull-Based Reliability Curve (Conceptual)

The reliability of a bearing population follows a **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta \approx 1.5$ for ball bearings and $\beta \approx 1.0$ for roller bearings.

| Reliability $R$ | Weibull $x_u$ | Life Multiplier $(x_u / x_{90})^{\beta}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **90% (L10)** | 1.0 | **1.0** |
| 95% (L5) | 1.43 | ~1.40 |
| 99% (L1) | 2.09 | ~2.96 |
| 99.9% (L0.1) | 2.71 | ~5.65 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Going from 90% to 99% reliability reduces the **load capacity** by a factor of $a_2 = (1/2.96)^{10/9} \approx 0.30$. Designers must select a bearing with $C$ roughly **3× larger** to achieve L1 instead of L10 reliability.

## 4.4 Schematic of Fatigue Failure Location

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Rolling Element] --> R2[Subsurface Shear Stress Max]
    R2 --> R3[Micro-crack Initiation below Surface]
    R3 --> R4[Crack Propagation to Surface]
    R4 --> R5[Spalling / Pitting of Raceway]
    R5 --> R6[Vibration and Noise Increase]
    R6 --> R7[Bearing Failure End of Life]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This explains *why* the Lundberg–Palmgren theory uses **subsurface shear stress**, not the surface contact pressure. The life equation is fundamentally about **fatigue crack initiation in the bulk material** below the hardened layer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "rating life" (L10) of a rolling contact bearing. Why is it not a guaranteed life for every individual bearing?**

**Model Answer:**

The **rating life** (L10 life) of a rolling contact bearing is the number of revolutions (or hours at a constant speed) that **90% of a group of identical bearings** can complete under a given load before the first signs of fatigue failure appear on the rolling elements or raceways. It is a **statistical measure** of performance, based on fatigue testing of large bearing populations.

It is **not a guaranteed life** for each individual bearing because fatigue is a probabilistic phenomenon — the actual life of any single bearing follows a Weibull distribution around the L10 value. Specifically:
- 10% of bearings may fail **before** the L10 life.
- 50% of bearings will typically last several times L10.
- The L10 life is therefore a **90% reliability figure**, not a 100% guarantee.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** Mentioning the Weibull distribution and the 90% reliability assumption fetches the third mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is the load-life exponent $p$ different for ball bearings and roller bearings? State both values.**

**Model Answer:**

The exponent $p$ reflects how the **Hertzian contact stress field** scales with applied load, and how the resulting **shear stress** drives subsurface fatigue.

| Bearing Type | Contact Type | Stress-Load Relation | Exponent $p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ball** | Point | $\tau \propto P^{1/3}$ | **$p = 10/9 \approx 1.111$** |
| **Roller** | Line | $\tau \propto P^{1/2}$ | **$p = 10/3 \approx 3.333$** |

Because roller bearings have a **line contact** with a sharper stress concentration, the life is **more sensitive** to load changes. This is why even small overloads drastically reduce roller-bearing life.

> [!NOTE]
> A complete answer must include both numerical values of $p$ and the physical reasoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 4]`

**A radial ball bearing is to be selected for a shaft running at 1,200 rpm under the following conditions:**
- Radial load: $F_r = 4{,}000$ N
- Axial load: $F_a = 1{,}500$ N
- The shaft is supported by a deep-groove ball bearing series 62
- For this series, the following data is available (from the standard catalogue table):
  - $C = 28{,}100$ N, $C_0 = 16{,}600$ N
  - When $F_a/C_0 = 0.090$: $e = 0.30$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.30$
- Desired rating life: $L_{10h} = 10{,}000$ hours
- Application factor: $a_1 = 1.2$ (light shock)

**Part (a)** [7 marks]: Calculate the equivalent dynamic radial load $P$ and check whether the bearing's basic dynamic load rating is sufficient to deliver the desired life of 10,000 hours.

**Part (b)** [7 marks]: If the actual duty cycle has the load varying as shown below, find the equivalent constant load using the Palmgren–Miner rule. Then find the new life in hours.

| Interval | $F_{r,i}$ (N) | $F_{a,i}$ (N) | Revolutions (millions) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3,000 | 1,000 | 5 |
| 2 | 5,000 | 1,500 | 3 |
| 3 | 7,000 | 2,000 | 2 |

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (Part a)

**Step 1 — Compute $F_a / C_0$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{F_a}{C_0} &= \frac{1{,}500}{16{,}600} \approx 0.0903
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Select $X, Y$ from catalogue**

Since the calculated ratio is essentially equal to the catalogue entry $F_a/C_0 = 0.090$, we use:
- $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.30$

**Step 3 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X F_r + Y F_a = 0.56 \times 4{,}000 + 1.30 \times 1{,}500 \\
&= 2{,}240 + 1{,}950 = 4{,}190 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the rating life in revolutions**

Using $p = 10/9$ for ball bearings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/9} = \left(\frac{28{,}100}{4{,}190}\right)^{10/9} \\
&= (6.706)^{1.1111} \\
&= e^{1.1111 \cdot \ln(6.706)} = e^{1.1111 \times 1.9028} = e^{2.1142} \\
&\approx 8.28 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6 \times L_{10}}{60 \times N} = \frac{10^6 \times 8.28}{60 \times 1{,}200} \\
&= \frac{8.28 \times 10^6}{72{,}000} = 115 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the application factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{na} &= a_1 \times L_{10} = 1.2 \times 115 = 138 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with the desired life**

Desired life: **10,000 hours**. Available life: **138 hours**. The bearing is **massively undersized**.

**Conclusion of Part (a):** The bearing with $C = 28{,}100$ N is **inadequate**; a much larger bearing (or a roller bearing) is required.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (Part b)

**Step 1 — Compute the equivalent load for each interval using $X, Y$ from Part (a)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 0.56 \times 3{,}000 + 1.30 \times 1{,}000 = 1{,}680 + 1{,}300 = 2{,}980 \text{ N} \\
P_2 &= 0.56 \times 5{,}000 + 1.30 \times 1{,}500 = 2{,}800 + 1{,}950 = 4{,}750 \text{ N} \\
P_3 &= 0.56 \times 7{,}000 + 1.30 \times 2{,}000 = 3{,}920 + 2{,}600 = 6{,}520 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Palmgren–Miner formula for ball bearings ($p = 10/9$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= \left(\frac{\sum P_i^{\,p} n_i}{\sum n_i}\right)^{1/p} \\
\sum n_i &= 5 + 3 + 2 = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ revs}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute each $P_i^{10/9}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1^{10/9} &= (2{,}980)^{1.1111} = e^{1.1111 \cdot \ln(2{,}980)} = e^{1.1111 \times 8.0002} = e^{8.8891} \approx 7{,}217 \\
P_2^{10/9} &= (4{,}750)^{1.1111} = e^{1.1111 \cdot \ln(4{,}750)} = e^{1.1111 \times 8.4661} = e^{9.4068} \approx 12{,}180 \\
P_3^{10/9} &= (6{,}520)^{1.1111} = e^{1.1111 \cdot \ln(6{,}520)} = e^{1.1111 \times 8.7821} = e^{9.7579} \approx 17{,}340
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted sum**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum P_i^{\,p} n_i &= 7{,}217 \times 5 + 12{,}180 \times 3 + 17{,}340 \times 2 \text{ (in units of } N^{10/9} \cdot 10^6\text{)} \\
&= 36{,}085 + 36{,}540 + 34{,}680 \\
&= 107{,}305
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\sum n_i$ and take the $1/p$ power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= \left(\frac{107{,}305}{10}\right)^{9/10} = (10{,}730.5)^{0.9} \\
&= e^{0.9 \cdot \ln(10{,}730.5)} = e^{0.9 \times 9.2812} = e^{8.3531} \\
&\approx 4{,}236 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new rating life**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\frac{28{,}100}{4{,}236}\right)^{10/9} = (6.633)^{1.1111} \\
&= e^{1.1111 \times 1.8913} = e^{2.1015} \approx 8.18 \text{ million revs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hours and apply $a_1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{nah} &= \frac{8.18 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1{,}200} \times 1.2 = 113.6 \times 1.2 \approx 136 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion of Part (b):** The variable-load equivalent $P_{eq} = 4{,}236$ N is slightly higher than the constant-load $P = 4{,}190$ N, so the life is correspondingly reduced (from 138 h to 136 h) — the design is still inadequate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A cylindrical roller bearing carries a constant equivalent dynamic load of $P = 12{,}000$ N at a speed of 800 rpm. The following catalogue data is available:**

- $C = 76{,}500$ N, $C_0 = 64{,}000$ N

The application requires a **99% reliability** (use $a_2 = 0.21$), a **moderate shock** ($a_1 = 1.4$), and a **standard material** ($a_3 = 1.0$). The operating temperature is normal ($a_4 = 1.0$).

**Part (a)** [7 marks]: Calculate the **basic rating life** in hours and the **modified rating life** in hours. Comment on the suitability of the bearing.

**Part (b)** [7 marks]: The same bearing is required to operate **continuously for 5 years** (8760 hours per year). Determine the **minimum required $C$** for the modified life criterion to be satisfied. If the existing $C = 76{,}500$ N is not sufficient, what is the **percentage increase in $C$** required?

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (Part a)

**Step 1 — Calculate the basic rating life (in millions of revolutions)**

For a roller bearing, $p = 10/3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\frac{76{,}500}{12{,}000}\right)^{10/3} \\
&= (6.375)^{3.3333} \\
&= e^{3.3333 \cdot \ln(6.375)} = e^{3.3333 \times 1.8526} = e^{6.1754} \\
&\approx 481.2 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6 \times 481.2}{60 \times 800} = \frac{4.812 \times 10^8}{48{,}000} \approx 10{,}025 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the correction factors for modified life**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{na} &= a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot a_4 \cdot L_{10} \\
&= 1.4 \times 0.21 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 10{,}025 \\
&\approx 2{,}947 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The basic L10h of ~10,025 h is a fair design, but the 99% reliability demand shrinks the modified life to ~2,947 h, which is generally too short for industrial equipment. A larger bearing is needed.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (Part b)

**Step 1 — Required modified life**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{req} &= 5 \text{ years} \times 8{,}760 \text{ h/year} = 43{,}800 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to revolutions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10,req} \text{ in revs} &= \frac{43{,}800 \times 60 \times 800}{10^6} = \frac{2.1024 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 2{,}102.4 \text{ million revs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for correction factors**

The modified life equation rearranged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= P \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10,req}}{a_1 a_2 a_3 a_4}\right)^{3/10} \\
&= 12{,}000 \cdot \left(\frac{2{,}102.4}{1.4 \times 0.21 \times 1.0 \times 1.0}\right)^{0.3} \\
&= 12{,}000 \cdot \left(\frac{2{,}102.4}{0.294}\right)^{0.3} \\
&= 12{,}000 \cdot (7{,}150.6)^{0.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(7{,}150.6)^{0.3} &= e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(7{,}150.6)} = e^{0.3 \times 8.8742} = e^{2.6623} \approx 14.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute required $C$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= 12{,}000 \times 14.32 \approx 1.72 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 172{,}000 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage increase**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Increase} &= \frac{172{,}000 - 76{,}500}{76{,}500} \times 100\% \approx 124.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion of Part (b):** The existing bearing's $C$ must be **increased by ~125%** — i.e., the designer must select a bearing with $C \geq 172$ kN, which typically means moving up **two bearing sizes** in the catalogue.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks on this Topic:**
> 1. **Wrong exponent:** Using $p = 3$ for *ball* bearings (should be $p = 10/9$). Result: life overestimated by ~75%.
> 2. **Forgetting the 90% reliability assumption:** If the problem states 99% reliability, the factor $a_2 = 0.21$ is **mandatory** and multiplies *the life*, not the load.
> 3. **Confusing L10 in revolutions vs. hours:** L10 (millions of revs) and L10h (hours) are linked by the formula $L_{10h} = 10^6 L_{10} / (60 N)$. Many students divide by $N$ directly and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Using $X, Y$ in the wrong direction:** $X$ multiplies $F_r$ and $Y$ multiplies $F_a$ — never the reverse.
> 5. **Variable-load error:** Forgetting the $1/p$ power in the Palmgren–Miner formula. The formula is $(\sum P_i^p n_i / \sum n_i)^{1/p}$, not $(\sum P_i n_i / \sum n_i)$.
> 6. **Units of $L_{10}$:** When applying $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, the result is in **millions of revolutions**. Multiply by $10^6$ before converting to hours.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Bearing Life**

- **Rating life (L10)** = life at which **10% of bearings have failed** (90% reliability).
- **Load-life equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, where $C$ = basic dynamic load rating, $P$ = equivalent dynamic load.
- **Exponent $p$:** $\mathbf{10/9}$ for **ball** bearings, $\mathbf{10/3}$ for **roller** bearings.
- **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$, with $X, Y$ from catalogue using $F_a/C_0$.
- **Modified life:** $L_{na} = a_1 a_2 a_3 a_4 (C/P)^p$, with $a_1$ (shock), $a_2$ (reliability), $a_3$ (material), $a_4$ (temperature).
- **Variable load equivalent:** $P_{eq} = \left( \sum P_i^{\,p} n_i / \sum n_i \right)^{1/p}$ (Palmgren–Miner).
- **Reverse selection:** $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$ for required life.
- **Hours conversion:** $L_{10h} = 10^6 L_{10} / (60 N)$, where $N$ is in rpm.
- **Static load check:** $C_0 \geq S_0 P_0$, with $S_0$ = 1.0 (rotating), 1.5 (vibrating), etc.
- **Sensitivity:** A 10% load increase reduces ball-bearing life by ~11% and roller-bearing life by ~24%.
- **High-load dominance:** In the Palmgren–Miner sum, **the highest load contributes the most damage** because of the $p$ exponent.
- **Roller bearings last longer** at the *same* $C$ value because they have higher $C$ for a given size — but their life drops faster with overload.
- **Always state** the basic dynamic load rating $C$ and the bearing series/number in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Load Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Load Capacities in Rolling Element Bearings

In the design of rolling element bearings, the term **Load Capacity** refers to the maximum load that a bearing can sustain without undergoing permanent deformation (static) or without failing due to material fatigue (dynamic). These two capacities form the very foundation of bearing selection as per **ISO 15:2017** and **ISO 281:2007**, which are the global standards adopted universally (and hence implicit in KTU Machine Design evaluations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** The design of rolling contact bearings revolves around TWO fundamental capacities: **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** and **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)**. Confusing these two is the single largest reason students lose marks in KTU Board Examinations.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Standard Terminology)

### 1.2.1 Static Load Capacity (C₀)

The **Static Load Capacity**, denoted as **C₀**, is defined as the load (radial for radial bearings, axial for thrust bearings) that corresponds to a calculated contact stress of:

- **4600 MPa** at the centre of the most heavily loaded rolling element / raceway contact for **ball bearings**.
- **4000 MPa** for **roller bearings** (due to line contact).

At this stress, a permanent deformation of approximately **0.0001 × D (ball diameter)** occurs at the contact point. Beyond C₀, the deformation is permanent and the bearing's running accuracy is permanently impaired.

### 1.2.2 Dynamic Load Capacity (C)

The **Basic Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** — also called the **Basic Dynamic Load Rating** — is defined, per **ISO 281:2007**, as:

> *"The constant radial (or axial, for thrust bearings) load which a group of apparently identical bearings can theoretically endure for a rating life of 1 million revolutions."*

It is a fatigue-based capacity and is used in conjunction with the **Basic Rating Life (L₁₀)** equation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **small steel stool** placed on a soft rubber mat.

> **Static Capacity (C₀) Analogy:** Think of placing an **elephant** on the stool and keeping it stationary. As long as the elephant stays still, the stool might survive (it depends on the deformation, not fatigue). The deformation in the rubber mat is permanent plastic deformation — this is **static failure**.

> **Dynamic Capacity (C) Analogy:** Now imagine making the elephant **dance** on the stool continuously for hours. Even though no single step exceeds the stool's strength, the **repeated cyclic loading** (fatigue) will cause the wooden legs to develop cracks and eventually snap. This is **dynamic (fatigue) failure**.

| Aspect | Static Loading | Dynamic Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Loading Mode | Constant, no relative motion | Continuous rotation, cyclic |
| Failure Type | Permanent Brinelling / Plastic Indentation | Material Fatigue (Spalling / Pitting) |
| Governing Equation | C₀ (load-stress) | C (rating life equation) |
| Analogy | Elephant sitting on stool | Elephant dancing on stool |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards (Bold-Flagged)

The following constants/values are **universally fixed** and must be memorized by every KTU student:

- **Contact stress for ball bearings (static):** σ_c = **4600 MPa**
- **Contact stress for roller bearings (static):** σ_c = **4000 MPa**
- **Reference rating life:** L₁₀ = **1 million revolutions (10⁶ revs)**
- **Weibull exponent (slope):** a₁ = 1.0 (for ball), 1.43 (for roller) — *used in modified life factor e_c*
- **Factor of safety for static load (S₀):** ranges from **0.5 to 3.0** based on application severity (smooth → 0.5; high shock → 3.0).

> [!NOTE]
> The static and dynamic load capacities are **NOT** the same numerical value. A typical deep-groove ball bearing 6205 has C = 14.0 kN and C₀ = 6.95 kN — the static capacity is always LOWER than the dynamic capacity, because fatigue life cycles distribute the load more gracefully than a single static indentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Two Capacities

A machine designer must answer TWO distinct questions before selecting a bearing:

1. **At Rest / Slowly Rotating (n < 10 rpm):** Can the bearing withstand the maximum applied load without yielding / permanent indentations? → **Use C₀ (Static Capacity)**
2. **Continuously Rotating (n ≥ 10 rpm):** How many hours/revolutions will the bearing run before fatigue failure? → **Use C (Dynamic Capacity)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When a problem states "machine operates at 1440 rpm for 8 hours/day with light shock", the examiner expects a **dynamic rating life** problem. When the problem says "a slowly oscillating joint subjected to a steady 25 kN load", the examiner expects a **static load check** using C₀.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Dynamic Load Capacity (C)

The dynamic load capacity C is derived from **Lundberg–Palmgren fatigue theory** (1947), which states that subsurface shear stress drives fatigue spalling. The key result, after integration of the Hertzian contact stress field over the volume, yields:

$$
C = f_c \cdot (i \cdot \cos\alpha)^{0.7} \cdot Z^{2/3} \cdot D_w^{1.8}
$$

where:
- **f_c** = geometry/series factor (depends on type: deep-groove, cylindrical, etc.)
- **i** = number of rows of rolling elements
- **α** = nominal contact angle
- **Z** = number of rolling elements per row
- **D_w** = diameter of the rolling element

This equation is **rarely** used by students in exams; rather, **C is read directly from manufacturer's catalogue** (SKF, NTN, FAG) for a chosen bore diameter.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Basic rating life (millions of revs), p = 3 (ball), p = 10/3 (roller) | ISO 281:2007 |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Rating life in hours | ISO 281:2007 |
| 3 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load (radial + axial combination) | ISO 15:2017 |
| 4 | $C = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic load capacity (design eq.) | ISO 281:2007 |
| 5 | $C_0 = i \cdot Z \cdot D_w^2 \cdot \cos\alpha$ | Static load capacity (theoretical, used for design verification) | ISO 15:2017 / Hertz contact |
| 6 | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent static load | ISO 15:2017 |
| 7 | $S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0}$ | Static safety factor (must exceed required S₀ from tables) | Design verification |
| 8 | $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$ | Modified rating life (with reliability and material factors) | ISO 281:2007 (Annex) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **rotating machinery** (motors, gearboxes, pumps, wind turbines, jet engines, automotive wheel hubs), the dynamic load capacity C dictates the **L10 life** in service. Conversely, in **slow oscillating joints** — such as crane slewing rings, robotic arm pivots, low-speed hydraulic actuators, and excavator swing bearings — the static load capacity C₀ is the **governing design criterion** because fatigue is negligible.

KTU frequently sets problems involving:
- **Centrifugal pumps** (high n, dynamic) → C
- **Helicopter rotor swashplates** (low oscillating n, high impact) → C₀
- **Conveyor idlers** (medium n, light load) → C
- **Hydraulic cylinder rod ends** (very low n) → C₀

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Static Load Capacity (C₀) from Hertzian Contact

We start with the **Hertzian contact stress** between a sphere of diameter D_w and a raceway of radius R. The maximum contact pressure at the centre of the contact ellipse for a ball on a curved raceway is:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a b}
$$

For ball-on-raceway, the contact ellipse semi-axes a and b are functions of the load Q per ball. After substituting Hertz's geometry relations and integrating over the maximum permissible Hertz stress (**σ_max = 4600 MPa** for balls), we solve for Q (load per ball):

$$
Q_{max} = K \cdot D_w^2 \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

where K is a geometry constant that absorbs elastic modulus and Poisson's ratio. The **total static load capacity** for the entire bearing (assuming load is shared equally among all i·Z rolling elements under pure radial load):

$$
C_0 = i \cdot Z \cdot Q_{max} = i \cdot Z \cdot K \cdot D_w^2 \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

**For the typical case (single row, i = 1, Z balls, contact angle α):**

$$
C_0 = K_0 \cdot Z \cdot D_w^2 \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

> **Interpretation:** Static load capacity scales with the **square of the ball diameter** — doubling D_w quadruples C₀. This is why designers prefer slightly larger rolling elements when loads are high.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dynamic Load Capacity from Fatigue Theory (Lundberg–Palmgren)

The Lundberg–Palmgren (1947) fatigue hypothesis states that the **subsurface orthogonal shear stress** is the primary driver of fatigue spalling. For a single rolling element under load Q, the probability of survival is:

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{1}{S}\right) \sim \tau_0^e \cdot N^e \cdot V
$$

where:
- $\tau_0$ = maximum reversed subsurface shear stress
- N = number of stress cycles
- V = stressed volume
- e = exponent (≈ 31/3 for ball, ≈ 10 for roller — later generalized)

For a bearing, integrating the shear-stress field and equating 90% reliability at N = 1×10⁶ revolutions gives the **basic dynamic load capacity**:

$$
C = f_c \cdot (i \cos\alpha)^{0.7} \cdot Z^{2/3} \cdot D_w^{1.8}
$$

This is the equation used by manufacturers to **build the catalogue tables**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Basic Rating Life Equation (L₁₀)

We start from the dynamic load capacity definition: **C is the load that gives 1 million revolutions of L10 life.** For a different applied load P, the cycles-to-failure scale by a power law (since fatigue is a power-law process):

$$
\dfrac{L_{10}}{10^6} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

where L₁₀ is in **millions of revolutions**, and:

- **p = 3** for ball bearings
- **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333** for roller bearings

**Conversion to hours:**

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

**Solving for required C (design form):**

$$
C = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Illustration (typical KTU 2-mark intermediate step):**
>
> A 6308 deep-groove ball bearing carries equivalent dynamic load P = 5 kN at 1500 rpm. The catalogue gives C = 33.2 kN. Find L₁₀h.
>
> $$
> L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{33.2}{5}\right)^3 = (6.64)^3 = 292.86 \text{ million revs}
> $$
>
> $$
> L_{10h} = \dfrac{292.86 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500} = \dfrac{292.86 \times 10^6}{90000} = 3254 \text{ hours}
> $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)

A real bearing often sees **both radial and axial loads simultaneously**. ISO 15:2017 provides:

$$
P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a
$$

where X and Y depend on the bearing type, the ratio F_a/F_r, and the design factor e.

**Step-by-step procedure (Standard KTU approach):**

1. Calculate the load ratio $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r}$.
2. Compare with e (from catalogue, depends on bearing type and series).
3. If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$: then X = 1, Y = 0, so **P = F_r** (purely radial effect dominates).
4. If $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$: then use the **catalogue X, Y** values; P = X·F_r + Y·F_a.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Static Safety Factor (S₀)

$$
S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0}
$$

where P₀ is the **equivalent static load**:

$$
P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a
$$

For **deep-groove ball bearings**, ISO 15 gives:
- X₀ = 0.6
- Y₀ = 0.5

**Check:** S₀ computed must be **≥ required S₀** from KTU/ISO table based on application severity.

| Application | Required S₀ |
|---|---|
| Smooth, no vibration, high precision | 0.5 |
| Normal operation | 1.0 |
| Shock loads | 1.5 – 3.0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Static vs Dynamic Capacity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Given Application Data: Loads, Speed, Hours]) --> q1{Rotational Speed n}
    q1 -- n less than 10 rpm --> staticPath[STATIC LOAD CHECK]
    q1 -- n greater than or equal 10 rpm --> dynamicPath[DYNAMIC LOAD CHECK]
    staticPath --> s1[Compute P0 = X0*Fr + Y0*Fa]
    s1 --> s2[Obtain C0 from Catalogue]
    s2 --> s3[Compute S0 = C0 / P0]
    s3 --> s4{Is S0 greater than Required S0}
    s4 -- Yes --> sSafe[Design Safe for Static]
    s4 -- No --> sFail[Increase Bearing Size or Series]
    dynamicPath --> d1[Compute Fa/Fr Ratio]
    d1 --> d2{Compare Fa/Fr with e}
    d2 -- less than or equal --> d3[P = Fr only]
    d2 -- greater than --> d4[Use Catalogue X, Y values]
    d3 --> d5[Compute P = X*Fr + Y*Fa]
    d4 --> d5
    d5 --> d6[Compute Required C from Design Equation]
    d6 --> d7[Select Bearing with Catalogue C greater than Required]
    d7 --> d8[Compute Actual L10h Life]
    d8 --> dCheck{L10h greater than Required}
    dCheck -- Yes --> dSafe[Design Safe for Dynamic]
    dCheck -- No --> dFailDyn[Select Higher Capacity Bearing]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Relationship between C, C₀, P, P₀

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Loads]
        A1[Radial Load Fr]
        A2[Axial Load Fa]
    end
    INPUT --> EQ1[Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    INPUT --> EQ0[Equivalent Static Load P0]
    EQ1 --> LC[L10 Rating Life Equation]
    LC --> CAT[Required C from Catalogue]
    EQ0 --> ST[S0 Safety Check]
    ST --> CAT0[Required C0 from Catalogue]
    CAT --> SEL[Final Bearing Selection]
    CAT0 --> SEL
    SEL --> VER[Verify both C and C0]
    VER --> OUT[Selected Bearing Number]
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematic: Ball Bearing Contact Geometry (Description)

A ball bearing under load shows **Hertzian contact ellipses** between each rolling element and the inner/outer raceways. Under **static load**:

- Contact ellipse is large but stationary.
- Stress stays at **4600 MPa peak** at the centre.
- All balls carry their share → **C₀ is reached** when the most loaded ball has Q_max.

Under **dynamic load (rotating)**:

- Load zone **rotates with the shaft** → each ball passes through load zone once per revolution.
- Fatigue damage accumulates with cycles → spalling initiates at subsurface location.
- **C is the load at which 10% of a population fails at 10⁶ revolutions.**

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical KTU exam, students are NOT expected to draw complex Hertz contact ellipses. They are expected to draw a **labelled ball-bearing schematic** with inner race, outer race, cage, balls, and arrows showing radial and axial loads.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the terms:**
**(a)** Basic Static Load Rating (C₀)
**(b)** Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Basic Static Load Rating (C₀):**
It is the static load (radial for radial bearings, axial for thrust bearings) that produces a Hertzian contact stress of:
- **4600 MPa** at the centre of the most heavily loaded ball-raceway contact (ball bearings), or
- **4000 MPa** (roller bearings).

This load causes a permanent indentation of approximately **0.0001 × D (ball diameter)** in the raceway. Beyond C₀, the bearing suffers **permanent brinelling** and loss of running accuracy.

**(b) Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C):**
It is the constant radial (or axial, for thrust bearings) load under which a population of apparently identical bearings can theoretically achieve a rating life of **1 million revolutions (10⁶ revs)** with a **90% reliability**. It is the fatigue-based capacity used for continuous-rotation applications.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between static and dynamic load capacities of a rolling contact bearing. When is each used?**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Static Load Capacity (C₀) | Dynamic Load Capacity (C) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Hertzian contact stress (4600/4000 MPa) | Fatigue life (Lundberg–Palmgren) |
| Failure mode | Permanent plastic indentation (brinelling) | Material fatigue spalling |
| Used when | Slow speeds (n < 10 rpm), oscillating motion, stationary loaded bearings | Continuous rotation (n ≥ 10 rpm) |
| Verification | S₀ = C₀/P₀ ≥ required S₀ | C_required from design equation, then L₁₀h check |
| Governing value | Always numerically lower than C | Always numerically higher than C₀ |

**Static (C₀) is used** for slow-oscillating bearings, crane slewing rings, hinges, and shaft supports under heavy steady loads.

**Dynamic (C) is used** for rotating shafts in motors, gearboxes, pumps, turbines, machine tool spindles, etc.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing up p:** Students use p = 3 for roller bearings (MUST be 10/3). This gives wrong L₁₀ and wrong C, costing 3–5 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to revolutions per unit life:** L₁₀h = (10⁶ × L₁₀)/(60n), not (10⁶)/n.
> 3. **Static check forgotten:** Even when the problem asks only for dynamic life, examiners often deduct 1–2 marks if the static safety factor check is omitted.
> 4. **Mixing X₀, Y₀ (static) with X, Y (dynamic):** These are DIFFERENT values; using X instead of X₀ is a common trap.
> 5. **Units:** kN must be converted to N (or use consistent units throughout).

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A 6308 deep-groove ball bearing in a textile machine spindle is subjected to:**
- Radial load F_r = 3.5 kN
- Axial load F_a = 1.2 kN
- Speed n = 1450 rpm
- Required life = 20,000 hours
- Light shock loading

**Catalogue data for 6308 bearing:** C = 33.2 kN, C₀ = 6.95 kN, e = 0.24, X = 0.56, Y = 1.45 (when F_a/F_r > e), X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5.

**Compute:**
**(a)** The equivalent dynamic load P and the actual L₁₀h life of the bearing.
**(b)** The static safety factor S₀ and check if the bearing is safe for static loading.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute load ratio**
$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1.2}{3.5} = 0.343
$$

**Step 2: Compare with e**
$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = 0.343 > e = 0.24
$$

Hence, use catalogue values **X = 0.56, Y = 1.45**.

**Step 3: Compute equivalent dynamic load**
$$
P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = 0.56 \times 3.5 + 1.45 \times 1.2
$$

$$
P = 1.96 + 1.74 = 3.70 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Compute L₁₀ (in millions of revolutions)**
For ball bearings, p = 3.
$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{33.2}{3.70}\right)^3
$$

$$
L_{10} = (8.973)^3 = 722.45 \text{ million revolutions}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to hours**
$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{722.45 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1450} = \dfrac{722.45 \times 10^6}{87000}
$$

$$
L_{10h} \approx 8304 \text{ hours}
$$

**Conclusion for Part (a):** Actual L₁₀h ≈ **8304 hours**, which is **less than** the required 20,000 hours. The bearing is **undersized** for the desired life — a larger bearing (e.g., 6310 or 6309) must be selected.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute equivalent static load P₀**
$$
P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a = 0.6 \times 3.5 + 0.5 \times 1.2
$$

$$
P_0 = 2.10 + 0.60 = 2.70 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Compute static safety factor**
$$
S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{6.95}{2.70} = 2.574
$$

**Step 3: Compare with required S₀ for "light shock"**

For light shock loading, typical required S₀ ≈ **1.0 – 1.5** (per IS/ISO design tables).

**Conclusion for Part (b):** S₀ = 2.574 > 1.5 → **bearing is SAFE for static loading**, but fails the dynamic life requirement.

**Final Recommendation:** Increase to 6310 series or 6309 — a common KTU 14-mark follow-up.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A cylindrical roller bearing NU2210 in a conveyor gearbox experiences:**
- Radial load F_r = 8 kN
- Axial load F_a = 0.5 kN (negligible compared to radial)
- Speed n = 720 rpm
- Required life = 40,000 hours
- Application: industrial conveyor, moderate shock

**Catalogue data:** C = 65.5 kN, C₀ = 64 kN, e = 0.2, X = 1, Y = 0 (cylindrical roller carries only radial).

**Compute:**
**(a)** Required dynamic load capacity C_required for the design life and verify if NU2210 is adequate.
**(b)** Compute the static safety factor and recommend a replacement bearing if needed.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute equivalent dynamic load P**
For cylindrical roller bearing, F_a is non-existent (axial load not supported), so:
$$
P = F_r = 8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Compute C_required for 40,000 hours**

Using the design equation:
$$
C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}
$$

For roller bearing, **p = 10/3**:
$$
C_{req} = 8 \cdot \left(\dfrac{60 \times 720 \times 40000}{10^6}\right)^{3/10}
$$

$$
C_{req} = 8 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.728 \times 10^9}{10^6}\right)^{0.3}
$$

$$
C_{req} = 8 \cdot (1728)^{0.3}
$$

Now, $(1728)^{0.3}$:

Take $\log(1728) = 3.2375$, multiply by 0.3 → $0.9713$, then $10^{0.9713} \approx 9.36$

$$
C_{req} = 8 \times 9.36 = 74.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with catalogue**
Catalogue C = 65.5 kN < C_required = 74.9 kN → **NU2210 is NOT adequate**.

**Conclusion for Part (a):** NU2210 is undersized. Select a bearing with C ≥ 75 kN, e.g., NU2212 (C ≈ 78 kN) or NU310 (C ≈ 76 kN).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Equivalent static load**
For cylindrical roller, F_a not supported:
$$
P_0 = F_r = 8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Static safety factor**
$$
S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{64}{8} = 8.0
$$

**Step 3: Required S₀ for "moderate shock"**

For moderate shock industrial application, required S₀ ≈ **1.5 – 2.0**.

**Conclusion for Part (b):** S₀ = 8.0 ≫ 2.0 → **highly safe for static loading**, but the **dynamic fatigue life is the binding constraint**. The selection must be driven by the **dynamic capacity criterion**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam!)**

- ⭐ **C₀ is for static** (slow, oscillating, brinelling); **C is for dynamic** (rotating, fatigue).
- ⭐ Static stress limit: **4600 MPa (ball)**, **4000 MPa (roller)**.
- ⭐ Dynamic reference: **1 million revolutions at 90% reliability**.
- ⭐ Exponent **p = 3** for ball, **p = 10/3** for roller.
- ⭐ Basic life equation: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ (in millions of revolutions).
- ⭐ Hourly life: $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60n}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$.
- ⭐ Design form: $C = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{60nL_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$.
- ⭐ Equivalent dynamic load: $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ (use X = 1, Y = 0 when $F_a/F_r \leq e$).
- ⭐ Equivalent static load: $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ (X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5 for deep-groove ball).
- ⭐ Static safety factor: $S_0 = C_0/P_0$. Required S₀: 0.5 (smooth) → 1.0 (normal) → 3.0 (high shock).
- ⭐ The static check is **always** done **after** dynamic check; never skipped.
- ⭐ A bearing must satisfy **BOTH** criteria: $C_{cat} \geq C_{req}$ AND $S_0 \geq S_{0,req}$.
- ⭐ Reliability factor a₁: 1.0 for 90% (L₁₀), 0.62 for 96%, 0.21 for 99% (per ISO 281:2007).
- ⭐ Common ball-bearing types to know for KTU: **6200, 6300, 6400** series (deep-groove), **NJ, NU** (cylindrical roller), **30200, 30300** (tapered roller), **51100, 51200** (thrust ball).
- ⭐ In NU/NJ cylindrical roller bearings, F_a is essentially **not supported** → P = F_r.
- ⭐ In deep-groove ball bearings, both F_r and F_a are supported → use X, Y.
- ⭐ **Modified life equation** (advanced): $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ where a_{ISO} accounts for material/cleanliness/lubrication.
- ⭐ For high-reliability applications (aerospace, defense), a₁ < 1 and the design L_n is **less** than L₁₀.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Equivalent Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Equivalent Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Equivalent Load (P):** The dynamic equivalent load on a rolling element bearing is defined as **a constant, stationary radial load (for radial bearings) or axial load (for thrust bearings) which, if applied to the bearing, would produce the same life as the actual combined radial and thrust loads to which the bearing is subjected under operating conditions.**

Mathematically, the dynamic equivalent load is a **fictitious, single load value** that mathematically represents the combined effect of the actual radial load ($F_r$) and the actual axial (thrust) load ($F_a$) acting simultaneously on a rolling element bearing.

The general form, as per **ISO 281:2007** and **ANSI/ABMA standards** adopted in KTU syllabus:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where:
- $P$ = Dynamic equivalent radial load (**N**)
- $F_r$ = Actual radial load on the bearing (**N**)
- $F_a$ = Actual axial/thrust load on the bearing (**N**)
- $X$ = Radial load factor (dimensionless)
- $Y$ = Thrust load factor (dimensionless)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Backpack on a Hiking Trip**
> Imagine you are carrying a backpack (radial load, $F_r$) weighing 10 kg on your shoulders, while simultaneously holding a heavy water bottle (axial/thrust load, $F_a$) of 5 kg in your hands. The combined stress on your body is not simply 10 + 5 = 15 kg. Because of the biomechanics of how your body distributes the loads, the actual fatigue you feel is equivalent to carrying **a single load** of perhaps 13.5 kg in one specific orientation.
>
> The **Dynamic Equivalent Load (P)** plays the same role for a bearing: it is the "single magic number" that captures the combined effect of $F_r$ and $F_a$ when computing bearing life. The coefficients $X$ and $Y$ are essentially the body's biomechanical "transfer functions."

### 1.3 Why We Need Equivalent Load

The fundamental bearing life equation is:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

This equation **only accepts a single load value (P)**. But in real machinery, bearings rarely experience purely radial or purely axial loads — they experience **both simultaneously**. Hence, we mathematically combine $F_r$ and $F_a$ into one **equivalent load (P)** before plugging it into the life equation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Skipping the conversion to equivalent load and using just $F_r$ in the life equation is one of the most common mistakes in board exams and leads to **over-design errors of 30–60%**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

| Standard | Governing Body | Purpose |
|----------|---------------|---------|
| **ISO 281:2007** | International Organization for Standardization | Defines $X$, $Y$, $e$ values |
| **ANSI/ABMA Std 9 & 11** | American Bearing Manufacturers | Equivalent national standard |
| **SKF General Catalogue** | SKF (industry reference) | Tabulated values for $X$, $Y$, $e$ |

The exponents used in bearing life equations are:
- $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**
- $p = \frac{10}{3}$ for **roller bearings**

These are **empirically determined constants** derived from Hertzian contact stress theory and Weibull fatigue statistics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind X and Y

The factors $X$ and $Y$ are **not constants** — they depend on:
1. The **ratio** $F_a / F_r$ (load combination ratio)
2. The **ratio** $F_a / C_0$ (where $C_0$ is the static load rating)
3. The **type of bearing** (deep groove ball, angular contact, tapered roller, etc.)

### 2.2 The Critical Parameter "e"

A special parameter called the **"e" factor** (limiting value of $F_a/F_r$) is tabulated for each bearing type. The decision logic is:

- **If** $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$  →  Then $X = 1, \ Y = 0$, so $P = F_r$
- **If** $\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$  →  Then use tabulated $X$ and $Y$ values, so $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of "e":** The value of $e$ represents the **boundary** at which the axial load becomes significant enough to alter the internal load distribution inside the bearing. Below this threshold, thrust has negligible effect on fatigue life; above it, the combined formula is mandatory.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Bearing Type | Dynamic Equivalent Load ($P$) | Exponent $p$ |
|---|--------------|--------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Radial Ball Bearing (no thrust)** | $P = F_r$ | 3 |
| 2 | **Radial Ball Bearing (combined $F_r$ + $F_a$)** | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | 3 |
| 3 | **Radial Roller Bearing (no thrust)** | $P = F_r$ | 10/3 |
| 4 | **Radial Roller Bearing (combined)** | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | 10/3 |
| 5 | **Single-Direction Thrust Ball Bearing** | $P = F_a$ | 3 |
| 6 | **Thrust Ball Bearing (combined)** — If $F_a \leq 0.34 \cdot F_r$ | $P = F_a$ | 3 |
| 7 | **Thrust Ball Bearing (combined)** — If $F_a > 0.34 \cdot F_r$ | $P = 0.66 \cdot F_r + 1.2 \cdot F_a$ | 3 |
| 8 | **Thrust Roller Bearing (combined)** | $P = F_r + F_a$ | 10/3 |

### 2.4 Standard X, Y, e Values (Deep Groove Ball Bearing — Single Row)

| $F_a / C_0$ | $e$ | $X$ | $Y$ |
|:-----------:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.025 | 0.22 | 1.00 | 0 |
| 0.040 | 0.24 | 1.00 | 0 |
| 0.070 | 0.26 | 0.56 | 1.80 |
| 0.130 | 0.28 | 0.56 | 1.60 |
| 0.250 | 0.30 | 0.56 | 1.40 |
| 0.500 | 0.34 | 0.56 | 1.20 |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **catalogue-dependent**. For KTU exams, values are typically given in the question. Always cross-check manufacturer tables for design work.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The concept of Dynamic Equivalent Load is the **gateway to all bearing selection work** in:
- **Gearboxes** (combined radial + thrust from helical/bevel gears)
- **Electric motors** (radial load from belt drive + axial from rotor weight)
- **Automotive wheel hubs** (combined cornering + braking forces)
- **Pumps and compressors** (radial impeller + axial thrust)

Without computing $P$, you **cannot** use the bearing life equation, **cannot** select a bearing from a catalogue, and **cannot** predict maintenance schedules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bearing Life Equation (Foundation for P)

The dynamic equivalent load equation is rooted in **Hertzian contact stress theory** and **Weibull's statistical fatigue model**.

**Step 1:** The maximum Hertzian contact stress $\sigma_{max}$ for a sphere-on-sphere contact (ball-on-race) is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot E^{*2}}{R^{2}} \right)^{1/3}$$

where $F$ is the contact force, $E^{*}$ is the effective elastic modulus, and $R$ is the effective radius.

**Step 2:** Solving for $F$:

$$F = \frac{\pi^3 \cdot R^2 \cdot \sigma_{max}^3}{6 \cdot E^{*2}}$$

This shows $F \propto \sigma_{max}^3$.

**Step 3:** For a bearing under load $P$, each rolling element carries a fraction of $P$ based on the angular position $\phi$. Summing contributions:

$$P \propto \sigma_{max}^3$$

**Step 4:** The fatigue life $L$ (in revolutions) is empirically related to stress by:

$$L \propto \frac{1}{\sigma_{max}^9}$$

This 9th power relationship comes from Weibull's weakest-link theory for highly stressed contact volumes.

**Step 5:** Combining Steps 3 and 4:

$$L \propto \frac{1}{P^3}$$

**Step 6:** Introducing the proportionality constant via the **dynamic load rating $C$** (defined as the load at which a bearing achieves 1 million revolutions of life):

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 \times 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

This is the **fundamental bearing life equation** for ball bearings ($p = 3$). For roller bearings, the line contact changes the stress exponent, giving $p = 10/3$.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Deep Groove Ball Bearing

**Problem Statement:** A 6308 deep groove ball bearing carries a radial load $F_r = 3000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 1500$ N. The shaft rotates at $N = 1440$ rpm. From the manufacturer's catalogue: $C = 41{,}000$ N and $C_0 = 22{,}400$ N. Determine:
1. The dynamic equivalent load $P$
2. The expected bearing life in hours

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio $F_a / C_0$:**

$$\frac{F_a}{C_0} = \frac{1500}{22400} = 0.0670$$

**Step 2 — Identify $e$ from the catalogue table:**

Since $0.040 < 0.0670 \leq 0.070$, we read from the table:
$$e = 0.26$$

**Step 3 — Compute the load ratio $F_a / F_r$:**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{3000} = 0.500$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = 0.500 \quad > \quad e = 0.26$$

Therefore, the **combined formula** applies: $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$

**Step 5 — Read $X$ and $Y$ from the table for $F_a/C_0 = 0.0670$:**

$$X = 0.56, \quad Y = 1.80$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the dynamic equivalent load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
&= 0.56 \times 3000 + 1.80 \times 1500 \\
&= 1680 + 2700 \\
&= 4380 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the rating life in millions of revolutions** (ball bearing, $p = 3$):

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^3 \text{ million revolutions} \\
&= \left( \frac{41000}{4380} \right)^3 \\
&= (9.361)^3 \\
&= 820.4 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Convert life to hours:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times N} \\
&= \frac{820.4 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1440} \\
&= \frac{820.4 \times 10^6}{86400} \\
&= 9495 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree Summary

| Input Conditions | Formula Used | Output ($P$) |
|------------------|--------------|--------------|
| $F_a = 0$ (purely radial) | $P = F_r$ | $P = 3000$ N |
| $F_a / F_r \leq e$ | $P = F_r$ (thrust negligible) | $P = 3000$ N |
| $F_a / F_r > e$ | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | $P = 4380$ N (computed) |
| Thrust ball, $F_a \leq 0.34 F_r$ | $P = F_a$ | $P = F_a$ |
| Thrust ball, $F_a > 0.34 F_r$ | $P = 0.66 F_r + 1.2 F_a$ | Case-specific |
| Thrust roller (combined) | $P = F_r + F_a$ | Sum of both |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When $F_a / F_r \leq e$, setting $Y \neq 0$ (using the combined formula directly) gives an artificially high $P$ and **over-designs** the bearing. Always perform the $e$-check first.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart for Computing Dynamic Equivalent Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bearing Subjected to F_r and F_a] --> B{Identify Bearing Type}
    B -->|Radial Ball Roller| C{Is F_a = 0?}
    B -->|Thrust Bearing| D{Is F_r = 0?}
    B -->|Tapered Roller| C

    C -->|Yes| E[P = F_r]
    C -->|No| F[Calculate F_a / C_0]
    F --> G[Read e and X, Y from Table]
    G --> H[Calculate F_a / F_r]
    H --> I{Is F_a / F_r less or equal to e?}
    I -->|Yes| E
    I -->|No| J[P = X times F_r plus Y times F_a]

    D -->|Yes| K[P = F_a]
    D -->|No| L{Ball or Roller Thrust?}
    L -->|Ball| M{Is F_a less or equal to 0.34 F_r?}
    M -->|Yes| K
    M -->|No| N[P = 0.66 F_r plus 1.2 F_a]
    L -->|Roller| O[P = F_r plus F_a]

    E --> P[Compute L_10 using C and P]
    J --> P
    K --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Convert to Hours using N rpm]
    Q --> R[End: Bearing Life Report]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Interaction in a Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["External Loads Applied"]
        A1[Radial Load F_r] --> M1[Load Combining Block]
        A2[Axial Load F_a] --> M1
        M1 --> M2[Compare F_a to F_r ratio]
    end

    subgraph CATALOGUE["Manufacturer Data"]
        B1[Static Rating C_0] --> T1[X Y e Table]
        B2[Dynamic Rating C] --> T1
        T1 --> M2
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["Equivalent Load Calculation"]
        M2 --> P1{Decision Logic}
        P1 --> P2[Apply X F_r plus Y F_a]
        P1 --> P3[Apply P equals F_r only]
        P2 --> OUT[Dynamic Equivalent Load P]
        P3 --> OUT
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Life Calculation Stage"]
        OUT --> L1[Bearing Life L_10]
        B2 --> L1
    end

    L1 --> FINAL[Final Life in Hours]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Load Zones in a Deep Groove Ball Bearing

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BEARING["Cross Section: Deep Groove Ball Bearing 6308"]
        OUTERRING[Outer Ring] --> TOP[Top Contact Zone 12 o clock]
        OUTERRING --> BOT[Bottom Contact Zone 6 o clock]
        INNERRING[Inner Ring] --> TOP
        INNERRING --> BOT
        BALLS[Rolling Elements Balls] --> TOP
        BALLS --> BOT
    end

    TOP --> FR[Radial Load F_r Applied Here]
    FR --> STRESS[Hertzian Contact Stress Distribution]
    STRESS --> EQUIV[Equivalent Stress to Single Load P]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The above diagrams are decision/architecture maps. The actual physical appearance of a 6308 deep groove ball bearing has a single row of balls, an inner race, an outer race, a cage (retainer), and shields/seals.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define dynamic equivalent load on a rolling element bearing. Why is it necessary to compute it before calculating bearing life?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The dynamic equivalent load $P$ is a hypothetical, single stationary load (radial for radial bearings, axial for thrust bearings) that produces the same bearing life as the actual combined radial ($F_r$) and thrust ($F_a$) loads.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$
>
> **Necessity:**
> 1. The bearing life equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ **requires a single equivalent load value** $P$ as input.
> 2. Real machines always have combined $F_r$ and $F_a$, so a method to mathematically combine them is mandatory.
> 3. Without $P$, bearing catalogue ratings ($C$, $C_0$) **cannot be utilized** for life prediction.
> 4. It ensures the design is **realistic** and not over/under-engineered.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: For a single-row deep groove ball bearing, under what condition does the dynamic equivalent load reduce to $P = F_r$?**

**Model Answer:**

> The dynamic equivalent load reduces to $P = F_r$ when the **ratio of the actual axial load to the actual radial load is less than or equal to the limiting factor $e$**, i.e.,
>
> $$\frac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$$
>
> Under this condition:
> - $X = 1$ (radial load factor)
> - $Y = 0$ (thrust load factor)
>
> Therefore, $P = 1 \cdot F_r + 0 \cdot F_a = F_r$. This means the thrust load is **negligible** in its contribution to bearing fatigue, and only the radial load is considered.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: A 6308 deep groove ball bearing is subjected to a radial load of 3000 N and an axial load of 1500 N. The shaft rotates at 1440 rpm. The dynamic load rating is 41,000 N and the static load rating is 22,400 N. Determine:**

**(a)** The dynamic equivalent load on the bearing. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** The expected life of the bearing in hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution (a): Dynamic Equivalent Load**

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio $F_a / C_0$:**

$$\frac{F_a}{C_0} = \frac{1500}{22400} = 0.0670$$

**Step 2 — From the deep groove ball bearing catalogue table:**

For $F_a / C_0 = 0.0670$ (lies between 0.040 and 0.070):
$$e = 0.26, \quad X = 0.56, \quad Y = 1.80$$

**Step 3 — Compute $F_a / F_r$:**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{3000} = 0.500$$

**Step 4 — Compare with $e$:**

$$0.500 > 0.26 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Combined formula applies}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the equivalent load formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
&= 0.56 \times 3000 + 1.80 \times 1500 \\
&= 1680 + 2700 \\
&= \boxed{4380 \text{ N}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

##### **Solution (b): Bearing Life in Hours**

**Step 1 — Bearing life equation (ball bearing, $p = 3$):**

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\frac{41000}{4380}\right)^3 \\
&= (9.361)^3 \\
&= 820.4 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours using $N = 1440$ rpm:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times N} \\
&= \frac{820.4 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1440} \\
&= \frac{820.4 \times 10^6}{86400} \\
&= \boxed{9495 \text{ hours} \approx 1.08 \text{ years}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: A 30308 tapered roller bearing operates under a radial load of 4500 N and an axial load of 2000 N. The shaft speed is 1000 rpm. The dynamic load rating $C = 75{,}000$ N. The limiting factor is $e = 0.83$ and the thrust factor is $Y = 1.1$. Calculate:**

**(a)** The dynamic equivalent load and verify whether the bearing is safely rated. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** The expected life of the bearing in hours. **(7 Marks)** (Use $p = 10/3$)

---

##### **Solution (a): Dynamic Equivalent Load**

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio:**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2000}{4500} = 0.444$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $e$:**

$$0.444 < 0.83 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$$

**Step 3 — Apply the radial-only formula** ($X = 1, Y = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 1 \times 4500 + 0 \times 2000 \\
&= \boxed{4500 \text{ N}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (Safety Check):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{P} &= \frac{75000}{4500} = 16.67 > 1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Since $C > P$, the bearing is **safely rated** (it can carry 16.67 times the equivalent load before reaching rated life).

---

##### **Solution (b): Bearing Life in Hours**

**Step 1 — Use roller bearing life equation with $p = 10/3$:**

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= (16.67)^{10/3} \\
&= (16.67^{1/3})^{10} \\
&= (2.555)^{10}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(2.555)^{10}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(2.555)^2 &= 6.528 \\
(2.555)^4 &= 42.62 \\
(2.555)^8 &= 1816.5 \\
(2.555)^{10} &= 1816.5 \times 6.528 = 11{,}857
\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore L_{10} \approx 11{,}857 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{11{,}857 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{11{,}857 \times 10^6}{60{,}000} \\
&= \boxed{197{,}617 \text{ hours} \approx 22.55 \text{ years}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $e$-check:** Many students directly substitute $X$ and $Y$ values without first verifying whether $F_a/F_r > e$. This **costs 2–3 marks** in part (a) of any equivalent load problem.
> 2. **Wrong exponent $p$:** Using $p = 3$ for a **roller bearing** is a fatal error. Always check the bearing type first.
> 3. **Unit errors in life:** Forgetting to multiply by $10^6$ when stating $L_{10}$ in revolutions (vs. millions of revolutions) is the **most common** 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using $F_r$ instead of $P$ in the life equation:** Some students plug $F_r = 3000$ N into the life equation instead of $P = 4380$ N. This gives a life overestimate of $\approx (4380/3000)^3 \approx 3.1\times$ — a major design error.
