> 5. **Catalogue values:** KTU may provide a **simplified $X$-$Y$-$e$ table** in the question paper. Do not memorize a specific manufacturer's data; always use the table given in the exam.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Dynamic Equivalent Load (P):** A fictitious single load representing combined $F_r$ and $F_a$.
- [x] **Master formula:** $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ for radial bearings.
- [x] **Purely radial case:** $P = F_r$ (when $F_a = 0$ or $F_a/F_r \leq e$).
- [x] **Limiting factor $e$:** Tabulated for each bearing; depends on $F_a/C_0$ ratio.
- [x] **Decision rule:** Always check $F_a/F_r$ vs $e$ before applying $X, Y$ values.
- [x] **Thrust ball bearing (combined):** $P = 0.66 F_r + 1.2 F_a$ if $F_a > 0.34 F_r$; else $P = F_a$.
- [x] **Thrust roller bearing (combined):** $P = F_r + F_a$ (always additive).
- [x] **Exponents:** $p = 3$ (ball), $p = 10/3$ (roller) — **memorize**.
- [x] **Life equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ in **millions of revolutions**.
- [x] **Life in hours:** $L_{10h} = (L_{10} \times 10^6) / (60 \times N)$.
- [x] **Units check:** $C$, $F_r$, $F_a$, $P$ all in **Newtons (N)**.
- [x] **Catalogue dependency:** $X$, $Y$, $e$ values are **manufacturer-specific**; use whatever is given in the KTU question paper.
- [x] **Physical meaning of $X < 1$:** A fraction is used because the thrust load **partially relieves** the radial load distribution.
- [x] **Physical meaning of $Y > 1$:** Thrust loads are **amplified** in their fatigue effect because they concentrate stress at the contact line/point.
- [x] **Common error pattern in KTU papers:** Skipping the $e$-check → over-designed bearings → 2–3 marks lost.
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** Equivalent load is the **gateway** to catalogue-based bearing selection in gearboxes, motors, pumps, and automotive systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET411 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Selection of Bearings"?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET411 – Machine Design)**, the *Selection of Bearings* refers to the systematic engineering procedure used to choose the correct type, size, and configuration of a bearing (rolling-element or sliding/journal) for a given mechanical application. The selection process is driven by a set of well-defined operational parameters: the **magnitude and direction of the applied load**, the **rotational speed (rpm)**, the **required service life (in hours or revolutions)**, the **operating temperature**, the **lubrication regime**, the **alignment and mounting conditions**, and the **environmental constraints (presence of dust, moisture, chemicals)**.

A bearing is a machine element whose primary function is to **constrain relative motion to the desired degree of freedom and to reduce friction between moving parts**, effectively transmitting loads from a shaft to its supporting structure. The word *"Selection"* emphasizes that the bearing is not arbitrarily chosen; it is the *result* of an iterative, code-based, and design-catalog-driven decision process, where the designer evaluates a candidate bearing against the requirements of **ISO 15 / IS 6455** dimensional standards and **ISO 281 / IS 6456** dynamic load rating and life calculation standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Selection of bearings is the process of determining the most suitable bearing type and size such that the bearing can transmit the imposed loads (radial, axial, or combined) for the desired operating life under given speed, temperature, and lubrication conditions, while satisfying constraints of cost, availability, and reliability."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Right Shoe for the Right Journey"

Imagine you are going on a journey of **5000 km** on foot.

- You are carrying a **backpack of weight 30 kg** (the load).
- You must complete the journey in a **specific time** (the life / speed).
- The road is **either smooth asphalt or rough gravel** (the operating conditions).
- The temperature is **hot or cold** (thermal environment).

Would you wear **sandals**, **sneakers**, or **hiking boots**? You would intuitively select the shoe whose *capacity* (durability, load-bearing ability, comfort, friction characteristics) best matches the *demand* (load, distance, surface, climate). A sandal is cheap and works for short distances on smooth roads but will fail quickly on rough terrain with a heavy load.

A **bearing selection works identically**:

| Journey Parameter | Bearing Parameter |
|---|---|
| Weight of backpack | **Equivalent dynamic load (P)** |
| Distance to cover | **Required life (L10h in hours or L10 in million revolutions)** |
| Speed of walking | **Rotational speed (n in rpm)** |
| Road roughness | **Lubrication regime + contamination level** |
| Climate | **Operating temperature** |
| Shoe type | **Bearing type** (ball, roller, tapered, needle, journal) |

The **shoe's size and capacity** corresponds to the **basic dynamic load rating (C)** of the bearing — the load at which a statistically large population of identical bearings will achieve a rating life of **1 million revolutions** with a **90% reliability**.

## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Bearing Families

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4) treats both families, but the *selection procedure* of rolling element bearings is more code-driven and hence more heavily tested.

**1. Rolling-Element (Antifriction) Bearings**
Use rigid balls or rollers interposed between the rotating and stationary races. Friction is primarily **rolling friction** (≈ 0.001–0.005). Examples: deep-groove ball bearing, cylindrical roller, spherical roller, tapered roller, needle roller, angular contact.

**2. Sliding-Contact (Plain / Journal) Bearings**
Operate with a continuous film of lubricant (hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, boundary, mixed) between the journal and the bearing shell. Friction is **sliding friction** (≈ 0.01–0.1 dry, 0.001–0.01 hydrodynamic). Examples: ring-oiled journal, tilting-pad, gas bearings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Heuristic (KTU Board Examiner's favorite one-liner):**
> *"Use rolling-element bearings for high speeds and moderate loads with minimum maintenance; use plain bearings for very high loads, very low speeds, or where damping and shock absorption are critical."*

## 1.4 Why is Selection So Important in Machine Design?

A wrong bearing selection can cause:

1. **Premature fatigue failure** — spalling, brinelling, false brinelling.
2. **Thermal runaway** — insufficient lubrication → overheating → seizure.
3. **Excessive deflection / misalignment** — leading to shaft whip and vibration.
4. **Catastrophic economic loss** — unscheduled downtime, replacement costs, warranty claims.
5. **Safety hazard** — failure of bearings in critical applications (rotor shafts of turbines, aircraft landing gear, automotive wheel hubs) endangers human life.

Hence, bearing selection is not a "look-up" exercise; it is an **engineering judgment process grounded in codified standards**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Selection Philosophy

A rigorous bearing selection follows a logical flow. The KTU 2024 expected answer structure is built around these six stages:

### Step 1 — Gather Application Data
- Magnitude of **radial load Fr (N)**
- Magnitude of **axial/thrust load Fa (N)**
- **Rotational speed n (rpm)**
- **Desired life** (typically **L10h = 20,000 to 40,000 h** for general machinery; **100,000 h** for machine tools; **8,000 to 12,000 h** for household appliances)
- **Temperature of operation**
- **Mounting arrangement** (fixed/floating, single row, paired)
- **Reliability target** (default 90%, can be raised to 95%, 96%, 99%)

### Step 2 — Choose the Bearing Type
Use the following **decision matrix** to identify candidate families:

| Load Type | Speed | Common Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Pure radial, moderate speed | High | **Deep-groove ball bearing** |
| Radial + moderate axial | Medium–High | **Angular contact ball bearing** (paired) |
| Pure radial, heavy load, low speed | Low–Medium | **Cylindrical roller bearing** |
| Heavy radial + heavy axial | Low–Medium | **Tapered roller bearing** (paired) |
| Heavy radial, possible misalignment | Low | **Spherical roller bearing** |
| Limited radial space, high radial load | Any | **Needle roller bearing** |

### Step 3 — Compute the Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)
Most bearings carry a *combination* of radial and axial loads. To convert this combination into a single equivalent load that would cause the *same fatigue life* as the actual combination, the **ISO 281 / IS 6456** formula is used:

$$
P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a
$$

where:
- $X$ = radial load factor
- $Y$ = axial load factor
- $F_r$ = applied radial load
- $F_a$ = applied axial load

If $F_a / F_r \le e$, then $P = F_r$ (axial load is negligible). If $F_a / F_r > e$, the full expression applies. The values of $X$, $Y$, and $e$ are tabulated in manufacturer catalogs and depend on the **bearing type and the ratio $F_a / C_0$** (where $C_0$ is the static load rating).

### Step 4 — Calculate the Required Dynamic Load Rating (C_required)
Using the **basic rating life equation**:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

where $L_{10}$ is the rating life in **millions of revolutions** and $p$ is the exponent:
- $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**
- $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings**

Solving for C:

$$
C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert Required Life into Millions of Revolutions
The required life in revolutions depends on the operating hours $L_{10h}$ and the speed $n$:

$$
L_{10} = \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} \quad \text{(million revolutions)}
$$

### Step 6 — Select a Standard Bearing from the Catalog
Choose a bearing whose **basic dynamic load rating $C \ge C_{req}$**. Then perform a **static load check** using:

$$
P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a
$$

and ensure the **static safety factor** $f_s = C_0 / P_0$ lies within the recommended range (typically $f_s \ge 1.0$ for smooth operation, $f_s \ge 1.5$ for shock loads, $f_s \ge 2.0$ for very critical applications).

## 2.2 The Modified Life Equation (Advanced — ISO 281:2007)

For modern, high-quality bearings operating in clean, well-lubricated conditions, the simple $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ underestimates the achievable life. The **modified rating life** introduces three correction factors:

$$
L_{na} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10}
$$

where:
- $a_1$ = **reliability factor** ($a_1 = 1$ for 90% reliability, $a_1 > 1$ for 95%, 96%, 99%)
- $a_2$ = **material and manufacturing factor** (typically 0.7 to 1.5)
- $a_3$ = **operating conditions / lubrication factor** (depends on viscosity ratio $\kappa = \nu / \nu_1$)
  - $a_3 \le 1$ if $\kappa < 1$ (lubricant too thin)
  - $a_3 \approx 1$ if $\kappa = 1$
  - $a_3 \ge 1$ if $\kappa > 1$ (very well lubricated)

The viscosity ratio $\kappa$ is the **actual operating kinematic viscosity $\nu$ of the lubricant** divided by the **reference viscosity $\nu_1$ required for adequate lubrication at the operating temperature and speed**.

## 2.3 Viscosity Requirements (Reynolds / Hamrock-Dowson Approximation)

The reference viscosity $\nu_1$ for ball/roller bearings can be approximated as:

$$
\nu_1 = 45000 \cdot n^{-0.83} \cdot d^{-0.5} \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{/s)}
$$

where $n$ is in rpm and $d$ is the **mean bearing diameter (mm)** = $(d + D)/2$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *only* formulas a KTU 2024 student is expected to memorize for the bearing-selection module. Examiners frequently ask students to write down these formulas **without a book**.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent dynamic load | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | N; $X, Y$ from catalog |
| 2 | Basic dynamic load rating relationship | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ | $L_{10}$ in $10^6$ rev; $p=3$ (ball), $p=10/3$ (roller) |
| 3 | Required dynamic load rating | $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$ | N |
| 4 | Life in hours | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \, n}$ | h; $n$ in rpm |
| 5 | Life in million revolutions | $L_{10} = \dfrac{60 \, n \, L_{10h}}{10^6}$ | dimensionless multiplier |
| 6 | Equivalent static load | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | N |
| 7 | Static safety factor | $f_s = C_0 / P_0$ | dimensionless; $f_s \ge 1$ to 2 |
| 8 | Modified life (ISO 281) | $L_{na} = a_1 a_2 a_3 L_{10}$ | h or rev |
| 9 | Viscosity ratio | $\kappa = \nu / \nu_1$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Reference viscosity (Hamrock-Dowson) | $\nu_1 = 45000 \, n^{-0.83} d^{-0.5}$ | $\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 11 | Angular contact thrust contribution | $F_{ae} = 0.68 \, F_r \cot\alpha$ (approx.) | N; $\alpha$ = contact angle |
| 12 | Operating speed factor (thermal check) | $f_n = \sqrt[3]{d \cdot n}$ | used in thermal balance |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bearing selection procedure is a **cornerstone of every rotating machine**:

- **Automotive industry**: Wheel hub bearings, transmission gear-box bearings, engine crankshaft main and connecting-rod bearings (both rolling and plain).
- **Wind turbines**: Main rotor shaft bearing — typically a spherical roller bearing capable of carrying 5–10 MN of axial thrust.
- **Aerospace**: Turbofan engine main shaft bearings — operate above 10,000 rpm with $d \cdot n$ values exceeding 2 million.
- **Machine tools**: Spindle bearings (angular contact or cylindrical roller) — high precision, low runout, life of 20,000–40,000 h.
- **Electric motors**: Deep-groove ball bearings — universal, simple, cheap.
- **Pumps and compressors**: Roller or angular contact bearings — high radial loads, misalignment tolerant (spherical).

In all of these domains, the **selection method is essentially identical** — it is the *application parameters* and the *bearing configuration* that differ.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Rating Life Equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$

The **rating life equation** is empirical, derived from a vast experimental database of bearing endurance tests by **Palmgren (1924)** and later formalized by **Lundberg and Palmgren (1947)** and refined by **Ioannides and Harris (1985)**. The derivation of its *mathematical form* rests on the **Weibull weakest-link theory of fatigue**.

### Step-by-step logical derivation:

**Step 1:** The probability of survival of a single bearing at a load $P$ after $N$ revolutions follows a **Weibull distribution**:

$$
\ln \frac{1}{S(N)} \propto \left(\frac{N}{a(P)}\right)^b
$$

where $S(N)$ is the survival probability, $a(P)$ is the Weibull scale parameter (which depends on load), and $b$ is the Weibull shape parameter (typically $b = 1.5$ for ball bearings, $b = 1.1$ for roller bearings).

**Step 2:** For two different loads $P_1$ and $P_2$ leading to the same survival probability, the ratio of lives is:

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{a/b}
$$

**Step 3:** The exponent $a/b$ is found experimentally. For ball bearings it is $a/b = 3$, and for roller bearings it is $a/b = 10/3$. Define $p = a/b$.

**Step 4:** Define the **basic dynamic load rating $C$** as the constant load at which 90% of a population of bearings survives **$N_C = 1$ million revolutions**. Then for any load $P$ and rating life $L_{10}$ (in millions of revolutions):

$$
L_{10} \cdot P^p = 1 \cdot C^p
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** If the load on the bearing is doubled, the life is reduced by a factor of $2^3 = 8$ (ball) or $2^{10/3} \approx 10$ (roller). This is a *steep* penalty — bearing life is highly sensitive to overload.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Dynamic Load $P = X F_r + Y F_a$

This is a **linearization of the elliptical contact stress field**. In a bearing, the contact stresses (Hertzian) on each rolling element are determined by both the radial and axial components of the applied load. To keep the rating-life equation valid (which assumes a single equivalent load $P$), the combination $F_r, F_a$ is replaced by a single value $P$ that produces the *same* internal stress distribution.

**Step 1:** For a bearing with contact angle $\alpha$, the load on each rolling element can be resolved into a normal force $Q$ acting along the contact line.

**Step 2:** Summing the components of $Q$ over all rolling elements in the load zone gives the **radial stiffness** $K_r$ and **axial stiffness** $K_a$. Their ratio defines the geometry-dependent constants $X$ and $Y$:

$$
X = \frac{1}{1 + (Y/X) \tan\alpha}
$$

**Step 3:** Empirically catalogued by manufacturers, the constants $X$ and $Y$ are tabulated as functions of the dimensionless ratio $F_a / C_0$. When $F_a / F_r \le e$ (a threshold also tabulated), the axial term is too small to influence life, so $P = F_r$ and the constants $X = 1, Y = 0$.

> **Result:** The single equivalent dynamic load that represents any $(F_r, F_a)$ combination for rating-life calculation is $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Required Life in Hours from Revolutions

The rating life $L_{10}$ is expressed in **millions of revolutions**. To convert to operating hours, multiply by the revolutions per hour and divide by $10^6$:

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times n}
$$

The inverse is:

$$
L_{10} = \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU module-end question):**
> *A radial ball bearing is to be selected for an application with the following data:*
> *Radial load $F_r = 3500$ N, Axial load $F_a = 1200$ N, Rotational speed $n = 1450$ rpm, Required life $L_{10h} = 20{,}000$ hours, Ambient temperature 70 °C, light shock loads, single-row deep-groove ball bearing.*

> *Manufacturer's catalog data for the candidate bearing series:*
> *Static load rating $C_0 = 25{,}000$ N, $e = 0.30$, $Y = 1.65$ (when $F_a/F_r > e$), $X = 0.56$.*

> *Tasks:*
> *(a) Compute the equivalent dynamic load $P$.*
> *(b) Compute the required dynamic load rating $C_{req}$ and check if the candidate bearing is adequate.*
> *(c) Compute the static safety factor and comment on the selection.*

### Solution:

#### Part (a) — Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$

**Step 1:** Compute the load ratio $F_a / F_r$:

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1200}{3500} = 0.343
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with the catalog value $e = 0.30$. Since $0.343 > 0.30$, the axial term is **not** negligible; we must use $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.65$.

**Step 3:** Apply the equivalent dynamic load equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \\
&= 0.56 \times 3500 + 1.65 \times 1200 \\
&= 1960 + 1980 \\
&= 3940 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The equivalent dynamic load is $P = 3940$ N.

#### Part (b) — Required Dynamic Load Rating $C_{req}$

**Step 1:** Convert the required service life from hours to millions of revolutions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 1450 \times 20{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= \frac{1{,}740{,}000{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= 1740 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For a ball bearing, $p = 3$. Compute the required dynamic load rating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p} \\
&= 3940 \times 1740^{\,1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the cube root:

$$
1740^{\,1/3} \approx 12.01
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= 3940 \times 12.01 \\
&\approx 47{,}320 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the catalog $C$ of the candidate bearing. The candidate has $C \ge 47{,}320$ N? If yes, it is adequate. Since the candidate has not been specified numerically, we **conclude** that the designer must select a bearing whose $C \ge 47.32$ kN.

> The required dynamic load rating is $C_{req} \approx 47.3$ kN.

#### Part (c) — Static Safety Factor

**Step 1:** Compute the equivalent static load. For a deep-groove ball bearing, the static factors are typically $X_0 = 0.6$ and $Y_0 = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a \\
&= 0.6 \times 3500 + 0.5 \times 1200 \\
&= 2100 + 600 \\
&= 2700 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the static safety factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= \frac{C_0}{P_0} \\
&= \frac{25{,}000}{2700} \\
&\approx 9.26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result. For light shock loads and smooth operation, $f_s \ge 1.0$ is the typical minimum. With $f_s \approx 9.26$, the bearing is **massively over-rated from a static viewpoint**, indicating that the design is conservatively safe against permanent plastic deformation (brinelling).

> **Verdict:** If the catalog $C$ of the candidate bearing is ≥ 47.3 kN, the bearing is **adequate** for the 20,000 h service life requirement.

## 3.5 Analytical Selection Matrix — A 4-Quadrant Trade-off Table

| Property | Ball Bearings | Roller Bearings |
|---|---|---|
| Load capacity (radial) | Lower (line contact absent) | Higher (line contact) |
| Speed capacity | Higher (less friction) | Lower (line contact friction) |
| Sensitivity to misalignment | Low (some self-aligning types) | High (cylindrical, tapered) |
| Manufacturing cost | Lower | Higher |
| Noise level | Lower | Higher |
| Typical applications | Electric motors, pumps, gearboxes | Heavy machinery, rolling mills, gearboxes |
| Life exponent $p$ | 3 | 10/3 |
| Load-life sensitivity | $L \propto P^{-3}$ | $L \propto P^{-10/3}$ (steeper) |
| Axial load capacity | Limited (only angular contact) | Moderate (tapered, spherical) |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Selection Procedure Summary (Algorithmic Logic)

1. Compute $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (or $P = F_r$ if $F_a/F_r \le e$).
2. Compute $L_{10} = (60 n L_{10h})/10^6$.
3. Compute $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$ (with $p = 3$ or $10/3$).
4. Select a bearing from catalog with $C \ge C_{req}$.
5. Compute $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$.
6. Check $f_s = C_0 / P_0 \ge f_{s,\min}$ (catalog value).
7. Check thermal balance: $Q_{gen} \le Q_{diss}$ using $\mu P v$ generated heat vs convection/radiation dissipation.
8. If any check fails, **upsize the bearing** and repeat from Step 3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bearing Selection Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Gather Application Data] --> B[Determine Fr, Fa, n, L10h, Temp]
    B --> C[Choose Bearing Type from Decision Matrix]
    C --> D[Compute Fa / Fr and Compare with e]
    D --> E{Is Fa/Fr less than or equal to e?}
    E -- Yes --> F[P equals Fr]
    E -- No --> G[P equals X times Fr plus Y times Fa]
    F --> H[Convert L10h to L10 in million revolutions]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Choose p equals 3 for Ball or 10/3 for Roller]
    I --> J[Compute Required Dynamic Load Rating Creq]
    J --> K[Browse Manufacturer Catalog]
    K --> L{Is C of selected bearing greater than or equal to Creq?}
    L -- No --> M[Select Next Larger Bearing]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[Compute Equivalent Static Load P0]
    N --> O[Compute Static Safety Factor fs]
    O --> P{Is fs greater than or equal to fs min?}
    P -- No --> M
    P -- Yes --> Q[Perform Thermal Balance Check]
    Q --> R{Is Heat Generated less than Heat Dissipated?}
    R -- No --> S[Improve Lubrication or Upsize]
    S --> K
    R -- Yes --> T[Finalize Bearing Selection]
    T --> U[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Factors Influencing Bearing Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load: Fr and Fa] --> Z[Selection Decision]
    B[Speed: n rpm] --> Z
    C[Required Life: L10h] --> Z
    D[Operating Temperature] --> Z
    E[Lubrication Regime] --> Z
    F[Alignment and Mounting] --> Z
    G[Contamination and Sealing] --> Z
    H[Reliability Target] --> Z
    I[Cost and Availability] --> Z
    Z --> O[Chosen Bearing]
    O --> P[Verification: P, Creq, fs, Thermal]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — Ball vs Roller vs Plain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BallB[Ball Bearings]
        B1[Deep Groove]
        B2[Angular Contact]
        B3[Self Aligning]
        B4[Thrust Ball]
    end
    subgraph RollerB[Roller Bearings]
        R1[Cylindrical]
        R2[Tapered]
        R3[Spherical]
        R4[Needle]
    end
    subgraph PlainB[Plain Bearings]
        P1[Journal: Hydrodynamic]
        P2[Journal: Boundary]
        P3[Tilting Pad]
        P4[Gas Bearing]
    end
    BallB --> Decision{Load and Speed}
    RollerB --> Decision
    PlainB --> Decision
    Decision --> Out1[High Speed, Low Load: Ball]
    Decision --> Out2[High Load, Medium Speed: Roller]
    Decision --> Out3[High Load, Low Speed, Damping Needed: Plain]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Bearing Operating States and Failure Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Healthy
    Healthy --> Stressed: Apply Load and Speed
    Stressed --> Lubricated: Supply Oil or Grease
    Stressed --> Overheated: Inadequate Lubrication
    Lubricated --> Healthy: Steady State
    Overheated --> Failed: Thermal Runaway
    Stressed --> Fatigued: Long Term Cyclic Loading
    Fatigued --> Spalling: Subsurface Crack Initiation
    Spalling --> Failed
    Healthy --> Misaligned: Shaft Deflection
    Misaligned --> EdgeLoaded: Stress Concentration
    EdgeLoaded --> Failed
    Failed --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** *Define "Basic Dynamic Load Rating" of a rolling-element bearing. Why is the rating life expressed in millions of revolutions and not in hours?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** of a rolling-element bearing is defined as the **constant, stationary radial load** which a sufficiently large population of apparently identical bearings can endure for a **rating life of one million revolutions** with a **90% reliability**, under specified test conditions of lubrication, temperature, and alignment.

It is expressed in **millions of revolutions** (not hours) because:

1. **It makes the rating independent of rotational speed** — a load rating is a *purely mechanical property* of the bearing, and a million revolutions is the same number of "stress cycles" regardless of how fast the bearing rotates.
2. **It enables direct comparison** between bearings used at different speeds.
3. **Hours can be derived from revolutions and speed** using the conversion $L_{10h} = (10^6 \cdot L_{10}) / (60 \cdot n)$, but the reverse is not always accurate if the speed changes during operation.

Hence, the manufacturer catalogs the basic dynamic load rating in revolutions, and the user converts it to hours using the application's specific rotational speed. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** *State the function of the "Equivalent Dynamic Load" P in bearing selection. Why are factors X and Y required in its computation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)** is defined as that *single, hypothetical constant load* (purely radial in nature for radial bearings) which, if applied to the bearing, would produce the *same rolling-contact fatigue life* as the actual combined radial ($F_r$) and axial ($F_a$) loads acting on the bearing in service.

Mathematically, $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (when $F_a / F_r > e$), or $P = F_r$ (when $F_a / F_r \le e$).

The factors **X and Y** are required because:

1. **Internal load distribution is non-uniform:** In a rolling bearing, the load is shared among multiple rolling elements, and the share depends on the contact geometry (contact angle $\alpha$), the number of rolling elements, the raceway curvature, and the presence of an axial component.
2. **Empirical linearization:** The relationship between internal contact stress and external load is non-linear, but for rating-life calculation, ISO 281 / IS 6456 allows a *linear combination* using tabulated coefficients $X$ (radial load factor) and $Y$ (axial load factor).
3. **They depend on the load ratio $F_a / C_0$:** $X$ and $Y$ are not constants; they vary with the ratio of the applied axial load to the static load rating, reflecting the shift in load zone and contact ellipse.

Hence, $X$ and $Y$ are the *empirical constants* that convert a complex biaxial loading into an equivalent uniaxial load suitable for use in the rating life equation. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern with Module Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Selection Design

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Question Style]**

A **single-row deep-groove ball bearing** is to be selected for the **output shaft of a helical gearbox**. The following data is given:

- Radial load on the bearing: $F_r = 5400$ N
- Axial load on the bearing: $F_a = 1850$ N
- Rotational speed: $n = 960$ rpm
- Required life: $L_{10h} = 25{,}000$ hours
- Operating temperature: 80 °C
- Application: Light shock loads, smooth operation

Catalog data for the candidate bearing family:

| Bore (mm) | Outer Dia. (mm) | Width (mm) | C (kN) | C₀ (kN) | e | X | Y | X₀ | Y₀ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 35 | 72 | 17 | 25.5 | 15.3 | 0.28 | 0.56 | 1.71 | 0.6 | 0.5 |
| 40 | 80 | 18 | 30.7 | 19.0 | 0.26 | 0.56 | 1.85 | 0.6 | 0.5 |
| 45 | 85 | 19 | 33.2 | 21.6 | 0.25 | 0.56 | 1.93 | 0.6 | 0.5 |

**Tasks:**
- **(a)** Compute the equivalent dynamic load $P$ and the required dynamic load rating $C_{req}$. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Select a suitable bearing from the catalog and verify the static safety factor and the modified life (assume $\kappa = 0.9$, $a_1 = 1.0$, $a_2 = 1.0$). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equivalent dynamic load $P$:**

Compute the load ratio $F_a / F_r$:

$$
\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1850}{5400} = 0.343
$$

This ratio will be tested against $e$ values. The smallest $e$ in the table is $0.25$. Since $0.343 > 0.25$ for all rows, the axial term matters and we use the full expression $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.

**Step 2 — Apply the equivalent dynamic load formula (using the 40 mm bore row as a working candidate, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.85$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= X F_r + Y F_a \\
&= 0.56 \times 5400 + 1.85 \times 1850 \\
&= 3024 + 3422.5 \\
&= 6446.5 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 6447 \text{ N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert required life to millions of revolutions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 960 \times 25{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= \frac{1{,}440{,}000{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= 1440 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required dynamic load rating (using $p = 3$ for ball bearing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/3} \\
&= 6447 \times 1440^{\,1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the cube root:

$$
1440^{\,1/3} \approx 11.30
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= 6447 \times 11.30 \\
&\approx 72{,}851 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 72.85 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{req} \approx 72.85 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 5 — Bearing selection:** Looking at the catalog:

| Bore (mm) | C (kN) | Adequate? |
|---|---|---|
| 35 | 25.5 | No, far below 72.85 kN |
| 40 | 30.7 | No, far below 72.85 kN |
| 45 | 33.2 | No, far below 72.85 kN |

**Conclusion:** None of the listed bearings in the given catalog meet the requirement $C \ge 72.85$ kN. The designer must **upsize** and select a larger bearing. Suppose the next series in the manufacturer's catalog (not shown here) has a bearing with **bore 60 mm, $C = 78$ kN, $C_0 = 49$ kN, $e = 0.23$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 2.10$**. We re-evaluate $P$ for this candidate.

**Step 6 — Re-compute $P$ with the new candidate's $Y$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{new} &= 0.56 \times 5400 + 2.10 \times 1850 \\
&= 3024 + 3885 \\
&= 6909 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Re-compute $C_{req}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req,new} &= 6909 \times 11.30 \\
&\approx 78{,}072 \text{ N} \approx 78.07 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **just satisfied** by the candidate with $C = 78$ kN. So this bearing is *marginally adequate*.

**Step 8 — Static safety factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \\
&= 0.6 \times 5400 + 0.5 \times 1850 \\
&= 3240 + 925 \\
&= 4165 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= \frac{C_0}{P_0} = \frac{49{,}000}{4165} \approx 11.76
\end{aligned}
$$

This is far above $f_{s,min} = 1.0$, so **static check passes**.

**Step 9 — Modified life:**

Given $a_1 = 1.0$, $a_2 = 1.0$, $\kappa = 0.9$. For $\kappa = 0.9$, the lubrication factor $a_3 \approx 0.8$ (from ISO 281 charts; can be approximated as $a_3 = \kappa^{0.5}$ in the range $0.5 < \kappa < 1.5$).

$$
a_3 \approx \sqrt{0.9} \approx 0.95
$$

**Actually, using the standard ISO 281 chart:** for $\kappa = 0.9$, $a_3 \approx 0.9$ approximately. We will use $a_3 = 0.9$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{na} &= a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10h} \\
&= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.9 \times 25{,}000 \\
&= 22{,}500 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Final verdict:** The modified life of 22,500 h is close to the required 25,000 h. If the application is critical, **upsize further** to the next bearing. If 22,500 h is acceptable, **finalize the selection**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Selected bearing: } d = 60 \text{ mm, } C = 78 \text{ kN, } f_s = 11.76, L_{na} = 22{,}500 \text{ h}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Question Style]**

A **single-row cylindrical roller bearing** is subjected to the following operating conditions:

- Pure radial load: $F_r = 9000$ N (axial load is negligible)
- Rotational speed: $n = 1800$ rpm
- Required life: $L_{10h} = 30{,}000$ hours
- Operating temperature: 65 °C
- Moderate shock loads
- Bore diameter: 50 mm; Pitch diameter: 80 mm; Width: 20 mm
- Manufacturer's rated dynamic load: $C = 76$ kN
- Manufacturer's rated static load: $C_0 = 60$ kN

**Tasks:**
- **(a)** Compute the required dynamic load rating $C_{req}$ and verify the selection. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Compute the modified rating life using $a_1 = 1.0$ (90% reliability), $a_2 = 0.9$ (typical material factor), and $a_3 = 1.0$ (well-lubricated, $\kappa = 1.2$). Also comment on the effect of doubling the load on the bearing life. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equivalent dynamic load:** Since the load is purely radial and the bearing is a cylindrical roller bearing, $P = F_r = 9000$ N.

$$
\boxed{P = 9000 \text{ N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert life to millions of revolutions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 1800 \times 30{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= \frac{3{,}240{,}000{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= 3240 \text{ million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required dynamic load rating** (using $p = 10/3$ for roller bearing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p} \\
&= 9000 \times 3240^{\,3/10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $3240^{\,3/10}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(3240) &\approx 8.083 \\
\frac{3}{10} \times 8.083 &\approx 2.425 \\
e^{2.425} &\approx 11.30
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — that is the cube root, not 3/10. Let me redo:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3240^{0.3} &= e^{0.3 \times \ln 3240} \\
&= e^{0.3 \times 8.083} \\
&= e^{2.425} \\
&\approx 11.30
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= 9000 \times 11.30 \\
&= 101{,}700 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 101.7 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{req} \approx 101.7 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with catalog:** The candidate bearing has $C = 76$ kN, which is **less than** $C_{req} = 101.7$ kN. Therefore, **the selected bearing is NOT adequate**. The designer must select a larger bearing (next series) with $C \ge 101.7$ kN.

**Step 5 — Comment:** The current bearing can deliver a rating life of:

$$
L_{10,\text{actual}} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{76000}{9000}\right)^{10/3} = (8.444)^{3.333}
$$

$$
(8.444)^{3.333} = e^{3.333 \times \ln 8.444} = e^{3.333 \times 2.134} = e^{7.114} \approx 1227 \text{ million rev}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
L_{10h, \text{actual}} = \frac{1227 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1800} \approx 11{,}360 \text{ hours}
$$

So the selected bearing gives only **~11,360 h of life** instead of the required 30,000 h. The design fails the rating-life requirement.

**Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 6 — Modified rating life** (assuming the bearing is upsized so that $C = 102$ kN, marginally satisfying $C_{req}$):

$$
L_{10h} = 30{,}000 \text{ h}
$$

With $a_1 = 1.0$, $a_2 = 0.9$, $a_3 = 1.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{na} &= a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10h} \\
&= 1.0 \times 0.9 \times 1.0 \times 30{,}000 \\
&= 27{,}000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{na} = 27{,}000 \text{ hours}}
$$

This is **less than 30,000 h** because $a_2 = 0.9$ (a slightly inferior material factor) reduces the achievable life. To achieve 30,000 h, the designer must either:
- Upsize the bearing further (e.g., $C = 110$ kN), or
- Use a higher-grade bearing material ($a_2 = 1.0$ or higher).

**Step 7 — Effect of doubling the load:**

For a roller bearing, $p = 10/3$. If the load doubles:

$$
\frac{L_{new}}{L_{old}} = \left(\frac{P_{old}}{P_{new}}\right)^p = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10/3} = 2^{-10/3}
$$

$$
2^{-10/3} = \frac{1}{2^{10/3}} = \frac{1}{2^{3.333}} = \frac{1}{10.08} \approx 0.0992
$$

So the life is reduced to **approximately 9.92% of the original life** — a drop of nearly **90%**.

For a 30,000 h life, doubling the load gives:

$$
L_{new} = 30{,}000 \times 0.0992 \approx 2976 \text{ hours}
$$

This is a **devastating penalty** and explains why bearing life is so sensitive to overload.

> **Final verdict:** The selected 76 kN bearing is **inadequate**; the design must be revised to a bearing with $C \ge 102$ kN. The modified life on the next-size bearing is **27,000 h**, which is just below the requirement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L_{10}$ (million rev) with $L_{10h}$ (hours):** A large number of students forget to multiply by $10^6$ when converting revolutions to hours. Always write: $L_{10h} = (10^6 \cdot L_{10})/(60 n)$. A slip here costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the condition $F_a/F_r \le e$:** Many students apply $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ even when $F_a/F_r \le e$, leading to over-estimation of $P$ and an overly conservative (and over-sized) bearing. The 1-mark is for checking this threshold.
>
> 3. **Wrong exponent $p$:** Ball bearings: $p = 3$. Roller bearings: $p = 10/3$. Mixing them up leads to a major error in $C_{req}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the static check:** $C_{req}$ is a dynamic rating. The static safety factor $f_s = C_0/P_0$ must be checked separately. Many students omit this and lose 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not commenting on the modified life:** ISO 281 modified life $L_{na} = a_1 a_2 a_3 L_{10}$ is increasingly asked in KTU 2024. At least mention the three factors and their typical values.
>
> 6. **No thermal check:** For high-speed applications ($d \cdot n > 500{,}000$), a thermal balance check is mandatory. Forgetting it costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Not stating the assumption of 90% reliability:** The rating life $L_{10}$ is *defined* at 90% reliability. A student who doesn't say so is implicitly missing 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Bearing selection = choosing bearing type, size, and configuration** such that it can transmit the applied loads for the desired life under given conditions.
- [x] **Two families:** Rolling-element (antifriction) and Sliding-contact (plain/journal).
- [x] **Basic Dynamic Load Rating $C$** is the load at which 90% of bearings survive 1 million revolutions.
- [x] **Basic Rating Life $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$** in millions of revolutions.
- [x] **$p = 3$ (ball bearings)**, **$p = 10/3$ (roller bearings)**.
- [x] **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ when $F_a / F_r > e$, else $P = F_r$.
- [x] **Life conversion:** $L_{10} = (60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}) / 10^6$.
- [x] **Required dynamic load rating:** $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$.
- [x] **Equivalent static load:** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$.
- [x] **Static safety factor:** $f_s = C_0 / P_0 \ge 1$ to 2 (depending on shock).
- [x] **Modified life (ISO 281:2007):** $L_{na} = a_1 a_2 a_3 L_{10}$.
  - $a_1$: reliability factor (1.0 for 90%, 1.62 for 99%).
  - $a_2$: material/quality factor.
  - $a_3$: lubrication/viscosity ratio factor.
- [x] **Viscosity ratio:** $\kappa = \nu / \nu_1$ where $\nu_1 \approx 45000 \, n^{-0.83} d^{-0.5}$.
- [x] **Selection algorithm:** Compute $P \rightarrow$ Compute $L_{10} \rightarrow$ Compute $C_{req} \rightarrow$ Pick bearing with $C \ge C_{req} \rightarrow$ Check $f_s \rightarrow$ Check thermal balance.
- [x] **Doubling the load reduces life to $\approx 12.5\%$ (ball) or $\approx 9.9\%$ (roller)** — a steep penalty.
- [x] **Always state assumptions:** 90% reliability, standard lubrication, normal alignment, room temperature (or specify actual).
- [x] **Catalog use:** Bore $\rightarrow$ Outer Diameter $\rightarrow$ Width $\rightarrow$ $C$, $C_0$ $\rightarrow$ Check $C \ge C_{req}$ and $f_s \ge f_{s,min}$.
- [x] **Thermal check:** For $d \cdot n$ values above 500,000, a heat-balance calculation is required: $Q_{gen} = \mu P v$ vs $Q_{diss} = h A \Delta T$.
- [x] **Failure modes to remember:** Spalling (rolling fatigue), Brinelling (static overload), False brinelling (vibration at rest), Abrasive wear (contamination), Corrosion (moisture), Seizure (overheating).
